Module 00 · §0 AI Security Overview · OWASP AI Exchange

The OWASP AI Exchange: mission, scope, and how to use it

~16 minCovers guide pp. 11–23

Before you can defend AI systems you need to know where the authoritative knowledge lives, what it covers, and how to navigate it. This lesson introduces the OWASP AI Exchange — the 300+ page consensus resource on AI security and privacy — its mission, its unusually broad definition of "AI", how it feeds international standards like ISO/IEC 27090 and the EU AI Act, and the practical entry points you will use for the rest of this academy.

What the AI Exchange is

The guide opens with its own summary, worth quoting because every phrase is load-bearing:

"Welcome to the go-to comprehensive resource for AI security & privacy - over 300 pages of practical advice and references on protecting AI, and data-centric systems from threats - where AI consists of ALL AI: Analytical AI, Discriminative AI, Generative AI and heuristic systems. This content serves as key bookmark for practitioners, and is contributed actively and substantially to international standards such as ISO/IEC and the AI Act through official standard partnerships. Through broad collaboration with key institutes and SDOs, the Exchange represents the consensus on AI security and privacy."

The OWASP AI Exchange is an open collaborative OWASP Flagship project that has "open sourced the global discussion on the security and privacy of AI and data-centric systems". It provides a comprehensive framework of AI threats, controls, and related best practices. Through a unique official liaison partnership, its content feeds into standards work: 70 pages contributed to the EU AI Act security standardization, 70 pages contributed to ISO/IEC 27090 (AI security), contributions to ISO/IEC 27091 (AI privacy), and integration with OpenCRE — which links the AI Exchange with many AI security standards and guidelines and makes it accessible through the security chatbot OpenCRE-Chat.

It is an open-source living publication: displayed at owaspai.org, edited via a GitHub repository ("Edit on Github" links), and evolving constantly through open-source continuous delivery. The authors group consists of over 70 carefully selected experts — researchers, practitioners, vendors, data scientists — and the wider community is welcome to contribute. The license is CC0 1.0: you can use any part freely, without copyright and without attribution (crediting or linking is appreciated but not required).

"AI" means all AI — not just GenAI

The Exchange deliberately scopes "AI" far wider than the current generative wave. The scope statement (p. 23) reads: "The AI exchange covers both heuristic artificial intelligence (e.g., expert systems) and machine learning. This means that when we talk about an AI system, it can for example be a Large Language Model, a linear regression function, a rule-based system, or a lookup table based on statistics." Throughout the guide it is made clear which threats and controls play a role and when.

  • Analytical AI — models used for prediction and analytics (e.g., forecasting, scoring).
  • Discriminative AI — models that classify or decide (e.g., fraud classifiers, image recognition).
  • Generative AI — models that produce content (text, images, code), such as LLMs.
  • Heuristic systems — hand-built rule-based systems and expert systems, with no learned model at all.

Beyond AI, the guide covers data-centric systems: AI systems plus "big data" systems that don't have an AI model (data warehousing, BI, reporting, big data). Many Exchange threats and controls — data poisoning, data supply chain management, data pipeline security — apply to those systems too.

⚠ Common misconception

"AI security" is not a synonym for "LLM security". A linear regression function scoring loan applications and a statistics lookup table are AI systems in scope here, and they suffer poisoning, evasion, and data-disclosure threats just as LLMs do. The guide stresses: "AI is much more than Generative AI." If you only secure your chatbots, you have not secured your AI estate.

The working definition of security

The guide fixes its definition early: "Security here means preventing unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction. Modification includes manipulating the behaviour of an AI model in unwanted ways." That last sentence is the pivotal extension over classic infosec: for an AI system, integrity includes the integrity of learned behaviour — a model that still runs but decides wrongly because an attacker nudged it has been "modified" just as surely as tampered code.

Mission, vision, and why the Exchange exists

The mission: "to be the global go-to resource for security and privacy practitioners working with AI and data-centric systems—bringing alignment and encouraging collaboration across initiatives", creating "a safe, open, and independent space where everyone can find and share insights". Also stated as: "to enable people to find and use information to ensure that AI systems are secure and privacy preserving."

The vision names the real problem: the main challenge for people is finding the right information and then understanding it so it can be turned into action. The standards and guideline landscape is complex, inconsistent, fragmented, and incomplete — with quality and staleness issues — caused by a general lack of AI security expertise in the industry. "What resource to use?" The Exchange answers with eight achieved properties:

  • AUTHORITATIVE — active alignment with other resources through careful analysis and close collaboration, particularly substantial contribution to ISO/IEC standards and the AI Act, so the Exchange represents consensus.
  • OPEN — anybody can contribute, with strong quality assurance including a screening process for authors.
  • FREE — anybody can use it in any way; free of copyright and attribution.
  • COVERAGE — comprehensive guidance instead of a selected set of issues (a top 10 is for awareness), and about all AI and data-intensive systems.
  • UNIFIED — one coherent resource instead of fragmented, disconnected resources.
  • CLEAR — clear explanation including the why and how, not just the what.
  • LINKED — referring to other sources instead of complex text that incorrectly suggests completeness; this makes the Exchange the place to start.
  • EVOLVING — continuous updates instead of occasional publications.

The guide adds a candid note: "Producing and continuously updating a comprehensive and coherent quality resource requires a strong coordinated approach. It is much harder than an approach of every person for themselves. But necessary."

History and leadership

The Exchange was founded in 2022 by Rob van der Veer — bridge builder for security standards, Chief AI Officer at Software Improvement Group, 33 years of experience in AI & security, lead author of ISO/IEC 5338 (AI lifecycle), founding father of OpenCRE, and active in ISO/IEC 27090, ISO/IEC 27091 and the EU AI Act work in CEN/CENELEC, where he was elected co-editor by the EU member states. The project started as the "AI security and privacy guide" (October 2022) and was rebranded a year later as "AI Exchange" to highlight global collaboration. In March 2025 it was awarded OWASP Flagship status because of its critical importance, together with the OWASP GenAI Security Project.

Project leadership: Rob van der Veer (Project Founder and Lead Editor), Aruneesh Salhotra (Promotion, Collaboration, and Fundraising), Behnaz Karimi (Red Teaming, Training, and People Matters).

▸ Worked example

Industry testimonials in the guide show who relies on this material. Dimitri van Zantvliet (Director Cybersecurity, Dutch Railways): "A risk-based, context-aware approach—like the one OWASP Exchange champions—not only supports the responsible use of AI, but ensures that real threats are mitigated without burdening engineers with irrelevant checklists." Sri Manda (Chief Security & Trust Officer, Peloton Interactive) praises it for "bringing the security community to the table to ensure we get standards that work". Prateek Kalasannavar (Staff AI Security Engineer, Lenovo): "OWASP AI Exchange serves as a vital anchor for mapping evolving attack surfaces, codifying AI-specific testing methodologies, and driving community-aligned standards for AI risk mitigation. It bridges the gap between theory and engineering."

The OWASP AI ecosystem: Exchange vs GenAI Security Project

OWASP runs two flagship AI projects, and knowing which to reach for matters:

  • The OWASP AI Exchange — the comprehensive core framework of AI security fundamentals: threats, controls and best practices for all AI, actively aligned with international standards and feeding into them; covers privacy as well as security.
  • The OWASP GenAI Security Project — an umbrella project publishing documents on Generative AI security, including the LLM AI Security & Governance Checklist and the LLM Top 10, featuring the most severe security risks of Large Language Models.

Rules of thumb from the guide: if you want to ensure security or privacy of your AI or data-centric system (GenAI or not), or align with AI security standards, start at the AI Exchange — it refers you onward to GenAI project material where necessary. If you want quick awareness of top LLM security concerns, use the LLM Top 10 — knowing that it is intentionally not complete: for example, it does not include the security of prompts.

OpenCRE.org, established under the OWASP Integration Standards project (from Project Wayfinder), holds a catalog of common requirements across security standards inside and outside OWASP. OpenCRE links all AI security controls and threats across standards and guidelines, and the Exchange has a mechanism to insert correct references for all sections at owaspai.org — effectively making the Exchange a hub for the AI security standards landscape. Further AI projects exist at OWASP beyond these two.

flowchart TD
  EX["OWASP AI Exchange - comprehensive foundational framework"]
  ISO["ISO/IEC 27090 - AI security standard"]
  ACT["EU AI Act security standard"]
  SANS["SANS Institute"]
  IND["Strong and diverse industry and institute collaboration"]
  LLM["OWASP LLM Top 10 - quick risk awareness"]
  AGT["OWASP Agentic AI Top 10 - quick risk awareness"]
  EX -->|feeds content into| ISO
  EX -->|feeds content into| ACT
  SANS -->|formal two-way collaboration| EX
  IND -->|two-way collaboration| EX
  EX -.->|complements| LLM
  EX -.->|complements| AGT

Figure — the OWASP AI Exchange ecosystem (rebuilt from the guide's p. 17 illustration): the Exchange feeds international standards upward, collaborates bidirectionally with institutes like SANS, and complements the awareness-oriented Top 10 lists.

The guide also carries an introductory "AI Overview Video" (p. 13) summarizing the Exchange: aligned with the LLM Top 10, NIST, MITRE ATLAS, CISA, CSA, the Alan Turing Institute and AISI; free and open; contributions from academia, start-ups, data scientists, legal experts and vendors; and the official CEN/CENELEC liaison partnership feeding the EU AI Act.

The FOUNDATION acrostic

What makes the Exchange special is summarized as FOUNDATION:

  • F – Fundamentals: comprehensive threat & control model
  • O – One integrated body of knowledge (as opposed to a collection of documents)
  • U – Universal: covers all AI & data-centric systems
  • N – No copyright restrictions (fully shareable)
  • D – Data & privacy included
  • A – Aligned with international standards
  • T – Technically grounded (engineering-oriented)
  • I – Iteratively updated (continuous instead of yearly)
  • O – Open (any expert can contribute)
  • N – Networked – bridge between standards, researchers, and practitioners

The Exchange also maintains "strong and diverse collaboration with industry and institutes", including a formal ongoing collaboration with SANS Institute to share expertise and support broad education.

Why does the Exchange position itself as a complement to — not a competitor of — the LLM Top 10?
The LLM Top 10 is deliberately a quick-awareness document: a curated shortlist of the most severe LLM risks, intentionally incomplete (it omits, for example, the security of prompts). The Exchange is the comprehensive foundational framework covering all AI, all threats, and all controls. Awareness lists get attention; the Exchange is where you go to actually engineer the defenses.

Roadmap: where the Exchange is heading

Priority content workstreams aim to deliver a major update before summer 2026:

  • Agentic AI — extending the Exchange with the emerging security challenges of autonomous multi-agent systems (led by Chris Cochran).
  • Harmonization — solving gaps and mapping the Exchange to leading frameworks including NIST, MITRE ATLAS, and ETSI (led by Rob van der Veer and Yuvaraj).
  • Red Teaming (Testing) — extending the testing section and evaluating open-source tools for Predictive and Generative AI to withstand adversarial attack (led by Behnaz Karimi).
  • EU AI Act Flow Back — integrating the group's extensive research for its EU AI Act security-standard contributions (led by Rob van der Veer).
  • Step-by-Step Guide — a structured path for organizations to implement AI security processes (led by Aruneesh Salhotra).
  • Improved AI-findability of owaspai.org — for training sets and web search; mostly getting links.

Specialized project groups: Risk Analysis (formal processes for AI risk assessment and responsibility assignment), Education Guidance (training guidance and security mindsets for data scientists and developers), Legislation (tracking global laws and regulations), Requirements (the AI Act and ISO 27090 contributions group), and Mathematical (completeness of the mathematical data-science topics for AI robustness and security). Strategic and operational workstreams: Standards alignment (engagements, partnerships, events, and mapping to drive alignment — goal: clarity for practitioners and supervisors; led by Rob van der Veer and Aruneesh Salhotra), Promotion & Outreach, and Sponsorship & Fundraising (both led by Aruneesh Salhotra).

How to use the document

The Exchange is "a single coherent resource on the security and privacy of AI systems", presented as a website divided over several pages — containing threats, controls, guidelines, tests and references. Your entry point depends on your need:

  • Ask any question on AI security — the AI Exchange AGENT answers AI security/privacy questions based on Exchange content (uses a Google service, requires a Google account).
  • Learn what the AI Exchange is — see About.
  • Start AI security as an organization — see "How to organize AI security" (next lesson).
  • Start AI security as an individual — follow the "Learn AI security" path below, or use the references section's large table of training material.
  • Threat model your system — "If you want your AI system to be secure, start with threat modeling to guide you through a number of questions, resulting in the threats that apply. And when you click on those threats you'll find the controls (countermeasures) to check for, or to implement." Alternatively, the THREAT ADVISOR asks you questions about your system and threat-models for you: describe your system briefly; your data remains in your own Google workspace.

The 11-step "Learn AI security" path

  1. First study the brief AI security essentials for the big picture (lesson 3).
  2. Select the threats relevant to your practice via threat modeling — or let AI interview you — or skip this step to learn the complete threat picture.
  3. If you're involved in Agentic AI, see the brief discussion of how agentic threats are covered (lesson 6).
  4. If you run a ready-made model, look at the threat model on ready-made models.
  5. See your threats in context in the AI threat model and the AI security matrix.
  6. Click on your relevant threats in that overview for more information and protection guidance.
  7. To find what to do against a specific threat, check that threat's Controls section, or the periodic table which lists the controls for every threat.
  8. To learn how controls play a role and interact in the bigger picture: see the controls overview.
  9. If privacy is in scope: see the privacy section.
  10. If you're involved in testing: see the testing section.
  11. A great way to understand AI threats is to act as an attacker — the guide recommends PwnzzAI!, a hacking lab project with the Exchange as founding partner. "A great exercise!"

To learn more about education programs, see #SEC EDUCATE (control card in lesson 3). If you prefer one document, a PDF snapshot of the Exchange is downloadable. For lookup: use the search function or the index; for more information or training material, see the references.

Document structure

On larger screens, page structure appears in the left sidebar and within-page structure on the right; on smaller screens both are in the menu. The main structure:

PageContents
(0) AI security overviewOverview of AI security and discussions of various topics — the chapter this module covers
(1) General controlsSuch as AI governance
(2) Input threatsSuch as evasion attacks
(3) Development-time threatsSuch as data poisoning
(4) Runtime conventional security threatsSuch as leaking input
(5) AI security testingTesting methodology and tools
(6) AI privacyPrivacy threats and controls
(7) ReferencesStandards, training material, further reading

The overview chapter continues with: a high-level overview of threats; overviews of threats and controls (the matrix, the periodic table, and the navigator); risk analysis to select relevant threats and controls; and other topics — heuristic systems, responsible AI, generative AI, the NCSC/CISA guidelines, and copyright. The table of contents also flags highlights: the Threat matrix, the Agentic AI perspective, the Navigator, the Periodic table of threats and controls, Threat modeling, and Prompt injection protection.

Your organization uses only third-party SaaS AI (no model training). Which parts of the Exchange's guidance matter most to you?
Threat modeling to select applicable threats, the section on ready-made models, and supply chain management — because most controls become your supplier's responsibility, which you must verify. The guide notes that most relevant controls are familiar conventional security countermeasures unless you train your own model.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 11–23. Permalinks: owaspai.org/go/toc/, owaspai.org/go/about/, owaspai.org/go/aiatowasp/, owaspai.org/go/roadmap/, owaspai.org/go/document/. Standards fed: EU AI Act (CEN/CENELEC), ISO/IEC 27090 (AI security), ISO/IEC 27091 (AI privacy), ISO/IEC 5338 (AI lifecycle), OpenCRE. Harmonization targets: NIST, MITRE ATLAS, ETSI. Related canon: the NIST AI Risk Management Framework organizes AI risk work into Govern / Map / Measure / Manage — you will see a similar governance-first logic in the Exchange's G.U.A.R.D method next lesson.

Key Points

  • The OWASP AI Exchange is a 300+ page open-source living publication — the comprehensive consensus framework of AI security threats, controls and best practices, licensed CC0 1.0.
  • "AI" here means ALL AI: Analytical, Discriminative, Generative, and heuristic systems — an LLM, a linear regression function, a rule-based system, or a statistics lookup table; plus non-AI data-centric ("big data") systems.
  • Security is defined as preventing unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction — where modification includes manipulating model behaviour in unwanted ways.
  • Through an official CEN/CENELEC liaison, the Exchange contributed ~70 pages each to the EU AI Act security standard and ISO/IEC 27090, and links the standards landscape via OpenCRE.
  • Exchange = comprehensive foundation; OWASP GenAI Security Project's LLM Top 10 = quick awareness, intentionally incomplete (e.g., no prompt security).
  • FOUNDATION summarizes its qualities; founded 2022 by Rob van der Veer; OWASP Flagship since March 2025; formal SANS collaboration.
  • Entry points: threat modeling (or the THREAT ADVISOR agent), the 11-step learning path, the AI Exchange AGENT, the periodic table, and the PwnzzAI! hacking lab.
Module 00 · §0 How to organize AI security · OWASP AI Exchange

G.U.A.R.D. — organizing AI security in your organization

~11 minCovers guide pp. 24–26

"Organizations: start here!" — the guide's five-step G.U.A.R.D method turns AI security from an intimidating research field into an ordered management program: Govern, Understand, Adapt, Reduce, Demonstrate. After this lesson you can lay out the adoption path for any organization, and argue its most contrarian principle: sometimes the best AI security control is not building the AI at all.

The premise: "While Artificial Intelligence (AI) offers tremendous opportunities, it also brings new risks including security threats. It is therefore imperative to approach AI applications with a clear understanding of potential threats and the controls against them. AI can only prosper if we can trust it."

5 steps for AI security — G.U.A.R.D (guide p. 24)
1 · GovernAI governance program
→
2 · Understandthreats & controls that apply
→
3 · Adaptexisting security practices
→
4 · Reducepotential impact
→
5 · Demonstrateevidence & assurance

Step 1 — Govern

"Start implementing general AI Governance so the organization can manage AI: know where it is applied, what people's responsibilities are, establish policies, do impact assessment, arrange compliance, organize education, et cetera." The guide points to #AI PROGRAM for guidance, including a quickstart. Crucially, this is "a general AI management process — not just security": you cannot secure what you have not inventoried, and you cannot assign controls without assigned responsibilities.

#AI PROGRAMInstall an AI governance programgovernance · general controls
IntentGive the organization the ability to manage AI at all: an inventory of where AI is applied, defined responsibilities, policies, impact assessment, compliance arrangements, and education. Security controls hang off this scaffolding — without it nobody owns the risk.
ImplementThe Exchange provides guidance including a quickstart. Start from the AI inventory, assign responsibilities, establish policy, run impact assessments, arrange compliance and education. It is a general AI management process — broader than security alone.
LimitsGovernance enables but does not itself stop attacks; it must be followed by the Understand/Adapt/Reduce steps. Detailed treatment lives in the guide's General controls chapter (§1.1).
StandardsReferenced from G.U.A.R.D step 1 (owaspai.org/go/organize/); aligns with EU AI Act governance duties and NIST AI RMF "Govern" function.

Step 2 — Understand

  • Based on the inventory of your AI applications and AI ideas, understand which threats apply, using the decision tree in the risk analysis section.
  • Make sure engineers and security professionals understand those relevant threats and their controls, using the relevant threat sections and the corresponding process controls and technical controls. Note: "most of these controls are about familiar conventional security countermeasures, unless you are training your own model."
  • Use the courses and resources in the references section to support the understanding.
  • Distinguish between controls your organization must implement and those that are your supplier's responsibility — make the latter part of your supply chain management. See the section on ready-made models if you don't train your own models.
◆ Senior insight

The "unless you are training your own model" clause is the single most useful scoping heuristic in the whole chapter. Organizations consuming ready-made models mostly need conventional security done well, plus supplier assurance. The exotic data-science controls (adversarial training, poison-robust learning) only enter your backlog when you own the training pipeline. Scoping this early saves months of misdirected effort.

Step 3 — Adapt

  • Adapt your security practices to include AI security assets, threats and controls from the guide. New regulation can be outcome-based (e.g., the EU AI Act) instead of control-focused (like ISO/IEC 27001) — so extend the Information Security Management System (ISMS) with assurance processes showing that risks have been sufficiently mitigated.
  • Document risk assessment, control selection, the rationale, and the risk-mitigation evidence — a good idea in general, and mandatory for the AI Act.
  • Adapt your threat modelling to include the AI security threat modeling approach, and do cross-team threat modelling involving representation from all engineers.
  • Adapt your testing to include AI-specific security testing.
  • Adapt your supply chain management to include data, model, and hosting management, and make sure suppliers take care of the identified threats.
  • If you develop AI systems (even without training your own models): adapt your software development practices and secure development program to involve AI engineering activities.
Why does outcome-based regulation like the EU AI Act change how your ISMS must work?
A control-focused standard (ISO/IEC 27001 style) lets you demonstrate compliance by showing prescribed controls are in place. An outcome-based law requires you to show the outcome — risks sufficiently mitigated. That demands assurance processes: documented risk assessment, control selection with rationale, and evidence that mitigations work, all of which the AI Act makes mandatory.

Step 4 — Reduce

"Reduce potential impact by minimizing or obfuscating sensitive data and limiting the impact of unwanted behaviour (e.g., managing privileges, guardrails, human oversight etc. Basically: apply Murphy's law). This is critical because AI models can always be wrong or manipulated." This is the guide's core defensive philosophy: no combination of preventive controls makes model behaviour trustworthy, so cap what a wrong or hijacked model can leak (data minimization) and do (privilege and oversight limits).

Step 5 — Demonstrate

"Establish evidence of responsible AI security through transparency, testing, documentation, and communication. Prove to management, regulators, and clients that your AI systems are under control and that the applied safeguards work as intended." Security you cannot demonstrate does not exist for the regulator — or for the customer deciding whether to trust you.

Think before you build

The section closes with a warning worth internalizing verbatim in substance: think before you build an AI solution. AI can have fantastic benefits, but it always needs to be balanced with risks. Securing AI is typically harder than securing non-AI systems — first because it's relatively new, but also because there is a level of uncertainty in all data-driven technology. In the case of LLMs, we are dealing with the fluidity of natural language: "LLMs essentially offer an unstable, undocumented interface with an unclear set of policies." Security measures applied to AI often cannot offer security properties to the standard you are used to with other software. Consider whether AI is the appropriate technology choice for the problem. "Removing an unnecessary AI component eliminates all AI-related risks."

◆ Senior insight

This is the cheapest control in the entire guide, and the one most rarely proposed: deletion. When a deterministic rule, a regex, or a lookup table solves the problem, choosing it over a model removes prompt injection, poisoning, inversion, exfiltration and hallucination in one stroke — at zero runtime cost. A senior engineer's first threat-modeling question should be "does this component need to be AI at all?"

Why does the guide invoke Murphy's law for AI specifically?
Because model behaviour is statistical, not specified: an AI model "can always be wrong or manipulated", no matter how well it was trained or filtered. Murphy's law — assume it will go wrong — dictates designing for failure: minimize the sensitive data available to leak, and limit privileges and add oversight so unwanted behaviour has a bounded blast radius.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 24–26, permalink owaspai.org/go/organize/. EU AI Act (outcome-based; documentation mandatory), ISO/IEC 27001 (control-focused ISMS, contrasted). The G.U.A.R.D sequence parallels NIST AI RMF: Govern maps to RMF "Govern", Understand to "Map", Demonstrate to "Measure", Adapt/Reduce to "Manage".

Key Points

  • G.U.A.R.D = Govern, Understand, Adapt, Reduce, Demonstrate — the ordered path for organizing AI security.
  • Govern first: inventory, responsibilities, policies, impact assessment, compliance, education — via #AI PROGRAM (general AI management, not just security).
  • Understand: use the risk-analysis decision tree; most controls are conventional unless you train your own model; split control ownership with suppliers.
  • Adapt: extend ISMS with assurance processes for outcome-based regulation (EU AI Act vs control-focused ISO/IEC 27001); document risk assessment, selection, rationale, evidence — mandatory under the AI Act.
  • Reduce: apply Murphy's law — models can always be wrong or manipulated; minimize/obfuscate data and limit behaviour impact.
  • Demonstrate: prove to management, regulators, and clients that safeguards work as intended.
  • LLMs are "an unstable, undocumented interface with an unclear set of policies" — removing an unnecessary AI component eliminates all AI-related risks.
Module 00 · §0 Essentials · OWASP AI Exchange

Essentials I — AI-specific threats and the "Manage" controls

~16 minCovers guide pp. 26–30

The Essentials section is THE starting point the guide gives you for understanding AI security foundations — established in close collaboration with industry and institutes, with complete alignment to the SANS Critical AI security guidelines, ISO/IEC 27090 (AI security), and prEN18282 (Cybersecurity for the AI Act). This lesson teaches the AI-specific threat families and the first of four essential control categories: Manage.

The AI-specific threats, in three groups

The Essentials organizes what is new about AI security into three groups: threats through the model's input, threats from new kinds of suppliers, and conventional threats against new kinds of assets.

1. Model input threats

These are attacks executed simply by using the model — providing input and reading output (inference). No break-in required.

  • Evasion: misleading a model by crafting data to force wrong decisions.
  • Prompt injection: misleading a model by crafting instructions to manipulate behaviour.
  • Extracting data from the model: training data, augmentation data (including system prompts), or input.
  • Extracting the model itself by querying the model.
  • Resource exhaustion through use.
⚔ Attacker's view

Input threats are the cheapest attacks in AI: the attack channel is the product's own front door, often anonymous and internet-facing. The guide adds a note that compounds this: "Attackers that obtain the model (or a similar one) can perform or prepare input attacks efficiently and without being noticed" — offline, against their own copy, with unlimited queries, then fire the perfected input once at the real system. Model confidentiality is therefore also an input-attack economics problem.

2. New suppliers

AI introduces suppliers you didn't have before, each a channel for corruption: external data, external models, and external model hosting. Supply chain management must extend to cover all three.

3. New AI assets with conventional threats

  • Training data / augmentation data (e.g., system prompts) — can leak, and poisoning this data manipulates model behaviour.
  • Model — can suffer leaking during development or leaking during runtime; and for integrity: poisoning during development or poisoning during runtime.
  • Input — can leak.
  • Output — can contain injection attacks.
⚠ Common misconception

"AI threats" are not all exotic. Most of this third group is classic CIA (confidentiality-integrity-availability) security applied to assets that didn't exist before: a system prompt is a secret, a model file is intellectual property, a training database is an integrity-critical input to production behaviour. The attack is old; the asset is new.

Why does the guide treat a system prompt as "augmentation data", and why does that matter?
Augmentation data is data used to augment model operation at runtime — system prompts and, e.g., retrieval data. Classifying the system prompt as an asset means it inherits both confidentiality threats (it can be extracted through the model's output) and integrity threats (manipulating it manipulates model behaviour, like runtime poisoning). Naming the asset is what makes the threats enumerable.

The essentials map: threats against architecture, controls in layers

The guide's "AI security essentials" figure (p. 30) maps the threat groups onto a system architecture and stacks eight color-coded control bars beside it — colors tying each control sub-category to the threat family it addresses (green = supply chain, orange = conventional, purple = input/model-engineering; grey, blue and brown bars are cross-cutting).

AI security essentials — threats, architecture, and the eight control sub-categories (guide p. 30)
Threats
Conventional threatsleaking and poisoning — target training data, model, augmentation data
Input threatsMislead: evasion, prompt injection · Resource exhaustion · Extract: model, data — target the Input
Supply chain threatscorrupted external data, models, hosting
Architecture
(Training data)optional / externally suppliable
(Machine learning)training pipeline
AI Modelparameters
(Model hosting)runtime serving
Augmentation dataincl. system prompts
Input / Outputon Application & infrastructure
Controls (8 sub-categories)
Governance / Risk / ComplianceManage
Minimize/obfuscate dataLimit
Extended supply chain managementManage
Conventional security controlsManage
Data/model engineering controlsHave resilient models
Model I/O handlingWatch
MonitoringWatch
Limit model behaviourLimit

The controls for AI security divide into four essential categories and 8 essential sub-categories, linking to over 50 individual controls discussed in depth at the Exchange:

CategorySub-categoriesIdea
ManageGovernance/Risk/Compliance · Supply chain management · Conventional security controlsExtend what you already run — GRC, supplier management, classic security — to the new AI assets
Have resilient modelsData/model engineering controlsBuild models that resist input attacks — the work of data and model engineers
WatchModel I/O handling · MonitoringFilter, stop or alert on suspicious input/output at runtime; monitor inference and general usage
LimitMinimize/obfuscate sensitive data · Limit model behaviourCap what a wrong or manipulated model can leak and do

Attribution: many experts and organizations contributed to the essentials overview — including close collaboration with SANS Institute, ensuring alignment with SANS' Critical AI security guidelines; SANS and the Exchange have a formal ongoing collaboration to share expertise and support broad education.

Category "Manage" — extend what you already have

Extend Governance, Risk, and Compliance

"In order to secure AI, you need overview, analysis, policy, training, responsibilities, and control." Four controls (the first, #AI PROGRAM, was carded in lesson 2):

#DEV PROGRAMInclude AI development in your development programgovernance · Manage / GRC
IntentBring AI engineering (data science work) under the organization's development governance, so AI is built with the same discipline — requirements, review, lifecycle — as other software.
ImplementExtend existing development processes to cover data and model engineering activities; deep-dive in the guide's General controls chapter.
LimitsProcess control: it enables quality and accountability but must be paired with technical controls to stop specific attacks.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/GRC (owaspai.org/go/essentials/); ISO/IEC 5338 (AI lifecycle) is the natural companion standard.
#CHECK COMPLIANCEArrange and verify regulatory compliancegovernance · Manage / GRC
IntentEnsure AI applications meet applicable laws and regulations (e.g., the EU AI Act) — compliance is one of the things the Govern step must "arrange".
ImplementTrack applicable regulation per AI application from the inventory; the Exchange's Legislation project group tracks global laws and regulations.
LimitsCompliance demonstrates diligence, not immunity: outcome-based laws still require evidence that risks are actually mitigated (see G.U.A.R.D "Demonstrate").
StandardsEssentials, Manage/GRC; EU AI Act; ISO/IEC 27001 ISMS context.
#SEC EDUCATEEducate people on AI securitygovernance · Manage / GRC
IntentClose the expertise gap the Exchange's vision names as the root cause of the fragmented standards landscape: train engineers, data scientists and security professionals on AI threats and controls.
ImplementThe guide points here for education programs; use the references section's training-material table, the Exchange's Education Guidance project group output, and hands-on practice such as the PwnzzAI! hacking lab.
LimitsAwareness decays and tooling changes fast in AI security — education must be continuous, mirroring the Exchange's own continuous-delivery model.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/GRC; SANS collaboration supports broad education; owaspai.org/go/essentials/.

Extend supply chain management

Extend supply chain management "to incorporate suppliers providing data, models, and hosting" — the Exchange treats this as a control group (Supply chain management) with its own deep-dive section. Every one of the three supplier types is a poisoning or leakage channel: a corrupted public dataset, a backdoored downloaded model, a hosting provider with access to your parameters.

Extend conventional security controls to AI-specific assets

The largest sub-category: point your existing security machinery at the new assets. The guide splits it into managing security, development-time, and runtime controls.

Managing security

#SEC PROGRAMInclude AI assets and threats in your security programprocess · Manage / conventional
IntentMake the information security program aware of AI-specific assets (training data, models, augmentation data, input/output) and their threats — the Threats overview section explicitly defines AI-specific threats as "threats to AI assets (see #SEC PROGRAM)".
ImplementRegister AI assets in the ISMS; the security program subsection lists the assets appearing in the threat map. Treat the Exchange as an extension of your existing security program, not a replacement.
LimitsCovers AI-specific assets only; threats to other assets (e.g., a user database) remain covered by your other resources — per the AI security formula in lesson 5.
StandardsEssentials, Manage; ISO/IEC 27001 ISMS extension; owaspai.org/go/essentials/.
#SECDEV PROGRAMInclude AI engineering in secure developmentprocess · Manage / conventional
IntentExtend the secure software development program (threat modeling, security requirements, security testing in the SDLC) to AI engineering activities — required by G.U.A.R.D "Adapt" even if you never train your own models.
ImplementAdd AI security threat modeling (cross-team, with representation from all engineers), AI-specific security testing, and AI supply chain checks to the existing secure SDLC.
LimitsEffectiveness depends on engineers actually understanding AI threats — pair with #SEC EDUCATE.
StandardsEssentials, Manage; aligns with secure-SDLC practice (e.g., OWASP SAMM as adjacent canon).

Development-time (model, data, and documentation)

#DEV SECURITYSecure the development environmentdevelopment-time · Manage / conventional
IntentProtect the engineering environment where data is prepared and models are trained — the attack surface behind direct development-time data leaks, model leaks, and poisoning ("break into train/finetune data" in the AI Security Matrix).
ImplementConventional environment security applied to the training pipeline: access control, hardening, logging over data stores, training jobs, model artifacts and documentation.
LimitsDoes not stop poisoning that arrives via the supply chain or through inference-time channels — those need supply chain management and runtime controls.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/development-time; conventional-control details via OpenCRE.
#SEGREGATE DATASegregate data environmentsdevelopment-time · Manage / conventional
IntentKeep training/test data separated in controlled environments so a compromise of one system doesn't expose or corrupt all data.
ImplementSeparate storage and processing zones for sensitive data used in engineering, with their own access control.
LimitsSegregation raises the attacker's cost but adds operational friction for data scientists; balance with workflow needs.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/development-time; OpenCRE for conventional detail.
#DISCRETEBe discrete about your model and engineeringdevelopment-time · Manage / conventional
IntentLimit publicly available details about your model and engineering approach (documentation exposure), making it harder for attackers to prepare targeted input attacks or replicate the model.
ImplementTreat model/engineering documentation as a protected asset; publish only what is needed.
LimitsSecurity by obscurity is a cost-raiser, not a guarantee — attackers can still probe the live model (transferability of attacks from similar models remains).
StandardsEssentials, Manage/development-time.

Runtime (model storage, model use, augmentation data, and model input/output)

#MODEL ACCESS CONTROLControl access to model useruntime · Manage / conventional
IntentRestrict who can query the model. Every input threat — evasion, prompt injection, inversion, exfiltration, exhaustion — requires model access; authentication and authorization shrink the attacker population and enable attribution.
ImplementConventional authN/authZ on the model API and application front-ends.
LimitsUseless against malicious authorized users and public-facing systems; combine with #RATE LIMIT and #MONITOR USE.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/runtime; OpenCRE access-control requirements.
#RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITYProtect the deployed model from tamperingruntime · Manage / conventional
IntentPrevent direct runtime model poisoning ("reprogramming") — an attacker who can modify deployed parameters controls model behaviour completely.
ImplementIntegrity protection of stored/loaded model files and memory (checksums, signing, protected storage).
LimitsProtects the artifact, not the behaviour learned before deployment — development-time poisoning passes integrity checks unchanged.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/runtime.
#RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITYProtect model input/output integrity in transitruntime · Manage / conventional
IntentStop attackers from tampering with data flowing between application and model — altered input is equivalent to an input attack; altered output deceives users directly.
ImplementTransport integrity (e.g., authenticated encrypted channels) across every hop of the model I/O path.
LimitsGuards the channel only — malicious content inside a legitimate channel (prompt injection) passes untouched.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/runtime; OpenCRE.
#RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITYProtect model parameters at runtimeruntime · Manage / conventional
IntentPrevent direct runtime model leak — theft of the parameters devalues the investment and, per the essentials note, lets attackers prepare input attacks efficiently and unnoticed.
ImplementThe guide's flagship example of an AI-specific aspect of a conventional control: "protection of model parameters can be implemented using a Trusted Execution Environment" (TEE) — plus access control and encryption of model storage.
LimitsDoes not stop model exfiltration through the front door (input-output harvesting) — that needs #RATE LIMIT, #MONITOR USE and related runtime watching.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/runtime; controls overview p. 37 (TEE example).
#MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITYProtect confidentiality of model inputruntime · Manage / conventional
IntentPrevent input data leak — model input can be highly sensitive: "a prompt with a sensitive question, enhanced with retrieved company secrets" (AI Security Matrix harm description).
ImplementConventional confidentiality controls (encryption in transit/at rest, access control, retention limits) on the input path and its logs.
LimitsAttack surface is "All IT" — every component that sees the input must be covered, including logging and analytics pipelines.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/runtime; OpenCRE.
#ENCODE MODEL OUTPUTEncode model output before downstream useruntime · Manage / conventional
IntentStop "output contains conventional injection": model output is attacker-influencable text, and if passed raw into an interpreter (browser, SQL, shell) it becomes an injection payload against any asset (C, I and A impact).
ImplementContext-appropriate output encoding/escaping — treat model output like untrusted user input in classic web security.
LimitsEncoding protects downstream parsers, not humans reading deceptive output, and not agentic tool calls — those need #OVERSIGHT and #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/runtime; classic injection canon (OWASP Top 10) applies.
#LIMIT RESOURCESLimit resources per requestruntime · Manage / conventional
IntentBlunt AI resource exhaustion: cap compute/time/cost a single request or user can consume, so attacks cause bounded damage instead of "excessive running costs and/or unavailable AI system".
ImplementQuotas, timeouts, and size limits on model invocations.
LimitsLegitimate heavy users hit the same caps — tune limits to real usage distributions; distributed attacks need #RATE LIMIT plus monitoring.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/runtime.
#AUGMENTATION DATA CONFIDENTIALITYProtect augmentation data from leakingruntime · Manage / conventional
IntentKeep system prompts and retrieval (RAG) data confidential — both against direct break-in ("direct augmentation data leak") and mindful that disclosure-in-output through model use is a parallel path.
ImplementConventional confidentiality controls on augmentation data stores and their delivery path to the model.
LimitsCannot stop the model itself revealing augmentation data in output — pair with #SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING and prompt-injection defenses.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/runtime.
#AUGMENTATION DATA INTEGRITYProtect augmentation data from manipulationruntime · Manage / conventional
IntentPrevent "augmentation data manipulation" — tampering with system prompts or retrieval data manipulates model behaviour at runtime, an integrity attack equivalent in effect to poisoning.
ImplementIntegrity protection (access control, versioning, signing) of augmentation data stores and pipelines.
LimitsProtects stored data; attacker-controlled content entering retrieval legitimately (indirect prompt injection) is a separate threat needing I/O handling.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/runtime.
#CONF COMPUTEConfidential computingruntime · Manage / conventional
IntentProtect model and data while in use — hardware-isolated execution shields parameters and inference data even from a compromised or curious host (relevant when model hosting is a supplier).
ImplementHardware trusted execution (TEE-class technology) for inference and, where feasible, training workloads.
LimitsCost, performance overhead, and hardware availability (especially for GPUs); the model's input/output interface remains exposed.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/runtime; TEE named in controls overview p. 37.
#MODEL OBFUSCATIONObfuscate the deployed modelruntime · Manage / conventional
IntentRaise the cost of extracting parameters from a model that must ship to less-trusted environments (edge devices, client-side deployment).
ImplementDeploy models in obfuscated/encrypted form so simple artifact theft doesn't yield usable parameters.
LimitsObfuscation delays, not prevents, a determined reverse engineer; functionality-preserving extraction via querying remains possible.
StandardsEssentials, Manage/runtime.
Which of the Manage controls would have to be in place before the others can even be scoped, and why?
#AI PROGRAM (with #SEC PROGRAM). The governance inventory tells you which AI applications and assets exist, and the security program registers those assets — only then can you decide which development-time and runtime conventional controls apply to which systems, and which are your suppliers' responsibility.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 26–30, permalink owaspai.org/go/essentials/. Essentials aligned with: SANS Critical AI security guidelines, ISO/IEC 27090 (AI security), prEN18282 (Cybersecurity for the AI Act). Threat mapping canon: evasion ↔ MITRE ATLAS AML.T0015 (Evade ML Model); prompt injection ↔ OWASP LLM01 and ATLAS AML.T0051; training-data extraction ↔ LLM02 (Sensitive Information Disclosure) and ATLAS AML.T0024; model extraction ↔ ATLAS AML.T0048.004-adjacent extraction techniques and LLM10 (Unbounded Consumption covers resource exhaustion).

Key Points

  • Essentials is the guide's official starting point, fully aligned with SANS Critical AI security guidelines, ISO/IEC 27090 and prEN18282.
  • AI-specific threats come in three groups: model input threats (evasion, prompt injection, data extraction, model extraction, resource exhaustion), new-supplier threats (data, models, hosting), and conventional threats against new AI assets (train/augmentation data, model, input, output).
  • An attacker who obtains your model (or a similar one) can prepare input attacks efficiently and unnoticed — model confidentiality protects behaviour integrity too.
  • Controls form 4 categories and 8 sub-categories linking to 50+ individual controls: Manage, Have resilient models, Watch, Limit.
  • Manage = extend existing GRC (#AI PROGRAM, #DEV PROGRAM, #CHECK COMPLIANCE, #SEC EDUCATE), supply chain management (data/model/hosting suppliers), and conventional security (#SEC PROGRAM, #SECDEV PROGRAM; dev-time #DEV SECURITY, #SEGREGATE DATA, #DISCRETE; runtime #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL through #MODEL OBFUSCATION).
  • A Trusted Execution Environment is the guide's example of the AI-specific aspect of a conventional control (protecting model parameters).
  • System prompts are augmentation data: assets with both confidentiality and integrity threats.
Module 00 · §0 Essentials · OWASP AI Exchange

Essentials II — resilient models, watching I/O, and limiting impact

~18 minCovers guide pp. 26–30

The remaining three essential control categories divide the defensive labor: data and model engineers build resilience into the model itself; runtime filters and monitors watch what flows in and out; and architectural limits cap what a wrong or hijacked model can leak or do. Here you meet all 31 remaining essential controls and — more importantly — learn which category to reach for first and why.

Category "Have resilient models" — data/model engineering controls

"Apply data/model engineering controls during model development for resilience against input attacks. This is the work of data and model engineers. Input attacks include triggering poisoned models — which of course can also be mitigated by preventing the model being poisoned using conventional controls." These controls live inside the training pipeline, involving mathematics, statistics and machine learning rather than firewalls.

#MODEL ALIGNMENTAlign GenAI model behaviourdevelopment-time · resilient models
IntentMake a generative model inherently more likely to refuse unwanted instructions and behaviours — the first layer against prompt injection and misuse (appears as "3a. GenAI Model alignment" on the threat map with controls).
ImplementAlignment tuning of the model's behaviour during development (safety training/instruction tuning by the model maker).
LimitsThe agentic-AI section is explicit: after model alignment and filtering and detection, "it should be assumed that prompt injection can still happen" — alignment is a probability shift, not a boundary.
StandardsEssentials, resilient models (owaspai.org/go/essentials/); part of the guide's "seven layers" of prompt-injection protection.
#FEDERATED LEARNINGTrain without centralizing datadevelopment-time · resilient models
IntentReduce training-data confidentiality exposure by training across decentralized data holders so sensitive raw data never aggregates into one breachable store.
ImplementFederated training where participants share model updates, not data.
LimitsIntroduces its own attack surface: malicious participants can send poisoned updates, and model updates can still leak information about local data.
StandardsEssentials, resilient models.
#CONTINUOUS VALIDATIONContinuously validate model behaviourdevelopment-time/runtime · resilient models & limit behaviour
IntentDetect drift or manipulation of model behaviour by validating against test sets on an ongoing basis — poisoning that shifts overall behaviour shows up as unexplained metric changes. Listed under both "resilient models" and "limit model behaviour" in the essentials.
ImplementOngoing evaluation runs on curated validation data as part of the ML pipeline and in production.
LimitsBackdoor (trigger-based) poisoning is designed to keep validation metrics intact — behaviour is only wrong on inputs containing the trigger, which validation sets rarely contain.
StandardsEssentials, resilient models + limit.
#UNWANTED BIAS TESTINGTest for unwanted biasdevelopment-time/runtime · resilient models & limit behaviour
IntentDetect unwanted systematic behaviour — whether accidental or induced by manipulation. Like #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION it does double duty: an engineering quality control and a behaviour-limiting safeguard.
ImplementStructured bias testing across relevant groups/segments during development and periodically after.
LimitsOnly finds bias you test for; targeted poisoning of narrow segments can evade coarse bias metrics.
StandardsEssentials, resilient models + limit.
#EVASION ROBUST MODELEngineer the model to resist evasiondevelopment-time · resilient models
IntentMake crafted-input misclassification (adversarial examples) harder by choosing/training models whose decision boundaries are less brittle.
ImplementRobustness-oriented model engineering (architecture, regularization, robust training practices) — data-scientist territory.
LimitsRobustness trades against accuracy; the controls overview warns that changes to the learning process "can have unintended downstream side effects" and must be introduced with care.
StandardsEssentials, resilient models; MITRE ATLAS evasion techniques (AML.T0015) are the adversary side.
#POISON ROBUST MODELMake training robust against poisoned samplesdevelopment-time · resilient models
IntentReduce the influence a minority of manipulated training samples can exert on learned behaviour.
ImplementPoisoning-resilient training/model-processing techniques applied by model engineers.
LimitsRaises the poisoning budget an attacker needs, but large-scale or supply-chain poisoning still requires prevention (conventional + supply chain controls) and detection (#DATA QUALITY CONTROL, #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION).
StandardsEssentials, resilient models.
#TRAIN ADVERSARIALAdversarial trainingdevelopment-time · resilient models
IntentTeach the model to classify adversarial examples correctly by including them in training — directly targeting evasion.
ImplementGenerate adversarial examples against your own model and add them (correctly labeled) to the training set, iteratively.
LimitsComputationally expensive; robustness tends to be strongest against the attack types trained on; possible clean-accuracy cost.
StandardsEssentials, resilient models.
#TRAIN DATA DISTORTIONDistort training datadevelopment-time · resilient models
IntentAlter training data (the guide's example of an AI-specific control is "adding noise to the training set") so that precise attacker-crafted patterns are blunted and individual records are harder to recover from the model.
ImplementNoise addition/smoothing/aggregation applied to training data before learning.
LimitsDistortion trades directly against model accuracy — a data-quality vs privacy/robustness dial that must be tuned deliberately.
StandardsEssentials, resilient models; controls overview p. 37 (noise example).
#ADVERSARIAL ROBUST DISTILLATIONRobustness through distillationdevelopment-time · resilient models
IntentUse knowledge distillation (training a student model on a teacher's softened outputs) to smooth decision boundaries, making small adversarial perturbations less effective.
ImplementDistillation configured for robustness as part of model engineering.
LimitsDefensive distillation has historically been broken by stronger attacks — treat as one resilience layer, never a sole defense.
StandardsEssentials, resilient models.
#MODEL ENSEMBLEUse an ensemble of modelsdevelopment-time · resilient models
IntentAvoid a single point of behavioural failure: multiple diverse models vote or cross-check, so an input crafted to fool (or a poison implanted in) one model doesn't decide the outcome alone.
ImplementTrain/deploy several diverse models and combine their outputs.
LimitsMultiplies training and inference cost; adversarial examples can transfer across similar models, eroding the diversity assumption.
StandardsEssentials, resilient models.
#MORE TRAINDATATrain with more datadevelopment-time · resilient models
IntentDilute the influence of any individual (possibly poisoned) sample and produce better-generalizing, less brittle decision boundaries.
ImplementIncrease quantity and variation of legitimate training data.
LimitsMore data means more collection/curation cost and a bigger confidentiality asset; sourcing more external data can widen the supply-chain poisoning surface it was meant to dilute.
StandardsEssentials, resilient models.
#SMALL MODELKeep the model as small as feasibledevelopment-time · resilient models
IntentLess capacity means less memorization of individual training records (reducing disclosure via inversion/membership inference) and less room for hidden manipulated behaviour.
ImplementChoose the simplest model that meets the requirement — echoing the guide's "think before you build" economics.
LimitsCapability ceiling: some tasks genuinely need large models; smallness is a design preference, not always available.
StandardsEssentials, resilient models.
#DATA QUALITY CONTROLControl the quality of training datadevelopment-time · resilient models
IntentDetect and remove anomalous, wrong, or manipulated samples before they shape model behaviour — the detection counterpart to preventing poisoning.
ImplementData validation, anomaly screening and provenance checks in the data pipeline (also relevant to non-AI data-centric systems, per the guide's scope).
LimitsWell-crafted poison is designed to look statistically normal; quality control raises attacker effort rather than guaranteeing purity.
StandardsEssentials, resilient models.
◆ Senior insight

Notice the economics of this whole category: every resilient-model control is paid for in scarce data-science time, training compute, and usually some accuracy. The controls overview says it plainly: "many of the controls are expensive to implement and are subject to trade-offs with other AI properties". That is why the guide's essentials put "Manage" first — conventional controls on the pipeline are cheaper per unit of risk removed — and why "Limit" exists: when you can't make the model trustworthy, make its failures affordable.

Category "Watch" — Model I/O handling and monitoring

"Apply Model I/O handling for runtime filtering, stopping or alerting to suspicious input or output. It is typically the territory of data and model engineers e.g. data scientists, involving elements from mathematics, statistics, linguistics and machine learning."

#ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLINGHandle statistically anomalous inputruntime · Watch / model I/O
IntentInputs unlike anything in the training distribution are where models misbehave and where attacks often live — detect them and filter, stop, or alert.
ImplementStatistical out-of-distribution/anomaly detection in front of the model.
LimitsStrong evasion inputs are engineered to look in-distribution; thresholds trade false positives (blocked users) against false negatives.
StandardsEssentials, Watch (owaspai.org/go/essentials/).
#EVASION INPUT HANDLINGDetect evasion-style inputruntime · Watch / model I/O
IntentSpecifically detect the signatures of adversarial-example manipulation in inputs, complementing generic anomaly detection.
ImplementAdversarial-input detectors built with ML/statistical techniques, tuned to the deployed model.
LimitsDetector and attack co-evolve; an attacker with model access (see the essentials note on stolen models) can optimize against the detector too.
StandardsEssentials, Watch.
#UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLINGDetect suspicious series of inputsruntime · Watch / model I/O
IntentSome attacks are invisible per-request but obvious in aggregate: model exfiltration (input-output harvesting), model inversion, and membership inference all require many systematic queries.
ImplementSession/user-level analysis of query sequences for systematic probing patterns.
LimitsAttackers distribute queries across identities and time; needs identity anchoring (#MODEL ACCESS CONTROL) and throttling (#RATE LIMIT) to bite.
StandardsEssentials, Watch.
#PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLINGFilter and detect prompt injection in I/Oruntime · Watch / model I/O
IntentCatch injected instructions in prompts (direct) and in processed content (indirect), and catch their effects in output, before they steer behaviour or reach users/tools.
ImplementInput/output filtering and detection for injection patterns — one of the guide's "seven layers" of prompt injection protection.
LimitsThe agentic-AI section's rule stands: after filtering and detection "it should be assumed that prompt injection can still happen" — blast radius control must follow.
StandardsEssentials, Watch; OWASP LLM01; ATLAS AML.T0051.
#DOS INPUT VALIDATIONValidate input against resource abuseruntime · Watch / model I/O
IntentReject inputs designed to consume disproportionate resources (an AI resource exhaustion vector) before they hit the model.
ImplementSize/complexity/format validation at the input boundary.
LimitsVolume-based exhaustion passes per-request validation — combine with #RATE LIMIT and #LIMIT RESOURCES.
StandardsEssentials, Watch; LLM10 (Unbounded Consumption) adjacent.
#INPUT DISTORTIONDistort input before inferenceruntime · Watch / model I/O
IntentAdversarial examples depend on precise perturbations; slightly modifying every input (noise, smoothing, re-encoding) can break that precision without much affecting legitimate content.
ImplementSystematic input transformation in the inference path.
LimitsCosts accuracy on legitimate inputs; robust adversarial attacks can be optimized to survive expected distortions.
StandardsEssentials, Watch.
#SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLINGFilter sensitive data out of outputruntime · Watch / model I/O
IntentLast line against disclosure-in-output: stop training data, augmentation data (system prompts) or personal data from leaving in responses.
ImplementOutput scanning/redaction for sensitive patterns before delivery.
LimitsRecognizing "sensitive" reliably is hard — paraphrased or encoded leaks evade filters; reduce what the model knows (#DATA MINIMIZE) rather than relying on catching leaks.
StandardsEssentials, Watch; LLM02 (Sensitive Information Disclosure).
#OBSCURE CONFIDENCEDon't expose detailed confidence scoresruntime · Watch / model I/O
IntentDetailed output confidences are an attacker's gradient: membership inference works "by feeding that person's data into the model and looking at the details of the model output", and inversion/exfiltration/evasion tuning all sharpen with confidence access.
ImplementReturn only what the use case needs — labels or coarse bands instead of raw probabilities.
LimitsSome legitimate consumers need confidence values (e.g., downstream risk thresholds); label-only attacks still exist, just slower.
StandardsEssentials, Watch.
#RATE LIMITThrottle access frequencyruntime · Watch / model I/O
IntentQuery-hungry attacks — exfiltration, inversion, membership inference, exhaustion, brute-force evasion search — become slow and expensive when per-user frequency is capped.
ImplementPer-identity throttling on model use (appears as "monitor, rate limit, access control" in the threat map with controls).
LimitsAttackers spread load across accounts/IPs; a single perfected evasion input needs only one query — rate limiting mainly taxes the many-query attack classes.
StandardsEssentials, Watch; OpenCRE rate-limiting requirements.
#MONITOR USEMonitor model useruntime · Watch / monitoring
IntentObservability over inference: "Monitoring of model inference — extending Model I/O handling, Oversight and overlooking general usage of the AI system." In agentic AI it is the entire "Observability" pillar.
ImplementLog and analyze model usage; alert on suspicious inputs, outputs, and patterns; feed incident response.
LimitsDetection after the fact — bounded value without response capacity; logs of prompts themselves become a confidentiality asset (#MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITY).
StandardsEssentials, Watch.
Why is Model I/O handling described as the territory of data scientists rather than the security team?
Because the filtering decisions are statistical, not rule-based: telling anomalous from normal input, detecting adversarial perturbations, or spotting injection patterns involves "mathematics, statistics, linguistics and machine learning". A conventional WAF engineer has no signal to write rules against; the detectors are themselves models built by people who understand the data distribution.

Category "Limit" — minimize data, limit behaviour

Two sub-categories, both instances of G.U.A.R.D's "Reduce" (Murphy's law): a model "can accidentally leak data or be misled to", and "can accidentally make a mistake or be misled to".

#DATA MINIMIZEMinimize datadevelopment-time/runtime · Limit / data
IntentData the model never sees cannot leak through it, be poisoned in it, or be stolen with it. Appears twice on the threat map with controls: at development-time near training data (impact T) and at runtime near input/output (impacts P, A, I).
ImplementRemove unneeded fields/records from training, augmentation and input data.
LimitsTension with #MORE TRAINDATA and model utility — minimize per-purpose, not blindly.
StandardsEssentials, Limit; core privacy principle (GDPR data minimization) — the guide's privacy section extends this.
#ALLOWED DATAUse only allowed datadevelopment-time · Limit / data
IntentEnsure data used (especially for training) is data you are permitted to use for that purpose — limiting legal/compliance blast radius alongside security exposure.
ImplementPurpose/consent/licensing checks in data acquisition and pipeline governance.
LimitsProvenance for large scraped datasets is genuinely difficult — a supply-chain-management problem as much as a policy one.
StandardsEssentials, Limit; relates to EU AI Act and privacy regulation.
#SHORT RETAINRetain data brieflyruntime · Limit / data
IntentShrink the time window in which stored data (inputs, logs, training snapshots) can be breached — old data is pure liability.
ImplementRetention schedules and automated deletion for AI data stores.
LimitsConflicts with debugging, audit and monitoring needs (#MONITOR USE) — set differentiated retention per data class.
StandardsEssentials, Limit.
#OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATAObfuscate sensitive training datadevelopment-time · Limit / data
IntentIf training data must be sensitive, make records unattributable — so a leak of the dataset or a disclosure through the model reveals less about real individuals.
ImplementAnonymization/pseudonymization/distortion of sensitive values before training.
LimitsObfuscation-utility trade-off; poorly done anonymization can be reversed by linkage — and the model may still memorize residual patterns.
StandardsEssentials, Limit; bridges to the guide's AI privacy section.
#OVERSIGHTHuman or rule-based oversight of model behaviourruntime · Limit / behaviour
IntentPut a check between model output and consequence: guardrails and human approval catch wrong or manipulated behaviour before it causes harm. In agentic AI, autonomy makes #OVERSIGHT "important" — agents triggering each other removes the human by default.
ImplementRule-based guards and human-in-the-loop approval for consequential actions (the guide's voice-assistant example: "you'd probably want it to check with you first before sending a thousand emails").
LimitsHuman review doesn't scale to high-volume decisions and habituates to rubber-stamping; rule-based guards only catch what rules anticipate.
StandardsEssentials, Limit; EU AI Act human-oversight requirements; agentic "blast radius control" pillar.
#LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGEGive the model least privilegeruntime · Limit / behaviour
IntentCap what manipulated behaviour can do: a model/agent with minimal permissions and capabilities cannot be leveraged far even when fully hijacked. The agentic section names it "a key control" because agents invoke functions (Action property).
ImplementMinimal permission sets for model-driven components; and critically: "Don't assign the responsibility of access control to GenAI models/agents. Build that into your architecture."
LimitsPressure to grant broad capability for convenience is constant; privileges accumulate — needs periodic review like any least-privilege regime.
StandardsEssentials, Limit; agentic blast radius control; LLM06 (Excessive Agency) is the matching OWASP GenAI risk.
#AI TRANSPARENCYBe transparent about AI use and limitsruntime · Limit / behaviour
IntentUsers who know they face an AI system, and know its limitations, calibrate their reliance — reducing the harm of wrong or manipulated output (deception depends on misplaced trust).
ImplementDisclose AI use and known limitations to users and stakeholders.
LimitsDisclosure fatigue is real; transparency mitigates consequence, not the manipulation itself.
StandardsEssentials, Limit; EU AI Act transparency obligations.
#EXPLAINABILITYExplain model decisionsruntime · Limit / behaviour
IntentExplanations give users and overseers a handle to notice wrong behaviour — a manipulated decision with an incoherent rationale gets challenged instead of trusted.
ImplementExpose decision explanations appropriate to the audience (supports #OVERSIGHT and #AI TRANSPARENCY).
LimitsExplanations of complex models are approximations and can themselves mislead; explanation detail can also aid attackers probing the model (tension with #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE / #DISCRETE).
StandardsEssentials, Limit.
🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Map each of your AI systems to the 8 sub-categories: which are covered, which are consciously skipped, which are your supplier's?
  • Before buying any resilient-model control, confirm you own the training pipeline — otherwise it's your supplier's control to demonstrate.
  • Deploy Watch controls in log-only mode first; measure false-positive rates on real traffic before enforcing.
  • Treat Limit controls as non-negotiable for GenAI: assume the model will be wrong or hijacked (Murphy's law) and cap data exposure and privileges accordingly.
  • Re-check #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE and #OVERSIGHT whenever an agent gains a new tool or connection.
A poisoned model passed all validation metrics and is in production. Which essential categories can still save you?
Watch and Limit. Trigger-based poisoning evades #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION, but at exploitation time the trigger input may be caught by anomaly/evasion input handling or monitoring (Watch), and the damage of the corrupted behaviour is capped by #OVERSIGHT and #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE (Limit). This layering is why the essentials are categories, not alternatives.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 26–30, permalink owaspai.org/go/essentials/. Aligned: SANS Critical AI security guidelines, ISO/IEC 27090, prEN18282. Canon mappings for this lesson's controls: #OVERSIGHT/#LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE ↔ OWASP LLM06 Excessive Agency; #SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING ↔ LLM02; #PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING ↔ LLM01; #DOS INPUT VALIDATION/#RATE LIMIT/#LIMIT RESOURCES ↔ LLM10 Unbounded Consumption; adversarial training and input distortion counter ATLAS AML.T0015 (Evade ML Model); NIST AI RMF "Measure" covers continuous validation and bias testing.

Key Points

  • "Have resilient models" = 13 data/model-engineering controls (#MODEL ALIGNMENT, #FEDERATED LEARNING, #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION, #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING, #EVASION ROBUST MODEL, #POISON ROBUST MODEL, #TRAIN ADVERSARIAL, #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION, #ADVERSARIAL ROBUST DISTILLATION, #MODEL ENSEMBLE, #MORE TRAINDATA, #SMALL MODEL, #DATA QUALITY CONTROL) — data-scientist work, paid in compute and accuracy.
  • "Watch" = Model I/O handling (9 controls from #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING to #RATE LIMIT) plus #MONITOR USE — statistical runtime filtering, stopping and alerting.
  • "Limit" = minimize/obfuscate data (#DATA MINIMIZE, #ALLOWED DATA, #SHORT RETAIN, #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA) and limit behaviour (#OVERSIGHT, #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE, #AI TRANSPARENCY, #EXPLAINABILITY, plus #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION and #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING again).
  • #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION and #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING appear in two categories — engineering quality controls that double as behaviour limiters.
  • Input attacks include triggering poisoned models — resilience and poisoning-prevention are complementary paths to the same integrity goal.
  • Many controls are expensive and trade off against accuracy and normal operations; learning-process changes can have unintended downstream side effects.
  • Layering is the point: prevention (resilience) → detection (watch) → containment (limit).
Module 00 · §0 Threats overview & Controls overview · OWASP AI Exchange

The threat landscape: scope, impacts, and the threat map

~15 minCovers guide pp. 30–32, 37–39

Every AI threat can be pinned to a precise location: a lifecycle phase (development-time or runtime), an attack surface (using the model vs breaking into a component), and one of six impact types serving three attacker goals. The threat map is the single diagram that organizes the entire rest of the guide — learn to read it here, then see the same map with control groups overlaid.

Scope: what counts as an AI-specific threat

The Exchange focuses on AI-specific threats: threats to AI assets (see #SEC PROGRAM), such as training data. Threats to other assets — say, protecting a user database — are already covered by many other resources. AI systems are IT systems, so they suffer various conventional security threats too; when securing AI systems, the Exchange must be seen as an extension of your existing security program. The guide compresses this into a formula:

AI security = threats to AI-specific assets (AI Exchange) + threats to other assets (other resources)

Three types of threats

  1. Threats during development-time — when data is obtained and prepared, and the model is trained or obtained. Example: data poisoning.
  2. Input threats — through attackers using the model (inference: providing input and getting the output). Examples: prompt injection, evasion.
  3. Other threats to the system during runtime — in operation, not through inference. Example: stealing model input.

Six impacts, three attacker goals

The guide outlines 6 types of impacts aligned with three attacker goals: disclose, deceive, and disrupt:

#GoalImpact
1discloseHurt confidentiality of train/test data
2discloseHurt confidentiality of model intellectual property (the model parameters, or the process and data that led to them)
3discloseHurt confidentiality of input or augmentation data
4deceiveHurt integrity of model behaviour — the model is manipulated to behave in an unwanted way and consequentially deceive users
5disruptHurt availability of the model — it doesn't work, or behaves in an unwanted way, not to deceive users but to disrupt normal operations
6disrupt/discloseConfidentiality, integrity, and availability of non-AI-specific assets

Attack-surface nuance: the same impact can be reached via different surfaces. The guide's example: the confidentiality of training data can be compromised by hacking into the database during development — but it can also be leaked by a membership inference attack, which "can find out whether a certain individual was in the train data, simply by feeding that person's data into the model and looking at the details of the model output". Same impact (impact 1), completely different defenses.

THREAT
Membership inferenceReveals whether an individual's record was in the training data, purely through model queries. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: ATLAS AML.T0024 (Infer Training Data Membership); OWASP LLM02-adjacent.
▸ Worked example

Why membership alone is damaging: suppose a model is trained on records of patients of an addiction clinic. An attacker feeding a person's data into the model and reading the output details learns that the person was a patient — sensitive fact disclosed, no database breached, no record contents read. The confidence details of the output are the side channel, which is exactly why #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE exists.

Reading the threat map

The map (donated to the Exchange by Software Improvement Group, free of copyright and attribution) shows threats as arrows into or out of the asset they attack, split by a dashed line into development-time (top) and runtime (bottom). Each threat carries an impact letter: (T) train data confidentiality, (B) model behaviour, (P) intellectual property, (E) exhaustion, (I) input confidentiality, (A) augment confidentiality. For the assets list, see the security program subsection (#SEC PROGRAM); the control overview repeats this diagram with control groups added.

flowchart TD
  subgraph DEV["Development-time"]
    TDA["Training data"] --> ML["Machine learning - optional"]
    ML --> MODEL["AI Model"]
  end
  subgraph RT["Runtime"]
    AUG["Augment data"] --> MODEL
    INPUT["Input"] --> APP["Application and infrastructure"]
    MODEL --> APP
    APP --> OUTPUT["Output"]
  end
  T1["Direct training data leak - impact T"] -.-> TDA
  T2["Data poisoning - impact B - direct or in supply chain"] --> TDA
  T3["Development-time model leak - impact P"] -.-> MODEL
  T4["Development-time model poisoning - impact B - direct or in supply chain"] --> MODEL
  T5["Direct runtime model leak - impact P"] -.-> MODEL
  T6["Direct runtime model poisoning - impact B"] --> MODEL
  T7["Augmentation leak or manipulation - impacts A and B"] --> AUG
  IT["Input threats: Evasion B, Prompt injection B, Model exfiltration P, Model inversion T, Membership inference T, Disclosure in output T and A, AI resource exhaustion E"] --> INPUT
  INPUT -.->|"Input data leak - impact I"| ATT["Attacker"]
  OUTPUT -.->|"Output contains conventional injection"| DS["Downstream systems"]
  T10["Conventional security threats e.g. SQL injection, password guessing"] --> APP
  INPUT -. "Continuous learning" .-> ML

Figure — the AI threat map (guide p. 32; AI threat model by Software Improvement Group, donated to the AI Exchange). Dashed "Continuous learning" shows the optional loop where runtime input feeds back into training — which turns input channels into poisoning channels.

THREAT
