ImplementContextual output encoding exactly as for any untrusted input. Periodic table: output contains conventional injection → “Encode model output”.
StandardsOWASP LLM Top 10 LLM05:2025 (Improper Output Handling); OpenCRE; navigator band 4.
#LIMIT RESOURCESCap compute/spend per request and tenantmanage · conventional · runtime
IntentBounds the blast radius of AI resource exhaustion (impact E): even a successful flood or pathological input cannot burn unbounded cost or capacity.
ImplementQuotas, timeouts, budget caps. Periodic table: AI resource exhaustion → “Dos input validation, limit resources” on top of monitor/rate-limit/access-control.
StandardsLLM10:2025 Unbounded Consumption; navigator band 2 availability group.
#AUGMENTATION DATA CONFIDENTIALITYProtect augmentation data from leakingmanage · conventional · runtime
IntentAugmentation data — including system prompts and e.g. RAG data — is a runtime asset that can leak directly (break-in) or via disclosure in output (impact letter A).
ImplementConventional confidentiality controls on the stores and channels feeding the model at runtime.
StandardsNavigator band 4 (confidentiality of augmentation data); LLM07:2025 System Prompt Leakage is the GenAI special case.
#AUGMENTATION DATA INTEGRITYProtect augmentation data from manipulationmanage · conventional · runtime
IntentCounters “augmentation data manipulation” (matrix): whoever edits your system prompt or RAG store steers model behaviour as effectively as poisoning the model.
ImplementIntegrity protection (access control, change control, verification) on augmentation stores; shown on the maps as the “Augmentation leak/manipulate” arrow's counterpart.
StandardsNavigator band 4 (integrity of augmentation data); LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses is adjacent canon.
#CONF COMPUTEConfidential computing for data & model in usemanage · conventional · development & runtime
IntentProtect data and model parameters even while being processed — shrinking what a compromised host or curious operator can read.
ImplementTrusted Execution Environments and similar isolation (the guide names TEE for model-parameter protection); navigator lists CONF COMPUTE among band-3 base controls.
LimitsDoesn't help once data must be readable by an external provider's model (lesson 5's clear-text reality).
StandardsOpenCRE; owaspai.org/go/controlsoverview/.
#MODEL OBFUSCATIONObfuscate the deployed modelmanage · conventional · runtime
IntentRaise the cost of exploiting a runtime model leak: even with file access, extracting usable parameters becomes harder.
ImplementPeriodic table: direct runtime model leak → “Runtime model confidentiality, Model obfuscation” — a second layer behind confidentiality.
LimitsObfuscation delays, it doesn't prevent — a determined attacker with the artifact eventually wins; the value is in buying detection time.
StandardsNavigator band 4 (confidentiality of intellectual property).

Category: HAVE RESILIENT MODELS

Apply data/model engineering controls during model development for resilience against input attacks. This is the work of data and model engineers. Note the subtlety the guide flags: input attacks include triggering poisoned models — which can also be mitigated by preventing the poisoning in the first place with conventional controls. Resilience and prevention are complementary, not alternatives.

#MODEL ALIGNMENTAlign GenAI model behaviour with intentresilient models · data/model engineering
IntentTrain/tune the model to refuse manipulation — the “3a GenAI Model alignment” overlay attached directly to the AI Model on the controls map; the first defense layer against prompt injection.
ImplementPeriodic table pairs it with direct prompt injection; navigator lists it under “Limit unwanted behaviour” in band 1 — alignment serves both resilience and behaviour limitation.
LimitsThe agentic lesson's iron rule applies: after alignment (and filtering, and detection) it must be assumed prompt injection can still happen.
StandardsLLM01:2025; owaspai.org/go/periodictable/.
#FEDERATED LEARNINGTrain without centralizing the dataresilient models · data/model engineering · development-time
IntentKeep training data at its sources so there is no central trove to steal — reduces training-data confidentiality risk during development.
ImplementPeriodic table lists it alongside development environment security and data segregation for every development-time threat row.
LimitsDistributed participants become distributed attack surface (poisoned updates) — a trade-off the “introduce with care” warning covers.
StandardsNavigator band 3 base control.
#CONTINUOUS VALIDATIONContinuously validate model behaviourresilient models & limit · dual-listed
IntentKeep testing the model against expected behaviour in production-like conditions — catches degradation and manipulation (e.g. a poisoning that shifted behaviour) early.
ImplementListed BOTH under data/model engineering (resilient models) AND under limit model behaviour — it hardens and it bounds.
LimitsOnly detects what your validation set expresses; targeted backdoors with rare triggers can pass.
StandardsNavigator band 1, “Limit unwanted behaviour” group; NIST AI RMF MEASURE function.
#UNWANTED BIAS TESTINGTest for unwanted biasresilient models & limit · dual-listed
IntentDetect unwanted bias in model decisions — both a trustworthiness concern and a security signal: manipulation (poisoning) can express itself as induced bias.
ImplementDual-listed like continuous validation: under resilient models and under limit model behaviour (navigator band 1).
StandardsNIST AI RMF (fair/valid & reliable characteristics); EU AI Act quality expectations.
#EVASION ROBUST MODELEngineer the model to resist adversarial inputsresilient models · data/model engineering
IntentMake small crafted perturbations less able to flip decisions — the core anti-evasion resilience control.
ImplementPeriodic table, evasion row: “evasion robust model, train adversarial, input distortion, adversarial robust distillation” on top of the always-on runtime controls.
LimitsRobustness–accuracy trade-off: hardening the boundary can cost clean-input accuracy (the guide's “select with care” warning).
StandardsATLAS AML.T0043 (attack side); navigator band 2 evasion group.
#POISON ROBUST MODELEngineer the model to resist poisoned samplesresilient models · data/model engineering
IntentReduce the influence a minority of poisoned training samples can exert on learned behaviour.
ImplementPeriodic table, data-poisoning row, together with more training data, data quality control, and train data distortion.
StandardsLLM04:2025 Data and Model Poisoning; ATLAS AML.T0020.
#TRAIN ADVERSARIALAdversarial trainingresilient models · data/model engineering
IntentInclude adversarial examples in training so the model learns to classify them correctly — inoculation against evasion.
ImplementPeriodic table pairs it with BOTH evasion and data poisoning rows.
LimitsLonger/costlier training; robustness tends to be strongest against the attack types you trained on.
StandardsNavigator band 2 evasion group.
#TRAIN DATA DISTORTIONDistort/noise the training dataresilient models · data/model engineering
IntentThe guide's canonical example of an AI-specific control: adding noise to the training set. Distortion blunts precise poisoning patterns and reduces recoverable detail of individuals (helps against inversion/membership inference).
ImplementPeriodic table, data-poisoning row.
LimitsTextbook accuracy trade-off — a changed data distribution can have unintended downstream side effects; introduce with care and re-evaluate.
Standardsowaspai.org/go/controlsoverview/.
#ADVERSARIAL ROBUST DISTILLATIONDistill for robustnessresilient models · data/model engineering
IntentUse model distillation (student learns a teacher's smoothed outputs) so decision surfaces are less brittle against adversarial perturbations.
ImplementPeriodic table, evasion row (with evasion robust model, train adversarial, input distortion).
StandardsNavigator band 2 evasion group.
#MODEL ENSEMBLEMultiple models decide togetherresilient models · data/model engineering
IntentIf one model is poisoned or fooled, independently built ensemble members outvote it — the periodic table pairs “model ensemble” with development-time model poisoning, data poisoning, and supply-chain model poisoning.
LimitsMultiplies training and inference cost; only helps if members don't share the poisoned source (independence is the whole trick).
StandardsNavigator band 3.
#MORE TRAINDATAMore training data dilutes poisonresilient models · data/model engineering
IntentAttacker economics: to shift behaviour, poisoned samples must be a meaningful fraction of the data. More legitimate data forces the attacker to inject proportionally more — raising cost and detectability.
ImplementPeriodic table, data-poisoning row (“More training data”).
StandardsNavigator band 3 data-poisoning group (MORE TRAIN DATA).
#SMALL MODELPrefer a smaller modelresilient models · data/model engineering
IntentSmaller-capacity models memorize fewer training specifics, so model inversion and membership inference recover less — the periodic table pairs “Small model” with the inversion/membership row.
LimitsCapability trade-off: smaller usually means less capable — the same catch-22 the guide notes for self-hosting smaller models.
StandardsNavigator band 2 confidentiality group.
#DATA QUALITY CONTROLInspect and clean the training dataresilient models · data/model engineering · development-time
IntentSystematic quality checks over train/finetune data catch anomalies — including poisoned samples — before they shape the model.
ImplementPeriodic table, data-poisoning row (with more training data, train data distortion, poison robust model, train adversarial).
LimitsClean-label and semantically valid poison can pass statistical checks — layer with provenance (supply chain management).
StandardsNavigator band 3; LLM04:2025.
Why does the guide say input attacks “include triggering poisoned models”, and what does that imply for control selection?
A poisoned model misbehaves only when the attacker presents the trigger input at runtime — so the final step of a poisoning campaign is an input attack. Implication: you can defend on either side — prevent the poisoning with conventional controls (dev security, supply chain management, data quality control) or blunt the trigger with resilience controls (poison robust model, ensemble) and I/O filtering. Defense in depth uses both.
Which single conventional control example does the guide give for protecting model parameters, and in which control does it surface?
A Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) — the guide cites it as the AI-specific aspect of a conventional control, implementing #RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY (and confidential computing, #CONF COMPUTE, more broadly).

Key Points

  • Many controls are expensive and trade off against accuracy and normal operation; learning-process and data-distribution changes can have unintended downstream side effects — introduce with care.
  • Conventional controls (e.g. encrypting the training database) are specified elsewhere (OpenCRE); the Exchange details the AI-specific ones (e.g. adding noise to the training set) plus AI-specific aspects of conventional ones (e.g. TEE for model parameters).
  • The controls map overlays numbered groups on the threat map: 1 governance, 2 supply chain / dev environment / runtime security / monitor-rate-limit-access, 3a–c model alignment, specialized model controls, input filtering, 4 minimize data (dev & runtime), 5 behaviour impact control.
  • Yellow = conventional, purple = AI-specific on all the guide's maps.
  • MANAGE = GRC (#AI PROGRAM, #DEV PROGRAM, #CHECK COMPLIANCE, #SEC EDUCATE), supply chain management, and conventional security extended to AI assets (11 runtime + 3 development-time + 2 managing-security controls).
  • HAVE RESILIENT MODELS = 13 data/model-engineering controls, from alignment and adversarial training to ensembles, small models, and data quality control.
  • Resilience complements prevention: stop the poisoning conventionally AND survive it statistically.
Module 01 · Controls overview & Ready-made models · OWASP AI Exchange

Watch & Limit — and the Ready-Made Model Scenarios

~19 minCovers guide pp. 40–44

Two categories remain: Watch (filter and monitor the model's I/O) and Limit (assume failure, bound the damage). Then we apply the whole map to the most common real-world architecture: using a ready-made model — self-hosted or at an external provider — where the security question becomes “who owns which controls?”.

Category: WATCH

Model I/O handling does runtime filtering, stopping or alerting on suspicious input or output. It is typically the territory of data and model engineers (e.g. data scientists), involving elements from mathematics, statistics, linguistics and machine learning. The guide's PDF even carries a small typo here (“<odel I/O handing”) — the content is what matters:

#ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLINGDetect and handle anomalous inputwatch · model I/O handling · runtime
IntentInputs far from the expected distribution are suspicious by construction — catch them before or as the model processes them.
ImplementStatistical/ML out-of-distribution detection on inputs; periodic table lists it in the evasion row and the navigator among always-on input controls.
LimitsAdversarial inputs are engineered to look normal; expect evasion of the detector too — layer, don't rely.
StandardsNavigator band 2; owaspai.org/go/periodictable/.
#EVASION INPUT HANDLINGDetect evasion attempts in inputwatch · model I/O handling · runtime
IntentSpecifically target the fingerprints of adversarial examples (crafted perturbations) rather than general anomalies.
ImplementPeriodic table, evasion row: “Anomalous input handling, Evasion input handling, Unwanted input series handling” plus the model-side resilience controls.
StandardsATLAS AML.T0043 (attack side); navigator band 2.
#UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLINGDetect suspicious series of inputswatch · model I/O handling · runtime
IntentExtraction attacks (model exfiltration by input-output harvesting, model inversion, membership inference) and probing evasions need MANY queries — the series is the signature even when each single input looks innocent.
ImplementPeriodic table pairs it with evasion, model inversion/membership inference, AND model exfiltration.
LimitsDistributed attacks (many accounts/IPs) dilute the series — combine with model access control and rate limiting.
StandardsATLAS AML.T0024; navigator band 2 always-on group.
#PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLINGFilter/detect injection in prompts and outputswatch · model I/O handling · runtime
IntentDetect and neutralize injected instructions on the way IN (direct and indirect prompt injection) and manipulation symptoms on the way OUT.
ImplementPeriodic table: paired with direct prompt injection (with model alignment) and indirect prompt injection (with input segregation, see lesson 6).
LimitsNatural language cannot be parsed like SQL — assume bypass is possible and pair with blast radius control (the agentic-AI rule from lesson 2).
StandardsLLM01:2025; ATLAS AML.T0051.
#DOS INPUT VALIDATIONValidate input against resource abusewatch · model I/O handling · runtime
IntentReject inputs designed to consume disproportionate compute (size, structure, pathological content) before they reach the model — anti-exhaustion at the gate.
ImplementPeriodic table, AI resource exhaustion row: “Dos input validation, limit resources”.
StandardsLLM10:2025 Unbounded Consumption; ATLAS AML.T0029.
#INPUT DISTORTIONSlightly distort input before inferencewatch · model I/O handling · runtime
IntentAdversarial perturbations are precise; small random transformations of the input can break that precision while barely affecting legitimate accuracy.
ImplementPeriodic table, evasion row.
LimitsCosts some accuracy; strong adaptive attackers can optimize against known distortions.
StandardsNavigator band 2 evasion group.
#SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLINGFilter sensitive data out of model outputwatch · model I/O handling · runtime
IntentLast line against disclosure in output: scan/redact training-data fragments, secrets, and personal data before output leaves the system.
ImplementPeriodic table, disclosure-in-output row, on top of sensitive data limitation and monitor/rate-limit/access-control.
LimitsYou can only redact what you can recognize — which is why the Limit category minimizes what's there to leak in the first place.
StandardsLLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure; navigator band 2.
#OBSCURE CONFIDENCEHide detailed confidence scoreswatch · model I/O handling · runtime
IntentMembership inference and model inversion feed on the DETAILS of model output (lesson 2's example: “looking at the details of the model output”). Withholding fine-grained confidence starves those attacks — and makes extraction and evasion optimization harder.
ImplementPeriodic table, model inversion / membership inference row (printed there with a stray “>” as “>Obscure confidence” — a quirk of the original).
LimitsSome legitimate use cases need confidence values — scope the hiding to untrusted callers.
StandardsATLAS AML.T0024; navigator band 2 always-on group.
#RATE LIMITThrottle access frequencywatch · model I/O handling · runtime
IntentEvery input attack needs queries; extraction and exhaustion need LOTS of them. Throttling turns cheap attacks into slow, expensive, noisy ones.
ImplementOne third of the recurring “Monitor, rate limit, model access control” trio — paired in the periodic table with prompt injection, evasion, disclosure in output, inversion/membership, exfiltration, and resource exhaustion; overlay “2b” on the controls map.
LimitsDoesn't stop single-shot attacks (one good injection suffices); calibrate against legitimate burst traffic.
StandardsLLM10:2025; OpenCRE (rate limiting).
#MONITOR USEMonitor model inference and general usagewatch · monitoring · runtime
IntentThe Watch category's second sub-category: monitoring of model inference — extending Model I/O handling and Oversight, and overlooking general usage of the AI system. Detection is what makes all other controls verifiable.
ImplementLog and analyze inference traffic and system usage; in agentic AI this is the “Observability” layer (lesson 2).
LimitsMonitoring infrastructure itself needs protection (the external-hosting question “how is that infrastructure protected?” applies internally too).
StandardsNavigator band 2 always-on; NIST AI RMF MEASURE/MANAGE.

Category: LIMIT

Limit exists because of Murphy's law: a model can accidentally leak data or make a mistake — or be misled to. Two sub-categories: minimize/obfuscate sensitive data, and limit model behaviour.

#DATA MINIMIZEHold less sensitive datalimit · sensitive data limitation
IntentData that isn't there cannot leak — not through break-ins, not through disclosure in output, not through inversion. The “4 Minimize data” overlay appears TWICE on the controls map: at training data (T) and at runtime input/output (P, A, I).
ImplementStrip, aggregate, or pseudonymize before data enters training sets, prompts, or augmentation stores; periodic table's “Sensitive data limitation” bundle heads every training-data-confidentiality row.
StandardsNavigator band 1 sensitive-data-limitation group; GDPR data-minimisation principle is adjacent canon.
#ALLOWED DATAOnly use data you are allowed to uselimit · sensitive data limitation
IntentConstrain training/augmentation data to what policy, consent, and licensing permit — reducing both leak impact and compliance exposure (fines, legal fees are the matrix's harm text for confidentiality rows).
ImplementData governance gates in the pipeline; part of the (#DATA MINIMIZE, #ALLOWED DATA, #SHORT RETAIN, #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA) quartet.
StandardsEU AI Act documentation duties; navigator band 1.
#SHORT RETAINRetain sensitive data brieflylimit · sensitive data limitation
IntentShrink the time window in which stored data (inputs, logs, training snapshots) can be stolen or disclosed.
ImplementRetention schedules and automated deletion; note the external-hosting twist — a court required OpenAI to retain logs for litigation for a period of time, overriding vendor retention promises.
StandardsNavigator band 1; owaspai.org/go/readymademodel/.
#OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATAObfuscate/anonymize training datalimit · sensitive data limitation
IntentIf the model never saw the raw sensitive values, inversion/membership inference and disclosure in output surface only obfuscated data.
ImplementAnonymization, pseudonymization, synthetic substitution before training; periodic table's sensitive-data-limitation bundle (“obfuscate training data”).
LimitsUtility trade-off; poor anonymization can be re-identified — treat as reduction, not elimination.
StandardsNavigator band 1 (OBFUSCATE TRAINDATA).
#OVERSIGHTRule-based / human oversight of model actionslimit · limit model behaviour
IntentPut a checkpoint between model output and real-world effect. In agentic AI this is a headline control (the “check with you first before sending a thousand emails” example) and one half of blast radius control.
ImplementHuman approval for high-impact actions, rule-based guards for the rest; the controls-map overlay “5 — Control behaviour impact e.g. oversight, least privilege (B)”.
LimitsHuman review doesn't scale to every action; reserve it for irreversible/high-impact operations and automate the rest with rules.
StandardsLLM06:2025 Excessive Agency; EU AI Act human-oversight expectations; navigator band 1.
#LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGEGive the model minimal permissionslimit · limit model behaviour
IntentA manipulated model can only abuse the privileges it holds. Key control for agentic Action (lesson 2), the other half of blast radius control, and the architectural answer to the confused deputy.
ImplementScope every tool, API, and data access per agent per task; never assign access-control responsibility to the model via instructions — build it into the architecture.
LimitsPressure to “just give it admin so it works” is the natural enemy; privilege creep re-arms the trifecta's Access and Send legs.
StandardsLLM06:2025; navigator band 1 limit-unwanted-behaviour group.
#AI TRANSPARENCYBe transparent that (and how) AI decideslimit · limit model behaviour
IntentUsers who know AI is involved — and know its limits — can compensate, question, and catch unwanted behaviour instead of trusting it blindly. Also feeds adoption step 5 (Demonstrate).
ImplementDisclosure of AI use, capabilities, and limitations to users and stakeholders.
StandardsEU AI Act transparency obligations; NIST AI RMF (transparency & accountability); navigator band 1.
#EXPLAINABILITYMake model decisions explainablelimit · limit model behaviour
IntentIf you can see WHY the model decided, manipulated behaviour (poisoning triggers, injected goals) becomes detectable instead of silent — explanation is a tripwire for integrity attacks.
ImplementExplanation techniques appropriate to the model class, surfaced to overseers (pairs naturally with #OVERSIGHT and #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION, which are dual-listed here).
StandardsNIST AI RMF explainability characteristic; navigator band 1.

Rounding out the Limit list, the guide re-lists #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION and #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING here too — validation and bias testing both harden the model AND bound its behaviour. The section closes: “All threats and controls are explored in more detail in the corresponding threat sections of the AI Exchange.”

Why is #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE listed against model inversion and membership inference specifically?
Those attacks work by analyzing the details of model output — e.g. how confidently the model scores a candidate record reveals whether that record was in the training data. Removing or coarsening confidence details starves the attacker's signal while normal users keep the label/answer.

Ready-made models: who owns which controls?

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 41–44, permalink owaspai.org/go/readymademodel/. Both figures: “AI Security Threats and controls - GenAI as-is”, AI threat model by Software Improvement Group, donated to AI Exchange, free of copyright and attribution.

If possible, and depending on price, organizations can prefer a ready-made model instead of training or fine-tuning themselves — e.g. an open source model to detect people in a camera image, or a general-purpose LLM such as Google Gemini, OpenAI ChatGPT, Anthropic Claude, Alibaba QWen, Deepseek, Mistral, Grok, or Falkon. Training such models yourself can cost millions of dollars, requires deep expertise and vast amounts of data. The provider did the training/fine-tuning and is therefore responsible for part of security — hence proper supply chain management regarding the model provider is required.

Deployment optionDescription & trade-off
1. Closed source model, hosted by the providerFor the largest models typically the only available option.
2. Self-hosted: open source model (open weights) on-premise or in your virtual private cloudOn-premise is most secure; VPC is secure if the cloud provider is trusted. More security, may be the best option cost-wise — but does not support the largest models.
3. Open source model (open weights) at a paid hosting serviceConvenient.

Scenario A — Self-hosted ready-made model

GenAI as-is, SELF-HOSTED (guide p. 42): supplier zone vs. your runtime, split by a dashed trust line
Model supplier (their controls)
Supplier's GenAI Model alignmentplus specialized controls against poisoning, evasion and data disclosure
Supplier's Monitor, rate limit, access controlplus specialized input filtering and detection
Supplier's AI supply chain managementplus development environment security
Supplier's AI governanceplus Minimize data (T,P)
Supplier's AI Modelthe artifact you download — it crosses into your zone
Your runtime (your controls)
Your Model supply chain managementAI-specific · you vetted what you downloaded
Your AI governanceAI-specific
Control behaviour impact (B)e.g. oversight, least privilege — near the poisoning arrow
(Additional monitor, rate limit, access control)guarding the Input path
(Additional specialized input filtering and detection)guarding the Input path
Runtime security controls · Minimize data (T,I,A)along the application bottom
Threats in your runtime
Direct runtime model poisoning (B)exists BECAUSE you host the model artifact
Augmentation leak/manipulateat the Augment data store feeding the application
Input threatsEvasion (B), Prompt injection (B), Disclosure in output (T)(A), AI resource exhaustion (E)
Input data leak (I) · Output contains conventional injectionunder Input and Output
Conventional security threatse.g. SQL injection, password guessing — runtime conventional banner

Figure — reconstruction of the self-hosted map. Legend: green arrow = threat, yellow box = conventional controls, purple box = AI-specific controls; impact letters (T)(B)(P)(E)(I)(A) as in lesson 2. Responsibility split: the supplier secures training, alignment, and the model supply chain; YOU inherit all runtime threats — including direct runtime model poisoning, because the model artifact now lives in your infrastructure — plus conventional runtime security, augmentation-data protection, and your own supply-chain management and governance over the model you obtained.

Scenario B — External-hosted ready-made model

GenAI as-is, EXTERNALLY HOSTED (guide p. 43): the model stays above the trust line
Model supplier & hosting provider
Provider's runtime security controlsNEW vs. scenario A — runtime protection is now the provider's job
Provider's Monitor, rate limit, access controlwas “Supplier's” in scenario A
Supplier's alignment, specialized controls, input filtering, supply chain mgmt, dev security, AI governance, Minimize data (T,P)unchanged
Supplier's AI Model — runs at the providerbidirectional arrow crosses the dashed line to your application
Your runtime (your controls)
Your model AND hosting supply chain managementhosting is now part of your supply chain
Your AI governanceunchanged
Control behaviour impact (B) · additional monitor/rate-limit/access-control · additional input filtering · runtime security controls · Minimize data (T,A)as in scenario A
Threats in your runtime
Input threatsEvasion (B), Prompt injection (B), Disclosure in output (T)(A), AI resource exhaustion (E)
Augmentation leak/manipulate · Input data leak (I) · Output contains conventional injection · Conventional security threatsas in scenario A
Direct runtime model poisoning: ABSENTyou don't hold the model — that threat (and its control) moved to the provider

Figure — reconstruction of the externally hosted map. Your input/output crosses the trust boundary into the provider's environment; the provider takes over runtime model protection; your supply chain management must now also cover the hosting; and your data confidentiality depends on the provider.

The four questions to ask an external host

  1. Where does the model run? In the vendor's processes or in your own virtual private cloud? Some vendors say you get a “private instance” — but that may refer to the API, not the model. If the model runs on the vendor's cluster, your data leaves your environment in clear text. Vendors will minimize storage and transfer, but they may log and monitor.
  2. What are the data retention rules? Has a court required the vendor to retain logs for litigation? This happened to OpenAI in the US for a period of time — a real incident that overrode normal retention promises.
  3. What is exactly logged and monitored? Read the small print. Is logging enabled, and if so, what is logged? What is monitored — by operators or by algorithms? And if by algorithms: how is that infrastructure protected? Some vendors allow opting out of logging, but only with specific licenses.
  4. Is your input used for training? A common fear, but in the vast majority of cases the input is not used — if vendors did this secretly, it would get out, because there are ways to tell.
◆ Senior insight

Three nuances the guide insists on. First: if you can't accept the risk for certain data, hosting your own (smaller) model is the safest option — typically it won't be as good, and there's the catch-22. Second: a provider-hosted model needs your input in clear text — the model must read the data to process it; your sensitive data will exist unencrypted outside your infrastructure. This is not unique to LLM providers: the same holds for other multi-tenant SaaS, such as commercial hosted Office suites. Third: compare the risk fairly — the vendor may still protect that environment better than you can protect your own. The right analysis is comparative, not absolutist.

Shadow AI

A typical organizational challenge: controlling the use of ready-made general-purpose GenAI (e.g. ChatGPT), since employees can access many of them, even for free. Some of those models may not satisfy the organization's security and privacy requirements — yet employees are tempted to use them for lack of a better alternative. This is shadow AI. The best solution: provide a good alternative — an AI model deployed and configured in a secure, privacy-preserving way, of sufficient quality, complying with the organization's values and policies. In addition, make the risks of shadow AI very clear to users.

⚠ Common misconception

“We blocked ChatGPT at the proxy, so we're safe.” Prohibition without alternative just moves usage to personal devices where you have zero visibility. The guide's remedy is constructive: a sanctioned, secure, good-enough alternative plus honest risk communication — because the demand doesn't disappear.

Why does “direct runtime model poisoning” appear on the self-hosted map but not on the external-hosted map?
Because the threat targets the deployed model artifact. Self-hosted, that artifact lives in YOUR infrastructure, so an attacker breaking into your runtime can tamper with it — your threat, your control. Externally hosted, the model lives at the provider; the same threat exists but in the provider's zone, covered by the provider's runtime security controls — which is exactly why hosting joins your supply chain management.
A vendor offers you a “private instance”. What must you verify before concluding your data stays private?
Whether “private” refers to the model runtime or merely the API endpoint. If the model still runs on the vendor's shared cluster, your data leaves your environment in clear text regardless of the private API; then the retention, logging/monitoring, and training-use questions determine the real exposure.

Key Points

  • WATCH = 9 Model-I/O-handling controls + #MONITOR USE; the domain of data/model engineers using maths, statistics, linguistics, ML.
  • The “Monitor, rate limit, model access control” trio recurs against almost every input threat — it's the always-on baseline.
  • LIMIT = data quartet (#DATA MINIMIZE, #ALLOWED DATA, #SHORT RETAIN, #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA) + behaviour controls (#OVERSIGHT, #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE, #AI TRANSPARENCY, #EXPLAINABILITY, plus dual-listed validation/bias testing).
  • Ready-made model options: provider-hosted closed source (only option for the largest models), self-hosted open weights (most secure on-prem; VPC if provider trusted; won't run the largest models), open weights at a paid host (convenient).
  • Self-hosted: you inherit ALL runtime threats including direct runtime model poisoning; supplier keeps development-time duties.
  • External-hosted: provider takes runtime model protection; your supply chain management must cover model AND hosting; your data exists in clear text at the provider.
  • Ask the four questions: where does it run, what's retained (remember the OpenAI court-ordered log retention), what's logged/monitored, is input used for training.
  • Weigh risk fairly — the vendor may protect their environment better than you protect yours; and fight shadow AI with a good sanctioned alternative, not just bans.
Module 01 · Periodic table, Navigator & Risk analysis · OWASP AI Exchange

The Periodic Table, the Navigator, and Turning Threats into Risks

~20 minCovers guide pp. 44–53

Here is the module's payoff: the Periodic Table of AI Security — every threat paired with its full control stack in one table — plus the navigator that structures the four deep-dive sections, and the risk-analysis process that turns this catalogue into a short list of risks YOUR system must treat.

The Periodic Table of AI Security

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 44–49, permalink owaspai.org/go/periodictable/. Created by the OWASP AI Exchange: threats and controls organized by asset, impact and attack surface, with deep links into the Exchange. Note: general governance controls apply to all threats — they are omitted from each row precisely because they are universal.

Asset & ImpactAttack surface with lifecycleThreat/Risk categoryControls
Model behaviour IntegrityRuntime — Model use (provide input/read output)Direct prompt injectionLimit unwanted behavior, Monitor, rate limit, model access control plus: Prompt injection I/O handling, Model alignment
Runtime — Model use (provide input/read output)Indirect prompt injectionLimit unwanted behavior, Prompt injection I/O handling, Input segregation
Runtime — Model use (provide input/read output)Evasion (e.g., adversarial examples)Limit unwanted behavior, Monitor, rate limit, model access control plus: Anomalous input handling, Evasion input handling, Unwanted input series handling, evasion robust model, train adversarial, input distortion, adversarial robust distillation
Runtime — Break into deployed modelDirect runtime model poisoning (reprogramming)Limit unwanted behavior, Runtime model integrity, runtime model input/output integrity
Development — Engineering environmentDirect development-time model poisoningLimit unwanted behavior, Development environment security, data segregation, federated learning, supply chain management plus: model ensemble
Development — Engineering environmentData poisoning of train/finetune dataLimit unwanted behavior, Development environment security, data segregation, federated learning, supply chain management plus: model ensemble plus: More training data, data quality control, train data distortion, poison robust model, train adversarial
Development — Supply chainSupply-chain model poisoningLimit unwanted behavior, Supplier: Development environment security, data segregation, federated learning. Producer: supply chain management plus: model ensemble
Training data ConfidentialityRuntime — Model useDisclosure in outputSensitive data limitation (data minimize, short retain, obfuscate training data) plus: Monitor, rate limit, model access control plus: Sensitive output handling
Runtime — Model useModel inversion / Membership inferenceSensitive data limitation (data minimize, short retain, obfuscate training data) plus: Monitor, rate limit, model access control plus: Unwanted input series handling, >Obscure confidence [sic], Small model
Development — Engineering environmentDirect training data leakSensitive data limitation (data minimize, short retain, obfuscate training data) plus: Development environment security, data segregation, federated learning
Model confidentialityRuntime — Model useModel exfiltration (input-output harvesting)Monitor, rate limit, model access control plus: Unwanted input series handling
Runtime — Break into deployed modelDirect runtime model leakRuntime model confidentiality, Model obfuscation
Development — Engineering environmentDirect development-time model leakDevelopment environment security, data segregation, federated learning
Model behaviour AvailabilityModel useAI resource exhaustion (model resource depletion)Monitor, rate limit, model access control plus: Dos input validation, limit resources
Model input data ConfidentialityRuntime — All ITInput data leakModel input confidentiality
Any asset, CIARuntime — All ITOutput contains conventional injectionEncode model output
Runtime — All ITGeneric runtime security threatsConventional runtime security controls
Runtime — All IT [sic — as printed, though the threat is development-environment/supply-chain]Generic development-environment and supply chain threatsConventional development security and supply chain management controls

Table — the Periodic table of AI security (guide pp. 45–49), reproduced verbatim including the original's quirks (the stray “>Obscure confidence” and the “Runtime - All IT” label on the final development row). In the original, every threat and control entry is a hyperlink into the deep-dive sections.

◆ Senior insight

Read the Controls column for its grammar: entries before “plus:” are the broad, shared control groups (limit unwanted behavior; monitor/rate-limit/access-control; sensitive data limitation; development environment security & friends); after “plus:” come the threat-specific specialists. That grammar IS defense in depth: general layers first, specialists second, governance over everything. Note also the one row where responsibility splits mid-cell: supply-chain model poisoning names Supplier: controls and Producer: controls — the periodic table encodes the shared-responsibility model you saw in the ready-made maps.

Two controls appear here (and in the navigator) that you haven't carded yet:

#INPUT SEGREGATIONSeparate untrusted data from instructionswatch · model I/O handling · runtime
IntentIndirect prompt injection works because untrusted content and trusted instructions share one prompt. Segregating them (marking, isolating, structurally separating untrusted data) denies injected text the authority of an instruction.
ImplementPeriodic table, indirect prompt injection row: “Limit unwanted behavior, Prompt injection I/O handling, Input segregation” — the one control unique to the indirect variant.
LimitsLLMs don't enforce hard privilege boundaries inside a context window — segregation reduces, not eliminates; keep blast radius control behind it.
StandardsLLM01:2025; ATLAS AML.T0051; navigator band 2.
#MODEL WATERMARKINGWatermark to prove model theftwatch · IP protection · runtime
IntentEmbed identifiable markers so a stolen/extracted copy of your model can be attributed — deterrence and evidence rather than prevention.
ImplementAppears in the navigator's band 2 under confidentiality of intellectual property / model theft through use (the poster's small text is of limited legibility there; the entry mirrors the Exchange's control catalogue).
LimitsDoes not stop exfiltration — it changes post-theft economics (attribution, legal recourse).
Standardsowaspai.org/go/navigator/.
Which controls does the periodic table give for indirect prompt injection, and how do they differ from the direct variant's?
Indirect: limit unwanted behavior, prompt injection I/O handling, input segregation. Direct: limit unwanted behavior, monitor/rate-limit/model-access-control, plus prompt injection I/O handling and model alignment. The difference tracks the attacker: direct injection comes from an authenticated user (so access control, rate limiting and alignment matter); indirect injection rides on data, so the unique defense is segregating untrusted data from instructions.

The navigator: how the deep-dive sections are structured

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 49–50, permalink owaspai.org/go/navigator/. The navigator poster (“OWASP AI EXCHANGE — AI security threats and controls navigator”) “outlines the structure of the deep-dive sections, illustrating the relationships between threats, controls, associated risks, and the types of controls applied”; clicking the image yields a PDF with clickable links. Bottom-right note: “Threat model based on Software Improvement Group AI framework.”

The details of threats and controls are covered in four deep-dive sections, each grouping threats with their controls:

  1. General controls (against all threats)
  2. Input threats and controls
  3. Development-time threats and controls
  4. Runtime conventional security threats and controls

The poster's legend colour-codes each control by WHO implements it: standard information security control (with attention points), runtime AI engineer control, development-time AI engineer control, other control — plus an indication of impact on Confidentiality, Integrity or Availability. Many rows say “see always”: the always-on controls of that band apply before any specialist.

flowchart LR
  NAV["AI security threats and controls navigator"] --> B1["1. General controls against all threats"]
  NAV --> B2["2. Controls against input threats"]
  NAV --> B3["3. Controls against development-time threats"]
  NAV --> B4["4. Runtime conventional security controls"]
  B1 --> B1a["Governance: AIPROGRAM, SECPROGRAM, SECDEV PROGRAM, DEVPROGRAM, CHECK COMPLIANCE, SEC EDUCATE"]
  B1 --> B1b["Sensitive data limitation: DATA MINIMIZE, ALLOWED DATA, SHORT RETAIN, OBFUSCATE TRAINDATA, DISCRETE"]
  B1 --> B1c["Limit unwanted behaviour: OVERSIGHT, LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE, MODEL ALIGNMENT, AI TRANSPARENCY, CONTINUOUS VALIDATION, EXPLAINABILITY, UNWANTED BIAS TESTING"]
  B2 --> B2a["Always for input threats: MONITOR USE, RATE LIMIT, MODEL ACCESS CONTROL, ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING, UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING, OBSCURE CONFIDENCE"]
  B2 --> B2b["Evasion: EVASION INPUT HANDLING, EVASION ROBUST MODEL, TRAIN ADVERSARIAL, INPUT DISTORTION, ADVERSARIAL ROBUST DISTILLATION"]
  B2 --> B2c["Direct prompt injection: PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING"]
  B2 --> B2d["Indirect prompt injection: PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING, INPUT SEGREGATION"]
  B2 --> B2e["Disclosure in output: SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING"]
  B2 --> B2f["Model inversion and membership inference: SMALL MODEL"]
  B2 --> B2g["Model theft through use: MODEL WATERMARKING"]
  B2 --> B2h["AI resource exhaustion: DOS INPUT VALIDATION, LIMIT RESOURCES"]
  B3 --> B3a["Always dev-time: DEV SECURITY, SEGREGATE DATA, CONF COMPUTE, FEDERATED LEARNING, SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE"]
  B3 --> B3b["Dev-time model poisoning: MODEL ENSEMBLE"]
  B3 --> B3c["Data poisoning: MORE TRAIN DATA, DATA QUALITY CONTROL, TRAIN DATA DISTORTION, POISON ROBUST MODEL"]
  B3 --> B3d["Data, model and source code leaks dev-time: see always controls"]
  B4 --> B4a["All CIA risks: technical application controls, operational security"]
  B4 --> B4b["Runtime model poisoning: RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITY, RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY"]
  B4 --> B4c["Runtime model leak: RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY, MODEL OBFUSCATION"]
  B4 --> B4d["Output contains injection: ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT"]
  B4 --> B4e["Input data leak: MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITY"]
  B4 --> B4f["Augmentation data: AUGMENTATION DATA CONFIDENTIALITY, AUGMENTATION DATA INTEGRITY"]

Figure — the navigator's four bands (guide p. 50): a single-page mental map of the whole Exchange, mapping four control layers to the risk categories (integrity of model behaviour, confidentiality of data, confidentiality of IP, availability, injection, input/augmentation confidentiality), colour-coded by implementer.

From catalogue to YOUR risks: risk analysis

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 50–52, permalink owaspai.org/go/riskanalysis/. Standards named: ISO/IEC 27005 (the international standard for security risk management), ISO 31000 and ISO 23894 (common bases for risk management frameworks).

The relevance and severity of each threat — and the appropriate controls, and who is responsible for implementing them — depend on your use case and how AI is deployed in your environment. That determination should be guided by a risk assessment based on your architecture and intended use.

Framing: organizations classify risks into key areas — Strategic, Operational, Financial, Compliance, Reputation, Technology, Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG). A threat becomes a risk when it exploits one or more vulnerabilities. AI threats can hit multiple risk domains at once: adversarial attacks can disrupt operations, distort financial models, and cause compliance issues (see the AI security matrix in lesson 3 for threats, risks and impact).

Two governance anchors: general AI risk management is driven by AI governance (#AIPROGRAM) and covers risks BY AI systems as well as risks TO them; security risk assessment is driven by the security management system (#SECPROGRAM), which must include AI assets, AI threats, and AI systems in its repositories.

The risk management process (ISO 31000 / ISO 23894 style)
1 · Identify risksrecognize potential risks to the organization or others
→
2 · Evaluate riskslikelihood × severity = risk level; presented as a heatmap
→
3 · Risk treatmentMitigation, Transfer, Avoidance, or Acceptance
→
4 · Communicate & monitorstakeholder awareness; effective treatments; the Risk Register
→
Repeatregularly, and when changes warrant it

The Risk Register is the working artifact of step 4: a comprehensive list of risks and their attributes — severity, treatment plan, ownership, status, and so on.

Step 1: identifying risks — the decision tree / threat model

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 52–53, permalink owaspai.org/go/threatmodel/. NOTE: the guide's scope here is AI-specific risks only — risks to the AI-specific assets.

The AI Exchange's catalogue of threats identifies which risks apply to a specific AI system, depending on architecture, context, domain and use case. This is a threat modeling process — the bridge between a list of threats and a set of concrete, prioritized risks. The catalogue represents “attacks that could happen”; threat modeling answers three key questions:

  1. Which threats theoretically apply to this system?
  2. How could they realistically happen?
  3. What would the impact be?

The next step (subsection 2 of the risk analysis section) then examines likelihood and impact in more detail.

THREAT ADVISOR: there is an AI assistant that uses all this material to take you through the threat-model process. Go to the advisor (requires a Google account), briefly describe your system, and it interviews you — your data remains in your own Google workspace.

The AI Exchange threat modelling one-pager (the image itself sits on the guide's next page, p. 54) summarizes the step-by-step decision-tree approach. How to use it:

  1. Walk by each threat.
  2. Based on the 'When' column, determine whether that threat applies in theory.
  3. If it does, use the 'Impact' column to decide whether the risk needs to be treated, depending on the level of harm for the use case.

The result: you start big, but you end up with a relatively small list of risks to focus on.

▸ Worked example

From the guide, first-person: “You don't have to protect against model inversion attacks that try to steal your training data, if that data isn't sensitive. It sounds obvious, but I've seen many cases of protections in place for threats that effectively don't matter.” The When-column logic kills the threat before you spend a euro on defenses.

▸ Worked example

Also from the guide: if your agentic system uses an LLM, it is in theory susceptible to indirect prompt injection — malicious instructions in untrusted data that manipulate agent behaviour. But if your only concern is that sensitive company data leaks, and there is no way for the system to send data to an attacker (e.g. email), then the threat remains theoretical — the risk does not have to be treated. Notice this is the lethal trifecta from lesson 2 in decision-tree form: the missing Send leg zeroes the risk.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Inventory the AI system's architecture, context, domain, use case — the four relevance drivers.
  • Walk the full threat catalogue (matrix / periodic table rows) — skip nothing at this stage.
  • Apply the 'When' test per threat: can it apply here in theory?
  • Apply the 'Impact' test: does the harm matter for this use case?
  • Record decisions and rationale in the risk register (mandatory evidence under the AI Act).
  • Re-run on every architecture change — a new egress integration can resurrect a “theoretical” risk overnight.
State the difference between a threat and a risk as the guide defines it, and why the distinction changes your spending.
A threat becomes a risk when it exploits one or more vulnerabilities — a catalogue entry is only a candidate. Spending follows risks, not threats: model inversion is always a threat to any model, but it is only a risk (worth treating) if the training data is sensitive and the attack path exists.
Name the four risk treatment strategies and give an AI-flavoured example of “avoidance”.
Mitigation, Transfer, Avoidance, Acceptance. Avoidance in AI terms is lesson 1's closing move: remove the unnecessary AI component — eliminating all AI-related risks — or decline to feed sensitive data into an external-hosted model at all.

Key Points

  • The Periodic Table organizes all 18 threat rows by asset & impact and attack surface with lifecycle, pairing each with its full control stack; general governance controls apply to all threats.
  • Controls-column grammar: shared groups first, “plus:” specialists second — defense in depth in table form; the supply-chain row splits Supplier vs Producer responsibilities.
  • #INPUT SEGREGATION is the control unique to indirect prompt injection; #MODEL WATERMARKING is the IP-attribution control in the navigator.
  • Deep dives come in four sections: general controls, input threats, development-time threats, runtime conventional threats — the navigator is their one-page map, colour-coded by implementer.
  • Risk domains: Strategic, Operational, Financial, Compliance, Reputation, Technology, Environmental, Social, Governance (ESG); a threat becomes a risk when it exploits a vulnerability.
  • #AIPROGRAM drives AI risk management (risks BY and TO AI); #SECPROGRAM drives security risk assessment; ISO/IEC 27005 governs security risk management; ISO 31000 / ISO 23894 frame the process: identify → evaluate (heatmap) → treat (mitigate/transfer/avoid/accept) → communicate & monitor (risk register) → repeat.
  • Threat modeling answers: which threats apply in theory, how could they realistically happen, what would the impact be — use the one-pager's 'When' and 'Impact' columns (THREAT ADVISOR automates the interview).
  • Start big, end with a small list: don't defend training data nobody cares about, and don't treat injection risks whose Send leg doesn't exist.
Module 02 · How to address AI security · OWASP AI Exchange

The Control Landscape I — Extending Conventional Security to AI Assets

~15 minCovers guide pp. 39–41

Before you can analyse risk, you need to know what the defensive toolbox contains. The guide closes its opening summary with a grouped inventory of every control family: extend supply chain management, extend conventional security, build resilient models, watch, and limit. This lesson teaches the first two groups — the controls that are extensions of security work your organization already does — and gives you a reference card for each control ID you will select from during risk treatment.

Five strategies, one system

The guide's high-level advice for addressing AI security resolves into five strategy groups. Each group names the #ALLCAPS control IDs that implement it — these IDs are the vocabulary of the whole AI Exchange, and every risk-treatment decision you make in this module ends with selecting some of them. The guide closes the summary with: "All threats and controls are explored in more detail in the corresponding threat sections of the AI Exchange."

The five strategy groups for addressing AI security (guide pp. 39–41)
Extend supply chain management
Suppliers of data, models, hosting#SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE
Extend conventional security
Managing security#SECPROGRAM · #SECDEV PROGRAM
Development-time#DEV SECURITY · #SEGREGATE DATA · #DISCRETE
Runtimemodel storage, use, augmentation data, I/O
Have resilient models
Data/model engineeringrobustness against input attacks and poisoning
Watch
Model I/O handlingfilter, stop, alert
Monitoring#MONITOR USE
Limit
Minimize sensitive dataa model can leak data
Limit model behavioura model can make or be misled into mistakes
◆ Senior insight

Notice how much of AI security is not new. Two of the five groups — supply chain management and conventional security controls — are extensions of practices your organization already runs. The senior move is not to build a parallel "AI security" function, but to feed AI assets (models, training data, augmentation data, pipelines) into the existing ISMS, SDLC and supplier-management machinery, and then add the genuinely AI-specific layers (resilience, I/O handling, behaviour limitation) on top.

Strategy 1 — Extend supply chain management

AI introduces new supplier types: providers of data, of models, and of hosting. Your supplier-management process must incorporate all three. This single control carries enormous weight later in the module: it is the primary mitigation for supply-chain model poisoning, and the mechanism through which you gain assurance when a provider runs your model.

#SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGESupply chain management for data, models and hostinggovernance · development-time · all threats via suppliers
IntentPrevent poisoned, leaked or otherwise compromised AI assets entering your system through third parties — by selecting a trustworthy supplier and verifying the authenticity of the model (guide p. 55).
ImplementGain assurance on the security posture of the provider: the provider prevents model poisoning during development, including data poisoning, and uses uncompromised data. For supplier-run models, verify the deployed model cannot be manipulated at runtime — "just the way you would expect any supplier to protect their running application from manipulation."
LimitsProviders are typically not very open about their security countermeasures, so gaining sufficient assurance can be challenging. The guide notes regulations will hopefully drive more provider transparency. If residual poisoning risk is unacceptable, fall back to post-training countermeasures (#POISON ROBUST MODEL) — only possible with expertise and access to model parameters (e.g., open-source weights).
StandardsDeep dive: development-time controls, owaspai.org/go/navigator/. Related canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM03 (Supply Chain), MITRE ATLAS AML.T0010 (ML Supply Chain Compromise).

Strategy 2 — Extend existing conventional security controls

These controls protect the AI-specific assets — model, data, documentation, augmentation data (including system prompts) and model input/output — with the same discipline you apply to any other crown-jewel asset. The guide sub-groups them into managing security, development-time, and runtime.

Managing security

#SECPROGRAMSecurity management program including AIgovernance · all threats
IntentMake the organization's information security management system (ISMS) cover AI: security risk assessment is driven by this system, "as this system is tasked to include AI assets, AI threats, and AI systems, provided that these have been added to the corresponding repositories" (guide p. 51).
ImplementRegister AI assets, threats and systems in the ISMS repositories; run security risk assessment through it. After control selection, this program carries "further management of the selected controls": continuous monitoring, documentation, reporting, and incident response (risk-analysis step 9, guide p. 64).
LimitsOnly works if AI assets are actually inventoried — an unregistered model is invisible to the ISMS and to every downstream risk process.
StandardsISO/IEC 27005 is the international standard for security risk management (guide p. 51). See also NIST AI RMF GOVERN function.
#SECDEV PROGRAMSecure development program including AI engineeringgovernance · development-time
IntentExtend the secure software development lifecycle so that AI engineering (data pipelines, training, model deployment) is in scope of secure-development practices, not a shadow process beside them.
ImplementListed by the guide under "managing security" in the conventional-controls extension group (p. 39): apply your secure-development standards, reviews and tooling to AI teams and artifacts.
StandardsDeep dive: general controls, owaspai.org/go/navigator/.

Development-time: model, data, and documentation

#DEV SECURITYDevelopment environment securitydevelopment-time · conventional extension
IntentProtect the engineering environment where models and data live during development — the attack surface behind direct development-time model poisoning, data poisoning, direct training data leak and direct development-time model leak.
ImplementConventional environment hardening (access control, logging, network segmentation) applied to training pipelines, data stores and model artifacts and their documentation.
StandardsBase control of deep-dive section 3 (development-time threats), owaspai.org/go/navigator/.
#SEGREGATE DATASegregate data in the development environmentdevelopment-time · conventional extension
IntentSeparate training/finetune data and model artifacts from other environments and users, so a compromise elsewhere cannot silently reach the data that shapes model behaviour.
ImplementIsolation of data stores, restricted paths between engineering zones; appears in the periodic table as a standard companion of development environment security against poisoning and development-time leaks.
StandardsDeep dive: development-time controls (navigator band 3).
#DISCRETEMinimize information an attacker can learn about your AI approachdevelopment-time · sensitive data limitation
IntentRaise attacker cost by not advertising model details, technology choices and documentation that make targeted attacks (evasion tuning, poisoning, theft) cheaper to prepare.
ImplementTreat model/system documentation as sensitive; the navigator lists it under sensitive-data-limitation general controls.
LimitsObscurity is a cost-raiser, never a substitute for the other groups.
StandardsDeep dive: general controls (navigator band 1).

Runtime: model storage, model use, augmentation data (including system prompts), and model input/output

#MODEL ACCESS CONTROLAuthenticate and authorize model usersruntime · threats through use
IntentShrink the population that can attack through the model interface at all. Anonymous access means "number of potential attackers" (a likelihood factor in step 2) is effectively unbounded.
ImplementPart of the recurring periodic-table trio "Monitor, rate limit, model access control" applied against prompt injection, evasion, disclosure in output, model inversion, model exfiltration and resource exhaustion.
StandardsDeep dive: input-threat controls (navigator band 2).
#RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITYProtect the integrity of the deployed modelruntime · conventional extension
IntentStop attackers who break into your deployment from altering model parameters or files — the direct runtime model poisoning (reprogramming) threat.
ImplementIntegrity protection of the stored/loaded model (e.g., verification of the artifact you serve). Paired in the periodic table with runtime model input/output integrity.
StandardsDeep dive: runtime conventional security controls (navigator band 4).
#RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITYProtect integrity of model input/output in transitruntime · conventional extension
IntentPrevent manipulation of data flowing to and from the model — an attacker who can rewrite input or output in transit controls behaviour without touching the model itself.
ImplementConventional transport/message integrity applied to the model's I/O path; listed against direct runtime model poisoning in the periodic table.
StandardsDeep dive: runtime conventional security controls (navigator band 4).
#RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITYProtect the deployed model from theftruntime · conventional extension
IntentDefend against direct runtime model leak — theft of model parameters from the serving environment, which loses IP and hands attackers a white-box copy to craft input attacks against.
ImplementAccess control and protection of the model at rest and in memory in the runtime environment; combined with #MODEL OBFUSCATION in the periodic table.
StandardsDeep dive: runtime conventional security controls (navigator band 4).
#MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITYProtect confidentiality of model inputruntime · conventional extension
IntentCounter the input data leak threat: sensitive prompts and inputs (including RAG-retrieved data) leaking in transit, at rest, or from logs.
ImplementConventional data-in-transit/data-at-rest protection on the whole input path — the periodic table lists it as the sole named control for "Input data leak" (attack surface: Runtime - All IT).
StandardsDeep dive: runtime conventional security controls (navigator band 4).
#ENCODE MODEL OUTPUTEncode model output before downstream interpretationruntime · conventional extension
IntentNeutralize "output contains conventional injection": model text rendered in a browser or fed to a database can carry XSS or SQL injection. Treat model output as untrusted data, exactly like user input.
ImplementContext-appropriate output encoding/escaping wherever model output is interpreted downstream.
StandardsDeep dive: runtime conventional security controls (navigator band 4). Canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM05 (Improper Output Handling).
#LIMIT RESOURCESCap compute and spend per request/userruntime · availability
IntentBound the damage of AI resource exhaustion — actors overusing your AI causing availability problems and/or high cost (fund depletion when the model runs as a paid service).
ImplementResource quotas and limits on model serving; paired with #DOS INPUT VALIDATION in the periodic table.
StandardsDeep dive: input-threat controls (navigator band 2). Canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM10 (Unbounded Consumption), ATLAS AML.T0029 (Denial of ML Service).
#AUGMENTATION DATA CONFIDENTIALITYProtect augmentation data from disclosureruntime · conventional extension
IntentPrevent direct augmentation data leak: theft of RAG repositories (e.g., a vector database), agent memory, or sensitive system prompts — all data your system inserts into model input.
ImplementConventional confidentiality controls on the stores holding augmentation data. The guide stresses this "also counts for system prompts, if they are sensitive."
StandardsDeep dive: runtime conventional security controls (navigator band 4). Canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM07 (System Prompt Leakage), LLM08 (Vector and Embedding Weaknesses).
#AUGMENTATION DATA INTEGRITYProtect augmentation data from manipulationruntime · conventional extension
IntentCounter augmentation data manipulation: because RAG data, memory and system prompts "play a role in determining the model behaviour", corrupting them is behaviour-hijacking without touching the model.
ImplementIntegrity protection (access control, change control, verification) on augmentation data stores and their write paths.
StandardsDeep dive: runtime conventional security controls (navigator band 4).
#CONF COMPUTEConfidential compute — protect data and model in usedevelopment-time/runtime · conventional extension
IntentProtect data and model parameters even while being processed (in memory), reducing exposure to a compromised or curious host — relevant when compute is shared or outsourced.
ImplementHardware-backed trusted execution; listed by the navigator among the base development-time controls.
LimitsDoes not remove the need for the provider-trust analysis of Lesson 3: a provider-hosted model still needs your input in clear text at the model boundary.
StandardsDeep dive: development-time controls (navigator band 3).
#MODEL OBFUSCATIONObfuscate the stored modelruntime · conventional extension
IntentMake a stolen model artifact harder to use: raises attacker cost after a runtime break-in, complementing #RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY against direct runtime model leak.
ImplementObfuscation/packing of the deployed model artifact.
LimitsRaises cost only; a determined attacker with full runtime access can usually de-obfuscate. Pair with detection (#MONITOR USE).
StandardsDeep dive: runtime conventional security controls (navigator band 4).
Why does the guide treat "extend conventional security" as its own strategy instead of assuming it happens automatically?
Because AI assets often live outside the inventories that conventional controls key on. A model file, a training set, a vector database or a system prompt only gets access control, integrity protection and monitoring if someone explicitly declares it an asset. The strategy is "extension": same controls, new assets — but the extension must be deliberate (#SECPROGRAM requires AI assets to be added to the corresponding repositories).
An attacker never touches your model but rewrites the documents in your vector database. Which control family failed, and what is the threat called?
Augmentation data integrity (#AUGMENTATION DATA INTEGRITY) failed; the threat is augmentation data manipulation. Because augmentation data (RAG data, memory, system prompts) is inserted into model input, corrupting it steers model behaviour without any attack on the model artifact itself.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 39–41 (control-group summary). The runtime/development-time placement of each control follows the AI Exchange navigator, owaspai.org/go/navigator/. ISO/IEC 27090 (AI security guidance, under development at the time of the guide) is the ISO home for these AI-security controls; NIST AI RMF places this inventory work in its GOVERN and MAP functions.

Key Points

  • The guide organizes all defence into five strategy groups: extend supply chain management, extend conventional security, resilient models, watch, and limit.
  • #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE now covers suppliers of data, models and hosting — and is the main lever against supply-chain model poisoning.
  • #SECPROGRAM (ISMS including AI) and #SECDEV PROGRAM (secure development including AI engineering) anchor "managing security".
  • Development-time conventional extensions: #DEV SECURITY, #SEGREGATE DATA, #DISCRETE.
  • Runtime conventional extensions protect model storage, use, augmentation data (including system prompts) and I/O: #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL, #RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITY, #RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY, #RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY, #MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITY, #ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT, #LIMIT RESOURCES, #AUGMENTATION DATA CONFIDENTIALITY, #AUGMENTATION DATA INTEGRITY, #CONF COMPUTE, #MODEL OBFUSCATION.
  • Model output is untrusted data: encode it (#ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT) or inherit XSS/SQL injection through the model.
  • System prompts are augmentation data and can be sensitive — protect their confidentiality and integrity.
Module 02 · How to address AI security · OWASP AI Exchange

The Control Landscape II — Resilient Models, Watch, and Limit

~17 minCovers guide pp. 39–41

Conventional security cannot make a model resist adversarial inputs, and it cannot stop a model from confidently doing the wrong thing. That takes the three AI-specific strategy groups: engineering resilient models, watching model I/O at runtime, and limiting both the sensitive data a model can leak and the behaviour a model is allowed to have. This lesson completes your control vocabulary — 31 more control cards you will draw on when selecting controls in step 7 of the risk analysis.

Strategy 3 — Have resilient models

Apply data/model engineering controls during model development for resilience against input attacks. This is the work of data and model engineers. Input attacks include triggering poisoned models — which, the guide notes, can of course also be mitigated by preventing the model being poisoned in the first place using conventional controls. Resilience is defence-in-depth: assume some poison or adversarial input gets through, and make the model shrug it off.

#MODEL ALIGNMENTAlign GenAI behaviour with intended valuesdevelopment-time · limit unwanted behaviour
IntentTrain/tune a GenAI model so harmful instructions are refused — the model-side defence against direct prompt injection (the periodic table pairs it with prompt injection I/O handling).
ImplementFor ready-made models this is largely the supplier's work ("Supplier's GenAI Model alignment" in the threat maps); your job is to verify it meets your risk appetite via testing.
LimitsAlignment is statistical, not absolute — jailbreaks exist; hence the layered I/O handling controls.
StandardsNavigator band 1 (limit unwanted behaviour). Canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM01.
#FEDERATED LEARNINGTrain without centralizing the datadevelopment-time · data engineering
IntentKeep training data distributed at its sources so there is no central trove to steal or poison wholesale; listed in the periodic table against development-time poisoning and training-data leaks.
ImplementDecentralized training where parties exchange model updates rather than raw data.
LimitsEach participating node becomes a potential poisoning entry point — it redistributes, not eliminates, the integrity problem.
StandardsNavigator band 3 (development-time base controls).
#CONTINUOUS VALIDATIONContinuously test model behaviourruntime · limit unwanted behaviour · shared with safety
IntentSafeguard accuracy over time: catch drift, degradation, and the behavioural fingerprints of poisoning. The guide names it a shared concern between safety and security (p. 66).
ImplementOngoing evaluation of model outputs against expected quality; appears in both the "resilient models" and "limit model behaviour" groups.
StandardsNavigator band 1. NIST AI RMF MEASURE function.
#UNWANTED BIAS TESTINGTest for unwanted biasdevelopment-time/runtime · limit unwanted behaviour
IntentDetect systematic mistreatment of groups — and, as a security side-benefit, surface attacks: the guide's example is a data-poisoning attack that "at first goes unnoticed, but then is discovered by an unexplained detection of bias in the model" (p. 67).
ImplementBias measurement across protected attributes as part of validation; listed in both "resilient models" and "limit model behaviour" groups.
StandardsNavigator band 1.
#EVASION ROBUST MODELBuild the model to resist adversarial inputsdevelopment-time · model engineering
IntentReduce the model's sensitivity to crafted perturbations so evasion attacks (fooling a classifier with data, not instructions) need larger, more detectable changes.
ImplementRobust training and architecture choices by model engineers; one of the periodic table's anti-evasion set.
StandardsNavigator band 2 (evasion). ATLAS AML.T0015 (Evade ML Model).
#POISON ROBUST MODELPost-training countermeasures against poisoningdevelopment-time · model engineering
IntentReduce the effect of poison already present in a model — the fallback when supplier assurance leaves the data-poisoning risk unacceptable (guide p. 55).
Implement"Implementing post-training countermeasures can be an option if you have the expertise and if you have access to the model parameters (e.g., open source weights)."
LimitsUnavailable for closed provider-hosted models (no parameter access); demands specialist expertise.
StandardsNavigator band 3 (data poisoning). ATLAS AML.T0020 (Poison Training Data).
#TRAIN ADVERSARIALAdversarial trainingdevelopment-time · model engineering
IntentImmunize by exposure: include adversarial examples in training so the decision boundary hardens where attackers probe it. Listed against both evasion and data poisoning in the periodic table.
ImplementGenerate attack samples during training and train the model to classify them correctly.
LimitsRobustness to known attack styles; novel perturbations may still work — an arms race, not a fix.
StandardsNavigator band 2 (evasion).
#TRAIN DATA DISTORTIONDistort/smooth training datadevelopment-time · data engineering
IntentWeaken fine-grained poison patterns and triggers by transforming training data before use — the poison signal degrades more than the legitimate signal.
ImplementData transformation/filtering in the training pipeline; part of the periodic table's data-poisoning set.
LimitsDistortion trades some accuracy for integrity — a tuning parameter to test (see step 7's control-tuning guidance).
StandardsNavigator band 3 (data poisoning).
#ADVERSARIAL ROBUST DISTILLATIONDistill a robust student modeldevelopment-time · model engineering
IntentUse distillation to produce a model with smoother decision surfaces, harder to steer with small adversarial perturbations.
ImplementTrain a student model on a teacher's outputs with robustness objectives; part of the anti-evasion set.
StandardsNavigator band 2 (evasion).
#MODEL ENSEMBLECombine multiple modelsdevelopment-time · model engineering
IntentOutvote the compromised: if one model in an ensemble is poisoned, the others can mask its manipulated behaviour. The periodic table lists it against all three model-poisoning variants (development-time, data, supply-chain).
ImplementServe predictions from several independently built models and aggregate.
LimitsMultiplies training/serving cost; correlated poisoning (same supplier, same dataset) defeats it.
StandardsNavigator band 3 (development-time model poisoning).
#MORE TRAINDATAMore (clean) training datadevelopment-time · data engineering
IntentDilute poison: a fixed number of malicious samples has less influence in a larger clean dataset, raising the attacker's required injection volume (and detection risk).
ImplementExpand the training set from trusted sources; part of the data-poisoning set.
StandardsNavigator band 3 (data poisoning).
#SMALL MODELPrefer a smaller modeldevelopment-time · training data confidentiality
IntentLess capacity, less memorization: smaller models retain fewer verbatim training samples, blunting model inversion and membership inference (its pairing in the periodic table).
ImplementChoose model capacity fitted to the task rather than maximal.
LimitsCapacity trade-off against task performance.
StandardsNavigator band 2 (model inversion / membership inference). ATLAS AML.T0024 (Exfiltration via ML Inference API).
#DATA QUALITY CONTROLInspect and clean training datadevelopment-time · data engineering
IntentCatch poisoned or corrupt samples before they teach the model anything — quality assurance doubling as an integrity control.
ImplementData validation, anomaly detection and provenance checks in the pipeline; part of the periodic table's data-poisoning set. The risk-treatment example on p. 62 is exactly this: "enhancing data validation processes to prevent data poisoning attacks."
StandardsNavigator band 3 (data poisoning).

Strategy 4 — Watch

Apply Model I/O handling for runtime filtering, stopping or alerting on suspicious input or output. The guide is precise about whose job this is: "typically the territory of data and model engineers e.g. data scientists, involving elements from mathematics, statistics, linguistics and machine learning." These are not WAF rules a security engineer writes alone — detecting an adversarial example or an injection attempt is itself a modelling problem. On top of I/O handling sits monitoring of model inference, extending Model I/O handling and Oversight, and overlooking general usage of the AI system.

#ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLINGDetect and handle out-of-distribution inputruntime · model I/O handling
IntentFlag inputs that look nothing like legitimate traffic — a generic tripwire in front of evasion and probing attacks.
ImplementStatistical/ML detection of anomalous inputs with filtering, blocking or alerting responses; in the navigator's "always for input threats" set.
LimitsThresholds are quality parameters to tune between false positives and misses (step 7 tuning guidance).
StandardsNavigator band 2.
#EVASION INPUT HANDLINGDetect evasion patterns in inputruntime · model I/O handling
IntentSpecifically recognize adversarial-example characteristics (crafted perturbations) rather than generic anomalies.
ImplementDetection models/heuristics tuned to adversarial patterns; periodic-table anti-evasion set.
StandardsNavigator band 2 (evasion).
#UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLINGDetect suspicious series of inputsruntime · model I/O handling
IntentSome attacks are invisible per-request and obvious per-campaign: model inversion, membership inference, and model exfiltration (input-output harvesting) all need many queries. Watch the sequence, not the sample.
ImplementTrack per-client query patterns over time; the periodic table lists it against model inversion/membership inference and model exfiltration.
StandardsNavigator band 2. ATLAS AML.T0024 (Exfiltration via ML Inference API).
#PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLINGFilter and detect prompt injection in input/outputruntime · model I/O handling
IntentCatch injected instructions on the way in, and injected outcomes on the way out — the runtime layer against both direct and indirect prompt injection.
ImplementInput/output classifiers and rules; the periodic table applies it to direct prompt injection (with model alignment) and to indirect prompt injection (with input segregation).
LimitsDetection is probabilistic; language is too flexible for complete filters — layer it, never rely on it alone.
StandardsNavigator band 2. Canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM01, ATLAS AML.T0051 (LLM Prompt Injection).
#DOS INPUT VALIDATIONValidate input against resource abuseruntime · model I/O handling · availability
IntentReject inputs designed to consume disproportionate compute, defending against AI resource exhaustion alongside #LIMIT RESOURCES.
ImplementSize/complexity limits and validation on model input.
StandardsNavigator band 2 (AI resource exhaustion). Canon: LLM10 (Unbounded Consumption).
#INPUT DISTORTIONPerturb input before inferenceruntime · model I/O handling
