IntentAdversarial perturbations are brittle: slightly transforming input (noise, resampling) often destroys the carefully-optimized attack signal while barely affecting legitimate accuracy.
ImplementInput transformation at inference time; part of the anti-evasion set. "How much noise to add to input data" is the guide's own example of a control parameter to tune (p. 64).
LimitsAccuracy trade-off; adaptive attackers can optimize against known distortions.
StandardsNavigator band 2 (evasion).
#SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLINGFilter sensitive data from model outputruntime · model I/O handling
IntentLast line against disclosure in output: if training or augmentation data can surface in responses, detect and redact it at the output boundary.
ImplementOutput scanning/redaction for sensitive patterns; periodic-table pairing for "Disclosure in output".
LimitsPattern-based redaction misses paraphrased leaks — pair with #DATA MINIMIZE so there is less to leak.
StandardsNavigator band 2. Canon: LLM02 (Sensitive Information Disclosure).
#OBSCURE CONFIDENCEHide or round confidence scoresruntime · model I/O handling
IntentConfidence scores are an attacker's gradient: precise scores let model-inversion and evasion attackers measure how close each probe gets. Coarsen or drop them to blind that feedback loop.
ImplementReturn labels only, or rounded/bucketed scores; listed in the periodic table against model inversion / membership inference.
LimitsSome legitimate consumers need calibrated scores — scope by client.
StandardsNavigator band 2.
#RATE LIMITThrottle access frequencyruntime · threats through use
IntentMost query-based attacks (inversion, exfiltration harvesting, exhaustion, brute-force probing) are volume businesses. Throttling turns a weekend attack into a months-long one — often below the attacker's economic threshold.
ImplementPer-user/per-key request limits; one third of the ubiquitous "Monitor, rate limit, model access control" trio in the periodic table.
LimitsDistributed attackers (many accounts/IPs) dilute per-client limits; combine with #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL and #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING.
StandardsNavigator band 2 (always for input threats).
#MONITOR USEMonitor model inference and general usageruntime · watch
IntentThe "Watch" strategy's umbrella: monitoring of model inference — extending Model I/O handling and Oversight — and overlooking general usage of the AI system, so attacks in progress and misuse become visible.
ImplementLog and review inference activity; feed alerts into incident response (which #SECPROGRAM's step-9 management loop requires).
StandardsNavigator band 2 (always for input threats). NIST AI RMF MANAGE function; ISO/IEC 27005 monitoring feeds step 10's continuous risk assessment.

Strategy 5 — Limit

The final group accepts a hard truth: a model can accidentally leak data or be misled into leaking it, and a model can accidentally make a mistake or be misled into making one. So limit what there is to leak, and limit what the model's behaviour can cause.

Minimize or obfuscate sensitive data

#DATA MINIMIZERemove or reduce sensitive datageneral · sensitive data limitation
IntentData that isn't there cannot be leaked by any attack — the highest-leverage confidentiality control, heading the periodic table's "sensitive data limitation" set against disclosure in output, inversion and training-data leaks.
ImplementStrip fields and records not needed for the task from training/augmentation/input data.
StandardsNavigator band 1 (sensitive data limitation).
#ALLOWED DATAUse only data you are allowed to usegeneral · sensitive data limitation
IntentConstrain data use to what is permitted for the purpose (legal basis, consent, licensing) — reducing both compliance risk and the sensitive surface a leak would expose.
ImplementData governance checks before data enters training or augmentation; ties into the p. 59 warning to check licenses, warranties and contracts for supplier training data.
StandardsNavigator band 1 (sensitive data limitation).
#SHORT RETAINRetain data only brieflygeneral · sensitive data limitation
IntentShrink the window in which stored data (inputs, logs, training snapshots) can be stolen — directly relevant to the provider-log leak scenario and the litigation-hold retention risk from Lesson 3.
ImplementRetention limits and deletion schedules on AI data stores and logs.
StandardsNavigator band 1 (sensitive data limitation).
#OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATAObfuscate or anonymize training datadevelopment-time · sensitive data limitation
IntentIf sensitive data must be trained on, transform it (anonymization, pseudonymization, noise) so what the model memorizes — and what inversion or disclosure could recover — is no longer the raw secret.
ImplementPrivacy-preserving transformations in the data pipeline; part of the "sensitive data limitation" set in the periodic table.
LimitsUtility/privacy trade-off — another parameter for step 7's simulation-based tuning.
StandardsNavigator band 1 (sensitive data limitation).

Limit model behaviour

#OVERSIGHTOversight of model behaviour and actionsruntime · limit unwanted behaviour · shared with safety
IntentRestrict unsafe behaviour by keeping a human or guarding system between model output and consequence. The guide names oversight a shared concern between safety and security (p. 66): the model can misbehave from an attack or from bad training data alike.
ImplementHuman-in-the-loop approval, guardrails, output checks — the decision tree's always-do panel lists "output filtering, monitoring, oversight, human in the loop, guardrails, least/scoped privileges" together.
LimitsHuman review does not scale to every inference; reserve blocking oversight for high-impact actions (the ship's watertight door, not the chat reply).
StandardsNavigator band 1 (limit unwanted behaviour). EU AI Act Article 14 mandates human oversight for high-risk AI systems.
#LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGEGive the model minimal privilegesruntime · limit unwanted behaviour
IntentA manipulated model can only cause the harm its privileges allow. Removing an agent's ability to send data outward is precisely what breaks the "lethal trifecta" (Lesson 6) — the guide's own worked example shows the indirect-prompt-injection risk staying theoretical for exactly this reason.
ImplementScope API access, tools, actions and reachable data to the minimum the use case needs; connected to #OVERSIGHT ("assigning least privileges to the model", p. 66).
StandardsNavigator band 1. Canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM06 (Excessive Agency).
#AI TRANSPARENCYBe transparent about the AI and its risksgeneral · limit unwanted behaviour · shared with privacy and safety
IntentShare information about the approach "to warn users and depending systems of accuracy risks"; in many cases users have the right to know details about a model being used and how it has been created (p. 66).
ImplementDisclosure of AI use, capability limits and accuracy risks to users and downstream systems.
StandardsNavigator band 1. Shared concern between security, privacy and safety; EU AI Act transparency obligations.
#EXPLAINABILITYExplain how results came to begeneral · limit unwanted behaviour · shared concern
IntentHelp users validate accuracy by explaining in more detail how a specific result was produced — supporting transparency and showing what needs to change for a different outcome (p. 66).
ImplementResult-level explanations surfaced to users; a shared concern between security, privacy, safety and business function — plus compliance where explainability is required by law.
StandardsNavigator band 1 (limit unwanted behaviour).
🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Resilience controls are the model team's job — budget data-scientist time for them, not just security-engineer time.
  • Deploy the "always" input trio everywhere: #MONITOR USE, #RATE LIMIT, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL.
  • Watch series of inputs (#UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING), not just single requests — extraction attacks hide in volume.
  • Minimize before you protect: #DATA MINIMIZE beats any downstream leak control.
  • For agents, treat #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE and #OVERSIGHT as the primary risk reducers — they cap impact regardless of which manipulation threat fires.
Why does the guide assign Model I/O handling to data scientists rather than to the security team?
Because deciding whether an input is anomalous, adversarial or an injection is a statistical/linguistic modelling problem, "involving elements from mathematics, statistics, linguistics and machine learning". A rule-based security filter cannot characterize the input distribution of a model; the people who built the model can.
An attacker needs 100,000 queries to invert your model. Which three runtime controls jointly make that campaign uneconomical, and how?
#RATE LIMIT stretches the campaign over months; #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL forces identifiable accounts, raising the cost of parallelizing; #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING detects the query pattern before completion. Adding #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE degrades the signal per query, multiplying the queries needed.
What does "limit" protect against that "watch" cannot?
Watch controls detect and react — they can miss, and they act after input arrives. Limit controls change what is possible at all: minimized data cannot leak, and a least-privileged model cannot execute the harmful action even when manipulation succeeds undetected.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 39–41. Control placements per the AI Exchange navigator (owaspai.org/go/navigator/) and periodic table (owaspai.org/go/periodictable/). Canon mappings added: OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) LLM01, LLM02, LLM05, LLM06, LLM10; MITRE ATLAS AML.T0015, AML.T0020, AML.T0024, AML.T0029, AML.T0051.

Key Points

  • Resilient-model controls are data/model-engineering work: #MODEL ALIGNMENT, #FEDERATED LEARNING, #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION, #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING, #EVASION ROBUST MODEL, #POISON ROBUST MODEL, #TRAIN ADVERSARIAL, #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION, #ADVERSARIAL ROBUST DISTILLATION, #MODEL ENSEMBLE, #MORE TRAINDATA, #SMALL MODEL, #DATA QUALITY CONTROL.
  • Watch = Model I/O handling (#ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING, #EVASION INPUT HANDLING, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING, #PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING, #DOS INPUT VALIDATION, #INPUT DISTORTION, #SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING, #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE, #RATE LIMIT) plus #MONITOR USE.
  • Limit = minimize/obfuscate sensitive data (#DATA MINIMIZE, #ALLOWED DATA, #SHORT RETAIN, #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA) plus limit model behaviour (#OVERSIGHT, #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE, #AI TRANSPARENCY, #EXPLAINABILITY, #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION, #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING).
  • Models can accidentally leak data or be misled to; they can accidentally err or be misled to — that dual "accident or attack" framing is why limit-controls are shared between safety and security.
  • Query-based attacks are volume businesses: rate limiting, access control and series detection attack the attacker's economics.
  • Bias testing doubles as attack detection — unexplained bias can be the first visible symptom of poisoning.
Module 02 · Threat map with controls — ready-made model · OWASP AI Exchange

Ready-Made Models — Hosting Choices, Shared Responsibility, and Shadow AI

~15 minCovers guide pp. 41–44

Most organizations don't train models — they buy or download them. That single architectural fact decides which threats are yours to defend and which belong to a supplier. This lesson teaches the guide's ready-made-model threat maps for self-hosted and externally hosted deployments, the four questions you must put to any model provider, and the organizational disease called shadow AI.

Why ready-made?

If possible, and depending on price, organizations can prefer to use a ready-made model instead of training or fine-tuning themselves. Examples: an open-source model to detect people in a camera image, or a general-purpose LLM such as Google Gemini, OpenAI ChatGPT, Anthropic Claude, Alibaba QWen, Deepseek, Mistral, Grok or Falkon. Training such models yourself can cost millions of dollars, requires deep expertise and vast amounts of data. The provider (e.g., OpenAI) has done the training and fine-tuning — and is therefore responsible for part of security. That makes proper supply chain management regarding the model provider (#SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE) a hard requirement, not a nicety.

Three deployment options

OptionDescriptionSecurity character
1. Closed-source model, hosted by the providerModel runs in the vendor's environment; you call an APIFor the largest models typically the only available option; your data crosses the trust boundary
2. Self-hosted open-source model (open weights)Deployed on-premise (most secure) or in your virtual private cloud (secure if the cloud provider is trusted)More security, and may be the best option cost-wise — but does not support the largest models
3. Open-source model (open weights) at a paid hosting serviceThird party hosts open weights for youConvenient; hosting becomes part of your supply chain

The self-hosted threat map

The guide's map "AI Security Threats and controls - GenAI as-is" (p. 42) splits the world with a dashed trust line: Model supplier above, Your runtime below. The supplier applies its controls during model creation — GenAI model alignment; specialized controls against poisoning, evasion and data disclosure; monitor/rate limit/access control; specialized input filtering and detection; AI supply chain management; development environment security; AI governance; and data minimization. Below the line, everything at runtime is yours — including direct runtime model poisoning, because the model artifact now lives in your infrastructure.

The map's legend assigns each threat an impact code: (T) Train data confidentiality, (B) Model behaviour, (P) Intellectual property, (E) Exhaustion, (I) Input confidentiality, (A) Augment confidentiality. Green arrows are threats, yellow boxes conventional controls, purple boxes AI-specific controls.

flowchart TD
  subgraph SUP["Model supplier - above the dashed trust line"]
    SC1["Suppliers GenAI model alignment plus specialized controls against poisoning, evasion and data disclosure"]
    SC2["Suppliers monitor, rate limit, access control plus specialized input filtering and detection"]
    SC3["Suppliers AI supply chain management, development environment security, AI governance, minimize data T,P"]
    MODEL["Suppliers AI Model - artifact handed to you"]
  end
  subgraph RT["Your runtime - below the dashed trust line"]
    YG["Your model supply chain management and your AI governance"]
    AUG["Augment data"]
    IN["Input"]
    APP["Application and infrastructure"]
    OUT["Output"]
    C1["Additional monitor, rate limit, access control"]
    C2["Additional specialized input filtering and detection"]
    C3["Control behaviour impact e.g. oversight, least privilege B"]
    C4["Runtime security controls plus minimize data T,I,A"]
    T1["Threats: evasion B, prompt injection B, disclosure in output T,A, AI resource exhaustion E"]
    T2["Threat: augmentation leak or manipulate"]
    T3["Threat: direct runtime model poisoning B"]
    T4["Threat: input data leak I"]
    T5["Threat: output contains conventional injection"]
    T6["Runtime conventional security threats e.g. SQL injection, password guessing"]
  end
  MODEL --> APP
  T3 -.-> MODEL
  C3 -.-> T3
  AUG --> APP
  T2 -.-> AUG
  IN --> APP
  APP --> OUT
  T1 -.-> IN
  C1 -.-> IN
  C2 -.-> IN
  T4 -.-> IN
  T5 -.-> OUT
  T6 -.-> APP
  C4 -.-> APP

Figure — ready-made model, self-hosted (guide p. 42). Original: "AI threat model by Software Improvement Group, donated to AI Exchange, free of copyright and attribution. Owaspai.org".

The externally hosted threat map

When the model is hosted externally, the map changes in three telling ways (guide p. 43): the top zone becomes "Model supplier & hosting provider" and keeps the model cylinder above the trust line, connected by a bidirectional arrow to your application; the provider zone gains "Provider's runtime security controls" and "Provider's monitor, rate limit, access control" (runtime model protection is now their job); and direct runtime model poisoning disappears from your runtime — you don't hold the model. Your purple control becomes "Your model and hosting supply chain management": hosting itself is now part of your supply chain. Everything else on your side is identical — augmentation threats, input threats (evasion, prompt injection, disclosure in output, resource exhaustion), input data leak, output injection, behaviour-impact controls, runtime security controls and data minimization (T,A).

flowchart TD
  subgraph PROV["Model supplier and hosting provider - above the dashed trust line"]
    P1["Providers runtime security controls plus providers monitor, rate limit, access control"]
    P2["Suppliers GenAI model alignment plus specialized controls against poisoning, evasion and data disclosure plus specialized input filtering"]
    P3["Suppliers AI supply chain management, development environment security, AI governance, minimize data T,P"]
    MODEL["Suppliers AI Model - runs at the provider"]
  end
  subgraph RT["Your runtime - below the dashed trust line"]
    YG["Your model AND hosting supply chain management plus your AI governance"]
    AUG["Augment data"]
    IN["Input"]
    APP["Application and infrastructure"]
    OUT["Output"]
    C1["Additional monitor, rate limit, access control"]
    C2["Additional specialized input filtering and detection"]
    C3["Control behaviour impact e.g. oversight, least privilege B"]
    C4["Runtime security controls plus minimize data T,A"]
    T1["Threats: evasion B, prompt injection B, disclosure in output T,A, AI resource exhaustion E"]
    T2["Threat: augmentation leak or manipulate"]
    T4["Threat: input data leak I"]
    T5["Threat: output contains conventional injection"]
    T6["Runtime conventional security threats e.g. SQL injection, password guessing"]
  end
  MODEL <--> APP
  AUG --> APP
  T2 -.-> AUG
  IN --> APP
  APP --> OUT
  T1 -.-> IN
  C1 -.-> IN
  C2 -.-> IN
  T4 -.-> IN
  T5 -.-> OUT
  T6 -.-> APP
  C3 -.-> APP
  C4 -.-> APP

Figure — ready-made model, externally hosted (guide p. 43). Note the bidirectional model connection crossing the trust boundary: your input and output travel to the provider.

◆ Senior insight

Read the two maps as a responsibility transfer diagram. Self-hosting buys you control and gives you the runtime-poisoning threat in exchange; external hosting removes that threat but converts your data-confidentiality posture into a supplier-assurance problem. Neither is "more secure" in the abstract — the guide's fair-comparison point cuts both ways: "the vendor may still protect that environment better than you can protect your own."

External hosting: the four questions

If the model is hosted externally, security largely depends on how the supplier handles data, including the security configuration. The guide gives four questions to ask:

  1. Where does the model run? In the vendor's processes, or in your own virtual private cloud? Some vendors say you get a "private instance", but that may refer to the API, not the model. If the model runs on the vendor's cluster, your data leaves your environment in clear text. Vendors will minimize storage and transfer — but they may log and monitor.
  2. What are the data retention rules? Has a court required the vendor to retain logs for litigation? This happened to OpenAI in the US for a period of time.
  3. What is exactly logged and monitored? Read the small print. Is logging enabled, and what is logged? What is monitored — by operators or by algorithms? If algorithms: how is that infrastructure protected? Some vendors allow you to opt out of logging, but only with specific licenses.
  4. Is your input used for training? A common fear — but in the vast majority of cases input is not used. If vendors did this secretly, it would get out, because there are ways to tell.
▸ Worked example

The OpenAI litigation hold. A US court ordered OpenAI to retain user logs for litigation for a period of time — overriding whatever deletion policy customers had relied on. The lesson: retention promises in a contract are subordinate to the legal system of the provider's jurisdiction. When you model the "input data leak" threat for provider-hosted AI, the provider's government is part of your threat model (the guide repeats this at p. 60: a government "may be forced to store and keep input and output to serve for subpoenas").

Three further truths the guide insists on:

  • If you can't accept the risk for certain data, hosting your own (smaller) model is the safest option. Typically it won't be as good — and there's the catch-22.
  • A provider-hosted model needs your input data in clear text: the model must read the data to process it. Your sensitive data will exist unencrypted outside your infrastructure. This is not unique to LLM providers — the same holds for other multi-tenant SaaS, such as commercial hosted Office suites. Providers minimize storage, limit retention and reduce movement, but the fact remains: your data leaves in readable form.
  • Weigh the risk fairly: the vendor may protect that environment better than you can protect your own.
A vendor's sales deck promises a "private instance". What exactly must you verify before treating your data as staying private?
Whether "private" refers to the model runtime or merely to the API endpoint. If the model executes on the vendor-operated cluster, your data leaves your environment in clear text regardless of how private the API is. You then need answers on retention rules, logging/monitoring specifics (and protection of the monitoring infrastructure), and training-use policy — the guide's four questions.
Why does the self-hosted map show "direct runtime model poisoning" in your zone while the external-hosted map does not?
The threat targets the deployed model artifact. Self-hosting places that artifact in your infrastructure, so protecting it (runtime model integrity) is your job. With external hosting the artifact stays with the provider — the threat still exists but sits above the trust line, and your lever becomes supply chain management: selecting a provider who protects their running application, as you'd expect of any supplier.

Shadow AI

A typical organizational challenge: employees can access many general-purpose GenAI models (e.g., ChatGPT), often for free — including models that do not satisfy the organization's security and privacy requirements. Lacking a better alternative, employees are tempted to use them anyway. This is shadow AI. The guide's best solution is constructive, not punitive: provide a good alternative — an AI model deployed and configured in a secure and privacy-preserving way, of sufficient quality, complying with the organization's values and policies. In addition, make the risks of shadow AI very clear to users.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Offer a sanctioned, secure, sufficiently good GenAI service before you police the unsanctioned ones.
  • Ask the four provider questions for every external model — and get answers in the contract, not the sales deck.
  • Match hosting to data sensitivity: unacceptable-risk data means self-hosting a (smaller) model, and accepting the quality catch-22 consciously.
  • Put the hosting provider in your supply chain register ("your model and hosting supply chain management").
  • Communicate shadow-AI risks explicitly — awareness is half the control.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 41–44, permalink owaspai.org/go/readymademodel/. Both maps: "AI threat model by Software Improvement Group, donated to AI Exchange, free of copyright and attribution." Related canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM03 (Supply Chain); MITRE ATLAS AML.T0010 (ML Supply Chain Compromise); NIST AI RMF MAP function (context and supplier mapping).

Key Points

  • Ready-made models shift training-time security to the supplier; supply chain management is how you inherit assurance.
  • Three deployment options: provider-hosted closed source (only option for the largest models), self-hosted open weights (most secure on-premise; VPC secure if the cloud provider is trusted), and hosted open weights (convenient).
  • Self-hosting adds direct runtime model poisoning to your threat list; external hosting removes it but sends your I/O across the trust boundary.
  • Provider-hosted models need your input in clear text — same as any multi-tenant SaaS; compare vendor protection to your own honestly.
  • Four provider questions: where does the model run, what are retention rules (OpenAI court-ordered log retention is precedent), what exactly is logged/monitored, is input used for training.
  • Impact codes on the maps: (T) train data confidentiality, (B) model behaviour, (P) IP, (E) exhaustion, (I) input confidentiality, (A) augment confidentiality.
  • Shadow AI is solved by offering a secure, high-quality sanctioned alternative plus clear risk communication.
Module 02 · Periodic table & navigator · OWASP AI Exchange

The Periodic Table and the Navigator — Your Map from Threats to Controls

~15 minCovers guide pp. 44–50

Risk analysis needs a lookup structure: given a threat, which controls apply, and where do they live? The AI Exchange provides two. The periodic table organizes every threat by asset, impact and attack surface with its control set; the navigator shows how the four deep-dive sections structure those same controls by lifecycle and by who implements them. Learn to read both and step 7 of the risk analysis ("select controls") becomes mechanical.

The periodic table of AI security

Created by the OWASP AI Exchange, the table shows the various threats to AI and the controls you can use against them — all organized by asset, impact and attack surface, with deep links to comprehensive coverage on the AI Exchange website. One rule to remember before reading any row: general governance controls apply to all threats.

Its two organizing dimensions:

  • Asset & Impact (row groups): Model behaviour Integrity · Training data Confidentiality · Model confidentiality · Model behaviour Availability · Model input data Confidentiality · Any asset, CIA.
  • Attack surface with lifecycle: Runtime - Model use (provide input/read output) · Runtime - Break into deployed model · Development - Engineering environment · Development - Supply chain · Model use · Runtime - All IT.
Asset & ImpactAttack surface with lifecycleThreat/Risk categoryControls
Model behaviour IntegrityRuntime - Model use (provide input/read output)Direct prompt injectionLimit unwanted behavior, Monitor, rate limit, model access control plus: Prompt injection I/O handling, Model alignment
Model behaviour IntegrityRuntime - Model use (provide input/read output)Indirect prompt injectionLimit unwanted behavior, Prompt injection I/O handling, Input segregation
Model behaviour IntegrityRuntime - Model use (provide input/read output)Evasion (e.g., adversarial examples)Limit unwanted behavior, Monitor, rate limit, model access control plus: Anomalous input handling, Evasion input handling, Unwanted input series handling, evasion robust model, train adversarial, input distortion, adversarial robust distillation
Model behaviour IntegrityRuntime - Break into deployed modelDirect runtime model poisoning (reprogramming)Limit unwanted behavior, Runtime model integrity, runtime model input/output integrity
Model behaviour IntegrityDevelopment - Engineering environmentDirect development-time model poisoningLimit unwanted behavior, Development environment security, data segregation, federated learning, supply chain management plus: model ensemble
Model behaviour IntegrityDevelopment - Engineering environmentData poisoning of train/finetune dataLimit unwanted behavior, Development environment security, data segregation, federated learning, supply chain management plus: model ensemble plus: More training data, data quality control, train data distortion, poison robust model, train adversarial
Model behaviour IntegrityDevelopment - Supply chainSupply-chain model poisoningLimit unwanted behavior, Supplier: Development environment security, data segregation, federated learning. Producer: supply chain management plus: model ensemble
Training data ConfidentialityRuntime - Model useDisclosure in outputSensitive data limitation (data minimize, short retain, obfuscate training data) plus: Monitor, rate limit, model access control plus: Sensitive output handling
Training data ConfidentialityRuntime - Model useModel inversion / Membership inferenceSensitive data limitation (data minimize, short retain, obfuscate training data) plus: Monitor, rate limit, model access control plus: Unwanted input series handling, >Obscure confidence (sic), Small model
Training data ConfidentialityDevelopment - Engineering environmentDirect training data leakSensitive data limitation (data minimize, short retain, obfuscate training data) plus: Development environment security, data segregation, federated learning
Model confidentialityRuntime - Model useModel exfiltration (input-output harvesting)Monitor, rate limit, model access control plus: Unwanted input series handling
Model confidentialityRuntime - Break into deployed modelDirect runtime model leakRuntime model confidentiality, Model obfuscation
Model confidentialityDevelopment - Engineering environmentDirect development-time model leakDevelopment environment security, data segregation, federated learning
Model behaviour AvailabilityModel useAI resource exhaustion (model resource depletion)Monitor, rate limit, model access control plus: Dos input validation, limit resources
Model input data ConfidentialityRuntime - All ITInput data leakModel input confidentiality
Any asset, CIARuntime-All ITOutput contains conventional injectionEncode model output
Any asset, CIARuntime - All ITGeneric runtime security threatsConventional runtime security controls
Any asset, CIARuntime - All IT (sic — as printed, though the threat is development-environment/supply-chain)Generic development-environment and supply chain threatsConventional development security and supply chain management controls

The periodic table of AI security (guide pp. 45–49), reproduced verbatim including the original's quirks (">Obscure confidence" and the "Runtime - All IT" label on the final development-environment row).

⚠ Common misconception

"The table is the complete control list per threat." It is the complete AI-specific and named list — but general governance controls apply to every row, and the guide's step 7 explicitly tells you to review controls "listed directly under the threat (or its parent category) and the general controls, which apply universally". Skipping governance because the row doesn't name it is the most common reading error.

Which asset-and-impact group would a stolen system prompt fall under, and why is that a trick question?
Sensitive system prompts are augmentation data, and their theft is a "direct augmentation data leak" — which surfaces in the one-pager (Lesson 5) under unauthorized access to augmentation data rather than as a periodic-table row group of its own. The trick: the periodic table's "Disclosure in output" and the threat-map "(A) Augment confidentiality" code cover the same asset. The mapping structures overlap — always cross-check the one-pager when classifying.

The navigator: how the deep dives are structured

The details of threats and controls are covered in four deep-dive sections of the Exchange, each grouping threats and discussing the corresponding controls:

  1. General controls (apply to all threats)
  2. Input threats and controls
  3. Development-time threats and controls
  4. Runtime conventional security threats and controls

The navigator diagram (guide p. 50) "outlines the structure of the deep-dive sections, illustrating the relationships between threats, controls, associated risks, and the types of controls applied." Its legend color-codes each control by implementer type: standard information security control (with attention points), runtime AI engineer control, development-time AI engineer control, other control — plus the impact on Confidentiality, Integrity or Availability. On the website, clicking the image yields a PDF with clickable links. Bottom note: "Threat model based on Software Improvement Group AI framework."

AI security threats and controls navigator — four bands (guide p. 50)
1 · General controls against all threats
GovernanceAIPROGRAM · SECPROGRAM · SECDEV PROGRAM · DEVPROGRAM · CHECK COMPLIANCE · SEC EDUCATE
Sensitive data limitationDATA MINIMIZE · ALLOWED DATA · SHORT RETAIN · OBFUSCATE TRAINDATA · DISCRETE
Limit unwanted behaviourOVERSIGHT · LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE · MODEL ALIGNMENT · AI TRANSPARENCY · CONTINUOUS VALIDATION · EXPLAINABILITY · UNWANTED BIAS TESTING
2 · Controls against input threats
Always for input threatsMONITOR USE · RATE LIMIT · MODEL ACCESS CONTROL · ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING · UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING · OBSCURE CONFIDENCE
Evasion+ EVASION INPUT HANDLING · EVASION ROBUST MODEL · TRAIN ADVERSARIAL · INPUT DISTORTION · ADVERSARIAL ROBUST DISTILLATION
Direct / indirect prompt injection+ PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING (indirect also: INPUT SEGREGATION)
Disclosure in output+ SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING
Model inversion / membership inference+ SMALL MODEL
Model theft through use+ MODEL WATERMARKING
AI resource exhaustion+ DOS INPUT VALIDATION · LIMIT RESOURCES
3 · Controls against development-time threats
Base controlsDEV SECURITY · SEGREGATE DATA · CONF COMPUTE · FEDERATED LEARNING · SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE
Dev-time model poisoning+ MODEL ENSEMBLE
Data poisoning+ MORE TRAIN DATA · DATA QUALITY CONTROL · TRAIN DATA DISTORTION · POISON ROBUST MODEL
Train/test data, dev-time model, source code/config leakssee base controls
4 · Runtime conventional security controls
All CIA riskstechnical application controls · operational security
Runtime model poisoningRUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITY · RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY
Runtime model leakRUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY · MODEL OBFUSCATION
Output contains injectionENCODE MODEL OUTPUT
Input data leakMODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITY
Augmentation dataAUGMENTATION DATA CONFIDENTIALITY · AUGMENTATION DATA INTEGRITY

The navigator (guide p. 50) — a single-page mental map: four control layers, each mapped to risk categories (integrity of model behaviour, confidentiality of data, confidentiality of IP, availability, injection, input/augmentation confidentiality), color-coded by implementer.

The navigator names five controls we haven't carded yet — complete your catalog:

#DEVPROGRAMSoftware development program including AI engineeringgovernance · general controls
IntentBring AI engineering under general software-engineering best practice (versioning, review, quality gates) — the quality counterpart of #SECDEV PROGRAM.
ImplementListed in the navigator's governance group (band 1); apply your development lifecycle discipline to data pipelines and model builds.
StandardsNavigator band 1, owaspai.org/go/navigator/.
#CHECK COMPLIANCECheck laws and regulationsgovernance · general controls
IntentEnsure the AI system meets applicable regulation — compliance is one of the guide's nine risk domains, and regulatory penalties are an explicit indirect impact in step 2.
ImplementMap applicable regimes (e.g., the EU AI Act's risk-based obligations) to the system and verify obligations are met.
StandardsNavigator band 1. EU AI Act; ISO 23894 for AI risk management alignment.
#SEC EDUCATESecurity education for AI teamsgovernance · general controls
IntentData scientists own resilience and I/O-handling controls (Lesson 2) — they can only build them if they know the threats. Education closes that gap; it is also the operative control against shadow-AI risk ("the risks of shadow AI need to be made very clear to users").
ImplementThreat-aware training for engineers and users of AI systems.
StandardsNavigator band 1 (governance).
#MODEL WATERMARKINGWatermark the modelruntime · model confidentiality
IntentDeterrence and attribution for model theft through use: a watermark embedded in model behaviour lets you prove a stolen or surrogate model derives from yours.
ImplementListed in the navigator under confidentiality of intellectual property — model theft through use (legibility of that entry is limited in the printed poster, as the guide's rendering note concedes).
LimitsDetects and proves theft after the fact; it does not prevent extraction — pair with rate limiting and series detection.
StandardsNavigator band 2. ATLAS AML.T0024.002 (Extract ML Model).
#INPUT SEGREGATIONSegregate untrusted data from instructionsruntime · input threats
IntentThe structural defence against indirect prompt injection: keep untrusted retrieved content (a resume, a web page, agent-fetched data) clearly separated from the instruction channel so it is processed as data, not commands.
ImplementThe periodic table pairs it with prompt injection I/O handling for the indirect variant specifically — segregation is what distinguishes the indirect defence-set from the direct one.
LimitsCurrent LLMs cannot perfectly honour channel boundaries; segregation reduces, not eliminates — hence the layered set.
StandardsNavigator band 2. Canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM01; ATLAS AML.T0051.
You've identified "data poisoning of train/finetune data" as a treated risk. Using the periodic table, list the control stack — and say which part a ready-made-model user cannot implement.
Limit unwanted behavior + development environment security, data segregation, federated learning, supply chain management + model ensemble + more training data, data quality control, train data distortion, poison robust model, train adversarial — plus general governance controls. A ready-made-model user cannot implement the development-environment set (that's the supplier's environment) and can only apply #POISON ROBUST MODEL with access to model parameters; their levers are supply chain management and the behaviour-limiting runtime controls.
What do the navigator's colors encode, and why does that matter for step 5 of the risk analysis (arrange responsibility)?
Implementer type: standard information security control, runtime AI engineer control, development-time AI engineer control, or other. Responsibility arrangement needs exactly this: a control colored "development-time AI engineer" belongs to whoever trains the model (possibly a supplier), while "standard infosec" controls usually stay with your security organization. The color is a responsibility hint.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 44–50. Permalinks: owaspai.org/go/periodictable/, owaspai.org/go/navigator/. The navigator mirrors the document's own control catalog; "Threat model based on Software Improvement Group AI framework." OpenCRE links standard-infosec entries to conventional control catalogs; ISO/IEC 27090 is the emerging ISO home for the AI-specific ones.

Key Points

  • The periodic table organizes all AI threats by asset & impact and attack surface with lifecycle, each with its named control set — and general governance controls apply to every row.
  • Six asset/impact groups: model behaviour integrity, training data confidentiality, model confidentiality, model behaviour availability, model input data confidentiality, and any-asset CIA.
  • The recurring trio "Monitor, rate limit, model access control" guards nearly every threat-through-use row.
  • Four deep-dive sections structure the Exchange: general controls; input threats; development-time threats; runtime conventional security.
  • The navigator color-codes controls by implementer — standard infosec vs. runtime AI engineer vs. development-time AI engineer vs. other — which prefigures responsibility arrangement.
  • #INPUT SEGREGATION is what separates the indirect-prompt-injection defence set from the direct one; #MODEL WATERMARKING addresses model theft through use.
  • Use the table in step 7: look up your treated threats, read off the row's controls plus the general ones.
Module 02 · Risk analysis · OWASP AI Exchange

The Risk-Analysis Framework and the Threat-Model One-Pager

~18 minCovers guide pp. 50–54

The Exchange describes dozens of threats and controls — but "the relevance and severity of each threat and the appropriate controls depend on your specific use case and how AI is deployed within your environment." This lesson gives you the machinery to decide: the risk-management framing (domains, standards, four steps), the ten-step Exchange process, and the threat-modelling entry point — including the complete one-pager table that turns the whole catalog into a checklist.

Risk management: the frame

Organizations classify risks into key areas: Strategic, Operational, Financial, Compliance, Reputation, Technology, Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG). The core definition to internalize: a threat becomes a risk when it exploits one or more vulnerabilities. AI threats can land across multiple domains at once — the guide's example: adversarial attacks can disrupt operations, distort financial models, and create compliance issues. (See the AI security matrix for the full threat/risk/impact overview.)

Two management systems drive the work:

  • AI governance (see #AIPROGRAM, below) drives general risk management for AI systems — covering both risks by relevant AI systems and risks to those systems.
  • The security management system (#SECPROGRAM) drives security risk assessment — it is "tasked to include AI assets, AI threats, and AI systems, provided that these have been added to the corresponding repositories." ISO/IEC 27005 is the international standard for security risk management.

Organizations often adopt a Risk Management framework, commonly based on ISO 31000 or similar standards such as ISO 23894 (AI risk management). These frameworks manage risk through four key steps, plus a rule:

  1. Identifying Risks — recognizing potential risks that could impact the organization or others.
  2. Evaluating Risks — estimating the likelihood and the severity of impact should the risk materialize; likelihood and severity combined represent the level of the risk, typically presented as a heatmap of likelihood versus severity.
  3. Risk Treatment — choosing a strategy: Mitigation, Transfer, Avoidance, or Acceptance.
  4. Risk Communication and Monitoring — regularly sharing risk information with stakeholders and ensuring effective treatments are applied; requires a Risk Register (a comprehensive list of risks and their attributes: severity, treatment plan, ownership, status, etc.).
  5. Repeat the above process regularly and when changes warrant it.
#AIPROGRAMAI governance programgovernance · general controls
IntentInstitutionalize AI risk management: know which AI systems exist, what risks they create (risks BY the AI) and face (risks TO the AI), and who owns them.
ImplementGeneral risk management for AI systems "is typically driven by AI governance — see #AIPROGRAM" (guide p. 51); it feeds AI assets into #SECPROGRAM's repositories so security risk assessment can see them.
LimitsGovernance without inventory is theatre — the program is only as good as its registration coverage (shadow AI is exactly what escapes it).
StandardsISO 31000 / ISO 23894 framework basis; NIST AI RMF GOVERN; EU AI Act obligations for providers/deployers.

The Exchange's ten-step process

The guide operationalizes the four framework steps into ten numbered subsections. Learn the sequence — the rest of this module walks through it:

The ten steps of AI security risk analysis (guide pp. 52–64)
1 · Identify risksdecision tree / threat model per impact type
↓
2 · Evaluate riskslikelihood × impact → heatmap
↓
3 · Risk treatmentmitigate / transfer / avoid / accept
↓
4 · Communicate & monitorrisk register, stakeholders
↓
5 · Arrange responsibilityper threat; responsibility matrix
↓
6 · Verify external responsibilitiesassurance on third parties
↓
7 · Select controlsthreat controls + general controls; cost vs. risk
↓
8 · Residual risk acceptanceaccept what remains, explicitly
↓
9 · Manage selected controls#SECPROGRAM: monitor, document, report, respond
↓
10 · Continuous risk assessmentrepeat on change and new threats

Step 1 — Identifying risks: the decision tree to threat model

The AI Exchange's threat catalog identifies the risks that apply to a specific AI system depending on architecture, context, domain and use case. Scope note: the document focuses on AI-specific risks only — risks to the AI-specific assets. What you are doing here is a threat modelling process: the bridge between a list of threats and a set of concrete, prioritized risks. Threats are a catalogue of "attacks that could happen"; threat modelling answers three key questions:

  1. Which threats theoretically apply to this system?
  2. How could they realistically happen?
  3. What would the impact be?

The step after that — looking in detail at likelihood and impact — is subsection 2 (Lesson 7).

▸ Worked example

THREAT ADVISOR. The Exchange offers an AI assistant that uses all this material to take you through the threat-model process: THREAT ADVISOR (requires a Google account). You briefly describe your system, it interviews you — and "your data will remain in your own Google workspace." Notice the tool practises what the guide preaches: the data-residency assurance is answered up front, exactly question 1 of Lesson 3's provider checklist.

How to use the one-pager

The AI Exchange threat modelling one-pager summarizes the step-by-step decision-tree approach:

  1. Walk by each threat.
  2. Based on the column 'When', determine whether that threat applies in theory.
  3. If it applies in theory, use the column 'Impact' to decide whether the risk needs to be treated, depending on the level of harm for the use case.

"The result: you start big, but you end up with a relatively small list of risks to focus on."

▸ Worked example

Model inversion that doesn't matter. "You don't have to protect against model inversion attacks that try to steal your training data, if that data isn't sensitive. It sounds obvious, but I've seen many cases of protections in place for threats that effectively don't matter." — the guide's author, first person. Wasted controls are not free: they cost budget, latency and attention that the real risks needed.

▸ Worked example

Indirect prompt injection that stays theoretical. "If your agentic system uses an LLM, then it is in theory susceptible to indirect prompt injection: malicious instructions in untrusted data that manipulate agent behaviour. But if your only concern is that sensitive company data leaks, and there is no way for the system to send data to an attacker (e.g., email), then this threat remains theoretical. The risk does not have to be treated." The 'When' column fired; the 'Impact' column cleared it.

The one-pager, in full

Reading note: in the original (guide p. 54, header permalink owaspai.org/go/threatmodel/), cells are merged into groups — "AI" spans every row down to "Output contains conventional injection", "Conventional" spans the last two, and shared When/Impact cells span their whole cluster. The clusters are: (1) unwanted model behaviour, (2) unauthorized access to augmentation data, (3) unauthorized access to training data, (4) model stolen, (5) availability/fund depletion, (6) input leak, (7) conventional injection, (8) generic conventional C/I/A.

AI-specific?LifecycleAttack surfaceThreatWhenImpact
AIRuntimeInput attackDirect prompt injectionIf the model is GenAI (e.g., a Large Language Model) and not classic machine learning or a heuristic modelUnwanted model behaviour. The impact depends very much on if and how it can actually cause harm: — What (cascading) actions can the model trigger, leading to harm. Execute code? Physical actions? — Can the model trigger actions that retrieve sensitive data, and actions that send it out? — Could output be harmful content or misinformation leading to harm? — Does untrusted data go in a prompt of a model with elevated privileges?
AIRuntimeInput attackIndirect prompt injectionIf the model is GenAI and the system inserts untrusted data into the prompt
AIRuntimeInput attackEvasionIf the model performs a classification and an adversary can influence input.
AIRuntimeBreak into deployed modelDirect runtime model poisoningThis conventional attack is always possible in theory. Protection depends on where the model is deployed
AIRuntimeBreak into augmentation dataAugmentation data manipulationIf the system inserts data in the model input (e.g. RAG, system prompts, or data from memory)
AIDevelopmentBreak into train/finetune dataDirect development-time model poisoning
AIDevelopmentBreak into train/finetune dataData poisoningIf you train your own model
AIDevelopmentCompromise supply chainSupply-chain model poisoningIf the model is from a supplier
AIDevelopmentCompromise supply chainSupply chain data poisoningIf the data is from a supplier
AIRuntimeInput attackDisclosure in outputIf the system inserts data in the model input (e.g. RAG, system prompts, or data from memory)Unauthorized access to augmentation data.
AIRuntimeBreak into deployed systemDirect augmentation data leak
AIRuntimeInput attackDisclosure in outputIf you train your own model and training data is sensitive (for membership inference: the fact of being in the set is sensitive).Unauthorized access to training data.
AIRuntimeInput attackModel inversion / Membership inference
AIDevelopmentBreak into training dataDirect training data leak
AIRuntimeInput attackModel exfiltrationIf you train your own model and it is intellectual property (e.g. not public) or the model is susceptible to an input attack.The model is stolen. It may be used by a competitor, or used by an attacker to prepare or perform an input attack on.
AIRuntimeBreak into deployed modelDirect runtime model leak
AIDevelopmentBreak into model dataDirect development-time model leak
AIRuntimeInput attackAI resource exhaustionAlways a possibility, depending on if the model or system are sensitive to it.Model unavailability or fund depletion (if the model runs as a paid service).
AIRuntimeAll ITInput data leakIf the input data is sensitive and there is a threat of a conventional attack on it, in transit or at rest.Unauthorized access to input. Example: the model input may be stored at a cloud AI provider log, and leak from there.
AIRuntimeAll ITOutput contains conventional injectionIf the output is text and is interpreted downstream (web browser, database).C, I, A of any asset. The injection may succeed in a cross site scripting, sql injection etc.
ConventionalRuntimeAll ITGeneric runtime security threatsRequire their own conventional threat modeling on the runtime architecture.C, I, A of any asset.
ConventionalDevelopmentAll ITConventional development-environment and supply-chain threatsRequire their own conventional threat modeling on the development environment.C, I, A of any asset.

The OWASP AI Exchange threat model one-pager (guide p. 54). The companion decision-tree poster carries three permalinks: AI — owaspai.org/go/threatadvisor/, Guide — owaspai.org/go/threatmodel/, Tree — owaspai.org/go/threattree/.

◆ Senior insight

The one-pager's genius is the 'When' column as a cheap negative filter. "If you train your own model" instantly clears four training-data threats for every ready-made-model user; "if the model performs a classification" clears evasion for most pure-chat systems. Senior threat modellers spend their scarce analysis time only on rows that survive 'When' — and then argue about 'Impact', which is where context lives and where the two worked examples show risks legitimately dying.

Name the three questions threat modelling answers, and map each to a one-pager column.
(1) Which threats theoretically apply — the 'When' column; (2) how could they realistically happen — the 'Lifecycle' and 'Attack surface' columns plus your architecture; (3) what would the impact be — the 'Impact' column interpreted against your use case's level of harm.
Why does the guide bother stating "we focus on AI-specific risks only"?
Because the last two one-pager rows delegate: generic runtime threats and conventional development-environment/supply-chain threats "require their own conventional threat modeling". The AI risk analysis complements, not replaces, your normal application threat model — AI systems are software systems and still need conventional application and operational security.
Your team wants to deploy differential-privacy noise on a model whose training data is public web text. What does the one-pager method say?
Walk to model inversion / membership inference: 'When' requires that you train your own model AND the training data is sensitive. Public data fails the sensitivity condition, so the threat does not need treatment — this is exactly the guide's first worked example of protections "for threats that effectively don't matter".
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 50–54. Permalinks: owaspai.org/go/riskanalysis/, owaspai.org/go/threatmodel/. Standards: ISO/IEC 27005 (security risk management), ISO 31000 (risk management), ISO 23894 (AI risk management). NIST AI RMF mirrors the cycle as MAP (identify), MEASURE (evaluate), MANAGE (treat), GOVERN (communicate/own).

Key Points

  • Nine risk domains: Strategic, Operational, Financial, Compliance, Reputation, Technology, Environmental, Social, Governance (ESG); a threat becomes a risk when it exploits vulnerabilities.
  • #AIPROGRAM drives AI governance (risks by and to AI); #SECPROGRAM drives security risk assessment; ISO/IEC 27005 is the security-risk standard, ISO 31000/23894 the framework basis.
  • Four framework steps — identify, evaluate (likelihood × severity heatmap), treat (mitigate/transfer/avoid/accept), communicate & monitor (risk register) — repeated regularly and on change.
  • The Exchange refines this into ten steps ending in residual-risk acceptance, control management and continuous assessment.
  • Threat modelling = the bridge from threat catalogue to prioritized risks; three questions: applies in theory? realistic path? impact?
  • One-pager method: walk every threat, filter with 'When', judge with 'Impact' — start big, end with a short list.
  • Risks can legitimately die at the impact stage: no sensitive training data kills inversion; no outbound channel keeps indirect prompt injection theoretical.
  • THREAT ADVISOR automates the interview; data stays in your Google workspace.
Module 02 · Identifying risks — decision tree · OWASP AI Exchange

Identifying Risks I — Unwanted Model Behaviour and the Lethal Trifecta

~17 minCovers guide pp. 54–58

The largest impact cluster on the one-pager is unwanted model behaviour — nine threats share it. The guide walks it as a question-driven decision tree: is it GenAI? who trains it? is input augmented? who runs it? does it classify? Then the analysis turns to what makes misbehaviour actually dangerous: the actions a model can trigger, culminating in the "lethal trifecta". This lesson is the heart of AI threat modelling.

Scope statement first (guide p. 54): "Regarding model behaviour, we focus on manipulation by attackers, as the scope of this document is security. Other sources of unwanted behavior are general inaccuracy (e.g., hallucinations) and/or unwanted bias regarding certain groups (discrimination)." Security owns the attack-induced slice of the accuracy problem — not the whole of it.

Question: Is the model GenAI (e.g., an LLM)?

THREAT
Direct prompt injectionAttacker provides prompt input to cause harmful output or trigger harmful functions. Lifecycle: runtime/input attack. Maps to: LLM01, ATLAS AML.T0051.

Consider direct prompt injection when: a) an attacker can provide input to the model (e.g., a prompt), and b) the model could theoretically create output that results in harm — offensive output, information leading to harm, or triggering harmful functions (Agentic AI). The guide's risk-assessment guidance is bracingly pragmatic: "the first question is: has the model supplier done enough according to your risk appetite. For this, you can check tests that the supplier or others have performed, and when not available: have these tests done based on what harm means in your use case." What you accept — what you find too much effort in combination with too harmful — depends on your context: "If a user wants the AI to say something offensive: do you regard it as a problem if that user succeeds in getting offended? Do you regard it as a problem if users can get a recipe to make poison — given that they can get this from many other AI's out there?"

THREAT
Indirect prompt injectionMalicious instructions in untrusted data inserted into a prompt manipulate (agent) behaviour. Lifecycle: runtime/input attack. Maps to: LLM01, ATLAS AML.T0051.

Consider indirect prompt injection when your system inserts untrusted data in a prompt — e.g., you retrieve somebody's resume and include it in a prompt, or an agent retrieves data that is untrusted (may have been manipulated or placed by an attacker). Key control pairing (periodic table): prompt injection I/O handling + #INPUT SEGREGATION.

Question: Who trains/finetunes the model?

THREAT
Supply-chain model poisoningObtaining or working with a model manipulated to behave in unintended ways. Lifecycle: development/supply chain. Maps to: LLM03/LLM04, ATLAS AML.T0010.

The supplier trains it: your mitigation is proper supply chain management — selecting a trustworthy supplier and verifying the authenticity of the model — to gain assurance that the provider prevents model poisoning during development, including data poisoning, and uses uncompromised data. If the remaining risk of data poisoning is unacceptable, post-training countermeasures (#POISON ROBUST MODEL) are an option if you have the expertise and access to model parameters (e.g., open-source weights). Sobering caveat: "providers are typically not very open about their security countermeasures, which means that it can be challenging to gain sufficient assurance. Regulations will hopefully help achieve more provider transparency."

THREAT
Data poisoning (of train/finetune data)Poisoned data in your development environment or obtained from a third party (supply chain data poisoning). Lifecycle: development. Maps to: LLM04, ATLAS AML.T0020.
THREAT
Direct development-time model poisoningAttackers alter your model directly during development. Lifecycle: development/engineering environment. Maps to: LLM04, ATLAS AML.T0010/T0020 family.

You train it: consider data poisoning in the development environment or via poisoned third-party data, and attackers altering your model directly (direct development-time model poisoning).

◆ Senior insight

Why not train or finetune yourself? The guide's economics: many third-party and open-source models can perform the task, perhaps after fine-tuning. External GenAI models win because they are general-purpose and training is difficult and expensive — often millions of dollars. Even fine-tuning is uncommon given compute cost and complexity. Running an obtained model on your own infrastructure can mean lower cost (open source) and keeping sensitive input internal; the countervailing reason to use an externally hosted model is quality. Every one of these business choices flips 'When' conditions in the one-pager — the architecture decision is the threat-model decision.

Question: Does your system augment model input?

That is: does it insert data into the input, as in RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation), or from memory (stored state for an agent), or via system prompts (standard instructions automatically added to the input)?

THREAT
Augmentation data manipulationCorrupting RAG data, memory or system prompts steers model behaviour. Lifecycle: runtime/break into augmentation data. Maps to: LLM08.

If yes: consider augmentation data manipulation, "as this data plays a role in determining the model behaviour."

THREAT
Direct augmentation data leakTheft of augmentation data from its store (e.g., vector database) — includes sensitive system prompts. Lifecycle: runtime/break into deployed system. Maps to: LLM07/LLM08.

Is the augmentation data stored in a database for the AI system (e.g., a vector database)? Then protect against direct augmentation data leak. "Note that this also counts for system prompts, if they are sensitive."

Question: Who runs the model?

THREAT
Direct runtime model poisoningAttackers change the deployed model. Always possible in theory; protection depends on where the model is deployed. Lifecycle: runtime/break into deployed model.

The supplier runs it: select a trustworthy supplier through supply chain management, so the deployed model cannot be manipulated — "just the way you would expect any supplier to protect their running application from manipulation." You run it: the runtime model poisoning threat is yours (see the self-hosted threat map in Lesson 3; controls: #RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITY, #RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY).

Question: Is the model used in a classification task?

THREAT
Evasion attackA user fools the model into a wrong decision using data — not instructions. Lifecycle: runtime/input attack. Maps to: ATLAS AML.T0015.

If the model (predictive or generative) is used in a classification task — spam detection, fraud detection — consider evasion. The guide flags this as the place where risk level matters most: "Here, the level of risk is an important aspect to evaluate — see below. The risk of an evasion attack may be acceptable."

flowchart TD
  Q1{"Model is GenAI, not classic ML or a heuristic model?"} -->|yes, attacker can provide input and output can cause harm| T1["Direct prompt injection"]
  Q1 -->|yes, system inserts untrusted data into the prompt| T2["Indirect prompt injection"]
  Q2{"Who trains or finetunes the model?"} -->|the supplier| T3["Supply-chain model poisoning"]
  T3 --> M1["Mitigate: supply chain management, then POISON ROBUST MODEL if weights accessible"]
  Q2 -->|you| T4["Data poisoning"]
  Q2 -->|you| T5["Direct development-time model poisoning"]
  Q3{"System augments input? RAG, memory, system prompts"} -->|yes| T6["Augmentation data manipulation"]
  Q3 -->|yes, stored e.g. in a vector database| T7["Direct augmentation data leak - includes sensitive system prompts"]
  Q4{"Who runs the model?"} -->|the supplier| M2["Select trustworthy supplier via supply chain management"]
  Q4 -->|you| T8["Direct runtime model poisoning"]
  Q5{"Classification task, adversary can influence input?"} -->|yes| T9["Evasion attack - risk may be acceptable"]

Figure — decision-tree panel 1: identify risks with the impact of unwanted model behaviour (guide pp. 54–57; poster legend: question = decision diamond, red box = threat, green box = guidance/control, grey box = context).

Analysing the risk of unwanted model behaviour

Can the model trigger actions? — API calls driven by a classification output, or an LLM agent that triggers actions or other agents? "This typically increases the impact of the risks mentioned above, depending on the action. The action can for example be adding an entry to a database, but also closing a watertight door in a ship. The threat is for example prompt injection or augmentation data manipulation, but the risk is that this leads to a specific impact (e.g., exfiltrating data, or an unsafe action in the physical world)." (The original prints "un unsafe".)

RISK PATTERN
The Lethal TrifectaManipulated behaviour + ability to send data + access to sensitive data → data exfiltration. Maps to: LLM01 + LLM06 (Excessive Agency). See Agentic threats.

If the model triggers actions: is there an action able to send data where an adversary can see it, and is sensitive data accessible by one of the agents or actions reachable by the manipulated agent? "This combination of 1) manipulated model behaviour, 2) ability for the model to send data, and 3) ability for the model to access sensitive data is called the 'Lethal trifecta'. The impact is: data exfiltration. For more details, see Agentic threats."

The lethal trifecta — remove any leg and the exfiltration risk collapses
1 · Manipulated behaviourprompt injection / augmentation manipulation
+
2 · Can send data outemail, HTTP, links, images
+
3 · Can reach sensitive datavia agent tools or actions
→
Data exfiltrationthe impact
THREAT
Injecting exfiltration in model outputThe model is manipulated to embed sensitive data in output code or links. Lifecycle: runtime. Maps to: LLM05 (Improper Output Handling).

A special form of sending data to an adversary: the model outputs sensitive data inside code that transmits it. "This can be javascript executed in a browser (XSS), but more simple: a link to an image on the server of an adversary, where the URL contains the sensitive data. Or in a similar fashion: the output containing a link for the user to click on, to that same server." No email tool needed — a markdown image tag is an outbound channel.

⚔ Attacker's view

The trifecta is a target-selection checklist. An attacker surveying your agent asks: can I get instructions in (a poisoned web page, a crafted resume, a manipulated RAG document)? Once behaviour is mine, what egress exists — an email tool is gold, but an image URL rendered by the user's browser is enough? And what can the agent read — vault secrets, CRM records, the vector store? If all three answer yes, exfiltration is engineering, not research. The defender's cheapest move is usually leg 2: strip or proxy every outbound channel (#LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE, #ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT) — which is exactly why the guide's worked example declared the risk untreatable-not-needed when no send-path existed.

Attacker motivation: turning theory into risk level

To assess the level of risk for unwanted model behaviour through manipulation, the guide says, consider what an attacker could gain: "Just a claim to fame? Could it be a disgruntled employee? Maybe a competitor? What could an attacker gain by a less conspicuous model behaviour attack, like an evasion attack or data poisoning with a trigger? Is there a scenario where an attacker benefits from fooling the model?"

▸ Worked example

Spam vs. skin disease. Evasion IS interesting and possible: adding certain words to a spam email so it is not recognized as spam. Evasion is NOT interesting when a patient gets a skin-disease diagnosis from a skin photo: the patient has no interest in a wrong decision — and typically no control ("well maybe by painting the skin"). Yet even here context can flip it: the patient might want a (faked) diagnosis to be eligible for compensation if the skin disease was supposedly caused by certain restaurant food. "This demonstrates that it all depends on the context whether a theoretical threat is a real threat or not."

The conclusion carries the whole method: "Depending on the probability and impact of the threats, and on the relevant policies, some threats may be accepted as risk. When not accepted, the level of risk is input to the strength of the controls. For example: if data poisoning can lead to substantial benefit for a group of attackers, then the training data needs to be given a high level of protection."

Your GenAI chatbot has no tools, no agent actions, and answers from a public FAQ. A red-teamer demonstrates a jailbreak producing an offensive reply. Walk the guide's direct-prompt-injection reasoning.
First ask whether the supplier has done enough for your risk appetite — check available tests or commission tests defined by what "harm" means here. Then judge context: if a user deliberately extracts offensive text, do you regard the user offending themselves as a problem? With no actions, no sensitive data and no elevated privileges, the one-pager's impact bullets mostly answer "no" — the risk may be accepted, or treated lightly (alignment verification, I/O handling).
Why does the guide treat "can the model trigger actions" as an impact multiplier rather than a separate threat?
Because the threat (prompt injection, augmentation manipulation) is unchanged — what changes is the consequence chain. A manipulated chat reply is words; a manipulated agent that closes a watertight door or writes to a database converts the same manipulation into physical or data impact. Threat stays; risk level moves.
Which single privilege change breaks the lethal trifecta in most RAG agents, and which controls implement it?
Removing the ability to send data out (leg 2) — no email/HTTP tools, and neutralizing covert channels like attacker-URL images or clickable links in rendered output. #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE scopes the tools; #ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT / output handling neutralizes the injected-exfiltration channel; #OVERSIGHT gates any remaining outbound action.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 54–58, permalink owaspai.org/go/threatmodel/ (tree: owaspai.org/go/threattree/). Canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM01 (Prompt Injection), LLM04 (Data and Model Poisoning), LLM06 (Excessive Agency), LLM08 (Vector and Embedding Weaknesses); MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051 (LLM Prompt Injection), AML.T0020 (Poison Training Data), AML.T0010 (ML Supply Chain Compromise), AML.T0015 (Evade ML Model).

Key Points

  • Security's scope in model behaviour is attacker manipulation — hallucinations and discrimination are adjacent accuracy/bias problems.
  • GenAI ⇒ direct prompt injection (judge supplier testing against your risk appetite; harm is contextual) and, with untrusted data in prompts, indirect prompt injection.
  • Supplier-trained ⇒ supply-chain model poisoning: mitigate via supply chain management, fall back to #POISON ROBUST MODEL only with expertise + weight access; self-trained ⇒ data poisoning + direct development-time model poisoning.
  • Augmented input (RAG, memory, system prompts) ⇒ augmentation data manipulation; stored augmentation data (vector DB, sensitive system prompts) ⇒ direct augmentation data leak.
  • Supplier-run ⇒ supply-chain assurance; self-run ⇒ direct runtime model poisoning is yours.
  • Classification + adversary-influenced input ⇒ evasion — a risk that may legitimately be accepted.
  • Actions multiply impact (database entry → watertight door); the lethal trifecta (manipulated behaviour + send capability + sensitive-data access) = data exfiltration.
  • Exfiltration can hide in output itself: XSS, image URLs carrying data, clickable links.
  • Attacker motivation calibrates risk level, and risk level sets control strength (e.g., high-value poisoning ⇒ high protection of training data).
Module 02 · Identifying & evaluating risks · OWASP AI Exchange

Identifying Risks II — Data Leaks, Model Theft, Further Risks, and Evaluating Likelihood & Impact

~16 minCovers guide pp. 58–61

Unwanted behaviour is only the first impact cluster. This lesson finishes risk identification — leaking training data, model theft, leaking input data, and the always-do further risks — then moves to step 2: estimating likelihood with named factors from OWASP, FAIR and ISO/IEC 27005, evaluating impact, and prioritizing on a heatmap.

Impact: leaking training data

Question: Do you train/finetune the model yourself — and is the training data sensitive? If yes, four threats apply:

THREAT
Disclosure in model outputSensitive training (or augmentation) data appears in output. Runtime/input attack. Maps to: LLM02, ATLAS AML.T0024 family.

Applies in case the output can contain the sensitive data. Controls (periodic table): sensitive data limitation + monitor/rate limit/access control + #SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING.

THREAT
Model inversionReconstructing training data from the model. Runtime/input attack. Maps to: ATLAS AML.T0024.001.
THREAT
Direct training data leakTraining data stolen from your engineering environment. Development lifecycle.
THREAT
Membership inferenceDeducing that someone/something was in the training set. Runtime/input attack. Maps to: ATLAS AML.T0024.000.

Membership inference matters only when the fact of being in the training set is itself sensitive. The guide's example: "when the training set consists of criminals and their history to predict criminal careers. Membership of that set gives away the person is a convicted or alleged criminal."

Question: Do you insert data into the input (e.g., RAG)? (The original prints "do you use insert data".) If yes: "apply the above to your augmentation data, as if it was part of the training set: as the repository data feeds into the model and can therefore be part of the output as well."

⚠ Common misconception

"We use a supplier's model, so training-data content isn't our problem." The guide's correction: if you don't train/finetune, the supplier is responsible, but not accountable per se, for unwanted content in the training data — poisoned data, confidential data, or copyrighted data. "It is important to check licenses, warranties and contracts for these matters, or accept the risk based on your circumstances." Accountability for what your system emits stays with you.

Impact: model theft

Question: Do you train/finetune the model yourself, and is it intellectual property or susceptible to an evasion attack? If yes, consider four theft paths — note the second condition: even a non-IP model is worth stealing if a white-box copy helps attackers prepare an input attack.

THREAT
Model exfiltration (input-output harvesting)Training a surrogate from your model's I/O — attack on the runtime deployed model. Maps to: ATLAS AML.T0024.002.
THREAT
Direct development-time model leakModel stolen from the engineering environment. Development lifecycle.
THREAT
Source code/configuration leakEngineering IP (code, config) stolen — model-theft-adjacent when the approach is the secret. Development lifecycle.
THREAT
Direct runtime model leakTheft of the deployed model artifact. Runtime/break into deployed model.

One-pager impact for the cluster: "The model is stolen. It may be used by a competitor, or used by an attacker to prepare or perform an input attack on."

Impact: leaking input data

THREAT
Input data leakSensitive input (including RAG-retrieved data) leaks in transit, at rest, or from supplier logs. Runtime/All IT. Maps to: LLM02 context; control #MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITY.

Question: Is your input data sensitive? Then protect against input data leak. The guide's guidance in full: "Especially if the model is run by a supplier, proper care needs to be taken to ensure that this data is minimized and transferred or stored securely. Review the security measures provided by the supplier, including any options to disable logging or monitoring on their end. Realise that most Cloud AI models have your input and output unencrypted in their infrastructure (just like documents in Google Suite and Microsoft 365). If you use the right license and configuration, you can prevent it from being stored or analysed. One risk that remains is that the government of the supplier may be forced to store and keep input and output to serve for subpoenas. If you're using a RAG system, remember that the data you retrieve and inject into the prompt also counts as input data. This often includes sensitive company information or personal data."

Further risks — and the always-do list

THREAT
Output contains conventional injectionModel text interpreted downstream — e.g., malicious JavaScript displayed on a website (XSS) or SQL to a database. Maps to: LLM05.

Question: Does your model create text output? Consider output containing conventional injection, "for example, when you display the output of the model on a website and the output contains malicious Javascript." Control: #ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT.

THREAT
AI resource exhaustionActors overusing your AI causing availability problems and/or high cost. Always a possibility. Maps to: LLM10, ATLAS AML.T0029.

ALWAYS DO: "Make sure to protect against AI resource exhaustions (actors overusing your AI causing availability problems and/or high cost)." (The original carries a doubled closing parenthesis.) If a supplier runs your model, certain countermeasures may already be in place. Impact per the one-pager: model unavailability or fund depletion when the model runs as a paid service.

THREAT
Generic runtime security threats · Generic development-environment and supply-chain threatsConventional threats needing their own conventional threat modeling. Impact: C, I, A of any asset.

Closing note of the identification step: "Since AI systems are software systems, they require appropriate conventional application security and operational security, apart from the AI-specific threats and controls mentioned in this section."

flowchart TD
  Q8{"You train or finetune AND training data is sensitive?"} -->|yes| T11["Disclosure in model output"]
  Q8 -->|yes| T12["Model inversion"]
  Q8 -->|yes| T13["Direct training data leak"]
  Q8 -->|yes, if membership itself is sensitive| T14["Membership inference"]
  Q9{"Insert data into input, e.g. RAG?"} -->|yes| N3["Apply the training-data threats to augmentation data too"]
  Q10{"You train or finetune AND model is IP or evasion-prone?"} -->|yes| T15["Model exfiltration"]
  Q10 -->|yes| T16["Direct development-time model leak"]
  Q10 -->|yes| T17["Source code or configuration leak"]
  Q10 -->|yes| T18["Direct runtime model leak"]
  Q11{"Is your input data sensitive?"} -->|yes| T19["Input data leak - RAG-retrieved data counts as input"]
  Q12{"Model creates text output?"} -->|yes| T20["Output contains conventional injection"]
  A1["ALWAYS: protect against AI resource exhaustion"]
  A2["ALWAYS: conventional application and operational security"]
  A3["ALWAYS: limit model behaviour - output filtering, monitoring, oversight, human in the loop, guardrails, scoped privileges"]

Figure — decision-tree panels 3–6: training-data leaks, model theft, input-data leak, further risks and always-do items (guide pp. 58–60; poster panels 3, 4, 5, 6).

Step 2 — Evaluating risks by estimating likelihood and impact

"To determine the severity of a risk, it is necessary to assess the likelihood of the risk occurring and evaluating the potential consequences should the risk materialize."

Estimating the likelihood

This "requires a thorough understanding of both the technical and contextual aspects of the AI system in scope." Influencing factors: the complexity of the AI algorithms, the data quality and sources, the conventional security measures in place, and the potential for adversarial attacks. The guide's example: an AI system that processes public data is more susceptible to data poisoning and inference attacks — a financial institution's loan-application AI using public credit scores is exposed to data poisoning that "could manipulate creditworthiness assessments, leading to incorrect loan decisions."

Aspect involved in rating probabilitySource framework(s)
Opportunity regarding attacker accessOWASP, FAIR (Factor Analysis for Information Risk)
Risk of getting caughtFAIR
Capabilities/tools/budgetISO/IEC 27005, OWASP, FAIR
Susceptibility of the systemISO/IEC 27005, FAIR
MotiveOWASP, FAIR, ISO/IEC 27005
Number of potential attackersOWASP
Data regarding incidents and attemptsISO/IEC 27005

Likelihood factors with their framework attributions, exactly as the guide lists them (p. 61).

Evaluating the impact

Impact evaluation covers direct consequences (compromised data integrity, system downtime) and indirect consequences (reputational damage, regulatory penalties). "The impact is often magnified in AI systems due to their scale and the critical nature of the tasks they perform. For instance, a successful attack on an AI system used in healthcare diagnostics could lead to misdiagnosis, affecting patient health and leading to significant legal, trust, and reputational repercussions for the involved entities."

Prioritizing risks
