"The combination of likelihood and impact assessments forms the basis for prioritizing risks and informs the development of Risk Treatment decisions. Commonly, organizations use a risk heat map to visually categorize risks by impact and likelihood. This approach facilitates risk communication and decision-making. It allows the management to focus on risks with highest severity (high likelihood and high impact)."

◆ Senior insight

Note which factors you can actually move. Motive and attacker budget are the attacker's; opportunity (access control, rate limits), risk of getting caught (monitoring, series detection) and susceptibility (robust models, minimized data) are yours. Every control you selected in Lessons 1–2 maps onto one of these likelihood factors — that's how "level of risk is input to the strength of the controls" becomes an equation instead of a slogan.

Your team fine-tunes a model on anonymized public forum posts. Which training-data-leak threats survive the 'When' filter?
You train yourself, so the cluster applies in principle — but the data must be sensitive. Anonymized public posts likely fail the sensitivity test for disclosure/inversion/direct leak; membership inference needs the extra condition that being in the set is itself sensitive (it isn't, for public posts). If anonymization is weak, the sensitivity assumption fails and all four return — the analysis is only as good as that premise.
Why does the one-pager put "model is susceptible to an evasion attack" in the model-theft 'When' condition?
Because a stolen model is an attack-preparation asset, not just lost IP: with a local copy an attacker can craft and test adversarial examples offline (no rate limits, no monitoring) and then replay them against your production classifier. Theft converts your black-box into their white-box.
A colleague says RAG data is "just context, not input". What two consequences does the guide attach to RAG data that contradict this?
First, retrieved-and-injected data counts as input data — it often includes sensitive company or personal data, so the input-data-leak analysis covers it. Second, it must be treated like training data for leak purposes: it feeds the model and can appear in output (disclosure), so the training-data threat set applies to it as augmentation data.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 58–61. Likelihood-factor frameworks as attributed: OWASP risk rating, FAIR (Factor Analysis for Information Risk), ISO/IEC 27005. Canon: MITRE ATLAS AML.T0024 (Exfiltration via ML Inference API — .000 membership inference, .001 model inversion, .002 model extraction), AML.T0029 (Denial of ML Service); OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM02, LLM05, LLM10; NIST AI RMF MEASURE function for the evaluation step.

Key Points

  • Training-data leak cluster (only if you train and the data is sensitive): disclosure in output, model inversion, direct training data leak, membership inference (the last only when set-membership itself is sensitive — the criminals-dataset example).
  • RAG/augmentation data must be analysed as if it were training data — and it also counts as input data.
  • Supplier-trained models: supplier is responsible but not accountable per se for poisoned/confidential/copyrighted training content — check licenses, warranties, contracts, or accept.
  • Model theft (if self-trained and IP or evasion-prone): model exfiltration, dev-time model leak, source code/config leak, runtime model leak; stolen models arm competitors and input-attackers.
  • Input data leak: cloud AI providers hold input/output unencrypted (like Google Suite / Microsoft 365); licenses/config can prevent storage/analysis, but government subpoena retention remains.
  • Text output ⇒ conventional injection risk; ALWAYS protect against AI resource exhaustion; AI systems still need conventional appsec and ops security.
  • Likelihood factors: attacker opportunity, risk of getting caught, capabilities/tools/budget, susceptibility, motive, number of attackers, incident data — attributed to OWASP, FAIR and ISO/IEC 27005.
  • Impact = direct + indirect consequences, magnified by AI scale/criticality; prioritize on a likelihood × impact heatmap.
Module 02 · Risk treatment through continuous assessment · OWASP AI Exchange

Treating Risk — Responsibility, Control Selection, Residual Acceptance, and the Responsible-AI Frontier

~18 minCovers guide pp. 62–67

Identification and evaluation produce a prioritized list; steps 3–10 turn it into decisions and running controls: choose a treatment, communicate through the risk register, pin responsibility (including on suppliers), verify it, select and tune controls, accept what remains — and keep re-assessing. The lesson closes with the guide's two "How about" discussions: AI beyond machine learning, and where security meets responsible/trustworthy AI.

Step 3 — Risk treatment

"Risk treatment is about deciding what to do with the risks: transfer, avoid, accept, or mitigate. Mitigation involves selecting and implementing controls. This process is critical due to the unique vulnerabilities and threats related to AI systems such as data poisoning, model theft, and adversarial attacks. Effective risk treatment is essential to robust, reliable, and trustworthy AI."

OptionWhat it meansGuide's example
MitigationImplementing controls to reduce the likelihood or impact of a risk — often the most common approach for AI cybersecurity risks; see the controls in this resource and the 'Select controls' subsection.Enhancing data validation processes to prevent data poisoning attacks, where malicious data is fed into the model to corrupt its learning process and negatively impact its performance.
TransferShifting the risk to a third party — typically through transfer learning, federated learning, insurance or outsourcing certain functions.Using third-party cloud services with robust security measures for AI model training, hosting, and data storage — transferring the risk of data breaches and infrastructure attacks.
AvoidanceChanging plans or strategies to eliminate the risk altogether — possibly not using AI where the risk is too high.Deciding against deploying an AI system for processing highly sensitive personal data where the risk of data breaches cannot be adequately mitigated.
AcceptanceAcknowledging the risk and bearing the potential loss without specific action — chosen when the cost of treating the risk outweighs the potential impact.Accepting the minimal risk of model inversion attacks (an attacker attempting to reconstruct publicly available input data from model outputs) in non-sensitive applications where the impact is considered low.
Treatment decision per identified risk
Prioritized riskfrom the heatmap
→
Mitigateselect + tune controls
→
Transferthird party / insurance
→
Avoidchange or drop the plan
→
Acceptexplicit, recorded

Step 4 — Risk communication & monitoring

Regularly share risk information with stakeholders to ensure awareness and support. The central tool is the Risk Register: "a comprehensive repository of all identified risks, their attributes (such as severity, treatment plan, ownership, and status), and the controls implemented to mitigate them. Most large organizations already have such a Risk Register. It is important to align AI risks and chosen vocabularies from Enterprise Risk Management to facilitate effective communication of risks throughout the organization."

Step 5 — Arrange responsibility

"For each selected threat, determine who is responsible for addressing it. By default, the organization that builds and deploys the AI system is responsible — but building and deploying may be done by different organizations, and some parts may be deferred to others, e.g. hosting the model, or providing a cloud environment for the application to run. Some aspects are shared responsibilities."

If components of your AI system are hosted, you share responsibility for all controls of the relevant threats with the hosting provider — arrange it with a tool like the responsibility matrix. Components can be: the model, model extensions, your application, or your infrastructure (see the ready-made-model threat maps in Lesson 3). And if an external party is not open about how certain risks are mitigated: "consider requesting this information and when this remains unclear you are faced with either 1) accept the risk, 2) or provide your own mitigations, or 3) avoid the risk, by not engaging with the third party."

Step 6 — Verify external responsibilities

"For the threats that are the responsibility of other organisations: attain assurance whether these organisations take care of it. This would involve the controls that are linked to these threats. Example: Regular audits and assessments of third-party security measures."

Step 7 — Select controls

For the threats that are relevant to your use case and fall under your responsibility, review the associated controls — both those listed directly under the threat (or its parent category) and the general controls, which apply universally. The Periodic table gives the overview per threat. For each control: consider its purpose, and assess whether it's worth implementing and to what extent, weighing implementation cost against effectiveness and the level of the associated risk. These factors also set your ordering: "Start with the highest-risk threats and prioritize low-cost, quick-win controls (the 'low-hanging fruit')."

Then tune: "Controls often have quality-related parameters that need to be adjusted to suit the specific situation and level of risk. For example, this could involve deciding how much noise to add to input data or setting appropriate thresholds for anomaly detection. Testing the effectiveness of these controls in a simulation environment helps you evaluate their performance and security impact to find the right balance. This tuning process should be continuous, using insights from both simulated tests and real-world production feedback."

When have you done enough? "The AI system is sufficiently secure when all identified risks can be treated, meaning transferred, avoided or accepted — where acceptance in some cases can be done directly, without first taking action, and in other cases require you to implement controls to bring the risk to an acceptable level."

Steps 8, 9, 10 — Accept, manage, repeat

  • 8 · Residual risk acceptance: "In the end, you need to be able to accept the risks that remain regarding each threat, given the controls that you implemented."
  • 9 · Further management of the selected controls (see #SECPROGRAM): continuous monitoring, documentation, reporting, and incident response.
  • 10 · Continuous risk assessment: "Implement continuous monitoring to detect and respond to new threats. Update the risk management strategies based on evolving threats and feedback from incident response activities. Example: Regularly reviewing and updating risk treatment plans to adapt to new vulnerabilities."
🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Every surviving risk gets exactly one recorded treatment: mitigate, transfer, avoid, or accept — no silent acceptance.
  • Put AI risks in the existing enterprise Risk Register with ERM-aligned vocabulary; don't fork a parallel register.
  • Draw the responsibility matrix per hosted component (model / extensions / application / infrastructure) before contract signature.
  • Verify external responsibility with audits and assessments — assurance, not assumption.
  • Sequence controls by risk level and cost: highest-risk threats first, low-hanging fruit first.
  • Tune control parameters (noise levels, detection thresholds) in simulation, then keep tuning on production feedback.
  • Re-run the analysis on every architecture change — a new agent tool can create a lethal trifecta overnight.
Your provider refuses to disclose how it prevents runtime model poisoning. Enumerate your options per the guide.
First request the information. If it remains unclear: (1) accept the risk, (2) provide your own mitigations, or (3) avoid the risk by not engaging with that third party. Note what is absent: you cannot "transfer" accountability by pointing at the provider's silence — step 6 requires you to attain assurance, e.g. through audits.
Why does the guide list transfer learning and federated learning under risk *transfer*?
Both shift risk-bearing work to third parties: transfer learning reuses a supplier's pre-trained model (their training-time risks, their controls), and federated learning leaves raw data with the participating parties instead of centralizing it with you. Alongside insurance and outsourcing, they move where the risk materializes — at the price of new supply-chain dependencies to verify.

How about AI outside of machine learning?

A helpful lens: AI consists of machine-learning models and heuristic models — a model can be learned from data, or engineered from human knowledge (e.g., a rule-based system). Heuristic models still require data for testing and sometimes for analysis supporting further development and validation of the human-derived knowledge. The document focuses on ML, but these threats carry over to heuristic systems:

  • Model evasion — attackers can find loopholes or weaknesses in the defined rules.
  • Model exfiltration — a machine-learning model can be trained on input/output combinations from a heuristic model.
  • Overreliance in use — heuristic systems can also be relied on too much; the applied knowledge can be false.
  • Data poisoning and model poisoning — tampering with the data used to enhance knowledge, or manipulating the rules during development or at runtime.
  • Leaks of data used for analysis or testing.
  • Knowledge base, source code and configuration — sensitive as intellectual property; needs protection.
  • Leak of sensitive input data — e.g., when a heuristic system diagnoses a patient.

How about responsible or trustworthy AI?

Positive-outcome AI with mitigated risks goes by two names: responsible AI emphasises ethics, society, and governance; trustworthy AI emphasises the technical and operational aspects. The guide's advice to security people: master AI security first, then expand — "security professionals are often skilled at spotting potential failure points", and some aspects (such as safety) can be a consequence of compromised AI. The principles, each with its security connection:

PrincipleMeaningConnection to security
AccuracyThe model is sufficiently correct for its business function; being incorrect can cause harm — (physical) safety problems (car trunk opens during driving) or harmful wrong decisions (wrongfully declined loan).Attacks causing unwanted model behaviour are by definition an accuracy problem — but security's scope is mitigating those attacks, NOT solving the whole accuracy problem (representative training data etc.).
SafetyProtection from / unlikelihood of causing harm: the level of accuracy where harm is at stake (typically physical, not exclusively), plus risk mitigations beyond accuracy.Shared concern: security safeguards accuracy against attack; safety measures — oversight (with least model privileges), continuous validation, transparency, explainability — must exist anyway because the model can make unsafe decisions for other reasons (e.g., bad training data).
TransparencySharing information about the approach to warn users and depending systems of accuracy risks; users often have the right to know details about the model and its creation.Shared concern between security, privacy and safety.
ExplainabilitySharing information to help users validate accuracy by explaining how a specific result came to be; also shows what to change for a different outcome.Shared between security, privacy, safety and business function — plus compliance when required by law separate from privacy.
RobustnessMaintaining accuracy under expected or unexpected input variations.Security owns the malicious variations (adversarial robustness), which often need different countermeasures than normal variations (generalization robustness); where they overlap it becomes a safety/security shared concern, case by case.
Free of discriminationNo unwanted bias on protected attributes — no systematic inaccuracy 'mistreating' groups (gender, ethnicity); undesired for legal and ethical reasons.Bias detection can reveal attacks: a data-poisoning attack going unnoticed until "an unexplained detection of bias in the model". Note: 'fairness' in privacy is broader — fair treatment of individuals including transparency, ethical use, and privacy rights.
EmpathyRecognizing the practical limits of what security can achieve when evaluating an AI application.If individuals or organizations cannot be adequately protected, empathy means rethinking the idea — rejecting it altogether or adding precautions to reduce potential harm.
◆ Senior insight

The principles table is secretly a stakeholder map. Every "shared concern" row names the colleagues whose controls overlap yours: safety engineers co-own oversight and continuous validation, privacy officers co-own transparency and data minimization, compliance co-owns explainability. When you arrange responsibility (step 5), these are internal responsibility-matrix rows — the same discipline you applied to hosting providers, applied inside the building.

Adversarial robustness vs. generalization robustness — whose problem is each, and why does the guide refuse a hard boundary?
Generalization robustness (normal variations) belongs to model quality/safety; security is "not involved per se". Adversarial robustness (malicious variations) is security's, and often needs different countermeasures. But when robustness work overlaps — a control helping against both — it becomes a shared safety/security concern, and "whether it falls more under one or the other depends on the specific case".
Give the guide's example of security benefiting from a responsible-AI control rather than the other way around.
Unwanted-bias detection: a data-poisoning attack inserted malicious training samples that initially went unnoticed — and was discovered through an unexplained detection of bias in the model. The discrimination control acted as an intrusion-detection signal for an integrity attack.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 62–67. Permalink for the responsible-AI discussion: owaspai.org/go/responsibleai/. Treatment/register/responsibility practice aligns with ISO/IEC 27005 and ISO 31000; NIST AI RMF MANAGE covers treatment and monitoring, and its trustworthiness characteristics (valid & reliable, safe, secure & resilient, accountable & transparent, explainable, privacy-enhanced, fair) parallel the guide's principles. EU AI Act Article 14 (human oversight) and transparency obligations back the safety-shared controls.

Key Points

  • Four treatments — mitigate (most common; controls), transfer (transfer learning, federated learning, insurance, outsourcing), avoid (don't use AI there), accept (cost of treatment outweighs impact) — each with a canonical example.
  • The Risk Register carries severity, treatment plan, ownership, status and implemented controls; align vocabulary with Enterprise Risk Management.
  • Responsibility defaults to whoever builds and deploys, but hosting splits it — use a responsibility matrix over model, model extensions, application, infrastructure; opaque suppliers leave you accept / self-mitigate / avoid.
  • Verify external responsibilities through regular audits and assessments; then select controls per threat plus the universal general controls, ordered by risk level and quick wins.
  • Controls have tunable quality parameters (noise amounts, anomaly thresholds) — tune in simulation and with production feedback, continuously.
  • Done = every identified risk treated: transferred, avoided, or accepted (directly or after mitigation brings it to an acceptable level); residual risk is accepted explicitly, then #SECPROGRAM manages controls and step 10 keeps the assessment alive.
  • Heuristic (rule-based) AI inherits most ML threats: rule-loophole evasion, exfiltration via I/O harvesting, poisoning of knowledge/rules, data leaks, IP-sensitive knowledge bases, sensitive input, overreliance.
  • Responsible AI (ethics/society/governance) vs. trustworthy AI (technical/operational); principles — accuracy, safety, transparency, explainability, robustness, non-discrimination, empathy — interlock with security as shared concerns.
Module 03 · § How about AI outside of machine learning? · OWASP AI Exchange

AI Beyond Machine Learning: Heuristic Systems Have (Almost) the Same Threats

~9 minCovers guide p. 65

Most AI security writing assumes "AI = machine learning". But a large share of deployed AI is not learned from data at all — it is engineered from human knowledge: rule-based fraud engines, clinical decision trees, expert systems. The guide's uncomfortable news: nearly every ML threat has a heuristic-model twin. After this lesson you will be able to walk a rule-based system through the AI Exchange threat list and say precisely which threats carry over, and why.

Two kinds of models

The guide offers a helpful decomposition: AI consists of machine learning models (the current dominant type of AI), which have learned how to compute based on data, and heuristic models, engineered based on human knowledge — e.g. a rule-based system. The knowledge is written down by people instead of extracted from a training set.

A subtlety that matters for security scoping: heuristic models still require data — for testing, and in some cases for conducting analysis that supports further development and validation of the human-derived knowledge. So "we have no training data" does not mean "we have no data-leak threat surface". The AI Exchange document focuses on machine learning, but it gives a quick summary of which ML threats also apply to heuristic systems — and that summary is worth internalising because auditors and threat modellers routinely skip rule-based components.

THREAT
Model evasion — against rules, not weightsAttackers probe for loopholes or weaknesses in defined rules to force a wrong decision. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: MITRE ATLAS AML.T0015 (Evade ML Model) — same tactic, non-ML target.

The threat carry-over map

Here is the guide's full list of machine-learning threats that also apply to heuristic systems, with the mechanism for each:

ML threatHow it manifests against a heuristic model
Model evasionAlso possible with heuristic models: attackers may try to find loopholes or weaknesses in the defined rules. A fraudster who learns that transactions under a threshold skip review is evading a rule set exactly the way an adversarial example evades a classifier.
Model exfiltrationIt is possible to train a machine learning model based on input/output combinations from a heuristic model. The attacker never sees your rules — they clone the behaviour into a surrogate model, stealing the functional IP.
Overreliance in useHeuristic systems can also be relied on too much. The applied knowledge can be false — a wrong rule fails just as confidently as a hallucinating LLM.
Data poisoning and model poisoningBoth can occur by tampering with the data used to enhance knowledge, or by manipulating the rules either during development or at runtime. The "model parameters" here are the rules themselves.
Leaks of dataData used for analysis or testing can still be an issue — it may be sensitive even though it is not a "training set".
Knowledge base, source code and configuration leakThese can be regarded as sensitive data when they are intellectual property, so they need protection — the direct analogue of development-time model theft.
Leak sensitive input dataFor example when a heuristic system needs to diagnose a patient: the input is medical data regardless of what kind of model consumes it.
⚔ Attacker's view

Against a rule-based system the attacker's economics are often better than against ML: rules are deterministic, so probing converges fast. Send inputs, observe accept/reject boundaries, and you have reverse-engineered the decision surface with a few hundred queries — no gradient estimation, no transfer attacks needed. And once the rules are known, evasion is exact, not probabilistic. This is why spam and fraud rule engines have always been locked in an update arms race.

◆ Senior insight

The strategic takeaway: threat applicability follows the system function and data flows, not the model technology. When you threat-model a hybrid architecture (rules pre-filter, ML scores, LLM explains), run the same threat catalogue over every decision-making component. The only threats that genuinely drop out for heuristic models are the ones tied to statistical learning internals — e.g. training-set inference attacks like model inversion and membership inference have no training set to invert. Everything driven by input/output behaviour or by asset theft stays.

▸ Worked example

A hospital runs a hand-written triage decision tree. No ML anywhere. Threats that still apply: a patient gaming symptom answers to jump the queue (evasion); a competitor scripting thousands of symptom combinations to clone the tree into their own product (model exfiltration via input/output pairs); an insider editing one branch so a class of cases is always down-triaged (runtime model/rule poisoning); patient symptom inputs leaking from logs (sensitive input data leak); and clinicians trusting a stale rule that no longer reflects guidance (overreliance — the applied knowledge can be false).

Your system is 100% rule-based. Which two ML threats can you actually cross off, and which one do people wrongly cross off?
You can cross off training-set inference threats such as model inversion and membership inference — there is no learned training set to reconstruct. People wrongly cross off model exfiltration ("there's no model to steal") — but an attacker can train an ML surrogate on your system's input/output combinations and walk away with functionally equivalent IP.
Why do heuristic models still create a data-leak threat surface?
Because they still require data for testing, and sometimes for analysis supporting development and validation of the human-derived knowledge. That test/analysis data (e.g. real patient records) can be sensitive, and the knowledge base, source code and configuration are themselves sensitive assets when they are intellectual property.
§ Source & standards

Guide p. 65, section "How about AI outside of machine learning?" (part of the "How about…" discussion sections). Evasion of non-ML decision logic is classic adversarial behaviour long predating adversarial ML — MITRE ATLAS AML.T0015 describes the ML variant; surrogate-model theft maps to ATLAS AML.T0048.004-style extraction via the query interface (LLM-agnostic idea). See the GenAI lesson for how the same catalogue shifts for LLMs.

Key Points

  • AI = machine learning models (learned from data) + heuristic models (engineered from human knowledge, e.g. rule-based systems).
  • Heuristic models still need data for testing and analysis — so data-leak threats persist.
  • Carry-over threats: model evasion (rule loopholes), model exfiltration (surrogate trained on I/O pairs), overreliance (rules can be false), data and model poisoning (tamper with knowledge data or the rules, dev-time or runtime), test/analysis data leaks, knowledge base/source code/configuration leaks (IP), and sensitive input data leaks.
  • Threats tied to statistical training internals (model inversion, membership inference) are the ones that genuinely drop away.
  • Threat-model by function and data flow, not by model technology — hybrid systems get the full catalogue on every component.
  • Deterministic rules can make an attacker's probing cheaper than against ML: fewer queries, exact evasion.
Module 03 · § How about responsible or trustworthy AI? · OWASP AI Exchange

Responsible & Trustworthy AI: Eight Principles Through a Security Lens

~16 minCovers guide pp. 65–68

"Responsible AI" and "Trustworthy AI" are umbrella terms you will meet in every AI governance meeting. The AI Exchange does something rare: instead of listing principles as ethics slogans, it maps each one to security — where security owns it, shares it, or merely benefits from it. After this lesson you can articulate, for any principle, exactly where the security team's scope starts and ends. That boundary-drawing skill is what keeps security teams from being handed the entire "make AI good" problem.

Two umbrella terms, one landscape

There are many aspects of AI when it comes to positive outcomes while mitigating risks. This is often referred to as responsible AI or trustworthy AI. The distinction the guide draws: responsible AI emphasises ethics, society, and governance, while trustworthy AI emphasises the more technical and operational aspects.

The guide's career advice for security people is refreshingly direct: if your primary responsibility is security, start by focusing on AI security. Once you have a solid grasp of that, expand to other AI aspects — even if just to support the colleagues who own those areas and help them stay vigilant. Security professionals are often skilled at spotting potential failure points. And some aspects, such as safety, can be a consequence of compromised AI, so understanding them is part of the security job anyway.

Permalink for this section: owaspai.org/go/responsibleai/.

The principles, one by one

Accuracy

Accuracy is about the AI model being sufficiently correct to perform its "business function". Being incorrect can lead to harm, including (physical) safety problems — the guide's example: a car trunk opens during driving — or other harmful wrong decisions, e.g. a wrongfully declined loan. The security link: some attacks cause unwanted model behaviour, which is by definition an accuracy problem. But the security scope is restricted to mitigating the risks of those attacks — not solving the entire problem of creating an accurate model (selecting representative data for the trainset, etc.).

⚠ Common misconception

"The model is inaccurate — that's a security bug." No. Inaccuracy has three sources: general inaccuracy (e.g. hallucinations), unwanted bias, and attacker manipulation. Only the third is security's problem to prevent. If you let the boundary blur, the security team becomes accountable for data science quality — a scope it cannot control and should not own. Conversely, data scientists cannot dismiss adversarially induced errors as "model quality issues": those need security controls, not more training epochs.

Safety

Safety refers to the condition of being protected from / unlikely to cause harm. Safety of an AI system is therefore about the level of accuracy when there is a risk of harm (typically implying physical harm, but not restricted to that), plus the things in place to mitigate those risks (apart from accuracy) — which includes security to safeguard accuracy, plus a number of safety measures important for the business function of the model. These need to be taken care of not just for security reasons, because the model can make unsafe decisions for other reasons (e.g. bad training data). They are a shared concern between safety and security:

  • Oversight (linked control) to restrict unsafe behaviour, and connected to that: assigning least privileges to the model;
  • Continuous validation (linked control) to safeguard accuracy;
  • Transparency (linked control) — see below;
  • Explainability (linked control) — see below.

Transparency

Transparency means sharing information about the approach, to warn users and depending systems of accuracy risks — plus, in many cases, users have the right to know details about a model being used and how it has been created. It is therefore a shared concern between security, privacy and safety.

Explainability

Explainability means sharing information to help users validate accuracy by explaining in more detail how a specific result came to be. Beyond validating accuracy, it also supports users to get transparency and to understand what needs to change to get a different outcome. It is therefore a shared concern between security, privacy, safety and business function. A special case: when explainability is required by law separate from privacy, which adds compliance to the list of aspects sharing this concern.

Robustness

Robustness is about the ability of maintaining accuracy under expected or unexpected variations in input. The split that every security engineer must know:

  • Adversarial robustness — when those variations are malicious. This is the security scope, and it often requires different countermeasures than those against normal variations.
  • Generalization robustness — against normal variations. Just as with accuracy, security is not involved per se in creating a robust model for normal variations.

The exception: when generalization robustness or adversarial robustness is involved, this becomes a shared concern between safety and security — whether it falls more under one or the other depends on the specific case.

◆ Senior insight

Why do adversarial and generalization robustness need different countermeasures? Natural variation is random and distribution-shaped — you fight it with more diverse training data and regularisation. Adversarial variation is worst-case and optimised: an attacker searches for the exact input perturbation your model handles worst. Defences must therefore change the optimisation landscape (adversarial training, input distortion, detection), not just widen the training distribution. Budget accordingly: a model that generalises beautifully can still fold to a targeted perturbation.

Free of discrimination

Without unwanted bias of protected attributes: no systematic inaccuracy where the model "mistreats" certain groups (e.g. gender, ethnicity). Discrimination is undesired for legal and ethical reasons. The security relation is subtle and useful: detection of unwanted bias can help identify unwanted model behaviour caused by an attack. The guide's example: a data poisoning attack inserts malicious samples into the training set, goes unnoticed at first, and is then discovered by an unexplained detection of bias in the model. Note on vocabulary: sometimes "fairness" is used for discrimination issues, but mostly fairness in privacy is a broader term — fair treatment of individuals, including transparency, ethical use, and privacy rights.

Empathy

Empathy's connection to security lies in recognizing the practical limits of what security can achieve when evaluating an AI application. If individuals or organizations cannot be adequately protected, empathy means rethinking the idea — either rejecting it altogether or taking additional precautions to reduce potential harm.

Accountability

The relation of accountability with security: security measures should be demonstrable, including the processes that led to those measures. In addition, traceability as a security property is important — just like in any IT system — to detect, reconstruct and respond to security incidents and provide accountability.

AI security

The security aspect of AI is the central topic of the AI Exchange. In short, it breaks down into four attack classes:

AI security — the four-part breakdown (guide p. 68)
Input attacksperformed by providing input to the model
Model poisoningaimed to alter the model's behavior
Stealing AI assetstrain data, model input, output, or the model itself — development time or runtime
Conventional attacksfurther runtime conventional security attacks

The principles at a glance

PrincipleEssenceWho shares the concern with security?
AccuracyModel sufficiently correct for its business functionSecurity only mitigates attack-caused inaccuracy; making the model accurate is data science's job
SafetyAccuracy where harm is at stake, plus mitigations (oversight + least model privilege, continuous validation, transparency, explainability)Safety ↔ security shared concern
TransparencyShare information about the approach; warn of accuracy risks; users' right to knowSecurity, privacy, safety
ExplainabilityExplain how a specific result came to be, so users can validate accuracySecurity, privacy, safety, business function (+ compliance when legally required)
RobustnessMaintain accuracy under input variations; adversarial vs generalizationAdversarial robustness = security; shared with safety, case-dependent
Free of discriminationNo systematic inaccuracy mistreating protected groupsLegal/ethical concern; bias detection doubles as an attack-detection signal for security
EmpathyRecognise limits of protection; rethink or add precautionsGoverns go/no-go decisions on AI applications
AccountabilityDemonstrable security measures and processes; traceabilitySecurity provides evidence and incident reconstruction

The AI wayfinder — one map of the whole landscape

The section closes with the "AI wayfinder" figure (attributed to SIG — Software Improvement Group, with details at owaspai.org/goto/responsibleai/). It shows how model-quality aspects, attack types, information duties and the affected individual relate to one AI system, colour-coded by discipline: blue = security, purple = privacy, teal = shared security & privacy, asterisk = fairness, with the side note "Bias = systematic inaccuracy". Reconstructed:

flowchart LR
  subgraph MQ["Model quality"]
    PERF["Performance - precision etc."]
    GEN["Generalization robustness"]
    DISC["Discrimination bias - fairness, privacy"]
    ADV["Adversarial robustness - security"]
  end
  IN1["Input not in the trainset"] --> GEN
  IN2["Input from different protected groups"] --> DISC
  subgraph IA["Input attacks - security"]
    EV["Adversarial input - evasion attack"]
    TDE["Training data extraction"]
    ME["Model extraction"]
    DOS["Denial of model service"]
    PI["Direct or indirect prompt injection"]
  end
  IA --> AIS["AI system"]
  USE["Lawful and ethical use / use to attack"] --> AIS
  subgraph FA["Further attacks"]
    MP["Model poisoning - data, tamper, supply"]
    ST["Stealing train data, input, output or model"]
    CSA["Conventional security attacks"]
  end
  MP --> AIS
  AIS --> ST
  CSA --> AIS
  MQ --> OUT["Output with some inaccuracy"]
  AIS --> OUT
  OUT --> IND["Affected individual - potential harm: invasion of privacy, safety, etc."]
  IND -->|"Right to object, correct, request, erase"| AIS
  subgraph INF["Inform"]
    AD["Approach description"]
    MC["Model calculation"]
  end
  AD -->|"Transparency"| IND
  MC -->|"Explainability"| IND

Figure — the AI wayfinder (guide p. 68): security, privacy, safety and fairness concerns divided and overlapping across one AI system. Input attacks (evasion, training data extraction, model extraction, denial of model service, prompt injection) hit the system from the left; further attacks (model poisoning; stealing train data, input, output or model; conventional attacks) act on it directly; output with some inaccuracy reaches the affected individual, who holds privacy rights (object, correct, request, erase) and is owed transparency (via the approach description) and explainability (via the model calculation).

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Write down, per principle, whether security owns, shares, or informs the concern in your organisation — and get sign-off from privacy, safety and legal.
  • Wire bias-monitoring alerts into security triage: an unexplained bias shift is a possible poisoning indicator, not just an ethics ticket.
  • For any model that can cause harm, confirm the four shared safety measures exist: oversight with least model privilege, continuous validation, transparency, explainability.
  • Make security measures demonstrable (accountability): keep decision records and ensure traceability/logging supports incident reconstruction.
  • Apply the empathy test before launch: if users cannot be adequately protected, escalate the option to reject or redesign the idea.
A regulator asks why the security team doesn't guarantee model accuracy. What is the correct boundary statement?
Attacks that manipulate model behaviour are by definition accuracy problems, and security mitigates the risk of those attacks. But overall accuracy — representative training data, model quality, hallucination rates — is a data science / business-function responsibility. Security safeguards accuracy against adversaries; it does not create accuracy.
How can a fairness control become a security detection control?
Bias detection watches for systematic inaccuracy against groups. A data poisoning attack that slipped malicious samples into training can surface as an unexplained bias shift — so unwanted-bias detection doubles as an indicator of compromise for poisoning attacks.
Name the four measures that safety and security share for a model that can make unsafe decisions.
Oversight (restricting unsafe behaviour, with least privileges assigned to the model), continuous validation (safeguarding accuracy), transparency, and explainability. They are shared because unsafe decisions can come from attacks or from non-security causes like bad training data.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 65–68, permalink owaspai.org/go/responsibleai/. The same principle family appears in the NIST AI Risk Management Framework's trustworthiness characteristics (valid & reliable; safe; secure & resilient; accountable & transparent; explainable & interpretable; privacy-enhanced; fair with harmful bias managed) — the AI Exchange's mapping tells you which of these land on the security desk. The EU AI Act makes transparency and human oversight legal obligations for high-risk systems, which is exactly the "compliance" special case the guide flags for explainability.

Key Points

  • Responsible AI emphasises ethics/society/governance; trustworthy AI emphasises technical/operational aspects.
  • Security's scope on accuracy: mitigate attack-caused unwanted behaviour — not build accurate models.
  • Safety = accuracy under risk of harm + mitigations; oversight (least model privilege), continuous validation, transparency and explainability are shared safety/security concerns.
  • Transparency (security+privacy+safety) warns of accuracy risks and honours the right to know; explainability (adds business function and, when legally required, compliance) lets users validate individual results.
  • Robustness splits into adversarial (security) vs generalization (quality/safety) — different countermeasures.
  • Bias detection can reveal data poisoning; "fairness" in privacy is broader than discrimination.
  • Empathy = recognise the limits of protection; reject or add precautions when people can't be protected.
  • Accountability = demonstrable measures + traceability; AI security itself = input attacks, model poisoning, stealing AI assets, conventional runtime attacks.
Module 03 · § How about Generative AI (e.g. LLM)? · OWASP AI Exchange

How About Generative AI? What Actually Changes for Security

~16 minCovers guide pp. 68–72

GenAI is leading the current AI revolution and is the fastest-moving subfield of AI security — but the guide's most valuable claim is a calm one: from a security-threat perspective, GenAI is not that different from other forms of AI. Some risks are (much) higher, some are lower, only a few are GenAI-specific. This lesson gives you the full delta: what is genuinely new, which risks moved up or down, and the exact mapping of each GenAI particularity to the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications.

Predictive AI is not going away

The guide coins predictive AI for the other types of algorithms, and stresses they will remain applied to many important use cases: credit scoring, fraud detection, medical diagnosis, product recommendation, image recognition, predictive maintenance, process control, etc. GenAI-relevant content is marked "GenAI" throughout the guide.

The important note, near-verbatim: GenAI threats and controls largely overlap and are very similar to AI in general. Nevertheless, some risks are (much) higher. Some are lower. Only a few risks are GenAI-specific. Some control categories differ substantially — mostly the data science controls (e.g. adding noise to the training set). In many cases, GenAI solutions use a model as-is, with no training by the organization whatsoever, shifting some security responsibilities from the organization to the supplier. But if you use a ready-made model, you still need to be aware of those threats.

◆ Senior insight

"We don't train, so poisoning isn't our problem" is the classic ready-made-model fallacy. Responsibility shifts, it doesn't disappear: you now carry supply-chain assurance duties (did the supplier prevent poisoning? can you verify?) instead of pipeline-hardening duties. The threat still lands on your users if it materialises — you traded engineering controls for vendor-assurance controls, which are harder to verify because providers are typically not very open about their security countermeasures.

What is mainly new to the threat landscape because of LLMs?

  • LLMs as a source of harm elsewhere: they may be used to create code with vulnerabilities, be used by attackers to create malware, or cause harm through hallucinations. These concerns are outside the scope of the AI Exchange, which focuses on security threats to AI systems themselves.
  • Input: prompt injection — attackers manipulate the behaviour of the model with crafted and sometimes hidden instructions. Also new: organizations sending huge amounts of data in prompts, with company secrets and personal data.
  • Output: the fact that output can contain injection attacks, or can contain sensitive or copyrighted data, is new (see the copyright lesson).
  • Overreliance: we let LLMs control and create things and may have too much trust in how correct they are — and also underestimate the risk of them being manipulated. The result: attacks can have much impact.
  • Training: since training sets are so large and based on public data, it is easier to perform data poisoning. Poisoned foundation models are also a big supply chain issue.
  • Actions: like any AI system, a GenAI system can trigger actions based on output — but with GenAI, the model output can contain function calls to perform actions (e.g. send mail) or trigger other AI systems. See the guide's Agentic AI section for details.
THREAT
Prompt injection (direct & indirect)Natural-language control of the model lets users (direct) or third parties via injected content (indirect) subvert behaviour. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: OWASP LLM01, MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051.

The nine GenAI security particularities

The guide's table maps each particularity to the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications — reproduced in full:

Nr.GenAI security particularityOWASP for LLM Top 10
1GenAI models are controlled by natural language in prompts, creating the risk of Prompt injection. Direct prompt injection is where the user tries to fool the model to behave in unwanted ways (e.g. offensive language), whereas with indirect prompt injection it is a third party that injects content into the prompt for this purpose (e.g. manipulating a decision).OWASP for LLM 01: Prompt injection
2GenAI models have typically been trained on very large datasets, which makes it more likely to output sensitive data or licensed data — for which there is no control of access privileges built into the model. All data will be accessible to the model users. Some mechanisms may be in place in terms of system prompts, model alignment, or output filtering, but those are typically not watertight.OWASP for LLM 02: Sensitive Information Disclosure
3Data and model poisoning is an AI-broad problem, and with GenAI the risk is generally higher since training data can be supplied from different sources that may be challenging to control, such as the internet. Attackers could for example hijack domains and place manipulated information.OWASP for LLM 04: Data and Model Poisoning
4GenAI models can be inaccurate and hallucinate. This is an AI-broad risk factor, and LLMs can make matters worse by coming across as very confident and knowledgeable. In essence this is the risk of underestimating the probability that the model is wrong or has been manipulated — which connects it to each and every security control. The strongest link is with controls that limit the impact of unwanted model behavior, in particular Least model privilege.OWASP for LLM 06: Excessive agency, and OWASP for LLM 09: Misinformation
5Input data leak: GenAI models mostly live in the cloud — often managed by an external party — which increases the risk of leaking prompts. Not limited to GenAI, but GenAI has 2 particular risks: 1) model use involves user interaction through prompts, adding user data and corresponding privacy/sensitivity issues, and 2) GenAI model input (prompts) can contain rich context information with sensitive data (e.g. company secrets). The latter occurs with in-context learning or Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) (adding background information to a prompt) — for example data from all reports ever written at a consultancy firm. First, this information travels with the prompt to the cloud; second, the system will likely not respect the original access rights to the information.Not covered in LLM top 10
6Pre-trained models may have been manipulated. Pretraining is not limited to GenAI, but the approach is quite common in GenAI, which increases the risk of supply-chain model poisoning.OWASP for LLM 03: Supply chain vulnerabilities
7Model inversion and membership inference are typically low to zero risks for GenAI.Not covered in LLM top 10, apart from LLM06 which uses a different approach — see above
8GenAI output may contain elements that perform an injection attack, such as cross-site-scripting.OWASP for LLM 05: Improper Output Handling
9Resource exhaustion can be an issue for any IT system, but GenAI models typically cost more to run, so overloading them can create unwanted costs.OWASP for LLM 10: Unbounded consumption
How the risk landscape shifts from predictive AI to GenAI
New / GenAI-specific
Prompt injectionnatural-language control; direct and indirect
Function-calling outputoutput triggers actions, mail, other AI systems
Higher risk with GenAI
Data & model poisoninginternet-scale, hard-to-control sources
Supply-chain model poisoningpretraining is the norm
Sensitive data in outputhuge trainsets, no access privileges in the model
Input data leakcloud-hosted, prompt-rich context, RAG
Resource exhaustion costexpensive inference
Overreliance impactconfident tone, trusted with control
Lower with GenAI
Model inversiontypically low to zero risk
Membership inferencetypically low to zero risk
THREAT
Input data leak via cloud prompts (RAG / in-context learning)Rich prompt context (company secrets, personal data) travels to an externally managed cloud, and retrieved data likely loses its original access rights. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Not covered in the LLM Top 10.
⚔ Attacker's view

Particularity 3 describes a genuinely cheap attack: web-scale training pipelines ingest whatever is at a URL at crawl time. An attacker who hijacks an expired domain that is known to be in common crawl sources can place manipulated information and poison future foundation models — cost: the price of a lapsed domain registration. Carlini et al. demonstrated this "split-view poisoning" against popular web-scale datasets for a few tens of dollars. Compare that to poisoning a curated in-house predictive-AI trainset, which requires breaching a development environment.

▸ Worked example

The consultancy-firm scenario from particularity 5: a RAG assistant is given "data from all reports ever written at the firm" as retrievable context. Two failures follow. First, every retrieved fragment travels inside prompts to an external cloud provider. Second, the assistant answers a junior analyst with content distilled from partner-only M&A reports — the vector store never inherited the document ACLs. No attacker was needed; the architecture itself is the leak. Fixes live in data minimisation, access-aware retrieval, and supplier configuration (see the guide's input data leak controls).

⚠ Common misconception

"The LLM Top 10 is our complete GenAI threat model." The guide's own table disproves this: particularity 5 (input data leak via prompts/RAG) is not covered in the LLM Top 10, and model inversion / membership inference are covered only obliquely. The LLM Top 10 is an awareness list, not a threat-modelling method — use the AI Exchange threat catalogue and map to the Top 10 for communication.

References

  • OWASP LLM top 10
  • Demystifying the LLM top 10
  • Impacts and risks of GenAI
  • LLMsecurity.net
Why does the guide say sensitive information disclosure (LLM02) is structurally hard to prevent inside a GenAI model?
Because there is no control of access privileges built into the model: everything in the training set is in principle accessible to all model users. System prompts, model alignment and output filtering can reduce disclosure but are typically not watertight — so the durable fix is controlling what goes into training/augmentation data, not fencing it afterwards.
Which two classic privacy attacks become largely irrelevant for GenAI, and which risks rise in exchange?
Model inversion and membership inference are typically low-to-zero risks for GenAI. In exchange, data/model poisoning (internet-sourced trainsets), supply-chain model poisoning (ubiquitous pretraining), sensitive-data disclosure in output, input data leak via cloud prompts, and the cost impact of resource exhaustion all rise.
Why is hallucination (particularity 4) said to connect to every security control?
Because it is fundamentally about underestimating the probability that the model is wrong or manipulated — overreliance. That misjudgement inflates the impact of every threat, so every control matters; the strongest lever is limiting the impact of unwanted behaviour, especially least model privilege.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 68–72, permalink owaspai.org/go/genai/. Top 10 ids referenced by the guide's table: LLM01 Prompt Injection, LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure, LLM03 Supply Chain, LLM04 Data and Model Poisoning, LLM05 Improper Output Handling, LLM06 Excessive Agency, LLM09 Misinformation, LLM10 Unbounded Consumption. MITRE ATLAS parallels: AML.T0051 (LLM Prompt Injection), AML.T0020 (Poison Training Data), AML.T0010 (ML Supply Chain Compromise), AML.T0029 (Denial of ML Service), AML.T0024 (Exfiltration via ML Inference API).

Key Points

  • GenAI threats largely overlap with predictive AI; some risks are (much) higher, some lower, few are GenAI-specific — the data science control category differs most.
  • Using a ready-made model shifts (not removes) security responsibility toward the supplier.
  • Genuinely new: natural-language prompt control (injection), massive sensitive data in prompts, injection/sensitive/copyrighted content in output, function-calling output, and easier web-scale poisoning.
  • LLM harms like vulnerable generated code, attacker-written malware and hallucination damage are out of the AI Exchange's scope (threats to AI systems).
  • Know the nine particularities and their LLM Top 10 mappings — including the two gaps (input data leak; inversion/membership inference).
  • Model has no internal access-privilege concept: all training data is reachable by all users; alignment and filtering are not watertight.
  • Overreliance multiplies the impact of every attack; least model privilege is the strongest counterweight.
Module 03 · § How about the NCSC/CISA guidelines? · OWASP AI Exchange

The NCSC/CISA Joint Guidelines and the DSIT Code of Practice, Mapped to Controls

~12 minCovers guide pp. 72–74

When your CISO asks "are we following the government guidance on secure AI?", this is the section that answers it. The UK NCSC and US CISA published the joint Guidelines for secure AI system development; the UK's DSIT then built on them with a 13-principle Code of Practice for the Cyber Security of AI. The AI Exchange maps every guideline line-item to its concrete controls — turning high-level government prose into an implementable checklist. This lesson walks the full mapping, phase by phase.

What these documents are

The guide provides a mapping of the UK NCSC / CISA Joint Guidelines for secure AI system development to the controls at the AI Exchange. To see those controls linked to threats, it refers you to the Periodic table of AI security (an AI Exchange resource).

The UK Government, through its DSIT department, drove an initiative to build on these joint guidelines and produce the DSIT Code of Practice for the Cyber Security of AI, which reorganizes things according to 13 principles, does a few tweaks, and adds a bit more governance. The DSIT principle numbers are woven into the mapping below. What DSIT adds is mostly post-market aspects:

  • Principle 10: Communication and processes associated with end-users and affected entities
  • Principle 13: Ensure proper data and model disposal
The four phases of the joint guidelines
1. Secure designawareness, threat modelling, design trade-offs
→
2. Secure developmentsupply chain, assets, documentation, tech debt
→
3. Secure deploymentinfrastructure, model protection, incidents, responsible release
→
4. Secure operation & maintenancemonitor behaviour and inputs, updates, lessons learned

Phase 1 — Secure design

Joint guidelineDSIT principleAI Exchange controls
Raise staff awareness of threats and risks1#SECURITY EDUCATE
Model the threats to your system3See Risk analysis under #SECURITY PROGRAM
Design your system for security as well as functionality and performance2#AI PROGRAM, #SECURITY PROGRAM, #DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM, #SECURE DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM, #CHECK COMPLIANCE, #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE, #DISCRETE, #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE, #OVERSIGHT, #RATE LIMIT, #DOS INPUT VALIDATION, #LIMIT RESOURCES, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL, #AI TRANSPARENCY
Consider security benefits and trade-offs when selecting your AI model—All development-time data science controls (currently 13), #EXPLAINABILITY

Phase 2 — Secure development

Joint guidelineDSIT principleAI Exchange controls
Secure your supply chain7#SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE
Identify, track and protect your assets5#DEVELOPMENT SECURITY, #SEGREGATE DATA, #CONFIDENTIAL COMPUTE, #MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITY, #RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY, #DATA MINIMIZE, #ALLOWED DATA, #SHORT RETAIN, #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA, and part of #SECURITY PROGRAM
Document your data, models and prompts8Part of #DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM
Manage your technical debt—Part of #DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM

Phase 3 — Secure deployment

Joint guidelineDSIT principleAI Exchange controls
Secure your infrastructure6Part of #SECURITY PROGRAM, and see "Identify, track and protect your assets"
Protect your model continuously—#INPUT DISTORTION, #FILTER SENSITIVE MODEL OUTPUT, #RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY, #MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITY, #PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING, #INPUT SEGREGATION
Develop incident management procedures—Part of #SECURITY PROGRAM
Release AI responsibly—Part of #DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM
Make it easy for users to do the right things4 ("Enable human responsibility for AI systems")Part of #SECURITY PROGRAM, also involving #EXPLAINABILITY, documenting prohibited use cases, and #HUMAN OVERSIGHT

Phase 4 — Secure operation and maintenance

Joint guidelineDSIT principleAI Exchange controls
Monitor your system's behaviour12, and similar to 9 (appropriate testing and validation)#CONTINUOUS VALIDATION, #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING
Monitor your system's inputs—#MONITOR USE, #DETECT ODD INPUT, #DETECT ADVERSARIAL INPUT
Follow a secure by design approach to updates11 ("Maintain regular security updates, patches and mitigations")Part of #SECURE DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM
Collect and share lessons learned—Part of #SECURITY PROGRAM and #SECURE DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM
◆ Senior insight

Notice the shape of the mapping: government guidance speaks in lifecycle verbs ("monitor", "protect", "document"), while the AI Exchange speaks in named, testable controls. When you answer an assurance questionnaire built on the NCSC/CISA guidelines or the DSIT Code, don't write essays — cite the control IDs you implement and their evidence. The mapping also exposes gaps in the guidance itself: several guideline items have no DSIT principle number, and conversely DSIT adds post-market duties (P10 end-user communication, P13 data and model disposal) that the original joint guidelines did not cover. Disposal is easy to forget: decommissioned models and their training snapshots keep leaking long after the service is switched off.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Build a traceability matrix: guideline item → DSIT principle → AI Exchange control ID → your implementation evidence.
  • Phase 1: confirm staff AI-threat awareness training (#SECURITY EDUCATE) and a documented AI threat model exist before design sign-off.
  • Phase 2: treat data, models and prompts as documented, tracked assets — prompts are configuration that changes behaviour.
  • Phase 3: "protect your model continuously" is runtime work — input distortion, sensitive-output filtering, I/O integrity, prompt-injection I/O handling, input segregation.
  • Phase 4: monitor both behaviour (continuous validation, unwanted-bias testing) and inputs (use monitoring, odd/adversarial input detection).
  • Add the DSIT extras: end-user communication processes (P10) and proper data and model disposal (P13).
What does the DSIT Code of Practice change relative to the joint guidelines?
It reorganizes the guidance into 13 principles, makes a few tweaks, and adds more governance — mostly post-market aspects: Principle 10 (communication and processes with end-users and affected entities) and Principle 13 (proper data and model disposal).
Under "Monitor your system's inputs", which three AI Exchange controls apply, and what threat class do they primarily catch?
#MONITOR USE, #DETECT ODD INPUT and #DETECT ADVERSARIAL INPUT. They primarily catch runtime input attacks — evasion attempts, prompt-injection probing, model-extraction query patterns and other anomalous use — early enough to respond.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 72–74, permalink owaspai.org/go/jointguidelines/. The NCSC/CISA joint guidelines (November 2023) were co-sealed by agencies from over 20 countries; their four phases deliberately mirror a secure development lifecycle. The DSIT Code of Practice feeds into a planned ETSI global standard. For threat-to-control linkage, the guide points to the Periodic table of AI security.

Key Points

  • The NCSC/CISA joint guidelines organise secure AI development into four phases: secure design, secure development, secure deployment, secure operation & maintenance.
  • The DSIT Code of Practice restructures them into 13 principles and adds post-market governance: P10 end-user communication, P13 data and model disposal.
  • Every guideline item maps to named AI Exchange controls — use control IDs as your assurance evidence language.
  • Model selection is a security decision: all 13 development-time data science controls plus #EXPLAINABILITY inform the trade-off.
  • "Protect your model continuously" translates to six concrete runtime controls, including #PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING and #INPUT SEGREGATION.
  • Operation means monitoring both behaviour (#CONTINUOUS VALIDATION, #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING) and inputs (#MONITOR USE, #DETECT ODD INPUT, #DETECT ADVERSARIAL INPUT).
Module 03 · § How about copyright? · OWASP AI Exchange

AI & Copyright: Training Data, Generated Code, Lawsuits and Indemnification

~16 minCovers guide pp. 75–79

Copyright is where AI security meets the courtroom. Training sets scraped from the web may contain protected works; generated output may resemble them; and nobody is entirely sure who owns what an AI produces. This lesson gives you the guide's full treatment: the legal state of play, the security angle, the landmark lawsuits, the special case of AI-generated source code, vendor indemnification, the "do models actually copy?" question, and ten concrete mitigation strategies you can start this quarter.

The legal state of play

AI and copyright are two (of many) areas of law and policy — both public and private — that raise complex and often unresolved questions. Three anchor facts from the guide:

  • AI output or generated content is not yet protected by US copyright laws. Many other jurisdictions have yet to announce any formal status as to intellectual property protections for such materials.
  • The human contributor who provides the input content, text, training data, etc. may own a copyright for such materials.
  • The usage of certain copyrighted materials in AI training may be considered fair use — a legal doctrine that can permit limited use of protected works.
THREAT
Copyright infringement via training dataProtected works inserted into training data without the owner's permission — and without consent of the AI model operator or provider — create financial and reputational infringement risk and can corrupt the entire data set. Lifecycle: development-time. Related: OWASP LLM02 (sensitive/licensed data in output), LLM03 (supply chain).

AI & copyright security

In AI, companies face a myriad of security threats that could have far-reaching implications for intellectual property rights, particularly copyrights. As AI systems — including large data training models — become more sophisticated, they inadvertently raise the specter of copyright infringement. This is due in part to the need for development and training of AI models that process vast amounts of data, which may contain copyright works. If copyright works were inserted into the training data without the permission of the owner, and without consent of the AI model operator or provider, such a breach could pose significant financial and reputational risk of infringement — and corrupt the entire data set itself.

The legal challenges are multifaceted. On one hand: does the use of copyrighted works to train AI models constitute infringement, potentially exposing developers to legal claims? On the other: the majority of the industry grapples with the ownership of AI-generated works and the use of unlicensed content in training data. This legal ambiguity affects all stakeholders — developers, content creators, and copyright owners alike.

The lawsuits

Recent lawsuits (the guide's writing is April 2024) highlight the urgency:

  • A class action against Stability AI, Midjourney, and DeviantArt alleges infringement of the rights of millions of artists by training their tools on web-scraped images.
  • Getty Images v. Stability AI: using images from Getty's catalog without permission to train an art-generating AI — showing how copyright disputes can escalate.
  • The guide's thought experiment: imagine a supplier providing vast quantities of training data for your systems that has been compromised by protected work, data sets, or blocks of materials not licensed or authorized for such use. The supply-chain risk is yours.

Copyright of AI-generated source code

Source code constitutes a significant intellectual property asset of a software development company — it embodies the innovation and creativity of its developers, and is protected through copyrights, patents, and trade secrets. In most cases, human-generated source code carries copyright status as soon as it is produced.

AI systems capable of generating source code without human input break the model. Open questions the guide poses: Who is the author of the AI-generated source code? Who can claim the IP rights over it? How can it be licensed and exploited by third parties? The current IP legal and regulatory framework does not adequately address the IP status of AI-generated works. Furthermore, AI-generated code may not be entirely novel — it may be derived from existing code or data sources. The guide's prescription: conduct a thorough analysis of the origin and process of the AI-generated source code to determine its IP implications, and consult legal professionals specializing in IP and technology.

▸ Worked example

The live case the guide cites: a suit filed against GitHub, OpenAI, and Microsoft by creators of certain code who claim the three entities violated their rights — the GitHub Copilot copyright case, covered by The Register as "GitHub Copilot copyright case narrowed but not neutered". "Narrowed but not neutered" is the key phrase: courts dismissed parts of the claim but let core questions proceed, so the legal exposure of code-generation tooling remained unresolved at the guide's time of writing.

Copyright damages indemnification

AI vendors have started to take responsibility for copyright issues of their models — under certain circumstances:

  • Microsoft — Copilot Copyright Commitment: indemnifies users from legal damages regarding copyright of code that Copilot has produced, provided a number of things — including that the client has used the content filters and other safety systems in Copilot and uses specific services.
  • Google Cloud — Generative AI indemnification.

Further reading named by the guide: The Verge on Microsoft indemnification, and Direction Microsoft on the requirements of the indemnification.

◆ Senior insight

Indemnification is a risk-transfer control (in risk-treatment terms: transfer), but read the conditions like a security engineer: Microsoft's commitment is void if you disabled the content filters. That turns a "legal" clause into an operational configuration requirement — someone must verify, continuously, that the qualifying safety systems are on and that you are on the covered services. An indemnity you silently disqualified yourself from is worse than none, because leadership believes the risk is covered.

Do generative AI models really copy existing work?

In essence: no. A generative AI model does not have sufficient capacity to store all the examples of code or pictures in its training set. Instead, during training it extracts patterns about how things work in the data it sees, and later generates new content based on those patterns. Parts of that content may show remnants of existing work, but that is more of a coincidence. The model doesn't recall exact blocks of code — it uses its "understanding" of coding to create new code. Just as with human beings, this understanding may result in reproducing parts of something seen before, but not per se from exact memory.

The guide is honest that this remains a difficult discussion, and offers the music-industry analogy: did a musician come up with a chord sequence because she learned from many songs that this type of sequence works — coincidentally recreating something that already existed — or did she copy it exactly from that existing song?

⚠ Common misconception

Both extremes are wrong. "The model is a database of stolen works" fails technically — the parameters cannot store the training set verbatim at that scale. But "therefore output can never infringe" also fails: remnants of existing work can surface in output, and infringement analysis looks at the output's similarity, not at the model's storage mechanism. This is precisely why mitigation strategy 1 (limit training-data disclosure in output) and the vendors' content filters exist.

Mitigating risk — ten strategies

Organizations have several key strategies to mitigate copyright-infringement risk in their AI systems. Implementing them early can be much more cost-effective than fixing at later stages of AI system operations. Each carries financial and operating costs, but the "hard savings" may result in a positive outcome:

  1. Mitigate the output of certain training data. The OWASP AI Exchange covers this through the corresponding threat: sensitive data disclosure in model output.
  2. Comprehensive IP audits: identify all intellectual property related to the AI system as a whole — not only data sets but overall source code, systems, applications, interfaces and other tech stacks.
  3. Clear legal framework and policy: develop and enforce legal policies and procedures for AI use, aligned with current IP laws including copyright.
  4. Ethics in data sourcing: use only data that is created in-house, obtained with all necessary permissions, or sourced from public domains providing sufficient license for the organization's intended use.
  5. Define AI-generated content ownership: clearly define ownership of AI-generated content, including under what conditions it can be used, shared, disseminated.
  6. Confidentiality and trade secret protocols: strict protocols to protect confidentiality of materials while preserving and maintaining trade secret status.
  7. Training for employees: train staff on the organization's AI IP policies and the implications of IP infringement, to make behaviour more risk-averse.
  8. Compliance monitoring systems: an updated, properly utilized monitoring system to check against potential infringements by the AI system.
  9. Response planning for IP infringement: an active plan to respond quickly and effectively to any potential infringement claims.
  10. Licenses and warranties from AI suppliers regarding the organization's intended use and all future uses by the AI system — plus, with legal counsel, other contractually binding obligations on suppliers to cover potential infringement claims.
🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Map copyright exposure into the existing risk register — it is a supply-chain and output-handling risk, not only a legal abstraction.
  • Verify indemnification prerequisites operationally (filters on, covered services) and alert on drift.
  • Gate training/fine-tuning data intake on provenance: in-house, permissioned, or sufficiently licensed public domain.
  • Apply output controls for disclosure of training data (the guide's sensitive-data-disclosure-in-output threat) to licensed content too.
  • Keep an infringement-response runbook alongside the security incident-response plan.

Helpful resources regarding AI and copyright

  • Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Copyright — Copyright Alliance
  • AI industry faces threat of copyright law in 2024 — Digital Watch Observatory
  • Using generative AI and protecting against copyright issues — World Economic Forum (weforum.org)
  • Legal Challenges Against Generative AI: Key Takeaways — Bipartisan Policy Center
  • Generative AI Has an Intellectual Property Problem — hbr.org
  • Recent Trends in Generative Artificial Intelligence Litigation in the United States — HUB, K&L Gates (klgates.com)
  • Generative AI could face its biggest legal tests in 2024 — Popular Science (popsci.com)
  • Is AI Model Training Compliant With Data Privacy Laws? — termly.io
  • The current legal cases against generative AI are just the beginning — TechCrunch
  • (Un)fair Use? Copyrighted Works as AI Training Data — AI: The Washington Report — Mintz
  • Potential Supreme Court clash looms over copyright issues in generative AI training data — VentureBeat
  • AI-Related Lawsuits: How The Stable Diffusion Case Could Set a Legal Precedent — Fieldfisher
Is AI-generated content protected by US copyright, and who might hold copyright in an AI pipeline?
No — AI output/generated content is not yet protected by US copyright law, and many jurisdictions have announced no formal status. But the human contributor who provides input content, text or training data may own copyright in those materials, and training use of protected works may or may not qualify as fair use.
Why does the guide answer "no" to "do generative models copy existing work?" — and why doesn't that end the debate?
Because a model lacks the capacity to store its training examples; it extracts patterns and generates new content, so resemblance is more coincidence than recall. The debate persists because remnants of existing work can still appear in output — the musician/chord-sequence analogy: learned patterns can coincidentally recreate an existing work, and courts judge the output, not the mechanism.
Which of the ten mitigation strategies is also a named AI Exchange security threat/control pairing?
Strategy 1 — taking measures to mitigate the output of certain training data — corresponds to the AI Exchange threat "sensitive data disclosure in model output" and its controls. It shows copyright risk and security controls converging on the same mechanism: keeping training data out of output.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 75–79, permalink owaspai.org/go/copyright/. Named cases: class action v. Stability AI/Midjourney/DeviantArt; Getty Images v. Stability AI; the GitHub/OpenAI/Microsoft Copilot case ("narrowed but not neutered", The Register). Vendor programs: Microsoft Copilot Copyright Commitment; Google Cloud Generative AI indemnification. Output of licensed data also maps to OWASP LLM02 (Sensitive Information Disclosure).

Key Points

  • US copyright does not (yet) protect AI-generated content; human contributors may hold copyright in inputs and training data; training use may be fair use — all unresolved terrain.
  • Unpermissioned copyrighted works in training data create financial/reputational infringement risk and can corrupt the data set itself.
  • Landmark suits: artists' class action v. Stability AI/Midjourney/DeviantArt; Getty v. Stability AI; Copilot case v. GitHub/OpenAI/Microsoft.
  • AI-generated source code has unclear authorship, ownership and licensing — analyse origin and process, involve IP counsel.
  • Vendor indemnification (Microsoft Copilot Copyright Commitment, Google Cloud) is conditional — e.g. content filters and specific services must be in use.
  • Models extract patterns rather than store works; remnants in output are possible, so the question stays hard (music analogy).
  • Ten mitigations, from output controls (= sensitive data disclosure in output) and IP audits to ethical sourcing, ownership definitions, training, monitoring, response planning and supplier licenses/warranties — cheaper early than late.
Module 03 · § 1. General controls → 1.1 Governance · OWASP AI Exchange

From Discussion to Action: General Controls and the #AI PROGRAM

~10 minCovers guide pp. 80–81

Everything so far in this module has been orientation — heuristic AI, trustworthiness, GenAI deltas, government guidance, copyright. Page 80 is where the guide switches from discussion to prescription: the General controls chapter opens, its first subgroup is general governance controls, and the first control of the entire catalogue is #AI PROGRAM — the control that makes an organization take responsibility for AI at all. This lesson covers that opening; the guide's Quickstart continues in the next module.

Where we are in the guide's structure

1. General controls (permalink owaspai.org/go/generalcontrols/) is the group of controls that apply universally — regardless of which specific threat you are treating. Its first subgroup, 1.1 General governance controls (permalink owaspai.org/go/governancecontrols/), holds the organizational controls. Governance is first for a reason: without an inventory and assigned responsibility, no technical control gets selected, funded, or verified.

#AI PROGRAMInstall and execute a program to govern AIgovernance · general controls
IntentTake responsibility for AI as an organization and make sure that all AI initiatives are known and under control — including their security. One could argue this control is out of scope for cybersecurity, but it initiates the action to get in control of AI security. It covers not just risks TO AI (such as security risks) but also risks BY AI (threats to fairness, safety, etc.).
