ImplementThe governance challenge seems daunting, but numerous existing organizational controls can be extended to include AI (policies, risk analysis, impact analysis, inventory of used services, etc.). See the guide's "How to organize AI security" for the 5 GUARD steps and where governance fits. An AI Program includes: keeping an inventory of AI initiatives; performing impact analysis on initiatives; organizing AI innovation; including AI risks in risk management; assigning responsibilities (e.g. model accountability, data accountability, risk governance); AI literacy (e.g. training); organizing compliance; and incorporating AI assets in the security program.
Impact analysisWhen analysing an AI initiative, consider at least: laws and regulations — the application type may be prohibited (e.g. social scoring under the EU AI Act; see #CHECKCOMPLIANCE); can required transparency into how the AI works be provided; can privacy rights be achieved (access, erase, correct, update personal data, and the right to object); can unwanted bias regarding protected groups be sufficiently mitigated; is AI really needed to solve the problem; is the right expertise available (e.g. data scientists); is it allowed to use the data for the purpose — especially personal data collected for a different purpose; can unwanted behaviour be sufficiently contained by mitigations (see Controls to limit unwanted behaviour); and see Risk management under #SECPROGRAM for security-specific risk analysis, also involving privacy.
QuickstartA typical first iteration of AI governance starts with: 1. Raise attention and awareness at board level, when needed. (The Quickstart list continues on guide p. 82 — picked up in the next module.)
LimitsA program is an enabler, not a technical mitigation: it produces inventory, accountability and decisions, but every identified risk still needs its concrete controls. Its cybersecurity classification is debatable — the guide keeps it because it initiates control of AI security.
StandardsPermalink owaspai.org/go/aiprogram/; EU AI Act (prohibited practices, e.g. social scoring); ties into #SECPROGRAM (risk management) and #CHECKCOMPLIANCE.
◆ Senior insight

The guide's "risks TO AI vs risks BY AI" framing is the single most useful sentence to bring to an AI governance kickoff. Security teams instinctively scope to risks to AI (poisoning, theft, injection). The AI Program must also own risks by AI (unfairness, unsafety, privacy violation, prohibited use) — otherwise those land nowhere. The impact-analysis checklist doubles as a go/no-go filter: note that two of its questions ("Is AI really needed?" and "Can unwanted behaviour be sufficiently contained?") echo the empathy principle from the responsible AI lesson — sometimes the right control is not building the system.

▸ Worked example

Impact analysis in action: a team proposes an AI tool scoring employees' "collaboration quality" from chat logs. Walking the checklist: social-scoring-adjacent under the EU AI Act (legal review required); chat logs were collected for communication, not evaluation (purpose limitation problem); transparency into scoring is hard for a fine-tuned LLM; bias against non-native speakers is plausible and hard to mitigate; and simpler non-AI metrics may exist ("Is AI really needed?"). The program's value: this dies in review for a few hours of analysis rather than in production with regulatory exposure.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 80–81. Permalinks: go/generalcontrols/, go/governancecontrols/, go/aiprogram/. Canon: this control corresponds to the NIST AI RMF GOVERN function (policies, accountability, inventory) and to ISO/IEC 42001 (AI management systems); the EU AI Act's prohibited-practices list (Art. 5, e.g. social scoring) is the hard legal boundary the impact analysis must check first.

Why does the guide include #AI PROGRAM in a security document if it is arguably out of cybersecurity scope?
Because it initiates the action that gets an organization in control of AI security: without an inventory of AI initiatives, assigned responsibilities, and risk-management integration, no downstream security control gets systematically applied. It is the enabler for everything else in the catalogue.
Give four of the impact-analysis questions an AI Program should ask about a new initiative.
Any four of: is the application type legally prohibited (e.g. EU AI Act social scoring); can required transparency be provided; can privacy rights (access, erase, correct, update, object) be achieved; can unwanted bias against protected groups be mitigated; is AI really needed; is the right expertise available; is the data allowed for this purpose; can unwanted behaviour be contained by mitigations.

Key Points

  • General controls apply universally; their first subgroup is governance, and its first control is #AI PROGRAM.
  • #AI PROGRAM objective: the organization takes responsibility for AI — all initiatives known and under control, including security.
  • Extend existing organizational machinery (policies, risk analysis, impact analysis, service inventories) rather than building from scratch; see the 5 GUARD steps.
  • Program contents: inventory, impact analysis, innovation organisation, AI risks in risk management, assigned responsibilities (model/data accountability, risk governance), AI literacy, compliance, AI assets in the security program.
  • Impact analysis covers risks TO AI and risks BY AI — legality (EU AI Act), transparency, privacy rights, bias, necessity of AI, expertise, data-purpose legitimacy, containability of unwanted behaviour.
  • Quickstart step 1: raise attention and awareness at board level, when needed — the rest of the quickstart continues in the next module.
Module 04 · §1 / §1.1 · OWASP AI Exchange

The governance landscape: why AI security starts in the boardroom

~15 minCovers guide pp. 67–72, 80

Every technical AI security control you will ever deploy — input filters, poisoning defences, model access control — only exists if somebody in the organization decided it should. This lesson positions AI security inside the broader responsible-AI landscape, shows what is genuinely new about GenAI risk, and opens Section 1 of the guide: the general controls that apply to all AI threats, starting with governance.

Where security sits in responsible AI

The guide closes its responsible/trustworthy-AI discussion (details at owaspai.org/goto/responsibleai/) with a set of aspects and how each relates to security. You need these definitions because governance controls constantly refer to them — an AI program governs all of these concerns, not only security:

  • Transparency: sharing information about the approach, to warn users and depending systems of accuracy risks; in many cases users have the right to know details about a model being used and how it was created. A shared concern between security, privacy and safety.
  • Explainability: sharing information to help users validate accuracy by explaining in more detail how a specific result came to be. Beyond validating accuracy, it supports transparency and helps users understand what needs to change to get a different outcome. Shared between security, privacy, safety and business function — and when explainability is required by law separately from privacy, compliance joins the list.
  • Robustness: the ability to maintain accuracy under expected or unexpected variations in input. The security scope covers malicious variations (adversarial robustness), which often needs different countermeasures than normal variations (generalization robustness). Security is not involved per se in robustness for normal variations — when it is involved, it becomes a shared concern between safety and security, and which discipline leads depends on the case.
  • Free of discrimination: no unwanted bias of protected attributes — no systematic inaccuracy where the model ‘mistreats’ certain groups (e.g. gender, ethnicity). Discrimination is undesired for legal and ethical reasons. The security link: detection of unwanted bias can reveal an attack. Example: a data poisoning attack inserts malicious samples into the training set, goes unnoticed, and is later discovered through an unexplained detection of bias in the model. The term ‘fairness’ is sometimes used for discrimination issues, but in privacy it is a broader term covering fair treatment of individuals, including transparency, ethical use, and privacy rights.
  • Empathy: recognizing the practical limits of what security can achieve. If individuals or organizations cannot be adequately protected, empathy means rethinking the idea — rejecting it altogether or adding precautions to reduce potential harm.
  • Accountability: security measures should be demonstrable, including the processes that led to them. Traceability as a security property matters here just as in any IT system — to detect, reconstruct and respond to incidents and provide accountability.
  • AI security: the central topic of the AI Exchange. It breaks down into: input attacks (performed by providing input to the model), model poisoning (aimed at altering the model’s behavior), stealing AI assets (train data, model input, output, or the model itself — development-time or runtime), and further runtime conventional security attacks.
flowchart LR
  subgraph MQ["Model quality"]
    PERF["Performance - precision etc."]
    GEN["Generalization robustness"]
    DISC["Discrimination bias - fairness, privacy"]
    ADV["Adversarial robustness - security"]
  end
  IN1["Input not in the trainset"] --> GEN
  IN2["Input from different protected groups"] --> DISC
  subgraph IA["Input attacks - security"]
    EV["Adversarial input - evasion"]
    TDE["Training data extraction"]
    ME["Model extraction"]
    DOS["Denial of model service"]
    PI["Direct and indirect prompt injection"]
  end
  IA --> AIS["AI system"]
  USE["Lawful and ethical use vs. use to attack"] --> AIS
  subgraph FA["Further attacks"]
    MP["Model poisoning - data, tamper, supply"]
    ST["Stealing traindata, input, output or model"]
    CSA["Conventional security attacks"]
  end
  MP --> AIS
  AIS --> ST
  CSA --> AIS
  AIS --> OUT["Output with some inaccuracy"]
  OUT --> IND["Affected individual - potential harm: privacy, safety"]
  IND -->|"Right to object, correct, request, erase"| AIS
  subgraph INF["Inform"]
    AD["Approach description"]
    MC["Model calculation"]
  end
  AD -->|"Transparency"| IND
  MC -->|"Explainability"| IND

Figure — the “AI wayfinder” (guide p. 68, attributed to SIG — Software Improvement Group). One map showing how model-quality aspects, attack types, information duties and affected individuals relate to an AI system. In the original, colour codes ownership: blue = security, purple = privacy, teal = shared security & privacy, asterisk = fairness; side note: “Bias = systematic inaccuracy”.

A model suddenly shows unexplained bias against one demographic group. Why should the security team be paged, not only the ethics board?
Unwanted bias detection doubles as attack detection: a data poisoning attack that inserted malicious training samples can first surface as an unexplained bias shift. The guide's discrimination bullet gives exactly this scenario — bias monitoring is a shared instrument of fairness and security.

How about Generative AI?

Permalink: owaspai.org/go/genai/. GenAI is leading the current AI revolution and is the fastest-moving subfield of AI security. But other algorithm types — the guide calls them predictive AI — will remain in use for critical work: credit scoring, fraud detection, medical diagnosis, product recommendation, image recognition, predictive maintenance, process control.

Important note: from a security threat perspective, GenAI is not that different from predictive AI. Threats and controls largely overlap. Some risks are (much) higher, some lower, and only a few are GenAI-specific. Control categories that differ most are the data science controls (e.g. adding noise to the training set). Crucially: many GenAI solutions use a model as-is, with no training by the organization at all — shifting some security responsibilities from the organization to the supplier. If you use a ready-made model, you still need to be aware of the threats.

What is mainly new to the threat landscape because of LLMs?

  • LLMs may be used to create code with vulnerabilities, be used by attackers to create malware, or cause harm through hallucinations — but these concerns are outside the scope of the AI Exchange, which focuses on security threats to AI systems themselves.
  • Input: prompt injection — attackers manipulate model behaviour with crafted, sometimes hidden instructions. Also new: organizations sending huge amounts of data in prompts, including company secrets and personal data.
  • Output: output can contain injection attacks, or sensitive/copyrighted data (see the copyright discussion in the compliance lesson).
  • Overreliance: we let LLMs control and create things, trust their correctness too much, and underestimate the risk of them being manipulated — so attacks can have much impact.
  • Training: training sets are so large and public-data-based that data poisoning is easier. Poisoned foundation models are also a big supply chain issue.
  • Actions: like any AI system, GenAI can trigger actions from output — but GenAI output can contain function calls (e.g. send mail) or trigger other AI systems. See the guide’s Agentic AI section.
THREAT
Prompt injection (direct & indirect)Crafted or hidden instructions in natural-language input steer the model into unwanted behaviour. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: OWASP LLM01, MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051.
THREAT
Sensitive information disclosure & input data leakHuge training sets and rich prompt context (in-context learning, RAG) leak data the model has no access-privilege concept for. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: OWASP LLM02.
THREAT
Data and model poisoningManipulated training data alters model behaviour; internet-scale sourcing makes it easier for GenAI. Lifecycle: development-time. Maps to: OWASP LLM04, ATLAS AML.T0020.
THREAT
Supply-chain model poisoningPre-trained models may have been manipulated before you obtained them. Lifecycle: development-time/supply chain. Maps to: OWASP LLM03, ATLAS AML.T0010.
THREAT
Output injection & unbounded consumptionModel output can carry injection payloads (e.g. XSS); overloading costly GenAI models creates unwanted costs. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: OWASP LLM05, LLM10.

GenAI security particularities

The guide summarizes the particularities in a table (guide pp. 70–72), mapped to the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications:

Nr.GenAI security particularityOWASP for LLM Top 10
1GenAI models are controlled by natural language in prompts, creating the risk of Prompt injection. Direct prompt injection is where the user tries to fool the model to behave in unwanted ways (e.g. offensive language), whereas with indirect prompt injection it is a third party that injects content into the prompt for this purpose (e.g. manipulating a decision).LLM01: Prompt injection
2GenAI models have typically been trained on very large datasets, making it more likely to output sensitive or licensed data, for which there is no control of access privileges built into the model. All data will be accessible to the model users. Mechanisms such as system prompts, model alignment, or output filtering may be in place, but those are typically not watertight.LLM02: Sensitive Information Disclosure
3Data and model poisoning is an AI-broad problem; with GenAI the risk is generally higher since training data can be supplied from different sources that may be challenging to control, such as the internet. Attackers could for example hijack domains and place manipulated information.LLM04: Data and Model Poisoning
4GenAI models can be inaccurate and hallucinate. This AI-broad risk factor gets worse because LLMs come across as very confident and knowledgeable. In essence: the risk of underestimating the probability that the model is wrong or has been manipulated. It connects to each and every security control; the strongest link is with controls that limit the impact of unwanted model behavior, in particular Least model privilege.LLM06: Excessive agency; LLM09: Misinformation
5Input data leak: GenAI models mostly live in the cloud — often managed by an external party — increasing the risk of leaking prompts. Two GenAI-particular risks: 1) model use involves user interaction through prompts, adding user data and privacy/sensitivity issues; 2) prompts can contain rich context with sensitive data (e.g. company secrets), which occurs with in-context learning or Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) — for example data from all reports ever written at a consultancy firm. This information travels with the prompt to the cloud, and the system will likely not respect the original access rights to the information.Not covered in LLM top 10
6Pre-trained models may have been manipulated. Pretraining is not limited to GenAI, but the approach is quite common in GenAI, which increases the risk of supply-chain model poisoning.LLM03: Supply chain vulnerabilities
7Model inversion and membership inference are typically low to zero risks for GenAI.Not covered in LLM top 10, apart from LLM06 which uses a different approach
8GenAI output may contain elements that perform an injection attack such as cross-site-scripting.LLM05: Improper Output Handling
9Resource exhaustion can be an issue for any IT system, but GenAI models typically cost more to run, so overloading them can create unwanted costs.LLM10: Unbounded consumption
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 68–72, permalink owaspai.org/go/genai/. GenAI references named in the guide: OWASP LLM top 10, “Demystifying the LLM top 10”, “Impacts and risks of GenAI”, LLMsecurity.net. The LLM Top 10 ids above follow the 2025 list (LLM01 Prompt Injection … LLM10 Unbounded Consumption).

⚠ Common misconception

“GenAI security is a brand-new discipline.” The guide is blunt: GenAI threats and controls largely overlap with AI in general. What changes is the risk weighting (poisoning easier, prompts leakier, output more dangerous) and the responsibility split — using a model as-is shifts duties to the supplier, but never all of them. A governance program that treats GenAI as exempt from “classic AI” controls, or vice versa, has a blind spot.

Section 1: General controls — and the governance subgroup

Permalinks: owaspai.org/go/generalcontrols/ (Home > 1. General controls) and owaspai.org/go/governancecontrols/. General controls apply regardless of which specific threat you face — they are the substrate every threat-specific control grows from. The first subgroup, 1.1 General governance controls, contains six controls:

ControlOne-line missionTaught in
#AI PROGRAMInstall and execute a program to govern AI — make all AI initiatives known and controlled.Lesson 2
#SEC PROGRAMInclude the whole AI lifecycle and AI-specific assets in the information security management system.Lesson 3
#SEC DEV PROGRAMBuild security into AI systems through the secure development lifecycle.Lesson 4
#DEV PROGRAMApply general software engineering best practice to AI development — do not isolate AI engineering.Lesson 4
#CHECK COMPLIANCEGet AI-relevant laws and regulations into compliance management — mind the blind spots.Lesson 5
#SEC EDUCATETeach engineers and security professionals the AI threats and controls that apply to them.Lesson 6
◆ Senior insight

Governance controls have the best economics in the whole guide: one policy decision (e.g. “every AI initiative gets a risk analysis”) scales across every current and future system, while a technical control protects one interface of one system. Attackers exploit unknown systems first — shadow AI that never entered an inventory gets no threat model, no monitoring, no ownership. The cheapest attack surface reduction you will ever buy is a complete inventory.

Your company only consumes a hosted foundation model via API — no training at all. Which security responsibilities shift to the supplier, and which stay?
Development-time concerns (training data protection, poisoning defences during training) shift largely to the supplier. What stays: awareness of those threats, supplier/supply-chain scrutiny, plus everything at your end — prompt handling, input data leakage (your prompts and RAG context travel to their cloud), output handling, and governance of the use case itself. The guide stresses you must still know the threats even for ready-made models.
Why does the guide bother distinguishing adversarial robustness from generalization robustness?
Because they need different countermeasures and different owners. Generalization robustness handles normal input variation — a quality/safety concern the security team is not involved in per se. Adversarial robustness handles malicious variation and is squarely a security concern; when either is involved it becomes a shared safety/security concern, decided case by case.

Key Points

  • Responsible AI aspects — transparency, explainability, robustness, non-discrimination, empathy, accountability — each have a defined relationship to security; bias detection can even reveal poisoning attacks.
  • AI security decomposes into input attacks, model poisoning, stealing AI assets, and runtime conventional attacks.
  • GenAI threats largely overlap with predictive AI; only the risk weighting and responsibility split change. Nine particularities map to the OWASP LLM Top 10.
  • Using a ready-made model shifts some responsibility to the supplier — but you must still know the threats.
  • Section 1 general controls apply to all threats; subgroup 1.1 holds six governance controls that create the conditions for every other control.
  • Prompt injection, sensitive data disclosure, poisoning (incl. supply chain), output injection and unbounded consumption are the headline GenAI-era threats governance must budget for.
Module 04 · §1.1 · OWASP AI Exchange

#AI PROGRAM — taking organizational responsibility for AI

~14 minCovers guide pp. 80–83

Before a single security control can be assigned, someone must know the AI system exists, what it does, and who answers for it. #AI PROGRAM is the control that creates that knowledge. It looks like management overhead — the guide even concedes one could argue it is out of scope for cybersecurity — but it is the ignition switch for AI security: it initiates the action to get in control.

#AI PROGRAMInstall and execute a program to govern AIgovernance · §1.1 general controls
IntentTake responsibility for AI as an organization and make sure all AI initiatives are known and under control, including their security. One could argue it is out of scope for cybersecurity, but it initiates action to get in control of AI security.
ImplementExtend existing organizational controls (policies, risk analysis, impact analysis, inventory of used services) rather than building from scratch. Program elements: keep an inventory of AI initiatives; perform impact analysis on initiatives; organize AI innovation; include AI risks in risk management; assign responsibilities (model accountability, data accountability, risk governance); AI literacy (e.g. training); organize compliance (#CHECK COMPLIANCE); incorporate AI assets in the security program (#SEC PROGRAM). See “How to organize AI security” for the 5 GUARD steps.
LimitsCovers risks TO AI and risks BY AI — but is only as good as the inventory and the impact analysis behind it. Eight AI particularities (inductive, staleness, data-driven, unfamiliar, incomprehensible, new assets, natural-language interaction, sound/vision) must be kept in mind during risk management.
StandardsISO/IEC 42001 AI management system (gap: covers this control fully); US Federal Reserve SR 11-07 Model Risk Management guidance. OpenCRE: AI management system. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/aiprogram/

Implementation: extend, don’t reinvent

The governance challenge may seem daunting because of all the new things to take care of, but organizations already run numerous controls that can be extended to include AI: policies, risk analysis, impact analysis, inventory of used services, and so on. The guide’s “How to organize AI security” section presents the 5 GUARD steps and shows how governance fits into the whole.

An AI Program includes:

  • Keeping an inventory of AI initiatives
  • Performing impact analysis on initiatives
  • Organizing AI innovation
  • Including AI risks in risk management
  • Assigning responsibilities, e.g. model accountability, data accountability, and risk governance
  • AI literacy (e.g. training)
  • Organizing compliance — see #CHECK COMPLIANCE
  • Incorporating AI assets in the security program — see #SEC PROGRAM

The impact-analysis checklist

When doing impact analysis on AI initiatives, consider at least the following. Note the first item carefully: an AI program is not just about risks TO AI (such as security risks) — it is also about risks BY AI, such as threats to fairness and safety.

  • Include laws and regulations — the type of AI application may be outright prohibited (e.g. social scoring under the EU AI Act). See #CHECK COMPLIANCE.
  • Can the required transparency be provided into how the AI works?
  • Can the privacy rights be achieved (right to access, erase, correct, update personal data, and the right to object)?
  • Can unwanted bias regarding protected groups of people be sufficiently mitigated?
  • Is AI really needed to solve the problem?
  • Is the right expertise available (e.g. data scientists)?
  • Is it allowed to use the data for the purpose — especially if it is personal data collected for a different purpose?
  • Can unwanted behaviour be sufficiently contained by mitigations (see the guide’s Controls to limit unwanted behaviour)?
  • See Risk management under #SEC PROGRAM for security-specific risk analysis, also involving privacy.
◆ Senior insight

“Is AI really needed?” is the highest-leverage security question on the list. Every AI component you decline to build removes an entire threat catalogue — poisoning, prompt injection, model theft — at zero control cost. A senior reviewer treats the impact analysis as a gate where the cheapest mitigation (not doing it, or doing it without ML) gets a fair hearing before anyone prices out guardrails.

Quickstart: the first governance iteration

A typical first iteration for AI governance in organizations:

#AI PROGRAM Quickstart — typical first iteration (guide pp. 81–82)
1. Board attentionraise attention and awareness at board level, when needed
↓
2. Stakeholdersform a group of stakeholders and assign responsibilities
↓
3. Laws & regulationsidentify laws and regulations
↓
4. Surveyinventory current AI use, AI ideas, concerns, and individuals with AI expertise
↓
5. Evaluateevaluate these AI applications and ideas
↓
6. Risk analysis + policyperform a risk analysis and establish a first policy
↓
7. Operationalizeimplement policy as much as possible in tools and procedures
↓
8. AI literacyinitiate an AI literacy program, based on the policy implementation plan

Bare minimum start

The very minimum first thing you can do for AI governance, focused on security:

  1. Make an inventory of current AI use and AI ideas.
  2. Perform risk analysis on them to identify threats, controls and who’s responsible for them.
  3. Continue with step 2 of the GUARD program, presented in the guide’s “How to organize” section.
Why does the Quickstart put the survey (step 4) before risk analysis (step 6)?
You cannot analyse risk on systems you don't know exist. The survey builds the inventory of current AI use, ideas, concerns and in-house expertise; risk analysis then operates over that inventory. Reversing the order produces a risk register full of the visible projects while shadow AI stays unmanaged.

Particularity: eight ways AI breaks your risk-management assumptions

In general risk management it may help to keep in mind these particularities of AI:

  1. Inductive instead of deductive — being wrong is part of the game for machine learning models, which can lead to harm.
  2. Connected to 1: models can go stale.
  3. AI organizes its behaviour based on data, so data becomes a source of opportunity (complex real-world problem-solving, adaptability) and of risk (unwanted bias, incompleteness, error, manipulation).
  4. Unfamiliar to organizations and people — risking implementation mistakes, underreliance, overreliance, and incorrect attribution of human tendencies.
  5. Incomprehensible, resulting in trust issues.
  6. New technical assets that form security threats: data/model supply chain, train data, model parameters, augmentation data, AI documentation.
  7. Can listen and speak: communicates through natural language instead of user interfaces.
  8. Can hear and see: sound and vision recognition abilities.
⚔ Attacker's view

Particularities 6–8 are an attacker’s shopping list. New assets (training data, model parameters) mean new theft targets your classic asset register never listed. Natural-language control (particularity 7) means the data channel doubles as the command channel — the root cause of prompt injection. And an organization for which AI is unfamiliar (particularity 4) will misconfigure it, over-trust it, and under-monitor it. Attackers rationally prefer targets where governance lags adoption — the exploit is often just “nobody was responsible”.

Standards: ISO/IEC 42001 vs ISO/IEC 5338

The guide draws a sharp line between the two big AI standards you will meet in this module:

ISO/IEC 42001 — AI management systemISO/IEC 5338 — AI lifecycle
What it extendsYour risk management system — governance focusYour software lifecycle practices — engineering focus (and everything around it)
AnalogyA management system for governance of responsible AI, similar to how ISO 27001 is a management system for information securityExtends ISO 12207 (software lifecycle) with AI-specific processes — see Lesson 4
What it does not coverLifecycle processes: how to train models, data lineage, continuous validation, versioning of AI models, project planning challenges, and how/when exactly sensitive data is used in engineeringNot a governance/management system standard
Maps to control#AI PROGRAM (gap: covers fully)#DEV PROGRAM (gap: covers fully)
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 80–83, permalink owaspai.org/go/aiprogram/. References named: OpenCRE “AI management system”, the AI Governance library, UNESCO on AI ethics and governance, and the GenAI security project’s LLM AI Cybersecurity & Governance checklist. Standards: ISO/IEC 42001 (gap: covers this control fully); US Federal Reserve SR 11-07 — supervisory guidance on Model Risk Management for banking organizations, notable as pre-GenAI proof that model inventories and model risk governance are established regulatory practice. In NIST AI RMF terms, #AI PROGRAM implements the GOVERN function (GOVERN 1–6: policies, accountability structures, workforce, culture).

▸ Worked example

A bank already runs SR 11-07-style model risk management for credit models: model inventory, validation, documented accountability. Its #AI PROGRAM does not start from zero — it extends that inventory to cover a new LLM-based customer-service pilot, adds the impact-analysis questions (is the use allowed? can transparency and privacy rights be delivered? can unwanted behaviour be contained?), assigns model and data accountability, and routes the security risks into the ISMS. That is exactly the “extend existing controls” pattern the guide prescribes.

Your CISO says: “an AI governance program is a compliance thing, not a security control.” What is the guide’s counter?
The guide anticipates this: one could argue #AI PROGRAM is out of scope for cybersecurity, but it initiates the action to get in control of AI security. No inventory means no risk analysis, no assigned responsibility, no security-program coverage of AI assets. Every downstream control in the guide presumes this one fired first.
Name three of the eight AI particularities and one concrete risk each creates.
Examples: inductive nature — being wrong is inherent, so harm can occur without any attacker; data-driven behaviour — data becomes a manipulation target (poisoning); natural-language control — instructions can hide in data (prompt injection); new technical assets — train data and model parameters become theft targets missing from classic asset registers.

Key Points

  • #AI PROGRAM makes the organization take responsibility for AI: all initiatives known and under control, including security.
  • Extend existing controls (policies, risk analysis, inventories) to AI — do not build a parallel bureaucracy. The 5 GUARD steps show how it fits together.
  • Eight program elements: inventory, impact analysis, innovation, risk management, responsibilities, AI literacy, compliance, security program.
  • Impact analysis covers risks TO AI and risks BY AI — legality (EU AI Act prohibitions), transparency, privacy rights, bias, necessity, expertise, data purpose, containment.
  • Quickstart: board awareness → stakeholders → laws → survey → evaluate → risk analysis + policy → tooling → literacy. Bare minimum: inventory, risk analysis, GUARD step 2.
  • Eight AI particularities (inductive, stale, data-driven, unfamiliar, incomprehensible, new assets, speaks, sees) reshape risk management.
  • ISO/IEC 42001 covers this control fully (governance); ISO/IEC 5338 is its engineering counterpart; SR 11-07 is the banking precedent.
Module 04 · §1.1 · OWASP AI Exchange

#SEC PROGRAM — putting AI inside the ISMS

~15 minCovers guide pp. 83–87

Your organization (hopefully) already runs a security program — an information security management system. #SEC PROGRAM says: do not build a second one for AI. Instead, feed the ISMS the twelve AI-specific asset types and the threats against them, and let its existing machinery — risk analysis, awareness, monitoring, incident response — do what it already does well. You will also meet the guide’s most creative detection idea: AI-specific honeypots.

#SEC PROGRAMInclude AI in the security program (ISMS)governance · §1.1 general controls
IntentEnsure adequate mitigation of AI security risks through information security management: the security program takes responsibility for the AI-specific threats and corresponding risks, covering the whole AI lifecycle and AI-specific aspects.
ImplementInclude the twelve AI-specific assets (training/validation/test data, model parameters, hyperparameters, documentation, model input/output, intended behaviour, externally obtained data and models, augmentation data) and the threats to them. Run a privacy and security risk analysis for every AI initiative, new and existing, guided by security goals (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) and privacy goals (Unlinkability, Transparency, Intervenability — ISO/IEC TR 27562:2023). Consider AI-specific honeypots. Extend monitoring and incident response with AI assets, threats and detection mechanisms.
LimitsExisting ISMS standards (ISO 27000–27005) know nothing of the three AI-specific attack surfaces: 1) AI development-time attacks including supply chain, 2) input attacks, 3) runtime conventional attacks — you must add these. Outcome-based regulation (EU AI Act) requires assurance processes on top of a control-focused ISMS.
StandardsISO/IEC 27000–27005 (gap: fully, with the attack-surface particularity); ISO 27002 controls 5.1, 5.8, 5.10; ISO/IEC 27005, 27563:2023, 23894, 24028, 5338; MITRE ATLAS; NIST AI RMF; NIST SP 800-53; NIST CSF; ETSI TVRA; OpenCRE security program management & security risk analysis. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/secprogram/

The twelve AI-specific assets

Make sure to include AI-specific assets and the threats to them (the threats are what the rest of the AI Exchange covers). The assets are:

  • training data
  • validation data
  • test data
  • the model — often referred to as model parameters: the values that change when a model is trained
  • hyperparameters — the configuration values of the training process
  • documentation of models and of the process of their development, including experiments
  • model input
  • model output — which needs to be regarded as untrusted if the training data or model is untrusted
  • intended model behaviour
  • data to train and test obtained from external sources
  • models to train and use from external sources
  • augmentation data that is automatically inserted into the model input (e.g. documents, system prompts)

By incorporating these assets and their threats, the security program takes care of mitigating the risks. The guide gives two concrete examples of what that looks like in practice: informing engineers in awareness training that they should not leave their documentation lying around; and installing malware detection on engineer machines because of the high sensitivity of the training data they work with.

⚠ Common misconception

“Model output is our own system’s data, so it’s trusted.” The asset list says otherwise: output must be regarded as untrusted if the training data or model is untrusted. A poisoned model or a prompt-injected LLM produces attacker-influenced output — treat it with the same suspicion as user input (this is also why OWASP LLM05, Improper Output Handling, exists).

Risk analysis for every AI initiative

Every AI initiative, new and existing, should perform a privacy and security risk analysis. AI programs have additional privacy and security concerns; while each implementation differs by contextual purpose, the same process applies, and it can run before development to guide the controls for the system. Controls are grounded in security protection goals — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — and privacy goals — Unlinkability, Transparency, Intervenability. ISO/IEC TR 27562:2023 provides a detailed list of points of attention for these goals and their coverage.

The general process for an AI Use Case Privacy and Security Analysis:

AI Use Case Privacy and Security Analysis — general process (guide p. 85)
1Describe the ecosystem
→
2Assess the system of interest
→
3Identify security & privacy concerns
→
4Identify security & privacy risks
→
5Identify security & privacy controls
→
6Identify assurance concerns

AI-specific honeypots

Because AI has specific assets (e.g. training data), AI-specific honeypots are a particularly interesting control: fake parts of the data/model/data-science infrastructure exposed on purpose, to detect or capture attackers before they reach the real assets. The guide’s examples:

  • Hardened data services, but with an unpatched vulnerability (e.g. Elasticsearch)
  • Exposed data lakes, not revealing details of the actual assets
  • Data access APIs vulnerable to brute-force attacks
  • “Mirror” data servers that resemble development facilities, but are exposed in production with SSH access and labeled with names like “lab”
  • Documentation ‘accidentally’ exposed, directing to a honeypot
  • Data science Python library exposed on the server
  • External access granted to a specific library
  • Models imported as-is from GitHub
⚔ Attacker's view

Honeypots exploit attacker economics. An attacker hunting AI assets must first perform reconnaissance — find the data lake, the model registry, the “lab” server. Every fake asset you plant raises their search cost and gives you a high-fidelity alarm: nobody legitimate touches a decoy, so a single hit is near-zero-false-positive detection. The defender pays once to build the decoy; the attacker pays on every engagement — and reveals their tradecraft when they do. MITRE Engage catalogues this deception discipline; the guide’s contribution is the AI-flavoured decoy list.

Why is a honeypot hit a better alert than an IDS signature match?
A decoy has zero legitimate users, so any interaction is by definition suspicious — near-zero false positives. Signature-based alerts fire on legitimate-but-odd traffic constantly. The honeypot also engages the attacker before they touch real assets, buying response time and intelligence.

Monitoring, incident response, and outcome-based regulation

Monitoring and incident response are standard security-program elements; AI joins them once the organization understands the relevant AI security assets, threats, and controls. The guide’s threat discussions include detection mechanisms that become part of monitoring.

One structural warning: new regulation can be outcome-based (e.g. the EU AI Act) instead of control-focused (like ISO/IEC 27001). This makes it important to extend the ISMS with assurance processes for showing that risks have been sufficiently mitigated — it is no longer enough to show the controls exist; you must show they achieve the outcome.

◆ Senior insight

Control-focused compliance asks “is the control implemented?” — auditable with a checklist. Outcome-based regulation asks “is the risk actually mitigated?” — which requires evidence: test results, continuous-validation metrics, red-team findings. Budget for the evidence pipeline, not just the controls. This is where #SEC PROGRAM meets the testing and continuous-validation controls elsewhere in the guide.

Standards landscape for #SEC PROGRAM

The entire ISO 27000–27005 range applies to AI systems in the general sense, as they are IT systems. Gap: covers this control fully regarding the processes, with the high-level particularity that three AI-specific attack surfaces must be added to information security management: 1) AI development-time attacks including supply chain, 2) input attacks, and 3) runtime conventional security attacks. The range:

  • ISO/IEC 27000 — ISMS overview and vocabulary
  • ISO/IEC 27001 — ISMS requirements
  • ISO/IEC 27002 — code of practice for information security management
  • ISO/IEC 27003 — ISMS implementation guidelines
  • ISO/IEC 27004 — information security management measurements
  • ISO/IEC 27005 — information security risk management

The ‘27002 controls’ cited throughout the guide are listed in the Annex of ISO 27001 and detailed with practices in ISO 27002. At high abstraction, the most relevant ones here are: 5.1 Policies for information security, 5.10 Acceptable use of information and other associated assets, and 5.8 Information security in project management. See also OpenCRE on security program management.

Risk analysis standards (guide p. 87):

  • The AI Exchange itself contains AI security threats and controls to facilitate risk analysis
  • MITRE ATLAS framework for AI threats
  • ISO/IEC 27005 — gap: covers this control fully, with the said particularity (27005 doesn’t mention AI-specific threats)
  • ISO/IEC 27563:2023 (AI use cases security & privacy) — discusses the impact of security and privacy in AI use cases; useful input to AI security risk analysis. It bases its list on the 132 use cases across 22 application domains of ISO/IEC TR 24030:2021 and identifies 11 use cases with maximum concern rating for security and 49 with maximum concern rating for privacy.
  • ISO/IEC 23894 (AI risk management) — gap: covers this control fully. It refers to ISO/IEC 24028 (AI trustworthiness) for AI security threats; however, 24028 is not as comprehensive as the AI Exchange or MITRE ATLAS, being focused on risk management rather than threat enumeration.
  • ISO/IEC 5338 (AI lifecycle) covers the AI risk management process — gap: same as 23894.
  • ETSI Method and pro forma for Threat, Vulnerability, Risk Analysis (TVRA)
  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework
  • OpenCRE on security risk analysis
  • NIST SP 800-53 for general security/privacy controls
  • NIST Cybersecurity Framework
  • GenAI security project — LLM and GenAI Security Center of Excellence guide
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 83–87, permalink owaspai.org/go/secprogram/. In NIST AI RMF terms this control operationalizes MAP (context/risks identified) and MANAGE (risks prioritized and treated) on top of GOVERN. MITRE ATLAS is the AI analogue of ATT&CK — use it in step 4 of the use-case analysis to enumerate techniques such as AML.T0010 (ML Supply Chain Compromise) against your twelve assets.

Name the three AI-specific attack surfaces the ISO 27000 range doesn’t know about.
1) AI development-time attacks including the supply chain (poisoning of data/models), 2) input attacks (evasion, prompt injection, extraction through use), and 3) runtime conventional security attacks against AI-specific runtime assets. The 27000-series processes still work — but these surfaces must be explicitly added.
Why are hyperparameters and documentation on the asset list — they aren’t data or the model?
Both leak attack-relevant knowledge. Documentation of models, development processes and experiments tells an attacker exactly how the model was built and where its weaknesses lie (hence the awareness-training example: don't leave documentation lying around). Hyperparameters describe the training configuration — valuable for replicating or attacking the model, and part of its intellectual property.

Key Points

  • #SEC PROGRAM extends the existing ISMS to the whole AI lifecycle — no parallel AI-security bureaucracy.
  • Twelve AI-specific assets, from training data through hyperparameters to augmentation data; model output is untrusted if the model or its data is untrusted.
  • Every AI initiative gets a privacy + security risk analysis: CIA goals plus Unlinkability, Transparency, Intervenability (ISO/IEC TR 27562:2023), via a six-step use-case process.
  • AI-specific honeypots (fake data lakes, “lab” servers, exposed libraries, GitHub-imported models) detect attackers before they reach real assets, with near-zero false positives.
  • ISO 27000–27005 covers the processes fully but you must add three AI attack surfaces: development-time (incl. supply chain), input attacks, runtime conventional attacks.
  • Outcome-based regulation (EU AI Act) demands assurance processes proving risks are mitigated — not just that controls exist.
  • Risk-analysis toolbox: MITRE ATLAS, ISO 27005/27563/23894/24028/5338, ETSI TVRA, NIST AI RMF, SP 800-53, NIST CSF.
Module 04 · §1.1 · OWASP AI Exchange

#SEC DEV PROGRAM & #DEV PROGRAM — engineering discipline for AI

~18 minCovers guide pp. 87–92

Data scientists are hired to make models work, not to write future-proof software. These two sibling controls fix that: #DEV PROGRAM brings general software-engineering best practice to AI development, and #SEC DEV PROGRAM builds security into it. The guide’s message is emphatic and repeated: do not isolate AI engineering — extend the practices you already have.

#SEC DEV PROGRAM: security built in, not bolted on

#SEC DEV PROGRAMSecure development program including AIgovernance · §1.1 general controls
IntentReduce security risks by proper attention to mitigating those risks during software development: have processes in place so that security is built into your AI system.
ImplementBuild on existing secure software development practices and include AI teams and AI particularities — data science development becomes part of the secure SDLC. Practices: secure development training, code review, security requirements, secure coding guidelines, threat modeling (including AI-specific threats), static analysis tooling, dynamic analysis tooling, penetration testing. There is no need for an isolated secure development framework for AI.
LimitsAI-specific controls mostly require deep AI expertise — technical teams depend on AI engineers for them. Standard application security testing tools miss AI-pipeline risks without added controls. ISO 27002 control 8.25 covers this fully but in one page — far too little detail for such a large topic.
StandardsISO 27002 8.25 Secure development lifecycle (gap: fully, with said particularity, but lacking detail); ISO/IEC 27115 (Cybersecurity evaluation of complex systems); OpenCRE secure software development processes, with notable links to NIST SSDF and OWASP SAMM (gap: fully, with said particularity). Permalink: owaspai.org/go/secdevprogram/

Particularities: what AI changes in the secure SDLC

  • New engineering, new engineers. AI adds data engineering and model engineering (e.g. model training), performed by data scientists, data engineers, AI engineers. Make this new engineering an integral part of the general #DEV PROGRAM with its best practices (versioning, portfolio management, retirement). Example: version management/traceability of the combination of code, configuration, training data and models — for troubleshooting and rollback.
  • New assets, threats and controls affect requirements, policies, coding guidelines, training, tooling, testing practices and more. Usually this is done by adding these elements to the ISMS (as described in #SEC PROGRAM) and aligning secure development to that — just as it was aligned on conventional assets, threats and controls. Both conventional and AI-specific threats apply, met with both conventional and AI-specific controls. Technical teams typically depend on AI engineers for the AI-specific controls, as they mostly require deep AI expertise. Example: if training data is confidential and collected in a distributed way, a federated learning approach may be considered.
  • Supply chain includes data and models, which may have been poisoned — which is why data provenance and model management are central in AI supply chain management.
  • Software runs in development too — tools to prepare training data or train a model. The AI development environment is therefore vulnerable to traditional software security risks: open source package vulnerabilities, CWEs, exposed secrets, sensitive data leaks. Without robust controls, these risks go undetected by standard application security testing tools, potentially exposing the entire lifecycle to breaches.
  • The AI development environment involves sensitive data — unlike conventional engineering, where engineer access to such data is normally avoided. So apply development security to the development environment. On top of the conventional assets (code, configuration, secrets), the AI-specific development assets are: potentially sensitive data needed to train, test and validate models; and model parameters, which often represent intellectual property and can also be used to prepare input attacks when obtained.
Assets inside the AI development environment (guide pp. 88–89)
Conventional dev assets
Codeincl. pipeline & training tools
Configurationbuild & deployment config
Secretskeys, tokens, credentials
AI-specific dev assets
Sensitive datato train, test and validate models
Model parametersIP — and raw material to prepare input attacks
Why attackers care
Undetected risksopen source vulns, CWEs, exposed secrets slip past standard AST tools
Lifecycle exposureone dev-environment breach can poison or steal everything downstream

AI-specific code practices — and the testing gap

New best practices (and pitfalls) in AI-specific code:

  • Run static analysis rules specific to big data/AI technology — e.g. the typical mistake of creating a new dataframe in Python without assigning it to a new one.
  • Run maintainability analysis on code: data and model engineering code is typically hindered by code quality issues.
  • Evaluate the percentage of code for automated testing. Industry average is 43% (SIG benchmark report 2023); an often cited recommendation is 80%. Research shows automated testing in AI engineering is often neglected (SIG benchmark report 2023), as the performance of the AI model is mistakenly regarded as the ground truth of correctness.
⚠ Common misconception

“The model scores 94% on the eval set, so the code is correct.” Model performance is not ground truth for code correctness — that is exactly the trap the SIG benchmark identifies. A data-pipeline bug can silently corrupt features while accuracy stays plausible; a leakage bug can even raise the score. Automated tests on pipeline and engineering code catch what the eval metric hides.

Model performance testing is essential. Run AI-specific dynamic performance tests before deployment (see the guide’s #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION):

  • Run security tests: data poisoning payloads, prompt injection payloads, adversarial robustness testing — see the guide’s testing section.
  • Run continual automated validation of the model, including discrimination bias measurement and detection of staleness: the input space changing over time, causing the training set to get out of date.

Model deployment is a new aspect to AI and may offer specific protection measures such as obfuscation, encryption, integrity checks, or a Trusted Execution Environment (TEE).

Risk-reduction guidance: depending on risk analysis, certain threats may require specific practices in the development lifecycle; those threats and controls are covered elsewhere in the guide.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 87–90, permalink owaspai.org/go/secdevprogram/. References: OWASP SAMM, NIST SSDF, NIST SSDF AI practices, GenAI security project solutions overview. Standards: ISO 27002 control 8.25 (one-page description — full coverage, little detail), ISO/IEC 27115, OpenCRE secure software development processes. The NCSC/CISA joint guidelines’ “Follow a secure by design approach to updates” and “Collect and share lessons learned” both land partly here — see Lesson 6.

Why do standard application security testing tools leave the AI pipeline exposed?
Because in AI, software also runs at development time — data preparation and training tools — and holds sensitive data and model parameters there. Standard AST tools scan the shipped application, not the training pipeline, so open source vulnerabilities, CWEs, exposed secrets and data leaks in the pipeline go undetected without dedicated controls on the development environment.

#DEV PROGRAM: general engineering rigour for AI

#DEV PROGRAMDevelopment lifecycle program for AIgovernance · §1.1 general controls
IntentApply general (not just security-oriented) software engineering best practices to AI development, so AI systems become easier to maintain, transferable, secure, more reliable, and future-proof. Data scientists focus on creating working models, not future-proof software — extend existing software practices to AI instead of treating it separately. Do not isolate AI engineering.
ImplementMix data scientist profiles with software engineering profiles in teams (each learns from the other). Continuously measure quality of data science code (maintainability, test code coverage) and coach data scientists to manage those levels. Cover automated testing, code quality, documentation, versioning. Add AI-specific engineering practices: data provenance & lineage, model traceability, AI-specific testing such as continuous validation, testing for model staleness and concept drift. ISO/IEC 5338 explains how to make these practices work for AI.
LimitsRelated controls are key parts of the lifecycle and must be linked in: #SEC DEV PROGRAM, supply chain management, continuous validation, unwanted bias testing. ISO 27002 5.37 and OpenCRE documentation-of-function cover this control only minimally.
StandardsISO/IEC 5338 AI lifecycle (gap: covers fully — extends ISO 12207 with new processes and AI particularities); ISO/IEC 27002 control 5.37 Documented operating procedures (gap: minimal); OpenCRE documentation of function (gap: minimal). Reference: research on code quality gaps in AI systems. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/devprogram/

Two named best practices from the guide deserve emphasis:

  • Mixed teams: software engineers typically need to learn more about data science, and data scientists generally need to learn more about creating future-proof, maintainable, and easily testable code. Pairing them transfers both.
  • Continuous quality measurement: measure maintainability and test code coverage of data science code, and provide coaching to data scientists in how to manage those quality levels.

ISO/IEC 5338 — the AI lifecycle, mapped

ISO/IEC 5338 covers the complete software development lifecycle for AI by extending the existing ISO 12207 software lifecycle standard: it defines several new processes and discusses AI-specific particularities for existing ones. The guide includes an interpretation diagram (p. 92) “to get an idea of the topics involved” — reconstructed here. Note at bottom of the original: “Iteration possible on all paths.”

flowchart LR
  START(["Start"]) --> INC
  subgraph LC["ISO IEC 5338 AI lifecycle - iteration possible on all paths"]
    INC["Inception: business or mission analysis - regulations may forbid your application - stakeholder needs, system requirements - many new requirement types"]
    DD["Design and Development: architecture, design, system analysis, knowledge acquisition NEW, AI data engineering - collection, labelling, lineage, quality, preparation, engineers work with real data - implementation with new model engineering: algorithm selection, training, tuning, knowledge programming - integration"]
    VV["Verification and Validation: special model testing best practices e.g. validation vs test"]
    DEP["Deployment: transition"]
    OM["Operation and Monitoring: operation, maintenance - continuous training and model updates"]
    RET["Retirement: disposal - consider data destruction"]
    CV["Continuous Validation: detection of staleness or drift"]
    REV["Re-evaluation: quality assurance, stakeholder needs, validation"]
    INC --> DD --> VV --> DEP --> OM --> RET
    OM --> CV
    OM -.-> REV
    REV -.-> DD
    REV -.-> INC
  end
  subgraph SUP["Supporting processes"]
    AGR["Agreement: acquisition, supply - monitoring and intervention is part of supply"]
    OPE["Organizational project-enabling: lifecycle model, infrastructure, portfolio - keep model inventory - human resources - data scientist role - quality and knowledge management"]
    TM["Technical management: project planning and control, decision management, risk management - new risks - configuration management - include train data and models - information, measurement, quality assurance"]
  end
  SUP -.-> LC

Figure — interpretation diagram of ISO/IEC 5338 (guide p. 92, caption “5338”). Shaded sub-topics in the original mark AI-specific “examples of topic” attached to generic lifecycle processes.

◆ Senior insight

Read the 5338 diagram as a diff against ISO 12207, not a new religion. Entirely new: knowledge acquisition, AI data engineering, continuous validation, re-evaluation loops. Modified: implementation gains model engineering (algorithm selection, training, tuning, knowledge programming); portfolio management gains the model inventory; configuration management now versions train data and models alongside code; risk management gains new risks; retirement gains data destruction. That diff is exactly the checklist for extending your existing SDLC — which is the entire point of #DEV PROGRAM.

▸ Worked example

An incident makes the case for lifecycle versioning: a model in production starts refusing valid loan applications. With #DEV PROGRAM in place, the team can trace the exact combination of code, configuration, training data and model version that shipped, diff it against the last good state, and roll back — the guide names troubleshooting and rollback as the purpose of that combined versioning. Without it, “which dataset trained the thing we deployed in March?” is unanswerable — and so is any poisoning investigation.

Your data science team wants its own separate delivery process “because notebooks aren’t software”. What does the guide say?
Do not isolate AI engineering. Extend existing practices — automated testing, code quality, documentation, versioning — to AI development, mix data scientists with software engineers, and use ISO/IEC 5338 to see how the practices adapt. Isolation is how AI code ends up at sub-43% test coverage with no traceability.
What is model staleness, and which lifecycle elements in ISO 5338 address it?
Staleness is the input space changing over time so the training set gets out of date (with concept drift as the related change in learned relationships). ISO 5338 addresses it through Continuous Validation (detection of staleness or drift), Operation & Maintenance (continuous training + model updates) and the Re-evaluation loop back to design or inception.

Key Points

  • #SEC DEV PROGRAM: extend existing secure SDLC practices (training, code review, requirements, coding guidelines, threat modeling incl. AI threats, SAST/DAST, pentesting) to data science — no isolated AI framework.
  • Version the combination of code, configuration, training data and models for troubleshooting and rollback.
  • The AI supply chain includes data and models that may be poisoned — data provenance and model management are central.
  • The dev environment runs software and holds sensitive data + model parameters; standard AST tools miss its risks — apply development security.
  • AI code quality is measurably poor: 43% average automated-test code vs the 80% recommendation (SIG benchmark 2023); model performance is not proof of code correctness.
  • Test before deployment: poisoning payloads, prompt injection payloads, adversarial robustness; continuously validate for bias and staleness; protect deployed models with obfuscation, encryption, integrity checks, TEE.
  • #DEV PROGRAM: mixed teams, continuous quality measurement, data provenance/lineage, model traceability — ISO/IEC 5338 (extending ISO 12207) covers it fully; ISO 27002 5.37 only minimally.
Module 04 · §1.1 · OWASP AI Exchange

#CHECK COMPLIANCE — laws, regulations, and the copyright minefield

~16 minCovers guide pp. 75–79, 92–95

Compliance can be a powerful driver to grow AI readiness — regulators do your internal lobbying for you. But the guide issues a warning most compliance teams miss: legislation has blind spots, and if you navigate by law alone you will sail straight into them. This lesson covers the #CHECK COMPLIANCE control, the global jurisdiction map, the standards pipeline (ISO/IEC 27090, prEN 18282), and the guide’s full discussion of AI & copyright.

#CHECK COMPLIANCEAI-relevant laws and regulations in compliance managementgovernance · §1.1 general controls
IntentMake sure AI-relevant laws and regulations are taken into account in compliance management (including security aspects). If personal data is involved and/or AI makes decisions about individuals, privacy laws and regulations are also in scope (see the guide’s Privacy section). Compliance as a goal can be a powerful driver for organizations to grow their readiness for AI.
ImplementLegal considerations: comply with privacy laws at all times (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA); require data governance frameworks from any 3rd party providing AI components (protection of personal data, structure/definitions for collection, processing, storage); require 3rd-party suppliers to answer how they store data and what security frameworks surround it (may include end-user personal data or IP). Non-security considerations: ethics (deep fake weaponization — how the system addresses, protects against and mitigates it); human control (“keep the human in the loop”, appropriate oversight based on ascertained risks, respecting dignity and rights — see Oversight); discrimination (a process to review datasets to avoid and prevent bias — see Unwanted bias testing); transparency (“Trust by Design”, proactive regulatory compliance); accountability (AI systems accountable for actions, outputs and data usage — see #AI PROGRAM).
LimitsLegislation’s scope does not necessarily include all relevant risks for the organization: many rules address potential harm to individuals and society, not business stakes. Example: the European AI Act does not include risks for protecting company secrets. Be mindful of blind spots when using laws and regulations as your guide.
StandardsISO 27002 control 5.36 Compliance with policies, rules and standards (gap: fully, with the particularity that AI regulation must be taken into account); OpenCRE on Compliance and AI compliance management (referring to ENISA Table 5: assess the regulations and laws the ML application must comply with); ISO/IEC 27090 (AI security) and 27091 (AI privacy) in development (as of Oct 2025, likely out 2026 — the AI Exchange contributed substantial content to 27090); prEN 18282, the European AI security standard. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/checkcompliance/

The blind-spot warning

Keep in mind: legislation has a scope that does not necessarily include all the relevant risks for your organization. Many rules are about potential harm to individuals and society, and don’t cover impact on business stakes per se. The guide’s example: the European AI Act does not include risks for protecting company secrets. If your risk register is a copy of the AI Act’s requirements, prompt-leaked trade secrets are legal — and still catastrophic.

◆ Senior insight

Use compliance as an engine, not a map. The engine: regulatory deadlines unlock budget and board attention faster than any internal risk memo. The map must remain your own risk analysis (#SEC PROGRAM), which covers business stakes — company secrets, model IP, service abuse costs — that harm-to-individuals legislation ignores by design.

Global jurisdictional considerations (as of end of 2023)

JurisdictionInstruments
CanadaArtificial Intelligence & Data Act
USA(i) Federal AI Disclosure Act; (ii) Federal Algorithmic Accountability Act
BrazilAI Regulatory Framework
IndiaDigital India Act
Europe(i) AI Act; (ii) AI Liability Directive; (iii) Product Liability Directive
China(i) Regulations on the Administration of Deep Synthesis of Internet Information Services; (ii) Shanghai Municipal Regulations on Promoting Development of AI Industry; (iii) Shenzhen Special Economic Zone AI Industry Promotion Regulations; (iv) Provisional Administrative Measures for Generative AI Services

References named by the guide: OpenCRE AI compliance management (referring to ENISA’s Table 5: “Assess the regulations and laws the ML application must comply with”), Vischer on legal aspects of AI, the Summary of the AI Act by SIG, and the Summary of US AI legislation by SIG.

The standards pipeline: ISO/IEC 27090, 27091 and prEN 18282

  • ISO/IEC 27090 (AI security) and ISO/IEC 27091 (AI privacy) are both in development at the moment of the guide’s writing (Oct 2025) and likely come out in 2026. The AI Exchange has contributed substantial content to 27090.
  • prEN 18282 is the European standard for AI Security — brought forward by CEN/CENELEC with a substantial part contributed by the AI Exchange. Exchange founder Rob van der Veer is liaison officer for the official partnership between the AI Exchange and CEN/CENELEC/ISO, as well as co-editor for 18282. In development for almost two years as of writing (Oct 2025), expected to go into public enquiry early 2026 and be published in 2026.
You pass an EU AI Act conformity assessment. Which class of risk may still be completely untreated?
Business-stake risks — the Act targets harm to individuals and society. The guide's named example: protection of company secrets is out of the Act's scope, so prompt or RAG-context leakage of trade secrets can be fully “compliant” and fully unmitigated. Your own risk analysis must cover it.

How about copyright?

Permalink: owaspai.org/go/copyright/. AI and copyright are two (of many) areas of law and policy — public and private — raising complex, often unresolved questions. AI output/generated content is not yet protected by US copyright laws; many other jurisdictions have yet to announce any formal status for such materials. Meanwhile the human contributor who provides input content, text, training data, etc. may own a copyright in those materials. And the usage of certain copyrighted materials in AI training may be considered fair use.

AI & copyright security

As AI systems and large data training models grow more sophisticated, they raise the specter of copyright infringement: training requires processing vast amounts of data that may contain copyright works. If copyright works were inserted into the training data without the owner’s permission, and without consent of the AI model operator or provider, such a breach could pose significant financial and reputational risk of infringement — and corrupt the entire data set itself. The legal challenges cut both ways: whether using copyrighted works to train models constitutes infringement (exposing developers to claims), and who owns AI-generated works trained on unlicensed content. This ambiguity affects developers, content creators, and copyright owners alike.

Lawsuits (as of the guide’s writing, April 2024)

  • A class action against Stability AI, Midjourney, and DeviantArt alleges infringement of the rights of millions of artists by training tools on web-scraped images.
  • Getty Images v. Stability AI: use of images from Getty’s catalog, without permission, to train an art-generating AI. Now imagine a supplier providing vast quantities of training data for your systems, compromised by protected work or unlicensed material.
  • The GitHub Copilot case: filed against GitHub, OpenAI, and Microsoft by creators of code they claim the three entities violated — still in adjudication; see The Register: “GitHub Copilot copyright case narrowed but not neutered”.

Copyright of AI-generated source code

Source code is a significant IP asset — it embodies the innovation and creativity of its developers, and is protected via copyrights, patents, and trade secrets. Human-generated source code generally carries copyright as soon as it is produced. AI-generated code breaks the frame: who is the author? Who can claim IP rights? How can it be licensed and exploited by third parties? The current IP legal framework does not adequately address AI-generated works — and the code may not be entirely novel, since it may derive from existing code or data sources. The guide’s advice: conduct a thorough analysis of the origin and process of AI-generated source code to determine IP implications and safeguard company IP assets, and consult legal professionals specializing in IP and technology.

Copyright damages indemnification

AI vendors have started taking responsibility for copyright issues of their models — under conditions. Microsoft’s Copilot Copyright Commitment indemnifies users from legal damages regarding copyright of Copilot-produced code, provided a number of things, including that the client used content filters and other safety systems in Copilot and uses specific services. Google Cloud offers its Generative AI indemnification. (The guide points to The Verge’s coverage of the Microsoft indemnification and Microsoft’s own directions on its requirements.)

◆ Senior insight

Indemnification is a control with preconditions — read it like a security policy, not a marketing promise. Microsoft’s commitment only fires if you kept the content filters and safety systems on and used the specified services. A team that disables filters “for productivity” silently voids the company’s legal shield. That makes filter configuration an item for #CHECK COMPLIANCE monitoring, not a developer preference.

Do generative AI models really copy existing work?

In essence: no. A generative model does not have sufficient capacity to store all the code or pictures in its training set. During training it extracts patterns about how things work in the data, and later generates new content from those patterns. Parts of the output may show remnants of existing work, but that is more of a coincidence — the model doesn’t recall exact blocks of code; it uses its ‘understanding’ of coding to create new code. As with humans, that understanding may reproduce parts of something seen before, though not necessarily from exact memory. The guide notes this remains a genuinely difficult debate — the same one the music industry has: did the musician learn that a chord sequence works from many songs and coincidentally recreate an existing one, or copy it exactly?

Mitigating copyright risk: ten strategies

Implementing these early can be much more cost-effective than fixing at later stages of AI system operations; each carries financial and operating costs, but the “hard savings” may result in a positive outcome:

  1. Mitigate output of certain training data — the AI Exchange covers this through the corresponding threat: sensitive data disclosure in model output.
  2. Comprehensive IP audits — identify all IP related to the AI system as a whole: not only datasets but source code, systems, applications, interfaces, and other tech stacks.
  3. Clear legal framework and policy — develop and enforce policies and procedures for AI use aligned with current IP laws including copyright.
  4. Ethics in data sourcing — all training data created in-house, obtained with all necessary permissions, or sourced from public domains providing sufficient license for the intended use.
  5. Define AI-generated content ownership — clearly defined ownership of AI-generated content, including under what conditions it can be used, shared, disseminated.
  6. Confidentiality and trade secret protocols — strict protocols to protect confidentiality while preserving and maintaining trade secret status.
  7. Training for employees — on the organization’s AI IP policies and the implications of IP infringement, promoting risk-averse behaviour.
  8. Compliance monitoring systems — an updated, properly utilized monitoring system to check against potential infringements by the AI system.
  9. Response planning for IP infringement — an active plan to respond quickly and effectively to potential infringement claims.
  10. Licenses and/or warranties from AI suppliers regarding the organization’s intended use and all future uses by the AI system — plus, with legal counsel, other contractually binding obligations on suppliers to cover potential infringement claims.
▸ Worked example

Map the three named lawsuits onto the ten strategies: the Getty Images scenario is defeated by strategy 4 (ethical data sourcing with sufficient license) and strategy 10 (supplier warranties); the Copilot scenario by strategies 5 (ownership of generated content) and 1 (limit training-data output) plus vendor indemnification; the Stability/Midjourney/DeviantArt class action is the supplier-side cautionary tale strategy 2 (IP audit) is designed to surface before a court does.

Helpful resources named by the guide regarding AI and copyright:

  • Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Copyright — Copyright Alliance
  • AI industry faces threat of copyright law in 2024 — Digital Watch Observatory
  • Using generative AI and protecting against copyright issues — World Economic Forum (weforum.org)
  • Legal Challenges Against Generative AI: Key Takeaways — Bipartisan Policy Center
  • Generative AI Has an Intellectual Property Problem — hbr.org
  • Recent Trends in Generative Artificial Intelligence Litigation in the United States — K&L Gates (klgates.com)
  • Generative AI could face its biggest legal tests in 2024 — Popular Science (popsci.com)
  • Is AI Model Training Compliant With Data Privacy Laws? — termly.io
  • The current legal cases against generative AI are just the beginning — TechCrunch
  • (Un)fair Use? Copyrighted Works as AI Training Data — AI: The Washington Report — Mintz
  • Potential Supreme Court clash looms over copyright issues in generative AI training data — VentureBeat
  • AI-Related Lawsuits: How The Stable Diffusion Case Could Set a Legal Precedent — Fieldfisher
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 75–79 (copyright discussion, permalink owaspai.org/go/copyright/) and pp. 92–95 (#CHECK COMPLIANCE, permalink owaspai.org/go/checkcompliance/). Sensitive/licensed data emerging in output maps to OWASP LLM02 (Sensitive Information Disclosure); under NIST AI RMF this control sits in GOVERN 1.1 (legal and regulatory requirements understood and managed).

Does a generative model “look up” copyrighted works when generating? Why does the answer matter legally?
In essence no — it lacks the capacity to store its training set and instead extracts patterns, so overlaps with existing work are more coincidence than retrieval. It matters because infringement analysis differs if output is a derived pattern rather than a stored copy — but the guide stresses this remains an unresolved debate, like the chord-sequence question in music.
List the five non-security compliance considerations and their linked controls.
Ethics (deep fake weaponization — address, protect, mitigate); Human control (“keep the human in the loop” — Oversight); Discrimination (dataset review process — Unwanted bias testing); Transparency (“Trust by Design”, proactive regulatory compliance); Accountability (accountable for actions, outputs, data usage — #AI PROGRAM).

Key Points

  • #CHECK COMPLIANCE brings AI-relevant law into compliance management; privacy law joins when personal data or decisions about individuals are involved.
  • Blind-spot warning: legislation covers harm to individuals/society, not business stakes — the EU AI Act ignores company-secret protection.
  • Jurisdictions (end 2023): Canada AIDA; US Federal AI Disclosure Act & Algorithmic Accountability Act; Brazil; India Digital India Act; EU AI Act + AI Liability + Product Liability Directives; four Chinese instruments.
  • Legal implementation: GDPR/CCPA/HIPAA always; 3rd-party data governance and breach answers required. Non-security: ethics, human-in-the-loop, discrimination review, Trust by Design, accountability.
  • Standards: ISO 27002 5.36 (full, with AI-regulation particularity); ISO/IEC 27090/27091 expected 2026; prEN 18282 (CEN/CENELEC, AI Exchange co-authored, Rob van der Veer co-editor).
  • Copyright: AI output not US-copyright-protected; training on copyrighted data is contested (Stability/Midjourney/DeviantArt, Getty, Copilot cases); models extract patterns rather than store copies — but the debate is open.
  • Vendor indemnification (Microsoft Copilot Copyright Commitment, Google Cloud) is conditional on using safety systems — and ten mitigation strategies, applied early, beat late fixes.
Module 04 · §1.1 · OWASP AI Exchange

#SEC EDUCATE — and how the six controls map to NCSC/CISA & DSIT

~12 minCovers guide pp. 72–74, 95–96

The last governance control is the multiplier: education. Every control in this guide is executed by a person who either understands AI threats or doesn’t. This lesson covers #SEC EDUCATE, then closes the module by mapping all the governance controls onto the UK NCSC / CISA Joint Guidelines for secure AI system development and the DSIT Code of Practice — the external frameworks your auditors and partners will speak.

#SEC EDUCATEAI security educationgovernance · §1.1 general controls
IntentEducation on AI security is a key prerequisite for AI engineers, development teams, and security professionals — people can only apply threats-and-controls knowledge they actually have.
ImplementFollow the “how to organize AI security” steps: 1) based on the inventory of your AI applications and AI ideas, understand which threats apply; 2) make sure engineers and security professionals understand those relevant threats and their controls — noting most controls are familiar conventional countermeasures, unless you are training your own model; 3) use courses and resources from the references to support understanding; 4) distinguish between controls your organization must implement and those that are the responsibility of your supplier.
