LimitsGeneric security awareness training is not enough: the material must cover AI security threats and controls specifically. Scope the education to the threats that actually apply to your inventory — not the whole catalogue.
StandardsISO 27002 control 6.3 Awareness training (gap: covers this control fully, but lacks detail and needs the particularity that training material must cover AI security threats and controls). OpenCRE: AI security Education, referring to MITRE ATLAS AML.M0018 (User Training) and ENISA Table 5 (“Integrate ML specificities to awareness strategy and ensure all ML stakeholders are receiving it”). Permalink: owaspai.org/go/seceducate/
#SEC EDUCATE — the four understanding steps (guide p. 95)
InventoryAI applications & ideas (from #AI PROGRAM)
→
Relevant threatsunderstand what applies to you
→
Threats + controlsengineers & security pros; mostly conventional unless training own models
→
You vs supplierwhich controls are yours to implement
🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Derive the syllabus from your AI inventory — teach the threats that apply, not a generic tour.
  • Tell teams the reassuring truth: most controls are familiar conventional countermeasures, unless you train your own model.
  • Make the you-vs-supplier responsibility split an explicit training outcome — it prevents both gaps and duplicated effort.
  • Reuse authoritative material: MITRE ATLAS AML.M0018 framing, ENISA’s awareness-strategy guidance, courses from the guide’s references.
  • Upgrade existing ISO 27002 6.3 awareness programs with AI-specific modules rather than creating a parallel track.
Why does #SEC EDUCATE start from the AI inventory rather than from a threat catalogue?
Training budget and attention are finite. The inventory (built by #AI PROGRAM) tells you which threats actually apply — a team consuming a hosted LLM needs prompt-injection and supplier-responsibility education, not a course on defending training pipelines it doesn't run. Step 4 then divides the controls between organization and supplier.

How about the NCSC/CISA guidelines?

Permalink: owaspai.org/go/jointguidelines/. The guide maps the UK NCSC / CISA Joint Guidelines for Secure AI System Development to AI Exchange controls (to see the controls linked to threats, the guide points to its Periodic table of AI security). The UK Government, through its DSIT department, built on these joint guidelines to produce the DSIT Code of Practice for the Cyber Security of AI: 13 principles that reorganize things, tweak a few, and add more governance — mostly post-market aspects. The two purely additive principles are: Principle 10 (communication and processes associated with end-users and affected entities) and Principle 13 (ensure proper data and model disposal).

Notice how many rows in this mapping resolve to the six governance controls of this module — external frameworks agree that AI security starts with programs, not patches:

PhaseGuideline (DSIT principle)AI Exchange controls
1. Secure designRaise staff awareness of threats and risks (DSIT 1)#SECURITY EDUCATE
Model the threats to your system (DSIT 3)Risk analysis under #SECURITY PROGRAM
Design your system for security as well as functionality and performance (DSIT 2)#AI PROGRAM, #SECURITY PROGRAM, #DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM, #SECURE DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM, #CHECK COMPLIANCE, #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE, #DISCRETE, #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE, #OVERSIGHT, #RATE LIMIT, #DOS INPUT VALIDATION, #LIMIT RESOURCES, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL, #AI TRANSPARENCY
Consider security benefits and trade-offs when selecting your AI modelAll development-time data science controls (currently 13), #EXPLAINABILITY
2. Secure developmentSecure your supply chain (DSIT 7)#SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE
Identify, track and protect your assets (DSIT 5)#DEVELOPMENT SECURITY, #SEGREGATE DATA, #CONFIDENTIAL COMPUTE, #MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITY, #RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY, #DATA MINIMIZE, #ALLOWED DATA, #SHORT RETAIN, #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA, and part of #SECURITY PROGRAM
Document your data, models and prompts (DSIT 8)Part of #DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM
Manage your technical debtPart of #DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM
3. Secure deploymentSecure your infrastructure (DSIT 6)Part of #SECURITY PROGRAM; see ‘Identify, track and protect your assets’
Protect your model continuously#INPUT DISTORTION, #FILTER SENSITIVE MODEL OUTPUT, #RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY, #MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITY, #PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING, #INPUT SEGREGATION
Develop incident management proceduresPart of #SECURITY PROGRAM
Release AI responsiblyPart of #DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM
Make it easy for users to do the right things (DSIT 4, “Enable human responsibility for AI systems”)Part of #SECURITY PROGRAM, also involving #EXPLAINABILITY, documenting prohibited use cases, and #HUMAN OVERSIGHT
4. Secure operation and maintenanceMonitor your system’s behaviour (DSIT 12; similar to DSIT 9 — appropriate testing and validation)#CONTINUOUS VALIDATION, #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING
Monitor your system’s inputs#MONITOR USE, #DETECT ODD INPUT, #DETECT ADVERSARIAL INPUT
Follow a secure by design approach to updates (DSIT 11: maintain regular security updates, patches and mitigations)Part of #SECURE DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM
Collect and share lessons learnedPart of #SECURITY PROGRAM and #SECURE DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 72–74 (permalink owaspai.org/go/jointguidelines/) and pp. 95–96 (permalink owaspai.org/go/seceducate/). Note the guide’s naming: #SECURITY EDUCATE = #SEC EDUCATE, #SECURITY PROGRAM = #SEC PROGRAM, #DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM = #DEV PROGRAM, #SECURE DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM = #SEC DEV PROGRAM — long and short forms of the same control ids. MITRE ATLAS AML.M0018 (User Training) is the mitigation-side twin of #SEC EDUCATE.

The six controls as one system

§1.1 governance controls — how they interlock
#AI PROGRAMinventory, impact analysis, responsibilities, literacy — feeds everything below
↓
#SEC PROGRAMISMS covers 12 AI asset types, risk analysis, honeypots, monitoring & IR
↓
#SEC DEV PROGRAMsecure SDLC extended with data science & AI particularities
↓
#DEV PROGRAMgeneral engineering rigour: ISO 5338 lifecycle, versioning, quality
↓
#CHECK COMPLIANCE + #SEC EDUCATElaws/regulations into compliance management; people who know the threats
🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Inventory first (#AI PROGRAM) — nothing else can target what isn’t listed.
  • Feed the twelve AI asset types into the ISMS (#SEC PROGRAM) and add the three AI attack surfaces.
  • Extend the secure SDLC to data science (#SEC DEV PROGRAM); version code + config + data + model together.
  • Keep AI engineering inside the general engineering program (#DEV PROGRAM); measure test coverage against the 43%/80% benchmark.
  • Track AI law per jurisdiction and mind the blind spots (#CHECK COMPLIANCE).
  • Train to your inventory’s threats and the supplier split (#SEC EDUCATE).
An auditor asks how your organization satisfies NCSC/CISA “Secure design — design your system for security”. Which module-04 controls do you cite?
All four program controls plus compliance: #AI PROGRAM, #SEC(URITY) PROGRAM, #DEV(ELOPMENT) PROGRAM, #SEC(URE) DEV(ELOPMENT) PROGRAM and #CHECK COMPLIANCE — alongside the technical controls that guideline also lists (least model privilege, oversight, rate limiting, etc.). The mapping shows secure design is mostly a governance outcome.
What do DSIT principles 10 and 13 add beyond the joint guidelines?
Mostly post-market aspects: principle 10 adds communication and processes associated with end-users and affected entities; principle 13 adds proper data and model disposal. The other 11 principles reorganize and tweak the four NCSC/CISA phases.

Key Points

  • #SEC EDUCATE follows four steps: inventory → relevant threats → threats-and-controls understanding → organization-vs-supplier responsibility split.
  • Most AI security controls are familiar conventional countermeasures — unless you train your own model. Teach accordingly.
  • ISO 27002 6.3 covers the control fully but generically; training material must specifically cover AI threats and controls. ATLAS AML.M0018 and ENISA Table 5 back it.
  • The NCSC/CISA joint guidelines’ four phases (secure design, development, deployment, operation & maintenance) map onto AI Exchange controls; DSIT’s 13 principles add post-market duties (end-user communication, data/model disposal).
  • Secure design resolves overwhelmingly to this module’s governance controls — external frameworks confirm governance is where AI security starts.
  • The six §1.1 controls interlock: inventory feeds the ISMS, the ISMS aligns secure development, engineering hosts it, and compliance and education crosscut everything.
Module 05 · §1.2 · OWASP AI Exchange

Shrinking the Data Attack Surface: Minimize, Allow, Retain Short

~14 minCovers guide pp. 95–98

You cannot leak what you never stored. This lesson teaches the first family of general controls for sensitive data limitation — reducing the amount, variety, and retention duration of data an AI system touches — so that when an attacker does get in, there is far less to steal, reconstruct, or manipulate. You'll leave able to argue, field by field, why a training set should be smaller than your data team wants it to be.

First, a prerequisite: educated engineers

Before any data-limitation control can be applied well, the people building and securing the system need to understand AI-specific threats. The guide closes its governance-controls section (just before §1.2) with #SEC EDUCATE, and with a reference worth knowing: prEN 18282, the European standard for AI Security brought forward by CEN/CENELEC, with a substantial part contributed by the AI Exchange itself. AI Exchange founder Rob van der Veer is liaison officer for the official partnership between the AI Exchange and CEN/CENELEC/ISO, and co-editor of 18282. At the time of writing in the guide (Oct 2025) the standard had been in development for almost two years, was expected to enter public enquiry early 2026, and to be published in 2026.

#SEC EDUCATEEducate engineers and security professionals on AI securitygovernance
IntentEducation on AI security is a key prerequisite for AI engineers, development teams, and security professionals — controls only work when the people applying them understand which threats are relevant to their systems.
ImplementFour steps: (1) based on the inventory of your applications of AI and AI ideas, understand which threats apply; (2) make sure engineers and security professionals understand those relevant threats and their controls — noting that most controls are familiar conventional security countermeasures, unless you are training your own model; (3) use courses and resources from the references to support understanding; (4) distinguish between controls your organization has to implement and those that are the responsibility of your supplier.
LimitsISO 27002 Control 6.3 (Awareness training) covers this control fully but lacks detail and needs the particularity that training material must cover AI security threats and controls.
StandardsOpenCRE: AI security Education → MITRE ATLAS AML.M0018 (User Training); ENISA Table 5 ("Integrate ML specificities to awareness strategy and ensure all ML stakeholders are receiving it"). Permalink: owaspai.org/go/seceducate/

§1.2 — Why limit sensitive data at all?

The guide's framing is precise: the impact of security threats on confidentiality and integrity can be reduced by limiting the data attack surface — the amount and the variety of data is reduced as much as possible, as well as the duration in which it is kept. Notice the three dimensions:

  • Amount — fewer records, fewer fields.
  • Variety — fewer distinct kinds of sensitive attributes.
  • Duration — data is deleted or anonymized as soon as it is no longer needed.

These are impact-reducing controls, not attack-preventing ones. A model-inversion attack, a training-set theft, or a leaky log file all become dramatically less damaging when the underlying data was already minimized. The controls in this group, in document order: #DATA MINIMIZE, #ALLOWED DATA, #SHORT RETAIN, #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA, and #DISCRETE (the last two are the subject of the next lesson and lesson 3).

The data attack surface — three dimensions, five controls (§1.2)
Data attack surfaceamount · variety · duration
→
#DATA MINIMIZEdrop fields/records
→
#ALLOWED DATApurpose-prohibited data out
→
#SHORT RETAINdelete/anonymize when done
→
#OBFUSCATE + #DISCRETEharder to reconstruct / find
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 96, group permalink owaspai.org/go/datalimit/. In NIST AI RMF terms these controls implement the Manage function (reducing residual risk impact); in OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) terms they are primary mitigations for LLM02: Sensitive Information Disclosure.

#DATA MINIMIZE — remove what the model doesn't need

#DATA MINIMIZERemove unnecessary data fields or recordsdevelopment-time & runtime · data limitation
IntentRemove data fields or records (e.g. from a training set) that are unnecessary for the application, to prevent potential data leaks or manipulation — because we cannot leak what isn't there in the first place. Objective: minimize the impact of data leakage or manipulation by reducing the amount of data processed by the system.
ImplementApplies during data collection, preparation, training, evaluation, and runtime logging — particularly when datasets contain personal, confidential, or exposure-restricted information. Beyond removing (or archiving) unused or low-impact fields and records: (a) remove data elements that do not materially affect model performance (correctness, robustness, fairness) based on experimentation or analysis; (b) retain certain identifiers only to support data removal requests or lifecycle management, while excluding them from model training; (c) update training datasets to reflect removals or corrections made in upstream source data (e.g. when personal data is destroyed from the source, the training data is updated to reflect the change); (d) original data can be preserved separately with access controls for future use.
LimitsProvider exception: when the deployer is better positioned to implement (part of) this control, the provider may hand it off — as long as the provider communicates this requirement to the deployer. Not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards.
StandardsOpenCRE: Data minimization → ENISA Table 5 ("Ensure that models respect differential privacy to a sufficient degree"); NIST AI 100-2 sec. 2.4.5 (Differential privacy). Permalink: owaspai.org/go/dataminimize/
◆ Senior insight

The guide's particularity for this control is the key negotiating point with your data science team: AI models often tolerate reduced feature sets and incomplete data better than traditional applications, enabling stronger minimization strategies without functional loss. A traditional CRUD app breaks if a column disappears; a gradient-boosted model or an LLM fine-tune usually just redistributes weight across remaining features. So "we might need it later" is a weak argument — run the ablation experiment, measure the performance delta, and cut what doesn't earn its risk.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Inventory every field in training, evaluation, and log data; classify which are exposure-restricted (personal, confidential).
  • Run ablation experiments: does removing the field materially change correctness, robustness, or fairness?
  • Keep identifiers needed for data-removal requests out of training, in a separate access-controlled store.
  • Wire upstream deletions through to training sets (a GDPR erasure in the source must propagate).
  • Archive originals separately, with access controls, rather than leaving them in the training pipeline.
  • If you're the provider and the deployer is better placed to minimize, document and communicate that requirement explicitly.
Why does the guide say AI systems allow stronger data minimization than traditional systems?
Traditional applications process data attributes directly and typically fail when fields are missing. AI models induce patterns statistically, so they often tolerate reduced feature sets and incomplete data with little or no functional loss — which means you can cut more aggressively, and should verify via experimentation rather than assumption.

#ALLOWED DATA — remove what you're not permitted to use

#ALLOWED DATARemove data prohibited for the intended purposedevelopment-time & runtime · data limitation
IntentEnsure allowed data: remove data (e.g. from a training set) that is prohibited for the intended purpose. Particularly important if consent was not given and the data contains personal information collected for a different purpose. Objective: apart from compliance, minimize the impact of data leakage or manipulation.
ImplementMap every dataset to the purpose for which it was collected and the consent attached to it; exclude anything whose use for model training would exceed that purpose. This is purpose-limitation (a core privacy principle) applied to the ML pipeline.
LimitsDistinct from #DATA MINIMIZE: minimization removes what is unnecessary; allowed-data removes what is impermissible — data can be useful to the model and still be prohibited.
StandardsISO/IEC 23894 (AI risk management) covers this in A.8 Privacy — gap: covers this control fully, with a brief section on the idea. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/alloweddata/
⚠ Common misconception

Teams often treat #ALLOWED DATA as "just a legal problem". The guide is explicit that the objective is security too: prohibited personal data sitting in a training set is a confidentiality liability exactly like any other sensitive field — if the set is stolen or the model regurgitates it, the breach is worse because you had no lawful basis to hold it at all.

#SHORT RETAIN — delete or anonymize once no longer needed

#SHORT RETAINLimit data retention durationdevelopment-time & runtime · data limitation
IntentRemove or anonymize data once it is no longer needed, or when legally required (e.g., due to privacy laws). Objective: minimize the impact of data leakage or manipulation.
ImplementLimiting the retention period is a special form of data minimization. Privacy regulations typically require personal data to be removed when it is no longer needed for the purpose for which it was collected. Sometimes exceptions apply because of other rules (e.g. keeping a record of proof). Beyond regulation, it is a general best practice to remove any sensitive data when it is no longer of use, to reduce the impact of a data leak.
LimitsLegal retention obligations can override deletion; document those exceptions. Not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards.
StandardsOpenCRE: Training data retention management. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/shortretain/
▸ Worked example

A support-chatbot team logs full conversations "for quality". Eighteen months later a log store is compromised. Under #SHORT RETAIN, those transcripts — containing names, order numbers, occasional passwords pasted by users — should have been deleted or anonymized after the quality-review window (say 30 days). The breach cost is the difference between 30 days of transcripts and 18 months of them. Attacker economics are unchanged; defender loss is cut by an order of magnitude. That asymmetry is the entire logic of §1.2.

⚔ Attacker's view

From the attacker's side, data-limitation controls are demoralizing precisely because they are invisible until after the compromise. An attacker who exfiltrates a minimized training set gets ablated features, purpose-filtered records, and only the last retention window — the "loot" may not justify the operation. Reducing expected yield is how defenders shift attacker economics without touching a single perimeter control.

How does #SHORT RETAIN relate to #DATA MINIMIZE?
The guide describes limiting retention period as a special form of data minimization: minimization operates on the amount and variety of data, retention limitation operates on the duration dimension. Both serve the same objective — minimizing the impact of data leakage or manipulation.
When can an AI system provider legitimately not implement #DATA MINIMIZE itself?
When the deployer is better positioned to implement (part of) the control — for example the deployer selects which of its own data flows into the system. The exception only holds if the provider communicates the requirement to the deployer.

Key Points

  • §1.2 reduces the data attack surface along three dimensions: amount, variety, and retention duration — you cannot leak what isn't there.
  • #SEC EDUCATE is the governance prerequisite: threat-inventory-driven training for engineers and security staff, distinguishing your controls from supplier controls (ISO 27002 6.3, ATLAS AML.M0018).
  • #DATA MINIMIZE removes unnecessary fields/records across collection, preparation, training, evaluation, and runtime logging; AI models tolerate reduced feature sets better than traditional apps.
  • Identifiers kept for erasure requests stay out of training; upstream deletions must propagate into training sets; originals live separately under access control.
  • #ALLOWED DATA removes data prohibited for the intended purpose (especially personal data collected without consent for another purpose) — ISO/IEC 23894 A.8 covers it.
  • #SHORT RETAIN deletes or anonymizes data once no longer needed or when legally required — a special form of minimization on the time axis, with documented legal exceptions.
  • prEN 18282 is the incoming CEN/CENELEC European AI-security standard, co-edited with substantial AI Exchange contribution, expected published in 2026.
Module 05 · §1.2 · OWASP AI Exchange

Obfuscating Training Data: PATE, Perturbation, Masking, Encryption, Tokenization

~16 minCovers guide pp. 99–104

Sometimes sensitive data cannot simply be removed — it's the signal the model needs. #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA is the answer: keep the statistical value, destroy the reconstructability. This lesson takes you through all five technique families in the guide, the differential-privacy framework that lets you quantify anonymity, and the sharp limitations (mapping tables, re-identification from auxiliary data) that a senior engineer must be able to recite.

The control at a glance

#OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATAMake sensitive training data less recognizable or harder to reconstructdevelopment-time · AI engineer · data limitation
IntentAttain a degree of obfuscation of sensitive data where possible. Objective: minimize the impact of data leakage or manipulation when sensitive data cannot be removed entirely, by making the data less recognizable or harder to reconstruct.
ImplementTechnique families: Private Aggregation of Teacher Ensembles (PATE), objective function perturbation, masking (tokenization, perturbation, generalization, feature engineering), encryption (two operating models), and tokenization. Particularly relevant when exposure-restricted data is necessary for training, compliance, or risk mitigation and cannot be removed. Provider exception applies: the deployer may be better positioned, if the provider communicates the requirement.
LimitsObfuscation reduces the risk of re-identification or inference but does not eliminate it. Removing/obfuscating PII is often insufficient — identity may be induced from remaining data (locations, times, visited websites, activities with date and time). Token-based approaches add risk if mapping tables are compromised. Effectiveness is evaluated through attack testing or formal guarantees (differential privacy or an equivalent mathematical framework). Residual risk remains when exposure-restricted data is still present, when obfuscation mechanisms fail, or when reconstruction/re-identification remains possible.
StandardsOpenCRE: Training data obfuscation → ETSI sec. 6.4.2 (Embedding privacy); ENISA Table 5 (differential privacy to a sufficient degree). Not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/obfuscatetrainingdata/

The particularity is what makes this control uniquely AI-shaped: AI models typically do not require exact or human-readable representations of training data, allowing obfuscation techniques that would be impractical in traditional systems, where data attributes are processed directly and leave less room for obfuscation.

Technique 1 — Private Aggregation of Teacher Ensembles (PATE)

PATE is a privacy-preserving machine learning technique that tackles the challenge of training models on sensitive data while maintaining privacy. The mechanism, step by step:

  1. Partition: the sensitive dataset is split into distinct subsets with no overlap between any pair. Each subset trains an independent "teacher" model. Since no single model sees the entire dataset, the risk of exposing sensitive information is reduced.
  2. Vote: when a new (unseen) data point is presented, each teacher gives its prediction. Predictions are aggregated to reach a consensus — considered more reliable and less prone to individual biases or overfitting to the teachers' respective training subsets.
  3. Noise: to further enhance privacy, noise is added to the aggregated predictions, ensuring the final output doesn't reveal specifics about any individual teacher's training data.
  4. Distill: a "student" model is trained not on the original sensitive data but on the aggregated, noised predictions of the teachers — learning from the collective wisdom and privacy-preserving outputs. The student can make accurate predictions without ever directly accessing the sensitive data.
PATE — teachers on disjoint data, noised consensus trains the student
Sensitive data (partitioned)
Subset 1no overlap
Subset 2no overlap
Subset Nno overlap
Teacher ensemble
Teacher 1independent training
Teacher 2independent training
Teacher Nindependent training
Aggregation
Consensus voteaggregate predictions
+ Noisehides any single teacher
Public-facing
Student modelnever sees sensitive data

The trade-off the guide flags: balancing the amount of noise (for privacy) against the accuracy of the student model. Too much noise degrades student performance; too little compromises privacy.

Technique 2 — Objective function perturbation

A differential-privacy technique that intentionally introduces a controlled amount of noise into the learning algorithm's objective function — the measure of discrepancy between the model's predictions and actual results. Because the objective is perturbed, the final model doesn't exactly fit the original data, thereby preserving privacy. The noise is calibrated to two things: the objective function's sensitivity to individual data points, and the desired privacy level as quantified by parameters like epsilon in differential privacy. Result: the trained model doesn't reveal sensitive information about any individual data point. The main challenge, again, is the privacy/accuracy dial — more noise means stronger privacy and weaker fit; the goal is the optimal balance where the model remains useful while individual data points stay private.

Technique 3 — Masking

Masking alters or replaces sensitive features within datasets with alternative representations that retain the essential information required for training while obscuring sensitive details. The guide names four masking methods:

Masking methodMechanismExample
TokenizationReplaces sensitive text data with unique identifiersCustomer name → TKN_8842
PerturbationAdds random noise to numerical data to obscure individual valuesSalary 84,120 → 84,910
GeneralizationGroups individuals into broader categoriesAge 37 → age band 30–39
Feature engineeringCreates derived features conveying relevant information without revealing sensitive detailsFull address → "urban / rural" flag

Once sensitive features are masked or transformed, models train on the modified dataset, learning useful patterns without exposing sensitive individual information. The balance is crucial: more aggressive masking may reduce model performance.

Technique 4 — Encryption

Encryption is a fundamental technique for pseudonymization and data protection. The guide stresses careful implementation, particularly of asymmetric encryption, to achieve robust pseudonymization, and the importance of randomized encryption schemes such as Paillier and Elgamal to ensure unpredictable pseudonyms. Homomorphic encryption — which allows computations on ciphertexts without the decryption key — presents potential advantages for cryptographic operations but poses challenges in pseudonymization. Also noted: asymmetric encryption for outsourcing pseudonymization, and cryptographic primitives like ring signatures and group pseudonyms in advanced pseudonymization schemes.

There are two operating models of encryption in machine learning:

  1. (Part of) the data remains encrypted for the data scientists at all times, and is only in its original form for a separate group of data engineers who prepare and then encrypt the data for the data scientists.
  2. The data is stored and communicated in encrypted form to protect against access from users outside the data-science group, but is used in its original form when analysed and transformed by the data scientists and the model. In this second model it is important to combine encryption with proper access control — it hardly offers protection to encrypt data in a database and then allow any user access to that data through the database application.
⚠ Common misconception

"Our training data is encrypted at rest, so it's protected." That is the guide's model 2 without the access-control half — and the guide explicitly calls it out: encryption at the storage layer is defeated by any application-layer path that decrypts on demand for every user. Encryption is an obfuscation control only in model 1, where the data-science role literally never holds the plaintext.

Technique 5 — Tokenization (as a standalone approach)

Beyond its role inside masking, the guide treats tokenization as a technique in its own right: sensitive information such as words or numerical values is replaced with unique tokens or identifiers, making it difficult for unauthorized users to derive meaningful information. Within personal data protection, tokenization aligns with the principles of differential privacy: applied to personal information, it ensures individual records remain indiscernible within the training data. Tokenization increases the difficulty of linking specific records back to individuals, and proves particularly advantageous in development-time data science — data scientists can work with valuable information without compromising individual privacy, striking a balance between leveraging data insights and safeguarding privacy.

⚔ Attacker's view

Token-based approaches concentrate risk into one artifact: the mapping table. An attacker who cannot break the tokenized training set will instead target the vault that maps TKN_8842 back to "Jane Doe" — one table, total de-obfuscation. The guide lists compromised mapping tables explicitly as residual risk. Defender economics: the mapping table needs stricter protection than the data it pseudonymizes, because it is the single point of reversal.

Measuring anonymity: k-anonymity, l-diversity, t-closeness — and differential privacy

The guide's limitations section is the intellectual core of this control. Obfuscation reduces re-identification and inference risk but does not eliminate it:

  • Removing or obfuscating PII / personal data is often not sufficient — someone's identity may be induced from the other data you keep about them: locations, times, visited websites, activities together with date and time, etc.
  • Token-based approaches introduce additional risk if mapping tables are compromised.

Re-identification risk can be assessed by experts using statistical properties: K-anonymity (each record is indistinguishable from at least k−1 others on quasi-identifiers), L-diversity (each equivalence class contains at least l distinct sensitive values), and T-closeness (the sensitive-value distribution in each class stays within t of the overall distribution). The guide's essential framing: anonymity is not an absolute concept, but a statistical one — even if someone's identity can only be guessed from data with some certainty, it can be harmful.

Differential privacy is the framework that formalizes this. It ensures the privacy of individual entries in a database is protected by making it possible to learn about the population as a whole while providing strong guarantees that the presence or absence of any single individual does not significantly affect the outcome of any analysis. This is typically achieved by adding a controlled amount of random noise to query results, carefully calibrated to mask the contribution of individual data points. The operational test: by observing the output, one should not be able to infer whether any specific individual's data was used in the computation.

▸ Worked example

The canonical re-identification result the guide's warning echoes: Latanya Sweeney's finding that 87% of the US population is uniquely identifiable from just {ZIP code, birth date, gender} — none of which is "PII" in the naive sense — and the 2006 Netflix Prize de-anonymization by Narayanan & Shmatikov, which linked "anonymized" movie ratings to public IMDb profiles using timing and rating patterns. This is precisely the guide's point about identity induced from locations, times, and activities with date and time.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 99–104, owaspai.org/go/obfuscatetrainingdata/. Named papers in the guide's references: SF-PATE: Scalable, Fair, and Private Aggregation of Teacher Ensembles; Differentially Private Objective Perturbation: Beyond Smoothness and Convexity; Data Masking with Privacy Guarantees; Abadi, Chu, Goodfellow, McMahan, Mironov, Talwar & Zhang (2016), Deep learning with differential privacy, ACM SIGSAC CCS, pp. 308–318; Dwork & Roth (2014), The Algorithmic Foundations of Differential Privacy, Foundations and Trends in Theoretical Computer Science. Related canon: NIST AI 100-2 §2.4.5 treats differential privacy as the principal mitigation for membership-inference and reconstruction attacks (MITRE ATLAS technique family AML.T0024, Exfiltration via ML Inference API).

One family of distortions, three purposes

The guide closes with a disambiguation you must internalize: distorting training data can make it effectively unrecognizable, which must be weighed against the negative effect on model performance that distortion typically creates. Three controls in the AI Exchange all distort training data, for different purposes:

ControlDistorts training data to…Threat addressed
#OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA (this control)protect confidentiality of sensitive dataData leakage, reconstruction, re-identification
#TRAINDATADISTORTIONbreak poisoned samplesData poisoning
#EVASIONROBUSTMODELharden decision boundariesEvasion attacks
Why is adding noise to teacher votes essential in PATE — the teachers already trained on disjoint subsets?
Disjoint subsets stop any single teacher from memorizing the whole dataset, but an unnoised consensus can still leak: if one teacher's vote flips the aggregate, an observer learns something about that teacher's subset — i.e., about specific individuals. The calibrated noise masks any single teacher's contribution, which is exactly the differential-privacy guarantee at the ensemble level.
Your team removed names, emails, and customer IDs from the training set. Is re-identification risk gone?
No. The guide warns that identity can be induced from remaining data — locations, times, visited websites, activities with date and time. Risk must be assessed statistically (k-anonymity, l-diversity, t-closeness) or bounded formally with differential privacy; anonymity is statistical, not absolute.
What is epsilon in differential privacy?
Epsilon quantifies the desired privacy level — how much the presence or absence of one individual may affect any analysis outcome. Noise (e.g., in objective function perturbation) is calibrated to the function's sensitivity to individual data points and to epsilon: smaller epsilon means stronger privacy and more noise, at cost to accuracy.

Key Points

  • #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA applies when sensitive data cannot be removed: keep statistical utility, destroy reconstructability. Development-time AI-engineer control.
  • PATE: teachers on disjoint subsets → noised consensus vote → student trained only on privacy-preserving outputs; noise/accuracy is the tuning dial.
  • Objective function perturbation: calibrated noise in the loss function (sensitivity + epsilon) so the model never exactly fits — and never reveals — individual points.
  • Masking = tokenization, perturbation, generalization, feature engineering; encryption has two models (always-encrypted-for-data-scientists vs. encrypted-at-rest + access control); tokenization also stands alone.
  • Limitations: PII removal is often insufficient (induction from locations/times/activities), and token mapping tables are a single point of de-obfuscation.
  • Anonymity is statistical — assess with k-anonymity, l-diversity, t-closeness; formalize with differential privacy (output indistinguishable whether an individual's data is present or not).
  • Three controls distort training data for different purposes: #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA (confidentiality), #TRAINDATADISTORTION (poisoning), #EVASIONROBUSTMODEL (evasion).
  • Key references: Abadi et al. 2016 (DP deep learning), Dwork & Roth 2014 (DP foundations), SF-PATE.
Module 05 · §1.2–1.3 · OWASP AI Exchange

Staying Discrete, and the Blast-Radius Mindset: #DISCRETE and #OVERSIGHT

~16 minCovers guide pp. 104–110

Two pivots happen in this lesson. First, from limiting data to limiting information about your system (#DISCRETE). Second, from preventing attacks to accepting that unwanted model behaviour will happen — from any of five causes — and controlling its blast radius. #OVERSIGHT is the final checkpoint standing between a wrong or manipulated model output and real-world harm; you'll learn its detection rules, grounding checks, rollback mechanisms, and the four hard limits of human oversight.

#DISCRETE — don't hand attackers the manual

#DISCRETEMinimize access to technical details that could help attackersdevelopment-time & runtime · data limitation
IntentReduce the information available to attackers that can assist them in selecting and tailoring their attacks, thereby lowering the probability of a successful attack.
ImplementIncorporate technical details as an asset into information security management — ensuring proper asset management, data classification, awareness education, policy, and inclusion in risk analysis. Concretely: consider this risk when publishing technical articles on the AI system; when choosing a model type or implementation, take into account the advantage of technology attackers are less familiar with; minimize technical details in model output.
LimitsMust be weighed against #AI TRANSPARENCY, which may require being more open about technical aspects of the model. The key: minimize information that helps attackers while being transparent.
StandardsOpenCRE: Protection of technical AI information → ENISA Table 5 ("Reduce the available information about the model"); MITRE ATLAS AML.M0000 (Limit Public Release of Information); AML.M0001 (Limit Model Artifact Release). ISO 27002 Control 5.9 (Inventory of information and other associated assets) covers this fully, with the particularity that technical data-science details can be sensitive; see also OpenCRE on data classification and handling (same gap). Related ATLAS technique: Acquire Public ML Artifacts. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/discrete/
⚔ Attacker's view

MITRE ATLAS begins most of its attack chains with reconnaissance: Acquire Public ML Artifacts (AML.T0002) and searching victim research publications. A conference paper naming your model architecture, feature set, and training-data source converts a black-box attack into a grey-box one — transfer-based evasion and model-inversion attacks become materially cheaper when the attacker can train a local surrogate of the right family. #DISCRETE raises attacker cost by forcing them back to blind probing, which is slower, noisier, and detectable by the monitoring controls you'll meet in lesson 6.

◆ Senior insight

#DISCRETE vs. #AI TRANSPARENCY is not a contradiction to resolve but a tension to manage. Transparency owes users abstract properties (what the system does, its accuracy, residual risks). Discretion denies attackers operational specifics (architecture, hyperparameters, feature engineering, confidence internals). You can fully satisfy the EU AI Act's chatbot-disclosure duty without ever publishing your embedding dimensions. Draw the line at: does this detail change how a user relies on the system, or only how an attacker targets it?

§1.3 — Controls to limit the effects of unwanted behaviour

Unwanted model behaviour is the intended result of many AI attacks — data poisoning, evasion, prompt injection. There are many ways to prevent and detect those attacks, but §1.3 asks a different question: when unwanted behaviour happens anyway, how do we control its effects, in order to reduce the impact — by constraining actions, introducing oversight, and enabling timely containment and recovery? The guide names this blast radius control.

Crucially, attacks are only one of five causes. AI systems can display unwanted behaviour for other reasons, making this control area a shared responsibility beyond just security:

  • Insufficient or incorrect training data
  • Model staleness / model drift — the model becoming outdated
  • Mistakes during model and data engineering
  • Feedback loops where model output ends up in the training data of future models, leading to model collapse (also known as recursive pollution)
  • Security threats — the attacks laid out throughout the guide
Five causes, one symptom, seven blast-radius controls (§1.3)
Causes
Bad training datainsufficient / incorrect
Staleness / driftworld moved on
Engineering mistakesmodel & data pipeline
Feedback loopsmodel collapse / recursive pollution
Attackspoisoning · evasion · prompt injection
Symptom
Unwanted model behaviourwrong, harmful, or manipulated output/action
Blast-radius controls
#OVERSIGHTfinal checkpoint
#LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGEconstrain actions
#MODEL ALIGNMENTbake in behaviour
#AI TRANSPARENCYcalibrate reliance
#CONTINUOUS VALIDATIONdetect behaviour change
#EXPLAINABILITYexpose reasoning
#UNWANTED BIAS TESTINGdetect attack-caused bias

The two threats of mitigating too little — trusting too much

The guide flags a subtle irony: successfully mitigating unwanted model behaviour has its own threats, both forms of misplaced trust.

THREAT
OverrelianceThe model is trusted too much by users. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: OWASP for LLM 09 (Overreliance); folded into LLM09 Misinformation in the 2025 list.

LLMs (GenAI), just like most AI models, induce their results from training data — meaning they can make up things that are false. The training data itself can contain false or outdated information. Yet LLMs can come across as very confident about their output. These aspects make overreliance on LLMs (OWASP for LLM 09) a real risk. Note the guide's caution: all AI models in principle can suffer from overreliance — not just Large Language Models.

THREAT
Excessive agencyThe model is trusted too much by engineers and gets excessive functionality, permissions, or autonomy. Lifecycle: design/runtime. Maps to: OWASP for LLM 08; LLM06 Excessive Agency in the 2025 list.

When Large Language Models can perform actions, the privileges around which actions and when become important (OWASP for LLM 07). Overreliance by users feeds excessive agency granted by engineers (OWASP for LLM 08): "the model handles it fine" becomes "give the model more permissions", and the blast radius grows silently.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 105–106, owaspai.org/go/limitunwanted/. The guide cites the OWASP Top 10 for LLM numbering LLM07/LLM08/LLM09 (2023–24 edition). In the 2025 edition, Excessive Agency is LLM06 and overreliance concerns live under LLM09 Misinformation. NIST AI RMF: this whole section operationalizes the Manage function; MITRE ATLAS mitigation AML.M0020 (Generative AI Guardrails) anchors the automated-oversight side.

#OVERSIGHT — the final checkpoint

#OVERSIGHTHuman or automated oversight of model behaviour (guardrails)runtime · limit unwanted behaviour
IntentOversight of model behaviour by humans or automated mechanisms (e.g., using rules); human oversight adds not only more intelligent validation through common sense and domain knowledge, but also clear accountability for decisions and outcomes. Objective: detect unwanted model behaviour and respond — by correcting, halting execution, deferring to a human-in-the-loop, or issuing an alert to be investigated. It is the nature of AI models that they can be wrong, and they can be manipulated (prompt injection, data poisoning, evasion) — so a layer that oversees model output is critical: the final checkpoint.
Implement(1) Detection rules for unwanted output: offensive language, toxicity, NSFW, misinformation, dangerous information (e.g. recipe for poison, medical misinformation); sensitive data (see #SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING — names, phone numbers, passwords, tokens; detections also applicable to model input and to APIs retrieving data that goes into the model); the special category of system prompts, which attackers can use to circumvent prompt-injection protection embedded in such prompts; suspicious function calls — ideally privileges are already hardened (#LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE) so detection reduces to alerting when the model attempts an unpermitted action, plus detecting unusual function calls in context, alerting for investigation, or asking human approval; manipulation of function flow is termed application flow perturbation, and an advanced detection is rule-based sanity checks during steps (verify that safety checks/filters ran before data was processed). (2) Grounding checks when rule-based recognition of unwanted output from context is too difficult and malicious-input detection is insufficient: a separate GenAI model decides whether input/output is off-topic or escalates capabilities (e.g. an LLM-powered food-recipes app suddenly trying to send emails). (3) General detection/response per #MONITOR USE, with human-in-the-loop as part of the response. (4) Rollback mechanisms to return to a known state after malfunction or manipulation when system state cannot be trusted or has been disrupted. (5) For checks requiring accountability and/or more expertise and common sense, present behaviour to a human to approve — possibly via a logic rule that escalates in specific circumstances. (6) Ensure human oversight is appropriate: qualified, instructed, motivated, and not suffering from approval fatigue.
LimitsAutomated: properties of wanted/unwanted behaviour often cannot be entirely specified, limiting guardrail effectiveness. Human: (1) more costly and slower; (2) approval fatigue — humans overwhelmed by approval requests when the large majority are okay; (3) lack of expertise to judge; (4) lack of involvement in the situation — a form of lack of expertise, tied to the out-of-the-loop phenomenon.
StandardsOpenCRE: Automated AI oversight → MITRE ATLAS AML.M0020 (Generative AI Guardrails); NIST AI 100-2 §3.4.5 (An LLM moderator). OpenCRE: Human AI oversight → ATLAS AML.M0030 (Restrict AI Agent Tool Invocation on Untrusted Data); AML.M0029 (Human In-the-Loop for AI Agent Actions); NIST AI 100-2 §3.4.5 (RLHF). ISO/IEC 42001 B.9.3 defines controls for human oversight and decisions regarding autonomy — gap: covers this control partly (human oversight only, not business logic); not covered further in ISO/IEC standards. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/oversight/

A boundary note from the guide: a separate form of oversight is #MODEL ALIGNMENT, which constrains behaviour through training, fine-tuning, and system prompts. It is treated as a separate control because its effectiveness is limited and therefore no guarantee — you'll see why in lesson 5.

Oversight as the final checkpoint — detect, decide, respond
Model output / action attemptpossibly wrong or manipulated
→
Detection rulestoxicity · sensitive data · system prompts · suspicious calls
→
Grounding checkseparate GenAI model: off-topic? capability escalation?
→
Human-in-the-loopapprove · investigate · shut down
→
Responsecorrect · halt · alert · rollback

The guide's five worked examples of oversight

  • Logic preventing the trunk of a car from opening while the car is moving — even if the driver seems to request it.
  • Logic signaling an alert when a software programming tool makes a series of updates to multiple projects in one go; a human processes the alert, decides to investigate and/or act — up to shutting down the complete system to prevent further harm.
  • Requesting user confirmation before sending a large number of emails as instructed by a model.
  • Allowing users to undo or revert actions initiated by the AI system, such as reversing changes made to a file — another form of human oversight.
  • A special form of guardrails: censoring unwanted output of GenAI models (e.g. violent, unethical).

Why human oversight degrades — approval fatigue and the out-of-the-loop phenomenon

#Limitation of human oversightMechanism
1More costly and slowerEvery human gate adds latency and salary to the loop.
2Approval fatigueHumans overwhelmed by approval requests, especially when the large majority are okay — the rubber stamp emerges.
3Lack of expertise to judgeThe reviewer cannot evaluate what they're approving.
4Lack of involvement in the situationA form of lack of expertise: without active participation, the reviewer has no situational grounding for the judgment.

On limitation 4, the guide goes deep: for human operators or drivers of automated systems like self-driving cars, staying actively involved in the control loop helps maintain situational awareness, prevents complacency, and keeps the human ready to take over if the automated system fails or hits a scenario it cannot handle. But with high levels of automation this is undermined by the "out-of-the-loop" phenomenon — the operator disengages from the task, leading to slower response times and decreased effectiveness in unexpected situations. The guide's blunt summary: if you are not actively involved in performing a task, you lose understanding of whether it is correct or what the impact can be — and if all you must do is say 'go ahead' or 'cancel', a badly informed 'go ahead' is easy to pick.

◆ Senior insight

Design the human gate for the minority of cases that matter. If 98% of approvals are fine, route them through automated rules and reserve humans for the 2% that a logic rule escalates — with enough surrounding context to judge. A human gate on everything is worse than a human gate on the risky subset: fatigue converts your accountability layer into a latency layer. And measure it: approval-rejection rate near zero is not a sign of a good model; it's a sign of a dead reviewer.

▸ Worked example

The car-trunk rule is the purest oversight example in the guide because it shows oversight is not AI: a deterministic business rule ("never open the trunk while moving") sits after the model and wins every disagreement — even against an apparently legitimate driver request. No amount of prompt injection, hallucination, or model drift can defeat a constraint the model never gets to vote on. When lives or money are at stake, prefer rules the model cannot argue with.

Why are system prompts called out as a "special category of sensitive data" in oversight detection rules?
Because leaked system prompts arm attackers: prompt-injection defences are often written into the system prompt, so an attacker who extracts it can craft inputs that circumvent those exact protections. Oversight should therefore detect system-prompt content in model output, alongside classic sensitive data like names, phone numbers, passwords, and tokens.
What is a grounding check and when do you need one?
A grounding check lets a separate Generative AI model decide whether an input or output is off-topic or escalates capabilities — e.g. a food-recipes LLM suddenly trying to send emails. You need it when unwanted output depends on context too subtle for rules, and detection of malicious input alone is insufficient to cover the attack scenarios.
What is application flow perturbation and how is it detected?
It is the manipulation of an AI application's function flow (which functions get called, in what order). Advanced detection uses rule-based sanity checks during steps — for example verifying that required safety checks or filters executed before data was processed — plus alerting on unusual function calls and on attempts to execute unpermitted actions.

Key Points

  • #DISCRETE treats technical details as security assets: classify, manage, and minimize them (in publications, model choice familiarity, and model output) — balanced against #AI TRANSPARENCY.
  • §1.3 is blast radius control: constrain actions, add oversight, enable timely containment and recovery — because unwanted behaviour has five causes, only one of which is attack.
  • Model collapse (recursive pollution) arises when model output feeds future training data; drift/staleness arise when the world changes.
  • Mitigation has its own threats: overreliance (users trust too much, OWASP for LLM 09) and excessive agency (engineers grant too much, OWASP for LLM 08) — and overreliance applies to all AI models, not just LLMs.
  • #OVERSIGHT is the final checkpoint: detection rules, grounding checks, #MONITOR USE integration, rollback mechanisms, and appropriate humans-in-the-loop.
  • Automated oversight is limited because wanted/unwanted behaviour can't be fully specified; human oversight is limited by cost, approval fatigue, missing expertise, and the out-of-the-loop phenomenon.
  • Standards anchors: ATLAS AML.M0020/M0029/M0030, NIST AI 100-2 §3.4.5, ISO/IEC 42001 B.9.3 (partial: human oversight only).
Module 05 · §1.3 · OWASP AI Exchange

#LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE: Hardening What a Model Can Do

~15 minCovers guide pp. 110–114

If oversight is the checkpoint after the model acts, least model privilege makes sure the model could never do much damage in the first place. This is the control that decides whether a prompt-injected email agent sends spam to your whole address book or fails with a permission error. You'll learn the three implementation pillars, the flexibility balance between one god-agent and many narrow ones, and all eight strategies for task-based minimization — the deepest agentic-AI security material in the guide so far.

The control

#LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGEMinimize what a model can do — actions and data accessruntime · information security · limit unwanted behaviour
IntentMinimize what a model can do (trigger actions or access data), to prevent harm in case the model is manipulated, or makes a mistake by itself.
Implement(1) Limit both permissions and attack surface: configure permissions in an authorization mechanism AND remove access to elements — e.g. isolating or sandboxing an agent by removing commands it could call from an image, or limiting network access. (2) Honor limitations of the served: execute AI actions with the rights and privileges of the user or service being served, so no actions are invoked and no data retrieved outside authorizations; the served is always just the initiator — if the initiator wants the AI to provide information to others, those others' authorization must also be taken into account. (3) Task-based minimization: reduce actions the model can potentially trigger — and what they can be triggered on — to the minimum necessary for reasonably foreseeable use cases; purpose is blast radius control. May require mechanisms beyond your IAM: ephemeral tokens, dynamic permissions, narrow permission control at scale, with trust establishment and potential revocation across domains. (4) Avoid implementing authorization in GenAI instructions — instructions are vulnerable to hallucinations and manipulation (prompt injection), especially in Agentic AI; also prevent GenAI outputting commands that include references to user context, which would allow privilege escalation by manipulating that output.
LimitsThe flexibility balance: strict minimization can break workflows, frustrate users, and add administrator effort; users/administrators are incentivized NOT to harden agents. Fine-grained runtime permission handoff (delegated scoped credentials) is not native even in frameworks like LangChain.
StandardsOpenCRE: Model action privilege minimization → MITRE ATLAS AML.M0028 (AI Agent Tools Permissions Configuration), AML.M0026 (Privileged AI Agent Permissions Configuration), AML.M0027 (Single-User AI Agent Permissions Configuration); ENISA Table 5 ("Apply a RBAC model, respecting the least privileged principle"). ISO 27002 control 8.2 (Privileged access rights) — gap: covers fully, with the particularity that privileges assigned to autonomous model decisions must be assigned with the risk of unwanted model behaviour in mind; OpenCRE on least privilege (same gap). Paper: "A Novel Zero-Trust Identity Framework for Agentic AI: Decentralized Authentication and Fine-Grained Access Control". Permalink: owaspai.org/go/leastmodelprivilege/
▸ Worked example

The guide's email case: an AI model is connected to an email facility to summarize incoming emails for an end user. Honor limitations of the served → the AI can only access the emails the end user can access. Task-based minimization → limit email access to read-only, so that even a fully prompt-injected model cannot send spam, cannot inject misinformation into outgoing mail, and cannot forward the user's sensitive emails to the attacker. One permission bit — read vs. read/write — is the difference between "bad summary" and "data exfiltration channel".

⚠ Common misconception

"We told the agent in its system prompt that it may only act on behalf of the current user." That is the anti-pattern the guide prohibits: authorization implemented in GenAI instructions is vulnerable to hallucination and prompt injection. Worse, if the model's output embeds user-context references (e.g. emitting run_as=user_id in a command), an attacker who manipulates the output escalates privileges. Authorization must live in deterministic infrastructure the model cannot talk its way past.

The flexibility balance — one god-agent vs. many narrow agents

The guide frames the core design question: how to strike the balance between (1) a general-purpose AI agent that has all permissions and can be assigned to anything, and (2) a large set of AI agents, each for a different type of task with the right permission set to prevent it stepping out of bounds? Option 1 is the easiest extreme; option 2 requires more effort and may cause workflows to fail because an agent lacked permissions — causing user frustration and administrator effort to further tailor agents and permissions.

Still: least model privilege is critical if successful manipulation is probable and the potential effects are severe. Best practice: at least have separate agents for permissions with severe effects (e.g. executing run commands). This shifts responsibility for choosing the right permissions to the actor selecting the agent — which introduces a new risk: the actor (person or agent) picks an over-privileged agent because they're not sufficiently informed, or because they prefer flexibility over security. If that risk is real, dynamic minimization of permissions is required: logic that sets action permissions based on knowledge of the intent (an agent assigned to summarize a ticket only gets read access to tickets) and knowledge of potential risks (automatically reducing permissions the moment untrusted input enters an agent workflow).

The hard case: code execution

One of the most powerful things to let AI agents do is execute code — and that is where task-based minimization becomes a genuine challenge, because you want to broaden possibilities for the agents while limiting them for attackers. The guide's solutions:

  • Replacing arbitrary code execution with the execution of a limited set of API calls
  • Removing commands — e.g. deleting them from a deployed operating system image
  • Sandboxing the code execution, for example via network segmentation, to minimize the attack surface of commands

The eight strategies for task-based minimization

Actions the model can trigger — and what they can be triggered on — must be minimized based on who or what is served, and on the task. The guide's full strategy catalogue:

StrategyMechanismNotes from the guide
Harden based on general intentAgents/agentic systems typically lack a single fixed task, so at minimum, minimize permissions to reasonably foreseeable use cases.The baseline every agent should get.
Harden based on prompt intentThe original prompt contains intent; mechanisms (typically LLM-based) interpret it and set permissions.Overlaps with #OVERSIGHT grounding checks — but least privilege is preventative permissions while oversight is reactive; permissions can also flow to subagents, enabling inheritance of context in the agentic flow.
Harden based on role assignmentOnce an agent/flow is assigned a specific task (e.g. an LLM reviewing new code in a merge request), permissions minimize to that role.Role = task-scoped permission set.
Harden based on risk elevationLogic hardens permissions the moment risky input enters the flow — from an untrusted agent, from an untrusted source (e.g. a public comment database), or the reverse: sensitive data entering the flow, after which e.g. all send-out actions are disabled.Balance against whether the intent is still achievable, and whether the risk-elevating element justifies downgrading agentic capability.
Downgrading subagentsInter-agent calls include reduced permission sets — e.g. an email-handling agent calling a summarizer agent hands down fewer rights.Best performed outside the LLM due to model reliability issues. LangChain supports the mechanism via tools and subagents, but fine-grained runtime permission handoff (delegated scoped credentials) is not native.
Hardening as incident responseBased on suspicion level, automated or manual response hardens an agentic flow to reduce blast radius of a potentially corrupted state — without fully stopping it, limiting response interference.Containment without shutdown.
Ephemeral permissionsIf a task should finish within a certain time, permissions are set as temporary, so manipulated agents can't use them later.The guide calls this temporal blast radius control.
Informing and nudging users to harden agentsUsers/administrators help harden permissions — but their incentive is NOT to (analysis takes time; wrong guesses fail tasks). Counter with awareness of risks and guidance on which permissions to set for which functions.A form of #AI TRANSPARENCY: user training and documentation, UI guidance with suggestions, warnings when riskful permissions are set.
 
flowchart TD
  A["Agent receives task"] --> B["Baseline: general intent hardening"]
  B --> C{"Prompt intent interpretable?"}
  C -->|yes| D["Set preventative permissions from prompt intent"]
  C -->|no| E["Role assignment scoping"]
  D --> F{"Untrusted input or sensitive data enters flow?"}
  E --> F
  F -->|yes| G["Risk elevation: disable send-out and risky actions"]
  F -->|no| H["Proceed with scoped permissions"]
  G --> H
  H --> I{"Calls a subagent?"}
  I -->|yes| J["Hand down reduced permission set outside the LLM"]
  I -->|no| K["Execute"]
  J --> K
  K --> L["Permissions expire: temporal blast radius control"]

Figure — layering the task-based minimization strategies across an agentic flow (synthesized from guide pp. 112–114).

⚔ Attacker's view

An attacker who lands an indirect prompt injection in an agent's context is doing privilege arbitrage: their payload runs with whatever the agent holds. Against a god-agent, one poisoned webpage yields email send, file write, and code execution. Against layered task-based minimization, the same payload hits read-only scopes, expired tokens, and a downgraded subagent — the attacker must now chain a permission-system exploit on top of the injection, which changes the economics from "one crafted comment" to "a real intrusion campaign". Blast-radius controls don't stop the injection; they make it not worth injecting.

◆ Senior insight

Notice where the guide insists mechanisms live outside the LLM: authorization decisions, and subagent permission hand-downs. The organizing principle: probabilistic components (models) may propose; deterministic components (IAM, sandboxes, token issuers) must dispose. Prompt-intent hardening looks like an exception — it uses an LLM to interpret intent — but even there the interpretation only ever narrows permissions below a deterministic ceiling. A model should never be the component that grants.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Run every AI action under the served user's identity — and check authorization of downstream recipients, not just the initiator.
  • Strip the attack surface physically: remove unused commands from agent images, segment networks, sandbox execution.
  • Replace arbitrary code execution with a curated API-call allowlist wherever feasible.
  • Separate agents for severe-effect permissions (run commands); never blend them into general-purpose agents.
  • Implement dynamic minimization if actors are likely to pick over-privileged agents.
  • Issue ephemeral, task-scoped tokens; expire them at expected task completion.
  • Hand down reduced permissions to subagents in code, not in prompts.
  • Nudge users: UI suggestions, warnings on riskful permission grants, documentation (a form of #AI TRANSPARENCY).
Why must authorization never be implemented in GenAI instructions?
Because instructions are processed probabilistically: the model can hallucinate them away or be manipulated out of them via prompt injection — especially in agentic AI. Additionally, if model output embeds user-context references, manipulating that output becomes a privilege-escalation path. Authorization belongs in deterministic mechanisms outside the model.
How does "harden based on prompt intent" differ from a grounding check under #OVERSIGHT?
Both may use an LLM to interpret what the flow should be doing, but least-privilege hardening is preventative — permissions are set before actions run — while oversight is reactive, judging behaviour as or after it happens. The guide adds that preventative permissions have an extra advantage: they can serve as permissions for subagents, giving inheritance of context across the agentic flow.
What is temporal blast radius control?
Ephemeral permissions: when an assigned task should complete within a certain time, permissions are made temporary so a manipulated agent cannot exploit them afterwards. It bounds the attack window in time, just as task-scoping bounds it in capability.

Key Points

  • #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE minimizes what a model can trigger and access, so manipulation or mistakes cause bounded harm — runtime information-security control.
  • Three pillars: limit permissions and attack surface (sandbox, remove commands, limit network); honor limitations of the served (including downstream recipients); task-based minimization for blast radius control.
  • Never implement authorization in GenAI instructions; never let model output carry user-context references an attacker could manipulate.
  • Flexibility balance: god-agents are easy but dangerous; per-task agents are safer but cost effort and can fail workflows — at minimum, isolate severe-effect permissions, and use dynamic minimization when actors will over-provision.
  • Code execution is hardened by API allowlists, command removal from images, and sandboxing/network segmentation.
  • Eight strategies: general intent, prompt intent (preventative, inheritable), role assignment, risk elevation, downgrading subagents (outside the LLM; LangChain partial), hardening as incident response, ephemeral permissions (temporal blast radius control), informing/nudging users.
  • Standards: ATLAS AML.M0026/M0027/M0028, ENISA RBAC least privilege, ISO 27002 8.2 with the autonomous-decision particularity.
Module 05 · §1.3 · OWASP AI Exchange

Alignment, Transparency, Validation, Explainability, Bias Testing

~16 minCovers guide pp. 114–121

Five controls complete the blast-radius toolkit: aligning the model's own behaviour (#MODEL ALIGNMENT), telling users what they're dealing with (#AI TRANSPARENCY), continuously re-testing behaviour for silent change (#CONTINUOUS VALIDATION), exposing decision reasoning (#EXPLAINABILITY), and probing for attack-induced bias (#UNWANTED BIAS TESTING). The unifying theme: probabilistic safeguards must be paired with deterministic ones, and behaviour must be watched over time, not certified once.

#MODEL ALIGNMENT — baking behaviour into the model

In the context of Generative AI (e.g., LLMs), alignment is the process of ensuring the model's behaviour and outputs are consistent with human values, intentions, and ethical standards. The guide positions it against the external controls you already know:

ControlWhere it actsCharacter
#OVERSIGHTConventional mechanisms responding to the actual outcome of the modelExternal, reactive
#LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGEConventional mechanisms that put boundaries on what the model can affectExternal, preventative
#PROMPT INJECTION I/O handlingDetection mechanisms on input and output to prevent unwanted behaviourExternal, detective
#MODEL ALIGNMENTBaked into the model itself, through training and instructionInternal, probabilistic
#MODEL ALIGNMENTConstrain behaviour through training and instructiondevelopment-time & runtime · AI engineer · limit unwanted behaviour
IntentEnsure model behaviour and outputs are consistent with human values, intentions, and ethical standards — achieving goals similar to oversight/least-privilege, but from inside the model.
ImplementTwo layers. (1) Training-time alignment — the model maker shaping core behaviour; "model alignment" in the strict sense: training data choices; fine-tuning on aligned examples (helpful, harmless, honest); reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) or other reward modeling. (2) Deployment-time alignment — even a trained-aligned model's actual behaviour during use is influenced by system prompts / instruction prompts, and guardrails built into the AI system plus external tools that oversee or control responses (content filters, output constraints). See the guide's appendix on culture-sensitive alignment.
LimitsAdvantages: training-time alignment can capture complex behavioural boundaries via many examples of wanted/less-wanted behaviour, and recognition of unwanted behaviour is very flexible given GenAI's powerful judgement abilities. Disadvantages: reliability issues — alignment is prone to manipulation and to imperfect memorization/application of what the model learned or was told; and the boundaries of unwanted behaviour may change after training (e.g. new findings), forcing reliance on system prompts and/or external controls. Verdict: alignment is a probabilistic, model-internal control that must be combined with deterministic, external mechanisms for high-risk or regulated use cases.
StandardsNIST AI 100-2 §3.4.5 (RLHF) via the OVERSIGHT reference cluster; culture-sensitive alignment appendix (guide-internal). Permalink: owaspai.org/go/modelalignment/
⚠ Common misconception

"The model is aligned, so we don't need guardrails." The guide treats alignment as a separate control from oversight precisely because its effectiveness is limited and therefore no guarantee. Every jailbreak published since 2023 is an existence proof: alignment bends under adversarial pressure. For high-risk or regulated use cases, the guide's verdict is explicit — probabilistic internal control plus deterministic external mechanisms, never either alone.

#AI TRANSPARENCY — calibrating user reliance

#AI TRANSPARENCYInform users of system properties so they can calibrate reliancegovernance & runtime · limit unwanted behaviour
IntentInform users on the AI system's properties to enable them to adjust how they rely on it, what data they are willing to send to it, and what additional mitigations to apply. Directly counters overreliance.
ImplementCommunicate: rough working of the model; the training approach; type of data used and the source; expected accuracy and robustness of the AI system's output; any residual (security) risks. The simplest form: inform users that an AI model is involved at all — required, for example, by the EU AI Act for chatbots.
LimitsTransparency is about abstract system properties — something else than explainability of individual model decisions. It must be balanced against #DISCRETE (don't reveal attacker-useful technical detail while being transparent).
StandardsOpenCRE: AI user transparency. ISO/IEC 42001 B.7.2 describes data management to support transparency — gap: covers this control minimally (only the data-management part); not covered further in ISO/IEC standards. OWASP top 10 for LLM 09 on over-reliance. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/aitransparency/
▸ Worked example

The guide's own example ties three controls together: inform users that when they choose an agent to perform a task, the agent could be manipulated if it reads untrusted data — and what the consequences could be (residual security risk) — followed by a recommendation to configure the agent's permissions to the minimal set for the task. That is #AI TRANSPARENCY delivering the "informing and nudging users" strategy of #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE, calibrated against #DISCRETE.

#CONTINUOUS VALIDATION — catching silent behaviour change

#CONTINUOUS VALIDATIONFrequently test model behaviour against a protected test setdevelopment-time & runtime · AI engineer · limit unwanted behaviour
IntentBy frequently testing model behaviour against an appropriate test set, detect sudden changes caused by a permanent attack (data poisoning, model poisoning) and some robustness issues (e.g. against evasion). Verifies the model continues to behave as intended over time, meeting acceptance criteria; also surfaces non-attack issues — system failures, and performance decline from real-world change (model drift/staleness). In some systems model behaviour directly implements security-relevant functions (access control, policy enforcement), making correctness validation itself a cybersecurity matter.
ImplementTiming and triggers: validate at lifecycle points where behaviour may change or be at risk of manipulation — after initial training, retraining, or fine-tuning; before deployment or redeployment; and periodically during operation when residual model-integrity risk is not acceptable. Operational validation matters most when models remain exposed to updates, external dependencies, or environments where unauthorized modification is plausible; frequency and scope follow risk analysis and output criticality. Degradation detection and response: monitor for unexpected/unexplained performance change (possible attack, configuration error, or environmental drift); responses include investigating the underlying cause; continuing operation when degradation is temporary and within acceptable bounds; rolling back to a previous model version with known behaviour; restricting usage to lower-risk scenarios or specific tasks; introducing additional human or automated oversight for high-risk outputs to limit error propagation; or temporarily disabling the system if continued operation is unsafe — the response choice influences both impact and recovery timeliness. Test data management and protection: test datasets are the reference for acceptable behaviour and need protection against manipulation; store test data separately from training data and model artifacts so attackers can't influence both the model and its evaluation baseline — if test data stays less exposed, validation surfaces integrity issues even when other parts are compromised. Choose performance metrics that align with the goal; metrics pertain to correctness but can also link to unwanted bias towards protected attributes.
LimitsTypically NOT suitable for detecting backdoor poisoning: those trigger on very specific inputs normally absent from test sets, and are often designed to pass validation. Not a strong countermeasure against evasion attacks. Relies on representativeness and integrity of the test set — if attackers manipulate both model and test data, results are untrustworthy. Detection timeliness depends on validation frequency and whether the manipulated model already deployed: development/training-time poisoning can be blocked entirely by pre-deployment validation, while operational poisoning may only be detected after some use. Validation alone does not replace other integrity and monitoring controls. Particularity: "performance testing" for AI means behavioural correctness, robustness, and consistency with intended use (possibly incl. bias checks) — unlike traditional performance testing's latency/throughput focus.
StandardsOpenCRE: AI model performance validation → MITRE ATLAS AML.M0008 (Validate AI Model); ENISA Table 5 ("Define and monitor indicators for proper functioning of the model"; "Implement processes to maintain security levels of ML components over time"). ISO 5338 (AI lifecycle) Continuous validation — gap: covers fully. ISO/IEC 24029-2:2023 (robustness of neural networks); ISO/IEC 24027:2021 (bias in AI systems and datasets); ISO/IEC 25059:2023 (SQuaRE quality model for AI systems); CEN/CLC JT021008 AI trustworthiness framework. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/continuousvalidation/
Continuous validation loop — triggers, comparison, graded responses
Triggertrain / retrain / fine-tune · pre-deploy · periodic in operation
→
Validateprotected, separately stored test set
→
Behaviour delta?unexpected / unexplained change
→
Respondinvestigate · continue · rollback · restrict · add oversight · disable
⚔ Attacker's view

A poisoner's dream target is a team that validates against a test set stored next to the training data with the same credentials — poison both, and the "validation" certifies the poisoned model. That's why the guide's test-data-management clause is a security control, not test hygiene. The attacker's counter-move to validation in general is the backdoor: a trigger pattern (a pixel patch, a rare token sequence) that no natural test set contains, engineered specifically to sail through validation. Hence the guide's blunt limitation — continuous validation typically cannot catch backdoor poisoning.

◆ Senior insight

Read the response ladder as a severity dial, not a menu: investigate → continue (if temporary and in bounds) → restrict to lower-risk tasks → add oversight to high-risk outputs → rollback → disable. Each rung trades availability for integrity. Pre-committing this ladder in a runbook is what turns "we detected degradation" into containment in minutes instead of an argument in a war room. Note also how rollback presumes you kept versioned models with known behaviour — a #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION response depends on an MLOps discipline decision made months earlier.

#EXPLAINABILITY — showing the reasoning

#EXPLAINABILITYExplain how individual model decisions are made (XAI)runtime · AI engineer · limit unwanted behaviour
IntentExplaining how individual model decisions are made — the field of Explainable AI (XAI) — aids user trust in the model.
