ImplementSurface decision reasoning to users and assessors. In some cases this prevents overreliance: a user who observes the simplicity of the 'reasoning', or even errors in that process, recalibrates their trust (see the Stanford article on explainability and overreliance referenced by the guide). Explanations of how a model works also aid security assessors in evaluating AI security risks of a model.
LimitsDistinct from #AI TRANSPARENCY: explainability covers individual decisions; transparency covers abstract system properties.
StandardsStanford article on explainability and overreliance (guide reference). Permalink: owaspai.org/go/explainability/

#UNWANTED BIAS TESTING — bias as an attack signal

#UNWANTED BIAS TESTINGTest runs measuring unwanted bias to detect attack effectsdevelopment-time & runtime · AI engineer · limit unwanted behaviour
IntentBy doing test runs of the model to measure unwanted bias, unwanted behaviour caused by an attack can be detected.
ImplementInclude bias measurement (e.g. towards protected attributes) in test runs; #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION metrics can link to unwanted bias as well as correctness.
LimitsThe details of bias detection fall outside the scope of the guide, as bias is not per se a security concern — other than that an attack on model behaviour can cause bias, which makes bias measurement a usable attack-detection signal.
StandardsISO/IEC 24027:2021 (Bias in AI systems and datasets) — via the continuous-validation standards cluster. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/unwantedbiastesting/
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 114–121. Permalinks: /go/modelalignment/, /go/aitransparency/, /go/continuousvalidation/, /go/explainability/, /go/unwantedbiastesting/. EU AI Act Article 50 is the canonical basis for the chatbot-disclosure duty the guide cites; MITRE ATLAS AML.M0008 (Validate AI Model) is the validation mitigation.

Why does the guide insist alignment must be combined with deterministic external mechanisms for high-risk use cases?
Because alignment is probabilistic and model-internal: it suffers reliability issues (manipulation, imperfect memorization/application of learned behaviour), and the boundaries of unwanted behaviour can change after training. Deterministic external mechanisms — oversight rules, least privilege — don't share those failure modes, so they backstop alignment where consequences are severe or regulated.
A model's accuracy on the validation set suddenly drops 6 points with no code change. Name three plausible causes and the first three responses.
Causes: a permanent attack (data/model poisoning), a configuration error, or environmental drift (world changed). Responses from the guide's ladder: investigate the underlying cause; meanwhile restrict usage to lower-risk scenarios or add oversight to high-risk outputs; and if the state can't be trusted, roll back to a previous model version with known behaviour (or temporarily disable if unsafe).
Transparency vs. explainability — one sentence each.
Transparency informs users about abstract properties of the AI system (rough working, training approach, data type/source, expected accuracy/robustness, residual risks) so they calibrate reliance; explainability explains how an individual model decision was made (XAI), building trust and sometimes preventing overreliance when users spot flimsy reasoning.

Key Points

  • #MODEL ALIGNMENT bakes behaviour into the model: training-time (data choices, fine-tuning on helpful/harmless/honest examples, RLHF/reward modeling) plus deployment-time (system prompts, guardrails/content filters).
  • Alignment's strengths — complex boundaries via examples, flexible judgement — are offset by reliability issues and post-training boundary shifts; it is probabilistic and must pair with deterministic external controls in high-risk/regulated cases.
  • #AI TRANSPARENCY communicates rough working, training approach, data type/source, expected accuracy/robustness, and residual risks; simplest form — "an AI is involved" — is required by the EU AI Act for chatbots; balance with #DISCRETE.
  • #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION detects permanent attacks (data/model poisoning) and drift by re-testing at triggers (post-training, pre-deploy, periodic); graded responses run investigate → continue → restrict → oversee → rollback → disable.
  • Protect test data separately from training data and model artifacts — otherwise attackers poison the model and its baseline; backdoor poisoning is designed to pass validation and typically evades this control.
  • AI "performance testing" means behavioural correctness/robustness/consistency — not latency/throughput as in traditional systems.
  • #EXPLAINABILITY (XAI) explains individual decisions — builds trust, can prevent overreliance, helps security assessors; #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING uses bias measurements as an attack-detection signal.
  • Standards: ISO 5338, ISO/IEC 24029-2:2023, ISO/IEC 24027:2021, ISO/IEC 25059:2023, CEN/CLC JT021008, ISO/IEC 42001 B.7.2, ATLAS AML.M0008.
Module 05 · §1.3→§2.0 · OWASP AI Exchange

Putting It Together — and the Bridge to Input Threats

~12 minCovers guide pp. 122–123 (+ module synthesis)

General controls end here; threat-specific defence begins. This closing lesson consolidates §1.2–1.3 into a single decision picture, then walks the guide's transition into Chapter 2: the input-threat families that all your new blast-radius controls exist to blunt, and #MONITOR USE — the first of the generic input-threat controls, whose description opens on the last page of this module's range.

The module in one picture

flowchart TD
  A["Sensitive data in your AI system?"] --> B{"Needed for the application?"}
  B -->|no| C["DATA MINIMIZE: remove fields and records"]
  B -->|yes| D{"Permitted for this purpose?"}
  D -->|no| E["ALLOWED DATA: remove prohibited data"]
  D -->|yes| F{"Still needed now?"}
  F -->|no| G["SHORT RETAIN: delete or anonymize"]
  F -->|yes| H["OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA: PATE, DP, masking, encryption, tokenization"]
  H --> I["DISCRETE: hide technical details from attackers"]
  I --> J{"Model misbehaves anyway - attack, drift, mistakes, feedback loops?"}
  J --> K["LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE: bound what it can do"]
  J --> L["OVERSIGHT: guardrails, grounding checks, human in the loop"]
  J --> M["MODEL ALIGNMENT: probabilistic, needs deterministic backup"]
  J --> N["CONTINUOUS VALIDATION plus UNWANTED BIAS TESTING: detect change"]
  J --> O["AI TRANSPARENCY plus EXPLAINABILITY: calibrate user reliance"]

Figure — the decision spine of Module 05: limit the data first, then limit the behaviour (synthesis of §1.2–1.3).

◆ Senior insight

The two halves of this module answer the two halves of risk = likelihood × impact for a compromise you couldn't prevent. §1.2 shrinks what a confidentiality breach can yield; §1.3 shrinks what an integrity failure can do. Neither prevents the attack — that's the job of the threat-specific controls starting in Chapter 2. A mature programme runs both layers: prevention where economical, blast-radius everywhere.

Chapter 2 opens: input threats

THREAT
Input threats (group)Attacker-crafted inputs to a deployed AI system achieve malicious goals. Also called "threats through use", "inference-time attacks", or "runtime adversarial attacks". Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: LLM01 (prompt injection), ATLAS AML.T0015/AML.T0051 among others.

Input threats occur when an attacker crafts inputs to a deployed AI system to achieve malicious goals. The guide's Chapter 2 threat roster (each treated in its own later section — and its own module of this academy):

  • Evasion — bypassing decisions
  • Prompt injection — manipulating behaviour of GenAI systems
  • Sensitive data extraction — in two flavours: disclosure in model output, and model inversion and membership inference
  • Model exfiltration
  • AI resource exhaustion

Controls for input threats in general

Before the per-threat controls, the guide lists what applies to input threats generally — and the first two items point straight back at this module:

  • See the General controls, especially Limiting the effect of unwanted behaviour (§1.3, lessons 3–5) and Sensitive data limitation (§1.2, lessons 1–2), depending on the attack.
  • Depending on the attack: controls that protect against the model being stolen or copied (unless the model is publicly available) — direct development-time model leak, direct runtime model leak, and model exfiltration — because many input attacks become dramatically easier when the attacker can access model attributes.
  • The generic runtime controls that limit access and volume, perform generic detection, and reduce output information (unless the attacker can use a similar or same model to perform the attack): #MONITOR USE, #RATE LIMIT, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL, #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING, and #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE — the last because many attacks rely on rich output information.
Input threats and the generic control belt (§2.0)
Input threats
Evasionbypassing decisions
Prompt injectionmanipulating GenAI behaviour
Sensitive data extractionoutput disclosure · inversion · membership inference
Model exfiltrationstealing the model through use
AI resource exhaustiondenial of service
Generic input-threat controls
#MONITOR USEobserve · correlate · log
#RATE LIMITlimit volume
#MODEL ACCESS CONTROLlimit who
#ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLINGodd single inputs
#UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLINGodd sequences
#OBSCURE CONFIDENCEstarve rich output info
Plus this module
§1.2 Data limitationless to extract
§1.3 Behaviour limitationless an attack can do
Model-theft controlsdev-time leak · runtime leak · exfiltration
⚔ Attacker's view

Why does the guide keep repeating "unless the attacker can use a similar or same model"? Because the generic control belt assumes the attack must be rehearsed against your endpoint — rate limits, anomaly detection, and confidence obscuring all tax that probing. If your model (or a close sibling) is public or stolen, the attacker rehearses offline at zero marginal cost and hits your endpoint once with a finished attack. That is also why model-theft controls are listed among input-threat controls: keeping the model secret keeps the attacker paying retail for every query.

#MONITOR USE — first of the generic controls (description begins p. 123)

#MONITOR USEObserve, correlate, and log model usageruntime · information security · input threats
IntentObserve, correlate, and log model usage (date, time, user), inputs, outputs, and system behaviour to identify events or patterns that may indicate a cybersecurity incident. Enables incident reconstruction; provides centralized visibility into how AI systems are used over time and across actors, sessions, and models.
ImplementMake it part of the existing incident-detection process, extended with AI-specific methods: improper functioning of the model (see #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION, #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING); suspicious patterns of model use, e.g. high frequency (see #RATE LIMIT and #OVERSIGHT); suspicious inputs or series of inputs (see #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING, #EVASION INPUT HANDLING and #PROMPT INJECTION I/O handling). Adding the model version used and the output to logs makes troubleshooting easier. Detection mechanisms are typically paired with predefined response actions to limit impact, preserve evidence, and support recovery when suspicious behaviour is identified.
LimitsThis module's extract covers the description through guide p. 123; the control's implementation detail and references continue on p. 124+ (next module). Remember #SHORT RETAIN and #DATA MINIMIZE apply to these logs too — monitoring must not become a new sensitive-data stockpile.
StandardsPermalink: owaspai.org/go/monitoruse/; input-threats group: owaspai.org/go/inputthreats/
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 122–123. Canon mapping for the Chapter 2 roster: prompt injection → OWASP LLM01 and ATLAS AML.T0051 (LLM Prompt Injection); evasion → ATLAS AML.T0015 (Evade ML Model); membership inference / inversion → ATLAS AML.T0024 (Exfiltration via ML Inference API); model exfiltration → ATLAS AML.T0048.004-adjacent model theft techniques and OWASP LLM10 (2023 numbering, Model Theft); resource exhaustion → OWASP LLM10 (2025: Unbounded Consumption). These are treated in depth in the following modules.

Why do model-theft controls appear in the list of controls for input threats?
Because many input attacks become dramatically easier when the attacker can access model attributes: with a stolen or similar model they can craft and rehearse attacks offline, bypassing the rate limits, anomaly detection, and confidence-obscuring that would otherwise tax their probing at your endpoint.
Name the three AI-specific detection extensions #MONITOR USE adds to a conventional incident-detection process.
(1) Improper functioning of the model — via #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION and #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING; (2) suspicious patterns of model use such as high frequency — via #RATE LIMIT and #OVERSIGHT; (3) suspicious inputs or input series — via #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING, #EVASION INPUT HANDLING and #PROMPT INJECTION I/O handling.
Which lessons of this module do the "general controls for input threats" point back to, and why?
The guide directs readers to "Limiting the effect of unwanted behaviour" (§1.3 — oversight, least privilege, alignment, validation, transparency) and "Sensitive data limitation" (§1.2 — minimize, allowed data, short retain, obfuscate, discrete). Input attacks aim to cause unwanted behaviour or extract data, so the blast-radius and data-limitation layers reduce what any successful input attack achieves.

Key Points

  • §1.2 cuts what a breach yields (impact on confidentiality); §1.3 cuts what misbehaviour can do (impact on integrity/actions); neither replaces the threat-specific prevention starting in Chapter 2.
  • Input threats = "threats through use" / "inference-time attacks" / "runtime adversarial attacks": evasion, prompt injection, sensitive data extraction (output disclosure; model inversion & membership inference), model exfiltration, AI resource exhaustion.
  • Generic input-threat controls: this module's general controls, model-theft protections (dev-time leak, runtime leak, exfiltration), and the access/volume/detection/output belt — #MONITOR USE, #RATE LIMIT, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL, #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING, #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE.
  • The belt's caveat: it works only while the attacker must probe your endpoint — a stolen or similar model lets them rehearse offline.
  • #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE exists because many attacks rely on rich output information; #MONITOR USE gives centralized visibility across actors, sessions, and models, and pairs detection with predefined responses to limit impact, preserve evidence, and support recovery.
  • Log model version and outputs for troubleshooting — then apply §1.2 to the logs themselves.
Module 06 · §2.0 Threats through use · OWASP AI Exchange

Threats Through Use: The Runtime Battleground

~10 minCovers guide p. 123

Every attack in this module arrives through the front door: the model's own inference interface. An attacker doesn't need to breach your network, poison your pipeline, or steal your weights — they just need to use your model, patiently and cleverly. This lesson gives you the map: the six generic runtime controls the OWASP AI Exchange prescribes against every threat through use, and the one caveat (transferability) that can undermine several of them at once.

THREAT
Attacks through use (input threats)An attacker interacts with the model's inference interface to fool it, probe it, extract data from it, or copy it. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM01/LLM06/LLM10, MITRE ATLAS AML.T0015, AML.T0024.

What "threats through use" means

An AI system in production accepts inputs and produces outputs. That interaction channel is itself an attack surface. The family of threats through use includes attacks with very different goals but one shared delivery mechanism — sending inputs and observing outputs:

  • Evasion — crafting inputs (adversarial examples) that fool the model into a wrong decision.
  • Jailbreaks and direct/indirect prompt injection — manipulative instructions that bypass protections or hijack behaviour.
  • Model inversion and membership inference — reconstructing or confirming training data by probing outputs.
  • Training data and model parameter extraction — pulling sensitive data or model internals out through the output channel.
  • Model exfiltration (model theft through use) — harvesting input–output pairs at scale to re-train a copy of the model.
  • Data poisoning exploration — probing to learn what poisoned behaviour was planted or could be planted.
  • Denial of service / resource exhaustion — flooding the model to degrade availability.

Because these attacks share the delivery channel, a set of generic controls works against all of them — not by understanding each attack's semantics, but by making the channel itself hostile territory for attackers: limiting who can use it, how often, watching how it is used, and starving the attacker of information.

The six generic runtime controls

The guide groups the controls that apply broadly to threats through use into three strategies: limit access and volume, perform generic detection, and reduce output information:

The six generic controls against threats through use, by strategy (guide p. 123)
Limit access & volume
#MODEL ACCESS CONTROLonly approved, identifiable actors may query
#RATE LIMITthrottle query frequency per actor and in total
Generic detection
#MONITOR USEobserve, correlate, log; respond to incidents
#ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLINGdetect odd individual inputs
#UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLINGdetect suspicious patterns across inputs
Reduce output information
#OBSCURE CONFIDENCEhide or coarsen confidence scores attackers optimize against

The guide adds one explicit caveat to the whole group: these controls limit access and volume, perform generic detection, and reduce output information — unless the attacker can use a similar or the same model to perform the attack. That parenthetical is a reference to transferability, and it is the single most important limitation to internalize before you rely on any of these controls.

◆ Senior insight

Transferability is the escape hatch under this entire control family. A transfer attack means the attacker develops the attack input on a surrogate model — an open-weights model similar to yours, or literally the same foundation model you fine-tuned — and then fires the finished attack at your system in one query. Rate limits, anomaly detection over series, and confidence obscuring all assume the attacker must experiment against your endpoint. If a good surrogate exists, that assumption fails. Your risk analysis must therefore ask: "how similar is the most similar model an attacker can freely obtain?" The more commoditized your model, the less these six controls buy you — and the more you need model-specific robustness controls (covered with evasion in the next module).

⚔ Attacker's view

From the attacker's chair, the six controls translate to costs: access control forces me to obtain or compromise an account (money, risk of attribution); rate limiting turns a weekend of experimentation into weeks (time); monitoring plus series detection means my probing pattern may be spotted before I finish (risk of losing the account and alerting the defender); obscured confidence means each query teaches me less (more queries needed — which loops back into the rate limit). None of these stops a finished attack; together they attack my economics. My best counter-moves: many cheap accounts, distributed sources, patience, or a surrogate model that lets me skip your endpoint entirely.

How the six controls interlock

These are not six independent gadgets; they form a system with explicit dependencies you will see repeatedly in the coming lessons:

ControlStrategyWhat it gives the othersMain circumvention
#MONITOR USEDetection + responseCorrelates every other control's signals; turns detections into responsesEvasion of detection criteria; log gaps
#RATE LIMITLimit volumeBuys detection time; caps experiment throughputMulti-accounting, parallel access, transfer attacks, single-try attacks
#MODEL ACCESS CONTROLLimit accessIdentity that makes per-actor rate limits and attribution possibleCompromised/insider accounts, in-session attacks
#ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLINGDetection (single input)Per-input suspicion signal feeding monitoringAdversarial input crafted to look normal
#UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLINGDetection (input series)Catches slow probing invisible per-inputDistributing inputs across identities/sources
#OBSCURE CONFIDENCEReduce output infoSlows optimization-based attacks that all the above try to detectIndirect confidence estimation; label-only attacks; surrogate models
Why does the guide attach the caveat "unless the attacker can use a similar or same model" to this whole control group?
Because all six controls assume the attack requires interaction with your endpoint — to experiment, probe, or read rich outputs. A transfer attack develops the malicious input on a surrogate model offline, then needs only a single, normal-looking query against your system. That defeats volume limits, series detection, and output-information reduction simultaneously.
Name the three strategies the six generic controls implement, and one control per strategy.
(1) Limit access and volume — #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL and #RATE LIMIT. (2) Perform generic detection — #MONITOR USE, #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING. (3) Reduce output information — #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE (because many attacks rely on rich output information).
§ Source & standards

Guide p. 123 (introduction to threats through use, continuation of the generic controls list). The six controls each carry their own permalink, covered in the following lessons. Canon mapping: this threat family spans OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM01 (Prompt Injection), LLM06 (Sensitive Information Disclosure) and LLM10 (Unbounded Consumption); MITRE ATLAS tactics include AML.T0015 (Evade AI Model) and AML.T0024 (Exfiltration via AI Inference API). In NIST AI RMF terms, these runtime controls sit squarely in the MANAGE function, fed by MEASURE.

Key Points

  • Threats through use share one delivery channel — the inference interface — so a set of six generic controls applies to all of them.
  • The three strategies: limit access and volume (#MODEL ACCESS CONTROL, #RATE LIMIT), generic detection (#MONITOR USE, #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING), and reduce output information (#OBSCURE CONFIDENCE).
  • #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE exists because many attacks rely on rich output information to optimize their inputs.
  • The explicit caveat: none of this holds if the attacker can perform the attack on a similar or identical surrogate model — transferability is the escape hatch.
  • The controls attack the attacker's economics (time, money, attribution risk, information per query), not the attack's semantics.
  • The controls interlock: access control provides the identity rate limits need; rate limits buy the time monitoring needs; monitoring correlates everything into a response.
Module 06 · §2.0 Threats through use · OWASP AI Exchange

#MONITOR USE — See, Correlate, Respond

~18 minCovers guide pp. 123–129

Monitoring is the nervous system of runtime AI security: every other control in this module emits signals, and #MONITOR USE is where those signals become detections, detections become responses, and incidents become lessons. This is the longest control treatment in the input-threat chapter for a reason — get it right and you can reconstruct any incident; get it wrong and you are blind while an attacker slowly extracts your training data at one polite query per minute.

#MONITOR USEObserve, correlate and log model usage; pair detection with responseruntime · information security · input threats
IntentObserve, correlate, and log model usage (date, time, user), inputs, outputs, and system behaviour to identify events or patterns that may indicate a cybersecurity incident; reconstruct incidents; extend the existing incident-detection process with AI-specific methods (improper model functioning, suspicious usage patterns, suspicious inputs or input series). Enables early identification and investigation, supports both real-time interception and retrospective analysis, and strengthens other controls by correlating their signals.
ImplementMonitor inputs/input streams, outputs/output streams, system and model behaviour, model-to-model and system-to-system interactions, and system logs — including chains of inferences across models. Ingest signals from complementary controls (#RATE LIMIT, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL, #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING, #OVERSIGHT, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING, #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE, #SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING, #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION, training-data scanning/filtering). Log trace metadata, request context, processing context, response context; retain per legal/contractual requirements. Apply secure (tamper-evident) logging. Qualify incidents, alert with rich context, and link every detection to a documented response (playbook/SOP): immediate containment, follow-up and investigation, broader response actions, and a learning loop.
LimitsDepends on completeness/accuracy of logged data, ability to correlate signals meaningfully, and legal/privacy constraints on retention. High-volume or opaque systems limit visibility. Responses carry trade-offs: overly aggressive responses cause false positives and disrupt legitimate use; delayed or manual responses lose to fast-moving attacks. Must be combined with preventive and response controls; needs periodic review and tuning.
StandardsOpenCRE "Monitor inference" (ENISA Table 5: define and monitor indicators for proper model functioning; MITRE ATLAS AML.M0024 AI Telemetry Logging). ISO 27002 8.15 Logging & 8.16 Monitoring (full coverage, but no AI-attack-pattern detail). ISO/IEC 42001 B.6.2.6 (full coverage, high abstraction). Permalink: owaspai.org/go/monitoruse/

What monitoring must watch — and why AI is different

The description is deceptively simple: observe, correlate, and log model usage (date, time, user), inputs, outputs, and system behaviour to identify events or patterns that may indicate a cybersecurity incident. Use it to reconstruct incidents, and make it part of your existing incident-detection process — but extended with AI-specific methods, including:

  • Improper functioning of the model (see #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION and #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING) — a model that suddenly behaves wrongly may be under attack, poisoned, or broken.
  • Suspicious patterns of model use, e.g. high frequency (see #RATE LIMIT and #OVERSIGHT).
  • Suspicious inputs or series of inputs (see #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING, #EVASION INPUT HANDLING and #PROMPT INJECTION I/O handling).

Adding the model version and the output to log entries makes troubleshooting far easier. The control's payoff is centralized visibility into how AI systems are used over time and across actors, sessions, and models — and detection mechanisms are typically paired with predefined response actions to limit impact, preserve evidence, and support recovery when suspicious behaviour is identified.

The guide's Particularity section states the AI-specific difference crisply: unlike conventional application monitoring, AI monitoring must observe not only system events but also model behaviour, inference patterns, and semantic signals derived from inputs and outputs. This makes correlation across controls and over time essential — no single signal source is sufficient.

Objective and applicability

The objective: early identification and investigation of potential attacks or misuse by detecting suspicious events or series of events; supporting both real-time interception and retrospective analysis by preserving sufficient context to reconstruct what happened, identify potential attack sources, and design appropriate incident responses. Incident response measures prevent and minimize damage or harm. Monitoring also strengthens other controls by correlating their signals and providing historical evidence during incident response.

Applicability is broad — any AI system exposed to users, integrations, or other systems where misuse, probing, or manipulation is possible. It is particularly relevant when:

  • multiple detection controls are in place and need correlation,
  • attacks may unfold over time (e.g., model inversion, probing),
  • post-incident reconstruction or attribution is required,
  • timely response may significantly reduce impact.

In some deployments, implementation may be more appropriate at the deployer or platform layer — provided monitoring requirements are clearly communicated. This provider/deployer split recurs throughout the module: the party best positioned to implement a control should do so, but the responsibility handoff must be explicit and documented.

Event and signal monitoring

Monitoring can observe signals across five surfaces:

  • inputs and input streams,
  • outputs and output streams,
  • system and model behaviour,
  • model-to-model or system-to-system interactions,
  • system logs.

The fourth surface matters more every year: it lets you observe a chain of thoughts in which various models perform a chain of inferences — the agentic case, where one model's output becomes another's input. Ideally, monitoring also ingests the signals generated by complementary controls: #RATE LIMIT, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL, #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING, #OVERSIGHT (both automated and human), #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING, #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE, #SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING, #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION, and training-data scanning and filtering. For each monitored risk, criteria can be defined to identify suspicious patterns, anomalies, or intent.

Logging and traceability

Logging supports both detection and later investigation. Depending on legal, privacy, and technical constraints, the guide recommends four context layers per event:

Log layerContents (guide p. 125)
Trace metadataTimestamps, trace or session identifiers, actor or session linkage, request rates
Request contextInput content, preprocessing steps, detection signals triggered
Processing contextModel version, execution time, errors
Response contextOutput content, post-processing steps, filtering or blocking actions

Logs are retained for a period sufficient to support analysis, in alignment with legal and contractual requirements. Note the tension you must manage: detection wants everything logged forever; privacy law wants minimization and deletion. The guide's Limitations section names "legal and privacy constraints on data retention" as one of the three things monitoring fundamentally depends on.

Recommended logging enrichment (guide p. 127): beyond core request/response logging, operational context improves incident analysis and prioritization — system-level signals such as memory utilization, CPU utilization, processing-node identifiers, and environment or deployment context (production, staging, or test). When alerts are generated, attach guidance on potential next steps — suggested actions such as blocking a request, slowing a session, or investigating a suspected source — so responders understand both the nature of the detected behaviour and the intended handling approach.

Secure logging: making logs court-worthy

Logs that an attacker (or a dishonest insider) can rewrite are worthless for forensics. The guide requires applying security best practices for logging, and highlights tamper-evident logging as a prime example — the ability to verify whether logs have been unmodified post-incident. The mechanism described:

  • A signed-receipt approach binds a hash of input, output, and processing context to a per-event signature.
  • That signature is paired with an independent time anchor — for example an RFC 3161 Time-Stamp Authority — so that "when" cannot be backdated by whoever holds the signing key.
  • Operators with multi-year retention or regulated-sector audit horizons should plan now for hybrid or post-quantum signature schemes alongside classical ones — a signature that must still prove integrity in 2035 has to survive quantum cryptanalysis.

One open profile setting out the receipt-format obligations under the EU AI Act, DORA, and US sector regulations is described in the IETF draft draft-marques-asqav-compliance-receipts-04.

◆ Senior insight

The separation of the signature from the time anchor is the whole point. If the same key both signs the event and asserts its time, whoever holds that key can forge history — including your own compromised infrastructure. An external RFC 3161 TSA means backdating requires compromising two independent parties. When a regulator asks "prove this prompt produced this output on this date under this model version," a signed receipt with an independent timestamp is the difference between evidence and anecdote.

Incident qualification, alerting, and the decision ladder

When suspicious behaviour is detected, monitoring supports: classifying the potential incident type; assigning confidence or severity levels; and generating alerts for follow-up investigation — when appropriate, with sufficient information such as a unique alert id, timestamp, threat classification, attack source, severity, request and response context, and a description of observed behaviour.

Decision rules then distinguish three rungs:

  1. No action — log and move on (appropriate when detection confidence is low or impact negligible),
  2. Automated responses — e.g., filtering, slowing, blocking,
  3. Follow-up requiring human investigation.

Thresholds and rules can be revisited as risks evolve, to balance detection accuracy, system usability, and alert fatigue — the quiet killer of security operations: a detector that cries wolf hourly trains your responders to ignore it.

Monitoring AI-specific lifecycle events

Beyond runtime activity, monitoring benefits from tracking AI-specific lifecycle events:

  • deployment or rollback of model versions,
  • updates to model parameters or prompts,
  • changes to detection mechanisms or safeguards.

These events support incident reconstruction — and may themselves indicate compromise or misconfiguration. An unexplained prompt change or a safeguard being silently disabled is an incident signal in its own right.

The detection-to-response loop

Detection without response is a diary. The guide makes the link explicit: detection mechanisms benefit from being explicitly linked to response actions — filtering, throttling, escalation, or containment — with response selection driven by confidence, threat type, and potential impact, ranging from automated safeguards to follow-up investigation. For each detection used in the system, a corresponding response approach can be documented (an incident-response playbook / SOP) specifying when actions are automated, when follow-up is required, and what escalation paths apply.

flowchart TD
  S["Signals: inputs, outputs, model behavior, other controls"] --> D["Detection and correlation"]
  D --> Q["Incident qualification: type, confidence, severity"]
  Q -->|"low confidence or negligible impact"| N["No action - log only"]
  Q -->|"high confidence, automatable"| A["Automated response: filter, slow, block, kill switch"]
  Q -->|"needs judgment"| H["Human investigation and triage"]
  A --> C["Containment and recovery"]
  H --> C
  C --> L["Learning loop: new tests, tuned thresholds, updated risk assessment"]
  L --> D

Figure — the detection-to-response loop with the three-rung decision ladder and the learning feedback loop (guide pp. 126–128).

Incident response and containment: the full menu

Response actions vary with the certainty of detection, the threat type, and the potential impact. The guide organizes them into four groups — memorize this menu, because it is what your playbooks should be built from.

Immediate containment

  • Stopping the current inference, workflow, or system — the kill switch — when confidence of malicious activity is high,
  • Sanitizing input or output (trimming prompts, removing sensitive content, normalizing input) and continuing execution,
  • Switching to a more conservative operating mode — reduced functionality, additional filtering, or temporary human oversight.

Follow-up and investigation

  • issuing alerts for triage and investigation,
  • preserving relevant system state and logs to support analysis,
  • increasing monitoring or sampling for affected actors or sessions,
  • throttling, rate-limiting, or suspending suspicious accounts or sessions,
  • restricting or disabling tools and functions that could cause harm,
  • adding noise to the output to disturb possible attacks,
  • rolling back models or data to a known-good state when compromise is suspected and/or the current state has been disrupted.

Broader response actions

  • informing users when the AI system may be unreliable or compromised,
  • notifying affected individuals if sensitive data may have been exposed,
  • engaging suppliers when external data or models are implicated,
  • involving legal, compliance, or communications teams where appropriate.

And the honest bottom rung: in some cases, no immediate action beyond logging may be appropriate — particularly when detection confidence is low or impact is negligible.

Learning and improvement

Incident response includes a feedback loop that improves security posture over time. Following detections or confirmed incidents, teams review events to determine whether additional controls, configuration changes, or detection improvements are required: adding new attack patterns to tests, refining detection thresholds, updating validation checks, or revisiting risk assessments to reflect new insights or accepted residual risks.

▸ Worked example

Your series detector flags a session sending near-duplicate images with slowly climbing output confidence — the signature of an evasion search. Confidence of malice: medium. Playbook says: automated response rung — silently add noise to outputs for that session (disturbing the optimization) and increase sampling; raise a triage alert with the session's full four-layer log context. An analyst confirms the pattern next morning, suspends the account (follow-up rung), and the red team adds the observed probing pattern to the regression test suite (learning loop). No user-visible blocking ever happened, so a false positive would have cost almost nothing — response proportionality in action.

How much risk does it actually reduce?

The guide's Risk-Reduction Guidance is precise about the mechanism. Monitoring reduces the probability of successful attacks through earlier detection and correlation — the degree depends on the accuracy and timeliness of detection and on the extent to which attackers can evade it. It reduces impact through the response it triggers:

  • Immediate automated responses (blocking, filtering, stopping inference) can reduce impact severity to zero when attacks are detected with sufficient confidence — but overly aggressive responses risk false positives that disrupt legitimate use or cause unintended system malfunction.
  • Follow-up responses (investigation, rollback, throttling, enhanced monitoring) significantly reduce impact when attacks unfold over time — limiting the amount of sensitive data extracted, or containing the blast radius of a compromised model or session. Effectiveness depends on response speed, operational readiness, and the severity of downstream consequences, including non-technical effects such as user trust, availability, and reputational impact.

The summary sentence is worth quoting: monitoring provides its strongest risk reduction when detection quality, response proportionality, and operational readiness are aligned. Weakness in any leg collapses the stool.

⚠ Common misconception

"We ship logs to the SIEM, so we monitor use." No — conventional SIEM rules watch system events. AI monitoring must additionally watch model behaviour, inference patterns, and semantic signals in inputs and outputs, and correlate across controls and time. ISO 27002's 8.15/8.16 formally "cover" this control, but the guide flags the gap explicitly: the ISO controls contain no details on looking for specific patterns of AI attacks through use. Compliance with ISO 27002 is not the same as detecting a model-inversion campaign.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Log all four context layers (trace, request, processing, response) plus model version; retain per legal/contractual requirements.
  • Make logs tamper-evident: per-event signed receipts + independent RFC 3161 time anchor; plan hybrid/post-quantum signatures for long retention horizons.
  • Wire every detection control's signals into one correlation point; define suspicion criteria per monitored risk.
  • Write a documented response (playbook/SOP) for every detection: automated vs. human, escalation paths.
  • Track AI lifecycle events (deploys, rollbacks, prompt/parameter changes, safeguard changes) as first-class signals.
  • Enrich alerts with next-step guidance; include unique alert id, timestamp, threat classification, source, severity, request/response context.
  • Review thresholds periodically against detection accuracy, usability, and alert fatigue; feed incidents back into tests and risk assessments.
Immediate automated blocking can reduce attack impact to zero. Why doesn't the guide recommend maximizing automated blocking everywhere?
Because overly aggressive responses introduce false positives that disrupt legitimate use or cause unintended system malfunction. Response selection must be proportional — driven by detection confidence, threat type, and potential impact. Low-confidence detections belong on lower rungs: log-only, or soft responses like noise addition and enhanced sampling.
What does the signed-receipt approach bind together, and why must the time anchor be independent?
It binds a hash of input, output, and processing context to a per-event signature. The time anchor (e.g., an RFC 3161 Time-Stamp Authority) must be independent so that "when" cannot be backdated by whoever holds the signing key — otherwise a compromised operator could forge event history.
Name the three things the guide says monitoring fundamentally depends on.
(1) The completeness and accuracy of logged data, (2) the ability to correlate signals meaningfully, and (3) legal and privacy constraints on data retention. High-volume or opaque systems may further limit visibility.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 123–129, permalink owaspai.org/go/monitoruse/. OpenCRE: Monitor inference → ENISA Table 5 (define and monitor indicators for proper functioning of the model); MITRE ATLAS AML.M0024 (AI Telemetry Logging). ISO 27002 controls 8.15 Logging and 8.16 Monitoring activities — full coverage but no AI-attack-pattern detail. ISO/IEC 42001 B.6.2.6 (AI system operation and monitoring) — full coverage at high abstraction. Secure logging: RFC 3161 TSA, IETF draft-marques-asqav-compliance-receipts-04 (receipt obligations under EU AI Act, DORA, US sector regulations). Canon: NIST AI RMF MEASURE/MANAGE; ISO/IEC 27090 (AI security guidance, under development) will elaborate AI-specific monitoring.

Key Points

  • #MONITOR USE = observe + correlate + log usage, inputs, outputs and system behaviour; reconstruct incidents; extend existing incident detection with AI-specific methods (improper functioning, suspicious use patterns, suspicious inputs/series).
  • Log four layers per event — trace metadata, request context, processing context, response context — plus model version; enrich with system signals (CPU, memory, node, environment) and next-step guidance on alerts.
  • Secure logging means tamper-evident logging: per-event signed receipts bound to an independent RFC 3161 time anchor; plan post-quantum signatures for long retention.
  • Decision ladder: no action → automated response (filter/slow/block) → human investigation; tune thresholds against alert fatigue.
  • Response menu: immediate containment (kill switch, sanitize, conservative mode), follow-up (preserve state, throttle, disable tools, add output noise, rollback), broader actions (notify users/individuals, engage suppliers, legal/compliance), learning loop.
  • Automated response can cut impact to zero but risks false positives; strongest risk reduction requires detection quality + response proportionality + operational readiness together.
  • AI particularity: monitor model behaviour, inference patterns, and semantic signals — not just system events; ISO 27002 covers the control but not the AI attack patterns.
Module 06 · §2.0 Threats through use · OWASP AI Exchange

#RATE LIMIT — Pricing Out the Attacker

~14 minCovers guide pp. 130–132

Most AI attacks through use are not lightning strikes — they are experiments: thousands of queries probing for the input that fools, inverts, or clones your model. Rate limiting attacks the experiment, not the exploit. This lesson teaches you the arithmetic that turns "queries per minute" into "days of attacker effort", the attack-by-attack query budgets, and the two attack classes rate limiting cannot touch.

THREAT
Model theft through use (model exfiltration)Large-scale harvesting of input–output pairs to re-train a copy of the model; query volume proportional to input-space diversity. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM10, MITRE ATLAS AML.T0024.
#RATE LIMITThrottle model access frequency, preferably per actorruntime · information security · input threats
IntentLimit the rate (frequency) of access to the model — preferably per actor (user, API key or session) — not only to prevent resource exhaustion but to severely slow down the experimentation underlying many AI attacks through use: searching for adversarial/evasion samples and jailbreaks, data-poisoning exploration and extraction of exposure-restricted data, prompt-injection experimentation, model inversion and membership inference, training-data or model-parameter extraction, and model exfiltration via large-scale harvesting. Raising the cost (effort, time, resources) makes attacks less practical and opens a window for detection and incident response.
Implement(a) Per-actor limiting: track and limit inference frequency per authenticated user id, API key, or session token; approximate via IP or device fingerprint when identity is unavailable or unreliable. (b) Total-use limiting: an overall cap across all actors against distributed or collusive attacks — fixed or sliding windows, adaptive limits, risk-based dynamic throttling. (c) Optimize & calibrate: base thresholds on usage analytics or theoretical workload; lower limits raise security but hurt UX — tune to acceptable residual risk. (d) Detection & response: breaching a limit must trigger event logging and potential incident workflows — integrate with #MONITOR USE and incident response (#SEC PROGRAM). Complement with #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL and detection mechanisms.
LimitsLow-frequency or single-try attacks (e.g., prompt injection or indirect leakage) are unaffected. Attackers may circumvent limits via parallel access, multi-instance use, or a transferability attack. Effective rate limiting can differ from configured limits due to multi-accounting or multi-model instances — factor this into risk evaluation.
StandardsOpenCRE "Rate limiting against AI input attacks" (ETSI 6.3.3 Limit the number of queries; NIST AI 100-2 §2.4.5 Limiting user queries; MITRE ATLAS AML.M0004 Restrict Number of AI Model Queries). ISO 27002 has no control for this. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/ratelimit/

Why frequency is the attacker's lifeline

The objective section is a catalogue of everything an attacker does with volume. Rate limiting exists to delay and discourage attackers who rely on many model interactions to:

  • Search for adversarial or evasion samples — pairs of (successful attack, unwanted output) data are useful for constructing evasion attacks and jailbreaks,
  • Perform data-poisoning exploration and extract exposure-restricted data,
  • Experiment with direct and indirect prompt-injection techniques — to exploit the system and/or study its behaviour,
  • Attempt model inversion and/or membership inference,
  • Extract training data or model parameters,
  • Copy or re-train a model via large-scale harvesting (model exfiltration).

By restricting the number and speed of interactions, the cost of attacks increases (effort, time, resources), making them less practical — and, crucially, allowing an opportunity for detection and incident response. That second effect is often the more valuable one: a rate limit converts a five-minute smash-and-grab into a week-long campaign that #MONITOR USE and #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING have time to spot.

The arithmetic of delay

Rate limiting slows attacks rather than preventing them outright. To evaluate effectiveness, the guide gives you a method: estimate how many inferences an attack requires, and calculate the delay imposed. The AI system's intended use, current best practices, and existing attack tests serve as indicators.

▸ Worked example (guide p. 131)

An attack needing 10,000 interactions at a limit of 1 per minute takes approximately 167 hours — about 7 days of continuous querying. That may move the residual risk below acceptance thresholds, especially if detection is active: seven days of machine-regular, minute-by-minute probing from one actor is exactly what series-detection is built to catch.

To run that arithmetic you need query budgets per attack class. The guide provides typical inference volumes for attack feasibility:

AttackTypical query volume on the targetNotes (guide pp. 131–132)
Evasion attacks & model inversionThousands of queriesWhen the attacker has no knowledge of the model; with full knowledge, typically an order of magnitude fewer
Adversarial patchesTens of queriesSmall, localized changes to inputs need far less search
Transfer attacksZero queries on the targetThe attack is performed on a similar surrogate model
Membership inference1 to manyDepends on the dataset: known target vs. scanning a large list of possible individuals
Model exfiltration (input–output replication)Proportional to input-space diversityRicher input spaces need more harvesting
Sensitive-training-data extraction / model manipulation (e.g., prompt injection)Dozens to hundreds of crafted inputs — sometimes far fewerDon't always rely on trial-and-error; attackers often reuse standard, pre-designed inputs known to expose weaknesses
⚔ Attacker's view

Read that table as my playbook. If my attack sits in the "thousands of queries" row and you cap me at one per minute, you've cost me a week per attempt and lit me up on your dashboards — I'll go find a surrogate model instead (zero queries, zero exposure). If my attack sits in the "tens" or "one" rows — an adversarial patch, a known jailbreak string, a targeted membership check — your rate limit is irrelevant; I'm done before the token bucket refills. Rate limiting partitions the attack space: it defends against search, never against known answers.

Where it applies — and where it must not

Whether and how to rate-limit is defined by risk management (see #RISK ANALYSIS). It is a primary control against many input threats. Some systems enjoy natural rate limits because their context inherently restricts query rates — e.g., medical imaging or human-supervised processes where a human is in the loop for every inference. But exceptions apply where throttling would block intended safety-critical or real-time functions:

  • Emergency dispatch or medical triage models,
  • Cybersecurity monitoring that must analyze all traffic,
  • Real-time identity or fraud detection under strict latency constraints.

And the recurring responsibility rule: when rate limiting is impractical for the provider but feasible for the deployer, the responsibility must be clearly delegated and documented (see #SEC PROGRAM).

Implementation: the four-step recipe

  1. Per-actor limiting. Track and limit inference frequency for each identifiable actor (authenticated user id, API key, session token). If identity is unavailable or unreliable (e.g., no access control), approximate using IP address or device fingerprint. This is what lets you distinguish legitimate use from brute-force experimentation.
  2. Total-use limiting. Set an overall cap across all actors to mitigate distributed or collusive attacks that stay under every per-actor limit. Use fixed or sliding windows, adaptive limits, or dynamic throttling based on risk.
  3. Optimize & calibrate. Base thresholds on usage analytics or theoretical workload to balance availability against risk reduction. Lower limits increase security but may hurt user experience — tune for acceptable residual risk, possibly with the help of additional controls.
  4. Detection & response. Breaching a rate limit must trigger event logging and potential incident workflows. Integrate with #MONITOR USE and incident response (see #SEC PROGRAM).

Complement this control with #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL, #MONITOR USE, and detection mechanisms — per-actor limits are only as strong as your ability to identify actors, which is the next lesson's subject.

How rate limiting converts attacker volume into defender time
Attackerneeds ~10,000 probe queries
→
#RATE LIMIT1 query / minute / actor
→
#MONITOR USE + series detectionweek-long window to detect
→
Model protectedaccount suspended before search completes

Particularity and limitations

Particularity: unlike traditional IT rate limiting — which protects performance — here the primary purpose is mitigating security threats to AI systems through experimentation. The performance benefits still come along for free: stability, cost control, and DoS resilience.

Limitations — know exactly what you have not bought:

  • Low-frequency or single-try attacks remain unaffected — e.g., prompt injection or indirect leakage that succeeds on the first, perfectly ordinary-looking request.
  • Attackers may circumvent limits by parallel access or multi-instance use (many accounts, many IPs), or through a transferability attack (develop on a surrogate, fire once).
  • The guide's calibration note: effective rate limiting can differ from configured limits due to multi-accounting or multi-model instances — your risk evaluation must use the effective number, not the config value.
⚠ Common misconception

"We set 60 requests/minute per API key, so an attacker gets 60 requests/minute." Only if the attacker has exactly one API key. Fifty free-tier accounts make your effective limit 3,000/minute. This is why #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL's anti-multi-account measures (MFA, CAPTCHA, identity verification) and a total-use cap are part of the same defence, and why the guide insists the risk evaluation consider multi-accounting explicitly.

An attacker uses a known, pre-designed jailbreak prompt that works on the first try. What did your rate limit do?
Nothing. Rate limiting defends against attacks that need many interactions (search/experimentation). Single-try or low-frequency attacks — prompt injection with standard known-bad inputs, indirect leakage — are explicitly listed as unaffected. You need input/output handling controls and monitoring for those.
Why does the guide require rate-limit breaches to trigger event logging and incident workflows rather than just returning HTTP 429?
Because in the AI context a breached rate limit is a security signal, not just a performance guardrail. Someone hammering an inference endpoint is plausibly running an experiment-based attack; the breach should feed #MONITOR USE for correlation with other signals and potentially start an incident workflow (#SEC PROGRAM).
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 130–132, permalink owaspai.org/go/ratelimit/. OpenCRE: Rate limiting against AI input attacks → ETSI §6.3.3 (Limit the number of queries), NIST AI 100-2 §2.4.5 (Limiting user queries), MITRE ATLAS AML.M0004 (Restrict Number of AI Model Queries). Further reading linked by the guide: an article on token-bucket and leaky-bucket rate limiting, and the OWASP Denial of Service Cheat Sheet featuring rate limiting. ISO 27002 has no control for rate limiting — an acknowledged standards gap. Canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM10 (Unbounded Consumption) names rate limiting as a core mitigation.

Key Points

  • #RATE LIMIT throttles access frequency, preferably per actor (user, API key, session; IP/device fingerprint as fallback) — to slow attack experimentation, not just protect performance.
  • It targets volume-hungry attacks: evasion-sample search, poisoning exploration, prompt-injection experimentation, model inversion, membership inference, data/parameter extraction, model exfiltration.
  • Evaluate effectiveness arithmetically: 10,000 needed queries at 1/minute ≈ 167 hours ≈ 7 days — enough delay to detect and respond.
  • Query budgets: evasion/inversion = thousands (10× less with model knowledge); adversarial patches = tens; transfer attacks = zero on target; membership inference = 1–many; exfiltration ∝ input-space diversity; prompt injection = dozens–hundreds or pre-designed one-shots.
  • Four implementation steps: per-actor limiting, total-use limiting (vs. distributed/collusive attacks), calibration to residual risk, and breach → logging + incident workflow.
  • Exceptions: safety-critical/real-time functions (emergency dispatch, triage, security monitoring, real-time fraud detection); delegate to deployer explicitly when the provider can't implement.
  • Limits: useless against single-try attacks and transferability; effective limits differ from configured limits under multi-accounting.
Module 06 · §2.0 Threats through use · OWASP AI Exchange

#MODEL ACCESS CONTROL — Know Who Is Querying

~13 minCovers guide pp. 133–136

Every control in this module that says "per actor" is writing a cheque that access control has to cash. Authentication and authorization on the inference interface shrink the attacker population, attach a name to every query, and make rate limits, anomaly detection, and forensics possible. This lesson covers the seven-step implementation, the residual-risk analysis for when authorized users go bad, and the crucial scoping note: this control protects inference, not the model file.

#MODEL ACCESS CONTROLRestrict inference to approved, identifiable usersruntime · information security · input threats
IntentRestrict access to model inference functions to approved and identifiable users through authentication (verifying who is accessing) and authorization (limiting what they can access), so only trusted actors can interact with the model. Reduces input-based and misuse attacks by limiting the number of potential attackers, enables attribution of actions to individuals or systems (adhering to privacy obligations), and strengthens rate limiting, activity monitoring, and incident investigation.
Implement(1) Authenticate users (accounts, API keys, tokens). (2) Apply least privilege — fine-grained access to specific models/features/datasets by sensitivity and user risk profile; role-based and purpose-based permissions per group (developers, testers, operators, end users). (3) Defence-in-depth: enforce at multiple layers (API gateway, application layer, model endpoint) so a single failure does not expose the model. (4) Log successful and failed access attempts (mind privacy obligations for identifiers like IPs, device IDs). (5) Reduce multi-account abuse: raise account-creation cost via MFA, CAPTCHA, identity verification, additional trust checks. (6) Detect and respond: temporarily block after repeated failed authentication; alert on suspicious access behaviour. (7) Integrate: use authenticated identity for per-user rate limiting, anomaly detection, incident reconstruction.
LimitsAttackers may exploit authorized accounts via compromise, insider misuse, or vulnerabilities; some attacks occur within allowed sessions (e.g., indirect prompt injection); publicly available models remain vulnerable without alternative protections. Protects who can query/experiment with the model — not the stored model artifact (see Model Confidentiality in the Periodic Table). Complement with #RATE LIMIT, #MONITOR USE, incident response (#SEC PROGRAM).
StandardsOpenCRE "Access control to AI inference" (ENISA Table 5: identity management, authentication and access control policy compliance for ML applications; MITRE ATLAS AML.M0019 Control Access to AI Models and Data in Production). ISO 27002 controls 5.15, 5.16, 5.18, 5.3, 8.3 — full coverage. OpenCRE on technical and centralized access control. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/modelaccesscontrol/

Authentication + authorization, applied to inference

Two distinct mechanisms, both required: authentication verifies who is accessing; authorization limits what they can access. Together they ensure only trusted actors can interact with the model. The objective is threefold — and the second and third points are what make this control the keystone of the module:

  • Reduce the attacker population. Only authorized users can send requests, so the set of potential attackers shrinks from "the internet" to "our user base".
  • Attribute actions to individuals or systems (adhering to privacy obligations) — deterrence plus forensics.
  • Strengthen related controls: rate limits, activity monitoring, and incident investigation all become per-identity instead of per-guess.

Applicability: whenever AI models are exposed for inference, especially in multi-use or public-facing systems — a primary safeguard against attacks through input or repeated experimentation. Exceptions exist:

  • The model must remain publicly accessible without authentication for its intended use,
  • Legal or regulatory conditions prohibit access control,
  • The physical or operational environment already ensures restricted access — e.g., an on-premise medical device requiring physical presence.

And once more the responsibility rule: if implementation is more practical for the deployer than the provider, document that delegation explicitly in accordance with risk-management policies.

The seven implementation steps

  1. Authenticate users. Actors accessing model inference are typically authenticated — user accounts, API keys, tokens.
  2. Apply least privilege. Grant access only to functions or models necessary for each user's role or purpose. Two refinements:
    • Fine-grained access control: restrict access to specific AI models, features, or datasets based on their sensitivity and the user's risk profile.
    • Role-based and purpose-based permissions: define permissions for different groups — developers, testers, operators, end users — and grant access only for the tasks they must perform.
  3. Apply defence-in-depth. Enforce access control at multiple layers — API gateway, application layer, model endpoint — so a single failure does not expose the model.
  4. Log access events. Record both successful and failed attempts, considering privacy obligations when storing identifiers (IPs, device IDs).
  5. Reduce the risk of multi-account abuse. Attackers create or use multiple accounts to dodge per-user rate limits. Increase the cost of account creation: multifactor authentication, CAPTCHA, identity verification, or additional trust checks.
  6. Detect and respond to suspicious activity. Temporarily block the AI system for users after repeated failed authentication attempts; generate alerts for investigation of suspicious access behaviour.
  7. Integrate with other controls. Use the authenticated identity for per-user rate limiting, anomaly detection, and incident reconstruction.
Defence-in-depth: access control enforced at every layer (guide p. 134)
Actoruser / system / API client
→
API gatewaykeys, tokens, coarse policy
→
Application layerrole- and purpose-based permissions
→
Model endpointfine-grained, per-model access
→
Model inferenceevery query attributed to an identity
◆ Senior insight

Step 5 is the one teams skip and regret. Per-user rate limits (previous lesson) implicitly assume identities are scarce. If a free-tier signup takes ten seconds and an email address, identities cost nothing and every per-actor control in this module degrades to its total-use fallback. The guide's remedy is economic, not technical: MFA, CAPTCHA, identity verification, and trust checks don't make accounts impossible to farm — they make each account cost enough that farming 500 of them stops being the cheapest attack path. Decide the account-creation cost as deliberately as you decide the rate limit.

Risk reduction and residual risk

Access control lowers the probability of attacks by reducing the number of actors who can interact with the model and by linking actions to identities. That traceability delivers:

  • Individualized rate limiting and behavioural detection,
  • Faster containment and forensic reconstruction of attacks,
  • Better accountability and deterrence for malicious use.

But you must analyze the residual risk honestly, by estimating:

  • The likelihood that an attacker already belongs to an authorized user group — an insider, or a legitimately authorized external user, can still misuse access to conduct attacks through the model,
  • The chance that authorized users are compromised — phishing, session hijacking, password theft, coercion,
  • The likelihood of bypassing the authentication or authorization mechanisms themselves,
  • The exposure level of systems that require open access.
⚔ Attacker's view

Access control changes my acquisition problem, not my attack. Options, in rising cost: sign up legitimately (if you allow it, I'm now an "authorized user" — your control did nothing but name me); buy stolen credentials or hijack a session (cheap, commodity market); phish a real user; or find an authz bypass in your gateway. And note what I never need an account for: if your system ingests external content, indirect prompt injection rides in inside another user's perfectly authorized session — the guide lists exactly this as a limitation.

The AI particularity: protecting queries, not files

In AI systems, access control protects model endpoints and data-dependent inference rather than static resources. Traditional IT access control safeguards files or databases; this control restricts who can query or experiment with a model. Two consequences the guide draws out:

  • Even publicly available models benefit from identity-based tracking — not to deny access, but to enable rate limits, anomaly detection, and incident handling per identity.
  • Scope boundary: this control governs access to model inference, not the protection of a stored model file. For protection of trained model artifacts, see "Model Confidentiality" in the Runtime and Development sections of the guide's Periodic Table of controls.
⚠ Common misconception

"Our model is public by design, so access control doesn't apply to us." Wrong conclusion. The guide is explicit that even publicly available models benefit from identity-based tracking — issue free API keys, track sessions, fingerprint devices. You aren't gating access; you're creating the per-actor identity that rate limiting, series detection, and forensics require. A public model with no notion of identity has effectively opted out of four of this module's six controls.

Limitations

  • Attackers may still exploit authorized accounts via compromise, insider misuse, or vulnerabilities.
  • Some attacks occur within allowed sessions — e.g., indirect prompt injection, where the malicious payload arrives through content an authorized user legitimately submitted.
  • Publicly available models remain vulnerable if alternative protections are not in place.

Complement this control with #RATE LIMIT, #MONITOR USE, and incident response (#SEC PROGRAM).

Why does defence-in-depth matter specifically for model access control?
Because enforcement at a single layer (say, only the API gateway) means one misconfiguration or bypass exposes the model completely. Enforcing at the gateway, the application layer, and the model endpoint means an attacker must defeat multiple independent checks — a single failure no longer exposes the model.
Your medical-imaging model runs on an on-premise device that requires physical presence to operate. Do you still need API-level authentication?
This is one of the guide's explicit exceptions: when the physical or operational environment already ensures restricted access, formal access control on the inference API may be unnecessary. But document that decision through risk management — and reconsider the moment the device gains any network-facing interface.
Access control is in place. Which two attack situations from the guide can still defeat it?
(1) The attacker is, or compromises, an authorized user — insider misuse, phishing, session hijacking, password theft, coercion. (2) The attack happens inside an allowed session — e.g., indirect prompt injection carried in content processed on behalf of a legitimate user. Both are why access control must be complemented by rate limiting, monitoring, and incident response.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 133–136, permalink owaspai.org/go/modelaccesscontrol/. OpenCRE: Access control to AI inference → ENISA Table 5 (ensure ML applications comply with identity management, authentication, and access control policies); MITRE ATLAS AML.M0019 (Control Access to AI Models and Data in Production). Technical access control: ISO 27002 controls 5.15 (access control), 5.16 (identity management), 5.18 (access rights), 5.3 (segregation of duties), 8.3 (information access restriction) — full coverage. The guide also links OpenCRE pages on technical access control and centralized access control. Canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 lists unauthorized inference access under LLM10; NIST AI RMF GOVERN/MANAGE cover access policy.

Key Points

  • #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL = authentication (who) + authorization (what) on model inference functions; primary safeguard against attacks through input and repeated experimentation.
  • It shrinks the attacker population, attributes every query to an identity, and powers per-actor rate limiting, anomaly detection, and forensics.
  • Seven steps: authenticate; least privilege (fine-grained + role/purpose-based); defence-in-depth across gateway/application/endpoint; log successes and failures; raise account-creation cost (MFA, CAPTCHA, identity verification); block-and-alert on suspicious access; integrate identity with other controls.
  • Residual risk: attacker already authorized (insider), authorized user compromised (phishing/hijack/theft/coercion), authn/authz bypass, and open-access exposure.
  • AI particularity: protects who can query/experiment with a model — not stored model files (that's Model Confidentiality in the Periodic Table); even public models need identity-based tracking.
  • Limits: in-session attacks like indirect prompt injection pass straight through; complement with #RATE LIMIT, #MONITOR USE, #SEC PROGRAM.
  • Exceptions: intentionally public models, legal prohibitions, or physically restricted environments — documented via risk management, with provider→deployer delegation made explicit.
Module 06 · §2.0 Threats through use · OWASP AI Exchange

#ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING — The Anomaly-Detection Taxonomy

~16 minCovers guide pp. 136–141

Can you spot a malicious input without knowing what malicious looks like? That is the promise — and the trap — of anomalous input handling: flag whatever is statistically "odd" relative to training data. This lesson teaches the full five-part taxonomy (OOD, OD, AD, OSR, ND) through the guide's self-driving-car examples, the similarity-measures toolbox per modality, and the brutal limitation you must be able to recite: adversarial examples are designed to not look anomalous.

#ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLINGDetect and respond to odd or invalid inputruntime · AI engineer · input threats
IntentImplement tools to detect whether input is odd — significantly different from the training data, or even invalid (also called input validation) — and potentially respond, without knowledge of what malicious input looks like. Unusual input is indicative of malicious activity; responses range from ignore, to alert, to stopping inference, to further threat-control steps (see #MONITOR USE). Critical for maintaining model integrity, addressing potential concept drift, and preventing adversarial attacks that exploit model behaviour on out-of-distribution data.
ImplementFollow #MONITOR USE for detection considerations and response options. Techniques span the anomaly-detection taxonomy: OOD detection (similarity measures between training and test data, model introspection for activated neurons, OOD sample generation and retraining, confidence-vector thresholding, supervised contrastive learning, clustering), outlier detection, anomaly detection (activation analysis; similarity-based analysis against a ground-truth reference set using per-modality similarity measures), open set recognition (reject unknowns via decision boundaries), and novelty detection (recognize benign-but-new input and choose a response: trigger another use case, log specifically, or let the model generalize).
LimitsNot all anomalous input is malicious, and not all malicious input is anomalous. Adversarial input can be specifically crafted to bypass anomaly detection. For evasion attacks, deviation-based detection is often ineffective because adversarial samples are designed to appear similar to normal input by definition — though some forms, like adversarial patches, may still produce detectable anomalies. Applicability hinges on detection rate vs. implementation effort, performance penalty, and false-positive rate; only a representative test on normal use gives the required insight, with the threshold set where false positives remain acceptable.
StandardsOpenCRE "Anomalous AI input handling" (ETSI 6.2.3 Adversarial example detection; ENISA Table 5: implement tools to detect if a data point is an adversarial example or not; NIST AI 100-2 §2.2.4 Randomized smoothing; MITRE ATLAS AML.M0015 Adversarial Input Detection). Not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards; ENISA Annex C: ensure the model is sufficiently resilient to its operating environment. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/anomalousinputhandling/

Detection without a signature

The core idea: detect whether input is odd — significantly different from the training data, or even invalid (which the guide notes is also called input validation) — without knowledge of what malicious input looks like. This is the inverse of classic security detection: no signatures, no rules describing the attack; instead, a statistical model of normal, and suspicion of everything outside it. The guide's Particularity section names this directly: unlike conventional systems that rely on predefined rules or signatures, AI systems typically rely on pattern-based detection (statistical/behavioural) in contrast to rule-based detection.

Anomalous input is suspicious for every attack through use, because attackers behave differently than normal users. But applicability depends on a cost/benefit calculation the guide spells out — the successful detection rate weighed against:

  1. implementation effort,
  2. performance penalty,
  3. the number of false positives, which can hinder users, security operations, or both.

Only a representative test provides the required insight: test the detection on normal use, and set the threshold at the level where the false-positive rate is still acceptable. Response options mirror #MONITOR USE: ignore, issue an alert, stop inference, or take further steps to control the threat.

The five-part taxonomy

Out-of-Distribution Detection (OOD), Novelty Detection (ND), Outlier Detection (OD), Anomaly Detection (AD), and Open Set Recognition (OSR) are all related and sometimes overlapping tasks that deal with unexpected or unseen data — but each has its own specific focus and methodology. In practice, the techniques used may be similar or the same. The guide teaches them through one running example: a machine-learning system for a self-driving car.

flowchart TD
  OOD["OOD - Out-of-Distribution Detection: input differs from training distribution - the broad category"] --> OD["OD - Outlier Detection: differs from the majority of data - a form of OOD"]
  OOD --> AD["AD - Anomaly Detection: irregularity that signals a problem or rare event - a form of OOD"]
  AD --> OSR["OSR - Open Set Recognition: classify knowns, reject unknowns via decision boundaries - a way to perform AD"]
  OOD --> ND["ND - Novelty Detection: OOD input recognized as benign and of interest"]

Figure — the anomaly-detection taxonomy and how its parts relate (guide pp. 137–139).

Out-of-Distribution Detection (OOD) — the broad category

Definition: identifying data points that differ significantly from the distribution of the training data. OOD is the broadest concept, and can include aspects of novelty, anomaly, and outlier detection depending on context.

Self-driving car: the system is trained on vehicles, pedestrians, and common animals like dogs and cats. One day it encounters a horse on the street. The system needs to recognize the horse as an out-of-distribution object.

Methods incorporate approaches from outlier detection, anomaly detection, novelty detection, and open set recognition, using techniques like:

  • similarity measures between training and test data,
  • model introspection for activated neurons,
  • OOD sample generation and retraining.

Approaches such as thresholding the output confidence vector classify inputs as in- or out-of-distribution, assuming higher confidence for in-distribution examples. Supervised contrastive learning — where a deep neural network learns to group similar classes together while separating different ones — and various clustering methods further enhance the ability to distinguish in-distribution from OOD inputs. For depth, the guide points to the survey by Yang et al. ("Generalized out-of-distribution detection: a survey", arXiv:2110.11334, 2021) and further resources on the learnability of OOD.

Outlier Detection (OD) — a form of OOD

Definition: identifying data points that are significantly different from the majority of the data. Outliers can be anomalies or novel instances — but not all outliers are necessarily out-of-distribution.

Self-driving car: the system is trained on cars and trucks moving at typical city speeds. One day it detects a car moving significantly faster than all the others. That car is an outlier in the context of normal traffic behaviour — a perfectly in-distribution object exhibiting extreme behaviour.

Anomaly Detection (AD) — a form of OOD

Definition: identifying abnormal or irregular instances that raise suspicion by differing significantly from the majority of the data. Anomalies can be outliers, and might also be out-of-distribution — but the key aspect is their significance: they indicate a problem or rare event.

Self-driving car: a vehicle going the wrong way down a one-way street. Not just an outlier — an anomaly indicating a potentially dangerous situation.

The guide gives two implementation patterns for AD:
