  • Activation Analysis: examine the activations of different layers in a neural network — adversarial inputs produce unusual internal activation patterns even when the input looks normal from outside. These anomalies serve as attack-detection signals.
  • Similarity-based analysis: compare incoming input against a ground-truth data set — typically the training data, representing the normal input space. If the input is sufficiently dissimilar from this reference data, treat it as deviating from expected behaviour and flag it. The right similarity metric depends on the modality:
ModalitySimilarity Measures — RecommendedNotes or Tools
TextCosine similarity, Jaccard Index, Embedding distance (e.g., BERT, Sentence-BERT), Word/Token HistogramsUse transformer-based embeddings
ImageStructural Similarity Index (SSIM), Euclidean distance, Pixel-Wise MSE, Perceptual Loss (VGG-based)Normalize lighting or scaling; patch-based SSIM helps detect targeted attacks in specific image regions
AudioMFCC-based distance, Dynamic Time Warping (DTW), Spectral Convergence, Cosine similarity on embeddingsUse frame-wise comparison for streaming; DTW corrects time shifts
TabularEuclidean distance, Mahalanobis distance, Correlation coefficient, Gower distanceEnsure normalization and categorical encoding before analysis; Mahalanobis distance offers strong outlier detection

Table — similarity measures per modality for similarity-based anomaly detection (guide pp. 138–139).

Open Set Recognition (OSR) — a way to perform Anomaly Detection

Definition: classifying known classes while identifying and rejecting unknown classes during testing. OSR performs anomaly detection by recognizing when an instance does not belong to any learned category — making use of the model's decision boundaries.

Self-driving car: the system identifies known objects — cars, trucks, pedestrians, bicycles. Then it encounters a fallen tree. It must classify the object as "unknown". OSR is critical because the system must recognize that this object fits none of its known categories, rather than force-fitting it into the nearest one.

Novelty Detection (ND) — OOD input recognized as not malicious

Definition: OOD input can sometimes be recognized as benign, relevant, or of interest. The system then decides how to respond: trigger another use case, log it specifically, or let the model process the input if it can be expected to generalize to a sufficiently accurate result.

Self-driving car: the system was trained on various car models, then encounters a newly released model it has never seen. Novelty detection recognizes it as a new car type — but understands it is still a car: a novel instance within a known category. Stopping the vehicle would be the wrong response; generalizing is the right one.

◆ Senior insight

The taxonomy is not academic hair-splitting — each type implies a different response policy. OD says "extreme but maybe fine" (watch it). AD says "this pattern means trouble" (act). OSR says "I don't know what this is" (reject or defer to a human). ND says "new but benign" (generalize or route to another use case). If your detector outputs only a single binary "weird / not weird" flag, you've thrown away the information that decides whether the correct response is blocking, alerting, or doing nothing — and you'll pay for it in false positives or missed incidents.

Risk reduction, and the limitation you must memorize

Risk-Reduction Guidance: detecting anomalous input is critical to maintaining model integrity, addressing potential concept drift (the world changing out from under your training distribution), and preventing adversarial attacks that take advantage of model behaviour on out-of-distribution data.

Limitations — the guide is unusually blunt here:

  • Not all anomalous input is malicious, and not all malicious input is anomalous.
  • There are examples of adversarial input specifically crafted to bypass anomalous-input detection.
  • Detection mechanisms may not identify all malicious inputs, and some anomalous inputs may be benign or relevant.
  • For evasion attacks, detecting anomalous input is often ineffective, because adversarial samples are specifically designed to appear similar to normal input by definition. Deviation-based methods miss them. The exception: some forms of evasion, such as adversarial patches, may still produce detectable anomalies — a visible sticker is statistically odd even if the classifier is fooled.
⚠ Common misconception

"We deployed OOD detection, so we're covered against adversarial examples." Backwards. A well-crafted diffuse-perturbation evasion input is engineered to sit inside the normal input distribution — that's the definition of the attack. OOD detection catches the horse on the road, the garbage input, the drifted distribution, and maybe the adversarial patch; it largely does not catch the imperceptibly perturbed stop sign. For that you need evasion-specific controls (#EVASION INPUT HANDLING, robust training — next module).

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Model "normal" from your actual training/production distribution; pick similarity measures per modality from the table above.
  • Calibrate thresholds on a representative test of normal use; fix the false-positive budget first, then read off the detection rate you actually bought.
  • Combine externally visible checks (similarity to reference data) with internal ones (activation analysis) — attackers must then fool both the input space and the network's internals.
  • Give each taxonomy outcome its own response: OD → observe, AD → alert/act, OSR → reject or defer, ND → generalize or reroute; wire all of it into #MONITOR USE.
  • Re-baseline periodically: concept drift silently turns tomorrow's normal into today's anomaly.
A car drives significantly faster than all surrounding traffic; another car drives the wrong way down a one-way street. Which taxonomy label fits each, and why does the difference matter?
The fast car is an outlier (OD): significantly different from the majority, but not necessarily meaningful. The wrong-way car is an anomaly (AD): the deviation's significance is that it indicates a problem or dangerous situation. The difference drives the response — outliers get watched; anomalies demand action.
Why is anomalous input handling often ineffective against evasion attacks, and what evasion sub-type is the exception?
Adversarial samples for evasion are specifically designed to appear similar to normal input — by definition they sit inside the expected distribution, so deviation-based detection misses them. The exception is adversarial patches: their localized, visible modifications may still produce detectable anomalies (e.g., via patch-based SSIM).
How does the guide say you should set the detection threshold?
Run a representative test of the detector against normal use, and set the threshold at the level where the false-positive rate is still acceptable. Applicability then follows from weighing the resulting detection rate against implementation effort, performance penalty, and the false-positive impact on users and security operations.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 136–141, permalink owaspai.org/go/anomalousinputhandling/. OpenCRE: Anomalous AI input handling → ETSI §6.2.3 (Adversarial example detection); ENISA Table 5 (implement tools to detect if a data point is an adversarial example or not); NIST AI 100-2 §2.2.4 (Randomized smoothing); MITRE ATLAS AML.M0015 (Adversarial Input Detection). Papers cited by the guide: Hendrycks & Gimpel, "A baseline for detecting misclassified and out-of-distribution examples in neural networks" (arXiv:1610.02136, ICLR 2017); Yang et al., "Generalized out-of-distribution detection: a survey" (arXiv:2110.11334, 2021); Khosla et al., "Supervised contrastive learning" (NeurIPS 33, 2020, pp. 18661–18673); Sehwag et al., "Analyzing the robustness of open-world machine learning" (12th ACM Workshop on AI and Security, 2019). Standards gap: not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards; ENISA Securing Machine Learning Algorithms Annex C: "Ensure that the model is sufficiently resilient to the environment in which it will operate."

Key Points

  • #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING detects "odd" input — significantly different from training data or invalid — without knowing what malicious looks like; responses: ignore, alert, stop inference, or escalate via #MONITOR USE.
  • Taxonomy: OOD (broad category — the horse), OD (extreme vs. majority — the speeding car), AD (significant, problem-indicating — the wrong-way car), OSR (reject unknown classes via decision boundaries — the fallen tree), ND (benign novelty — the new car model; generalize, don't block).
  • OOD methods: train/test similarity measures, neuron-activation introspection, OOD sample generation + retraining, confidence-vector thresholding, supervised contrastive learning, clustering.
  • AD implementations: activation analysis (internal signals) and similarity-based analysis against ground truth using per-modality metrics — cosine/Jaccard/embeddings (text), SSIM/MSE/perceptual loss (image), MFCC/DTW (audio), Mahalanobis/Gower (tabular).
  • Calibrate on normal use: fix acceptable false-positive rate, then judge detection value against implementation effort and performance penalty.
  • Hard limitation: not all anomalous input is malicious and not all malicious input is anomalous; evasion samples are designed to look normal — deviation-based detection largely fails on them (adversarial patches excepted).
  • AI particularity: pattern-based (statistical/behavioural) detection instead of the rule/signature-based detection of conventional systems; no ISO/IEC coverage yet.
Module 06 · §2.0 Threats through use · OWASP AI Exchange

#UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING & #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE — Catching Campaigns, Starving Optimizers

~15 minCovers guide pp. 141–146

Two controls close out the generic set, and they are mirror images. Series handling watches across inputs: no single request is suspicious, but a thousand near-duplicates with slowly rising confidence is a fingerprint. Obscure confidence starves that same optimization loop of its feedback signal. Together they attack the two things every iterative attack needs — repetition and information.

THREAT
Iterative probing: model inversion, membership inference, evasion search, model exfiltrationAttacks that only become visible — and only become efficient — across many inputs and rich outputs. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM06/LLM10, MITRE ATLAS AML.T0024, AML.T0015.

Part 1 — #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING

#UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLINGDetect suspicious patterns across a series of inputsruntime · AI engineer · input threats
IntentDetect and respond to suspicious or unwanted patterns across a series of inputs indicating abuse, reconnaissance, or multistep attacks — behaviour across multiple inputs rather than adversarial properties of a single sample. Attacks like model inversion, evasion search, and model exfiltration rely on iterative probing; individually each input looks benign. Secondary benefits: improved abuse monitoring, better attribution of malicious behaviour, stronger signals for investigation and response.
ImplementFollow #MONITOR USE for detection and response. Three concepts: (1) Statistical analysis of input series per user — small input-space deviations with generally increasing confidence values indicate inversion/evasion search; very random, very uniform, or input-space-covering series indicate exfiltration. (2) Behaviour-based detection — significantly higher-than-normal inference counts per actor over a period, complementing #RATE LIMIT. (3) Input optimization pattern detection — unusually high similarity across many inputs from one actor: cluster input series for dense groups of highly similar inputs; measure pairwise similarity within a time window (not just consecutive requests); analyze frequency and distribution of similar inputs to separate systematic probing from benign repetition. Considering a broader range of past inputs defeats attackers who alternate probes with unrelated requests. Rate-based signals add context.
LimitsLegitimate users can look like attackers (systematic testing, research-driven exploration). Attackers can distribute inputs across multiple identities or sources to reduce detectability. Does not prevent attacks on its own — most effective combined with rate limiting, access control, and investigation workflows. Effectiveness depends on baseline modeling of normal behaviour, careful false-positive tuning, and the ability to reliably group inputs by actor, source, or context.
StandardsOpenCRE "Unwanted AI input series handling" (ETSI 6.2.3 Adversarial example detection; ENISA Table 5: implement tools to detect if a data point is an adversarial example or not; MITRE ATLAS AML.M0015 Adversarial Input Detection). Not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/unwantedinputserieshandling/

Why the series is the signal

Many attacks — model inversion, evasion search, model exfiltration — rely on iterative probing rather than a single malicious input. Analyzed one at a time, every request looks benign; analyzed together, the campaign is unmistakable. The objective is to surface reconnaissance, abuse, and multistep attacks that would otherwise appear harmless at the individual-input level, with secondary benefits in abuse monitoring, attribution, and investigation.

Applicability: systems allowing repeated interaction over time — APIs, chat-based models, decision services exposed to external users — especially where many inputs can come from the same actor, source, or session. It is less applicable where inputs are isolated, rate-limited by design, or physically constrained, and its effectiveness depends on the ability to reliably group inputs by actor, source, or context (which is what #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL provides).

The three detection concepts

  1. Statistical analysis of input series. Adversarial attacks follow patterns visible per-user:
    • A series of small deviations in the input space indicates a search — model inversion or an evasion attack. These attacks also typically show a series of inputs with a general increase of confidence value in the outputs: the attacker's optimizer is climbing a gradient, and you can watch it climb.
    • Inputs that appear systematic — very random, very uniform, or covering the entire input space — may indicate a model exfiltration attack harvesting input–output pairs.
  2. Behaviour-based detection of anomalous input usage. Beyond analyzing individual inputs (see #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING), analyze inference usage: a significantly higher-than-normal number of inferences by a single actor over a defined period is anomalous behaviour and a signal to decide on a response. This complements input-based methods and aligns with rate-limiting principles (see #RATE LIMIT).
  3. Input optimization pattern detection. Attacks that repeatedly adjust inputs to gradually achieve an outcome — finding an adversarial example, extracting sensitive behaviour, manipulating responses (evasion attacks, model inversion, sensitive-training-data-output-from-instructions attacks) — appear as a series of closely related inputs from the same actor. Detection approaches:
    • clustering input series to identify dense groups of highly similar inputs,
    • measuring pairwise similarity across inputs within a time window — not limited to consecutive requests,
    • analyzing the frequency and distribution of similar inputs to distinguish systematic probing from benign repetition.

The window design matters: considering similarity across a broader range of past inputs defeats the evasion strategy where attackers alternate probing inputs with unrelated requests to avoid detection. And signals from rate-based controls — unusually frequent requests — complement similarity analysis with context about suspicious optimization behaviour.

What an optimization campaign looks like in the input stream (guide pp. 142–143)
Attacker sessionprobe v1 · v2 · v3 … near-duplicates
→
Series analysisclustering + windowed similarity + frequency
→
#MONITOR USEqualify, alert, respond
→
Search disruptedbefore an adversarial example is found
◆ Senior insight

The guide's particularity for this control is the sentence to remember: unlike traditional abuse detection, series handling focuses on how models are learned and probed, not on explicit violations or malformed inputs. Classic WAF thinking asks "is this request bad?" — series handling asks "is this actor learning my model?" Many AI-specific attacks only become visible through temporal or statistical analysis of interactions. That reframe also predicts the false-positive class: your own red team, researchers, and power users all "learn the model" too — which is why baselining and investigation workflows, not auto-blocking, are the right response at moderate confidence.

Part 2 — #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE

#OBSCURE CONFIDENCELimit or hide confidence information in outputsruntime · AI engineer · input threats
IntentLimit or hide confidence-related information in model outputs so it cannot be used for optimization-based attacks. Detailed confidence values facilitate model inversion, membership inference, evasion, and model exfiltration by aiding adversarial-sample construction and analysis; reducing precision — or removing the information entirely while still supporting the user task — makes those attacks harder, slower, and less reliable.
Implement(1) Reduce confidence precision: present the minimum detail needed for the intended task — round numbers, use coarse ranges, or remove confidence entirely. (2) Assess impact on accuracy: evaluate any modification of confidence/output to ensure it does not unacceptably degrade the system's intended function or model accuracy. Where confidence is essential (medical decision support, safety-critical decisions), minimize to the least necessary via rounding or noise. Note — confidence-based anomaly detection: unusually high confidence can itself signal misuse (membership inference probes known entities looking for exceptionally high confidence); treat as a weak indicator flagged for follow-up, never an automatic block. Provider→deployer delegation applies when the deployer is better positioned.
LimitsAttackers may still estimate confidence indirectly by probing with small input variations and observing output changes. Does not fully prevent label-only membership inference. Noise or reduced detail can hurt usability or accuracy if not carefully balanced. Can resemble gradient masking for zero-knowledge evasion attacks — known to be a fragile defense if used alone. Effectiveness depends heavily on model architecture, training method, and data distribution: validate through testing and evaluation, never assume.
StandardsOpenCRE "Obscuring confidence in AI output" (ETSI 6.4.3 Obfuscating confidence scores; ENISA Table 5: reduce the information given by the model; MITRE ATLAS AML.M0002 Passive AI Output Obfuscation). Not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/obscureconfidence/

Confidence as a side channel

The guide's particularity here is conceptually the most interesting in the module: in AI systems, confidence values are not just user-facing explanations — they act as side-channel signals that leak sensitive information about the model. Unlike traditional software outputs, probabilistic confidence can reveal internal model behaviour and training characteristics. A softmax vector is a window into the decision boundary; obscuring confidence is therefore a mitigation specifically relevant to machine-learning systems.

What attackers do with rich confidence output (Risk-Reduction Guidance): estimate decision boundaries, infer training-data membership, reverse-engineer the model, and construct adversarial inputs efficiently. Every optimization-based attack in this module runs faster when each query returns a precise gradient-like signal. Take the signal away and the attacker's optimizer goes from gradient ascent to blind search.

Applicability: any AI system whose outputs include confidence scores, probabilities, likelihoods, or similar certainty indicators. Whether it is required is a risk-management decision based on the likelihood of evasion attacks, model inversion or membership inference, and model exfiltration. The exception: when confidence information is essential for the intended use — medical decision support, safety-critical decision-making — keep it, but minimized to the least amount necessary, using techniques like rounding the number or adding noise. And as usual: if the deployer is better positioned than the provider to implement this, the provider must clearly communicate that expectation.

Implementation and the double-edged detection note

  1. Reduce confidence precision. Present confidence with the minimum detail needed for the task — rounding, coarse ranges ("high / medium / low"), or full removal.
  2. Assess impact on accuracy. Evaluate any modification of confidence or output to ensure it does not unacceptably degrade the system's intended function or the model's accuracy.

The guide adds a note that turns this control inside out — confidence-based anomaly detection: in some attack scenarios, unusually high confidence in model output is itself a misuse signal. Membership inference works by probing inputs associated with known entities and watching for the model to respond with exceptionally high confidence (the tell that the sample was in the training set). High confidence is common in normal operation, so it should not automatically block output — but it can be treated as a weak indicator and flagged for follow-up analysis. So: hide confidence from the attacker, but keep watching it yourself.

⚔ Attacker's view

You rounded your scores to two categories? Fine — I'll rebuild the gradient myself: submit an input, then dozens of tiny variations, and watch when your coarse label flips. Each flip point is a sample of your decision boundary. It costs me far more queries (hello, rate limit; hello, series detection — see how these controls chain), but the information leak is reduced, not eliminated. And if I'm running label-only membership inference, I never needed your confidence scores at all. Your defence bought queries and time, not immunity.

⚠ Common misconception

"Hiding confidence scores stops model extraction and evasion." The guide lists four reasons it doesn't, on its own: (1) indirect estimation via small input variations, (2) label-only membership inference works without confidence at all, (3) the usability/accuracy cost of noise can exceed the security benefit if unbalanced, and (4) against zero-knowledge evasion this control can resemble gradient masking — a defence with a documented history of being broken, fragile if used alone. Treat obscured confidence as friction, validated by testing — never as a wall.

▸ Worked example

A health-insurance risk model returns 0.9731 probability scores via API. A membership-inference attacker probes it with records of known individuals: exceptionally high confidence on a specific record suggests that person's data was in training — a privacy breach with regulatory teeth. Mitigation per the guide: the clinical UI keeps a minimized indicator (say, one decimal, or "high/moderate/low" bands — confidence is essential for medical decision support), the public API returns the label only, and the monitoring pipeline flags sessions that repeatedly trigger exceptionally-high-confidence responses on person-like records for follow-up. One control, three coordinated placements.

Closing the loop on the generic six

You now hold all six generic runtime controls. Watch how the last two complete the mesh: #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE forces the attacker into more queries (indirect probing) — which is exactly the behaviour #RATE LIMIT throttles and #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING detects, under identities #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL established, with everything correlated and answered by #MONITOR USE. No single control survives alone; the guide's limitation sections say so explicitly for each one. What none of them fully solve — inputs designed to look normal and succeed in one shot — is where the evasion-specific controls (#EVASION INPUT HANDLING, #DETECT ADVERSARIAL INPUT, #EVASION ROBUST MODEL, #TRAIN ADVERSARIAL, #INPUT DISTORTION, #ADVERSARIAL ROBUST DISTILLATION) pick up in the next module.

An attacker alternates one probing input with five unrelated innocuous requests. Which specific implementation detail of series handling defeats this, and how?
Measuring pairwise similarity across inputs within a time window, not limited to consecutive requests — plus analyzing the frequency and distribution of similar inputs. Considering similarity across a broader range of past inputs means the interleaved decoys don't break the near-duplicate cluster; the dense group of similar probes still emerges.
Why should unusually high output confidence be flagged but not auto-blocked?
Because high confidence is common in perfectly normal operation — auto-blocking would generate mass false positives. But since membership inference manifests as exceptionally high confidence on probed, known-entity inputs, it is a useful weak indicator: flag it for follow-up analysis and correlation with other signals in #MONITOR USE.
What two output-stream signatures does the guide give for distinguishing an inversion/evasion search from a model-exfiltration harvest?
Inversion/evasion search: a series of small deviations in the input space, typically with generally increasing confidence values — an optimizer climbing. Exfiltration: systematic inputs — very random, very uniform, or covering the entire input space — because the attacker is sampling broadly to replicate input–output behaviour.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 141–146. Permalinks: owaspai.org/go/unwantedinputserieshandling/ and owaspai.org/go/obscureconfidence/. OpenCRE: "Unwanted AI input series handling" and "Obscuring confidence in AI output" → ETSI §6.2.3 (Adversarial example detection) and §6.4.3 (Obfuscating confidence scores); ENISA Table 5 (adversarial-example detection tooling; reduce the information given by the model); MITRE ATLAS AML.M0015 (Adversarial Input Detection) and AML.M0002 (Passive AI Output Obfuscation). Both controls: not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. See also #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING for single-input anomaly detection and #EVASION INPUT HANDLING for single-input evasion detection. Canon: label-only membership inference per Choquette-Choo et al. (ICML 2021); gradient-masking fragility per Athalye et al., "Obfuscated Gradients Give a False Sense of Security" (ICML 2018) — consistent with the guide's warning.

Key Points

  • #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING detects behaviour across multiple inputs — abuse, reconnaissance, multistep attacks — that looks benign per input; it needs reliable grouping of inputs by actor/source/context.
  • Three detection concepts: statistical analysis of series (small input deviations + rising confidence = search; systematic/random/uniform coverage = exfiltration), behaviour-based usage detection (abnormal inference volume per actor), and input-optimization pattern detection (clustering, windowed pairwise similarity, frequency/distribution analysis).
  • Windowing across a broad range of past inputs defeats probe-decoy alternation; rate signals add context.
  • Series-handling limits: legitimate testers/researchers look like attackers; attackers distribute across identities; never a standalone control.
  • #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE removes or coarsens confidence output because confidence is a side channel: it lets attackers estimate decision boundaries, infer membership, reverse-engineer, and build adversarial inputs efficiently.
  • Implementation: minimum precision needed for the task (round, coarse ranges, remove) + verify accuracy impact; keep minimized confidence where essential (medical/safety-critical), via rounding or noise.
  • Unusually high confidence is itself a weak misuse indicator (membership-inference probing) — flag for follow-up, don't auto-block.
  • Obscure-confidence limits: indirect estimation via input variations, label-only membership inference, usability/accuracy costs, and gradient-masking-like fragility against zero-knowledge evasion — validate through testing, never assume.
Module 07 · §2.1 Evasion · OWASP AI Exchange

What Evasion Is — Adversarial Examples

~15 minCovers guide pp. 146–149

An evasion attack does not break into your model, steal it, or poison its training data. It simply hands the model an input that looks ordinary to a human but is crafted to make the model give the wrong answer. A stop sign that a car reads as "speed limit 35". A phishing email a filter waves through. This lesson defines evasion precisely, distinguishes it from the attack it is most often confused with (prompt injection), and shows the two figures the guide uses to make the mechanism concrete. By the end you can classify any evasion attempt by its goal and by how the input was manipulated.

THREAT
Evasion (adversarial examples) — §2.1Crafted input forces wrong model output; integrity of model behaviour is compromised. Lifecycle: runtime / through-use. Maps to: MITRE ATLAS AML.T0015 (Evade AI Model), OWASP ML01:2023, NIST AI 100-2 §2.2.

The definition, precisely

Evasion is when an attacker fools an AI system by crafting input to mislead it into performing its task incorrectly. The model behaves correctly on normal data but fails on these malicious inputs. The crafted inputs are called adversarial examples: an input that is a small modification of a normal input, where that small change — a perturbation — produces a large and false change in the output.

The word "small" is doing real work here. A tiny change is (1) harder for a human or an automated anomaly detector to notice, and (2) usually easier to produce. The guide's example: slightly change an email by adding a word so it still reads the same to a person, but the model now decides it is not a phishing message.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 146–149, permalink owaspai.org/go/evasion/. Category: group of input threats. Maps to MITRE ATLAS AML.T0015 (Evade AI Model / "Evade ML model"), ETSI §6.1 (Evasion attacks), ENISA Table 3 (Evasion), OWASP Top 10 for ML ML01:2023 (Input Manipulation Attack), BIML BIML-78(2020) input:1 (Adversarial Examples), NIST AI 100-2 §2.2 (Evasion Attacks).

Impact

Evasion affects the integrity of model behaviour. The downstream harm depends on what the model decides: a fraud-detection model that fails to flag fraud, an autonomous-driving decision that creates a safety issue, reputational damage, and legal liability.

Two goals: targeted vs untargeted

GoalWhat the attacker wantsExample
UntargetedAny incorrect output — the label just has to be wrong.Misclassifying a cat as anything other than a cat.
TargetedA specific wrong output chosen by the attacker.Misclassifying a panda as a gibbon.

A special case: evasion of a binary classifier (yes/no) belongs to both goals at once, because there is only one wrong answer to reach — flipping the decision is simultaneously "any wrong output" and "a specific wrong output".

How the input is manipulated

There are two axes. First, where the change happens:

  • Digital attacks directly alter data — pixels or text — in software.
  • Physical attacks modify real-world objects (stickers on a sign, adversarial clothing) which a camera then captures as the fooled input.

Second, how the perturbation is distributed across the input:

  • Diffuse perturbations apply tiny, imperceptible noise across the entire input — hard for humans to notice.
  • Localized patches concentrate a visible but innocuous-looking change in one area (a small sticker). Because they survive being printed and photographed, patches are the practical choice for physical-world attacks.
The evasion lifecycle — search, then apply
Attackerwants a wrong decision
→
Searchfind a perturbation that flips output; intensive
→
Applysubmit crafted input at runtime
→
Modelproduces false output

This two-phase structure — an expensive search for a working perturbation, followed by a cheap application — is the seam every runtime control tries to exploit. Rate limiting, series detection, and confidence-hiding all attack the search phase, because the search is where the attacker spends most of their effort. You will meet those controls in Lessons 4–5.

⚔ Attacker's view

The search phase is the cost. Against a fresh target with no knowledge of its internals, I may have to submit thousands of probing queries to map its decision boundary. If you rate-limit me, block me by IP, or hide confidence scores, you make that search slower and less reliable. But note: I can move the search off your system entirely by attacking a similar model I control (a transfer attack — Lesson 3), then apply the finished adversarial example to you in a single request. That is why runtime controls alone are never sufficient.

Evasion is not prompt injection

These two are constantly confused because both are "malicious input". The distinction is what the input is:

EvasionPrompt injection
Input carries…Data (a doctored image, email, or record)Instructions (manipulative commands)
GoalMake the model do its task incorrectlyMake the model do something other than its task
Typical targetAny classifier / detector / predictorPrompt-taking models (GenAI, LLMs)

Prompt-taking models (GenAI) also suffer an additional threat where manipulative instructions are supplied — not to make the model do its task correctly but for other goals, such as bypassing safety protections to obtain offensive answers. That is direct prompt injection, and it is a separate threat group (§2.2) covered in its own module. Keep it out of your evasion mental model.

⚠ Common misconception

"Adversarial examples are just noisy or corrupted inputs, so good data hygiene handles them." No. An adversarial example is syntactically and semantically valid — it is a real, well-formed image or sentence. The perturbation is optimised specifically against your model's learned behaviour, not random. Input schema validation and format checks (the SQL-injection mindset) do nothing against it.

The four worked examples from the guide

  • Example 1 — physical, targeted: slightly changing traffic signs so self-driving cars are fooled (see Figure 1 below).
  • Example 2 — digital, search-based: through a special search process, it is determined how a digital image can be changed undetectably, leading to a completely different classification (see Figure 2 below).
  • Example 3 — text: crafting an e-mail by carefully choosing words to avoid triggering a spam-detection algorithm.
  • Example 4 — text, against an LLM filter: by altering a few words, an attacker succeeds in posting an offensive message on a public forum despite a large-language-model content filter being in place.

Figure 1 — a physical, targeted attack on a traffic sign

flowchart LR
  subgraph Benign["Benign case"]
    A1["Photo SPEED LIMIT 35 unmodified"] --> M1["Model neural network"]
    M1 --> O1["Output Speed limit 35 correct"]
  end
  subgraph Attacked["Attacked case"]
    ATT["Attacker applies stickers"] --> A2["Photo SPEED LIMIT 35 with stickers"]
    A2 --> M2["Model same network"]
    M2 --> O2["Output Stop sign wrong and targeted"]
  end

Figure 1 — the same model, a physically realisable localized-patch perturbation (stickers), and a chosen wrong answer. A targeted, physical evasion attack against a vision model (guide p. 148a).

Figure 2 — crafting a digital adversarial example

flowchart TD
  NN["Neural network Person Yes or No classifier"] -->|parameters| AEC["Adversarial example crafting attacker driven search"]
  ATT["Attacker"] --> AEC
  AEC -->|produces| NOISE["Perturbation noise pattern"]
  ORIG["Original image classified Person Yes"] --> SUM["Combine input plus perturbation"]
  NOISE --> SUM
  SUM --> ADV["Adversarial image looks identical classified Person No"]

Figure 2 — a search/optimisation process, informed by model parameters, produces an imperceptible diffuse perturbation. Original image + perturbation = a visually identical image that flips "Person: Yes" to "Person: No" (guide p. 148b).

An attacker adds a single word to a spam email and it now gets through the filter. Is this evasion or prompt injection?
Evasion. The input is still data (an email to be classified), and the goal is to make the classifier do its own task — spam detection — incorrectly. Prompt injection would involve feeding instructions to a prompt-taking model to make it do something other than its intended task.
Why do physical-world evasion attacks favour localized patches over diffuse perturbations?
Diffuse, imperceptible pixel noise rarely survives the physical world — printing, lighting, angle, distance, and camera sensor noise wash it out. A localized patch (a sticker) is a concentrated, robust change designed to keep working after it is printed and re-photographed, which is exactly what a physical attack requires.

Key Points

  • Evasion = crafted data that makes a model perform its own task incorrectly; the crafted input is an adversarial example, the small change is a perturbation.
  • Goals are targeted (specific wrong output) or untargeted (any wrong output); binary classifiers are both at once.
  • Manipulation axes: digital vs physical, and diffuse perturbations vs localized patches.
  • An evasion attack has two phases — an expensive search for a perturbation and a cheap apply; runtime controls mostly attack the search.
  • Evasion (data) is distinct from prompt injection (instructions); confusing them leads to the wrong defences.
  • Impact is on integrity of model behaviour: failed fraud detection, unsafe decisions, reputational and legal damage.
Module 07 · §2.1.1–2.1.2, 2.1.4 · OWASP AI Exchange

The Attacker's Knowledge Spectrum — Zero, Perfect, Partial

~16 minCovers guide pp. 162–164, 166–167

Every evasion attack is shaped by one question: how much does the attacker know about your model? The guide organises evasion into subtypes along a knowledge axis. Zero-knowledge attackers see only inputs and outputs; perfect-knowledge attackers hold the weights; partial-knowledge sits between. The amount of knowledge determines the attack method, its cost, and which of your controls actually bite. This lesson walks the spectrum end to end and gives you the one equation — FGSM — that every white-box discussion assumes you know.

The knowledge spectrum — more access, cheaper and more reliable attacks
Zero-knowledgeblack-box; only I/O; query-based search
→
Partial-knowledgegray-box; some internals, no gradients
→
Perfect-knowledgewhite-box; full internals; gradient attacks

2.1.1 Zero-knowledge (black-box / closed-box) evasion

THREAT
Zero-knowledge evasion — §2.1.1Attacker crafts adversarial input with no access to code, training set, parameters, or architecture. Lifecycle: runtime. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/zeroknowledgeevasion/

"Black box" is the attacker's-eye view: the model is a closed box whose internal workings are unknown. The attacker has no internal knowledge or access — not the code, not the training set, not the parameters, not the architecture. Crucially, without the internals they cannot compute the model's gradients, so they cannot use the efficient gradient-based methods available to a white-box attacker. Instead they must experiment.

The strategy is query-based

The attacker systematically queries the target with carefully designed inputs and observes the outputs, searching for input variations that produce a false decision. By watching how outputs change, they indirectly reconstruct or estimate the model's decision boundaries and then craft inputs that cross them. Zero-knowledge attacks split by what the output reveals:

Sub-typeWhat the model exposesWhy it helps the attacker
Decision-based (label-based)Only the top prediction label.Least information; the attacker sees only whether the label flipped. Slowest search.
Score-basedA score such as a softmax vector, often top-k (e.g. top-10) predictions.Confidence scores show how close an example is to succeeding, allowing precise adjustments. An attacker can even approximate the gradient by evaluating the objective at two very close points.
◆ Senior insight

Score-based output is a gift to the attacker: it turns a blind search into a guided one, because the confidence vector is a numerical distance-to-the-boundary signal. This is precisely why #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE (Lesson 5) exists — reduce the precision of, or remove, confidence scores and you knock a score-based attacker down to a much slower decision-based regime. But note the guide's caution: it can resemble gradient masking, a fragile defence if used alone.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 162–163, owaspai.org/go/zeroknowledgeevasion/. Named methods in the guide's references: Papernot et al. "Practical black-box attacks"; Andriushchenko et al. "Square attack" (query-efficient, random search, ECCV 2020); Guo et al. "Simple black-box adversarial attacks" (ICML 2019); Chen et al. "ZOO" (zeroth-order optimisation black-box attacks, ACM AISec 2017); Bunzel & Graner "Pasting Attacks" (DSN-W 2023).

2.1.2 Perfect-knowledge (white-box / open-box) evasion

THREAT
Perfect-knowledge evasion — §2.1.2Attacker knows architecture, parameters, and weights, enabling efficient gradient-based attacks. Even certified defenses can be attacked. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/perfectknowledgeevasion/

In a perfect-knowledge attack the adversary knows the architecture, parameters, and weights of the target. With the model fully exposed, they can directly craft input designed to introduce errors — and they can do so efficiently, because they can differentiate the model.

FGSM — the equation to know

The canonical white-box method is the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) of Goodfellow et al. It computes a perturbation p for an image x with label l:

p = ε · sign( ∇ₓ J(θ, x, l) )

Reading it term by term:

  • J(θ, x, l) is the model's cost (loss) function, with parameters θ.
  • ∇ₓ J is the gradient of that cost with respect to the input x, computed via backpropagation — it points in the direction that most increases the loss.
  • sign(·) keeps only the direction of each pixel's gradient (+1 or −1).
  • ε (epsilon) is a scalar that sets the magnitude of the perturbation — small enough to stay imperceptible, large enough to flip the output.

In plain terms: FGSM nudges every pixel a tiny amount in exactly the direction that hurts the model most. Because the attacker has the gradient, one backpropagation pass produces a working adversarial example — no search required. The guide notes starkly that even attacks against certified defenses are possible (Ghiasi et al., "Breaking certified defenses").

⚔ Attacker's view

White-box is the cheapest regime by far. Where a black-box attacker burns thousands of queries mapping your boundary, I compute the exact steepest-ascent direction in one pass. This is why publishing or leaking model weights converts every attacker into a perfect-knowledge attacker, and why open-weight deployments must lean on model hardening (Lesson 6), not on secrecy.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 163–164, owaspai.org/go/perfectknowledgeevasion/. References: Goodfellow, Shlens & Szegedy arXiv:1412.6572 (FGSM); Madry et al. arXiv:1706.06083 (PGD / "Towards deep learning models resistant to adversarial attacks"); Ghiasi, Shafahi & Goldstein arXiv:2003.08937 (breaking certified defenses); Hirano & Takemoto (targeted universal perturbations); Eykholt et al. CVPR 2018 (robust physical-world attacks).

2.1.4 Partial-knowledge (gray-box) evasion

THREAT
Partial-knowledge evasion — §2.1.4The realistic middle ground: attacker knows some internals (architecture, training data) but not everything (e.g. gradients). Permalink: owaspai.org/go/partialknowledgeevasion/

Partial-knowledge (gray-box) attacks occupy the middle ground between perfect- and zero-knowledge. The attacker possesses some knowledge — perhaps the architecture, perhaps the training data — but lacks complete access to the inner workings, such as the gradients. They leverage that limited information two ways: by building surrogate models (transferability, Lesson 3), or by improving a known zero-knowledge attack with the extra insight. The additional information makes these attacks more efficient and more effective.

◆ Senior insight

Partial-knowledge is the most realistic threat model to design against. Full transparency is rare, but partial leakage is everywhere: a published paper describing your architecture, a known base model you fine-tuned, a dataset you disclosed for compliance. When you threat-model evasion, do not assume a pure black box — assume a competent adversary who has read your documentation and can guess your architecture family.

Why can a zero-knowledge attacker not simply use FGSM?
FGSM needs the gradient of the loss with respect to the input, ∇ₓJ, which requires access to the model's parameters and the ability to backpropagate through it. A pure black-box attacker sees only inputs and outputs, so they cannot compute that gradient directly. They must either approximate it from many queries (score-based) or move the search to a surrogate model they do control (transfer attack).
You hide confidence scores but still return the top label. Which black-box sub-type have you disabled, and which remains?
You disable score-based attacks (which rely on the confidence vector to estimate distance to the boundary and approximate gradients) and force the attacker into the slower decision-based (label-based) regime, which sees only whether the label flipped. Decision-based attacks still work, just far less efficiently — which is the point of #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE.

Key Points

  • Evasion subtypes are organised by attacker knowledge: zero (black-box), partial (gray-box), perfect (white-box).
  • Zero-knowledge attacks are query-based; they estimate decision boundaries from I/O and split into decision-based (label only) and score-based (confidence vector, enables gradient approximation).
  • Perfect-knowledge attacks compute gradients directly; FGSM: p = ε·sign(∇ₓJ(θ,x,l)) — one backprop pass, no search; even certified defenses can fall.
  • Partial-knowledge (gray-box) is the realistic case: partial internals used to build surrogates or sharpen black-box attacks.
  • Leaking or publishing weights promotes every attacker to perfect-knowledge — secrecy is a control, not a guarantee.
  • Hiding confidence scores demotes score-based attackers to the slower decision-based regime.
Module 07 · §2.1.3, 2.1.5 · OWASP AI Exchange

Transferability & Evasion After Poisoning

~14 minCovers guide pp. 165–167

Two evasion subtypes break the assumption that your runtime controls protect you. Transferability lets an attacker do all the expensive searching on a different model they fully control, then apply the finished adversarial example to you in a single request — sidestepping rate limits entirely. Evasion after poisoning is even more direct: the weakness was planted in your model during training, so the "adversarial example" is just the trigger key. This lesson explains both mechanisms and, importantly, how the control list changes for each.

2.1.3 Transferability-based evasion

THREAT
Transferability-based (transfer) attack — §2.1.3Craft adversarial examples on a surrogate model, then apply them to the target; relies on similar decision boundaries. A way to attack a black-box target with white-box efficiency. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/transferattack/

In a zero-knowledge situation, an attacker can still get white-box power by first creating adversarial examples on a surrogate model — a copy or approximation of the target — and then applying those examples to the real target. The surrogate can be any of six things:

  1. A perfect-knowledge model from another supplier that performs a similar task (e.g. recognising traffic signs), exposing all its internals.
  2. A zero-knowledge model from another supplier doing a similar task, accessible via e.g. an API.
  3. A perfect-knowledge model the attacker trained themselves, on available, self-collected, or self-labelled data.
  4. The exact target model, stolen development-time or runtime.
  5. The exact target model obtained by purchase or free download.
  6. A replica created by a model exfiltration attack (see owaspai.org/go/modelexfiltration/).

The advantage of a surrogate is that it exposes its internals (except the zero-knowledge surrogate, variant 2), enabling a full perfect-knowledge attack to craft the examples. But even a closed surrogate is useful if its detection and rate limiting are looser than the target's — making the black-box search easier and quicker to perform there than against the real target.

Why transfer works at all

The goal is to create adversarial examples that will "hopefully" transfer to the target even though the surrogate is internally different. The reason it works: because the two models solve a similar task, their decision boundaries are expected to be similar. Transfer success generally rises the more closely the surrogate resembles the target in complexity and structure — the ultimate surrogate being the target itself. Yet the guide is emphatic that even attacks developed on simpler surrogate models tend to transfer effectively.

⚔ Attacker's view

Transferability is how I defeat your rate limiting. Your controls that fight the search — rate limit, unwanted input series handling, obscure confidence — assume I search against you. I don't. I search against my own copy or a public model doing the same job, with no limits, at full white-box speed. Then I hit you once with the finished example. From your logs it looks like a single ordinary request.

🛡 Defender's checklist — transfer attacks change the control list
  • Drop (they don't help): the controls that protect against the search of adversarial samples — #RATE LIMIT, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING, and #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE — because the search happens on the surrogate, not on your system.
  • Add: controls that stop your model being stolen or copied (unless it is already publicly available) — protection against direct development-time model leak, direct runtime model leak, and model exfiltration.
  • Keep: model-hardening (Lesson 6) and single-input detection (#EVASION INPUT HANDLING), which can still catch transferred examples per-sample.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 165–166, owaspai.org/go/transferattack/. Key references: Papernot, McDaniel & Goodfellow arXiv:1605.07277 ("Transferability in machine learning"); Papernot et al. "Practical black-box attacks" (ACM Asia CCS 2017); Demontis et al. USENIX Security 2019 ("Why do adversarial attacks transfer?"); Klause & Bunzel arXiv:2501.18629 (network similarity and transferability); Cox & Bunzel arXiv:2511.05102 (quantifying risk of transferred black-box attacks).

2.1.5 Evasion after data poisoning

THREAT
Evasion after data poisoning — §2.1.5Poisoned training data plants a backdoor; a specific trigger input then forces unwanted output. The weakness is manufactured, not natural. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/evasionafterpoison/

After training data has been poisoned (see the data-poisoning threat group, §3.1.1), a specific input — called a backdoor or trigger — can force unwanted model output. The defining difference from every other evasion subtype: the vulnerability is not a natural property of the trained model, but a manipulated one. The attacker installed the flaw earlier, during training; the evasion at runtime is simply pressing the key that fits the lock they built.

◆ Senior insight

The distinction matters operationally. Ordinary evasion exploits accidental blind spots in a correctly-trained model, so model-hardening (adversarial training, robust design) genuinely reduces the attack surface. A poisoning backdoor is a deliberate association the model learned on purpose — hardening the natural decision boundary won't remove it. You have to attack it at the source: protect training-data integrity and the model pipeline.

🛡 Defender's checklist — evasion-after-poisoning changes the control list too
  • Use the evasion controls (Lessons 4–6), except those that protect against the search for adversarial samples — #RATE LIMIT, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING, #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE — because the attacker needs no search; the trigger was designed at poisoning time.
  • Add the controls against model poisoning (see the model-poisoning section) — this is where the root cause lives.
  • Note that #INPUT DISTORTION (Lesson 5) is doubly useful here: it defends against both evasion and data poisoning by disrupting brittle trigger features.
Why are rate limiting and confidence-hiding useless against both transfer attacks and evasion-after-poisoning?
Both controls fight the attacker's search for a working perturbation by making probing your model slow and uninformative. But in a transfer attack the search happens on a surrogate the attacker controls, and in evasion-after-poisoning there is no search at all — the trigger was fixed during poisoning. In neither case does the attacker probe your live model, so search-fighting controls have nothing to bite on.

Key Points

  • Transferability: craft adversarial examples on a surrogate model, apply to the target; works because similar tasks yield similar decision boundaries, and even simple surrogates transfer.
  • Six surrogate variants range from another supplier's model to the exact stolen/downloaded/exfiltrated target.
  • Transfer attacks defeat search-fighting controls (rate limit, series handling, obscure confidence) — instead add anti-theft controls (model-leak and exfiltration protection).
  • Evasion after poisoning: a backdoor/trigger planted at training time; the flaw is manufactured, not natural — add model-poisoning controls at the source.
  • Search-fighting controls are useless whenever the attacker does not probe your live model.
  • #INPUT DISTORTION helps against both evasion and poisoning by breaking brittle trigger features.
Module 07 · §2.1 controls · OWASP AI Exchange

Runtime Defenses I — Detecting Adversarial Input

~18 minCovers guide pp. 137–146, 149–155

You cannot fully prevent evasion, so a mature defence is layered. This lesson covers the runtime detection layer: the shared input-threat control stack that applies to every evasion attack, plus the two controls that decide, per input, whether a sample is adversarial or merely unusual. The first — #EVASION INPUT HANDLING — looks for adversarial fingerprints in a single sample. The second — #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING — asks a broader question: does this input even belong to the distribution the model was trained on? Both are essential and both have hard limits you must understand.

The evasion control stack

An evasion attack is a search followed by an application, so the guide's controls attack it at both phases and at both lifecycle stages. First, the shared controls for input threats that also serve evasion (fully treated elsewhere — summarised here for their evasion role):

ControlRole against evasion
#MONITOR USEDetect suspicious input or output — the foundation all detection controls report into.
#RATE LIMITLimit how many attack variants an attacker can try in a short time — attacks the search phase.
#MODEL ACCESS CONTROLReduce the number of potential attackers to a minimum.
#ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLINGFlag unusual input, which can be suspicious for evasion (covered below).
#OBSCURE CONFIDENCELimit the information an attacker can use to guide the search (Lesson 5).
#UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLINGSpot the multi-input probing pattern of a search (Lesson 5).

Then the controls specific to evasion. #DETECT ADVERSARIAL INPUT is the guide's older name for what is now #EVASION INPUT HANDLING — finding typical attack forms or multiple tries in a row. The rest are model-side: #EVASION ROBUST MODEL, #TRAIN ADVERSARIAL, #INPUT DISTORTION, and #ADVERSARIAL ROBUST DISTILLATION (Lessons 5–6). And a reminder from General controls: limiting the impact of unwanted model behaviour matters most, because evasion is hard to prevent.

Where each evasion control sits
Runtime · detect the input
#EVASION INPUT HANDLINGper-sample adversarial detection
#ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLINGout-of-distribution detection
#UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLINGmulti-input probing patterns
Runtime · deprive / distort
#OBSCURE CONFIDENCEhide the search signal
#INPUT DISTORTIONbreak the crafted perturbation
#RATE LIMIT · #MODEL ACCESS CONTROLslow / shrink the attacker set
Development-time · harden the model
#EVASION ROBUST MODELdesign for input insensitivity
#TRAIN ADVERSARIALcorrect the decision boundary
#ADVERSARIAL ROBUST DISTILLATIONsmooth the boundary

Control · #EVASION INPUT HANDLING

#EVASION INPUT HANDLINGDetect adversarial characteristics in a single inputruntime · AI engineer · input threats
IntentDetect and respond to individual adversarial inputs crafted to evade model behaviour, by identifying adversarial characteristics within a single input sample — whether it appears alone or as part of a broader attack. Reduces incorrect predictions, unsafe actions, and downstream failures even when attacks occur sporadically. Secondary benefits: better robustness testing, understanding of model blind spots, and early signals of adversarial adaptation.
ImplementFollow #MONITOR USE for detection considerations and response options. Four techniques: (1) Statistical methods — adversarial inputs deviate on some metric; use Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Bayesian Uncertainty Estimation (BUE), or the Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM). Because they decide per-sample (unlike series statistics in #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING), they can also catch transferred attacks. (2) Detection networks — a detector network spots adversarial examples, running as a preprocessing function or in parallel to the main model (parallel detectors are trained on the intermediate features of the main model's hidden layer). Training a preprocessing detector to separate benign from adversarial is itself hard, so it may rely on the raw input or on statistical metrics. (3) Input Distortion Based Techniques (IDBT) — modify the input to remove adversarial data, run the model on both the original and modified versions, and compare (see #INPUT DISTORTION). (4) Adversarial-patch detection — patches are localized, often visible, real-world modifications; the techniques above can find them but often need modification because of the patch's unique noise pattern, especially when captured through a camera whose benign noise (the camera fingerprint) complicates detection. Named techniques from the references: feature squeezing, MagNet, DefenseGAN, and local intrinsic dimensionality.
LimitsAdversarial examples can be crafted to evade both the primary model and a dedicated detector (the guide's explicit caution on detection networks). Some techniques add compute overhead or reduce accuracy. Physical-world attacks (patches) are especially hard due to environmental noise and variability. This control does not stop attackers repeatedly probing to refine evasion, and is less effective in isolation when attackers adapt quickly or rely on multistep probing across many inputs — pair it with series-based, usage, and access controls. Unlike traditional input validation (e.g. SQL injection), it addresses inputs that are syntactically and semantically valid but crafted to exploit learned behaviour. Tramèr (ICML 2022) shows detecting adversarial examples is nearly as hard as classifying them.
StandardsOpenCRE "Evasion attack input handling". ETSI §6.2.3 (Input restoration); ENISA Table 5 ("Implement tools to detect if a data point is an adversarial example"; "Apply modifications on inputs"), Annex C (same); MITRE ATLAS AML.M0010 (Input Restoration), AML.M0009 (Use Multi-Modal Sensors). Not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. Permalink owaspai.org/go/evasioninputhandling/.
⚠ Common misconception

"Add a detector network and adversarial examples are solved." The guide flags the opposite: an adaptive attacker can craft one example that fools both the detector and the main model simultaneously. A detector raises the attacker's cost — it does not close the gap. Treat detection as one layer among many, evaluate it continuously, and combine it with rate limiting, series detection, and model hardening.

Control · #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING

The other runtime detection question is not "is this an adversarial fingerprint?" but "does this input belong to the distribution the model was trained on at all?" That is out-of-distribution thinking. The guide groups five related, overlapping tasks:

TaskWhat it detectsExample (traffic scene)
Out-of-Distribution Detection (OOD)The broad category — data points that differ significantly from the training distribution; can include novelty, anomaly, and outlier aspects.Trained on vehicles, pedestrians, dogs and cats; a horse appears — recognise it as out-of-distribution.
Outlier Detection (OD)Points significantly different from the majority; a form of OOD, but not all outliers are OOD.A car moving far faster than all others is an outlier in normal traffic.
Anomaly Detection (AD)Abnormal/irregular instances that raise suspicion; significance is the key — it indicates a problem or rare event.A vehicle going the wrong way on a one-way street — not just odd, but dangerous.
Open Set Recognition (OSR)Classify known classes while identifying and rejecting unknown ones during testing, using the model's decision boundaries; a way to perform AD.Knows cars, trucks, pedestrians, bicycles; a fallen tree must be classed "unknown".
Novelty Detection (ND)OOD input recognised as not malicious and relevant/of interest; system may trigger another use case, log it, or let the model generalise.A newly released car model — recognised as a novel instance within the known "car" category.
#ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLINGDetect input that deviates from the training distributionruntime · AI engineer · input threats
IntentDetect anomalous input to maintain model integrity, address concept drift, and prevent adversarial attacks that exploit model behaviour on out-of-distribution data. Unusual input can be a signal of evasion.
ImplementTechniques draw on OOD/OD/AD/OSR/ND: similarity measures between training and test data, model introspection for activated neurons, and OOD-sample generation with retraining. Thresholding the output confidence vector classifies in- vs out-of-distribution (assuming higher confidence for in-distribution). Supervised contrastive learning (group similar classes, separate different ones) and clustering enhance the distinction. Two concrete implementations: Activation Analysis — examine the activations of different network layers to reveal unusual patterns when processing an adversarial input, used as a detection signal; and Similarity-based analysis — compare incoming input against a ground-truth reference set (typically the training data / normal input space) and flag sufficiently dissimilar input as anomalous, using the per-modality metrics below.
LimitsNot all anomalous input is malicious, and not all malicious input is anomalous. Adversarial input can be specifically crafted to bypass anomaly detection. Critically, for evasion, anomaly detection is often ineffective because adversarial samples are by definition designed to look like normal input — many evasion attacks will not be caught by deviation-based methods. Some forms (adversarial patches) may still produce detectable anomalies. AI systems rely on pattern-based (statistical/behavioural) detection rather than the rule/signature-based detection of conventional systems (the Particularity).
StandardsOpenCRE "Anomalous AI input handling". ETSI §6.2.3 (Adversarial example detection); ENISA Table 5 (detect if a data point is an adversarial example), Annex C ("Ensure the model is sufficiently resilient to the environment in which it will operate"); NIST AI 100-2 §2.2.4 (Randomized smoothing); MITRE ATLAS AML.M0015 (Adversarial Input Detection). Not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. References: Hendrycks & Gimpel arXiv:1610.02136 (baseline OOD detection); Yang et al. arXiv:2110.11334 (generalized OOD survey); Khosla et al. (supervised contrastive learning, NeurIPS 2020); Sehwag et al. (robustness of open-world ML, AISec 2019).

Similarity measures per modality

Similarity-based anomaly detection needs a distance metric appropriate to the data type. The guide's reference table:

ModalityRecommended similarity measuresNotes / tools
TextCosine similarity, Jaccard Index, embedding distance (BERT, Sentence-BERT), word/token histogramsUse transformer-based embeddings.
ImageStructural Similarity Index (SSIM), Euclidean distance, pixel-wise MSE, perceptual loss (VGG-based)Normalise lighting or scaling; patch-based SSIM helps detect targeted attacks in specific image regions.
AudioMFCC-based distance, Dynamic Time Warping (DTW), spectral convergence, cosine similarity on embeddingsUse frame-wise comparison for streaming; DTW corrects time shifts.
TabularEuclidean distance, Mahalanobis distance, correlation coefficient, Gower distanceEnsure normalisation and categorical encoding first; Mahalanobis distance offers strong outlier detection.
◆ Senior insight

Do not conflate #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING with #EVASION INPUT HANDLING. Anomaly detection asks "is this input weird?" — and a well-made adversarial example is deliberately not weird, so anomaly detection alone is a poor evasion defence (the guide says so outright). Evasion input handling asks a narrower question — "does this sample carry an adversarial fingerprint?" — using statistical detectors, detector networks, and distortion comparison, which is why it can catch even transferred examples that look perfectly normal. You need the second for evasion; the first mostly earns its keep against drift, novelty, and clumsier patch attacks.

Why is out-of-distribution anomaly detection a weak primary defence against evasion?
Because adversarial examples are, by design, minimal perturbations of normal inputs — they are built to sit inside the normal input distribution and look ordinary. Deviation-based detection keys on inputs that look unusual, so it systematically misses the very inputs evasion produces. It still helps against concept drift, novelty, and visible adversarial patches, which do register as anomalies.

Key Points

  • Evasion defence is layered: shared input-threat controls (#MONITOR USE, #RATE LIMIT, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL) plus evasion-specific detection and hardening.
  • #EVASION INPUT HANDLING detects adversarial fingerprints per single sample via statistical methods (PCA, BUE, SSIM), detector networks, IDBT, and patch detection — and can catch transferred attacks.
  • Detector networks can themselves be fooled by an adaptive adversarial example; detection raises cost, never eliminates the threat.
  • #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING spans OOD, OD, AD, OSR, ND — useful for drift, novelty, and patches, but weak against evasion because adversarial examples look normal by design.
  • Similarity-based detection needs modality-appropriate metrics (text: cosine/BERT; image: SSIM/MSE; audio: MFCC/DTW; tabular: Mahalanobis/Gower).
  • #DETECT ADVERSARIAL INPUT is the older name for #EVASION INPUT HANDLING.
Module 07 · §2.1 controls · OWASP AI Exchange

Runtime Defenses II — Series, Confidence & Distortion

~16 minCovers guide pp. 141–146, 158–161

Single-input detection catches the finished adversarial example. The three controls in this lesson attack the attacker earlier and differently: #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING sees the probing pattern of a search across many inputs; #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE removes the numerical signal that guides the search; and #INPUT DISTORTION physically breaks the crafted perturbation before the model ever sees it. Together they turn the attacker's cheap, precise search into an expensive, noisy one — and #INPUT DISTORTION doubles as a data-poisoning defence.

Control · #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING

Many evasion (and inversion, and exfiltration) attacks are not one malicious input — they are an iterative probe. Each individual request looks benign; the attack is only visible when you analyse the series.

#UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLINGDetect suspicious patterns across a series of inputsruntime · AI engineer · input threats
IntentIdentify suspicious behaviour that only emerges when multiple inputs are analysed together — surfacing reconnaissance, abuse, and multistep attacks (model inversion, evasion search, model exfiltration) that appear benign at the single-input level. Secondary benefits: better abuse monitoring, attribution, and investigation signals. Focuses on behaviour across inputs, not on a single sample's adversarial properties.
ImplementFollow #MONITOR USE. Three concepts: (1) Statistical analysis of input series per user — e.g. a series of small deviations in the input space (a search for an adversarial example, typically with a general increase in confidence value), or systematic inputs (very random, very uniform, or covering the entire input space) indicating a model-exfiltration attack. (2) Behaviour-based detection of anomalous usage — a much-higher-than-normal number of inferences by a single actor over a period is a signal (complements #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING and aligns with #RATE LIMIT). (3) Input optimization pattern detection — detect the repeated-adjustment pattern of attacks by finding unusually high similarity across many inputs: cluster input series into dense groups of highly similar inputs, measure pairwise similarity within a time window (not just consecutive requests), and analyse frequency/distribution to separate systematic probing from benign repetition. Considering similarity across a broad range of past inputs defeats attackers who alternate probing with unrelated requests. Signals from #RATE LIMIT add context.
LimitsLegitimate users can look like attackers (systematic testing, research-driven exploration). Attackers can distribute inputs across multiple identities or sources to reduce detectability. Effectiveness depends on reliably grouping inputs by actor/source/context, baseline modelling of normal behaviour, and careful tuning to avoid false positives on high-volume or exploratory use. Does not prevent attacks alone — combine with rate limiting, access control, and investigation workflows.
StandardsOpenCRE "Unwanted AI input series handling". ETSI §6.2.3 (Adversarial example detection); ENISA Table 5 (detect if a data point is an adversarial example); MITRE ATLAS AML.M0015 (Adversarial Input Detection). See also #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING and #EVASION INPUT HANDLING. Not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. Permalink owaspai.org/go/unwantedinputserieshandling/.
▸ Worked example

A black-box attacker running a score-based search submits 4,000 near-duplicate images over three hours, each a hair's-breadth from the last, with the model's confidence in "not a person" creeping upward request by request. No single image is anomalous. But clustered together they form a dense ball in input space with monotonically rising confidence — the exact fingerprint #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING is built to catch. The attacker's counter is to interleave unrelated queries; the guide's counter to that is to measure similarity across a wide window of past inputs, not just consecutive ones.

Control · #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE

Confidence scores are not just user-facing explanations — they are a side channel. A score-based attacker uses them as a distance-to-the-boundary readout that steers the search. Take the readout away and the search goes half-blind.

#OBSCURE CONFIDENCELimit or hide confidence information in outputsruntime · AI engineer · input threats
IntentReduce the usefulness of outputs for attackers who rely on confidence to probe, analyse, or copy the model. Detailed confidence aids model inversion, membership inference, evasion, and model exfiltration by helping construct adversarial samples. Reducing it makes these attacks harder, slower, and less reliable — harder to estimate decision boundaries, infer training-data membership, reverse-engineer the model, or craft adversarial inputs efficiently.
Implement(1) Reduce confidence precision — present the minimum detail needed: round numbers, use coarse ranges, or remove confidence entirely. (2) Assess impact on accuracy — evaluate any change so it does not unacceptably degrade the intended function. Determine applicability via risk management (likelihood of evasion, inversion/membership inference, exfiltration). Exception: when confidence is essential to the use case (medical decision support, safety-critical decisions), still minimise to the least necessary via rounding or added noise. If the deployer is better placed than the provider to implement this, the provider should communicate that expectation. Note — confidence-based anomaly detection: unusually high confidence can itself signal misuse (e.g. membership inference probing known entities); treat it as a weak indicator to flag for follow-up, not an automatic block, since high confidence is common in normal operation.
LimitsAttackers may still estimate confidence by probing with small input variations and watching whether outputs change. Does not fully prevent label-only membership inference. Adding noise or reducing detail can hurt usability/accuracy if not balanced. Can resemble gradient masking for zero-knowledge evasion — a fragile defence if used alone. Because effectiveness depends on architecture, training method, and data distribution, validate the actual risk reduction by testing rather than assuming it.
StandardsOpenCRE "Obscuring confidence in AI output". ETSI §6.4.3 (Obfuscating confidence scores); ENISA Table 5 ("Reduce the information given by the model"); MITRE ATLAS AML.M0002 (Passive AI Output Obfuscation). Not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. Permalink owaspai.org/go/obscureconfidence/.
⚠ Common misconception

"Hiding scores stops the attack." It slows it. A determined attacker still probes with tiny input variations and watches whether the label flips — a decision-based attack — and can approximate confidence indirectly. And because obscuring confidence resembles gradient masking, on its own it can give a false sense of security. It is a genuine friction control, not a wall; pair it with rate limiting, series detection, and model hardening.

Control · #INPUT DISTORTION

Detection asks "is this adversarial?" Distortion takes a different bet: deliberately damage every input just enough to break a crafted perturbation, while keeping the model correct on honest inputs. The attacker's perturbation is precisely tuned; a little noise, smoothing, or JPEG compression can knock it off its knife-edge.

#INPUT DISTORTIONModify / add noise to input to break crafted perturbationsruntime · AI engineer · input threats
IntentSlightly modify and/or add noise to the input to distort the adversarial attack, causing it to fail, while maintaining sufficient model correctness. Effective against both evasion attacks and data-poisoning attacks. Modification via adding noise (randomisation), smoothing, or JPEG compression.
ImplementAgainst evasion: because evasion relies on precisely prepared input, distorting it likely makes the attack fail. Distorting all input can reduce model correctness, so a better pattern is: run the model on the undistorted input first, then on one or more distortions; if the results deviate strongly, treat it as an evasion attack — use the distorted output and optionally alert; otherwise use the undistorted input for the most correct result. Distortion also hinders attackers who search using gradients. Random Transformations (RT) inject enough randomness — a random subset of input transformations with random parameters — to make crafting adversarial examples computationally difficult; because multiple transformations are applied per sample, accuracy may drop, so retrain the model with the random transformations in place. Against data poisoning (see §3.1.1): distortion disrupts embedded poisoning features. For adversarial-sample poisons it works as for evasion. For brittle features (e.g. high-frequency noise) even slight JPEG compression can neutralise the poison; for distinct/robust features (visible patches) apply stronger, more varied transformations — randomness and strength are key, since a uniform transformation may let the model still learn the malicious pattern.
LimitsZero-knowledge attacks are not affected by shattered gradients — they do not use gradients (they compute the adversarial input only from the model's input and output), so gradient-disruption offers them no obstacle. Attackers can work around distortion. Distorting all input costs accuracy unless the compare-two-versions pattern or retraining is used.
StandardsOpenCRE "AI Input distortion". ENISA Table 5 ("Apply modifications on inputs"); NIST AI 100-2 §2.2.4 (Randomized smoothing); MITRE ATLAS AML.M0010 (Input Restoration). See #EVASION INPUT HANDLING for using the distorted input as a detection mechanism. Not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. References: Xu, Evans & Qi "Feature Squeezing" (NDSS 2018); Das et al. arXiv:1705.02900 (JPEG vaccination); Xie et al. arXiv:1711.01991 (randomization); Raff et al. CVPR 2019 (barrage of random transforms); He et al. WOOT 2017; Athalye et al. (obfuscated gradients). Permalink owaspai.org/go/inputdistortion/.
◆ Senior insight

The relationship between #INPUT DISTORTION and #EVASION INPUT HANDLING is worth internalising. Distortion defends by cleaning the input. The IDBT variant detects by running the model on both original and cleaned input and comparing — a large disagreement is itself the alarm. Feature squeezing is exactly this detection variant: reduce input detail (fewer features, smoothing) and compare the model's output on original vs squeezed input. Same mechanism, two jobs: mitigation and detection.

Why does gradient-disrupting distortion fail against zero-knowledge (black-box) attacks?
Distortion frustrates gradient-based search by "shattering" the gradients an attacker would follow. But a zero-knowledge attacker never uses gradients — they compute the adversarial input purely from observed inputs and outputs. With no gradient in their method, there is nothing for shattered gradients to break, so this benefit of distortion simply does not apply to them.

Key Points

  • #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING catches the probing pattern of a search across many inputs via per-user statistics, usage-volume behaviour, and input-optimization (high-similarity clustering across a wide time window).
  • #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE removes the score signal a search-based attacker uses; keep the minimum needed for safety-critical use; it resembles gradient masking, so it is friction, not a wall.
  • Unusually high confidence can be a weak misuse indicator (flag, don't auto-block).
  • #INPUT DISTORTION breaks crafted perturbations with noise/smoothing/JPEG; compare undistorted vs distorted output to detect without always sacrificing accuracy; Random Transformations need retraining.
  • Distortion defends against evasion and data poisoning, but shattered gradients do nothing to zero-knowledge attacks, which use no gradients.
  • Feature squeezing = the detection variant of distortion (compare model output on original vs reduced-detail input).
Module 07 · §2.1 controls · OWASP AI Exchange

Development-Time Defenses — Building Robust Models

~15 minCovers guide pp. 155–162
