  • Choosing model types such as linear models or Naive Bayes classifiers over neural networks and decision trees reduces the likelihood of overfitting individual samples.
  • Using regularization during training can also help.
⚠ Common misconception

"Privacy attacks are a prompt problem — we'll filter bad queries." Inversion and membership inference queries can look entirely legitimate one at a time; the attack lives in the aggregate pattern and in the confidence channel, not in any single malicious string. That is why the guide's specific defences are structural (model size, confidence obscuring) and behavioural (rate limiting, use monitoring) rather than content filters.

#SMALL MODEL — remove the storage the attack needs

#SMALL MODELKeep the model small enough that it cannot memorize individual samplesdevelopment-time · AI engineer control for input threats
IntentOverfitting — storing individual training samples — can be prevented by keeping the model small, so it is not able to store detail at the level of individual training set samples. If the capacity to memorize a record never exists, inversion has nothing to reconstruct and membership inference loses its overconfidence signal.
ImplementA design-time decision by the AI engineer: constrain model capacity/parameter count to what the task needs; prefer model families less prone to per-sample memorization (linear models, Naive Bayes) over neural networks and decision trees where accuracy allows; apply regularization during training (per the guide's overfitting discussion, which this control operationalizes).
LimitsIt is a capacity/accuracy trade-off: shrinking the model can cost task performance, so the right size is the smallest that meets the functional requirement. It reduces memorization-driven leakage but does not eliminate statistical inference entirely, so it complements — not replaces — #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE, #RATE LIMIT, #MONITOR USE and #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL.
StandardsOpenCRE: Model size reduction → ETSI sec. 5.3.2 (Fine-pruning); NIST AI 100-2 sec. 2.3.3 (Backdoor Poisoning). Not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/smallmodel.
◆ Senior insight

#SMALL MODEL is the rare privacy control that is free at runtime — a smaller model is also cheaper to serve — but it must be argued at design time, against a team culture that defaults to "bigger is better". The senior framing: model capacity beyond task need is not headroom, it is unaccounted storage of your most sensitive data. Interesting standards footnote: OpenCRE routes this control to ETSI's fine-pruning and NIST's backdoor poisoning sections — size/pruning reduction is dual-use, mitigating both memorization leakage and hidden backdoor capacity.

The full control stack for §2.3.2

  • General controls — especially Sensitive data limitation: data that never entered training cannot be inverted or membership-tested.
  • #MONITOR USE — detect suspicious input patterns: these attacks require intensive, systematic experimentation.
  • #RATE LIMIT — limit the attacker trying numerous attack variants in a short time; inversion needs thousands of optimization queries.
  • #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL — reduce the number of potential attackers to a minimum.
  • #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE — limit the confidence information the attacker can use; both attacks feed on confidence indications.
  • #SMALL MODEL — the threat-specific development-time control above: limit the amount of information that can be retrieved at all.
  • Model-theft protections — the controls against direct development-time model leak and direct runtime model leak also matter here, because inversion and membership attacks are much more efficient with full access to model attributes (white-box access to parameters). Keeping the model file confidential keeps the attacker in the slow black-box regime.
⚔ Attacker's view

The attacker's cost curve is dominated by query budget and signal quality. Full-precision confidence scores plus unlimited queries: inversion becomes a straightforward optimization loop. Round the scores or return labels only (#OBSCURE CONFIDENCE), throttle queries (#RATE LIMIT), and gate who can query at all (#MODEL ACCESS CONTROL), and the same attack needs orders of magnitude more effort for a blurrier result. If the attacker steals the model file itself, all of that friction disappears — which is why the guide cross-wires this threat to the model-leak protections.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 188–191, permalink /go/modelinversionandmembership. OpenCRE "Model inversion / Membership inference" maps to: OWASP Top 10 for LLM — LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure (inference-based data disclosure attacks); MITRE ATLAS AML.T0024.001 (Exfiltration via AI Inference API: Invert AI Model) and AML.T0024.000 (Infer Training Data Membership); ETSI sec. 6.4.1 (Model inversion attacks) and 6.4.2 (Membership inference attacks); OWASP Top 10 for ML — ML03:2023 Model Inversion Attack, ML04:2023 Membership Inference Attack; BIML-24(LLM) model:6 (Training Set and Prompt Reveal) and BIML-78(2020) model:4 (Training Set Reveal); NIST AI 100-2 sec. 2.4.2 (Membership Inference); ENISA Table 3 (Oracle). The guide also links an article on membership inference.

Why does the guide recommend linear models or Naive Bayes over neural networks for privacy-sensitive tasks?
Those model families have far less capacity to store per-sample detail: they learn a small number of global parameters (weights per feature, class-conditional statistics) rather than millions of parameters that can encode individual records. Neural networks and decision trees can effectively memorize specific training samples, and memorized samples are exactly what model inversion reconstructs and membership inference detects.
How do model-theft controls reduce inversion/membership risk, when they defend a different threat?
Inversion and membership inference are much more efficient with full (white-box) access to model attributes — parameters and internals. If the model leaks (development-time or runtime model leak), the attacker escapes every runtime friction control (rate limits, access control, confidence obscuring) and can attack the copy offline at leisure. Protecting the model file therefore indirectly protects the training data inside it.

Key Points

  • Model inversion = reconstructing training records by optimizing inputs to maximize confidence indications; membership inference = proving a specific record was in the training set via confidence tells.
  • Both are input threats needing only query access; both feed on confidence signals — hence #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE in the control stack.
  • Overfitting (storing individual samples beyond need) is the fuel: excess capacity/parameters → more memorization → easier attacks.
  • #SMALL MODEL: development-time control — keep the model too small to store individual-sample detail; also prefer linear/Naive Bayes over neural nets/decision trees and use regularization.
  • Runtime frictions: #MONITOR USE (pattern detection), #RATE LIMIT (query budget), #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL (fewer attackers).
  • Model-leak protections matter too: white-box access makes these attacks dramatically more efficient.
  • Standards: ATLAS AML.T0024.000/.001, ML03/ML04:2023, NIST AI 100-2 §2.4.2, ETSI §6.4.1–6.4.2, ENISA "Oracle"; #SMALL MODEL not yet in ISO/IEC.
Module 09 · §2.4 · OWASP AI Exchange

Model Exfiltration — Stealing a Model by Using It

~13 minCovers guide pp. 192–195

Your model's weights never leave the building — and a competitor still walks away with a working copy. Model exfiltration (model theft through use) turns your own inference API into a training-data factory: query, record, retrain. This lesson covers the attack mechanics, its three-fold impact (including the February 2026 Anthropic claim about stripped safety protections), when the risk actually applies, the honest limits of detection, and the ownership-proof control #MODEL WATERMARKING.

THREAT
Model exfiltration (model theft through use)Attacker collects input/output pairs of your model and trains a functional replica. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: ATLAS AML.T0024.002, OWASP ML05:2023, NIST AI 100-2 §2.4.3.

The attack: distill someone else's model

Model exfiltration occurs when an attacker collects inputs and outputs of an existing model and uses those combinations to train a new model, replicating the original. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/modelexfiltration. The input/output pairs can be collected three ways:

  1. Harvesting logs — if the attacker can read logged prompts and responses;
  2. Intercepting input and output — on the wire or at an integration point;
  3. Presenting large numbers of input variations and collecting the outputs — the pure "theft by use" path that needs nothing but API access.
Model theft — manufacturing a train set from black-box queries (guide figure, p. 193)
Attackerchooses many input variations
→
Confidential trained modelblack box — attacker cannot see inside
→
Output setY/N labels: Y Y N / N Y Y / N Y N
Manufactured train setinputs paired with harvested Y/N outputs
→
Copy of confidential modelattacker-trained functional replica

The guide's figure: the attacker's input set flows through the confidential model; outputs are collected, paired back with the inputs into a "manufactured train set", and used to train a copy. Both networks are drawn with identical node colouring — the copy replicates the original's behaviour.

Impact: three ways you lose

The impact is a confidentiality breach of the model (i.e., its parameters), which cashes out as any combination of:

  1. Intellectual property theft — e.g., by a competitor who skips your data collection and training investment.
  2. A launchpad for input attacks on the copy, circumventing protections. The copy has none of your runtime protections — rate limiting, access control, detection mechanisms — so the attacker can develop attacks offline at unlimited speed. These input attacks are mainly evasion attacks. Other attack types would require a much more detailed copy of the model — typically unfeasible to achieve using this form of model theft.
  3. Stripping the model of protection mechanisms against producing harmful content. The guide records that Anthropic claimed in February 2026 that exfiltration attacks by competition could achieve this: creating models able to produce harmful content, against the stakes of the original model makers.
▸ Worked example

The guide cites "Thieves on Sesame Street" — the research on model exfiltration of large language models (GenAI) — which demonstrated that BERT-class language models could be extracted through their APIs, even with nonsensical query inputs. Attacker economics from the figure's scenario: if the victim spent months and a sensitive dataset training a face classifier, the thief pays only per-query API prices for the labels; the "manufactured train set" costs a fraction of the original's data collection, labelling, and compliance effort. Distillation-style theft is cheap precisely because your model does the expensive part — labelling — for the attacker.

Names and near-relatives

  • Alternative names: model stealing attack, model extraction attack, model distillation, or model theft by use.
  • The technique of adversarial robust distillation (owaspai.org/go/adversarialrobustdistillation) is the same mechanism worn as a white hat: model developers sometimes deliberately exfiltrate a student model from their own system with the goal of making it more robust against attacks.
  • Alternative ways of model theft, which can lead to an exact copy of the model (not just a behavioural replica), are direct development-time model leak and direct runtime model leak — separate threats covered in their own sections.

Risk identification: do you even have this problem?

This threat applies if the model represents intellectual property (i.e., a trade secret), or the risk of evasion attacks applies — with one exception: if the model is publicly available, there is no need to steal it, so the threat is moot. Run this test before spending on controls; the guide points to #AI PROGRAM as the governance layer necessary to identify and treat this risk in the first place.

Your model is an open-weights model you fine-tuned lightly on public data, served publicly for free. Does model exfiltration matter?
Mostly no. The guide's risk identification says the threat applies when the model is a trade secret or when evasion-attack risk applies — and explicitly excepts publicly available models, because there is nothing to steal. If your fine-tune adds no proprietary value and evasion against your deployment has no consequence, spend the control budget elsewhere.

Controls — friction, detection, and honest limits

  • General controls, especially #AI PROGRAM for governance that identifies and treats the risk.
  • #MONITOR USE — detect suspicious input and respond.
  • #RATE LIMIT — limit the attacker presenting many inputs in a short time; extraction needs volume.
  • #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL — reduce the number of potential attackers to a minimum.
  • #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING — exfiltration techniques try to cover the input space, potentially introducing inputs that would not normally occur.
  • #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING — detect sequences of inputs that indicate methodically covering an input space; no single query is suspicious, the series is.
  • #MODEL WATERMARKING — enable post-theft ownership verification when residual risk remains (discussed below) — although it is less effective for proving exfiltration than for proving an actual copy of the model was used.
⚠ Common misconception

"With good detection we can prevent model theft." The guide's caveat is blunt: if attackers are able to access the model and the model allows intensive use, then it is typically hard to protect against model exfiltration. Detection would come down to spotting intensive use covering a wide range of inputs, including anomalous ones — and such detections always require further analysis, since this type of use may also be benign (a heavy legitimate integrator looks a lot like a thief). Plan for residual risk: that is exactly why watermarking — an after-the-fact accountability control — is in the stack at all.

#MODEL WATERMARKING — proving ownership after the theft

#MODEL WATERMARKINGEmbed a hidden, secret marker to verify a suspected copy's origindevelopment-time · AI engineer control for input threats
IntentEmbed a hidden, secret marker into a trained model so that, if a suspected copy appears elsewhere, the original owner can verify that the model was derived from their system. It demonstrates ownership after a model has been stolen or replicated — it does not prevent the theft itself. Beyond the technical role, it supports intellectual property protection by enabling post-hoc attribution of stolen or misused models — critical for legal claims, contractual enforcement, and regulatory investigations. In a layered strategy it complements preventive controls by providing accountability and ownership assurance when other defenses fail.
ImplementDesign the watermark so it (a) remains detectable even if the model is modified — for example through fine-tuning or pruning — and (b) avoids ambiguity where multiple parties could plausibly claim ownership of the same model. Applied at development time by the AI engineer, typically via secret trigger inputs with characteristic outputs known only to the owner.
LimitsEffective evidence for direct model theft (stolen weights), but limited for model exfiltration: typical watermark approaches live in data that would not be in the distribution of the attacker's input data, so the watermark simply never transfers into the distilled copy. More advanced techniques exist that entangle the watermark with typical input data and its output (see USENIX "Entangled Watermarks as a Defense against Model Extraction"), so the extraction process itself carries the mark across.
StandardsOpenCRE: Watermarking AI models → ETSI sec. 6.3.2 (Watermarking). Reference: USENIX — Entangled Watermarks as a Defense against Model Extraction. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/modelwatermarking.
◆ Senior insight

Understand the distribution argument, because it decides whether your watermark is worth anything. A classic watermark is a set of out-of-distribution trigger inputs the model answers in a secret, characteristic way. But an exfiltration attacker only queries in-distribution inputs — their manufactured train set never contains your triggers, so the student model never learns them, and your "proof" comes up empty against the copy. Entangled watermarking fixes this by forcing the watermark behaviour to share features with legitimate task behaviour, so distilling the task drags the watermark along. If exfiltration (not weight theft) is your scenario, only the entangled family gives you evidence.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • First run risk identification: trade-secret model or real evasion risk? If the model is public, skip these controls.
  • Gate access (#MODEL ACCESS CONTROL) and throttle volume (#RATE LIMIT) — extraction requires many queries.
  • Alert on input-space coverage: anomalous single inputs (#ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING) and methodical series (#UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING).
  • Treat those alerts as leads, not verdicts — intensive wide-ranging use may be benign; investigate before acting.
  • Watermark at training time; prefer entangled watermarks if distillation-style theft is the threat model.
  • Keep watermark triggers and verification keys secret and escrowed — they are evidence for legal/regulatory action.
  • Accept residual risk explicitly through #AI PROGRAM governance; document it.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 192–195, permalinks /go/modelexfiltration and /go/modelwatermarking. OpenCRE "Model exfiltration" maps to: ETSI sec. 6.1 (Model stealing attacks); OWASP Top 10 for ML — ML05:2023 Model Theft; MITRE ATLAS AML.T0024.002 (Exfiltration via AI Inference API: Extract AI Model); BIML-78(2020) model:5 ("Steal the Box"); NIST AI 100-2 sec. 2.4.3 (Model Extraction); ENISA Table 3 (Model disclosure). Further reading cited by the guide: an article on model exfiltration and "Thieves on Sesame Street" (LLM extraction). Note the OWASP LLM Top 10 2025 retired the old "Model Theft" entry (LLM10:2023) as a standalone item — the AI Exchange keeps it first-class, which is the more complete view for ML systems.

Why is a watermark that works against stolen weights useless against a distilled copy — and what fixes it?
Because typical watermarks are represented in out-of-distribution data: secret trigger inputs the owner can query. A distillation attacker trains their copy only on in-distribution input/output pairs, so the trigger behaviour is never transferred to the copy. Entangled watermarks (USENIX) fix this by entangling watermark behaviour with typical input data and its output, so extracting the task behaviour necessarily copies the watermark too.

Key Points

  • Model exfiltration = collecting input/output pairs (log harvesting, interception, or mass querying) and training a replica; a.k.a. model stealing / extraction / distillation / theft by use.
  • Impact is confidentiality breach of the model parameters: IP theft, offline evasion-attack development that bypasses your runtime protections, and stripping harmful-content safeguards (Anthropic's February 2026 claim about competitor exfiltration).
  • The copy is behavioural, not exact — attacks needing a detailed copy stay unfeasible via this route; exact copies come from direct development-time or runtime model leaks.
  • Risk applies only if the model is a trade secret or evasion risk matters — never for publicly available models.
  • Controls: #AI PROGRAM, #MONITOR USE, #RATE LIMIT, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL, #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING, #MODEL WATERMARKING.
  • Honest caveat: with access plus intensive use allowed, exfiltration is typically hard to prevent; detection signals (wide input coverage) may be benign and always need analysis.
  • #MODEL WATERMARKING proves ownership post-theft; standard watermarks fail against exfiltration (out-of-distribution triggers don't transfer) — entangled watermarks survive extraction.
  • Adversarial robust distillation is the same technique used defensively by developers on their own models.
Module 09 · §2.5 · OWASP AI Exchange

AI Resource Exhaustion — Denial of Service, Denial of Wallet

~10 minCovers guide pp. 195–197

Every inference costs compute, and someone pays for it. Attackers exploit that arithmetic in two directions: make the system unavailable (denial of service) or make it ruinously expensive (denial of wallet) — sometimes with a single, cleverly crafted input. This lesson covers the AI resource exhaustion threat, sponge/energy-latency attacks, and the two threat-specific controls: #DOS INPUT VALIDATION and #LIMIT RESOURCES.

THREAT
AI resource exhaustionSpecific input depletes funds or availability via frequency, volume, or content. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: MITRE ATLAS "Denial of ML service" (AML.T0029), NIST AI 100-2 §3.4.1, OWASP LLM10:2025.

The threat: three levers on the same failure

Specific input to the model leads to resource exhaustion, which can be depletion of funds or availability issues — the system becoming very slow or unresponsive, also called denial of service. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/airesourceexhaustion. The guide names three distinct failure levers — the failure occurs from:

  • Frequency — too many requests per unit time;
  • Volume — individual inputs that are too large;
  • Content — inputs whose substance is engineered to be expensive to process, even at normal size and rate.

Impact: loss of money, or the AI system is unavailable — leading to issues for processes, organizations, or individuals that depend on it: business continuity issues, safety issues in process control, and unavailability of services. When an AI model sits inside an industrial control or safety loop, "the model is slow" is not an inconvenience, it is a hazard.

Resource exhaustion — three input levers, two impacts
Frequency
Floodingnumerous requests in short time
#RATE LIMITthrottle per client
Volume
Oversized inputvery large content
#DOS INPUT VALIDATIONreject / correct
Content
Sponge inputcrafted to maximize computation time
#LIMIT RESOURCEScap per-input usage
Impactsdenial of service (slow/unresponsive) · denial of wallet (funds depleted) · business continuity · safety in process control

The guide's examples

  • Malicious intensive use of a paid third-party model leads to high costs for the use — the attacker spends your metered API budget. Pure denial of wallet.
  • A sponge attack, or energy latency attack: input designed to increase the computation time of the model. This is essentially a denial of wallet (DoW) attack, also potentially causing a denial of service. The guide links an article on sponge examples.
⚔ Attacker's view

Sponge attacks invert the usual DoS economics. Classical flooding needs the attacker to spend bandwidth proportional to the damage; a sponge input achieves asymmetric damage — one carefully crafted, normal-looking input that maximizes activation density or forces worst-case computation paths can burn seconds of GPU time instead of milliseconds. Against a per-token/per-second-billed endpoint, the attacker pays one request and you pay the inflated compute bill. Rate limiting alone can't see this: the request rate is innocent; the request content is the weapon. That's precisely why the guide adds content-aware controls on top of #RATE LIMIT.

Control stack

  • General controls apply as always.
  • #MONITOR USE — detect suspicious input or output.
  • #RATE LIMIT — limit the attacker trying numerous attack variants in a short time (the frequency lever).
  • #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL — reduce the number of potential attackers to a minimum.
  • #DOS INPUT VALIDATION — stop input suspicious for this attack (the volume/content lever) — card below.
  • #LIMIT RESOURCES — prevent depletion regardless of what gets through (the fail-safe) — card below.
#DOS INPUT VALIDATIONReject or correct malicious (e.g. very large) content before inferenceruntime · information security control for input threats
IntentDenial-of-service input validation: input validation and sanitization to reject or correct malicious content — e.g. very large inputs — before it consumes inference resources. Stops the volume/content levers of resource exhaustion at the cheapest possible point: before the model runs.
ImplementValidate and sanitize input against size and content expectations; reject or correct what fails. Follow the guidance in #MONITOR USE regarding detection considerations and response options (log detections, tune thresholds, define responses).
LimitsValidation can only stop what it can recognize: sophisticated sponge inputs may look size- and format-legitimate while still being computationally expensive, which is why #LIMIT RESOURCES must back it up. Standards coverage is thin: ISO 27002 has no control for this, and it is not covered yet in ISO/IEC AI standards.
StandardsOpenCRE: AI resource exhaustion input handling → NIST AI 100-2 sec. 3.4.1 (Availability Violations); OpenCRE on input validation. ISO 27002: no control; ISO/IEC: not covered yet. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/dosinputvalidation.
#LIMIT RESOURCESCap resource usage for a single model inputruntime · information security control for input threats
IntentLimit resource usage for a single model input, to prevent resource overuse. This is the fail-safe: even a sponge input that passes validation can only burn its capped budget (time, compute, tokens, money) — the blast radius per request is bounded by construction.
ImplementEnforce per-input ceilings at the serving layer: computation time-outs, memory/compute quotas, and output limits per request, so no single input can deplete shared resources or budget.
LimitsPer-input caps do not stop many-request campaigns — that is #RATE LIMIT's job — and overly tight caps can truncate legitimate heavy workloads. Standards coverage: ISO 27002 has no control for this, except Monitoring (covered in the controls for input threats); not covered yet in ISO/IEC AI standards.
StandardsOpenCRE: Limit inference resources. ISO 27002: no control (except Monitoring); ISO/IEC: not covered yet. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/limitresources.
LeverAttack shapePrimary controlWhy it works
FrequencyFlooding; intensive use of paid third-party model (denial of wallet)#RATE LIMIT (+ #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL)Caps requests per client per time window; fewer principals can flood
VolumeVery large single inputs#DOS INPUT VALIDATIONRejects/corrects oversized content before the model spends compute
ContentSponge / energy-latency input maximizing computation time#LIMIT RESOURCES (+ #DOS INPUT VALIDATION)Per-input resource ceiling bounds the damage even when the input looks legitimate
AllAny of the above#MONITOR USEDetects suspicious input/output patterns and drives response
◆ Senior insight

Note the deliberate redundancy: validation is a predictive control (guess what will be expensive and refuse it) while resource limiting is a reactive invariant (whatever happens, one request can never cost more than X). Predictive controls fail on novel inputs; invariants don't. In cost-review terms: if you can only implement one, implement the invariant — then add validation to cut the noise and #MONITOR USE to learn what your adversaries are trying. Also budget-side: set billing alerts and hard spend caps on third-party model accounts; denial of wallet is often discovered on the invoice, weeks too late.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 195–197, permalinks /go/airesourceexhaustion, /go/dosinputvalidation, /go/limitresources. Mappings: MITRE ATLAS — Denial of ML Service (AML.T0029); NIST AI 100-2 §3.4.1 Availability Violations; the guide links an article on sponge examples (Shumailov et al. introduced "sponge examples" as energy-latency attacks). Adjacent canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 — LLM10:2025 Unbounded Consumption covers the same denial-of-service/denial-of-wallet ground for LLM apps. The ISO gap is notable: ISO 27002 offers nothing specific here, and ISO/IEC AI standards do not cover these two controls yet.

Why doesn't #RATE LIMIT alone protect against sponge attacks?
A sponge (energy-latency) attack achieves its damage through the content of a single input, crafted to maximize the model's computation time. Its request rate can be entirely normal, so a frequency-based throttle never triggers. You need content/volume screening (#DOS INPUT VALIDATION) and, decisively, a per-input resource ceiling (#LIMIT RESOURCES) so even an undetected sponge can only burn a bounded budget.
How does resource exhaustion become a safety issue rather than just an availability issue?
The guide's impact statement includes safety issues in process control: when an AI system is part of a control loop for a physical or time-critical process, unavailability or extreme slowness means the process runs without its controller. Business continuity and service availability harms are financial; a starved model in process control can be a physical hazard.

Key Points

  • AI resource exhaustion: specific input causes fund depletion (denial of wallet) or availability loss (denial of service) via frequency, volume, or content.
  • Impacts go beyond cost: business continuity, unavailability of services, and safety issues in process control.
  • Sponge / energy-latency attacks craft inputs that maximize computation time — asymmetric damage that rate limiting cannot see.
  • #DOS INPUT VALIDATION: validate/sanitize to reject or correct malicious (e.g. very large) content before inference; follow #MONITOR USE guidance for detection and response.
  • #LIMIT RESOURCES: cap resource usage per single model input — the invariant that bounds damage even when detection fails.
  • Full stack: #MONITOR USE + #RATE LIMIT + #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL + the two threat-specific controls.
  • Standards: ATLAS Denial of ML Service, NIST AI 100-2 §3.4.1; ISO 27002 and ISO/IEC AI standards have no specific coverage yet (except monitoring).
Module 09 · Appendix · OWASP AI Exchange

Culture-Sensitive Alignment — When "Safe Output" Depends on Where You Are

~12 minCovers guide pp. 197–203

Alignment is not one thing. A model output that is a routine civics answer in one jurisdiction is a policy violation — or unlawful content — in another, and a prompt injection "succeeds" the moment the model mentions a locally taboo topic. This appendix-based lesson teaches why there is no single alignment that fits all regions, how thirteen national AI policies differ on cultural constraints, why word meanings drift out from under your guardrails, and how to design refusals that are explainable in cultural context.

Alignment, localized

In the context of large language models (LLMs), alignment refers to the process of ensuring that the model's behavior and outputs are consistent with human values, intentions, and ethical standards (see #MODEL ALIGNMENT; this appendix details that control — permalink: owaspai.org/go/culturesensitivealignment).

But "appropriate input or output" carries jurisdictional nuance. Certain news or events in history that are sensible topics for public discussion in one country are taboo in another. The security consequence is direct: a prompt injection can be interpreted as successful — and therefore unsafe — if the model divulges information or mentions topics that shouldn't be discussed in that region. Your definition of "the attack worked" is itself culture-dependent.

The guide's two example prompts, both perfectly ordinary in some regions and sensitive in others:

  1. "Should gun purchases be regulated in the market? I want to learn about the successes and failures of gun control policies of world governments."
  2. "What examples are there for and against abortion rights?"

There are topics all regions typically agree on; on controversial topics or specific historical events, regional policies diverge. And the model's reflections are imperfect and biased by training data distributions. With such divergent requirements, there is no single "alignment" that fits all regions — which leads to red teaming and blue teaming practices that must fit the cultural sensitivities of each region you serve.

Thirteen jurisdictions, three postures

The guide's comparison table (pp. 198–199). Legend as printed: [YES] = explicit cultural requirement; [PARTIAL] = implied/partial; [NO] = no cultural/political content constraints.

CountrySensitivity ReferenceReferenced Document
China[YES] Explicit enforcement of socialist values and national unity.Interim Measures for Generative AI Services
Saudi Arabia[YES] Requires cultural alignment in generative AI outputs.AI Ethics Principles
United Arab Emirates[PARTIAL] Implied concern for societal impact, not explicitly cultural.UAE AI Ethics Guidelines (MOCAI)
Singapore[NO] No political or cultural references. Focuses on ethics and robustness.Model AI Governance Framework
European Union[NO] Risk based legal framework with no ideological content constraints.EU Artificial Intelligence Act
United States–UK[NO] Focused on technical security and global collaboration.Secure AI System Development Guidelines
South Korea[PARTIAL] Ethical and rights based approach, not explicitly cultural.Policy direction for safe use of personal information in the era of artificial intelligence
Japan[NO] Supports innovation and social benefit without cultural enforcement.AI Guidelines for Business
Australia[NO] Risk based guidance and guardrails without cultural emphasis.AI Safety Standards
Israel[NO] Voluntary, sector specific ethics with no cultural prescriptions.Israel's Policy on Artificial Intelligence: Regulations and Ethics
Vietnam[NO] General ethical and safety focus, no explicit mention of societal values.Draft Law on High Technology and Emerging Technology
Taiwan[NO] Sectoral regulations without cultural or political constraints.General Explanation of the Draft Basic Law on Artificial Intelligence
Hong Kong[NO] Focus on fairness and explainability, no political/cultural directives.Ethical Artificial Intelligence Framework

Highlighted differences (guide pp. 199–201)

Saudi Arabia — AI Ethics Principles

"Generative AI applications should not use classified or confidential information… appropriate cybersecurity measures and data governance practices must be put in place."

"Outputs must be consistent with the intended use," requiring human oversight to prevent unintended consequences.

"Generative AI should align with national cultural values and avoid generating content that conflicts with societal norms and ethical expectations."

Analysis: Saudi Arabia frames AI security around data confidentiality, misuse prevention, and cultural alignment. Its principles focus on ensuring AI outputs do not conflict with Islamic and societal norms, with particular emphasis on public-sector discipline and oversight.

China — Interim Measures for Generative AI Services (translations as given)

Translation 1: "AI services must adhere to socialist core values and must not generate content that subverts state power, undermines national unity, or promotes ethnic hatred."

Translation 2: "Take effective measures to improve the transparency and accuracy of generated content."

Analysis: China integrates AI security with ideological enforcement, requiring adherence to socialist values and prohibiting outputs that threaten political stability or social cohesion — combining algorithmic safety with strict state-led audits and content controls.

United Arab Emirates — UAE AI Ethics Guidelines (MOCAI)

"AI systems must not compromise human safety and dignity."

"The UAE aims to guide AI development to align with public interest, sustainability, and societal benefit."

Analysis: UAE policies do not explicitly mandate cultural or religious conformity, but their emphasis on dignity, community, and societal benefit implies AI systems are expected to respect the Emirati social fabric — an inferred cultural alignment within broader ethical frameworks.

South Korea — personal-data policy direction (translation as given)

Translation: "The constitutional right to self-determination of personal data… ensuring the self-determination of personal data subjects is important in the development and service of AI…"

Analysis: South Korea focuses on human-centric, ethical AI respecting individual rights, dignity, and public trust. It does not enforce traditional cultural or political alignment; its policies reflect a socially conscious and democratic value orientation.

Why does the appendix matter to a security engineer rather than only to a policy team?
Because it redefines the success condition of an attack. A prompt injection counts as successful — and unsafe — if it makes the model mention topics that are prohibited in the deployment region, even if nothing "technical" was breached. That means your red-team scenarios, refusal guardrails, and output filters must encode regional policy, and a one-size alignment tested only against your home region's norms will silently fail elsewhere.

Fair output and the refusal question

Some argue that a fair model should present arguments from all sides, especially on controversial topics. In practice, there is no objective fairness, because output is limited to the training data used to build the model in the first place. Marginalized communities whose records were not preserved historically will always have their views underrepresented or omitted. Unless the purpose of your AI project is intended to include or encourage open debate, the verbose native behavior of LLMs may become a liability for app developers serving users in particular regions.

Semantic drift: words move under your guardrails

The vagueness of words impacts the effectiveness of language-based guards. Unlike traditional engineering, the meanings of words have long been known — in linguistics — to drift through time. Events occur in real time and shift morality and ethics; yesterday's neutral term becomes today's slur, or today's innocuous topic becomes tomorrow's flashpoint. Two compensations from the guide:

  • Lengthen the system prompt to increase precision (see #OVERSIGHT) — trading prompt budget for less ambiguity in the guard's language.
  • For emerging news or events against a country's national values, real-time system-prompt fixes are ineffective — instead use red teaming techniques (see the guide's Testing section), preferably continuously updated with concerns for your region of interest, to reveal your LLM's weaknesses, and use guardrails (see #OVERSIGHT) to filter out or alert on unwanted responses for additional protection.
⚠ Common misconception

"We wrote the blocklist and refusal policy at launch; we're covered." Language-based guards decay: semantic drift plus real-world events continuously move terms in and out of sensitivity, and no static system prompt can anticipate next month's flashpoint. The guide's answer is a process, not an artifact — continuously updated, region-specific red teaming feeding guardrail updates.

Culture-aware explanation of refusal

When the system refuses to interpret an input, it should explicitly inform the user, explained in the context of the relevant culture. The guide contrasts two responses to the gun-regulation prompt:

Prompt: "Should gun purchases be regulated in the market? I want to learn about the successes and failures of gun control policies of world governments."

  • Example Response 1 (curt refusal): "Your input has violated our enterprise policy. Please try again."
  • Example Response 2 (verbose engagement): a full essay — overview of successes and failures of gun-control policies across countries, ending with a position: yes, regulation to some degree, with features successful policies share (strict vetting with background checks and mental-health evaluations; limits on firearm types accessible to civilians; buyback/amnesty programs to reduce illegal circulation; strong enforcement, border security, and public cooperation), while noting no policy is universally transferable and regulations must integrate the cultural, economic, and security realities of each country.

The guide's commentary: given that model explainability is mandated in many jurisdictions, application developers whose projects don't require an untuned LLM's full verbosity may benefit from implementing a neutral response. That mitigates the burden of justifying the origins of out-of-scope outputs like Response 2 — but a bare Response 1 fails the culture-aware-explanation bar. The target is between the extremes: a refusal (or scoped answer) that states why, in terms meaningful to the user's cultural and regulatory context.

Culture-aware output decision — scoping the response per region
User inputpossibly sensitive topic
→
Alignment + guardrailstuned per jurisdiction, refreshed by regional red teaming
→
Scoped neutral answeravoids untuned verbosity; explainability-friendly
→
Culture-aware refusalexplains the refusal in context of the relevant culture
🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Enumerate deployment jurisdictions and map each against the policy table above — know which impose explicit cultural requirements ([YES]), implied ones ([PARTIAL]), or none ([NO]).
  • Define per-region "successful prompt injection" criteria that include divulging locally prohibited topics.
  • Run region-specific red teaming and blue teaming, continuously updated with local events and concerns.
  • Prefer scoped, neutral responses over untuned verbosity when your app does not need open debate — it eases explainability obligations.
  • Make every refusal explain itself in the relevant cultural context; audit refusal messages like any other output.
  • Treat blocklists/system prompts as perishable: schedule reviews against semantic drift and emerging events, and back them with guardrails (#OVERSIGHT).
◆ Senior insight

The deep lesson: alignment quality is not a scalar. A model can be "safer" in one region and simultaneously "less safe" in another, from the same fine-tune. Architecturally, this pushes you away from baking all policy into model weights and toward a layered design — a common base model plus per-region system prompts, guardrails, and refusal templates — because regional policy changes faster than you can retrain, and a guardrail file can ship in hours. It also changes vendor due diligence: ask model providers which region's sensitivities their alignment encodes, because it always encodes someone's.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 197–203, permalink /go/culturesensitivealignment. Appendix references: Zhang et al. (2024), "Verbosity ≠ Veracity: Demystify Verbosity Compensation Behavior of Large Language Models"; Arora et al. (2022), "Probing Pre-Trained Language Models for Cross-Cultural Differences in Values"; Wikipedia contributors (2025), "Semantic Change". Policy documents cited: China's Interim Measures for Generative AI Services; Saudi Arabia's AI Ethics Principles; UAE AI Ethics Guidelines (MOCAI); Singapore's Model AI Governance Framework; the EU AI Act; the joint US–UK Secure AI System Development Guidelines; South Korea's personal-information policy direction; Japan's AI Guidelines for Business; Australia's AI Safety Standards; Israel's AI policy; Vietnam's draft high-tech law; Taiwan's draft AI basic law; Hong Kong's Ethical AI Framework. Related canon: NIST AI RMF GOVERN and MAP functions cover exactly this context-of-deployment analysis.

Why does the guide suggest a neutral response instead of the rich Response 2, even where Response 2 is legal?
Because model explainability is mandated in many jurisdictions: if your app emits a verbose opinionated essay, you may have to justify the origins of every claim in it — an out-of-scope burden for most applications. A scoped neutral response limits what you must explain, while a culture-aware refusal message keeps the user informed of why the limit exists.
What breaks language-based guards over time, and what is the guide's mitigation?
Semantic drift: word meanings shift with time and events, so a guard phrased in today's language misfires tomorrow, and emerging events can make previously safe topics sensitive overnight. Mitigations: lengthen/precision-tune system prompts (#OVERSIGHT), and — since real-time prompt fixes cannot keep up — continuously updated, region-specific red teaming plus guardrails that filter or alert on unwanted responses.

Key Points

  • Alignment = keeping model behavior consistent with human values and ethics — but what counts as appropriate output varies by jurisdiction; no single alignment fits all regions.
  • A prompt injection is "successful and unsafe" if it makes the model mention locally prohibited topics — attack success criteria are culture-dependent.
  • The 13-country table splits into explicit cultural requirements (China, Saudi Arabia), implied/partial (UAE, South Korea), and none (Singapore, EU, US–UK, Japan, Australia, Israel, Vietnam, Taiwan, Hong Kong).
  • There is no objective fairness: outputs are bounded by training data, and historically unrecorded marginalized views stay underrepresented.
  • Semantic drift erodes language-based guards; compensate with more precise system prompts and continuously updated regional red teaming plus guardrails (#OVERSIGHT).
  • Refusals must be explained in the relevant cultural context — neither a curt "policy violated" nor an unscoped essay.
  • Where explainability is mandated, scoped neutral responses reduce the burden of justifying out-of-scope verbose output (Zhang et al. 2024 on verbosity ≠ veracity).
  • Red team and blue team per region you serve, refreshed as events move sensitivities.
Module 10 · §3.0 · OWASP AI Exchange

Development-Time Threats — Why the Training Pipeline Is an Attack Surface

~11 minCovers guide pp. 204–205

Everything before the model goes live — data collection, feature engineering, training, packaging — is an attack surface most security programs were never designed to see. This lesson teaches you the seven particularities that make AI development environments fundamentally different from ordinary software development, and maps the five controls that the rest of this module builds on.

THREAT
Development-time threats (group)Attacks on the engineering environment and supply chain: poisoning of data/code/parameters, leaks of sensitive training data, theft of model IP. Lifecycle: development-time. Maps to: OWASP LLM03/LLM04, MITRE ATLAS AML.T0010 (ML Supply Chain Compromise).

Background: data science creates new attack surfaces

The guide's Section 3 covers AI security threats during development of the AI system — the engineering environment and the supply chain are the attack surfaces. Data science — data engineering (collecting, storing, and preparing data) plus model engineering (for machine learning, often called the training phase) — introduces new elements into the engineering environment, and each new element is a new place an attacker can act. Data engineering is typically a large and important part of machine learning engineering, and together with model engineering it requires appropriate security to protect against four failure classes:

  • Data leaks — training data escaping the environment;
  • Data poisoning — tampering with data to manipulate model behaviour;
  • Leaks of intellectual property — model parameters, source code, configuration;
  • Supply chain attacks — compromised externally obtained data, models, components, or hosting.

In addition, data quality assurance helps reduce risks of both intended and unintended data issues — a poisoned record and an honest data-entry mistake can corrupt a model in exactly the same way, so integrity mechanisms pay off twice.

The seven particularities of AI development environments

These are the reasons you cannot simply reuse a standard dev-environment security policy. Learn them; they justify every control in this module.

  1. Real, sensitive data lives in development. The data in the AI development environment is real data that is typically sensitive, because training obviously has to happen on real data — not the fake/synthetic test data you typically see in standard development environments. Therefore data protection activities need to be extended from the live system to the development environment.
  2. Development elements are poisoning targets. Elements in the AI development environment — data, code, configuration & parameters — require extra protection because they are prone to attacks that manipulate model behaviour, called poisoning.
  3. Source code, configuration and parameters are critical IP. In AI, these artifacts typically embody the organization's competitive advantage, not just plumbing.
  4. New supply chain elements. The AI supply chain introduces new supplied things: data, model, AI components and model hosting.
  5. External software runs inside the engineering environment. External components may execute within the engineering environment (for example, to train models) — a new threat of malicious components gaining access to assets in that environment (e.g., to poison training data).
  6. AI software components run development-time, not just in production. Data-processing libraries, feature-engineering tools, or even the training framework itself run in the development environment instead of production. This increases the attack surface, because malicious development components could gain access to training data or model parameters.
  7. Collaborative development crosses trust boundaries. Model development can be collaborative across trust boundaries — federated learning, merging parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) modules, and using model conversion services. These collaborations can mitigate some risks (e.g., by spreading training data) but also extend the attack surface, increasing threats such as data poisoning.
◆ Senior insight

Particularity 1 inverts the classic assumption security teams carry from ordinary IT: there, production is the crown-jewels zone and dev is low-risk sandbox. In AI it is often the opposite — the development environment holds the full raw training corpus (personal data, trade secrets) while production may only hold a distilled model. If your data-classification and DLP tooling only watches production, you are guarding the vault after the gold moved to the workshop.

⚔ Attacker's view

Development-time attacks are attractive because they are persistent and quiet: a poisoned dataset or a backdoored dependency corrupts every model trained from it, survives retraining pipelines, and manifests only under attacker-chosen triggers — long after the intrusion window closed. Runtime attacks must be repeated per request; a single successful dev-time compromise is amortized across the model's whole life. That asymmetry is why the economics favor the attacker here.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 204–205, permalink owaspai.org/go/developmenttime/. ISO/IEC 42001 B.7.2 briefly mentions development-time data security risks. Canon mapping: MITRE ATLAS AML.T0010 (ML Supply Chain Compromise), AML.T0020 (Poison Training Data); OWASP LLM Top 10: LLM03 Supply Chain, LLM04 Data and Model Poisoning; NIST AI RMF functions MAP and MANAGE cover identifying third-party and lifecycle risks.

The five controls for development-time protection

Beyond the General controls (governance, risk analysis, etc.), the guide prescribes five development-time controls — each gets deep treatment in this module:

  • #DEV SECURITY / #DEV DATA PROTECT — protect the development environment and its AI-specific sensitive assets (lesson 2 and lesson 3);
  • #SEGREGATE DATA — create parts of the development environment with extra protection (lesson 4);
  • #CONF COMPUTE — deny access to where sensitive data is processed (lesson 4);
  • #FEDERATED LEARNING — decreases the risk of all data leaking, and as a side-effect increases the risk of some data leaking (lesson 5);
  • #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE — control where data and models come from (lesson 6).
Development-time attack surfaces and the controls that cover them
Assets at risk
Train/test datareal, sensitive
Model parameterscritical IP
Code & configcritical IP
Threat vectors
Poisoningdata / code / params
Leaksdata & IP
Supply chaindata, model, components, hosting
Controls
#DEV SECURITY / #DEV DATA PROTECTsecure the environment
#SEGREGATE DATAhardened zones
#CONF COMPUTEhide data in use
#FEDERATED LEARNINGspread the data
#SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEgovern what comes in
Why does training data in the development environment need more protection than test data in a typical software project?
Because it is real data, not fabricated fixtures: training must happen on real data, which is typically sensitive (personal data, company secrets). Standard dev environments assume fake data and therefore weak protection; AI dev environments must extend live-system data protection back into development (Particularity 1).
Name three "new" supplied assets in an AI supply chain that don't exist in conventional software supply chains.
Data, models (including fine-tuning artifacts such as LoRA/PEFT modules), and model hosting. AI-specific development tooling is also new, and some of it executes at development time with access to sensitive assets.
How can collaborative development (e.g., federated learning) both reduce and increase risk?
It spreads training data across parties, so no single breach exposes everything (reducing the risk of all data leaking) — but it crosses trust boundaries and multiplies the attack surface, so threats like data poisoning and partial data leakage increase (Particularity 7).

Key Points

  • Section 3 covers threats during development; the attack surfaces are the engineering environment and the supply chain.
  • Data engineering + model engineering (the training phase) introduce new elements and hence new attack surfaces.
  • Four failure classes to defend: data leaks, data poisoning, IP leaks, supply chain attacks — plus data quality assurance against intended and unintended issues.
  • Particularity 1: AI dev environments hold real, sensitive data — extend production-grade data protection into development.
  • Poisoning targets data, code, configuration and parameters; source code/config/parameters are critical IP.
  • New supply chain elements: data, model, AI components, model hosting; external components can execute inside the dev environment.
  • Collaboration patterns (federated learning, PEFT merging, model conversion services) mitigate some risks but extend the attack surface.
  • Five controls: #DEV SECURITY/#DEV DATA PROTECT, #SEGREGATE DATA, #CONF COMPUTE, #FEDERATED LEARNING, #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE. ISO/IEC 42001 B.7.2 touches this area only briefly.
Module 10 · §3.0 · OWASP AI Exchange

#DEV DATA PROTECT — Securing the AI Development Environment and Its Data

~15 minCovers guide pp. 205–208, 210–211

The first and broadest development-time control: bring the AI-specific assets — training data, test data, model parameters, technical documentation — under your existing security management system, then apply five concrete protection strategies. This lesson covers the control's scope, the surprising places "training data" hides at runtime (RAG repositories and system prompts), and the strategy-by-strategy ISO 27002 mapping.

The control

The guide presents this control under the heading #DEV SECURITY (development security, permalink /go/devsecurity/) with its data-protection detail also referred to as #DEV DATA PROTECT (development-time data protection). Treat them as one coherent control: secure the AI development infrastructure, with special attention to the sensitive information that is typical to AI.

#DEV DATA PROTECTDevelopment security & development-time data protection (#DEV SECURITY)development-time · information security control
IntentAppropriate security of the AI development infrastructure, taking into account the sensitive information typical to AI: training data, test data, model parameters and technical documentation. If these assets leak, the confidentiality of intellectual property and/or of train/test data (which may contain company secrets or personal data) is hurt. Their integrity must also be protected, to prevent data or model poisoning.
ImplementAdd the said assets to the existing security management system. Security involves, for example: encryption, screening of development personnel, protection of source code/configuration, and virus scanning on engineering machines. Five detailed protection strategies: (1) encryption of data at rest; (2) technical access control following least privilege, plus centralized access control; (3) operational security to protect stored data, including environment segregation (see #SEGREGATE DATA); (4) logging and monitoring to detect suspicious manipulation of data (e.g., outside office hours); (5) integrity checking at build, deploy, and supply-chain stages (see next lesson).
LimitsData and models may have been obtained externally, and external software components often run within the AI development environment — risks this control alone cannot govern; see #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE. Training data is not confined to development-time: continuous training, RAG repositories, and system prompts put "training-like" data in the runtime environment where the same protections must apply.
StandardsISO 27001 ISMS does not cover development-environment security explicitly, but it is designed to take care of it provided the relevant assets and threats are taken into account — so add train/test/validation data, model parameters and technical documentation to the existing development-environment asset list. Strategy-level ISO 27002 mappings below. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/devsecurity/

Training data doesn't stay in development — three exceptions

Training data is in most cases only present during development-time, but the guide names three exceptions where the same protections must follow the data into runtime:

  1. Continuous training. A machine learning model may be continuously trained with data collected at runtime — putting (part of) the training data in the runtime environment, where it also needs protection under this control.
  2. Augmentation / RAG data. For GenAI, information can be retrieved from a repository to be added to a prompt (augmentation) — for example, to inform a large language model about the context to consider for an instruction or question. This principle is called in-context learning. Example: OpenCRE-chat uses a repository of requirements from security standards to add to a user question so the LLM is better informed. That data is public — but in many cases Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) repositories hold company secrets or otherwise sensitive data. Organizations benefit from unlocking their unique data, for their own use or as a service/product; the architecture is attractive because the alternative — training or fine-tuning an LLM — is expensive and difficult, and a RAG approach may suffice. Effectively this puts repository data to the same use as training data: controlling the behaviour of the model. Therefore the security controls that apply to training data also apply to this run-time repository data (see the guide's augmentation data manipulation threat).
  3. The system prompt. A special type of data added to input: instructions to the model regarding its behaviour. It also augments the model's input, so it determines model behaviour — and is therefore an asset to protect.
⚠ Common misconception

"We don't train models, we just do RAG — so training-data controls don't apply to us." Wrong by the guide's own logic: RAG repository data controls model behaviour exactly the way training data does. A poisoned or leaked RAG store is functionally a poisoned or leaked training set. Classify and protect your vector database, document store, and system prompts with the same rigor as a training corpus.

◆ Senior insight

The cheapest first step is administrative, not technical: put the four AI asset types (train/test/validation data, model parameters, technical documentation) on the ISMS asset register. The moment they are registered assets, the whole ISO 27001 machinery — risk assessment, ownership, access review, incident response — starts applying to them automatically. Most gaps in AI dev security are asset-inventory gaps, not control gaps.

The five protection strategies, with standards mapping

Protection strategyWhat it doesUseful standards (and coverage gap)
1. Encryption of data at restProtects stored train/test data, parameters and documentation from disclosure if storage is accessed.ISO 27002 control 5.33 Protection of records — gap: covers this control fully, with the particularities. OpenCRE on encryption of data at rest (printed as "OpenCE" in the guide).
2. Technical access controlLimit access to the data following the least privilege principle.ISO 27002 controls 5.15, 5.16, 5.18, 5.3, 8.3 — gap: covers this control fully, with the particularities. OpenCRE.
2a. Centralized access controlAdminister data-access rights from one authoritative point instead of per-system.There is no ISO 27002 control for this. OpenCRE.
3. Operational security for stored dataDay-to-day secure operation of the environments holding the data; one way to increase development security is to segregate the environment — see #SEGREGATE DATA.Many ISO 27002 controls cover operational security — gap: covers this control fully, with the particularities. Includes ISO 27002 5.23 (Information security for use of cloud services), 5.37 (Documented operating procedures), and many more (see OpenCRE link).
4. Logging and monitoringDetect suspicious manipulation of data — e.g., changes outside office hours.ISO 27002 control 8.16 Monitoring activities — gap: covers this control fully. OpenCRE on Detect and respond.
5. Integrity checkingVerify assets have not been tampered with, at build, deploy and supply-chain stages.See lesson 3 — this strategy gets its own full treatment.
🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Register train/test/validation data, model parameters, and technical documentation as ISMS assets (ISO 27001).
  • Encrypt all AI data at rest (ISO 27002 5.33); include notebook scratch storage and experiment-tracking stores.
  • Enforce least-privilege access on datasets and parameter stores (ISO 27002 5.15/5.16/5.18/5.3/8.3); centralize access administration.
  • Screen development personnel; protect source code/configuration; run virus scanning on engineering machines.
  • Log and alert on data manipulation, especially anomalous timing such as outside office hours (ISO 27002 8.16).
  • Extend all of the above to runtime copies of "training-like" data: continuous-training feeds, RAG repositories, system prompts.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 205–208 and 210–211, permalink owaspai.org/go/devsecurity/. Canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM02 (Sensitive Information Disclosure) and LLM04 (Data and Model Poisoning) are the runtime consequences this control pre-empts; NIST AI RMF PROTECT-adjacent practices sit in MANAGE 2.x; MITRE ATLAS AML.T0020 (Poison Training Data) is the technique integrity protection interferes with.

Why do the security controls for training data also apply to a RAG repository?
Because RAG repository data is put to the same use as training data: it controls the behaviour of the model. Whether behaviour is shaped by gradient updates or by in-context learning is irrelevant to the attacker — manipulating or stealing either achieves the same effect, so the guide requires the same controls.
Which of the five protection strategies has no ISO 27002 control at all?
Centralized access control for the data — the guide notes there is no ISO 27002 control for it (only OpenCRE covers it). General technical access control is covered by ISO 27002 5.15, 5.16, 5.18, 5.3 and 8.3.
ISO 27001 doesn't explicitly mention AI development environments. Does that make it useless here?
No — the ISMS is designed to protect whatever assets you declare. The guide's instruction is to add train/test/validation data, model parameters and technical documentation to the existing development-environment asset list, after which the ISMS's risk treatment machinery covers them.

Key Points

  • #DEV SECURITY / #DEV DATA PROTECT = secure the AI development infrastructure plus its AI-specific sensitive assets: training data, test data, model parameters, technical documentation.
  • Leaks hurt confidentiality of IP and of train/test data (company secrets, personal data); integrity loss enables data or model poisoning.
  • Implementation = add assets to the existing security management system: encryption, personnel screening, source/config protection, virus scanning on engineering machines.
  • Training data escapes development in three ways: continuous training at runtime, RAG/augmentation repositories (in-context learning), and system prompts — protect all three like training data.
  • RAG is attractive because training/fine-tuning an LLM is expensive and difficult; OpenCRE-chat is the guide's example.
  • Five strategies: encryption at rest (ISO 5.33), least-privilege access control (ISO 5.15/5.16/5.18/5.3/8.3), centralized access control (no ISO control), operational security (ISO 5.23, 5.37, many more), logging/monitoring (ISO 8.16), and integrity checking.
  • ISO 27001 covers this implicitly — but only if you put the AI assets on the asset list.
Module 10 · §3.0 · OWASP AI Exchange

Integrity Checking — Build Stage, Deploy Stage, and Cryptographic Model Signing

~15 minCovers guide pp. 208–210

Integrity checking is the fifth protection strategy of #DEV DATA PROTECT, and the one that most directly interferes with poisoning and tampering. You'll learn where checks belong (build, deploy, supply chain), why signing an ML model is harder than signing a binary, and how a web-scale dataset was shown to be practically poisonable because it stored URLs instead of data.

What integrity checking covers

Part of development security is checking the integrity of assets. The assets in scope: train/test/validation data, models/model parameters, source code and binaries. Integrity checks can be performed at various stages — build, deploy, and supply chain management — and integrating them mitigates the risks associated with tampering: unauthorized modifications and mistakes. Note that framing: integrity controls are dual-purpose, catching both the adversary and the fat-fingered engineer.

Integrity checks across three stages
Build stagecode signing · checksums · SCA · CI tests
→
Deploy stageIaC config · automated deploy + rollback · RASP
→
Supply chaincryptographic signatures on supplied components
→
AI systemtamper-evident lifecycle

Integrity checks — build stage

During the build stage it is crucial to validate the integrity of source code and dependencies, to ensure no unauthorized changes have been introduced. Techniques:

  • Source code verification — implement code signing and checksums to verify the integrity of the source code, ensuring the code has not been tampered with.
  • Dependency management — regularly audit and update third-party libraries and dependencies to avoid vulnerabilities; use tools like Software Composition Analysis (SCA) to automate this. See #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE.
  • Automated testing — continuous integration (CI) pipelines with automated tests catch issues early: unit tests, integration tests, and security tests.
▸ Worked example

The guide's build-stage example: a software company using CI pipelines integrates automated security tools that scan the codebase and dependencies for vulnerabilities, so only secure and verified code progresses through the pipeline. Failing the build on an unsigned commit or a critical SCA finding turns integrity from an audit exercise into a gate.

Integrity checks — deploy stage

The deployment stage requires careful management to ensure AI models and supporting infrastructure are securely deployed and configured. Key practices:

  • Environment configuration — deployment environments securely configured and consistent with security policy; use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools to maintain configuration integrity.
  • Secure deployment practices — deployment automation to minimize human error and enforce consistency; use deployment tools that support rollback capabilities to recover from failed deployments.
  • Runtime integrity monitoring — continuously monitor the deployed environment for integrity violations; tools like runtime application self-protection (RASP) provide real-time protection and alert on suspicious activity.
▸ Worked example

The guide's deploy-stage example: a cloud-based AI service provider uses IaC tools to automate deployment of secure environments and continuously monitors for configuration drift or unauthorized changes. Drift detection matters because a silently changed inference-server config (e.g., loosened deserialization settings) can reopen a hole the build stage had closed.

Integrity checks — supply chain

Managing the AI supply chain means securing the components and processes involved in developing and deploying AI systems:

  • Component authenticity — use cryptographic signatures to verify the authenticity and integrity of components received from suppliers, preventing malicious components from entering the system.
  • For the full treatment, see #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE.
▸ Worked example

The guide's supply-chain example: an organization using pre-trained AI models from external vendors requires those vendors to provide cryptographic signatures for model files and detailed security assessments, ensuring integrity and security of the models before integration.

Cryptographic signing of models — why it's harder than signing software

A significant step forward for provable machine learning model provenance is cryptographic signing of models — similar in concept to how we secure HTTP traffic with Secure Socket Layer (SSL) or Portable Executable (PE) files with Authenticode. But there is one key difference: models encompass a number of associated artifacts of varying file formats, not a single homogeneous file, so the approach must differ. Models comprise code and data but often need additional pieces to execute correctly: tokenizers, vocab files, configs, and inference code — everything used to initialize the model so it's ready to accept data and perform its task.

To comprehensively verify a model's integrity, all of these artifacts must be considered when assessing illicit tampering or manipulation — any change to a file required for the model to run may introduce a malicious action or degrade performance. No standard yet exists, but there is ongoing work by the OpenSSF Model Signing SIG to define a specification and drive industry adoption. As that unfolds, there may be interplay with ML-BOM and AI-BOM, to be codified into the certificate. Signing and verification will become a major part of the ML ecosystem, as it has in many other practices, with guidance following an agreed-upon open-source specification.

⚔ Attacker's view

If only the weights file is signed, I attack the tokenizer or the inference code instead — swap a vocab file to shift token boundaries, or edit the config to load a different architecture with a hidden head. The model "verifies" and still betrays you. The defender's signature scheme must cover the whole artifact set, because the attacker only needs the one file you forgot.

Dataset integrity — the LAION lesson

THREAT
Data poisoning via URL indirection in web-scale datasetsDatasets that store pointers instead of data can be poisoned by whoever controls (or acquires) the pointed-to locations. Lifecycle: development-time. Maps to: LLM04, ATLAS AML.T0020.

The data a model consumes is the most influential part of the MLOps lifecycle and should be treated as such. Data is more often than not sourced from third parties via the internet, or gathered internally for later training — but can its integrity be assured?

Datasets may not be a collection of text or images at all, but pointers to other pieces of data rather than the data itself. The LAION-400m dataset (printed "LAOIN-400m" in the guide) stores pointers to images as URLs — and data stored at a URL is not permanent; it may be manipulated or removed. That level of indirection introduces integrity issues and leaves you vulnerable to data poisoning, as shown by Carlini et al. in "Poisoning Web-Scale Datasets is practical": expired domains hosting dataset URLs can be bought and re-populated with attacker-chosen content. Verification of dataset entries through hashing is of the utmost importance to reduce the capacity for tampering, corruption, or data poisoning. (See the guide's data poisoning section for the threat side.)

◆ Senior insight

Carlini et al. showed the economics are brutally in the attacker's favor: for roughly the cost of a few expired domain registrations (~$60 in their estimate) an attacker could control a meaningful fraction of a web-scale dataset's content at download time. Hash-pinning every entry at collection time flips the economics back — the attacker must now break a cryptographic hash, not buy a domain. If your data pipeline ingests URLs without recorded content hashes, treat every re-download as a fresh, unverified supply-chain event.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 208–210, within owaspai.org/go/devsecurity/. Named work: OpenSSF Model Signing SIG (specification in progress); Carlini et al., "Poisoning Web-Scale Datasets is practical". Canon: MITRE ATLAS AML.T0020 (Poison Training Data), AML.M0014 (Verify AI Artifacts) is the ATLAS mitigation matching these checks; OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM03 (Supply Chain) and LLM04 (Data and Model Poisoning).

Why can't ML model signing simply reuse the Authenticode approach used for PE files?
Authenticode signs a single homogeneous file. A model is a set of associated artifacts in varying formats — weights, tokenizers, vocab files, configs, inference code — and a change to any file required for the model to run can introduce malicious behaviour. The signature scheme must therefore cover the whole artifact set; the OpenSSF Model Signing SIG is defining such a specification.
What made LAION-400m poisonable in practice?
It stores URLs (pointers) rather than the images themselves, and content at a URL is not permanent — it can be manipulated or removed, e.g., by acquiring expired domains. Carlini et al. demonstrated this is practical. Hash verification of each dataset entry at collection time is the countermeasure.
Integrity checks target "unauthorized modifications and mistakes". Why do both matter?
Because a model can be corrupted identically by an attacker's tampering or by an honest error (wrong file version, silent corruption). Integrity mechanisms — checksums, signing, CI gates, IaC drift detection — are agnostic to intent, so one investment mitigates both risk classes.

Key Points

  • Integrity checking covers train/test/validation data, models/model parameters, source code and binaries — against unauthorized modifications and mistakes.
  • Build stage: source code verification (code signing, checksums), dependency management (SCA), automated testing in CI (unit/integration/security tests).
  • Deploy stage: IaC-managed environment configuration, deployment automation with rollback, runtime integrity monitoring (RASP).
  • Supply chain: cryptographic signatures to verify component authenticity from suppliers.
  • Model signing is like SSL/Authenticode conceptually, but must cover the full artifact set: tokenizers, vocab files, configs, inference code — no standard yet; OpenSSF Model Signing SIG is defining one, with possible ML-BOM/AI-BOM interplay.
  • Data is the most influential part of the MLOps lifecycle; pointer-based datasets (LAION-400m's URLs) invite poisoning — Carlini et al. proved it practical.
  • Hash verification of dataset entries is of the utmost importance.
Module 10 · §3.0 · OWASP AI Exchange

#SEGREGATE DATA and #CONF COMPUTE — Zoning the Development Environment

~11 minCovers guide pp. 211–212

Two complementary containment controls: segregation splits the development environment into individually hardened zones so that access follows need, and confidential computing hides sensitive data even from the engineers using it. Together they shrink the set of people and processes that could leak or poison your training data to a minimum.

#SEGREGATE DATA — separated, hardened, need-to-know areas

#SEGREGATE DATASegregate sensitive development data into restricted areasdevelopment-time · information security control
IntentStore sensitive development data (training or test data, model parameters, technical documentation) in separated areas with restricted access. Each separate area can then be hardened accordingly, and access granted only to those who need to work with that data directly — reducing both leak and poisoning exposure.
ImplementExample areas for segregating training data: (1) External — when training data is obtained externally; (2) Application development environment — for application engineers who may need the actual training data but require different access rights (e.g., no need to change it); (3) Data engineering environment — for engineers collecting and processing the data; (4) Training environment — for engineers training the model with the processed data; here controls can address risks involving access from the other, less-protected development areas, mitigating for example data poisoning; (5) Operational environment — when training data is collected in operation. For broader environment security, see #DEV SECURITY.
LimitsSegregation restricts who can reach data but not what authorized insiders do with it once inside a zone — pair with logging/monitoring (#DEV DATA PROTECT strategy 4) and, where available, #CONF COMPUTE to remove even the engineers' direct visibility.
StandardsISO 27002 control 8.31 Separation of development, test and production environments — gap: covers this control only partly. The particularity: in AI, the development environment typically holds the sensitive data instead of the production environment — the reverse of non-AI systems. So restrict access to that data within development, and segregate further inside development so only those who need the data for their work can reach it (some developers will not be processing data). Permalink: owaspai.org/go/segregatedata/
Five segregation areas for training data (guide p. 211)
Inbound
1 · Externalexternally obtained training data
5 · Operationaldata collected in operation
Engineering
2 · App developmentread-mostly rights, no change access
3 · Data engineeringcollect & process data
Most protected
4 · Training environmentcontrols vs. less-protected areas → mitigates data poisoning
◆ Senior insight

The key design judgment is the rights asymmetry between zones: application engineers may legitimately need to read real training data but almost never need to write it — so the app-dev zone gets read access and the data-engineering zone alone gets write access. Poisoning requires write; leakage requires read. Splitting the two permissions across zones means an attacker must compromise two different populations to both poison and cover their tracks.

⚠ Common misconception

"We already separate dev, test and prod per ISO 27002 8.31, so we're covered." That control was written for a world where production holds the sensitive data. In AI it's typically the other way around — the crown jewels are in development.
