ISO 8.31 therefore covers #SEGREGATE DATA only partly: you must additionally restrict and subdivide access inside the development environment.

#CONF COMPUTE — hiding data even while in use

#CONF COMPUTEConfidential compute for training data and parametersdevelopment-time · information security control
IntentDeny access to where sensitive data is processed: if available and possible, use features of the data science execution environment to hide training data and model parameters from model engineers — even while the data is in use. Closes the "in use" gap that encryption at rest and in transit leave open, and removes the engineer population as a disclosure channel.
ImplementUse confidential-computing features of the data science execution environment where the platform offers them — the guide keys this on availability and possibility ("if available and possible"), acknowledging it depends on infrastructure support rather than being universally deployable.
LimitsOnly applicable where the execution environment supports it; it protects data during processing but does not by itself address poisoning of inputs before they enter the enclave, nor disclosure through the trained model's behaviour (see membership inference in the next lesson).
StandardsNot covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/confcompute/
▸ Worked example

Concrete instantiations of this control class (industry canon, beyond the guide's text): hardware trusted execution environments such as Intel SGX/TDX, AMD SEV-SNP, and cloud offerings built on them (Azure Confidential VMs, GCP Confidential Computing, AWS Nitro Enclaves). A training job runs inside an attested enclave: the platform operator and the model engineers can submit jobs and retrieve the resulting model, but cannot read the plaintext training data or intermediate parameters in memory.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Map which roles genuinely need direct data access; everyone else works in a zone without it.
  • Create the five areas (external, app development, data engineering, training, operational) with per-zone hardening and access lists.
  • Give the training environment the strongest posture — it is where poisoning attempts from less-protected areas must be stopped.
  • Where the platform supports it, run sensitive training under confidential compute so even model engineers cannot see data in use.
  • Document the ISO 27002 8.31 gap in your compliance mapping: separation of dev/test/prod alone is not sufficient for AI.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 211–212. Permalinks: owaspai.org/go/segregatedata/, owaspai.org/go/confcompute/. ISO 27002 8.31 (partial coverage, with the reversed-sensitivity particularity); #CONF COMPUTE not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. Canon: NIST AI RMF MANAGE; ATLAS mitigation AML.M0005 (Control Access to ML Models and Data at Rest) is the nearest ATLAS analogue to segregation.

Why does ISO 27002 8.31 cover #SEGREGATE DATA only partly?
8.31 separates development, test and production — assuming production holds the sensitive data. In AI the development environment typically holds it instead, so beyond that separation you must restrict access to the data within development and segregate further inside it, since some developers do not process data at all.
What specific risk does the training environment's extra hardening address?
Risks that involve access from the other, less-protected development areas — the guide's example being data poisoning: an attacker (or compromised account) in the app-dev or data-engineering zone must not be able to alter the processed data the training environment consumes.
What gap does confidential compute close that encryption at rest cannot?
Data in use. Encryption at rest protects stored data, but during training the data is decrypted in memory where engineers and the platform could observe it. Confidential compute hides training data and model parameters even while being processed — including from the model engineers themselves.

Key Points

  • #SEGREGATE DATA: store training/test data, model parameters and technical documentation in separated, individually hardened, access-restricted areas.
  • Five example areas: external, application development, data engineering, training, operational — with rights differentiated per role (app engineers may read but not change data).
  • The training environment gets controls against risks coming from less-protected areas — mitigating data poisoning.
  • ISO 27002 8.31 covers segregation only partly: in AI, sensitive data lives in development (the reverse of non-AI systems), so segregate further within development.
  • #CONF COMPUTE: use execution-environment features to hide training data and model parameters from model engineers even while in use — "if available and possible".
  • #CONF COMPUTE is not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards.
  • Segregation controls reach; confidential compute controls visibility; combine with logging to control behaviour.
Module 10 · §3.0 · OWASP AI Exchange

#FEDERATED LEARNING — Spreading the Data, Spreading the Risk

~15 minCovers guide pp. 212–215

Federated learning keeps training data where it was collected and moves the model instead — a genuine confidentiality and compliance win, but one that trades a single big attack surface for many small ones plus a powerful central party. This lesson teaches the four-step protocol, the four benefit domains, and the seven documented challenges, including why "the data never leaves the device" is not the guarantee it sounds like.

The control and the architecture

#FEDERATED LEARNINGDecentralized training without central data collectiondevelopment-time · AI engineer control
IntentApply when a training set is distributed over different organizations, preventing the data from needing to be collected in a central place. This decreases the risk of ALL data leaking — and increases the risk of SOME data leaking (more parties, more attack surface). Advantages include reduced central compute and the potential for privacy preservation, since training data may remain local to the client.
ImplementDecentralized ML architecture: a number of clients (e.g., sensor or mobile devices) participate in collaborative, decentralized, asynchronous training, orchestrated and aggregated by a controlling central server. Four high-level steps: (1) server-to-client broadcast; (2) local models updated on the client; (3) trained local models returned to the central server; (4) central server updates via model aggregation. Benefits span regulatory compliance, enhanced confidentiality, scalability & bandwidth, and data diversity.
LimitsData can still be reconstructed from weights by a knowledgeable attacker (notably the central party); membership inference and model theft still disclose training data; poisoning attack surface grows with the client network; device heterogeneity and broadcast-phase transmission create additional security concerns; central queries over client data need privacy-preserving techniques (SFE/SMPC) which all trade privacy against utility.
StandardsOpenCRE: Federated learning → ENISA Table 5 "Use federated learning to minimize risk of data breaches". Not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/federatedlearning/
flowchart TD
  S["Central server — orchestrates and aggregates"] -->|"1. Server-to-client broadcast"| C["Clients — e.g. sensor or mobile devices, data stays local"]
  C -->|"2. Local models updated on the client"| C
  C -->|"3. Trained local models returned to server"| S
  S -->|"4. Central server updates via model aggregation"| S

Figure — the four high-level steps of federated learning (reconstructed from the guide's prose, p. 213).

Benefits and use cases

Federated machine learning may offer significant benefits in several domains:

  • Regulatory compliance. Data collection is decentralized, which may allow greater ease of regulatory compliance — especially beneficial for international organizations where data transfer across borders may be unlawful.
  • Enhanced confidentiality. Data does not leave the client, reducing potential exposure of sensitive information. However — data can still be reconstructed from weights by a knowledgeable attacker (i.e., the central party in the FL protocol), so sensitive data exposure is still not guaranteed to be prevented.
  • Scalability & bandwidth. Decreased training-data transfer between clients and the central server benefits organizations with high data-transfer costs, and helps in resource-constrained environments where bandwidth would otherwise limit data uptake/availability for modeling. Because federated learning optimizes network resources, these benefits may on aggregate allow greater capacity and flexible scalability.
  • Data diversity. Aggregating updates from a plurality of models brings data and model diversity; efficiency in resource-constrained environments further increases heterogeneity of client devices and thus the diversity of available data.

The seven challenges

THREAT
Data disclosure and poisoning within federated learningGradient reconstruction by a dishonest central party, membership inference from the model, model theft in transit/at server, and poisoning by malicious clients. Lifecycle: development-time (collaborative training). Maps to: ATLAS AML.T0020 / AML.T0024, LLM04.
  1. Remaining risk of data disclosure by the model. Protect against data disclosure by use threats (e.g., membership inference): sensitive data may still be extracted from the model(s). Consequently model theft threats also need mitigation, since training data may be disclosed from a stolen model — and the FL architecture adds specific attack surfaces for model theft: transferring the model from client to server, and storage of the model at the server. Both require protection.
  2. FL does not sufficiently protect client data against the central party. An active and dishonest central party can extract user data from received gradients — by manipulating shared weights and isolating a user's training data via deltas between the client's weights and the central weights. Minimization and obfuscation (e.g., adding noise) is necessary to protect users' data from the central party.
  3. More attack surface for poisoning. Data/model poisoning attacks apply, and federated systems additionally introduce a vast network of clients, some of which may be malicious.
  4. Device heterogeneity. Devices vary widely in computational, storage, transmission and other capabilities — challenging deployments and introducing device-specific security concerns. While designing for connectivity, battery life and compute constraints, it is critical to also consider edge device security.
  5. Broadcast latency & security. Efficient communication across a federated network is hard; strategies that minimize broadcast-phase latency must also account for data security risks, because models are vulnerable during transmission phases — any communication optimization must preserve security of data in transit.
  6. Querying the data creates a risk. With data stored on many clients, central queries may be required for analysis work alongside FL — which would need the server to access data at all clients: a security risk. To analyse without collecting, privacy-preserving techniques exist — cryptographic and information-theoretic strategies such as Secure Function Evaluation (SFE), also known as Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMC/SMPC). However, all approaches entail tradeoffs between privacy and utility.
  7. (Implicit across the above) The aggregate lesson: federation redistributes rather than eliminates risk — the central party, the transmission channel, and every client are now trust decisions.
Benefit (why adopt)Counterpart challenge (what you inherit)
Data never centrally collected → easier cross-border complianceCentral analysis queries now require SFE/SMPC, with privacy–utility tradeoffs
Data stays on client → enhanced confidentialityDishonest central party can reconstruct data from gradients; noise/minimization needed
Less data transfer → bandwidth and scalability winsModels vulnerable during transmission; latency optimizations must not weaken transit security
Plurality of clients → data diversityVast client network → more poisoning surface, some clients may be malicious; heterogeneous edge devices to secure
⚔ Attacker's view

The strongest position in an FL protocol is the server. As documented by Boenisch et al. ("When the curious abandon honesty: Federated learning is not private", IEEE EuroS&P 2023), an actively dishonest central party doesn't passively hope for leakage — it crafts the weights it broadcasts so that the returned gradients isolate individual clients' training records. If I can't be the server, I enroll malicious clients instead and poison the aggregate (Sun et al., "Data Poisoning Attacks on Federated Machine Learning", IEEE IoT Journal, 2020). Either role is cheap to obtain in an open federation.

◆ Senior insight

Read the guide's one-line summary as a portfolio statement: FL decreases the risk of all data leaking (no central honeypot) and increases the risk of some data leaking (more parties, channels, and devices). Choose it when your threat model fears the catastrophic single breach or a legal bar on data transfer — not when your primary worry is targeted extraction of individual records, where a malicious aggregator is precisely the wrong party to empower.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 212–215, permalink owaspai.org/go/federatedlearning/. OpenCRE: Federated learning → ENISA Table 5: "Use federated learning to minimize risk of data breaches". Not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. Named papers: Boenisch, Dziedzic, Schuster, Shamsabadi, Shumailov, Papernot — "When the curious abandon honesty: Federated learning is not private", IEEE EuroS&P 2023, pp. 175–199; Sun, Cong, Dong, Wang, Liu — "Data Poisoning Attacks on Federated Machine Learning", IEEE Internet of Things Journal 9 (2020): 11365–11375; Wahab, Mourad, Otrok, Taleb — "Federated Machine Learning: Survey, Multi-Level Classification, Desirable Criteria and Future Directions in Communication and Networking Systems", IEEE Communications Surveys & Tutorials 23 (2021): 1342–1397; Yang, Liu, Chen, Tong — "Federated Machine Learning", ACM TIST 10 (2019): 1–19 (one of the most highly cited FML papers, >1,800 citations).

State the guide's double-edged risk summary for federated learning in one sentence.
Federated learning decreases the risk of ALL data leaking (no central collection point) while increasing the risk of SOME data leaking (more parties, more channels, more devices — each a smaller but real attack surface).
How does an actively dishonest central party extract a client's training data?
By manipulating the shared weights it broadcasts and then isolating the user's training data through computing deltas between the client's returned weights and the central weights — reconstructing data from gradients. Minimization and obfuscation (e.g., adding noise) are necessary countermeasures.
Which two FL-specific attack surfaces exist for model theft?
The transfer of the model from client to server (transmission phase) and the storage of the model at the server. Both require protection because a stolen model can disclose training data (e.g., via membership inference).
You need organization-wide analytics over data held on thousands of clients. What does the guide recommend?
Avoid giving the server raw access to client data; use privacy-preserving techniques — cryptographic and information-theoretic strategies such as Secure Function Evaluation (SFE), a.k.a. Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMC/SMPC) — while accepting that all approaches trade off privacy against utility.

Key Points

  • FL = decentralized, collaborative, asynchronous training by clients, orchestrated and aggregated by a central server; data stays local.
  • Four steps: server-to-client broadcast → local model update → return of local models → central aggregation.
  • Benefits: regulatory compliance (cross-border transfer), enhanced confidentiality, scalability & bandwidth, data diversity; plus reduced central compute.
  • Confidentiality is not guaranteed: a knowledgeable attacker — notably the central party — can reconstruct data from weights/gradients; add minimization and noise.
  • Membership inference and model theft still apply; FL adds theft surfaces at client-to-server transfer and server storage.
  • Poisoning surface grows: a vast client network, some clients possibly malicious; edge-device security and transmission-phase security are design requirements.
  • Central data queries need SFE/SMPC; every privacy-preserving approach trades privacy for utility.
  • Standards: OpenCRE → ENISA Table 5; not covered yet in ISO/IEC standards.
Module 10 · §3.0 · OWASP AI Exchange

#SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE — Provenance, Verification and Governance of AI Supply Chains

~19 minCovers guide pp. 215–221

Your model was fine-tuned by one vendor on a base model from another, trained on data labeled by a third — and one of your notebooks just pip-installed something with write access to it all. This lesson covers the full #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE control: what makes AI supply chains different, what records to keep (AIBOMs, Model Cards), how to verify and inspect supplied models before loading them, how to evaluate suppliers, and what governance can and cannot achieve.

THREAT
ML supply chain compromiseCorrupted, poisoned, outdated or mismanaged externally provided data, models, tooling or hosting — leading to unwanted model behavior, data exfiltration, service disruption, or loss of control. Lifecycle: development-time (and hosted-service runtime). Maps to: LLM03, MITRE ATLAS AML.T0010.

Why AI supply chains are different

Supply chain management minimizes security risk from externally obtained elements. In conventional software engineering those elements are source code or software components (e.g., open source). AI supply chains differ in four ways:

#DifferenceConsequence
1Three new supplied assets: data, models, and model hosting — models can also arrive as fine-tuning artifacts (e.g., LoRA modules).Classic component-tracking tools don't see these asset types at all.
2The supply chain may include the own organization, not just third parties — data and models may come from different departments and sources.Supply chain management effectively includes data provenance, even for internal flows.
3New AI-specific development tooling is typically required.A whole new dependency population to inventory and vet.
4Some tools are executed development-time rather than at runtime.If corrupted, these tools attack development-time assets directly — including training data and model parameters.

Because of these characteristics, classic supply chain management may not fully cover AI development environments — particularly notebook-based workflows and MLOps tooling.

Objective

Reduce the risk of corrupted, compromised, outdated, or mismanaged externally provided components and services — supplied assets (data, models, libraries, tools) as well as hosted AI models and third-party AI services. Risk reduction is achieved through verification, continuous monitoring, and governance of components and providers across the AI system lifecycle. Compromises or misconfigurations could lead to unwanted model behavior, data exfiltration, service disruption, or loss of control over critical functionality.

Effective AI supply chain management helps to:

  • identify compromised, poisoned, or untrustworthy data, models, and externally provided AI services before use;
  • detect unauthorized modifications to AI assets, APIs, model endpoints, or service configurations;
  • assess and monitor risks introduced by hosted foundation models and third-party AI providers;
  • limit the blast radius of upstream security failures, service outages, or malicious model updates;
  • manage the impact of provider-driven changes, such as silent model updates or altered system behavior;
  • enforce data handling requirements when using external AI services (e.g., training use, retention, logging);
  • ensure traceability of which external models or services were used in which system version;
  • support informed risk decisions when relying on external suppliers and AI service providers.

Applicability

This control applies throughout the AI system lifecycle — particularly during data acquisition, model sourcing, training, fine-tuning, and integration. It is especially relevant when:

  • datasets or models are obtained from external or partially trusted sources;
  • models are transferred across organizations or teams (e.g., base model → fine-tuning vendor);
  • development-time tools or dependencies have access to sensitive AI assets;
  • supply chains span multiple suppliers, jurisdictions, or labeling pipelines.

Risk management determines when deeper governance or verification is warranted — especially for threats of supply chain compromise, poisoning, or unauthorized modification.

Implementation 1 — provenance, record keeping, and traceability

The AI supply chain can be complex. The guide's example: a model is trained by one vendor and then fine-tuned by another vendor. Or: an AI system contains multiple models — one is a model fine-tuned with data from source X, using a base model from vendor A that claims data from sources Y and Z, where the data from source Z was labeled by vendor B. Because of this complexity, data and model provenance is a helpful activity.

The guide's worked supply chain example — five parties behind one model
Sources Y & Zclaimed training data · Z labeled by vendor B
→
Vendor Abase model
→
Fine-tuning vendortuned model
→
Your AI systemmultiple models inside

Maintain structured records for AI-specific assets and services to establish provenance and accountability. Relevant information:

  • origin and versioning of models and datasets (provenance), including pre-trained model lineage;
  • checksums or hashes to identify specific instances;
  • training data sources and augmentation steps, and data used to augment training data;
  • dependencies and environment requirements relevant to security (e.g., hardware, frameworks, packages);
  • ownership, authorship, and responsible teams or suppliers.

Such records are often referred to as Model Cards, AIBOMs, or MBOMs, and can complement traditional SBOM practices by including AI-specific artifacts.

Implementation 2 — lifecycle-aware record updates

Provenance and traceability records must be updated at meaningful lifecycle points. Typical update points: initial model development, major model version releases, pre-production deployment, significant architecture changes, introduction of new training datasets, and critical dependency updates. Additional checkpoints may be defined by team practice or risk posture. Making update points explicit keeps records accurate as models, data, and dependencies evolve.

Implementation 3 — integrity, verification, and vulnerability management

Verify the integrity and authenticity of supplied data and models. Common techniques:

  • checksum or hash verification;
  • signed attestations and integrity metadata;
  • content-addressable storage or verification at read time;
  • periodic integrity audits.

Monitor for known vulnerabilities affecting supplied models, data pipelines, and dependencies — via regular review of security advisories — and respond in a timely manner, informed by severity and exploitability, through updates, containment, or compensating controls. Integrate these activities into broader vulnerability management and incident response processes (see #DEV SECURITY).

Implementation 4 — supplier evaluation and assessment of supplied models

Evaluate the trustworthiness of suppliers — external vendors or internal teams — to contextualize risk. Review:

  • reputation;
  • activity;
  • supplier security posture;
  • development environments and access controls over AI assets;
  • provenance claims for data and models;
  • contractual assurances or warranties.

Models obtained from less trusted sources warrant additional assessment:

  • validating model formats and serialization to avoid unsafe loading;
  • inspecting architectures and layers for unexpected or custom components;
  • testing runtime behavior in isolated environments, observing resource usage, system calls, or network activity.

Concretely, the guide describes three assessment activities:

  • Artifact inspection prior to execution — validate file formats and signatures; scan for suspicious opcodes or serialized patterns associated with operating-system commands, subprocess invocation, or file access; check for corruption using checksums or error handling.
  • Architecture inspection — list model layers without loading the model; identify unknown, custom, or dynamically executed components; review model graphs for unexpected structures.
  • Runtime behavior testing — in isolated or sandboxed environments, execute standard validation inputs or randomized probes while monitoring system resources, runtime calls, and network activity for suspicious behavior.

These assessments reduce the risk of backdoors, malicious payloads, or poisoned artifacts entering the system.

⚔ Attacker's view

The cheapest model-supply-chain attack is a malicious serialized model: Python pickle-based formats can embed opcodes that execute OS commands the moment the file is loaded — before a single inference runs. That is why the guide insists on inspecting artifacts prior to execution and listing layers without loading. Publishing a plausible-looking "fine-tuned" model on a public hub costs the attacker nearly nothing; one careless load() in a notebook with dev-environment credentials pays it all back.

Implementation 5 — further practices to strengthen governance

  • Expanded asset records — add contextual information: processing and transformation steps, tools and methods used, model architecture details, training configurations/parameters, ownership and authorship, licensing information, and records of which actors or groups had access to the asset throughout its lifecycle. Improves auditability and post-incident analysis.
  • Contractual and legal risk coverage — address external data/model risks contractually as well as technically: warranties, terms and conditions, usage instructions, or supplier agreements covering data provenance, security practices, and liability for compromised or misrepresented assets.
  • Integration with configuration and version management — include code, configuration, documentation, and packaged artifacts in traceability, linked to existing version control/configuration management, keeping changes auditable and reproducible.
  • Alignment with vulnerability management processes — track and remediate vulnerabilities in data, models, and AI-specific dependencies in the same processes used for software components; this reduces fragmentation and keeps AI assets considered alongside traditional dependencies.

Risk-reduction guidance, particularity, and limitations

Supply chain management reduces risk by adding scrutiny and visibility to what the AI system depends on: knowing where data and models originate, how they were produced, and how they change over time makes it harder for compromised components to remain undetected. Residual risk depends on:

  • the number and trustworthiness of suppliers;
  • the depth of provenance and verification practices;
  • the effectiveness of integrity protections;
  • the ability to respond quickly to newly discovered vulnerabilities.

As with traditional software supply chains, governance does not eliminate risk — it helps detect, contain, and respond to supply chain issues earlier and with lower impact.

Particularity: unlike conventional software supply chains, AI supply chains include non-code artifacts (data, models, fine-tuning adapters) and development-time execution paths that expose sensitive assets — making governance of notebooks, MLOps tools, and training environments particularly important. Supply chain controls therefore extend beyond runtime binaries and must consider how AI assets are created, transformed, and reused across the lifecycle.

Limitations: the control relies on the accuracy and completeness of records and attestations. False or incomplete provenance claims, compromised suppliers, or insufficient visibility into upstream processes limit effectiveness. Complex multi-party supply chains can make full traceability difficult, and trust decisions often remain probabilistic rather than absolute.

#SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEGovern externally obtained data, models, tooling and hostingdevelopment-time · information security control
IntentReduce the risk of corrupted, compromised, outdated, or mismanaged externally provided components and services — data, models (incl. LoRA/fine-tuning artifacts), libraries, tools, hosted models and third-party AI services — via verification, continuous monitoring, and governance across the lifecycle. Prevents unwanted model behavior, data exfiltration, service disruption, and loss of control.
ImplementFive pillars: (1) provenance/record keeping/traceability — Model Cards, AIBOMs, MBOMs complementing SBOMs with origin, versions, hashes, data sources, dependencies, ownership; (2) lifecycle-aware record updates at defined checkpoints; (3) integrity/verification/vulnerability management — hashes, signed attestations, content-addressable storage, periodic audits, advisory monitoring with severity/exploitability-driven response; (4) supplier evaluation plus deep assessment of less-trusted models — safe-loading validation, opcode scanning, architecture inspection without loading, sandboxed runtime behavior testing; (5) further governance — expanded asset records, contractual/legal coverage, version-management integration, alignment with vulnerability management.
LimitsDepends on accuracy/completeness of records and attestations; false provenance claims, compromised suppliers, or poor upstream visibility limit effectiveness; full traceability is hard in complex multi-party chains; trust decisions remain probabilistic, not absolute. Governance detects, contains, and lowers impact — it does not eliminate risk.
StandardsOpenCRE: AI supply chain management → ENISA Table 5 "Ensure reliable sources are used"; MITRE ATLAS AML.M0014 Verify AI Artifacts; MITRE ATLAS AML.M0023 AI Bill of Materials; NIST AI 100-2 sec. 3.2 AI Supply Chain Attacks and Mitigations; see also MITRE ATLAS ML Supply chain compromise. ISO controls 5.19, 5.20, 5.21, 5.22, 5.23, 8.30 — gap: full coverage with the stated particularity, but lacking controls on data provenance. ISO/IEC 24368:2022 and ISO/IEC 24030:2024. ISO/IEC AWI 5181 (Data provenance) — completes coverage together with the ISO 27002 controls, provided provenance concerns all sensitive data (not only personal data). ISO/IEC 42001 (AI management) briefly mentions data provenance and refers to ISO 5181 in section B.7.5. ETSI GR SAI 002 V1.1.1 — Securing Artificial Intelligence: Data Supply Chain Security. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/supplychainmanage/
🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Inventory every externally obtained asset — datasets, base models, LoRA adapters, labeling services, hosted model APIs, MLOps tools — including internal-department sources.
  • Record provenance (origin, version, lineage), hashes, data sources, security-relevant dependencies, and ownership in an AIBOM/Model Card; update at the defined lifecycle checkpoints.
  • Verify supplied artifacts: hash/checksum checks, signed attestations, content-addressable or read-time verification, periodic integrity audits.
  • Never load an untrusted model directly: validate format/serialization, scan for OS-command/subprocess/file-access opcodes, inspect architecture without loading, then sandbox-test runtime behavior (resources, syscalls, network).
  • Assess suppliers: reputation, activity, security posture, dev-environment access controls, provenance claims, contractual assurances — with extra depth for less-trusted sources.
  • Watch advisories for supplied models/pipelines/dependencies; respond by severity and exploitability with updates, containment, or compensating controls.
  • Cover residual risk contractually: warranties and liability for compromised or misrepresented assets.
  • Extend governance to notebooks, MLOps tooling, and training environments — the AI-specific development-time execution paths.
▸ Worked example

Silent model updates are not hypothetical: hosted-LLM users have repeatedly reported behavior shifts after providers updated models behind stable API names. If your compliance evidence says "system version 3.2 used model M", but the provider silently swapped M's weights, your traceability claim is false. The guide's objective list addresses exactly this: manage provider-driven changes and ensure traceability of which external models/services were used in which system version — e.g., by pinning versioned model identifiers and recording provider change notices in the AIBOM.

◆ Senior insight

The guide's most transferable sentence is the last one: trust decisions remain probabilistic. A signed attestation from a compromised supplier verifies perfectly. So design for containment, not just verification: sandbox first executions, limit what credentials dev-time tools hold, and rehearse the "upstream model was malicious" incident. Verification lowers the probability of compromise; blast-radius limits cap its cost — you need both, and the budget split should follow which suppliers you can actually assess.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 215–221, permalink owaspai.org/go/supplychainmanage/. Canon: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM03 (Supply Chain); MITRE ATLAS AML.T0010 (ML Supply Chain Compromise) with mitigations AML.M0014 (Verify AI Artifacts) and AML.M0023 (AI Bill of Materials); NIST AI 100-2 §3.2; NIST AI RMF MAP function (third-party risks) and GOVERN (supplier policies). The EU AI Act's provider/deployer obligations make the traceability records here (which model version, which data) practically mandatory evidence for high-risk systems.

List the four ways AI supply chains differ from conventional software supply chains.
(1) Three new supplied asset types — data, models (incl. LoRA fine-tuning artifacts), and model hosting; (2) the supply chain includes your own organization (inter-department data/model flows → data provenance); (3) new AI-specific development tooling; (4) some tools execute at development time, exposing training data and model parameters if corrupted.
Why does the guide say to list model layers "without loading the model"?
Because unsafe loading is itself the attack: deserializing a malicious model file can execute embedded code (OS commands, subprocess calls, file access) immediately. Architecture inspection must therefore happen statically — parse the file, list layers, review the graph — before any code path that executes the artifact, and runtime testing then happens only in a sandbox.
Which ISO gap remains even after applying ISO 27002 controls 5.19–5.23 and 8.30?
Data provenance — the ISO 27002 supplier controls cover supply chain management fully (with the AI particularity) but lack provenance controls. ISO/IEC AWI 5181 (Data provenance) completes the coverage, provided provenance concerns all sensitive data and not just personal data; ISO/IEC 42001 B.7.5 references ISO 5181.
What does "governance does not eliminate risk" mean operationally for a team consuming external models?
Records and attestations can be false or incomplete, suppliers can be compromised, and multi-party chains resist full traceability — so treat governance as improving detection, containment and response speed. Complement it with technical blast-radius limits (sandboxing, least-privilege credentials for dev tools) and contractual liability coverage.

Key Points

  • AI supply chains add data, models (incl. LoRA adapters), and model hosting as supplied assets; include your own organization; require AI-specific tooling; and run tools at development time — so classic supply chain management doesn't fully cover notebooks and MLOps tooling.
  • Objective: reduce risk from corrupted/compromised/outdated/mismanaged external components via verification, continuous monitoring, and governance — countering unwanted behavior, exfiltration, disruption, and loss of control (including silent model updates).
  • Provenance records — Model Cards, AIBOMs, MBOMs — capture origin/versioning/lineage, hashes, data sources, security-relevant dependencies, and ownership; they complement SBOMs.
  • Update records at lifecycle checkpoints: initial development, major releases, pre-production, architecture changes, new datasets, critical dependency updates.
  • Verify integrity: hashes, signed attestations, content-addressable/read-time verification, periodic audits; manage vulnerabilities by severity and exploitability.
  • Less-trusted models get deep assessment: safe-loading validation, opcode/serialization scanning, architecture inspection without loading, sandboxed runtime behavior testing — against backdoors, malicious payloads, poisoned artifacts.
  • Strengthen governance with expanded asset records (incl. who had access), contractual liability coverage, version-management integration, and unified vulnerability management.
  • Residual risk scales with supplier count/trustworthiness, provenance depth, integrity effectiveness, and response speed; trust decisions are probabilistic — governance detects and contains, it does not eliminate.
  • Standards: OpenCRE → ENISA Table 5, ATLAS AML.M0014 & AML.M0023, NIST AI 100-2 §3.2; ISO 5.19–5.23/8.30 (no data-provenance coverage), ISO/IEC 24368:2022, 24030:2024, AWI 5181, 42001 B.7.5, ETSI GR SAI 002 V1.1.1.
Module 11 · §3.1 · OWASP AI Exchange

The Poisoning Threat Map: Broad Model Poisoning at Development Time

~14 minCovers guide pp. 221–225

A model is only as trustworthy as the pipeline that produced it. Poisoning attacks exploit that pipeline: instead of attacking the running system, the attacker corrupts what the model learns from — or the model artifact itself — so the deployed system misbehaves on demand. This lesson gives you the full attack-surface map: where poisoning can strike across the lifecycle, the three distinct poisoning types, and the first dedicated countermeasure, #MODEL ENSEMBLE. You will also close out #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE, the control that governs where your data and models come from.

What "broad model poisoning" means

THREAT
Broad model poisoning (development-time)Attacker manipulates development elements — the engineering environment and the supply chain — to alter model behaviour. Lifecycle: development-time. Maps to: LLM04:2025, ML02:2023, ATLAS AML.T0020.

Development-time model poisoning in the broad sense is when an attacker manipulates development elements — the engineering environment and the supply chain — to alter the behaviour of the model. The guide defines three types, each covered in its own subsection of this module:

  1. Data poisoning: an attacker manipulates training data, or data used for in-context learning (§3.1.1, next lesson).
  2. Development-environment model poisoning: an attacker manipulates model parameters, or other engineering elements that take part in creating the model, such as code, configuration or libraries (§3.1.2, lesson 5).
  3. Supply-chain model poisoning: using a supplied trained model which has been manipulated by an attacker (§3.1.3, lesson 5).

Impact: the integrity of model behaviour is affected. That plays out as unwanted model output — a fraud-detection model that fails to flag fraud, decisions that lead to safety issues, reputation damage, liability. Note the asymmetry with confidentiality threats: poisoning does not steal anything; it changes what the system does.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 222–224, permalink owaspai.org/go/modelpoison/. Canon mapping: OWASP Top 10 for LLM LLM04:2025 Data and Model Poisoning; OWASP Top 10 for ML ML02:2023 Data Poisoning Attack; MITRE ATLAS AML.T0020 Poison Training Data and ML Supply Chain Compromise (AML.T0010); NIST AI 100-2 §2.3 (poisoning attacks). Under NIST AI RMF, poisoning risk management sits primarily in the MAP and MANAGE functions applied to the training pipeline.

Where poisoning strikes: the lifecycle attack surface

Data and model poisoning can occur at various stages. The guide's "AI threat model — poisoning" diagram organizes the attack surface into four lifecycle zones — supplier, preparation-time, train-time, and runtime — with poisoning entry points marked at each:

  • Supplied data or a supplied model can have been poisoned before you ever see it.
  • Poisoning in the development environment can occur in the data preparation domain, or in the training environment. If the training environment is separated security-wise, it becomes possible to implement certain controls (including tests) against data poisoning that took place at the supplier or during preparation time.
  • If training data is collected at runtime (user feedback, live telemetry, reviews), that data is under poisoning threat while it is being collected.
  • Model poisoning alters the model directly — at the supplier, at development-time, or during runtime.
flowchart TD
  subgraph SG1["Supplier"]
    TDS["Training data to be supplied"] --> MLS["Machine learning by supplier"]
    MLS --> SM["AI Model supplied"]
  end
  subgraph SG2["Preparation-time"]
    TDP["Training data being prepared"] --> DP["Data preparation"]
    DP --> TDP
  end
  subgraph SG3["Train-time, trusted environment"]
    TD["Training data"] --> ML["Machine learning"]
    ML --> AIM["AI Model"]
  end
  subgraph SG4["Runtime"]
    TDC["Training data collected"]
    IN["Input"] --> APP["Application and infrastructure"]
    APP --> OUT["Output"]
  end
  TDS --> TDP
  TDP --> TD
  TDC --> TD
  SM -.->|"Finetune supplied AI model"| ML
  SM -.->|"Run supplied AI model"| AIM
  AIM --- APP
  T1["THREAT: Training data poisoning"] -.-> TDS
  T2["THREAT: Training data poisoning"] -.-> TDP
  T3["THREAT: Training data poisoning"] -.-> TD
  T4["THREAT: Training data poisoning"] -.-> TDC
  T5["THREAT: Model poisoning"] -.-> SM
  T6["THREAT: Model poisoning"] -.-> AIM

Figure — AI threat model: poisoning attack surface across the lifecycle (guide p. 223). Dashed arrows show optional supply-chain paths: fine-tuning a supplied model versus running it as-is; dotted threat labels mark every poisoning entry point.

◆ Senior insight

The most consequential design decision in this diagram is the trust boundary around the training environment. If train-time is security-separated from the rest of your engineering estate, you get a place to apply and trust anti-poisoning controls: whatever entered from the supplier or from data preparation can still be tested, filtered, and distorted inside the trusted zone before the model learns from it. Without that boundary, an attacker with engineering access can simply re-poison the data after your controls have run. Every data-poisoning control in this module implicitly assumes you know where your trust boundary sits.

Runtime-collected training data appears in the runtime zone of the diagram, yet data poisoning is a development-time threat. How do you reconcile that?
The threat is classified by what it corrupts — the learning process — not by where the manipulation physically happens. Data collected at runtime (reviews, user feedback) eventually feeds the training set, so manipulating it at collection time poisons future training. The attack surface spans the lifecycle; the damage lands at development time when the model learns.

The control stack for broad model poisoning

The guide layers controls for broad model poisoning in four tiers:

  • General controls — especially Limiting the effect of unwanted behaviour: because no anti-poisoning control is watertight, you cap the blast radius of a misbehaving model (oversight, least model privilege).
  • Controls for development-time protection: #DEV SECURITY to protect the development environment; #SEGREGATE DATA to create parts of the development environment with extra protection; #CONF COMPUTE for denying access to where sensitive data is processed; #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE especially to control where data and models come from.
  • Controls for data poisoning (each gets deep treatment later in this module): #MORE TRAIN DATA to try and overrule poisoned data; #DATA QUALITY CONTROL to try and detect or prevent poisoned data; #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION to try and corrupt poisoned data; #POISON ROBUST MODEL to reduce the ability to recall poisoned data; plus controls aimed at improving the generalization ability of the model — reducing memorization of any poisoned samples: training with adversarial samples (#TRAIN ADVERSARIAL) and adversarial robust distillation.
  • Controls specific to broad model poisoning: #MODEL ENSEMBLE, so that if one of the models is poisoned, it can be contained.

#MODEL ENSEMBLE — contain the poisoned model

#MODEL ENSEMBLEDetect poisoning by comparing models trained on random train-set splitsdevelopment-time · AI engineer control — including specific runtime implementation
IntentDeploy the model as an ensemble of models created by randomly splitting the trainset, to allow detection of poisoning. If one model's output deviates from the others, it can be ignored, as this indicates possible manipulation of the train set — the poisoned samples landed in that model's split.
ImplementRandomly split the training set; train one model per split; at runtime, compare outputs across the ensemble and discard/flag the deviating model's answer. Note the terminology: ensemble learning is a machine-learning term for using multiple learning algorithms with the purpose of better predictive performance — here the same machinery is repurposed for security.
LimitsEffectiveness degrades with poison volume: the more the dataset has been poisoned with samples, the less effective this approach is — heavily poisoned data lands in every split, so all ensemble members agree on the wrong answer. Also multiplies training and inference cost by the ensemble size.
StandardsNot covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. OpenCRE: Ensemble AI models → ETSI §6.2.3 Ensemble; ENISA Table 5 "Choose and define a more resilient model design"; MITRE ATLAS AML.M0006 Use Ensemble Methods. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/modelensemble/
⚔ Attacker's view

The ensemble forces me to poison enough samples that my trigger survives a random split into N subsets — roughly N times the injection volume, N times the chance your quality controls notice me. That is the defender's economic win: the control does not make poisoning impossible, it makes cheap, low-volume poisoning ineffective. If I can already inject at scale (say I control a data supplier), the ensemble buys you little.

Closing out #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE

The core description of #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE (its purpose and implementation) is covered with the development-time protection controls earlier in the guide. What matters here — because it is the front line against supplied poisoned data and models — are its AI-specific particularity, its honest limitations, and its standards mapping:

#SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEGovern where data, models and tools come from (closing subsections)development-time · development-time protection
IntentControl the provenance and integrity of everything the model-building process consumes — the primary defence against supplier-side data poisoning and supply-chain model poisoning.
ParticularityUnlike conventional software supply chains, AI supply chains include non-code artifacts (data, models, fine-tuning adapters) and development-time execution paths that expose sensitive assets. This makes governance of notebooks, MLOps tools, and training environments particularly important. Supply chain controls therefore extend beyond runtime binaries and must consider how AI assets are created, transformed, and reused across the lifecycle.
LimitsRelies on the accuracy and completeness of records and attestations. False or incomplete provenance claims, compromised suppliers, or insufficient visibility into upstream processes can limit effectiveness. Complex multi-party supply chains may make full traceability difficult, and trust decisions often remain probabilistic rather than absolute.
StandardsISO 27002 controls 5.19–5.23, 8.30 (gap: covers the control fully, with said particularity, but lacking controls on data provenance); ISO/IEC 24368:2022 and ISO/IEC 24030:2024; ISO/IEC AWI 5181 Data provenance (gap: completes coverage together with the ISO 27002 controls — provided provenance concerns all sensitive data, not only personal data); ISO/IEC 42001 briefly mentions data provenance and refers to ISO 5181 in §B.7.5; ETSI GR SAI 002 V1.1.1 Securing AI — Data Supply Chain Security; OpenCRE: AI supply chain management → ENISA Table 5 "Ensure reliable sources are used", MITRE ATLAS AML.M0014 Verify AI Artifacts, AML.M0023 AI Bill of Materials, NIST AI 100-2 §3.2 AI Supply Chain Attacks and Mitigations. See also MITRE ATLAS — ML Supply Chain Compromise.
⚠ Common misconception

"We generate an SBOM, so our AI supply chain is managed." An SBOM covers code. The AI supply chain also carries datasets, pre-trained models and fine-tuning adapters — none of which appear in a conventional SBOM. That is exactly why MITRE ATLAS added AML.M0023 AI Bill of Materials and why ISO/IEC AWI 5181 (data provenance) exists as a separate work item: the ISO 27002 supply-chain controls alone leave the data-provenance gap open.

Why can a heavily poisoned dataset defeat #MODEL ENSEMBLE?
The ensemble detects poisoning through disagreement: poison concentrated in one random split makes that member deviate. If the poison volume is high, poisoned samples appear in every split, so all members learn the same backdoor and agree — no deviation, no detection. The guide states this directly: the more the dataset has been poisoned, the less effective the approach is.
Name two reasons supply-chain trust decisions for AI remain "probabilistic rather than absolute".
First, the control depends on records and attestations that can be false or incomplete — a compromised supplier can attest to clean provenance. Second, multi-party supply chains limit visibility into upstream processes, so full traceability may be unachievable. You are estimating the likelihood of compromise, not proving its absence.

Key Points

  • Broad model poisoning = manipulating development elements (engineering environment + supply chain) to alter model behaviour; impact is on integrity of model behaviour.
  • Three types: data poisoning (§3.1.1), development-environment model poisoning (§3.1.2), supply-chain model poisoning (§3.1.3).
  • The attack surface spans supplier, preparation-time, train-time, and runtime-collected data; the model itself can be poisoned at supplier, development, or runtime.
  • A security-separated training environment lets you apply trustworthy anti-poisoning controls against upstream poisoning.
  • #MODEL ENSEMBLE detects poisoning via disagreement between models trained on random train-set splits — but weakens as poison volume grows.
  • #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE must cover non-code artifacts (data, models, adapters); its trust decisions are probabilistic, and ISO 27002 alone leaves a data-provenance gap (ISO/IEC AWI 5181 closes it).
  • Layer general controls (limit unwanted-behaviour effects), dev-time protection (#DEV SECURITY, #SEGREGATE DATA, #CONF COMPUTE, #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE), data-poisoning controls, and #MODEL ENSEMBLE.
Module 11 · §3.1.1 · OWASP AI Exchange

Data Poisoning: Backdoors, Trojans and Sabotage

~14 minCovers guide pp. 225–228

Data poisoning — the classic "causative attack" — is the art of teaching a model something its owners never intended. This lesson dissects the six injection routes, the two fundamental attack categories (targeted backdoors versus sabotage), why backdoors systematically evade testing, and the first counter-control: drowning the poison in more clean data with #MORE TRAIN DATA.

THREAT
Data poisoning (causative attack)Attacker manipulates data the model uses to learn, to affect the algorithm's behaviour. Lifecycle: development-time. Maps to: LLM04:2025, ML02:2023, ATLAS AML.T0020, NIST AI 100-2 §2.3.2.

An attacker manipulates data that the model uses to learn, in order to affect the algorithm's behaviour. These are also called causative attacks in the literature. The manipulated data can be training data, but also in-context-learning data that is used to augment the input (e.g., a prompt) to a model with information to use — poisoning is not exclusive to the training loop.

Six ways in

There are multiple ways to poison data (map each onto the attack-surface diagram from lesson 1):

  1. Changing the data while in storage during development-time (e.g., by hacking the database).
  2. Changing the data while in transit to the storage (e.g., by hacking into a data transfer).
  3. Changing the data while at the supplier, before the data is obtained from the supplier.
  4. Changing the data while at the supplier, where a model is trained and then that model is obtained from the supplier (this shades into supply-chain model poisoning).
  5. Manipulating data entry in operation, feeding into training data — for example by creating fake accounts to enter positive reviews for products, making these products get recommended more often.
  6. Several of the above are very much possible if executed by an insider attacker.

One structural note: collaborative mitigations like #FEDERATED LEARNING can reduce data centralization, but they require additional poisoning controls based on the extension of the attack surface — every participant becomes a potential poisoning entry point.

Worked examples from the guide

▸ Worked example

Example 1 — the missile camera. An attacker breaks into a training-set database and adds images of houses labeled as 'fighter planes', to mislead the camera system of an autonomous missile — which is then manipulated into attacking houses. With a good test set this unwanted behaviour may be detected. But the attacker can instead perform targeted data poisoning: make the poisoned data represent input that normally doesn't occur, and therefore would not be in a test set — say, houses with white crosses on the door. The attacker then creates that abnormal input in practice. See MITRE ATLAS — Poison Training Data.

▸ Worked example

Example 2 — the malicious supplier. A malicious supplier poisons data that is later obtained by another party to train a model. See MITRE ATLAS — Publish Poisoned Datasets.

▸ Worked example

Example 3 — false facts on the internet. Unwanted information (e.g. false facts) in documents on the internet causes a Large Language Model (GenAI) to output unwanted results (OWASP for LLM 04). That unwanted information can be planted by an attacker — but also arrive by accident. The accidental case is a real GenAI risk, but technically it comes down to having false data in a training set, which falls outside the security scope. Planted unwanted information in GenAI training data falls under the sabotage category, as the intention is to make the model behave in unwanted ways for regular input.

Two categories: targeted poisoning vs. sabotage

The guide draws one distinction that drives everything else — including which controls work:

Targeted data poisoning (Trojan attack / Backdoor)Sabotage
EffectTriggers unwanted responses to specific inputs — e.g., a money transaction wrongfully marked NOT fraud because it has a specific amount for which the model has been manipulated to ignore it.Unwanted results for regular inputs — leading to e.g. business-continuity problems or safety issues.
DetectabilityHard to detect: occurs only for really specific inputs.Relatively easy to detect: it manifests on regular inputs your tests already cover.
Attacker's goalA hidden capability the attacker can invoke on demand while the model looks healthy.Degrade or break the model for everyone.
Test-set coverageDeliberately outside normal test distributions — that is the point.Inside normal test distributions.

Why are backdoors so hard to find? The guide gives three reasons, and they compound: there is no code to review in a model to look for backdoors; the model parameters cannot be reviewed, as they make no sense to the human eye; and testing is typically done using normal cases, with blind spots for backdoors. This is the intention of attackers — to bypass regular testing.

⚔ Attacker's view

A backdoor is an economics play. I pay once — injecting a modest number of trigger-labeled samples — and I gain a permanent, invisible capability: any input carrying my trigger gets my chosen output, while every metric you track (accuracy, F1, validation loss) stays green. The named research canon shows how cheap this is: BadNets (Gu et al.) demonstrated backdoors via simple pixel-pattern triggers, and clean-label backdoor attacks (Turner et al.) showed I don't even need to mislabel anything — the poisoned samples carry correct labels and survive human review. A summary of 15 backdoor papers at CVPR '23 charts how far this line of work has industrialized.

⚠ Common misconception

"Our test accuracy is 99.2%, so the training data wasn't poisoned." Test accuracy measures behaviour on the test distribution. A targeted backdoor is engineered to live outside that distribution and fire only on the trigger. High test accuracy is exactly what a competent backdoor attacker guarantees you will see.

Why does the guide classify accidental false facts in LLM training data as outside the security scope, but planted false facts as a sabotage attack?
Security concerns intentional threat actors. Accidental falsehoods are a data-quality problem — a real GenAI risk, but not an attack. When the same false information is planted by an attacker, there is intent to make the model misbehave on regular input, which is precisely the definition of sabotage data poisoning.
An attacker poisons a fraud model so transactions of exactly €4,271.53 are never flagged. Which category is this, and why won't testing catch it?
Targeted data poisoning (a backdoor/Trojan). The trigger — that exact amount — is an input that normally doesn't occur, so it will not be in a test set built from normal cases. The model's parameters can't be reviewed like code, so the backdoor is invisible until the attacker sends the trigger in production.

The control stack for data poisoning

The controls mirror the broad-poisoning stack, then add four data-specific controls taught in the coming lessons:

ControlStrategy against the poisonWhen appliedTaught in
#MORE TRAIN DATAOverrule poisoned data — outnumber it with clean dataPre-training onlythis lesson
#DATA QUALITY CONTROLDetect or prevent poisoned dataData preparation / training / augmentationlesson 3
#TRAIN DATA DISTORTIONCorrupt the poison — make triggers unrecognizablePre-training onlylesson 4
#POISON ROBUST MODELReduce recall of poisoned data in the trained modelPost-training (incl. external models)lesson 5
#TRAIN ADVERSARIAL + adversarial robust distillationImprove generalization, reducing memorization of poisoned samplesPre-traininglesson 5

Plus, as always: General controls (especially Limiting the effect of unwanted behaviour) and the development-time protection controls — #DEV SECURITY to protect the development environment and primarily the training data, #SEGREGATE DATA for extra-protected zones, #CONF COMPUTE to deny access to where sensitive data is processed, and #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE to control where data and models come from.

Four strategies against poisoned training data
Poisoned samplesin the train set
→
#MORE TRAIN DATAoutnumber the poison
→
#DATA QUALITY CONTROLfilter / alert on anomalies
→
#TRAIN DATA DISTORTIONbreak the trigger
→
#POISON ROBUST MODELprune / fine-tune memory away

#MORE TRAIN DATA — drown the poison

#MORE TRAIN DATAIncrease non-malicious data to overrule poisoned examplesdevelopment-time · AI engineer control — pre-training
IntentIncreasing the amount of non-malicious data makes training more robust against poisoned examples — provided those poisoned examples are small in number. The goal is to 'outnumber' the poisoned samples so the model 'forgets' them: the poison's statistical influence on the learned parameters shrinks as clean data grows.
ImplementOne way is data augmentation — creating artificial training-set samples that are small variations of existing samples. This control can only be applied during training, not to an already trained model. A variation for trained models does exist: fine-tune with additional non-malicious data — see #POISON ROBUST MODEL.
LimitsOnly works when poisoned examples are few. Two side-effect risks: catastrophic forgetting — benign data points (especially out-of-distribution ones) are also lost as new data floods in; and overfitting, another potential side effect of this control (augmented near-duplicates can make the model overfit to the existing sample neighbourhoods).
StandardsNot covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. OpenCRE: Benign train data increase → ENISA Table 5 "Enlarge the training dataset"; NIST AI 100-2 §2.3.1 Availability Poisoning. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/moretraindata/
◆ Senior insight

Treat #MORE TRAIN DATA as a dilution control, and reason about it quantitatively: it shifts the poison ratio, not the poison count. Against a low-volume backdoor it is a cheap, always-on layer. Against an attacker who controls a data source and can scale injection with you, dilution buys nothing — you need detection (#DATA QUALITY CONTROL) or trigger corruption (#TRAIN DATA DISTORTION). And monitor for its own side effects: catastrophic forgetting silently costs you exactly the rare, out-of-distribution cases that often matter most in safety contexts.

Your model is already trained and you cannot retrain from scratch. Is #MORE TRAIN DATA available to you?
Not in its base form — it applies only during training. But the guide names the variation: fine-tune the trained model with additional non-malicious data, which is one of the strategies under #POISON ROBUST MODEL (post-training).

Key Points

  • Data poisoning (causative attack) manipulates training data or in-context-learning data; injection routes: storage, transit, supplier data, supplier-trained model, runtime data entry, insiders.
  • Two categories: targeted poisoning (Trojan/backdoor — fires on specific triggers, evades testing) and sabotage (breaks regular inputs, easier to detect).
  • Backdoors evade detection because models have no reviewable code, parameters are opaque to humans, and tests cover normal cases — by attacker design.
  • Named canon: BadNets (Gu et al.), clean-label backdoors (Turner et al.), 15-backdoor-paper summary at CVPR '23; ATLAS AML.T0020, LLM04:2025, ML02:2023, BIML data:1, NIST AI 100-2 §2.3.2.
  • Planted false facts in GenAI training data = sabotage attack; accidental false facts = data-quality issue outside security scope.
  • Federated learning reduces data centralization but extends the poisoning attack surface, requiring extra controls.
  • #MORE TRAIN DATA dilutes small-volume poison via added clean data / augmentation; risks catastrophic forgetting and overfitting; pre-training only.
Module 11 · §3.1.1 · OWASP AI Exchange

Detect Before It Learns: #DATA QUALITY CONTROL

~13 minCovers guide pp. 228–232

If poisoned samples never reach the training run, the attack dies before it starts. #DATA QUALITY CONTROL is the detection layer of the anti-poisoning stack: integrity checks, statistical deviation analysis, and pattern recognition applied to training data. This lesson teaches the five named detection methods, the two-threshold handling scheme, why standard data quality checks are structurally insufficient against a malicious adversary, and where the ISO standards fall short.

Why standard data quality checks are not enough

Every mature data organization already validates formats and ranges. The guide's particularity for AI is blunt: standard data quality checks are not sufficient for AI systems, because data may be maliciously altered to compromise model behaviour. Malicious changes are crafted to pass the checks that catch mistakes — a poisoned image with a subtle trigger patch is a perfectly well-formed image in the allowed value range. Detecting adversarial manipulation requires different checks than standard checks on quality issues from the source or that occurred by mistake. Nevertheless, standard checks (e.g., is the data in the correct format) do help to some extent to detect malicious changes — keep them, just don't stop there.

#DATA QUALITY CONTROLDetect poisoned samples via integrity checks, statistical deviation, pattern recognitiondevelopment-time · AI engineer control — pre-training
IntentReduce the risk of data poisoning by identifying anomalous or manipulated training samples before they influence model behaviour — i.e., before training and before augmentation of input. Early detection prevents persistent or hard-to-reverse impacts on model integrity.
ImplementFour implementation layers: (1) standard data quality controls — validation of format and allowed value ranges; versioning and rollback mechanisms to pinpoint quality incidents and restore data; data provenance (see #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE); (2) integrity checks — safely store hash codes of data elements and conduct regular checks for manipulations (see #DEV SECURITY); (3) anomalous-sample detection — five methods below, applied both to newly added samples before training/augmentation and to existing samples already in the training dataset; (4) detection-mechanism protection — the detectors and their data need protection against tampering themselves; segregation of development environments and integrity protections prevent attackers from tampering with the detection logic.
LimitsAnomaly detection is a trade-off engine: false positives cause unnecessary investigation or removal of rare-but-valid samples, potentially harming model accuracy; false negatives occur when poisoned samples closely resemble normal data. Sophisticated attackers deliberately design poisoned samples to blend into the normal distribution, reducing the effectiveness of purely anomaly-based approaches. Cannot be applied retroactively to an already-trained model. Where anomaly thresholds consistently fail to distinguish poison from benign data, rely on alternative or complementary controls.
StandardsISO/IEC 25012:2008(E) data quality characteristics (accuracy, completeness, consistency, currentness, credibility); ISO/IEC 5259 series on data quality for analytics and ML — gap: covers this control minimally; does not mention approaches to detect malicious changes (including statistical deviation detection), though standard data-quality control helps detect malicious changes that violate quality rules; ISO/IEC 42001 §B.7.4 briefly covers data quality for AI — same gap as 5259; not further covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. OpenCRE: Data quality control → ETSI §5.2.2 Enhance data quality; ENISA Table 5 "Use methods to clean the training dataset from suspicious samples"; MITRE ATLAS AML.M0007 Sanitize Training Data. Further reading: GS1 Data Quality Framework; IBM on data quality in AI; SAND on data quality vs integrity; paper 'Detection of Adversarial Training Examples in Poisoning Attacks through Anomaly Detection'. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/dataqualitycontrol/

Applicability: when and for whom

This control applies during data preparation, training, and data augmentation phases. It cannot be applied retroactively to a model that has already been trained — the poison has already shaped the parameters. Implementing it during training ensures the model learns from clean, high-quality data, enhancing both performance and security; the guide stresses knowing and implementing this early in the training process for adequate training results and long-term data quality.

Two situational qualifiers matter:

  • Applicability depends on the assessed risk of data poisoning — both sabotage poisoning and trigger-based poisoning. If anomaly-detection thresholds prove ineffective at separating poison from benign data (false-positive risk), alternative or complementary controls may be more appropriate.
  • Implementation may be more suitable for the deployer of the AI system in environments where training-data pipelines or supply chains are externally managed — the party who receives the data is the one positioned to check it.

The five detection methods

Training data can be analyzed to identify samples that deviate from expected distributions or patterns — poisoned samples may differ statistically or structurally from the rest of the dataset. The guide names five method families (see also #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING for more detail):

  1. Statistical deviation and outlier detection — classic distributional analysis: samples far from the population's statistics get flagged.
  2. Spectral signatures — based on the covariance of learned feature representations: poisoned subpopulations leave a detectable trace in feature-space covariance spectra.
  3. Activation clustering — poisoned triggers produce distinct neuron activation patterns; clustering activations separates the trigger-carrying samples from clean ones.
  4. Reject on Negative Impact (RONI) — evaluates the impact of individual samples on model performance: a sample whose inclusion degrades performance is rejected.
  5. Gradient fingerprinting — compares the influence of samples during retraining to spot poisoned ones.

The appropriateness of a method depends on your poisoning threat model, and can be assessed through targeted testing — including poisoned-dataset benchmarks and resistance testing. Testing detection approaches on known poisoned datasets validates both effectiveness and implementation choices.

◆ Senior insight

Notice that three of the five methods (spectral signatures, activation clustering, gradient fingerprinting) require training or partially training a model to obtain feature representations, activations, or gradients. "Pre-training data quality control" in practice is often an iterative loop: train a probe model, inspect its internals for poisoning evidence, clean the data, retrain. Budget for that loop — it is compute you spend on security, and it is invisible in naive project plans.

Two thresholds: filter vs. alert

Detected anomalies are handled proportionally to their degree of deviation:

Anomaly handling by degree of deviation
Training samplescored by detector
→
Filter outremoved from training data to reduce poisoning risk
→
Alert & investigateidentify attack sources or pipeline weaknesses
→
Trainsample accepted

Samples that strongly deviate may be filtered out of the training data; samples that moderately deviate may trigger alerts for further investigation, allowing identification of attack sources or pipeline weaknesses. Thresholds are typically established through experimentation, balancing detection effectiveness against model correctness. Using multiple thresholds (filtering versus alerting) helps balance false positives, investigation effort, and model accuracy.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Keep standard checks (format, range validation) running — they still catch crude manipulation.
  • Version your datasets with rollback, so a detected poisoning incident can be pinpointed and reversed.
  • Store hashes of data elements safely (separate integrity domain) and check them regularly.
  • Run deviation detection on new samples before training/augmentation and periodically on the existing training set.
  • Calibrate two thresholds experimentally: hard-filter for strong deviations, alert-and-investigate for moderate ones.
  • Benchmark your detectors against known poisoned datasets before trusting them.
  • Protect the detection mechanism itself — segregate environments so attackers in the pipeline can't tamper with detection logic.
  • Guide's implementation considerations: be proactive (during training, before production issues); combine automated methods with human oversight for critical data; continuously monitor and audit controls as threats evolve; adhere to ISO/IEC 5259 and 42001 while recognizing their limitations.

Risk-reduction reality check

Filtering anomalous samples reduces the probability of successful poisoning — particularly when poisoned samples introduce unusual triggers or patterns. Effectiveness depends on the representativeness of the data, the quality of deviation metrics, and the chosen thresholds. And the adversary adapts: the clean-label backdoor line of work (Turner et al., lesson 2) exists precisely to make poison blend into the benign distribution. Data quality control is a probability-reducer in a layered stack, not a guarantee.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 228–232, permalink owaspai.org/go/dataqualitycontrol/. MITRE ATLAS mitigation AML.M0007 Sanitize Training Data is the closest ATLAS analogue. In NIST AI RMF terms this control operationalizes MEASURE (assessing data integrity) feeding MANAGE (filter/alert decisions). ISO/IEC 25012's five quality characteristics — accuracy, completeness, consistency, currentness, credibility — give you the vocabulary auditors expect, but remember the documented gap: neither ISO/IEC 5259 nor ISO/IEC 42001 B.7.4 addresses detection of malicious changes.

Why does the guide recommend two different thresholds instead of one?
A single threshold forces a brutal trade: set it tight and false positives strip rare-but-valid samples (hurting accuracy) while drowning analysts; set it loose and poison slips through. Two thresholds split the response: strong deviations are auto-filtered, moderate deviations become alerts for human investigation — balancing false positives, investigation effort, and model accuracy.
How does activation clustering detect poisoned samples, mechanically?
A backdoor trigger forces the model to route trigger-carrying inputs to the attacker's chosen output, which produces neuron activation patterns distinct from clean samples of the same class. Clustering the activations of training samples separates the trigger-carrying cluster from the clean cluster, exposing the poison.
Who should implement data quality control when training data comes from an externally managed pipeline?
The guide says implementation may be more suitable for the deployer of the AI system in such environments: the party receiving the data is positioned to check it, since it cannot verify what happened upstream and supply-chain attestations are only probabilistic.

Key Points

  • Standard data-quality checks catch mistakes, not adversaries — malicious samples are crafted to pass format/range validation; AI needs dedicated detection (the control's particularity).
  • Apply during data preparation, training, and augmentation; never retroactively to a trained model.
  • Four implementation layers: standard checks (validation, versioning/rollback, provenance), integrity checks (safely stored hashes), anomaly detection, and protection of the detection mechanism itself.
  • Five detection methods: statistical/outlier detection, spectral signatures, activation clustering, RONI, gradient fingerprinting — choose per threat model, validate on poisoned benchmarks.
  • Two-threshold handling: strong deviation → filter out; moderate deviation → alert and investigate.
  • Trade-offs: false positives remove rare valid samples; false negatives happen when poison blends in — which sophisticated attackers engineer deliberately.
  • Standards gap: ISO/IEC 5259 and 42001 B.7.4 cover data quality but not malicious-change detection; ISO/IEC 25012 defines the quality characteristics.
Module 11 · §3.1.1 · OWASP AI Exchange

Corrupt the Trigger: #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION

~12 minCovers guide pp. 232–235

If you can't reliably find the poison, you can still spoil it. #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION deliberately degrades untrusted training data — noise, smoothing, compression, even deleting whole fields — so that any backdoor trigger hiding in it loses the precision it needs to be learned. This lesson covers the mechanism, the trust-boundary caveat that decides whether the control works at all, and six named defence techniques from the research literature.

The mechanism: why distortion kills triggers

Recall how a backdoor works: the attacker inserts a trigger — a precise pattern — into training samples together with an unwanted output. Whenever input data later contains a similar trigger, the model recognizes it and outputs the unwanted value. Distortion attacks the recognition step: smooth, noise, or compress the training data and the trigger's precise pattern is degraded, so the model never learns a crisp trigger→output association. The idea is essentially the same as #INPUT DISTORTION, where distortion is applied at runtime input to defend against evasion attacks — here it is applied to training data against poisoning.

#TRAIN DATA DISTORTIONSmooth or add noise to untrusted training data to break poisoned triggersdevelopment-time · AI-engineer control — pre-training
IntentMake poisoned 'triggers' ineffective by distorting untrusted training data, so the model cannot recognize the trigger pattern anymore and therefore never binds it to the attacker's chosen output.
ImplementDistortion by e.g. adding noise (randomization) or smoothing; for images, JPEG compression can be considered. See also #EVASION ROBUST MODEL on adding noise against evasion attacks, and #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA — which can serve two purposes at once: privacy and data-poisoning mitigation. A special form is complete removal of certain input fields: technically data minimization (see #DATA MINIMIZE), but here the purpose is not confidentiality — it is reducing the ability to memorize poisoned samples. Applies only during training; not to an already pre-trained model.
LimitsEffectiveness must be tested by experimenting, and the results are never conclusive — an attacker may find more clever ways to poison than the methods you tested against. Best practice: keep the original training data, to experiment with the amount of distortion. Crucially, the control has no effect against attackers with direct access to the training data after distortion: if the distorted data sits in a file or database the attacker can reach, poisoned samples are simply injected after your control ran. With zero trust in the engineering environment's protection, train data distortion is only effective against poisoning that took place outside it (runtime-collected or supply-chain data). Mitigate by creating a trusted, separated training environment: apply distortion inside it, and it then also protects against poisoning elsewhere in the engineering environment.
StandardsNot covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. OpenCRE: Train data distortion → ETSI §5.2.2 Data sanitization; ENISA Table 5 "Use methods to clean the training dataset from suspicious samples"; NIST AI 100-2 §2.3.1 Availability Poisoning. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/traindatadistortion/
◆ Senior insight

The ordering constraint is the whole game. Distortion is a point-in-time transformation: it neutralizes poison present before it runs and is helpless against poison injected after. That is why the guide ties this control to the trusted training environment from lesson 1: run distortion at the boundary of the trusted zone and everything upstream — supplier data, preparation-time tampering, runtime-collected data — passes through the trigger-shredder before the model sees it.
