§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 261–262. Predictive AI tools reviewed: Adversarial Robustness Toolbox (ART), Armory, Foolbox, DeepSec, TextAttack — permalink owaspai.org/go/testingtoolspredictiveai/. Generative AI tools: PyRIT, Garak, Prompt Fuzzer, Guardrails, Promptfoo. These map onto MITRE ATLAS techniques — e.g. evasion tooling exercises AML.T0015-family attacks; PI tooling exercises AML.T0051.

Tool profile — the Adversarial Robustness Toolbox (ART)

ART is the first Predictive-AI tool the guide profiles in detail. Note two things in the tables: it provides detection but not prevention, and it exposes an API — meaning you can wire it into an automated test harness rather than driving it by hand.

Tool Name: The Adversarial Robustness Toolbox (ART)
Developer / SourceIBM Research / the Linux Foundation AI & Data Foundation (LF AI & Data)
Github Referencegithub.com/Trusted-AI/adversarial-robustness-toolbox
LanguagePython
LicensingOpen-source under the MIT License.
Provides MitigationPrevention: No ✗   Detection: Yes ✓
API AvailabilityYes ✓
FactorDetails
PopularityGitHub Stars: ~4.9K stars (as of 2024); GitHub Forks: ~1.2K forks; Number of Issues: ~131 open issues, 761 closed issues

Per mind map 1, ART addresses four threats: development-time model poisoning, evasion, model theft through use, and model inference — the same coverage as Armory, making the two the broadest Predictive-AI tools in the set.

⚠ Common misconception

"ART will protect my model." Read the table: Provides Mitigation → Prevention: No. ART is a detection and evaluation toolkit — it helps you find and measure adversarial weaknesses, not block attacks at runtime. Treat its output as test evidence that feeds your remediation plan (steps 6–8 of the general approach), not as a deployed defence.

You are securing a fraud-detection model (Predictive AI) and worry about evasion. Which tools does the guide's mapping point you to?
Mind map 2 lists, for Evasion: ART, Armory, Foolbox, DeepSec, TextAttack (and, on the generative side, PyRIT and Garak). For a Predictive model you would look at the first five — Foolbox and DeepSec specialise in evasion, while ART and Armory also cover poisoning, model theft, and inference.
🛡 Defender's checklist — choosing tools
  • Start from your threat model, not from a tool: use mind map 2 to go threat → candidate tools.
  • Confirm the paradigm fit (Predictive vs Generative) before adopting a tool.
  • Check whether a tool provides prevention or only detection — ART is detection-only.
  • Prefer tools with an API so tests can be automated into your pipeline (Step 3 orchestration).
  • Remember the maps are non-exhaustive and unranked; supplement with the Periodic Table of AI Security for coverage gaps.
  • Add benchmarks (promptbench, AdvBench, OpenAI Evals) for positive-testing and quality baselines.

Key Points

  • Positive testing proves security mechanisms do not degrade functionality/UX; it must exercise detectors to keep false positives acceptable.
  • Benchmarks named: Microsoft's promptbench, Overview of benchmarks, AdvBench, OpenAI Evals.
  • Predictive-AI tools: ART, Armory, Foolbox, DeepSec, TextAttack. Generative-AI tools: PyRIT, Garak, Prompt Fuzzer, Guardrails, Promptfoo.
  • Two mind maps give tool→threat and threat→tool mappings; they are non-exhaustive and unranked.
  • ART (IBM Research / LF AI & Data, Python, MIT licence) provides detection but not prevention, and exposes an API.
  • ART covers dev-time model poisoning, evasion, model theft through use, and model inference — matching Armory's breadth.
Module 14 · §5 AI security testing — tool reviews · OWASP AI Exchange

How the Exchange Reviews Red-Teaming Tools

~12 minCovers guide pp. 263, 283, 303–304 (methodology)

Before you pick an AI red-teaming tool, you need a way to compare them that isn't marketing. The OWASP AI Exchange reviews ten open-source tools — five for predictive AI, five for generative AI — against one fixed template: who builds it, what it can attack, which frameworks it plugs into, and exactly which AI Exchange threats it exercises. This lesson teaches you that template so the nine tool profiles in the following lessons read like structured intelligence, not a link dump.

What this catalog is

Red teaming an AI system means simulating adversarial attacks against it — feeding it adversarial inputs, poisoned data, hostile prompts — to find weaknesses before a real attacker does. Doing that by hand is slow and unrepeatable, so the security community has built open-source tooling that automates attack generation and susceptibility measurement. The guide reviews this tooling in two subsections:

  • Predictive AI tools (owaspai.org/go/testingtoolspredictiveai/): "This subsection covers the following tools for security testing Predictive AI: Adversarial Robustness Toolbox (ART), Armory, Foolbox, DeepSec, and TextAttack." Predictive AI here means non-generative machine learning — classification, object detection, speech recognition — as opposed to models that generate content.
  • Generative AI tools (owaspai.org/go/testingtoolsgenai/): "This subsection covers the following tools for security testing Generative AI: PyRIT, Garak, Prompt Fuzzer, Guardrail, and Promptfoo."

The guide's own transition sentence sets the order we follow too: "The below section will cover the tools for predictive AI, followed by the section for generative AI." The generative-AI subsection adds that a list of GenAI test tools can also be found at the OWASP GenAI security project solutions page (click the category "Test & Evaluate"), and that the same project published a GenAI Red Teaming guide.

The ten reviewed tools, by target class
Predictive AI red teaming
ARTIBM / LF AI & Data
ArmoryMITRE
Foolboxbethgelab (academia)
DeepSecNUS researchers
TextAttackUMD + Google Research
Generative AI red teaming
PyRITMicrosoft
GarakNVIDIA
Prompt FuzzerPrompt Security
GuardrailGuardrails AI
PromptfooPromptfoo community

The fixed assessment template

Every tool is reviewed with the same eight-part structure. Learn it once and every profile becomes instantly comparable:

  1. Tool identity table — Developer/Source, GitHub Reference, Language, Licensing, Provides Mitigation, API Availability.
  2. Factor/Details table — Popularity (stars, forks, issues, trend), Community Support (issue responsiveness, documentation, forums, contributors), Scalability (framework support, large-scale deployment), Integration (compatibility).
  3. Tool Rating table — the four factors condensed to High/Medium/Low. (For Foolbox the document prints this heading as "Total Rating"; every other tool says "Tool Rating".)
  4. Data Modality table — Text, Image, Audio, Video, Tabular data.
  5. Machine Learning Tasks table — Classification, Object Detection, Speech Recognition.
  6. Framework Applicability table — which frameworks/platforms the tool works against, each categorized as DL (deep-learning framework), ML (classical machine-learning library), GenAI (usable for generative AI), Machine Learning Deployment, or Lightweight Inference.
  7. OWASP AI Exchange Threat Coverage table — a standard 15-threat checklist (below).
  8. Notes — one line per covered threat, each with an owaspai.org permalink.

Symbol convention throughout: ✅ means supported/covered/yes, ❌ means no, and an empty cell means not supported / not covered.

How to read one tool profile — the review pipeline
Identitywho, license, API
→
Factorspopularity · support · scale · integration
→
RatingHigh / Medium / Low
→
Capabilitymodality · tasks · frameworks
→
Threat coverage15-topic checklist + notes

Prevention vs. detection — the single most important field

The Provides Mitigation row asks: can the tool actively prevent attacks at runtime, or does it only detect — i.e. find and expose vulnerabilities by testing? Eight of the ten tools are detection-only (Prevention: No ❌, Detection: Yes ✅): they are red-teaming/assessment instruments, not runtime defenses. Only Guardrail and Promptfoo state Prevention: Yes ✅ alongside Detection: Yes ✅.

⚠ Common misconception

Running ART or Garak in your pipeline does not make your model safer by itself — these tools tell you where you are weak; they do not block anything. If you file a red-team report and change nothing, your risk is unchanged. Detection tools create work items; prevention capability (guardrails, filters, adversarial training) closes them.

The 15-threat coverage checklist

Each tool is scored against the same fifteen OWASP AI Exchange threat topics. This is the review's most valuable feature: it converts "cool attack library" into "which of my documented threats does this actually exercise?"

#OWASP AI Exchange threat topicLifecycle flavor
1Development time model poisoningdevelopment
2Runtime model poisoningruntime
3Model theft by useruntime / through use
4Training data poisoningdevelopment
5Training data leakdevelopment
6Runtime model theftruntime
7Evasion (tests model performance against adversarial inputs)runtime / through use
8Model inversion / Membership inferenceruntime / through use
9Denial of model serviceruntime
10Direct prompt injectionruntime (GenAI)
11Data disclosureruntime
12Model input leakruntime
13Indirect prompt injectionruntime (GenAI)
14Development time model theftdevelopment
15Output contains injectionruntime

Sobering headline finding you should internalize now: across all ten tools, only six of these fifteen topics get any coverage at all — development-time model poisoning, model theft by use, evasion, model inversion/membership inference, direct prompt injection, and indirect prompt injection. Nine threats (runtime model poisoning, training data poisoning, training data leak, runtime model theft, denial of model service, data disclosure, model input leak, development-time model theft, output contains injection) have no automated coverage in this open-source catalog. Your test plan must cover them by other means: manual pentesting, code review, infrastructure testing, process controls.

THREAT
EvasionAdversarial inputs at runtime cause misclassification or unwanted model behaviour — the one threat every tool in this catalog covers. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: MITRE ATLAS AML.T0015 (Evade ML Model), AML.T0043 (Craft Adversarial Data).

The guide's recurring one-line definition, used in every tool's notes, is: "Evasion: Tests model performance against adversarial inputs" (owaspai.org/go/evasion/). Mechanically, an evasion attack perturbs an input — pixels, characters, audio samples — just enough to flip the model's decision while remaining plausible to a human. Evasion is where adversarial-ML tooling began, which is why coverage is universal: it is the cheapest attack to automate (query the model, compute or estimate gradients, iterate).

◆ Senior insight

Read the coverage checklist as a statement about attacker economics, not tool quality. Evasion and prompt injection are covered everywhere because they need only query access to the model — cheap for attackers and cheap for tool authors to automate. Poisoning, theft-by-supply-chain, and leak threats require control of pipelines, storage, or infrastructure — expensive to simulate generically, so no open-source tool does. The gap in your tooling mirrors the part of the attack surface that is organization-specific.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 263 and 283 (subsection scope statements and permalinks /go/testingtoolspredictiveai/, /go/testingtoolsgenai/). The 15-topic checklist is the AI Exchange threat taxonomy. Added canon: evasion maps to MITRE ATLAS AML.T0015/AML.T0043; direct and indirect prompt injection map to OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM01 and ATLAS AML.T0051; model inversion/membership inference map to ATLAS AML.T0024 (Exfiltration via ML Inference API). Systematic red-team measurement supports the NIST AI RMF Measure function. The extracts record no ISO/IEC, EU AI Act, or OpenCRE citations in this page range.

All ten tools are "detection" tools except two. Which two provide prevention, and what does that distinction mean?
Guardrail (Guardrails AI) and Promptfoo state Prevention: Yes ✅ as well as Detection: Yes ✅. Detection means the tool finds and reports vulnerabilities through testing; prevention means it can actively block or mitigate attacks (e.g. guardrails wrapped around an LLM at runtime). The other eight tools only assess susceptibility — they change nothing about your runtime posture.
Why is evasion the only threat covered by all ten tools?
Evasion requires nothing more than query access to the model: generate a perturbed input, observe the output, iterate. That makes it the cheapest attack class to automate and package as a library. Threats that require control over training pipelines, storage, or serving infrastructure are organization-specific and much harder to ship as a generic open-source tool.

Key Points

  • The guide reviews five predictive-AI red-teaming tools (ART, Armory, Foolbox, DeepSec, TextAttack) and five generative-AI tools (PyRIT, Garak, Prompt Fuzzer, Guardrail, Promptfoo).
  • Every tool uses one fixed template: identity → factors → High/Medium/Low rating → data modality → ML tasks → framework applicability → 15-threat coverage → notes with permalinks.
  • ✅ = supported/covered, ❌ = no, empty cell = not supported/not covered.
  • Eight of ten tools are detection-only; only Guardrail and Promptfoo also provide prevention.
  • Only 6 of the 15 AI Exchange threat topics are covered by any tool; nine threats need non-tool testing approaches.
  • Evasion — "tests model performance against adversarial inputs" — is the universal capability, because it only needs query access.
  • More GenAI test tools: OWASP GenAI security project solutions page ("Test & Evaluate" category) and its GenAI Red Teaming guide.
Module 14 · §5 AI security testing — predictive AI tools · OWASP AI Exchange

Predictive-AI Tools I: ART and Armory

~15 minCovers guide pp. 263–271

The Adversarial Robustness Toolbox is the reference tool of predictive-AI red teaming — the only one in the catalog rated High on all four criteria and the widest in framework and threat coverage. Armory, from MITRE, is its defense-sector cousin: narrower, security-focused, and designed to compose with ART. This lesson reproduces both reviews in full.

The Adversarial Robustness Toolbox (ART)

ART, from IBM Research and now hosted by the Linux Foundation AI & Data Foundation (LF AI & Data), is a Python library implementing adversarial attacks and robustness evaluations across essentially every mainstream ML framework and data modality.

Tool Name: The Adversarial Robustness Toolbox (ART)
Developer / SourceIBM Research / the Linux Foundation AI & Data Foundation (LF AI & Data)
Github Referencegithub.com/Trusted-AI/adversarial-robustness-toolbox
LanguagePython
LicensingOpen-source under the MIT License.
Provides MitigationPrevention: No ❌   Detection: Yes ✅
API AvailabilityYes ✅
FactorDetails
Popularity
  • GitHub Stars: ~4.9K stars (as of 2024)
  • GitHub Forks: ~1.2K forks
  • Number of Issues: ~131 open issues, 761 closed issues
  • Trend: Steady growth, with consistent updates and industry adoption for adversarial robustness.
Community Support
  • Active Issues: Responsive team, typically addressing issues within a week.
  • Documentation: Detailed and regularly updated, with comprehensive guides and API documentation on IBM's website.
  • Discussion Forums: Primarily discussed in academic settings, with some presence on Stack Overflow and GitHub.
  • Contributors: Over 100 contributors, including IBM researchers and external collaborators.
Scalability
  • Framework Support: Scales across TensorFlow, Keras, and PyTorch with out-of-the-box support.
  • Large-Scale Deployment: Proven to handle large, enterprise-level deployments in industries like healthcare, finance, and defense.
Integration
  • Compatibility: Works with TensorFlow, PyTorch, Keras, MXNet, and Scikit-learn.

ART — Tool Rating

CriteriaHighMediumLow
Popularity✅
Community Support✅
Scalability✅
Ease of Integration✅

ART — Data Modality

Data ModalitySupported
Text✅
Image✅
Audio✅
Video✅
Tabular data✅

ART — Machine Learning Tasks

Task TypeData ModalitySupported
ClassificationAll (See Data modality section)✅
Object DetectionComputer Vision✅
Speech RecognitionAudio✅

ART — Framework Applicability

Framework / ToolCategorySupported
TensorflowDL, GenAI✅
KerasDL, GenAI✅
PyTorchDL, GenAI✅
MxNetDL✅
Scikit-learnML✅
XGBoostML✅
LightGBMML✅
CatBoostML✅
GPyML✅

Note what that framework table means: ART is the only reviewed tool that reaches beyond deep learning into classical ML — Scikit-learn, the gradient-boosting family (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost), even Gaussian processes (GPy). If your risk sits in a credit-scoring gradient-boosted model rather than a neural network, ART is effectively the only tool in this catalog that can test it.

ART — OWASP AI Exchange Threat Coverage

TopicCoverage
Development time model poisoning✅
Runtime model poisoning
Model theft by use✅
Training data poisoning
Training data leak
Runtime model theft
Evasion (Tests model performance against adversarial inputs)✅
Model inversion / Membership inference✅
Denial of model service
Direct prompt injection
Data disclosure
Model input leak
Indirect prompt injection
Development time model theft
Output contains injection

Four covered threats — the broadest coverage of any tool in the catalog. The guide's notes explain each, with permalinks:

  • Development-time Model poisoning: Simulates attacks during development to evaluate vulnerabilities. owaspai.org/go/modelpoison/
  • Evasion: Tests model performance against adversarial inputs. owaspai.org/go/evasion/
  • Model exfiltration: Evaluates risks of model exploitation during usage. owaspai.org/go/modeltheftuse
  • Model inference: Assesses exposure to membership and inversion attacks. owaspai.org/go/modelinversionandmembership/
THREAT
Development-time model poisoningThe model is manipulated during development so it misbehaves later; tools simulate these attacks to evaluate vulnerability. Lifecycle: development. Maps to: ATLAS AML.T0018 (Backdoor ML Model), AML.T0020 (Poison Training Data).
THREAT
Model theft by use (model exfiltration)An attacker reconstructs a functional copy of your model by systematically querying it. Lifecycle: runtime/through use. Maps to: ATLAS AML.T0024.002 (Extract ML Model); OWASP LLM Top 10 (2023) LLM10 Model Theft.
THREAT
Model inversion / Membership inferenceQueries against the model expose properties of the training data — reconstructing inputs (inversion) or revealing whether a record was in the training set (membership). Lifecycle: runtime/through use. Maps to: ATLAS AML.T0024.000/.001.
⚔ Attacker's view

ART's four covered threats trace one attacker journey against a deployed classifier: first probe with adversarial examples (evasion) — nearly free, just API calls; then harvest input/output pairs to clone the model (theft by use), converting per-query cost into a permanent offline copy; then run inversion/membership attacks against your own clone at zero marginal cost to mine the victim's training data. Poisoning is the same attacker moving left in the lifecycle when they can reach the training pipeline. Testing all four with one tool is why ART is the default choice for predictive AI.

Armory

Armory comes from the MITRE Corporation and grew out of defense-sector adversarial-ML evaluation work (its community discussions reference DARPA projects). Where ART is a library of attacks, Armory is closer to an evaluation harness — and it explicitly integrates IBM ART for enhanced robustness testing.

Tool Name: Armory
Developer / SourceMITRE Corporation
Github Referencegithub.com/twosixlabs/armory-library and github.com/twosixlabs/armory (the PDF prints the two links run together)
LanguagePython
LicensingOpen-source under the MIT License.
Provides MitigationPrevention: No ❌   Detection: Yes ✅
API AvailabilityYes ✅
FactorDetails
Popularity
  • GitHub Stars: ~176 stars (as of 2024)
  • GitHub Forks: ~67 forks
  • Number of Issues: ~59 open issues, 733 closed, 26 contributors
  • Trend: Growing, particularly within defense and cybersecurity sectors.
Community Support
  • Active Issues: Fast response to issues (typically resolved within days to a week).
  • Documentation: Comprehensive, but more security-focused, with advanced tutorials on adversarial attacks and defenses.
  • Discussion Forums: Active GitHub discussions, some presence on security-specific forums (e.g., in relation to DARPA projects).
  • Contributors: Over 40 contributors, mostly security experts and researchers.
Scalability
  • Framework Support: Supports TensorFlow and Keras natively, with some integration options for PyTorch.
  • Large-Scale Deployment: Mostly used in security-related deployments; scalability for non-security tasks is less documented.
Integration
  • Compatibility: Works well with TensorFlow and Keras; IBM ART integration for enhanced robustness
  • API Availability: Limited compared to IBM ART, but sufficient for adversarial ML use cases.

Armory's notes:

  • Development-time Model poisoning: Simulates attacks during development to evaluate vulnerabilities. owaspai.org/go/modelpoison/
  • Evasion: Tests model performance against adversarial inputs. owaspai.org/go/evasion/
  • Prompt Injection: Evaluates the robustness of generative AI models by exploiting weaknesses in prompt design, leading to undesired outputs or bypassing model safeguards. owaspai.org/go/promptinjection/
▸ Worked example

Armory is the one predictive-AI tool in the catalog with a checkmark for Direct prompt injection — a generative-AI threat. That reflects its evaluation-harness design: it orchestrates scenarios rather than shipping only gradient attacks, so a prompt-robustness scenario fits its model. Contrast the numbers: Armory has ~176 stars against ART's ~4.9K, yet its 733 closed issues and days-to-a-week response times show a small but disciplined maintainer team — popularity and support quality are different axes, which is exactly why the rubric scores them separately.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Default to ART for predictive models: widest threats (4), widest frameworks (all 9), all five modalities, High on every rating criterion.
  • Add Armory when you need scenario-style evaluation or work in defense/security contexts — and use its built-in ART integration rather than wiring the two yourself.
  • Neither tool prevents anything: schedule remediation (adversarial training, rate limiting, output controls) for every finding.
  • Check framework fit first: Armory covers only TensorFlow and PyTorch natively (Keras via its own tooling per the factor notes, but not checked in the applicability table).
Your fraud model is XGBoost. Which of ART/Armory can test it, and why?
Only ART. Its framework applicability table checks all nine listed frameworks including XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, Scikit-learn and GPy. Armory's table checks only TensorFlow and PyTorch — it targets deep-learning models, not classical gradient-boosting libraries.
ART covers "model theft by use" and "model inversion / membership inference". How do these two threats differ?
Theft by use reconstructs the model's function — the attacker clones its behaviour by querying it. Inversion/membership inference extracts information about the training data — reconstructing representative inputs or determining whether a specific record was used in training. One steals your IP; the other breaches the privacy of the people in your dataset.

Key Points

  • ART (IBM Research / LF AI & Data, MIT, Python): detection-only, API yes; rated High/High/High/High — the only all-High tool in the catalog.
  • ART supports all 5 modalities, all 3 ML tasks, and all 9 frameworks — uniquely including classical ML (Scikit-learn, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, GPy).
  • ART covers 4 threats: development-time model poisoning, model theft by use, evasion, model inversion/membership inference — the widest coverage reviewed.
  • Armory (MITRE, MIT, Python): detection-only; rated Low popularity (~176 stars), Medium community support and scalability, High ease of integration.
  • Armory supports all 5 modalities and all 3 tasks but only TensorFlow and PyTorch in the applicability table; it integrates IBM ART for enhanced robustness.
  • Armory covers development-time model poisoning, evasion, and — unusually for a predictive tool — direct prompt injection.
  • Armory's trend is growth in defense and cybersecurity sectors, with DARPA-related community discussion.
Module 14 · §5 AI security testing — predictive AI tools · OWASP AI Exchange

Predictive-AI Tools II: Foolbox, DeepSec, TextAttack

~15 minCovers guide pp. 271–283

The remaining three predictive-AI tools are specialists: Foolbox is the academic evasion workhorse, DeepSec a research benchmark platform rated Low across the board, and TextAttack the NLP-only framework that owns the text modality. Knowing where each is strong — and how narrow each is — keeps you from mistaking a research instrument for an enterprise scanner.

Foolbox

Foolbox is maintained by its authors/developers (the bethgelab group), and is a staple of academic adversarial-example research. Its review carries a document quirk worth knowing: its rating table is headed "Total Rating" where every other tool says "Tool Rating".

Tool Name: Foolbox
Developer / SourceAuthors/Developers of Foolbox
Github Referencegithub.com/bethgelab/foolbox

API AvailabilityYes ✅
FactorDetails
Popularity
  • GitHub Stars: ~2,800 stars (as of 2024)
  • GitHub Forks: ~428 forks
  • Number of Issues: ~21 open issues, 350 closed issues
  • Trend: Steady, with consistent updates from the academic community.
Community Support
  • Active Issues: Typically resolved within a few weeks.
  • Documentation: Moderate documentation with basic tutorials; more research-focused.
  • Discussion Forums: Primarily discussed in academic settings, with limited industry forum activity.
  • Contributors: Over 30 contributors, largely from academia.
Scalability
  • Framework Support: Compatible with TensorFlow, PyTorch, and JAX (the document duplicates the words "Framework Support:" in this cell).
  • Large-Scale Deployment: Limited scalability for large-scale industry deployments, more focused on research and experimentation.
Integration
  • Compatibility: Strong integration with TensorFlow, PyTorch, and JAX.

DeepSec

DeepSec was developed by a team of academic researchers in collaboration with the National University of Singapore. It stands out twice: it carries the only non-MIT license among the predictive tools (Apache License 2.0), and it is the only tool rated Low on all four criteria — a pure research/benchmarking platform. (In the printed guide, DeepSec's review straddles a page break at p.277: its rating and framework tables are split across pages, stitched back together here.)

Tool Name: DeepSec
Developer / SourceDeveloped by a team of academic researchers in collaboration with the National University of Singapore.
Github Referencegithub.com/ryderling/DEEPSEC
LanguagePython
LicensingOpen-source under the Apache License 2.0 (the only non-MIT license among the predictive tools).
Provides MitigationPrevention: No ❌   Detection: Yes ✅
API AvailabilityYes ✅

Popularity

  • GitHub Forks: ~70
  • Number of Issues: ~15 open issues
  • Trend: Stable with a focus on deep learning security
Community Support
  • Active Issues: Currently has ongoing issues and updates, suggesting active maintenance.
  • Documentation: Available through GitHub, covering setup, use, and contributions.
  • Discussion Forums: GitHub Discussions section and community channels support developer interactions.
  • Contributors: A small but dedicated contributor base.
Scalability
  • Framework Support: Primarily supports PyTorch and additional libraries like TorchVision.
  • Large-Scale Deployment: Suitable for research and testing environments but may need adjustments for production-grade scaling
Integration
  • Compatibility: Compatible with machine learning libraries in Python.

◆ Senior insight

Foolbox and DeepSec cover the identical single threat (evasion) — so why do their ratings diverge (Medium/Medium/Low/Medium vs. all-Low)? Because the rubric measures operational viability, not capability breadth: Foolbox has ~2,800 stars, 30+ contributors and steady academic updates; DeepSec has 209 stars and a small contributor base. When two tools tie on coverage, the rating tells you which one will still be maintained when you file a bug — that is a security property in itself, since an unmaintained testing tool silently stops matching current attacks.

TextAttack

TextAttack, developed by researchers at the University of Maryland and Google Research, is the specialist for natural-language models: adversarial attacks, adversarial training, and data augmentation for NLP. It is text-only by design and deeply integrated with the Hugging Face ecosystem.

Tool Name: TextAttack
Developer / SourceDeveloped by researchers at the University of Maryland and Google Research.
Github Referencegithub.com/QData/TextAttack
LanguagePython
LicensingOpen-source under the MIT License.
Provides MitigationPrevention: No ❌   Detection: Yes ✅
API AvailabilityYes ✅
FactorDetails
Popularity
  • GitHub Stars: ~3.7K (as of 2024)
  • GitHub Forks: ~455
  • Number of Issues: ~130 open issues
  • Trend: Popular with ongoing updates and regular contributions
Community Support
  • Active Issues: Issues are actively managed with frequent bug fixes and improvements.
  • Documentation: Detailed documentation is available, covering everything from attack configuration to custom dataset integration
  • Discussion Forums: GitHub Discussions are active, with support for technical queries and community interaction.
  • Contributors: Over 20 contributors, reflecting diverse input and enhancements.
Scalability
  • Framework Support: Supports NLP models in PyTorch and integrates well with Hugging Face's Transformers and Datasets libraries, making it compatible with a broad range of NLP tasks.
  • Large-Scale Deployment: Primarily designed for research and experimentation; deployment at scale would likely require customization.
Integration
  • Compatibility: Model-agnostic, allowing use with various NLP model architectures as long as they meet the interface requirements.

TextAttack's notes:

  • Development-time Model poisoning: Simulates attacks during development to evaluate vulnerabilities. owaspai.org/go/modelpoison/
  • Evasion: Tests model performance against adversarial inputs. owaspai.org/go/evasion/
⚔ Attacker's view

Text evasion is cheaper than image evasion: swapping synonyms, inserting typos, or paraphrasing costs the attacker nothing detectable, and the perturbation "budget" is semantic rather than pixel-norm-bounded. TextAttack packages exactly these transformations, which is why a spam filter or toxicity classifier that has never been run through it is almost certainly bypassable in minutes.

⚠ Common misconception

"Model-agnostic" in TextAttack's review does not mean it tests any model type — the modality table shows text only, and the applicability table checks only TensorFlow and PyTorch. It means: within NLP, any architecture works as long as it meets the interface requirements. Scope claims in tool marketing always need the modality and framework tables as a cross-check.

Predictive-tool comparison at a glance

Synthesizing the five predictive reviews (this summary table is derived from the per-tool tables; the guide prints the data per tool, not as one table):

AspectARTArmoryFoolboxDeepSecTextAttack
DeveloperIBM Research / LF AI & DataMITRE CorporationFoolbox authors (bethgelab)Academic researchers / Nat. Univ. of SingaporeUniv. of Maryland + Google Research
LicenseMITMITMITApache 2.0MIT
Prevention / DetectionNo / YesNo / YesNo / YesNo / YesNo / Yes
APIYesYesYesYesYes
Rating (Pop/Comm/Scal/Integ)High/High/High/HighLow/Medium/Medium/HighMedium/Medium/Low/MediumLow/Low/Low/LowHigh/High/Medium/High
ModalitiesAll 5All 5Text, ImageText, ImageText only
Threats coveredDev-time poisoning, Model theft by use, Evasion, Inversion/MembershipDev-time poisoning, Evasion, Direct prompt injectionEvasionEvasionDev-time poisoning, Evasion
Which predictive tool uses Apache 2.0 instead of MIT, and why might a legal team care?
DeepSec (academic researchers with the National University of Singapore) is Apache 2.0; ART, Armory, Foolbox and TextAttack are MIT. Apache 2.0 adds an explicit patent grant and requires stating changes — mostly friendlier for enterprises, but license review processes treat the two differently, so it matters for redistribution and internal forks.
Foolbox rates Medium popularity with ~2,800 stars while TextAttack rates High with ~3.7K. What rubric line explains this?
The rating rubric (lesson 6) defines Popularity as High above 3,000 stars, Medium at 1,000–3,000, Low below 1,000. Foolbox's ~2,800 sits in the Medium band; TextAttack's ~3,700 clears the High threshold.

Key Points

  • Foolbox (bethgelab, MIT): evasion-only; Text+Image; TensorFlow/Keras/PyTorch (+JAX per factors); rated Medium/Medium/Low/Medium — its rating table is printed as "Total Rating".
  • DeepSec (NUS-affiliated researchers, Apache 2.0): evasion-only, classification-only, Text+Image, TensorFlow+PyTorch (with TorchVision); the only all-Low-rated tool — a research benchmark, not an enterprise scanner.
  • TextAttack (UMD + Google Research, MIT): text-only NLP specialist covering evasion and development-time model poisoning; PyTorch + Hugging Face Transformers/Datasets; rated High/High/Medium/High.
  • All three are detection-only with APIs; none prevents attacks.
  • Equal threat coverage does not mean equal value: Foolbox outranks DeepSec purely on maintenance and community viability.
  • "Model-agnostic" claims must always be checked against the modality and framework tables.
Module 14 · §5 AI security testing — generative AI tools · OWASP AI Exchange

GenAI Red Teaming I: PyRIT and Garak

~14 minCovers guide pp. 283–291

Generative AI changes what "red teaming" means: the attack surface is the prompt, the target is behaviour rather than a class label, and the tools orchestrate conversations instead of computing gradients. PyRIT (Microsoft) and Garak (NVIDIA) are the two flagship open-source scanners here — both built to hammer LLMs with evasion and prompt-injection probes across nearly any provider API.

The generative-AI tools subsection

Permalink: owaspai.org/go/testingtoolsgenai/. Verbatim scope: "This subsection covers the following tools for security testing Generative AI: PyRIT, Garak, Prompt Fuzzer, Guardrail, and Promptfoo." The guide adds that more GenAI test tools are listed on the OWASP GenAI security project solutions page (category "Test & Evaluate"), and that this project also published a GenAI Red Teaming guide.

For GenAI tools, the Framework Applicability table swaps in provider platforms: Tensorflow, PyTorch, Azure OpenAI, Huggingface, Azure managed endpoints (Machine Learning Deployment), Cohere, Replicate Text Models, OpenAI API, GGUF (Llama.cpp) (GenAI, Lightweight Inference), and — for some tools — OctoAI. GGUF/Llama.cpp represents locally hosted, lightweight-inference models: a tool that supports it can test models you run yourself, not just cloud APIs.

THREAT
Direct prompt injectionThe attacker, as the user, crafts input that exploits weaknesses in prompt design to produce undesired outputs or bypass model safeguards. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM01, MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051.000.

The guide's standing definition, repeated in the notes of every GenAI tool: "Prompt Injection: Evaluates the robustness of generative AI models by exploiting weaknesses in prompt design, leading to undesired outputs or bypassing model safeguards." (owaspai.org/go/promptinjection/)

PyRIT — Python Risk Identification Toolkit

Tool Name: PyRIT
Developer / SourceMicrosoft
Github Referencegithub.com/Azure/PyRIT

Provides MitigationPrevention: No ❌   Detection: Yes ✅

  • GitHub Forks: ~384 forks
  • Number of Issues: ~63 open issues, 79 closed issues
  • Trend: Steady growth, with consistent updates and industry adoption for adversarial robustness.
Community Support
  • Active Issues: Issues are being addressed within a week.
  • Documentation: Detailed and regularly updated, with comprehensive guides and API documentation.
  • Discussion Forums: Active GitHub issues
  • Contributors: Over 125 contributors.
Scalability
  • Framework Support: Scales across TensorFlow, PyTorch and supports models on local like ONNX
  • Large-Scale Deployment: Can be extended to Azure pipeline.
Integration

PyRIT's notes:

  • Evasion: Tests model performance against adversarial inputs. owaspai.org/go/evasion/
  • Prompt Injection: Evaluates the robustness of generative AI models by exploiting weaknesses in prompt design, leading to undesired outputs or bypassing model safeguards. owaspai.org/go/promptinjection/
▸ Worked example

PyRIT is what Microsoft's own AI Red Team built to scale its internal engagements — the library-based API means you script attack "orchestrators" that send adversarial prompts, score the responses, and iterate automatically. With 125+ contributors and issues resolved within a week, it earned High for community support despite only ~2k stars (Medium popularity under the rubric) — a good reminder that star counts lag corporate-backed tools that launched recently (PyRIT went public in February 2024).

Garak

Garak is NVIDIA's LLM vulnerability scanner — think "nmap for LLMs": point it at a model endpoint and it runs batteries of probes for known failure modes. Its identity table carries a literature reference, unique in this catalog: the garak paper at arxiv.org/abs/2406.11036.

Tool Name: Garak
Developer / SourceNVIDIA
Github Referencedocs.garak.ai/garak — moved to github.com/NVIDIA/garak
Literaturearxiv.org/abs/2406.11036 (the garak paper)
LanguagePython
LicensingApache 2.0 License
Provides MitigationPrevention: No ❌   Detection: Yes ✅
API AvailabilityYes ✅
FactorDetails
Popularity
  • GitHub Stars: ~3,5K stars (as of Dec 2024)
  • GitHub Forks: ~306 forks
  • Number of Issues: ~303 open issues, 299 closed issues
  • Trend: Growing, particularly with in attack generation, and LLM vulnerability scanning. (wording as printed)
Community Support
  • Active Issues: Actively responds to the issues and tries to close it within a week
  • Documentation: Detailed documentation with guidance and example experiments.
  • Discussion Forums: Active GitHub discussions, as well as discord.
  • Contributors: Over 27 contributors.
Scalability
  • Framework Support: Supports various LLMs from hugging face, openai api, litellm.
  • Large-Scale Deployment: Mostly used in attack LLM, detect LLM failures and assessing LLM security. Can be integrated with NeMo Guardrails
Integration
  • Compatibility: All LLMs, Nvidia models

TopicCoverage

Data disclosure
Model input leak

Development time model theft
Output contains injection

Garak's notes (these are the notes printed at the top of p.291, following the tail of Garak's coverage table):

  • Evasion: Tests model performance against adversarial inputs. owaspai.org/go/evasion/
  • Prompt Injection: Evaluates the robustness of generative AI models by exploiting weaknesses in prompt design, leading to undesired outputs or bypassing model safeguards. owaspai.org/go/promptinjection/
◆ Senior insight

PyRIT and Garak cover the identical two threats (evasion, direct prompt injection) yet fill different roles. PyRIT is a library: you write orchestration code, which buys deep customization and Azure-pipeline scale — hence High scalability but only Medium ease of integration. Garak is a scanner: run it against an endpoint and get findings, with detect-and-mitigate synergy via NeMo Guardrails and support for litellm and OctoAI. Choose PyRIT for a bespoke, continuous red-team program; choose Garak for fast baseline scans across many models. Mature teams run both — and note Garak's cloud gap: no Azure OpenAI or Azure managed endpoints in its applicability table, where PyRIT (a Microsoft/Azure project) checks both.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Baseline every new LLM (or prompt change) with a Garak scan before it ships; treat new probe failures as regressions.
  • Codify your application-specific abuse cases as PyRIT orchestrators so they run in CI, not just in annual pentests.
  • Feed findings into runtime mitigations (e.g. NeMo Guardrails for Garak findings) — detection without a prevention loop is shelfware.
  • Neither tool tests indirect prompt injection — if your app ingests untrusted documents/web content, add Promptfoo (next lesson) or manual testing.
Both PyRIT and Garak cover only evasion and direct prompt injection. Why is neither able to tick "indirect prompt injection"?
Their probes are delivered through the user-input channel — direct injection. Indirect injection requires malicious instructions to arrive via third-party content the application retrieves (documents, web pages, email), which means the test harness must simulate the application's data-ingestion path, not just the chat interface. In this catalog only Promptfoo covers indirect prompt injection.
What is unique about Garak's identity table compared with every other tool in the catalog?
It includes a Literature row — the garak paper at arXiv:2406.11036 — and documents a repository move (docs.garak.ai to github.com/NVIDIA/garak). It is also one of only two GenAI tools here under Apache 2.0 (the other being Guardrail); the rest are MIT.

Key Points

  • GenAI subsection scope: PyRIT, Garak, Prompt Fuzzer, Guardrail, Promptfoo; more tools via the OWASP GenAI security project solutions page ("Test & Evaluate") and its GenAI Red Teaming guide.
  • PyRIT (Microsoft, MIT, library-based API): ~2k stars but 125+ contributors; rated Medium/High/High/Medium; supports every listed framework including Azure OpenAI, Azure managed endpoints, ONNX-style local models and GGUF; extendable to Azure pipelines.
  • Garak (NVIDIA, Apache 2.0, paper arXiv:2406.11036): ~3.5K stars; rated High/Medium/Medium/Medium; supports Hugging Face, OpenAI API, litellm, Cohere, Replicate, GGUF, OctoAI — but not Azure OpenAI/managed endpoints or TensorFlow.
  • Both are detection-only, text-modality, and cover exactly two threats: evasion and direct prompt injection.
  • Garak integrates with NeMo Guardrails, closing the loop from detection to runtime mitigation.
  • Library (PyRIT) vs scanner (Garak) is the real selection axis — customization depth vs speed to first finding.
Module 14 · §5 AI security testing — generative AI tools · OWASP AI Exchange

GenAI Red Teaming II: Prompt Fuzzer, Guardrail, Promptfoo

~15 minCovers guide pp. 291–303

The last three GenAI tools each break the mold: Prompt Fuzzer attacks one artifact only — your system prompt; Guardrail is the first tool in the catalog that can actually prevent attacks; and Promptfoo pairs prevention with the catalog's only coverage of indirect prompt injection, wrapped in the catalog's most complicated licensing. These differences decide which one belongs in your stack.

Prompt Fuzzer

From Prompt Security, Prompt Fuzzer does one job: "It only assesses the security of your GenAI application's system prompt against various dynamic LLM-based attacks" — so it can be integrated with your current environment. Fuzzing means bombarding a target with many varied hostile inputs and watching for failures; here the target is the system prompt that is supposed to constrain your app's behaviour.

Tool Name: Prompt Fuzzer
Developer / SourcePrompt Security
Github Referencegithub.com/prompt-security/ps-fuzz
LanguagePython
LicensingOpen-source under the MIT License.
Provides MitigationPrevention: No ❌   Detection: Yes ✅
API AvailabilityYes ✅
FactorDetails
Popularity

  • Large-Scale Deployment: It only assesses the security of your GenAI application's system prompt against various dynamic LLM-based attacks, so it can be integrated with current env.

⚠ Common misconception

"LLM model agnostic in the API mode of use" sounds like broad support, yet the applicability table checks only OpenAI API. Both statements are true: the tool can point at any LLM exposed via a compatible API, but only OpenAI API support was assessed. Also weigh the maintenance signals: "Not updating since Aug", no bugs solved since July, 6 closed issues total — a stale attack corpus is a real risk for a fuzzer whose value depends on tracking current jailbreak techniques.

Guardrail

Guardrail, from Guardrails AI ("Adding guardrails to large language models"), is the outlier of the entire catalog: Prevention: Yes ✅ and Detection: Yes ✅. It is primarily a runtime validation framework — you wrap model inputs/outputs in validators — which the review assesses in red-team terms because you can also use it to detect failures.

Tool Name: Guardrail
Developer / SourceGuardrails AI
Github ReferenceGitHub — guardrails-ai/guardrails: "Adding guardrails to large language models."
LanguagePython
LicensingApache 2.0 License
Provides MitigationPrevention: Yes ✅   Detection: Yes ✅
API Availability(blank in the document)

Promptfoo

Promptfoo is community-developed (Promptfoo community) LLM evaluation and red-teaming tooling in Python and NodeJS. Two things set it apart: it is the only tool in the catalog covering indirect prompt injection, and its licensing is genuinely mixed — read it before adopting.

Tool Name: Promptfoo
Developer / SourcePromptfoo community
Github Referencegithub.com/promptfoo/promptfoo
LanguagePython, NodeJS
LicensingOpen-source under the MIT License — with the multi-license detail below.
Provides MitigationPrevention: Yes ✅   Detection: Yes ✅ (this row and the next are flattened into raw text in the source PDF — a rendering artifact)
API AvailabilityYes ✅

Licensing detail, verbatim from the guide — "This project is licensed under multiple licenses:"

  1. The main codebase is licensed under the MIT License
  2. The /src/redteam/ directory is proprietary and licensed under the Promptfoo Enterprise License
  3. Some third-party components have their own licenses as indicated by LICENSE files in their respective directories

Direct prompt injection

Indirect prompt injection✅
Development time model theft
Output contains injection

Promptfoo's notes differ from every other GenAI tool — the first bullet is model exfiltration, not evasion:

  • Model exfiltration: Evaluates risks of model exploitation during usage. owaspai.org/go/modeltheftuse/
  • Prompt Injection: Evaluates the robustness of generative AI models by exploiting weaknesses in prompt design, leading to undesired outputs or bypassing model safeguards. owaspai.org/go/promptinjection/
THREAT
Indirect prompt injectionMalicious instructions arrive through third-party content your application ingests (documents, web pages, email) rather than from the user — Promptfoo is the only tool in this catalog that covers it. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM01, ATLAS AML.T0051.001.
◆ Senior insight

Prevention capability changes a tool's place in your architecture. Detection tools (PyRIT, Garak, Prompt Fuzzer) run before or beside production; Guardrail runs inside the request path, where its own latency, failure modes and bypasses become part of your attack surface. And a purchased "prevention" checkbox is not a strategy: Guardrail validators only block what someone taught them to recognize, so you still red-team the guarded system — ideally with the detection tools from the previous lesson.

▸ Worked example

Licensing trap, concretely: a team adopts Promptfoo "because it's MIT," then builds its CI red-teaming around the /src/redteam/ functionality — which is proprietary under the Promptfoo Enterprise License. The exact capability the AI Exchange catalogs it for (red teaming) is the part with commercial strings attached. The general lesson: in tool reviews, read the Licensing row per directory or feature, not per repository.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • RAG or agentic app ingesting untrusted content? Add Promptfoo — it is the only cataloged tool exercising indirect prompt injection.
  • Wrap production LLM calls with Guardrail validators (compatible with OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic; extends to Azure and LangChain), then re-test the wrapped system.
  • Fuzz every system-prompt change with Prompt Fuzzer — cheap, Docker-packaged, model-agnostic in API mode — but track its staleness (no updates since Aug 2024 per the review).
  • Before adopting Promptfoo commercially, have legal review the three-part license split (MIT core / Enterprise /src/redteam/ / third-party components).
Why does Prompt Fuzzer's narrow focus (system prompt only) still earn Medium scalability and integration?
Because narrowness aids adoption: it ships as Python plus a Docker image, runs on "any device," and — since it only assesses the system prompt against dynamic LLM-based attacks — it slots into an existing environment without touching model infrastructure. The Low ratings are for popularity (~427 stars) and community support (stale since July/August), not for deployability.
What is unusual about Promptfoo's threat-coverage row for prompt injection compared with PyRIT, Garak, Prompt Fuzzer and Guardrail?
Those four all tick direct prompt injection. Promptfoo instead ticks indirect prompt injection — attacks smuggled in via retrieved third-party content — making it unique in the catalog. Its notes also cite model exfiltration evaluation (owaspai.org/go/modeltheftuse/), a bullet no other GenAI tool's notes carry.

Key Points

  • Prompt Fuzzer (Prompt Security, MIT): fuzzes only your GenAI app's system prompt with dynamic LLM-based attacks; Python + Docker; LLM-agnostic in API mode (assessed: OpenAI API only); rated Low/Low/Medium/Medium; stale since mid-2024.
  • Guardrail (Guardrails AI, Apache 2.0): the catalog's first Prevention ✅ + Detection ✅ tool; Python and JavaScript; extends to Azure and LangChain; compatible with OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic; rated High/High/Medium/High; API Availability left blank in the review.
  • Promptfoo (community, ~4.3K stars, 113+ contributors, issues fixed within days): Prevention ✅ + Detection ✅, Python/NodeJS, Enterprise version with cloud deployment.
  • Promptfoo licensing is three-way: MIT core, proprietary /src/redteam/ (Promptfoo Enterprise License), third-party components under their own licenses.
  • Promptfoo is the only tool covering indirect prompt injection; its notes uniquely add model-exfiltration evaluation.
  • All three are text-modality, classification-task tools; coverage of Prompt Fuzzer and Guardrail is evasion + direct prompt injection.
Module 14 · §5 AI security testing → §6 AI privacy · OWASP AI Exchange

Tool Ratings, Choosing Your Stack, and the Turn to Privacy

~14 minCovers guide pp. 303–305

The catalog closes with the cross-tool ratings: a heatmap comparing all ten tools, the rubric that defines what High/Medium/Low actually mean, and an honest disclaimer about the assessment's shelf life. You'll leave this lesson able to shortlist a red-teaming stack in minutes — and with the bridge into the guide's next chapter, AI privacy.

Tool Ratings

Verbatim framing: "This section rates the discussed tools by Popularity, Community Support, Scalability and Integration." The guide presents this as a color-coded heatmap figure titled "Tool Ratings by Popularity, Community Support, Scalability, and Integration": ten tools on the y-axis (ART, Promptfoo, Guardrail, Prompt Fuzzer, Garak, PyRIT, Armory, Foolbox, DeepSec, TextAttack), the four criteria on the x-axis, and a color bar labeled "Rating (1 = Low, 2 = Moderate, 3 = High)" running 1.00–3.00 in 0.25 steps — cream/pale yellow = Low, teal/cyan = Moderate, dark navy = High. The table below reconstructs the figure using each tool's authoritative Tool Rating table:

Tool (heatmap row order)PopularityCommunity SupportScalabilityIntegration
ARTHigh (3)High (3)High (3)High (3)
PromptfooHigh (3)High (3)Medium (2)Medium (2)
GuardrailHigh (3)High (3)Medium (2)High (3)
Prompt FuzzerLow (1)Low (1)Medium (2)Medium (2)
GarakHigh (3)Medium (2)Medium (2)Medium (2)
PyRITMedium (2)High (3)High (3)Medium (2)
ArmoryLow (1)Medium (2)Medium (2)High (3)
FoolboxMedium (2)Medium (2)Low (1)Medium (2)
DeepSecLow (1)Low (1)Low (1)Low (1)
TextAttackHigh (3)High (3)Medium (2)High (3)

Figure (reconstructed) — "Tool Ratings by Popularity, Community Support, Scalability, and Integration", guide p. 303. In the original heatmap image some cells read slightly differently at low resolution; the per-tool Tool Rating tables are authoritative, and they are what is shown here.

The at-a-glance reading the figure is designed for: ART, TextAttack, Guardrail, Promptfoo and PyRIT are broadly strong; DeepSec and Foolbox are research-focused with weaker operational ratings; Prompt Fuzzer and Armory are niche-but-usable.

The rating rubric — what High/Medium/Low mean

AttributeHighMediumLow
Popularity>3,000 stars1,000–3,000 stars<1,000 stars
Community Support>100 contributors, quick response (<3 days)50–100 contributors, response in 3–14 days<50 contributors, slow response (>14 days)
ScalabilityProven enterprise-grade, multi-frameworkModerate scalability, limited frameworksResearch focused, small-scale
IntegrationBroad compatibilityLimited compatibility, narrow use-caseMinimal or no integration, research tools only
◆ Senior insight

The rubric makes the ratings falsifiable — you can recompute them yourself, today. That matters because the assessment is a snapshot: Prompt Fuzzer was rated on December-2024 data showing staleness since August; if it resumed active development next quarter, its Low ratings would be wrong. Treat published tool ratings as a methodology to rerun, never as a permanent verdict.

Disclaimer on the use of the Assessment

The guide attaches three emphasized caveats — quote them whenever you circulate this comparison internally:

  • Scope of Assessment: This review exclusively focuses on open-source RedTeaming tools. Proprietary or commercial solutions were not included in this evaluation.
  • Independent Review: The evaluation is independent and based solely on publicly available information from sources such as GitHub repositories, official documentation, and related community discussions.
  • Tool Version and Relevance: The information and recommendations provided in this assessment are accurate as of September 2024. Any future updates, enhancements, or changes to these tools should be verified directly via the provided links or respective sources to ensure continued relevance.

Tool Fit and Usage

"The recommendations in this report should be considered based on your organization's specific use case, scale, and security posture. Some tools may offer advanced features that may not be necessary for smaller projects or environments, while others may be better suited to specific frameworks or security goals."

Full-catalog threat-coverage matrix

Consolidating all ten coverage tables (a synthesis — the guide prints these per tool). Only the six threat topics any tool covers are shown; the other nine taxonomy topics have no coverage anywhere in the catalog:

Threat topicARTArmoryFoolboxDeepSecTextAttackPyRITGarakPrompt FuzzerGuardrailPromptfoo
Development time model poisoning✅✅✅
Model theft by use✅
Evasion✅✅✅✅✅✅✅✅✅✅
Model inversion / Membership inference✅
Direct prompt injection✅✅✅✅✅
Indirect prompt injection✅

Choosing a stack — a decision path

flowchart TD
  A["What are you testing?"] --> B{"Predictive model or GenAI app?"}
  B -->|"predictive"| C{"Classical ML - XGBoost, sklearn?"}
  C -->|"yes"| D["ART - only tool covering classical ML"]
  C -->|"no, deep learning"| E{"NLP text model?"}
  E -->|"yes"| F["TextAttack plus ART"]
  E -->|"no"| G["ART; add Armory for scenario evaluation"]
  B -->|"GenAI"| H{"Need runtime prevention?"}
  H -->|"yes"| I["Guardrail wraps the model; re-test after"]
  H -->|"assessment"| J{"App ingests untrusted content?"}
  J -->|"yes"| K["Promptfoo - covers indirect prompt injection"]
  J -->|"no"| L{"Custom program or quick scan?"}
  L -->|"custom, CI-scale"| M["PyRIT orchestrators"]
  L -->|"quick scan"| N["Garak probes; Prompt Fuzzer for system prompts"]

Figure — a selection path synthesized from the catalog's ratings, coverage tables and the Tool Fit and Usage guidance (guide p. 304).

Where the guide goes next: 6. AI privacy

Page 305 opens chapter 6 (owaspai.org/go/aiprivacy/, category: discussion): "This section of the AI Exchange covers how privacy principles apply to AI systems. The rest of the AI Exchange covers the security of AI systems, including the protection of personal data, but there is more to privacy than just that — which is the topic of this section." The chapter's opening list of privacy concerns (which continues past this module's page range) begins: "Just like any system that processes data, AI systems can have privacy risks. There are specific privacy concerns associated with AI:"

  • AI systems are data-intensive, adding risks around collection and retention. Personal data may come from many sources, each with different sensitivity and regulatory constraints; legislation often requires a legal basis and/or consent for collection and use, and grants individuals rights to correct, request, and remove their own data.
  • Protecting training data is hard because it must be retained long-term for retraining — and even with identities removed, individuals may be re-identifiable from what remains. This is where differential privacy becomes crucial: altering the data (e.g. adding noise or using aggregation) until individuals are sufficiently unrecognizable, while valuable insights can still be derived.
  • Training data is accessible in the engineering environment — so that environment needs more protection than usual, since conventional systems normally don't expose personal data to technical teams.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 303–305: Tool Ratings heatmap and rubric, disclaimer (assessment accurate as of September 2024; open-source scope; public-information basis), Tool Fit and Usage, and the AI privacy chapter opening (/go/aiprivacy/). Added canon: the "select tools → run → verify" loop implements the NIST AI RMF Measure function; membership-inference risk to training data connects the red-teaming catalog (ART's inversion/membership tests) to the privacy chapter's differential-privacy discussion. The extracts record no ISO/IEC 27090, EU AI Act, or OpenCRE citations in this page range.

Your CISO asks: "The catalog rates Guardrail High almost everywhere — can we standardize on it alone?"
No. Guardrail is a prevention/detection wrapper covering only evasion and direct prompt injection on text. It cannot test predictive models (ART's territory), does not exercise indirect prompt injection (Promptfoo), and standardizing on a runtime guard without independent red-teaming means you never measure whether the guard works. The guide's own Tool Fit and Usage note says recommendations depend on use case, scale, and security posture.
Why is the September-2024 validity date more than a formality for this catalog?
Because several ratings hinge on point-in-time repository activity: Prompt Fuzzer is rated Low partly for "not updating since Aug"; Garak's repo moved (docs.garak.ai to github.com/NVIDIA/garak); star counts drift across rubric thresholds (Foolbox sits at ~2,800, just below the 3,000 High cutoff). Rerun the rubric against live data before making procurement decisions.
Why does removing names from training data not eliminate privacy risk, and what does the guide propose?
Individuals can often be re-identified from the remaining attributes even after identity data is removed. The guide proposes differential privacy: perturb the data — by adding noise or aggregating — until individuals are sufficiently unrecognizable while the dataset still yields valuable insights. This matters especially because training data is retained long-term for retraining and is exposed to engineering teams.

Key Points

  • The Tool Ratings heatmap compares all ten tools on Popularity, Community Support, Scalability, Integration with a 1–3 (Low/Moderate/High) color scale; per-tool Tool Rating tables are the authoritative values.
  • Rubric: Popularity by stars (>3,000 / 1,000–3,000 / <1,000); Community Support by contributors and response time (>100 & <3 days / 50–100 & 3–14 days / <50 & >14 days); Scalability from enterprise-grade multi-framework down to research-scale; Integration from broad compatibility down to research-only.
  • Disclaimer: open-source tools only; independent, public-information-based; accurate as of September 2024 — verify before relying.
  • Tool Fit and Usage: match tools to your use case, scale, and security posture; advanced features may be unnecessary for small projects.
  • Across the whole catalog, evasion is covered by all ten tools; indirect prompt injection by exactly one (Promptfoo); nine of fifteen threat topics by none.
  • Chapter 6 (AI privacy) opens next: AI is data-intensive (legal basis/consent, individual rights), training data needs long retention and differential privacy (noise, aggregation), and engineering environments expose personal data and need extra protection.
Module 15 · §6 AI privacy · OWASP AI Exchange

What Makes AI a Privacy Problem

~12 minCovers guide pp. 305–306

The rest of the AI Exchange treats privacy as a security property — keep personal data confidential and intact. This section widens the lens: AI systems create privacy risk even when nothing is "hacked", because they hoard data, keep it for years, expose it to engineers, and make decisions about people. After this lesson you will be able to name the specific privacy concerns AI adds on top of any data-processing system, and split "AI privacy" cleanly into its security half and its individual-rights half.

Privacy is bigger than data security

Every system that processes data can have privacy risks. What the OWASP AI Exchange stresses is that there is more to privacy than protecting personal data against attackers. Encrypting a database perfectly does nothing for a person who is unlawfully profiled, denied credit by an opaque algorithm, or unable to get their data deleted. The guide's AI-privacy section (owaspai.org/go/aiprivacy/, a "discussion" category page) covers how privacy principles apply to AI systems — the part that security controls alone cannot deliver.

The six privacy concerns specific to AI

The guide lists concrete concerns that distinguish AI systems from conventional software:

  1. AI systems are data-intensive. They present additional risks around data collection and retention. Personal data may be collected from many sources, each with different levels of sensitivity and regulatory constraints. Legislation often requires a legal basis and/or consent for collecting and using personal data, and grants rights to individuals to correct, request, and remove their own data.
  2. Protecting training data is hard because it lives long. Training data typically must be retained for long periods, since many models need to be retrained. Often the actual identities of the people involved are irrelevant to the model — but removing identity data does not remove risk, because individual identities may be deduced from the remaining data. This is where differential privacy becomes crucial: alter the data (for example by adding noise or using aggregation techniques) until individuals are sufficiently unrecognizable, while the data still yields valuable insights.
  3. Training data is exposed to the engineering environment. Data scientists and ML engineers can see the training data day-to-day. Conventional systems normally do not put personal data in front of technical teams, so the AI engineering environment needs more protection than engineering environments usually get.
  4. ML enables unique privacy strategies. The nature of machine learning allows strategies unavailable to conventional systems — notably federated learning: splitting the training set across different separated systems, typically aligned with separated data collection, so raw data never has to be pooled.
  5. AI systems make decisions. When those decisions are about people, they may discriminate on protected attributes (e.g., gender, race), and the decisions may trigger actions that invade privacy — an ethical or legal concern. Legislation may prohibit some kinds of decisions outright, and set rules about transparency of decision logic and the individual's right to object.
  6. Models suffer model-attack risks. Attackers can extract training data from a model itself — e.g., model inversion, membership inference, and sensitive data disclosure by large language models. A model trained on personal data is itself a personal-data store with a query interface.
◆ Senior insight

Concern 3 is the one teams most often miss. A bank would never let developers browse production customer records — yet the same bank's data-science team may hold a full copy of those records for years "because we might retrain". Treat the ML engineering environment with production-grade access control, logging, and minimization, because from a privacy standpoint it is production.

Privacy = personal-data protection + further individual rights

The guide divides AI privacy into two parts. Learn this split — it decides who owns which risk in your organization.

The two halves of AI privacy (guide p. 306)
1 · Security threats & controls (rest of the AI Exchange)
Personal data C&Iin train/test data, model input, model output
Conventional securitypersonal data in transit and at rest
Model-attack defensesvs. inversion, membership inference
Minimization / differential privacyincluding minimized retention
Model-behaviour integritywhen behaviour can hurt individuals
2 · Further individual rights (this section — not security)
Use limitationpurpose specification
Consentgranular, auditable, withdrawable
Fairnessno unjustified adverse effects
Transparencydecision logic, documentation
Data accuracyplus rights of correction / objection / erasure / request

Spelled out, part 1 — the threats to AI security and their controls — includes:

  • Confidentiality and integrity protection of personal data in train/test data, model input, or output, consisting of: 'conventional' security of personal data in transit and at rest; protection against model attacks that try to retrieve personal data (e.g., model inversion); and personal-data minimization / differential privacy, including minimized retention.
  • Integrity protection of the model behaviour when that behaviour can hurt the privacy of individuals — for example when individuals are unlawfully discriminated against, or when model output leads to actions that invade privacy (e.g., a person undergoing a fraud investigation).

Part 2 covers threats and controls that are not about security but about further rights of the individual, as covered by privacy regulations such as the GDPR: use limitation, consent, fairness, transparency, data accuracy, and the rights of correction, objection, erasure, and request.

⚠ Common misconception

"We passed the pen test, so we're privacy-compliant." Security testing exercises only half of AI privacy. A perfectly hardened model can still violate use limitation (data reused for an incompatible purpose), fairness (discriminatory decisions), or privacy rights (no erasure process). Conversely, a model with impeccable consent flows can still leak its training set through membership inference. You need both halves.

§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 305–306, permalink owaspai.org/go/aiprivacy/. The security half maps to OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM02: Sensitive Information Disclosure and MITRE ATLAS techniques such as AML.T0024 (Exfiltration via ML Inference API). The rights half aligns with the NIST AI RMF GOVERN and MAP functions and the NIST Privacy Framework.

Why does removing names and identifiers from training data not fully solve the privacy problem?
Because individual identities may still be deducible from the remaining attributes (re-identification). The guide's answer is differential privacy: alter the data — add noise or aggregate — until individuals are sufficiently unrecognizable while the data still supports useful insights.
Name the two halves of AI privacy and one control belonging to each.
Half 1: security threats and controls protecting personal data and model-behaviour integrity — e.g., defenses against model inversion, or encryption of data in transit/at rest. Half 2: non-security individual rights from regulations like GDPR — e.g., consent management, use limitation, or the right to erasure.

Key Points

  • Privacy is more than data security: a secure system can still profile, discriminate, or deny rights.
  • AI's specific concerns: data intensity, long retention for retraining, engineer access to training data, decisions about people, and model attacks.
  • Differential privacy (noise, aggregation) protects individuals in retained training data where deletion of identifiers is not enough.
  • Federated learning is a uniquely ML privacy strategy: train across separated systems instead of pooling data.
  • AI privacy = (1) security threats/controls for personal data and model-behaviour integrity + (2) further individual rights: use limitation, consent, fairness, transparency, accuracy, correction/objection/erasure/request.
  • The ML engineering environment holds personal data and needs more protection than engineering environments usually get.
Module 15 · §6 AI privacy · OWASP AI Exchange

Legislation, Assessments, and the Radioactive-Gold Rule

~11 minCovers guide p. 307

Privacy requirements do not come from one law — they come from a patchwork of legislation and standards, and the AI Exchange explicitly does not guarantee compliance with any of them. What it gives you instead is an engineering attitude ("radioactive gold") and an assessment discipline (PIAs — plus their four AI blind spots). This lesson teaches both.

Where the requirements come from

Privacy principles and requirements come from different legislations and standards:

TypeExamples named in the guideWhat they give you
Privacy legislationGDPR (EU), LGPD (Brazil), PIPEDA (Canada), etc.Legal obligations: lawful basis, individual rights, consent rules, penalties
Privacy standardsISO 31700 (privacy by design), ISO 29100 (privacy framework), ISO 27701 (privacy information management), FIPS/FIPP, NIST Privacy Framework, etc.Implementable frameworks and vocabularies for building privacy into systems
Privacy-engineering referencesENISA, NIST, mplsplunk, OWASP, OpenCRE (all linked from the guide)How-to material for engineering privacy into systems in general

Two disclaimers the guide makes about itself, which you should repeat to stakeholders: this guideline does not guarantee compliance with privacy legislation, and it is not a general privacy-engineering guide — for that, use the ENISA, NIST, mplsplunk, OWASP and OpenCRE work it references.

Personal data is 'radioactive gold'

The general principle for engineers is to regard personal data as 'radioactive gold'. It's valuable, but it's also something to minimize, carefully store, carefully handle, limit its usage, limit sharing, keep track of where it is, etc.

The metaphor does real work. Gold: personal data has genuine value — better models, better products. Radioactive: every gram you hold is a liability that leaks harm unless it is shielded (stored carefully), handled with protocols (access control), used only for its licensed purpose (use limitation), transferred rarely (sharing limits), and inventoried at all times (data mapping). The correct engineering instinct is not "collect everything, we might need it" but "hold the minimum, know where every copy is".

◆ Senior insight

The radioactive-gold framing converts privacy from a legal problem into a cost model engineers respect: every extra attribute, every extra copy, every extra month of retention increases blast radius on breach, scope of subject-access requests, and re-training burden on erasure. Minimization is cheaper than remediation — argue it in those terms and you will win the design review.

Privacy Impact Assessments — and their four AI blind spots

Organizations often conduct Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs) — also referred to as Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) — to identify and manage privacy risks. This is a good idea for AI systems as well. A PIA evaluates data flows, use cases, and AI behaviours against applicable privacy laws and ethical standards. Done proactively, it guides the implementation of privacy controls and helps embed privacy-by-design, so privacy risks are minimized from the outset.

But note: PIAs are not per se specialized in AI systems and may overlook typical AI risks regarding:

  • AI input attacks with privacy risks — model inversion, membership inference, or sensitive data output from the model;
  • Bias and fairness risks — systematic discrimination originating in training data;
  • Ongoing learning or retraining — new accuracy and bias risks can appear after deployment;
  • Explainability and accountability gaps — decisions become harder to trace back.
Assessment flow — from generic PIA to AI-specific coverage (guide p. 307)
AI initiativedata flows, use cases, AI behaviours
→
PIA / DPIAvs. privacy laws & ethical standards
→
Gapsmodel attacks · bias · retraining drift · explainability
→
AI impact assessmente.g. Netherlands method, UK technique overview

To close those gaps, dedicated AI impact assessment methods exist. The guide names two: the AI impact assessment from the Netherlands and the UK government's overview of assessment techniques (both linked from the guide page).

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Run a PIA/DPIA for every AI system that touches personal data — before build, not before launch.
  • Extend the PIA template with the four AI blind spots: model attacks, bias/fairness, retraining drift, explainability gaps.
  • Adopt a dedicated AI impact assessment method (Netherlands method or UK technique overview) alongside the PIA.
  • Maintain a data inventory: know where every copy of personal data is — the "keep track of where it is" clause of radioactive gold.
  • Re-assess after every retraining cycle: new accuracy and bias risks can appear after deployment.
§ Source & standards

Guide p. 307. Related canon: NIST AI RMF 1.0 MAP function (context and risk identification) and MEASURE (ongoing evaluation) formalize exactly the "reassess after retraining" discipline; ISO/IEC 27701 extends ISO 27001/27002 for privacy information management; GDPR Art. 35 is the legal basis for DPIAs in the EU.

Your organization already runs DPIAs on all new systems. Why is that not sufficient for an ML fraud-detection system?
Generic PIAs/DPIAs are not specialized in AI and typically overlook four AI-specific risk classes: privacy-relevant model attacks (inversion, membership inference, sensitive output), bias/fairness from training data, new risks introduced by ongoing learning or retraining after deployment, and explainability/accountability gaps. A dedicated AI impact assessment is needed to cover them.
Unpack the 'radioactive gold' metaphor into at least four engineering obligations.
Minimize how much you hold; store it carefully (shielding/security); handle it carefully (access protocols); limit its usage to specified purposes; limit sharing; and keep track of where it is (inventory). Value alone never justifies unbounded accumulation.

Key Points

  • Requirements come from legislation (GDPR, LGPD, PIPEDA) and standards (ISO 31700, ISO 29100, ISO 27701, FIPS, NIST Privacy Framework).
  • The AI Exchange does not guarantee legal compliance and is not a general privacy-engineering guide — use ENISA, NIST, mplsplunk, OWASP, OpenCRE for that.
  • Treat personal data as radioactive gold: valuable, but minimize, store and handle carefully, limit use and sharing, and track its location.
  • PIAs/DPIAs evaluate data flows, use cases and AI behaviours against laws and ethics, and drive privacy-by-design.
  • PIAs have four AI blind spots: model attacks, bias/fairness, post-deployment retraining risks, explainability/accountability gaps.
  • Close the blind spots with dedicated AI impact assessments — e.g., the Netherlands method or the UK government's technique overview.
Module 15 · §6 AI privacy · OWASP AI Exchange

Principle 1 — Use Limitation and Purpose Specification

~12 minCovers guide p. 308

The first and most frequently violated privacy principle in ML: data collected for one purpose quietly becomes training data for another. Security data becomes marketing data; compliance data becomes analytics data. This lesson teaches the rule, its legal anchors (GDPR Art. 6 and 9, the EU AI Act's prohibited practices), the subtle case where harm needs no personal data at all, and the two techniques — data enclaves and federated learning — that make use limitation enforceable in engineering terms.

The rule

You should not simply use data collected for one purpose (e.g., safety or security) as a training dataset to train your model for other purposes (e.g., profiling, personalized marketing). The guide gives two concrete violations:

  • MFA phone numbers: if you collect phone numbers and other identifiers as part of your multi-factor-authentication flow (to improve security), that does not mean you may also use them for user targeting and other unrelated purposes.
  • KYC data: you may be required to collect sensitive data under know-your-customer rules, but such data must not feed ML models used for business analytics without proper controls.
▸ Worked example

The MFA example is not hypothetical: in 2019 the US FTC's action against Facebook covered exactly this pattern — phone numbers collected for two-factor authentication were used for advertising targeting. Purpose creep is cheap for the organization (the data is already there) and invisible to the user, which is precisely why the principle exists.

Lawful basis and prohibited purposes

Some privacy laws require a lawful basis for processing personal data — and bases, plural, if the data serves more than one purpose. See GDPR Articles 6 and 9 (Art. 6 lists the six lawful bases; Art. 9 governs special-category/sensitive data). There is a link with restrictions on the purpose of an AI application itself: the prohibited practices in the European AI Act, such as using machine learning for individual criminal profiling. Some practices are regarded as simply too risky in their potential harm and unfairness toward individuals and society — no amount of consent or controls makes them acceptable.

Harm without personal data: the army-selection example

A use case may not even involve personal data and still be potentially harmful or unfair to individuals. The guide's example: an algorithm that decides who may join the army, based on how much weight a person can lift and how fast they can run. This data cannot (with some exceptions) be used to re-identify individuals — yet the use case may still be unrightfully unfair towards gender, if for example the algorithm is based on an unfair training set. Purpose-level scrutiny applies even when data-level privacy is clean.

⚠ Common misconception

"No PII in the dataset means no privacy review needed." Wrong on two counts: first, individuals may be deducible from non-identifier attributes; second — the army example — a use case with genuinely non-identifying data can still be unfair to a protected group. Privacy review covers purposes and effects, not just fields.

Making it operational
