In practical terms, the guide prescribes four moves:

  • Reduce access to sensitive data;
  • Create anonymized copies for incompatible purposes (e.g., analytics);
  • Document a purpose/lawful basis before collecting the data;
  • Communicate that purpose to the user in an appropriate way.

Two newer techniques enable use limitation architecturally:

  • Data enclaves: store pooled personal data in restricted secure environments;
  • Federated learning: decentralize ML by removing the need to pool data into a single location — the model is trained in multiple iterations at different sites.
Centralized pooling vs. federated learning (guide p. 308)
Centralized (what use limitation must police)
Site A / B / C datapooled into one location
Central data lakeevery purpose can reach every record
Model trainingfull raw-data exposure
Federated (use limitation by architecture)
Local training at each siteraw data never leaves
Model updates exchangedmultiple iterations at different sites
Global modellearns without pooling data
Principle 1 · USE LIMITATION & PURPOSE SPECIFICATIONUse data only for its documented, communicated purposegovernance · privacy principle
IntentPrevent purpose creep: data collected for one purpose (safety, security, KYC, MFA) must not become training data for unrelated purposes (profiling, personalized marketing, business analytics) without proper basis and controls.
ImplementDocument a purpose/lawful basis before collecting; communicate it to the user; reduce access to sensitive data; create anonymized copies for incompatible purposes such as analytics; use data enclaves (restricted secure environments for pooled data) and federated learning (train in iterations at separate sites, never pooling raw data).
LimitsPurpose review is needed even without personal data — a use case on non-identifying data (army selection by lifting/running) can still be unfair to a group. Some purposes are prohibited outright (EU AI Act, e.g. individual criminal profiling) regardless of controls. Multiple purposes need multiple lawful bases.
StandardsGDPR Art. 6 (lawful bases) & Art. 9 (special categories); EU AI Act prohibited practices; guide p. 308, permalink owaspai.org/go/aiprivacy/.
Your security team offers the fraud team its MFA phone-number dataset "since we already have it". What is the correct response?
Refuse by default. The data was collected for a security purpose; reuse for another purpose requires its own lawful basis, documentation, and communication to users — and possibly anonymization. "We already have it" is exactly the purpose-creep pattern use limitation prohibits.
How does federated learning enforce use limitation architecturally rather than by policy?
Raw data never leaves the site where it was collected; only model updates travel, over multiple training iterations at different sites. Other purposes physically cannot reach the pooled raw data because no pool exists — the limitation is built into the topology, not merely written in a policy.

Key Points

  • Data collected for one purpose (security/MFA, KYC) must not train models for other purposes (marketing, analytics) without proper controls.
  • GDPR Art. 6 and 9 require a lawful basis per purpose; sensitive data has stricter rules.
  • The EU AI Act prohibits some purposes entirely, e.g. ML for individual criminal profiling — too risky for individuals and society.
  • Harm does not require personal data: the army lifting/running example is non-identifying yet can be unfair on gender.
  • Operational moves: restrict access, anonymized copies for incompatible purposes, document basis before collection, communicate purpose to users.
  • Data enclaves and federated learning make use limitation architectural: restricted environments, or no central pooling at all.
Module 15 · §6 AI privacy · OWASP AI Exchange

Principle 2 — Fairness, the Elephant in the Room

~15 minCovers guide pp. 309–310

Fairness is the privacy principle where honest engineering gets uncomfortable: sometimes the most accurate model is the discriminating one, and sometimes no amount of clever data work can fix it. This lesson covers the EDPS definition, the fairness metrics you can actually compute, NIST's FRVT testing, the accuracy-vs-discrimination trade-off — and the guide's bluntest advice: when accuracy cannot be reached within discrimination boundaries, abandon the algorithm.

THREAT
Unfair / discriminatory model decisionsModel output leads to unjustified adverse effects or privacy-invading actions (e.g., fraud investigation of an innocent person). Lifecycle: design & runtime. Maps to: NIST AI RMF "harmful bias" characteristic; GDPR Art. 5 fair processing.

What fairness means

Fairness means handling personal data in a way individuals expect, and not using it in ways that lead to unjustified adverse effects. The algorithm should not behave in a discriminating way. And note the security link: accuracy issues become a privacy problem when model output leads to actions that invade privacy — such as a person undergoing a fraud investigation. Accuracy issues can be caused by a complex problem, insufficient data, mistakes in data and model engineering, and manipulation by attackers — that last cause shows there is a direct relation between model security and privacy: a poisoning attack that degrades accuracy is simultaneously a fairness/privacy incident.

GDPR Article 5 refers to "fair processing", and the EDPS guideline defines fairness as the prevention of "unjustifiably detrimental, unlawfully discriminatory, unexpected or misleading" processing of personal data. GDPR does not specify how fairness is measured, but the EDPS recommends three families of measures and safeguards:

  • the right to information (transparency);
  • the right to intervene (access, erasure, data portability, rectification);
  • the right to limit processing (right not to be subject to automated decision-making, and non-discrimination).

Measuring fairness

The literature offers different fairness metrics. There is no industry standard yet on which to use, but you should assess fairness especially when your algorithm makes significant decisions about individuals — banning platform access, financial implications, denial of services or opportunities.

Metric family (from the guide)Core idea
Group fairnessStatistical parity of outcomes across protected groups.
False positive error rateEqual rates of wrongly adverse decisions (e.g., wrongly flagged for fraud) across groups.
UnawarenessThe model does not use protected attributes as inputs (weak alone — proxies leak them back in).
Counterfactual fairnessThe decision would not change if only the individual's protected attribute were different.

There are also efforts to test algorithms against multiple metrics: NIST's FRVT project tests different face-recognition algorithms on fairness using different metrics.

⚠ Common misconception

"We removed gender and race from the features, so the model can't discriminate." That is the unawareness metric — and it fails against proxies. Postcode, study program, first name, shopping patterns all correlate with protected attributes. Worse, without the protected attribute in your data you cannot even measure the bias, let alone correct it. Unawareness can make discrimination invisible rather than absent.

The elephant in the room: accuracy vs. discrimination

Here is the hard truth the guide refuses to soften: in some situations a model is more accurate if it DOES discriminate against protected attributes. Certain groups have, in practice, a lower success rate in certain areas because of societal aspects rooted in culture and history — some of it institutional discrimination, some with practical backgrounds (the guide's example: for language reasons, new immigrants statistically tend to be hindered in getting higher education). We want to get rid of that. Which means: being completely fair across groups often costs accuracy — you are deliberately declining predictive signal that encodes historical injustice.

The guide's decision rule: if sufficient accuracy cannot be attained while staying within discrimination boundaries, there is no other option than to abandon the algorithm idea. For fraud detection, that could mean transactions get selected randomly instead of by an algorithm.

Unsolvable bias — decide before you start

A machine learning use case may have unsolvable bias issues that are critical to recognize before you even start. Before any data analysis, ask: do any key data elements have a skewed representation of protected groups — not skewed in your training data, but in the real world (e.g., more men than women in certain types of education)? If so, bias is probably impossible to avoid — unless you can correct for the protected attributes. If you don't have those attributes (e.g., racial data) or proxies for them, there is no way to correct. You are then in a dilemma between the benefit of an accurate model and a certain level of discrimination. This dilemma can be decided before you even start — and save you a lot of trouble.

▸ Worked example

The guide's math-program example: a dataset of students with two variables — study program and score on a math test. Goal: select students good at math for a special math program. Suppose 'computer science' students score best, and far more males than females study computer science. The model will select more males than females. Without gender data in the dataset, this bias is impossible to counter. Note what this proves: even with a diverse team, an equally distributed dataset, and no historical bias in your data pipeline, your AI may still discriminate — and there may be nothing you can do about it.

flowchart TD
  A["Planned ML use case making decisions about people"] --> B{"Key data elements skewed for protected groups in the real world?"}
  B -->|no| C["Proceed - assess fairness metrics, esp. for significant decisions"]
  B -->|yes| D{"Protected attributes or proxies available in the data?"}
  D -->|yes| E["Correct for protected attributes and monitor"]
  D -->|no| F["Dilemma - accurate model vs a level of discrimination"]
  F --> G{"Sufficient accuracy attainable within discrimination boundaries?"}
  G -->|yes| H["Deploy with fairness thresholds and ongoing testing"]
  G -->|no| I["Abandon the algorithm - e.g. select transactions randomly"]

Figure — the guide's pre-start bias decision, reconstructed from pp. 309–310.

Principle 2 · FAIRNESSPrevent unjustified adverse effects and discriminationgovernance / design · privacy principle
IntentHandle personal data as individuals expect; prevent "unjustifiably detrimental, unlawfully discriminatory, unexpected or misleading" processing (EDPS). Covers both discriminatory decisions and accuracy failures that trigger privacy-invading actions like fraud investigations.
ImplementEDPS safeguards: right to information (transparency), right to intervene (access, erasure, portability, rectification), right to limit processing (no automated decision-making, non-discrimination). Assess with fairness metrics — group fairness, false positive error rate, unawareness, counterfactual fairness — especially for significant decisions (bans, financial impact, denial of services/opportunities). Test like NIST FRVT tests face recognition. Decide the bias dilemma before starting.
LimitsNo industry-standard metric exists. Full cross-group fairness usually costs accuracy because real-world signal encodes historical/institutional discrimination. Without protected attributes or proxies, correction is impossible (math-program example) — even a diverse team, balanced dataset and clean history cannot save you. If accuracy within discrimination boundaries is unattainable: abandon the algorithm (e.g., random selection for fraud checks).
StandardsGDPR Art. 5 fair processing; EDPS guideline on fairness; NIST FRVT; guide pp. 309–310, owaspai.org/go/aiprivacy/.
◆ Senior insight

The most senior call in this whole module is knowing when to say "don't build it". The guide gives you organizational cover: abandoning the algorithm is a listed, legitimate outcome of the fairness assessment, not a project failure. A fraud model replaced by random selection is a defensible position; a model that quietly discriminates is a lawsuit and a headline.

Why can a model discriminate even when trained by a diverse team on an equally distributed dataset with no historical bias?
Because the real world itself is skewed. In the math-program example, computer-science students score best on the math test and far more males study computer science, so selecting by program+score selects more males. The bias comes from real-world correlations, and without gender data in the dataset it cannot be measured or countered.
How can a security attack become a fairness/privacy problem?
Accuracy issues become privacy problems when model output triggers privacy-invading actions (e.g., a fraud investigation). One listed cause of accuracy issues is manipulation by attackers — so an integrity attack (e.g., data poisoning) that degrades accuracy directly produces unfair, privacy-invading outcomes. Model security and privacy are linked.

Key Points

  • Fairness = handling data as individuals expect, with no unjustified adverse effects; EDPS: no "unjustifiably detrimental, unlawfully discriminatory, unexpected or misleading" processing.
  • EDPS safeguards: right to information, right to intervene, right to limit processing.
  • Metrics: group fairness, false positive error rate, unawareness, counterfactual fairness — no industry standard yet; NIST FRVT tests face recognition across metrics.
  • The elephant in the room: models can be more accurate when they discriminate, because real-world data encodes historical/institutional injustice.
  • If sufficient accuracy is impossible within discrimination boundaries, abandon the algorithm — e.g., random transaction selection for fraud.
  • Check for real-world skew in key data elements before starting; without protected attributes or proxies, bias cannot be corrected.
  • Accuracy failures (including attacker manipulation) become privacy harms when output triggers actions like fraud investigations.
Module 15 · §6 AI privacy · OWASP AI Exchange

Principles 3 & 4 — Data Minimization and Transparency

~13 minCovers guide pp. 310–312

Minimization is radioactive gold turned into seven concrete engineering moves — fewer attributes, coarser granularity, shorter retention. Transparency is its outward-facing twin: individuals get GDPR Article 22 rights over algorithmic decisions, and your own developers, auditors and privacy engineers get model documentation, data cards, traceability and explainability. This lesson covers both principles end to end.

Principle 3: Data minimization and storage limitation

Minimize the amount, granularity, and storage duration of personal information in your training dataset. The guide makes this concrete with seven practices:

  • Do not collect or copy unnecessary attributes to your dataset if they are irrelevant for your purpose.
  • Anonymize where possible — and note this is not as trivial as "removing PII"; see the WP 29 Guideline on anonymization (linked in the guide).
  • If full anonymization is not possible, reduce granularity when you aim at aggregate insights: reduce lat/long to 2 decimal points if city-level precision suffices, remove the last octets of an IP address, round timestamps to the hour.
  • Use less data where possible: if 10k records are sufficient for an experiment, do not use 1 million.
  • Delete data as soon as it is no longer useful — data from 7 years ago may not be relevant to your model.
  • Remove links in your dataset: obfuscate user IDs, device identifiers, and other linkable attributes.
  • Minimize stakeholders with access, on a "need to know" basis.

Privacy-preserving techniques being developed that support minimization:

  • Distributed data analysis: exchange anonymous aggregated data instead of records;
  • Secure multi-party computation: store data distributed-encrypted, so no single party ever holds plaintext.

Further reading named by the guide: the ICO guidance on AI and data protection and the FPF case-law analysis on automated decision-making.

Principle 3 · DATA MINIMIZATION & STORAGE LIMITATIONMinimize amount, granularity and storage duration of personal datadevelopment-time · privacy principle
IntentShrink the privacy blast radius of the training dataset along three axes: how much data, how precise, how long kept. Less radioactive gold to shield.
ImplementSkip irrelevant attributes; anonymize (per WP 29 — harder than removing PII); else coarsen granularity (lat/long to 2 decimals, drop last IP octets, round timestamps to the hour); use 10k records not 1M when 10k suffices; delete stale data (7-year-old data may be irrelevant); obfuscate user IDs, device identifiers and other linkable attributes; need-to-know access. Emerging: distributed data analysis (exchange anonymous aggregates), secure multi-party computation (distributed-encrypted storage).
LimitsTrue anonymization is genuinely hard — residual attributes can re-identify. Aggregation/coarsening trades away signal, so validate the model still meets its purpose. Retention pressure from retraining needs must be balanced against storage limitation.
StandardsWP 29 anonymization guideline; ICO guidance on AI and data protection; FPF automated-decision-making case-law analysis; guide pp. 310–311, owaspai.org/go/aiprivacy/.
⚔ Attacker's view

Every minimization step raises attacker cost. Re-identification works by joining quasi-identifiers (precise location, timestamps, device IDs) against outside datasets; coarsening lat/long and rounding timestamps destroys the join keys. A membership-inference or inversion attack on a model trained on 10k coarse records has strictly less to extract than on 1M precise ones. Data you deleted last year cannot be in this year's breach.

Principle 4: Transparency

Privacy standards such as FIPP and ISO 29100 require maintaining privacy notices, providing a copy of the user's data upon request, and giving notice when major changes in personal-data processing occur.

GDPR adds a clause specific to algorithmic decision-making: Article 22 gives individuals, under specific conditions, the right to:

  • human intervention in an algorithmic decision;
  • the ability to contest the decision;
  • meaningful information about the logic involved — for examples of "meaningful information", see the EDPS guideline (linked in the guide).

In the US, the Equal Credit Opportunity Act requires detailed explanations of individual algorithmic decisions that deny credit — algorithmic opacity is not a defense.

Transparency for internal stakeholders

Transparency is not only for the end-user. Your models and datasets should be understandable by internal stakeholders: model developers, internal audit, privacy engineers, domain experts, and more. That typically requires four things:

RequirementWhat it contains (from the guide)
Proper model documentationModel type, intent, proposed features, feature importance, potential harm, and bias.
Dataset transparencySource, lawful basis, type of data, whether it was cleaned, age. Data cards are a popular industry approach — see the Google Research paper and Meta's research (both linked in the guide).
TraceabilityWhich model made which decision about an individual, and when?
ExplainabilityMethods to make black-box models more explainable: LIME, SHAP, counterfactual explanations, Deep Taylor Decomposition, etc. — see the guide's linked overview of ML interpretability and the article on pros and cons of explainable AI.
Principle 4 · TRANSPARENCYMake processing and algorithmic decisions understandablegovernance / runtime · privacy principle
IntentIndividuals can understand, contest and get human review of algorithmic decisions about them; internal stakeholders (developers, audit, privacy engineers, domain experts) can understand models and datasets well enough to hold them accountable.
ImplementExternally: privacy notices, data copies on request, notice of major processing changes (FIPP, ISO 29100); GDPR Art. 22 rights — human intervention, contestation, meaningful information about the logic. Internally: model documentation (type, intent, features, feature importance, potential harm, bias); dataset transparency via data cards (source, lawful basis, data type, cleaned or not, age); traceability (which model, which decision, when); explainability via LIME, SHAP, counterfactual explanations, Deep Taylor Decomposition.
Limits"Meaningful information about the logic" is a judgment call (see EDPS examples); post-hoc explainers like LIME/SHAP approximate the model and can mislead — the explainable-AI pros-and-cons debate the guide links to is real. Traceability requires versioned model registries most teams don't have by default.
StandardsFIPP; ISO 29100; GDPR Art. 22; EDPS guideline; US Equal Credit Opportunity Act; Google Research & Meta data-cards papers; guide pp. 311–312.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 310–312. Canon links: minimization implements the NIST Privacy Framework's disassociability property; NIST AI RMF's "accountable & transparent" and "explainable & interpretable" trustworthiness characteristics map to the transparency table above. Reduced/coarsened training data also shrinks the surface for OWASP LLM02 (Sensitive Information Disclosure).

Why is anonymization "not as trivial as removing PII"?
Because remaining attributes — precise location, timestamps, device identifiers, rare combinations of ordinary fields — can be joined with external data to re-identify individuals. That is why the guide points to the WP 29 Guideline and offers the fallback of granularity reduction (coarsen coordinates, strip IP octets, round timestamps) when full anonymization is not achievable.
A customer is denied credit by your model and asks why. Which two legal instruments from this lesson apply, and what must you provide?
GDPR Article 22 (if in scope): human intervention, ability to contest, and meaningful information about the logic involved. The US Equal Credit Opportunity Act: a detailed explanation of the individual decision that denied credit. Either way, "the model decided" is not an acceptable answer.

Key Points

  • Minimize amount, granularity and storage duration: skip attributes, anonymize (WP 29 — harder than removing PII), coarsen (lat/long, IP octets, timestamps), use less data, delete stale data, unlink IDs, need-to-know access.
  • Emerging minimization techniques: distributed data analysis (anonymous aggregates) and secure multi-party computation (distributed-encrypted storage).
  • FIPP and ISO 29100 require notices, data copies on request, and notice of major processing changes.
  • GDPR Art. 22: human intervention, contestation, and meaningful information about algorithmic decision logic; the US ECOA requires explanations for credit denial.
  • Internal transparency needs four things: model documentation, dataset transparency (data cards — Google/Meta), traceability, explainability (LIME, SHAP, counterfactuals, Deep Taylor Decomposition).
  • Further reading: ICO AI guidance, FPF case-law analysis.
Module 15 · §6 AI privacy · OWASP AI Exchange

Principles 5–7 — Privacy Rights, Data Accuracy, Consent

~13 minCovers guide pp. 312–313

These three principles are where individuals push back on your system: they demand their data, correct it, or withdraw permission for it — and in ML, each of those can mean re-training a model. This lesson covers the four privacy rights, the accuracy obligations, the eight consent requirements, and the cases (fraudsters, employees) where consent is not even possible.

Principle 5: Privacy rights ("individual participation")

Known under privacy standards as "individual participation", this principle lets individuals submit requests to your organization about their personal data. The most-referred rights:

RightObligationML-specific notes (from the guide)
1. Access / portabilityProvide a copy of user data, preferably machine-readable.If data is properly anonymized, it may be exempted from this right.
2. ErasureErase user data unless an exception applies.Good practice: re-train your model without the deleted user's data.
3. CorrectionAllow users to correct factually incorrect data.See the accuracy principle below.
4. ObjectionAllow users to object to a specific use of their data.E.g., objecting to use of their data for model training.
Principle 5 · PRIVACY RIGHTSIndividual participation — access, erasure, correction, objectionruntime / governance · privacy principle
IntentGive individuals operational handles on their own data: obtain it, delete it, fix it, or forbid specific uses of it — including its use as training data.
ImplementRequest-handling processes for all four rights; machine-readable export for access/portability; deletion pipeline that reaches training datasets; re-training without the deleted user's data after erasure; objection handling per use (e.g., exclude from model training while keeping service data).
LimitsErasure has exceptions; properly anonymized data may be exempt from access. The hard ML part: a trained model may retain influence of deleted data — hence the re-training practice, which costs compute and scheduling (batch erasures into re-training cycles).
StandardsGDPR rights of access/portability/erasure/rectification/objection; "individual participation" in privacy standards (FIPP, ISO 29100); guide p. 312.
◆ Senior insight

Deleting a row from the database does not delete its influence from a trained model — memorization means the model can still emit that person's data. That is why the guide pairs erasure and consent-withdrawal with re-training. Practically, nobody retrains per request: batch erasures and honor them at the next scheduled re-training cycle, and document that cadence in your privacy notice. ("Machine unlearning" research aims to make this cheaper, but re-training is today's defensible baseline.)

Principle 6: Data accuracy

Ensure your data is correct: an algorithmic decision based on incorrect data can have severe consequences for the individual. The guide's example: a user's phone number is incorrectly entered into the system, that number is associated with fraud, and the user is banned from the service unjustly. You need processes and tools to fix such accuracy issues as soon as possible when a proper request is made by the individual.

To satisfy the accuracy principle you should also ensure that data is obtained from reliable sources, that its validity and correctness claims are validated, and that data quality and accuracy are periodically assessed.

Principle 6 · DATA ACCURACYKeep data correct — wrong data means wrong decisions about peopledevelopment-time / runtime · privacy principle
IntentPrevent severe consequences of decisions made on incorrect data — e.g., a mistyped phone number associated with fraud getting an innocent user banned.
ImplementProcesses/tools to fix accuracy issues fast when an individual makes a proper request; source data from reliable origins; validate validity and correctness claims; periodically assess data quality and accuracy.
LimitsAccuracy is continuous work, not a one-time cleanse — quality decays and must be periodically re-assessed. Fix speed matters: the harm accrues while the wrong record is live.
StandardsGDPR accuracy principle; ties to right to correction (Principle 5); guide p. 312.

Principle 7: Consent

Consent may be used or required in specific circumstances. Where it is, consent must satisfy all eight of these requirements:

  1. Obtained before collecting, using, updating, or sharing the data;
  2. Recorded and auditable;
  3. Granular — consent per purpose; avoid blanket consent;
  4. Not bundled with terms & conditions;
  5. Consent records protected from tampering;
  6. Method and text adhere to the jurisdiction's requirements — e.g., GDPR requires unambiguous, freely given, written in clear and plain language, explicit, and withdrawable;
  7. Withdrawal as easy as giving consent;
  8. On withdrawal, all associated data is deleted and the model is re-trained.
Consent lifecycle in an ML system (guide pp. 312–313)
Obtain firstbefore collect / use / update / share; granular, unbundled, jurisdiction-compliant
→
Consent recordauditable, tamper-protected
→
Withdrawalas easy as giving
→
Delete + re-trainall associated data removed; model re-trained

When consent is impossible. Consent will not be possible in specific circumstances: you cannot collect consent from a fraudster, and an employer cannot collect consent from an employee because of the power imbalance — "freely given" is unattainable when refusing carries job risk. In those cases you need a different lawful basis. If you must collect consent, ensure it is properly obtained, recorded, and that proper actions are taken if it is withdrawn.

Principle 7 · CONSENTValid, granular, auditable, withdrawable permissiongovernance / runtime · privacy principle
IntentWhere consent is the lawful basis, make it real: informed, specific, provable, and revocable — with revocation actually propagating into the ML pipeline.
ImplementThe eight requirements: prior to any collection/use/update/sharing; recorded and auditable; granular per purpose (no blanket consent); unbundled from T&S; tamper-protected records; jurisdiction-compliant text (GDPR: unambiguous, freely given, clear plain language, explicit, withdrawable); withdrawal as easy as granting; on withdrawal delete all associated data and re-train the model.
LimitsConsent is impossible where there is no free choice: fraudsters (adverse interest) and employees (power imbalance vs. employer). Use another lawful basis there. Requirement 8 means consent architecture must reach your training pipeline, not just your cookie banner.
StandardsGDPR consent requirements (unambiguous, freely given, clear and plain language, explicit, withdrawable); guide pp. 312–313.
⚠ Common misconception

"We have consent — it's in the terms of service." Fails at least three of the eight requirements: bundled with T&S (4), not granular per purpose (3), and rarely "freely given, unambiguous, explicit" (6). And if your consent-withdrawal flow ends at the CRM without deleting training data and triggering re-training, requirement 8 is unmet even when the UI looks compliant.

An HR team wants to train an attrition-prediction model on employee data "with employee consent". What is wrong with that plan?
Employer–employee consent is invalid due to power imbalance — consent cannot be "freely given" when declining might carry job consequences. The guide lists this (with fraudsters) as a circumstance where consent is not possible; the team must find a different lawful basis or not proceed.
What must happen in the ML pipeline when a user withdraws consent?
All data associated with that consent must be deleted, and the model must be re-trained without it — because a trained model retains the influence (and possibly memorized content) of its training data. Deleting only the source record leaves the model as a residual copy.

Key Points

  • Four privacy rights: access/portability (machine-readable; anonymized data may be exempt), erasure (re-train without deleted data), correction, objection (e.g., to model training).
  • Accuracy: wrong data → wrong decisions → severe consequences (fraud-linked phone number → unjust ban); fix fast on request, use reliable sources, validate, assess periodically.
  • Consent has eight requirements: prior, recorded/auditable, granular, unbundled, tamper-protected, jurisdiction-compliant, easy withdrawal, delete + re-train on withdrawal.
  • GDPR consent must be unambiguous, freely given, in clear plain language, explicit, withdrawable.
  • Consent is impossible with fraudsters and employees (power imbalance) — use another lawful basis.
  • Erasure and consent-withdrawal both reach into the training pipeline: deletion plus model re-training is the guide's stated good practice.
Module 15 · §6 AI privacy · OWASP AI Exchange

Principle 8 — Model Attacks, Scope Boundaries, and the EU AI Act

~14 minCovers guide pp. 313–315

The final principle closes the loop back to security: model attacks are privacy attacks whenever the data is personal. Then two things every practitioner needs before starting any AI project: knowing what is not your job as a privacy professional (scope boundaries), and knowing what the law forbids you to build at all — the EU AI Act's four risk levels, its banned practices, and its generative-AI obligations.

Principle 8: Model attacks

Security threats to data confidentiality represent a privacy risk whenever that data is personal — see the security sections of the Exchange for the full treatment. The notable ones:

THREAT
Membership inferenceAttacker determines whether a specific person's record was in the training set — itself a privacy disclosure (e.g., presence in a medical dataset). Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: MITRE ATLAS AML.T0024, OWASP LLM02.
THREAT
Model inversionAttacker reconstructs training data from the model — recovering the personal data it was trained on. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: MITRE ATLAS AML.T0024, OWASP ML03.
THREAT
Sensitive data disclosure by LLMsModels disclose sensitive data unintentionally stored (memorized) during training. Lifecycle: runtime/use. Maps to: OWASP LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure.
THREAT
Training data leaking from the engineering processPersonal data exposed through the development environment where training data is accessible to technical teams. Lifecycle: development-time.

This is why Principle 8 sits in the privacy list at all: the controls live in the security half of the Exchange (rate limiting, obscuring confidence, differential privacy, training-data minimization, engineering-environment protection), but the obligation to apply them is a privacy obligation whenever training data is personal.

⚔ Attacker's view

Model attacks are attractive because the model is the most available copy of the training data: the raw dataset sits behind enterprise access control, but the model answers queries from the internet. Membership inference exploits the confidence gap between seen and unseen examples; inversion optimizes inputs to maximize class confidence until a recognizable record emerges; LLM extraction simply prompts for memorized sequences. Cost: API calls. That asymmetry is why "the dataset is locked down" is not a complete privacy answer.

Scope boundaries of AI privacy

Many discussion topics on AI are about human rights, social justice, and safety — only a part of it has to do with privacy. As a data protection officer or engineer, do not drag everything into your responsibilities. At the same time, organizations do need to assign those non-privacy AI responsibilities somewhere. The failure mode is symmetrical: the DPO who owns every AI question burns out and blocks delivery; the organization that assigns AI safety and justice questions to nobody ships harm.

Before you start: privacy restrictions on what you can do with AI

Three legal instruments constrain what you can build:

  • GDPR does not restrict AI applications explicitly, but its safeguards may limit what you can do — particularly regarding lawfulness and limitations on purposes of collection, processing, and storage. For lawful grounds, see Article 6.
  • The US Federal Trade Committee provides good (globally useful) guidance on communicating carefully about your AI — including not to overpromise.
  • The EU AI Act does pose explicit application limitations, such as mass surveillance and predictive policing, and restrictions on high-risk purposes such as selecting people for jobs. In addition, domain-specific regulations restrict data use and thereby limit some AI approaches (e.g., in the medical domain).

The EU AI Act in a nutshell

Safety, health and fundamental rights are at the core of the AI Act — risks are analyzed from the perspective of harmfulness to people. The Act identifies four risk levels:

Risk levelTreatmentContents (from the guide)
Unacceptable riskWill be bannedManipulation of people; social scoring; real-time remote biometric identification (e.g., face recognition with cameras in public space).
High riskMust comply with rulesProducts already under safety legislation, plus eight areas (including critical infrastructure and law enforcement). Requires a security risk assessment and conformity with harmonized (adapted) AI security standards OR the essential requirements of the Cyber Resilience Act (when applicable).
Limited riskMinimal transparency requirementsLimited potential for manipulation. Users must get enough transparency to make informed decisions; after interacting, the user can decide whether to continue using it.
Minimal / non riskNo specific obligationsAll remaining systems.
EU AI Act — four risk levels, obligation increases upward (guide p. 314)
Unacceptable risk — BANNEDmanipulation · social scoring · real-time remote biometric ID in public space
High risksafety-legislation products + 8 areas incl. critical infrastructure, law enforcement → security risk assessment + harmonized AI security standards OR Cyber Resilience Act essential requirements
Limited riskminimal transparency so users can make informed decisions and choose to continue
Minimal / non riskthe remaining systems

Consequences for organizations:

  • You must know your AI initiatives and perform a high-level risk analysis to determine each one's risk level.
  • AI is broadly defined here — it includes wider statistical approaches and optimization algorithms, not just deep learning. Your "it's just a scoring formula" system may be in scope.
  • Generative AI must disclose what copyrighted sources were used, and prevent illegal content. The guide's illustration of the stakes: if OpenAI, for example, violated this rule, it could face a 10 billion dollar fine.

The guide links to: the AI Act itself, the Guidelines on prohibited AI, and the AI Act page of the EU.

Principle 8 · MODEL ATTACKSApply the security half's controls wherever training data is personalruntime + development-time · privacy principle
IntentRecognize that confidentiality attacks on models — membership inference, model inversion, sensitive data disclosure, engineering-process leakage — are privacy violations when the data concerns people, and mandate the corresponding security controls.
ImplementDefer to the AI Exchange security sections for the control set: they cover data-confidentiality threats through use (inversion/membership inference on the runtime model), unintended memorization in LLMs, and development-time protection of the engineering environment where training data is accessible.
LimitsThese controls reduce but rarely eliminate leakage — differential-privacy noise trades accuracy; the model remains a queryable derivative of the training data. Include model attacks in the PIA (they are one of its four listed blind spots).
StandardsGuide p. 313; OWASP LLM02; MITRE ATLAS AML.T0024 Exfiltration via ML Inference API; see AI Exchange security sections for full control mappings.

Further reading on AI privacy

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework 1.0
  • PLOT4ai threat library
  • Algorithm audit — a non-profit organisation
  • For pure security aspects: the 'Further reading on AI security' listed earlier in the guide.
◆ Senior insight

"Before you start" is the operative phrase of this whole section. Prohibited-practice screening (AI Act), risk-level classification, the fairness pre-start check from Lesson 4, and the PIA all belong before the first line of training code. Retrofitting any of them after deployment is somewhere between expensive and impossible — you cannot "patch" a banned use case.

Why does the AI Act's broad definition of AI matter to a team running "only classical statistics"?
Because the Act's definition includes wider statistical approaches and optimization algorithms — not just machine learning or deep learning. A logistic-regression job-screening tool can be a high-risk AI system under the Act, requiring risk analysis and conformity, even though nobody on the team calls it "AI".
A colleague says AI ethics questions like social justice should all go to the Data Protection Officer. What does the guide say?
No — many AI discussion topics concern human rights, social justice, and safety, of which only part is privacy. The DPO/privacy engineer should not drag everything into their responsibilities. But the organization must still assign those non-privacy AI responsibilities somewhere; they cannot fall on the floor.
Which EU AI Act obligations apply specifically to generative AI?
Generative AI must disclose which copyrighted sources were used in training and must prevent illegal content. The guide illustrates enforcement scale with a hypothetical: a violation by OpenAI could mean a fine on the order of 10 billion dollars.

Key Points

  • Principle 8: membership inference, model inversion, LLM sensitive-data disclosure, and engineering-process leakage are privacy risks whenever data is personal — controls live in the security half of the Exchange.
  • Scope boundaries: privacy professionals should not own every AI question (human rights, justice, safety), but organizations must assign those responsibilities somewhere.
  • GDPR limits AI implicitly (lawfulness, purpose limits — Art. 6); the FTC says communicate carefully and don't overpromise; the EU AI Act limits explicitly (mass surveillance, predictive policing, high-risk hiring).
  • AI Act risk levels: unacceptable (banned — manipulation, social scoring, real-time remote biometric ID), high (risk assessment + harmonized standards or Cyber Resilience Act), limited (transparency), minimal (rest).
  • Organizations must inventory AI initiatives and run high-level risk analyses; "AI" includes statistical and optimization approaches.
  • Generative AI: disclose copyrighted training sources, prevent illegal content — with fines illustrated at the 10-billion-dollar scale.
  • Further reading: NIST AI RMF 1.0, PLOT4ai threat library, Algorithm Audit.
Module 15 · References · OWASP AI Exchange

The AI Exchange Reference Shelf

~12 minCovers guide pp. 316–319

The guide closes with its references section (owaspai.org/go/references/) — the broader publications behind the Exchange: OpenCRE oversight mappings into MITRE ATLAS and NIST, team papers, the threat-overview landscape, incident databases, and training resources. Knowing this shelf is what lets you answer "says who?" for every claim in this course.

OpenCRE oversight mappings

The Exchange's own references open with two OpenCRE entries (OpenCRE is the Open Common Requirement Enumeration — a cross-standard mapping layer) that tie AI-oversight controls to their sources:

OpenCRE entryRefers to
OpenCRE: Automated AI oversightMITRE ATLAS AML.M0020: Generative AI Guardrails; NIST AI 100-2 sec. 3.4.5: An LLM moderator
OpenCRE: Human AI oversightMITRE ATLAS AML.M0030: Restrict AI Agent Tool Invocation on Untrusted Data; MITRE ATLAS AML.M0029: Human In-the-Loop for AI Agent Actions; NIST AI 100-2 sec. 3.4.5: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF)

The pattern to internalize: automated oversight = guardrails and an LLM moderator watching the model; human oversight = a human in the loop for agent actions plus restrictions on tool invocation over untrusted data, with RLHF as the training-time human-feedback channel. The guide also points to its Media page for webinars and podcasts by and about the AI Exchange; topic-specific references appear throughout the content, so this section holds the broader publications.

Journal & conference papers by the Exchange team

#TitleAuthorsVenueYearDOI
1Ransomware Attacks on AI Systems: a Cross-Domain Threat and Control AnalysisBehnaz Karimi, Yuvaraj GovindarajuluIEEE202610.1109/ISDFS69419.2026.11459071
2Lifecycle-Integrated Security for AI-Cloud Convergence in Cyber-Physical InfrastructureS M Zia Ur Rashid et al.IEEE202610.1109/ISDFS69419.2026.11458983
3Securing Agentic AI: Threats, Risks and MitigationS M Zia Ur Rashid et al.Springer202610.1007/978-981-95-3701-3_44

Overviews of AI security threats

The threat-taxonomy landscape the Exchange draws on — when you need a second opinion on whether a threat is real, these are the places to look:

  • OWASP: GenAI security project · LLM Top 10 · Agentic AI Top 10 · ML Top 10
  • ENISA: Cybersecurity threat landscape · ML threats and countermeasures 2021
  • MITRE ATLAS framework for AI threats
  • NIST: threat taxonomy · NISTIR 8269 — A Taxonomy and Terminology of Adversarial Machine Learning
  • ETSI SAI (Securing Artificial Intelligence)
  • Microsoft: AI failure modes
  • BIML: ML threat taxonomy · LLM risk analysis (registration required)
  • PLOT4ai threat library
  • BSI (Germany): AI recommendations including security aspects — available in English
  • NCSC UK / CISA Joint Guidelines — see their published mapping with the AI Exchange

Overviews of AI security/privacy incidents

When you need real-world evidence — for a risk assessment, a business case, or a post-incident comparison:

  • AVID — AI Vulnerability Database
  • Sightline — AI/ML Supply Chain Vulnerability Database
  • OECD AI Incidents Monitor (AIM)
  • AI Incident Database
  • AI Exploits by ProtectAI
🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Justifying a privacy control to management? Pull comparable real incidents from the AI Incident Database or OECD AIM.
  • Assessing a supply-chain model or package? Check Sightline and AI Exploits for known vulnerabilities first.
  • Mapping controls across frameworks? OpenCRE is the rosetta stone between the Exchange, ATLAS, and NIST documents.
  • Threat-modeling for privacy specifically? PLOT4ai is the threat library the guide names in both its further-reading and references lists.

Miscellaneous references

  • ENISA: AI security standard discussion · multilayer AI security framework
  • Alan Turing Institute's AI standards hub
  • Microsoft/MITRE tooling for ML teams
  • Google's Secure AI Framework (SAIF)
  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework 1.0
  • ISO/IEC 20547-4 Big data security · IEEE 2813 Big Data Business Security Risk Assessment
  • Awesome MLSecOps references · Awesome AI security references
  • OffSec ML Playbook
  • MIT AI Risk Repository
  • Failure Modes in Machine Learning by Microsoft

Education: learning and training

The guide's education table (starting on p. 319, first category "Courses and Labs"; the table continues beyond p. 319 — the last row's description is cut at the page boundary):

CategoryTitleDescriptionProviderContent typeLevelCost
Courses and LabsAI Security FundamentalsLearn the basic concepts of AI security, including security controls and testing procedures.MicrosoftCourseBeginnerFree
Courses and LabsRed Teaming LLM ApplicationsExplore fundamental vulnerabilities in LLM applications with hands-on lab practice.GiskardCourse + LabBeginnerFree
Courses and LabsExploring Adversarial Machine LearningDesigned for data scientists and security professionals to learn how to attack realistic ML systems.NVIDIACourse + LabIntermediatePaid
Courses and LabsOWASP LLM VulnerabilitiesEssentials of securing Large Language Models (LLMs), covering… (description continues on p. 320)CheckmarxInteractive LabBeginnerFree with OWASP Membership
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 316–319, permalink owaspai.org/go/references/. The ATLAS mitigation IDs quoted (AML.M0020, AML.M0029, AML.M0030) and NIST AI 100-2 sec. 3.4.5 are exactly as recorded in the guide's OpenCRE entries; NIST AI 100-2 is NIST's "Adversarial Machine Learning: A Taxonomy and Terminology" report series companion.

What is the difference between "Automated AI oversight" and "Human AI oversight" in the OpenCRE mappings?
Automated oversight uses machinery to watch the model: generative-AI guardrails (ATLAS AML.M0020) and an LLM moderator (NIST AI 100-2 sec. 3.4.5). Human oversight puts people in the loop: human-in-the-loop for AI agent actions (AML.M0029), restricting agent tool invocation on untrusted data (AML.M0030), and RLHF as the human-feedback training channel.
You need evidence of real AI privacy incidents for a DPIA. Which references from this lesson do you use?
The incident overviews: AVID (AI Vulnerability Database), Sightline (AI/ML supply-chain vulnerabilities), the OECD AI Incidents Monitor, the AI Incident Database, and ProtectAI's AI Exploits. PLOT4ai additionally offers a privacy-oriented threat library for the threat-modeling side.

Key Points

  • OpenCRE "Automated AI oversight" → ATLAS AML.M0020 (Generative AI Guardrails) + NIST AI 100-2 §3.4.5 (LLM moderator).
  • OpenCRE "Human AI oversight" → ATLAS AML.M0030 (restrict agent tool invocation on untrusted data), AML.M0029 (human-in-the-loop for agent actions), NIST AI 100-2 §3.4.5 (RLHF).
  • Three 2026 team publications: ransomware attacks on AI (IEEE), lifecycle-integrated AI-cloud security (IEEE), securing agentic AI (Springer).
  • Threat-overview shelf: OWASP (GenAI, LLM/Agentic/ML Top 10s), ENISA, MITRE ATLAS, NIST/NISTIR 8269, ETSI SAI, Microsoft failure modes, BIML, PLOT4ai, BSI, NCSC-UK/CISA joint guidelines.
  • Incident databases: AVID, Sightline, OECD AIM, AI Incident Database, ProtectAI AI Exploits.
  • Training on p. 319's table: Microsoft (fundamentals, free), Giskard (LLM red teaming, free), NVIDIA (adversarial ML, paid), Checkmarx (OWASP LLM vulnerabilities, free with OWASP membership).
Module 16 · §6 AI Privacy · OWASP AI Exchange

AI Privacy: Two Halves of One Discipline

~14 minCovers guide pp. 305–307

Everything you have learned so far protects the AI system. This lesson flips the lens: it protects the person whose data flows through that system. The AI Exchange draws a precise boundary — AI privacy is partly a security problem (which the rest of the guide covers) and partly a set of individual-rights obligations that no firewall can satisfy. By the end you will be able to run that split for any AI system, know which legislation and standards apply, and know exactly where a classic Privacy Impact Assessment goes blind on AI.

Why AI systems carry privacy risk beyond ordinary software

Just like any system that processes data, AI systems can have privacy risks. But the guide (permalink: owaspai.org/go/aiprivacy/) lists six concerns that are specific to AI. Learn them as a checklist — each one maps to a distinct engineering response:

  • AI systems are data-intensive. They typically present additional risks regarding data collection and retention. Personal data may be collected from various sources, each subject to different levels of sensitivity and regulatory constraints. Legislation often requires a legal basis and/or consent for collection and use, and grants individuals rights to correct, request, and remove their own data.
  • Protecting training data is hard because it lives long. Training data typically needs to be retained for long periods, since many models need to be retrained. Often the actual identities of people are irrelevant for the model — yet removing identity fields does not end the risk, because individual identities might still be deduced from the remaining data. This is where differential privacy becomes crucial: altering the data (e.g., adding noise, or using aggregation techniques) so individuals are sufficiently unrecognizable while valuable insights can still be derived.
  • Training data is accessible in the engineering environment. Conventional systems normally don't expose personal data to technical teams; AI engineering does. The engineering environment therefore needs more protection than usual.
  • ML enables unique privacy strategies. For example federated learning: splitting up the training set into different separated systems — typically aligning with separated data collection — so data never pools centrally.
  • AI systems make decisions. If those decisions are about people they may discriminate on protected attributes (e.g., gender, race), and the decisions may trigger actions that invade privacy — an ethical or legal concern. Legislation may prohibit certain decision types outright and set transparency rules and a right to object.
  • Models suffer model attacks. Attackers can extract training data from a model itself — e.g., model inversion, membership inference, and sensitive data disclosure in large language model output.
◆ Senior insight

The second and third bullets combine into an uncomfortable truth: the training set is simultaneously the longest-lived and the most widely accessible copy of personal data in the whole organisation. In a conventional system the production database is the crown jewel and engineers see anonymised fixtures. In an ML shop, the raw material sits on data scientists' workstations for years. That inversion is why the AI Exchange pushes development-environment protection so hard.

The two-part definition

The guide divides AI privacy cleanly. Memorise this split — it decides whose job each problem is:

  1. The threats to AI security and their controls (the rest of the AI Exchange), consisting of:
    • Confidentiality and integrity protection of personal data in train/test data, model input, or output — which itself breaks into: (a) 'conventional' security of personal data in transit and at rest, (b) protecting against model attacks that try to retrieve personal data (e.g., model inversion), and (c) personal data minimization / differential privacy, including minimized retention.
    • Integrity protection of model behaviour where that behaviour can hurt the privacy of individuals — for example when individuals are unlawfully discriminated against, or when model output leads to actions that invade privacy (e.g., undergoing a fraud investigation).
  2. Threats and controls that are not about security, but about further rights of the individual, as covered by privacy regulations such as the GDPR: use limitation, consent, fairness, transparency, data accuracy, and the rights of correction, objection, erasure, and request.
AI privacy = personal data protection + respect for further individual rights
Half 1 — Security of the AI system (rest of the Exchange)
Conventional data securitypersonal data in transit & at rest
Model-attack defencesinversion, membership inference, output disclosure
Minimization & differential privacyincl. minimized retention
Behaviour integrityno privacy-invading or discriminating output
Half 2 — Individual rights (this section, GDPR-style)
Use limitationpurpose specification
Fairnessno unjustified adverse effects
Transparency & accuracyexplainable, correct data
Consent & rightscorrection / objection / erasure / request
A model unfairly flags a customer for fraud investigation. Which half of AI privacy is that — security or rights?
Potentially both. If the wrong output was caused by an attacker manipulating the model (poisoning, evasion), it is a security failure — integrity of model behaviour, half 1. If it stems from an unfair training set or inaccurate personal data, it is a rights failure (fairness, accuracy), half 2. The guide explicitly notes this overlap: accuracy issues become a privacy problem when output leads to privacy-invading actions like a fraud investigation.

Legislation and standards

Privacy principles and requirements come from different legislations — e.g., GDPR (EU), LGPD (Brazil), PIPEDA (Canada) — and privacy standards: ISO 31700 (privacy by design), ISO 29100 (privacy framework), ISO 27701 (privacy information management), FIPs, and the NIST Privacy Framework. The guide is explicit about its own scope: it does not guarantee compliance with privacy legislation and is not a general privacy-engineering guide. For that, it points to work from ENISA, NIST, mplsplunk, OWASP, and OpenCRE.

The general principle for engineers is to regard personal data as 'radioactive gold'. It's valuable, but it's also something to minimize, carefully store, carefully handle, limit its usage, limit sharing, keep track of where it is, etc.
§ Source & standards

Guide pp. 305–307, permalink owaspai.org/go/aiprivacy/. For the security half, the emerging dedicated standards are ISO/IEC 27090 (guidance for addressing security threats to artificial intelligence systems) and ISO/IEC 27091 (AI privacy protection) — the AI Exchange feeds its threat/control matrix directly into the 27090 drafting process. The model attacks named here map to MITRE ATLAS AML.T0024 (Exfiltration via ML Inference API, covering inversion and membership inference) and OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure.

Assessments: where PIAs go blind on AI

Organizations often conduct Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs) — also referred to as Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) — to identify and manage privacy risks. This is a good idea for AI systems as well: a PIA evaluates data flows, use cases, and AI behaviors against applicable privacy laws and ethical standards, guides the implementation of privacy controls, and helps embed privacy-by-design so risks are minimized from the outset.

But PIAs are not per se specialized in AI systems and may overlook typical AI risks in four areas:

  • AI input attacks with privacy risks — model inversion, membership inference, or sensitive data output from the model.
  • Bias and fairness risks — systematic discrimination from training data.
  • Ongoing learning or retraining — new accuracy and bias risks can appear after deployment.
  • Explainability and accountability gaps — decisions become harder to trace back.

Because of these blind spots, dedicated AI impact assessment methods exist, and the guide names two: the AI impact assessment from the Netherlands and the UK government overview of assessment techniques.

🛡 Defender's checklist
  • Run a PIA/DPIA for every AI system that touches personal data — then extend it with the four AI blind spots above.
  • Classify every data source feeding training by sensitivity and regulatory constraint before ingestion.
  • Treat the ML engineering environment as a production-grade personal-data zone: access control, logging, minimization.
  • Schedule re-assessment on every retrain — bias and accuracy risks are not one-shot.
  • Document who owns the non-security AI concerns (safety, social justice) so they don't fall between chairs.
Name the four typical AI risks a standard PIA may overlook.
(1) AI input attacks with privacy impact — model inversion, membership inference, sensitive data in output; (2) bias and fairness risks from training data; (3) ongoing learning/retraining introducing new accuracy and bias risks after deployment; (4) explainability and accountability gaps that make decisions hard to trace.

Key Points

  • AI privacy = (1) security threats and controls protecting personal data and model-behaviour integrity + (2) non-security individual rights: use limitation, consent, fairness, transparency, accuracy, correction/objection/erasure/request.
  • Six AI-specific concerns: data intensity, long-lived hard-to-protect training data, personal data exposed in the engineering environment, unique strategies like federated learning, decisions about people, and model attacks.
  • Differential privacy (noise, aggregation) protects individuals even after identity fields are removed — because identities can be deduced from remaining data.
  • Treat personal data as 'radioactive gold': valuable, but minimize, track, and contain it.
  • Relevant law and standards: GDPR, LGPD, PIPEDA; ISO 31700, ISO 29100, ISO 27701, FIPs, NIST Privacy Framework. The guide itself does not guarantee compliance.
  • PIAs/DPIAs are necessary but blind to model attacks, bias, retraining drift, and explainability gaps — use dedicated AI impact assessments (Netherlands method, UK government overview).
Module 16 · §6 AI Privacy · OWASP AI Exchange

Privacy Principles I — Purpose, Fairness, Minimization

~17 minCovers guide pp. 308–311

The first three privacy principles are where most AI programmes actually fail: reusing data collected for one purpose to train models for another, shipping models that discriminate in ways no amount of engineering can fix, and hoarding data "because more is better". This lesson gives you the guide's hard-edged treatment — including the fairness dilemma most vendors won't say out loud: sometimes the only compliant option is to abandon the algorithm.

1. Use Limitation and Purpose Specification

The principle: you should not simply use data collected for one purpose (e.g., safety or security) as a training dataset to train your model for other purposes (e.g., profiling, personalized marketing). The guide gives two concrete traps:

  • If you collect phone numbers and other identifiers as part of your MFA flow (to improve security), that does not mean you can also use them for user targeting and other unrelated purposes.
  • You may need to collect sensitive data under KYC requirements (know-your-customer), but such data should not feed ML models used for business analytics without proper controls.

Some privacy laws require a lawful basis for processing personal data — or bases, plural, if the data serves more than one purpose (see GDPR Articles 6 and 9). There is a direct link to restrictions on what an AI application may be for: the prohibited practices in the European AI Act, such as using machine learning for individual criminal profiling, mark some purposes as too risky in potential harm and unfairness toward individuals and society.

Subtle but important: a use case may not even involve personal data and still be potentially harmful or unfair. The guide's example: an algorithm that decides who may join the army, based on the amount of weight a person can lift and how fast the person can run. This data cannot be used to reidentify individuals (with some exceptions), yet the use case may be unrightfully unfair towards gender — if, for example, the algorithm is based on an unfair training set.

In practical terms:

  • Reduce access to sensitive data and create anonymized copies for incompatible purposes (e.g., analytics).
  • Document a purpose/lawful basis before collecting the data, and communicate that purpose to the user in an appropriate way.

Two newer techniques enable use limitation:

  • Data enclaves: store pooled personal data in restricted secure environments.
  • Federated learning: decentralize ML by removing the need to pool data into a single location; the model is trained in multiple iterations at different sites.
#ALLOWEDDATAUse only data you are allowed to use for this purposedevelopment-time · data governance
IntentEnforce use limitation and purpose specification: data collected for purpose A (security, KYC) must not silently become training data for purpose B (marketing, analytics). It works because it forces a lawful-basis decision to happen before ingestion, when refusal is still cheap.
ImplementDocument a purpose and lawful basis (GDPR Art 6/9 — one basis per purpose) before collection; communicate the purpose to the user appropriately; reduce access to sensitive data; create anonymized copies for incompatible purposes; check the use case itself against prohibited practices (EU AI Act, e.g. individual criminal profiling).
LimitsPurpose checks don't catch harm in use cases that involve no personal data at all (the army-selection example) — fairness review is still needed. Anonymization is non-trivial (see WP 29 guidance) and may be reversible by deduction.
StandardsGDPR Art 6 & 9; EU AI Act prohibited practices; guide p. 308, owaspai.org/go/aiprivacy/.
#FEDERATEDLEARNINGTrain without pooling the datadevelopment-time · data protection / use limitation
IntentRemove the single central pile of personal data that makes training sets such an attractive target and such a purpose-creep risk. The model travels; the data stays where it was collected.
ImplementSplit the training set across different separated systems — typically aligned with separated data collection sites — and train the model in multiple iterations at the different sites, aggregating model updates rather than raw records.
LimitsIt is a data-location control, not a full privacy guarantee: the resulting model can still memorise and leak personal data via model attacks (inversion, membership inference), so combine with minimization and differential privacy. Each site's engineering environment still needs protection.
StandardsGuide pp. 305–308 (unique ML privacy strategies; use-limitation enablers), owaspai.org/go/aiprivacy/.
Your security team collected device fingerprints for fraud prevention. Growth wants them as features in a churn-prediction model. What is the correct sequence of questions?
First: is churn prediction a compatible purpose with the documented collection purpose (fraud prevention)? Almost certainly not — so you need a new lawful basis (GDPR Art 6) for the new purpose, or you must not use the data. If analytics must proceed, use an anonymized copy, reduce access, and document the new basis before any training run. Purpose first, technique second.

2. Fairness

Fairness means handling personal data in a way individuals expect and not using it in ways that lead to unjustified adverse effects. The algorithm should not behave in a discriminating way. And note the security connection the guide draws: accuracy issues of a model become a privacy problem if the model output leads to actions that invade privacy (e.g., undergoing a fraud investigation). Accuracy issues can be caused by a complex problem, insufficient data, mistakes in data and model engineering, and manipulation by attackers — showing the relation between model security and privacy.

GDPR's Article 5 refers to "fair processing", and the EDPS guideline defines fairness as the prevention of "unjustifiably detrimental, unlawfully discriminatory, unexpected or misleading" processing of personal data. GDPR does not specify how fairness can be measured, but the EDPS recommends three safeguard families:

  • the right to information (transparency),
  • the right to intervene (access, erasure, data portability, rectify),
  • the right to limit the processing (right not to be subject to automated decision-making, and non-discrimination).

In the literature there are different fairness metrics: group fairness, false positive error rate, unawareness, and counterfactual fairness. There is no industry standard yet on which metric to use, but you should assess fairness especially if your algorithm makes significant decisions about individuals (banning access to the platform, financial implications, denial of services or opportunities). There are also efforts to test algorithms at scale: NIST's FRVT project tests different face recognition algorithms on fairness using different metrics.

The elephant in the room

The guide is unusually blunt here. For fairness across groups (protected attributes), the elephant in the room is that in some situations a model is more accurate if it DOES discriminate against protected attributes. Certain groups have, in practice, a lower success rate in some areas because of societal aspects rooted in culture and history — some of which can be regarded as institutional discrimination, others with more practical backgrounds (the guide's example: for language reasons, new immigrants statistically tend to be hindered in getting higher education). If we want to be completely fair across groups, we must accept that in many cases this means balancing accuracy with discrimination. And if sufficient accuracy cannot be attained while staying within discrimination boundaries, there is no other option than to abandon the algorithm idea. For fraud detection, that could mean transactions get selected randomly instead of by an algorithm.

A use case may have unsolvable bias issues you can recognise before you start. Before any data analysis, ask: do any key data elements have a skewed representation of protected groups — not skewed in your training data, but in the real world (e.g., more men than women in certain types of education)? If so, bias is probably impossible to avoid, unless you can correct for the protected attributes. And if you don't have those attributes (e.g., racial data) or proxies for them, there is no way to correct. Then you face a dilemma between the benefit of an accurate model and a certain level of discrimination — a dilemma you can decide before you even start, saving a lot of trouble.

▸ Worked example

From the guide: take a dataset of students with two variables: study program and score on a math test. The goal is to let the model select students good at math for a special math program. Say the study program 'computer science' has the best-scoring students, and much more males than females study computer science. The result: the model will select more males than females. Without having gender data in the dataset, this bias is impossible to counter. Even with a diverse team, an equally distributed dataset, and no historical bias in collection — your AI may still discriminate, and there may be nothing you can do about it.

flowchart TD
  A["New ML use case with decisions about people"] --> B{"Key data elements skewed for protected groups in the real world?"}
  B -->|no| C["Proceed - assess fairness metrics during development"]
  B -->|yes| D{"Protected attributes or proxies available to correct with?"}
  D -->|yes| E["Correct for protected attributes - balance accuracy vs discrimination"]
  D -->|no| F["Dilemma: accurate model vs certain discrimination"]
  E --> G{"Sufficient accuracy within discrimination boundaries?"}
  G -->|yes| C
  G -->|no| H["Abandon the algorithm idea - e.g. random selection instead"]
  F --> H

Figure — the fairness pre-flight decision the guide recommends making before any data analysis (guide pp. 309–310).

◆ Senior insight

Junior teams treat fairness as a metric to optimise at the end. The guide's position is that it is a go/no-go gate at the start: if the real-world distribution is skewed and you cannot correct (no protected attributes, no proxies), no amount of model engineering fixes it. The senior move is to surface the accuracy-vs-discrimination dilemma to business owners before a euro is spent — and to be the person in the room willing to say "random selection beats this model".

3. Data Minimization and Storage Limitation

Minimize the amount, granularity and storage duration of personal information in your training dataset. The guide makes it concrete with seven practices:

  • Do not collect or copy unnecessary attributes to your dataset if they are irrelevant for your purpose.
  • Anonymize the data where possible — and note this is not as trivial as "removing PII"; see the WP 29 Guideline on anonymization.
  • If full anonymization is not possible, reduce granularity when you aim for aggregate insights: reduce lat/long to 2 decimal points if city-level precision suffices, remove the last octets of an IP address, round timestamps to the hour.
  • Use less data where possible: if 10k records are sufficient for an experiment, do not use 1 million.
  • Delete data as soon as it is no longer useful (data from 7 years ago may not be relevant for your model).
  • Remove links in your dataset: obfuscate user IDs, device identifiers, and other linkable attributes.
  • Minimize the number of stakeholders who access the data, on a "need to know" basis.

Privacy-preserving techniques being developed that support minimization:

  • Distributed data analysis: exchange anonymous aggregated data.
  • Secure multi-party computation: store data distributed-encrypted.

Further reading named by the guide: ICO guidance on AI and data protection, and the FPF case-law analysis on automated decision-making.

#DATAMINIMIZEShrink amount, granularity and linkability of personal datadevelopment-time · data minimization
IntentData you never hold cannot leak, be poisoned with liability, or be extracted by model attacks. Minimization cuts the blast radius of every downstream threat at once — the cheapest control per unit of risk removed.
ImplementDrop irrelevant attributes; use 10k records instead of 1M when sufficient; reduce granularity (lat/long to 2 decimals, strip IP last octets, round timestamps to the hour); remove linkable identifiers (user IDs, device IDs); restrict stakeholder access to need-to-know; support with distributed data analysis (anonymous aggregates) and secure multi-party computation (distributed-encrypted storage).
LimitsAnonymization is not just PII removal (WP 29): identities can be deduced from remaining data. Aggressive minimization can degrade model accuracy — and accuracy failures are themselves a privacy risk when decisions harm individuals.
StandardsGDPR data-minimization principle; WP 29 anonymization guideline; ICO AI guidance; FPF case-law analysis; guide pp. 310–311.
#SHORTRETAINStorage limitation — delete as soon as no longer usefuldevelopment-time · data minimization
IntentTraining data is retained long because retraining needs it — which is exactly why retention must be an explicit, bounded decision rather than a default. Old data is often no longer even useful signal (7-year-old records may be irrelevant to the model).
ImplementSet retention periods per dataset tied to documented purpose; delete or anonymize on expiry; verify deletion propagates to copies in engineering environments and experiment stores.
LimitsDeleting source data does not un-train a model that already absorbed it — erasure requests and consent withdrawal require re-training without the deleted data (see the Privacy Rights and Consent principles in the next lesson).
StandardsGDPR storage limitation; guide pp. 305, 310–311, owaspai.org/go/aiprivacy/.
#OBFUSCATETRAININGDATADifferential privacy and other data alterationdevelopment-time · data minimization / confidentiality
IntentMake individuals sufficiently unrecognizable in the training data while still allowing valuable insights to be derived — protection that survives even when the data must be kept and even against deduction from remaining fields.
ImplementDifferential privacy via adding noise or using aggregation techniques; granularity reduction; anonymized copies for secondary purposes.
LimitsA privacy/utility trade-off: more noise means weaker signal. And "removing PII" alone is not anonymization — re-identification by deduction remains possible without formal guarantees (WP 29).
StandardsGuide pp. 305, 310–311; WP 29 anonymization guideline; NIST AI RMF privacy-enhanced characteristic.
Why is "we removed the names and emails" not a sufficient anonymization claim?
Because identities can be deduced from the remaining data — combinations of attributes (location, timestamps, rare values) can re-identify individuals. The guide points to the WP 29 Guideline: anonymization is not trivially "removing PII". Differential privacy (noise, aggregation) provides protection that holds even against deduction.

Key Points

  • Use limitation: one documented lawful basis per purpose (GDPR Art 6/9); MFA phone numbers and KYC data must not silently become marketing or analytics training data.
  • The EU AI Act prohibits some purposes outright (e.g., individual criminal profiling) — check the use case before checking the data.
  • Data enclaves and federated learning are structural enablers of use limitation.
  • Fairness (GDPR Art 5, EDPS): prevent "unjustifiably detrimental, unlawfully discriminatory, unexpected or misleading" processing; metrics include group fairness, false positive error rate, unawareness, counterfactual fairness — no industry standard yet; NIST FRVT tests face recognition fairness.
  • The elephant in the room: models can be more accurate when they discriminate; fairness may force balancing accuracy against discrimination — or abandoning the algorithm (random selection for fraud detection).
  • Bias can be unsolvable: real-world skew + no protected attributes or proxies = uncorrectable (math-students example). Decide the dilemma before starting.
  • Minimize amount, granularity, and storage duration: seven concrete practices, plus distributed data analysis and secure multi-party computation.
Module 16 · §6 AI Privacy · OWASP AI Exchange

Privacy Principles II — Transparency, Rights, Accuracy, Consent & Model Attacks

~15 minCovers guide pp. 311–313

The second half of the privacy principles is operational: what you must be able to explain, what individuals can demand from you, what consent really requires (eight conditions, not a checkbox), and why the model-attack threats you studied earlier in this curriculum re-enter here wearing a privacy hat. This is also where you learn the scope discipline that keeps a data protection officer sane.

4. Transparency

Privacy standards such as FIPP (Fair Information Practice Principles) and ISO 29100 refer to maintaining privacy notices, providing a copy of the user's data upon request, and giving notice when major changes in personal data processing occur.

GDPR covers such practices too, but adds a clause specific to algorithmic decision-making: Article 22 gives individuals, under specific conditions, the right to:

  • human intervention in an algorithmic decision,
  • the ability to contest the decision, and
  • meaningful information about the logic involved (for examples of "meaningful information", see the EDPS guideline).

In the US, the Equal Credit Opportunity Act requires detailed explanations on individual decisions by algorithms that deny credit.

Transparency is not only for the end-user. Your models and datasets should be understandable by internal stakeholders as well — model developers, internal audit, privacy engineers, domain experts, and more. That typically requires four capabilities:

  • Proper model documentation: model type, intent, proposed features, feature importance, potential harm, and bias.
  • Dataset transparency: source, lawful basis, type of data, whether it was cleaned, age. Data cards are a popular industry approach here — see Google Research's paper and Meta's research.
  • Traceability: which model made that decision about an individual, and when?
  • Explainability: several methods make black-box models more explainable, including LIME, SHAP, counterfactual explanations, and Deep Taylor Decomposition (the guide also links an overview of ML interpretability and an article on the pros and cons of explainable AI).
⚠ Common misconception

"Transparency" is often read as a user-facing privacy notice and nothing more. GDPR Article 22 makes it a capability requirement: to offer human intervention and meaningful information about the logic, you need traceability (which model version decided, when) and explainability tooling in place before the first contested decision arrives. You cannot retrofit an explanation onto a decision you didn't log.

5. Privacy Rights (individual participation)

Known as "individual participation" under privacy standards, this principle lets individuals submit requests related to their personal data. The most referred rights:

  1. Right to access/portability: provide a copy of user data, preferably machine-readable. Properly anonymized data may be exempted.
  2. Right to erasure: erase user data unless an exception applies. It is also good practice to re-train your model without the deleted user's data.
  3. Right to correction: allow users to correct factually incorrect data (see accuracy below).
  4. Right to object: allow users to object to usage of their data for a specific use (e.g., model training).

6. Data accuracy

Ensure your data is correct: an algorithmic decision made on incorrect data can have severe consequences for the individual.
