A threat is a potential attack or event; it only becomes a risk when it can exploit one or more vulnerabilities in your specific system. That distinction drives everything: the AI Exchange is deliberately a catalogue of threats, and threat modeling plus risk analysis is the bridge that turns that catalogue into a short, prioritized list of risks for your architecture, context, domain and use case.

AI risks land in the same enterprise risk domains organizations already track: Strategic, Operational, Financial, Compliance, Reputation, Technology, and ESG. For example, a data-poisoning attack (OWASP LLM04, MITRE ATLAS AML.T0020) on a loan-scoring model is simultaneously an operational risk (wrong decisions), a financial risk (bad loans), and a compliance risk (unfair credit outcomes). General AI risk management is driven by AI governance (#AIPROGRAM), which covers both risks BY the AI system (fairness, safety) and risks TO it (security). Security risk assessment specifically is driven by the security management system (#SECPROGRAM), provided AI assets, AI threats and AI systems have actually been added to its repositories — a step many organizations forget.

The framework layer: ISO/IEC 27005 is the international standard for security risk management, while overall risk management frameworks are commonly based on ISO 31000 or its AI-specific counterpart ISO/IEC 23894. The process is the classic loop: identify risks, evaluate likelihood and severity (visualized on a heatmap), treat (mitigate/transfer/avoid/accept), then communicate and monitor via a Risk Register — a repository of each risk with severity, treatment plan, ownership and status — and repeat regularly and when changes warrant it.

Go deeper

  • "Why does it matter that AI risks use the same Risk Register and vocabulary as the rest of the enterprise?" — Because risk communication fails otherwise; aligning AI risk vocabulary with Enterprise Risk Management is explicitly recommended so boards and risk committees can compare AI risks against everything else they carry.
  • "Who owns AI security risk — the CISO or the AI governance function?" — Both, with a split: #AIPROGRAM owns AI risk holistically (including risks BY AI); #SECPROGRAM owns the security slice, and its asset inventory must be extended to include models, training data, and augmentation data.
  • "Give an example of a threat that is not a risk." — Model inversion against a model trained only on public, non-sensitive data: the threat exists, but there is no vulnerability-to-harm path, so no risk to treat.

What interviewers listen for

  • Crisp threat-vs-risk definition ("a threat becomes a risk when it exploits a vulnerability") rather than fuzzy synonyms.
  • Places AI risk inside existing enterprise machinery (Risk Register, heatmaps, ISO 27005/31000/23894) instead of inventing a parallel process.
  • Knows the risks-TO-AI vs risks-BY-AI distinction and which program owns which.

Model answer

The four options are mitigation, transfer, avoidance, and acceptance. Mitigation — implementing controls to reduce likelihood or impact — is the most common approach for AI security risk; e.g., enhancing data validation to counter data poisoning (LLM04, ATLAS AML.T0020). Transfer shifts the risk to a third party — via outsourcing, insurance, or using a cloud provider with robust security for training, hosting, and storage; interestingly the AI Exchange also lists transfer learning and federated learning here, since they move risk-carrying work (training on raw data) to another party. Avoidance means changing plans to eliminate the risk — e.g., deciding not to deploy an AI system on highly sensitive personal data where breach risk cannot be adequately mitigated. Acceptance means knowingly bearing the potential loss because treatment cost outweighs impact — e.g., accepting the minimal risk of model inversion in a non-sensitive application.

The senior nuance is that these are not mutually exclusive per system; they are chosen per risk, and mitigation usually ends in acceptance anyway: you implement controls to bring the risk down to an acceptable level, then formally accept the residual. The Exchange's answer to "when have you done enough?" is precise: the AI system is sufficiently secure when all identified risks can be treated — transferred, avoided, or accepted — where acceptance is sometimes possible directly and sometimes only after controls bring the risk within appetite.

I would also flag that transfer is weaker for AI than it looks: you can transfer responsibility for hosting or training to a provider, but accountability for outcomes — regulatory, reputational — largely stays with the deployer. Contracts, warranties and indemnifications (e.g., Microsoft's Copilot Copyright Commitment) shift financial exposure, not the operational harm.

Go deeper

  • "Your CFO says buying cyber insurance means the prompt-injection risk is 'handled'. Respond." — Insurance transfers financial impact only; it does nothing for reputational or safety impact and does not reduce likelihood. Treatment choice must consider all impact dimensions.
  • "How do you decide the order in which to implement mitigating controls?" — Start with the highest-risk threats and prioritize low-cost quick wins ('low-hanging fruit'); weigh implementation cost against control effectiveness and the level of the associated risk.
  • "Can acceptance be the right answer for evasion attacks?" — Yes; the Exchange explicitly notes the risk of an evasion attack may be acceptable depending on attacker motivation and impact — context decides.

What interviewers listen for

  • All four options with AI-specific (not generic IT) examples.
  • Understands residual risk: mitigation ends in acceptance of what remains.
  • Distinguishes transferring responsibility from retaining accountability.

Model answer

Threat modeling is the bridge between a generic list of threats and a set of concrete, prioritized risks for one system. It answers three key questions: (1) which threats theoretically apply to this system, (2) how could they realistically happen, and (3) what would the impact be. The output feeds risk evaluation (likelihood x impact) and treatment.

The AI Exchange operationalizes this with a threat-model one-pager: a single table of every AI threat with a "When" column (the applicability condition) and an "Impact" column. You walk each threat; the When column tells you whether it applies in theory (e.g., direct prompt injection applies only if the model is GenAI; data poisoning of your training pipeline applies only if you train or fine-tune yourself; augmentation data manipulation applies only if you insert data into model input via RAG, memory, or system prompts). If it applies, the Impact column helps decide whether the risk actually needs treatment given the level of harm in your use case. The result: "you start big, but you end up with a relatively small list of risks to focus on."

Two canonical scoping examples: you don't need model-inversion protections if the training data isn't sensitive — the Exchange author notes seeing many real deployments with protections for threats that effectively don't matter. And an agentic LLM system is in theory susceptible to indirect prompt injection (LLM01, ATLAS AML.T0051), but if your only concern is data leakage and the system has no channel to send data out (no email, no external calls, no attacker-visible output), the threat remains theoretical and the risk does not have to be treated. Threat modeling is as much about deliberately not treating risks as about finding them.

Go deeper

  • "How is this different from STRIDE on a normal web app?" — Same discipline, different catalogue: AI adds assets (model parameters, training data, augmentation data, system prompts) and attack surfaces (model use I/O, break-into-deployed-model, engineering environment, supply chain). Conventional threats still require their own conventional threat model on the runtime and development architecture.
  • "What inputs do you need before you can start?" — Architecture (who trains, who hosts, who runs), data sensitivity classification, intended use, and the action surface (what can the model trigger).
  • "How often do you redo it?" — Continuously/at change: new tools for an agent, new data sources, or a hosting change can flip a 'When' condition from No to Yes.

What interviewers listen for

  • The three threat-modeling questions stated cleanly, not just "we brainstorm attacks".
  • Uses applicability conditions ("if you train yourself...", "if the system inserts untrusted data...") to prune the threat list.
  • Comfort with explicitly leaving theoretical risks untreated — a maturity signal, not negligence.

Model answer

The tree is a small set of architecture questions whose answers gate whole threat families. Is the model GenAI (an LLM) rather than classic ML or heuristic? If yes, direct prompt injection applies when an attacker can supply input and harmful output is possible, and indirect prompt injection applies when the system inserts untrusted data into a prompt (a retrieved resume, agent-fetched web content). Who trains or fine-tunes the model? If the supplier: supply-chain model poisoning (LLM03/LLM04, ATLAS AML.T0010), addressed via supply chain management — trustworthy supplier selection, model authenticity verification — with #POISONROBUSTMODEL as a post-training fallback if you have expertise and open weights. If you: data poisoning and direct development-time model poisoning of your own pipeline. Does the system augment model input — RAG, agent memory, system prompts? If yes: augmentation data manipulation, and if that data is stored (e.g., a vector database), direct augmentation data leak — which explicitly includes sensitive system prompts (LLM07, LLM08). Who runs the model? If you: direct runtime model poisoning of the deployed artifact; if the supplier: that becomes a supply-chain assurance item instead. Is it used for classification? If yes: evasion attacks (ATLAS AML.T0043), whose risk may well be acceptable.

Then the impact-analysis layer: can the model trigger actions? Actions multiply impact — from a database write up to closing a watertight door in a ship. The critical composite is the lethal trifecta: (1) manipulable model behaviour, (2) an ability to send data out, and (3) access to sensitive data — together they yield data exfiltration, including via injected exfiltration in output (an attacker-controlled image URL carrying secrets in its parameters, XSS, or a link the user is induced to click; LLM05 Improper Output Handling).

Separate branches cover training-data leakage (disclosure in output, model inversion, direct training-data leak, and membership inference — the latter only when set membership is itself sensitive, e.g. a training set of convicted criminals), model theft when the model is IP (model exfiltration via I/O harvesting — ATLAS AML.T0024 — plus dev-time and runtime model leaks and source/config leaks), input-data leakage when input is sensitive (remembering RAG-retrieved data counts as input), and two always-do items: output-contains-conventional-injection whenever the model emits text consumed downstream, and AI resource exhaustion / unbounded consumption (LLM10, ATLAS AML.T0029/AML.T0034) — always a possibility.

Go deeper

  • "Which single architecture change most reduces the threat list?" — Not training/fine-tuning yourself and using a supplier-run model removes the development-environment and runtime-model-poisoning families from your direct responsibility, converting them into supply-chain assurance obligations — smaller list, but a harder verification problem.
  • "Why do system prompts appear in the augmentation-data branch?" — They are automatically inserted input; if sensitive, their storage creates a direct augmentation data leak risk (LLM07 System Prompt Leakage), and they participate in behaviour manipulation.
  • "Where do conventional threats fit?" — Both the runtime architecture and the development environment still require their own conventional threat modeling; AI-specific modeling is additive, not a replacement.

What interviewers listen for

  • Organizes threats by gating question (GenAI? who trains? who runs? augmentation? classification?) rather than reciting a flat list.
  • Names the lethal trifecta and its three components precisely.
  • Cross-references OWASP LLM Top 10 and ATLAS technique IDs naturally, without forcing them.

Model answer

With any ready-made model, the provider did the training and fine-tuning, so they own development-time security: alignment, poisoning/evasion/disclosure countermeasures, development environment security, and their own supply chain. Your lever over that entire zone is supply chain management — supplier selection, model authenticity verification, and assurance — plus your own AI governance. That much is common to both deployment models.

Self-hosted (open weights, on-prem or VPC): the model artifact lives in your infrastructure, so you inherit all runtime threats, including direct runtime model poisoning of the deployed model — a threat that simply does not exist on your side in the hosted case. You also own runtime security controls, monitoring/rate-limiting/access control, augmentation-data protection, and data minimization. The payoff is confidentiality: sensitive input never leaves your environment. The catch-22 the Exchange notes: the self-hostable model typically won't be as good as the frontier hosted one.

External-hosted: your input and output cross the trust boundary in clear text — a provider-hosted model must read your data to process it, so your sensitive data exists unencrypted outside your infrastructure (same as any multi-tenant SaaS, e.g. hosted office suites). Security now largely depends on the supplier's data handling, so supply chain management extends to cover the hosting, and I would ask four questions: (1) Where does the model run? A "private instance" may mean a private API endpoint, not a private model — if it runs on the vendor's cluster, data leaves you in clear text. (2) What are the data retention rules? Including court-ordered retention — a US court actually required OpenAI to retain logs for litigation for a period. (3) What exactly is logged and monitored, by humans or algorithms, how is that monitoring infrastructure protected, and can logging be disabled (often only on specific licenses)? (4) Is input used for training? Usually not, and secret misuse would likely be detectable — but verify contractually. And weigh the risk fairly: a major provider may protect that environment better than you can protect your own.

Go deeper

  • "You still deploy input filtering and monitoring on your side of a hosted model — why, if the provider has them?" — The threat maps show 'additional' monitor/rate-limit/access-control and specialized input filtering in your runtime: provider controls serve the provider's risk appetite and all tenants, not your use-case-specific harm model.
  • "How do you formalize the split?" — A responsibility matrix per component (model, model extensions, application, infrastructure), agreed with the provider; for each threat that is theirs, attain assurance (audits, assessments) — that's the 'verify external responsibilities' step.
  • "The provider won't disclose how a risk is mitigated. Options?" — Exactly three: accept the risk, compensate with your own mitigations, or avoid by not engaging. Providers are typically not very open about countermeasures, which makes assurance genuinely hard; regulation should improve transparency.

What interviewers listen for

  • Identifies the one threat that moves: direct runtime model poisoning exists for you only when you host the artifact.
  • Knows the clear-text-input reality of hosted models and discusses it without either panic or dismissal — including the "vendor may protect it better than you" counterweight.
  • Asks the provider concrete, contract-testable questions (retention, logging opt-out, training use, where the model runs) instead of "is it secure?".

Model answer

I'd sort them by function. Management systems: ISO/IEC 42001 is the AI management system standard (the "ISO 27001 of AI") — organizational governance of AI, certifiable; it pairs with your existing ISMS rather than replacing it. Risk management: ISO 31000 is the generic risk framework, ISO/IEC 23894 its AI-specific application (covering risks by and to AI), and ISO/IEC 27005 the security-risk-management standard — the one the AI Exchange names for security risk assessment specifically. Lifecycle: ISO/IEC 5338 defines AI system life cycle processes — where development-time security controls attach. Security/privacy guidance for AI specifically: ISO/IEC 27090 is the emerging guidance for addressing security threats to AI systems, and ISO/IEC 27091 its privacy counterpart. These are the standards that will carry AI-security control detail into the ISO 27000 world.

NIST AI RMF 1.0 is the US voluntary framework: four functions — Govern, Map, Measure, Manage — spanning all trustworthiness characteristics (secure & resilient is one of seven, alongside valid & reliable, safe, accountable & transparent, explainable, privacy-enhanced, and fair). It's broader than security and strong on organizational process; NIST's Generative AI Profile (AI 600-1) specializes it for GenAI. In practice AI RMF answers "how do we govern and assess", while the AI Exchange and 27090-class material answer "which threats and which controls".

The OWASP AI Exchange positions itself as the open, single, comprehensive threats-and-controls body of knowledge that feeds standardization — it was created to align industry input into ISO/IEC 27090/27091 and EU AI Act security requirements (via the CEN/CENELEC process), so practitioners get one coherent control catalogue instead of divergent standards. Its controls also map outward: the Exchange publishes an explicit mapping to the UK NCSC / CISA Joint Guidelines for Secure AI System Development and the UK DSIT Code of Practice (13 principles), showing per-phase (secure design / development / deployment / operation) which #CONTROLS satisfy which principle. That mapping style is how I'd avoid duplicate compliance work across frameworks.

Go deeper

  • "We're ISO 27001 certified. What's the delta to be credible on AI security?" — Extend existing ISMS repositories with AI assets/threats (#SECPROGRAM), add AI governance (#AIPROGRAM), use 23894/27005 for the risk process, and adopt AI-specific controls from the Exchange/27090 drafts; consider 42001 if AI governance needs certifiable structure.
  • "NIST AI RMF vs ISO 42001 — pick one?" — Not either/or: RMF is a free framework good for structuring the program and language; 42001 is certifiable and contractually demandable. Many orgs run RMF functions inside a 42001-shaped management system.
  • "Why does a security engineer care about the DSIT Code of Practice principles 10 and 13?" — They add post-market duties the joint guidelines lacked: end-user/affected-entity communication and proper data and model disposal — both operational security obligations.

What interviewers listen for

  • Sorts standards by function (management system / risk / lifecycle / control guidance) rather than listing acronyms.
  • Knows 27090 = AI security guidance and 27091 = AI privacy, and that they're the ISO landing zone for AI-security controls.
  • Understands the Exchange's role as alignment input to ISO and the AI Act — i.e., why one open catalogue exists.

Model answer

Likelihood estimation for AI risks needs both technical and contextual understanding. The factor set the AI Exchange assembles comes from three sources: opportunity regarding attacker access (OWASP risk rating, FAIR), risk of getting caught (FAIR), attacker capabilities, tools and budget (ISO/IEC 27005, OWASP, FAIR), susceptibility of the system (ISO/IEC 27005, FAIR), motive (all three), number of potential attackers (OWASP), and incident/attempt data (ISO/IEC 27005). Technical drivers include algorithm complexity, data quality and sources, and existing conventional controls — e.g., a system trained on public data is inherently more exposed to poisoning and inference attacks, like a loan-scoring model consuming public credit data.

The motivation analysis is the heart of it: what does an attacker gain by misleading your model? Claim to fame, disgruntled employee, competitor? The Exchange's contrast pair: adding words to a spam email so it evades a spam classifier — evasion is clearly interesting and feasible. Versus a skin-disease diagnosis from a photo — the patient has no interest in a wrong diagnosis and little control... until context changes: if a faked diagnosis makes them eligible for compensation (say, disease allegedly caused by restaurant food), the theoretical threat becomes real. Whether a theoretical threat is a real threat depends entirely on context, and that judgment is what you're being paid for at senior level.

The output feeds two decisions: some threats get accepted as risk given probability, impact and policy; for those not accepted, the level of risk sets the required strength of controls — if data poisoning could yield substantial benefit to a group of attackers, training data gets a high protection level. Impact evaluation runs in parallel: direct consequences (integrity compromise, downtime) plus indirect ones (reputation, regulatory penalties), often magnified by AI's scale and criticality — a healthcare-diagnostics compromise means misdiagnosis, patient harm, and legal/trust fallout. Likelihood x impact then lands each risk on the heatmap for prioritization.

Go deeper

  • "How does FAIR differ from an OWASP-style rating here?" — FAIR is quantitative (loss event frequency x loss magnitude, factoring contact frequency, probability of action, resistance strength); OWASP risk rating is a structured qualitative scoring. For board-level AI risk decisions, FAIR's monetary framing communicates better; for triage, qualitative is faster.
  • "What's genuinely harder about likelihood for AI threats than for web vulns?" — Sparse incident data (the ISO 27005 'incidents and attempts' factor is thin), fast-moving attack techniques, and attack success being probabilistic (prompt injection success rates rather than deterministic exploits).
  • "Your heatmap says medium/medium for indirect prompt injection — what could move it?" — Adding tools/actions to the agent (impact up), exposure to untrusted content sources (likelihood up), or removing exfiltration channels (impact down). Likelihood/impact are functions of architecture, so re-score at every architecture change.

What interviewers listen for

  • Names factor sources (FAIR, OWASP, ISO 27005) and what each contributes, not just "we guess high/medium/low".
  • Leads with attacker motivation and can argue both directions of the evasion example, including the context flip.
  • Connects risk level to control strength — risk analysis as a dial for control investment, not a paperwork exercise.

Model answer

The AI Act (in force August 2024, phased application through 2025-2027) is risk-tiered. Unacceptable risk practices are prohibited outright — the AI Exchange's own example is social scoring; others include manipulative techniques and certain biometric uses; prohibitions carry the top fine tier (up to 35M EUR or 7% of global turnover). High-risk systems (Annex III areas like credit, employment, essential services, plus regulated products) carry the heavy obligations. Limited-risk systems get transparency duties (disclose AI interaction, label synthetic content), and minimal-risk systems are essentially unregulated. General-purpose AI models have their own track: documentation and copyright/training-data transparency for all GPAI providers, plus systemic-risk obligations (evaluations, adversarial testing, incident reporting, cybersecurity) above the compute threshold.

For a security team on a high-risk deployment, the operative articles are: Article 9 — a continuous, documented risk management system across the lifecycle (this is where the AI Exchange's risk-analysis process slots in directly); Article 10 — data governance for training/validation/test data (your anti-poisoning and data-quality controls become compliance evidence); Article 12 — logging/record-keeping (aligns with #MONITOR USE and traceability for incident reconstruction); Article 14 — human oversight (#OVERSIGHT, #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE); and Article 15 — accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity, which explicitly requires resilience against attempts to exploit AI-specific vulnerabilities, naming data poisoning, model poisoning, adversarial examples/evasion, and confidentiality attacks. Article 15 is effectively AI-security-by-law: the AI Exchange control catalogue is a ready implementation path, which is deliberate — the Exchange feeds the European standardization work (CEN/CENELEC) that will define the harmonized standards giving presumption of conformity.

Organizationally this lands in two controls: #AIPROGRAM must include impact analysis of each AI initiative — checking whether the application type is even permitted (prohibited-use screening), whether required transparency and privacy rights can be delivered — and #CHECKCOMPLIANCE keeps the obligations register current. A key role distinction: obligations differ for providers (who build/place on market) versus deployers (who use); if you fine-tune or substantially modify or white-label a GPAI system, you can slide into provider obligations without noticing.

Go deeper

  • "We fine-tune a foundation model and ship it under our brand for CV screening. Provider or deployer?" — CV screening is Annex III high-risk (employment); substantial modification/own-branding can make you the provider, inheriting conformity assessment, technical documentation, and Article 15 duties. This is the trap answer the question is fishing for.
  • "How do you evidence Article 15 compliance?" — Documented threat model and risk assessment, control mapping (Exchange/27090-style), adversarial testing results, monitoring and incident response records; align to harmonized standards as they land for presumption of conformity.
  • "Does the AI Act replace GDPR obligations?" — No, they stack: AI Act governs the system's placement and use; GDPR governs the personal data processing inside it — one system can trigger DPIA and AI Act conformity assessment simultaneously.

What interviewers listen for

  • Accurate tier model and the social-scoring prohibition example; no conflation of "high-risk" with "prohibited".
  • Cites Article 15 as the AI-security article and names the attack classes it covers.
  • Provider-vs-deployer role awareness, and the fine-tuning/white-labeling reclassification risk.

Model answer

OpenCRE — the Open Common Requirement Enumeration, from the OWASP Integration Standards project — is a graph of "common requirements": abstract, standard-neutral security requirements, each linked to the concrete places where different standards express it (ISO/IEC 27001/27002, NIST SSDF and 800-53, OWASP ASVS, SAMM, Top 10, CWE/CAPEC, PCI DSS, and others). Instead of maintaining N-squared pairwise mappings between standards, every standard maps once into the CRE layer, and cross-walks fall out for free. The AI Exchange plugs into this: its #ALLCAPS controls link to OpenCRE entries, which is how a control like #MONITOR USE or #DEV SECURITY connects back to your existing ISO 27002 control set and evidence base.

Practically, I use it in three ways. First, deduplication: when the AI Act, ISO 42001, a customer questionnaire, and internal policy all demand "logging and monitoring of the AI system", OpenCRE lets me implement once, evidence once, and map the single implementation to every framework's clause — the same philosophy as the Exchange's own NCSC/CISA and DSIT mapping tables. Second, gap analysis: starting from our ISO 27001 statement of applicability, follow CRE links to see which AI Exchange controls have no conventional counterpart (the genuinely new work: e.g., prompt injection I/O handling, unwanted-bias testing, model-poisoning robustness) versus which are extensions of controls we already run (access control, supply chain, dev security). Third, audit communication: auditors think in their framework's numbering; CRE links let me present the same control evidence in ISO language to one auditor and NIST language to another.

The strategic point: AI security governance fails most often not from missing controls but from parallel, inconsistent bookkeeping of the same controls. A requirements-graph approach — OpenCRE plus the Exchange's explicit standard mappings — keeps one source of truth while regulations multiply.

Go deeper

  • "What's the limitation of mapping-based compliance?" — Mappings assert topical equivalence, not implementation sufficiency: satisfying an ISO 27002 logging control doesn't automatically satisfy AI Act Article 12's specific record-keeping content. Mappings scope the work; an analyst still judges depth.
  • "Which AI Exchange controls will map cleanly to existing ISO 27002 controls, and which won't?" — Clean: #DEV SECURITY, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL, #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE, #MONITOR USE (extensions of classic controls to new assets). Poor fit: data-science controls like #TRAIN ADVERSARIAL, #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION, #EVASION ROBUST MODEL — no conventional counterpart exists; that's exactly the gap ISO/IEC 27090 is meant to fill.
  • "How does this help with the periodic table of AI security?" — The periodic table organizes threats/controls by asset, impact and attack surface with deep links; combined with CRE-style outward links you get threat → Exchange control → your framework clause traceability end to end.

What interviewers listen for

  • Explains the hub-and-spoke idea (map once to common requirements) versus pairwise mappings.
  • Concrete uses: dedupe evidence, find genuinely-new AI controls, speak each auditor's dialect.
  • Healthy skepticism: mappings scope work, they don't certify sufficiency.

Model answer

Control selection and tuning: for each threat that's relevant and yours, review both the threat-specific controls and the general controls that apply universally (the periodic table gives the threat-to-control mapping). Each control is a cost/effectiveness/risk-level judgment — start with the highest-risk threats and the low-hanging fruit. Crucially, AI controls have quality parameters: how much noise to add to training data, anomaly-detection thresholds, filter strictness. These are tuned in a simulation environment to find the balance between security effect and functional performance, and the tuning is continuous — fed by both simulated tests and production feedback. A poorly tuned control (e.g., an over-aggressive input filter) can be worse than none, because it creates false confidence while degrading the product.

Responsibility and verification: for each selected threat, determine who is responsible — by default the organization that builds and deploys, but hosting, model provision and cloud infrastructure split that. Shared responsibilities get pinned down with a responsibility matrix across components (model, model extensions, application, infrastructure). For threats owned by external parties, you must attain assurance that they handle the linked controls — regular audits and assessments of third-party security measures. Where a party won't disclose how a risk is mitigated: request it, and if it stays unclear you have exactly three options — accept, compensate with your own mitigations, or avoid the party entirely.

Residual risk acceptance: in the end you must be able to accept the risk remaining for each threat, given the controls implemented — per-threat, explicit, owned by someone with authority over the consequences, recorded in the Risk Register with severity, treatment plan, ownership and status. Then the loop continues: ongoing control management under #SECPROGRAM (monitoring, documentation, reporting, incident response) and continuous risk assessment — updating strategies from evolving threats and incident-response feedback. Acceptance of an AI risk is a dated decision about a moving target, so it needs review triggers (model swap, new tools, new data source), not just an annual calendar.

Go deeper

  • "Who can accept residual AI risk?" — The risk owner in the register — someone accountable for the business impact, not the engineer who built the mitigation; for AI Act high-risk systems the acceptance rationale becomes part of the Article 9 documentation.
  • "Give an example of a control quality parameter trading security against utility." — Noise added to training data (#TRAIN DATA DISTORTION / differential-privacy style): more noise, less inversion/membership-inference risk, lower model accuracy. The right point depends on data sensitivity — a risk-analysis output, not a data-science preference.
  • "How is 'verify external responsibilities' different from vendor due diligence at procurement?" — It's continuous and threat-linked: for each externally-owned threat you track the specific linked controls and re-verify (audits, SOC 2/pen-test evidence, contractual attestations), not a one-time onboarding checklist.

What interviewers listen for

  • Treats controls as tunable systems with parameters and a simulation/production feedback loop, not checkboxes.
  • Per-threat residual acceptance with named ownership — and the accept/compensate/avoid trichotomy for opaque vendors.
  • Review triggers tied to architecture change, not just periodic review.

Model answer

#AIPROGRAM is the governance control that makes the organization take responsibility for AI: all initiatives known and under control, including their security. One could argue it's out of cybersecurity scope, but it's the control that initiates getting in control of AI security — you can't secure an inventory you don't have. Its components: an inventory of AI initiatives; impact analysis per initiative; organizing AI innovation; including AI risks in enterprise risk management; assigning responsibilities (model accountability, data accountability, risk governance); AI literacy/training; organizing compliance; and incorporating AI assets into the security program. The good news for adoption: most of this extends existing machinery — policies, risk analysis, impact analysis, service inventories — rather than building from scratch.

The impact-analysis checklist is where governance earns its keep, and it's notable how many questions precede security: Is the application type even lawful (e.g., social scoring is prohibited under the EU AI Act — see #CHECKCOMPLIANCE)? Can required transparency into how the AI works be provided? Can privacy rights be honored (access, erasure, correction, objection)? Can unwanted bias against protected groups be sufficiently mitigated? Is AI really needed to solve this problem? Is the right expertise available? Is the data allowed for this purpose — especially personal data collected for a different purpose? And can unwanted behaviour be sufficiently contained by mitigations? That last one connects directly to the empathy principle: if people can't be adequately protected, the answer may be to reject or reshape the idea, not to bolt on controls.

The program must cover both directions of risk: risks TO AI (security — the Exchange's core) and risks BY AI (fairness, safety, societal harm). The security team owns the first and is a stakeholder in the second — e.g., unexplained bias detection can be the first signal of a data-poisoning attack, so the fairness monitoring and security monitoring pipelines should talk to each other.

Go deeper

  • "What's a realistic first iteration for an org with zero AI governance?" — Board-level awareness, then a basic inventory and lightweight impact-analysis gate on new initiatives; don't start with a 40-page policy nobody reads.
  • "How do you find the AI initiatives you don't know about?" — Procurement and expense data (API subscriptions), network egress to known AI endpoints, developer dependency scanning, and making the sanctioned path attractive enough that teams self-register — the shadow-AI lesson.
  • "Where does #AIPROGRAM end and #SECPROGRAM begin?" — #AIPROGRAM governs AI holistically and ensures AI is in scope of risk management; #SECPROGRAM runs security management (risk assessment per ISO 27005, control operation, incident response) over the AI assets #AIPROGRAM surfaces.

What interviewers listen for

  • Inventory-first thinking: governance as the precondition for security coverage.
  • Quotes non-security gates ("is AI really needed?", purpose limitation, bias mitigation) — shows they see security inside a larger duty of care.
  • The bias-detection-as-poisoning-signal crossover between responsible AI and security monitoring.

Model answer

If you don't train or fine-tune, the supplier is responsible for unwanted content in the training data — poisoned data, confidential data, copyrighted data — "but not accountable per se": accountability follows contracts and deployment context, so you must check licenses, warranties and contracts for these matters, or consciously accept the risk. This is the sharpest formulation of the ready-made-model dilemma: the risk surface was created upstream, but a large share of the consequences land downstream on you.

On the security side, the residual duties are: supply chain management (supplier trustworthiness, model authenticity verification, assurance on the provider's development security — hard, because providers are typically not very open about countermeasures); mitigation of what leaks through, e.g. sensitive-data disclosure in output via #SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING (LLM02); and, if poisoning risk remains unacceptable and you have open weights plus expertise, post-training countermeasures like #POISONROBUSTMODEL. GenAI raises the stakes structurally: enormous internet-sourced training sets make data poisoning easier (LLM04 — attackers can even hijack expired domains cited in datasets), and widespread pretraining makes supply-chain model poisoning (LLM03, ATLAS AML.T0010) a first-class risk.

On the legal side: training-data copyright litigation is live (Getty Images v. Stability AI; the artists' class action against Stability AI/Midjourney/DeviantArt; the GitHub Copilot case over generated code), AI-generated output currently lacks US copyright protection, and fair-use boundaries for training remain unsettled. Vendors have responded with conditional indemnifications — Microsoft's Copilot Copyright Commitment and Google Cloud's generative AI indemnification — but these require you to use the vendor's content filters and safety systems, which turns a legal shield into a technical configuration requirement the security team must implement and evidence. Mitigation strategy is layered: output-side controls against regurgitation, IP audits, ethical data-sourcing policy, defined AI-content ownership, employee training, compliance monitoring, an infringement response plan, and supplier licenses/warranties negotiated with counsel.

Go deeper

  • "Do generative models actually copy training works?" — Essentially no: they lack capacity to store the corpus; they extract patterns and generate anew, and overlaps are more coincidence than recall — the Exchange's musician/chord-sequence analogy. But memorization of duplicated or unique items does occur, which is why output-side controls still matter.
  • "A vendor indemnification exists — do we still treat the risk?" — Yes: it's a transfer of legal damages only, conditional on your correct configuration, and it does nothing for reputational harm or injunctive outcomes. Treat it as one layer, verify its preconditions continuously.
  • "How would regulation change this picture?" — AI Act GPAI transparency duties (training-data summaries, copyright policy) should improve the supplier-assurance problem the Exchange complains about; the hope stated in the Exchange is that regulation forces provider transparency.

What interviewers listen for

  • The responsible-vs-accountable distinction for supplier training data, and the contract/license checking that follows from it.
  • Sees indemnification preconditions as a security-team configuration obligation, not just a legal footnote.
  • Balanced view on model memorization — neither "models are plagiarism machines" nor "no risk at all".

Model answer

Threat model first. Running the decision tree: it's GenAI, so direct prompt injection applies; it inserts untrusted web content into prompts, so indirect prompt injection (LLM01, ATLAS AML.T0051) is the headline threat; it augments input, so augmentation-data manipulation and — if we store retrieved/user content in a vector DB — direct augmentation data leak (LLM08) apply; RAG-retrieved content and user prompts are input data, so input-data leak applies if hosted externally. Then the impact analysis flags the worst-case composite immediately: this system has all three legs of the lethal trifecta — manipulable behaviour (untrusted pages in prompts), an exfiltration channel (email, plus rendered output that can carry attacker-URL images or links), and access to sensitive data (the user's context and retrieval corpus). Impact: data exfiltration. That combination, not any single threat, is what the design must break.

Design: break the trifecta, then layer. The highest-leverage moves are architectural, not filter-based. (1) Constrain the exfiltration leg: email sending requires human confirmation showing the full recipient and content (#OVERSIGHT / human-in-the-loop for consequential actions — this is also the LLM06 Excessive Agency mitigation); restrict recipients to the user's own address or an allowlist; render output with strict sanitization and a CSP that blocks remote image loads, killing URL-parameter exfiltration (#ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT, LLM05). (2) Constrain the sensitive-data leg: least model privilege (#LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE) — the browsing/summarization step runs in a context without access to user secrets; retrieval respects the original access rights of documents per user. (3) Only then the probabilistic layer on the behaviour leg: input segregation marking untrusted web content as data-not-instructions (#INPUT SEGREGATION), prompt injection I/O detection (#PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING), and #MONITOR USE with rate limiting for campaign detection. Add #DOS INPUT VALIDATION and #LIMIT RESOURCES for unbounded consumption (LLM10), and conventional appsec on the whole pipeline.

Risk framing for sign-off. Detection-based injection controls are not watertight — the residual risk statement must say so. With confirmation-gated email and blocked remote rendering, the exfiltration impact drops from "silent bulk theft" to "user-assisted, rate-limited leakage", which is plausibly acceptable; likelihood factors (public-facing, many potential attackers, low cost to attempt, attacker motive: harvesting user data) stay high, so I spend on impact reduction rather than chasing likelihood to zero. Each risk lands in the register with an owner; the acceptance is re-triggered by any new tool, new data scope, or removal of the confirmation gate — the exact changes that would re-arm the trifecta.

Go deeper

  • "Product wants to remove the email confirmation for 'trusted' workflows." — That reinstates the trifecta; counter with scoped alternatives: pre-approved templates, recipient allowlists, per-session send quotas, anomaly-gated confirmation — and re-run the risk assessment as a formal change, since the prior acceptance is void.
  • "How do you test the defense?" — Adversarial testing with planted injection payloads on controlled web pages (canary exfiltration tokens), continuous red-team suites in CI, and production monitoring for injection markers and unusual send patterns; measure end-to-end exfiltration success, not filter-catch rates.
  • "Is this system high-risk under the EU AI Act?" — A generic consumer assistant likely isn't Annex III high-risk, but transparency duties (disclosing AI interaction) apply, and if it's repurposed into e.g. employment or credit contexts the classification — and the whole obligation set — changes.

What interviewers listen for

  • Names the lethal trifecta unprompted and designs to break a leg architecturally rather than stacking filters.
  • Deterministic controls (confirmation gates, CSP, least privilege, access-right-preserving retrieval) prioritized over probabilistic detection — with an honest residual-risk statement.
  • Ties acceptance to change triggers: knows which product decisions silently re-open the risk.

Model answer

Frame the irreducible fact first: a provider-hosted model must process our input in clear text — prompts, and everything RAG injects into them (which for us means clinical documents and company secrets), will exist unencrypted in the provider's infrastructure. That is not a control failure to be fixed; it is the same trust posture as hosted office suites, and the committee must decide on it explicitly. The honest comparison also cuts the other way: a frontier provider may protect that environment better than we protect our own. The alternative — self-hosting a smaller open-weights model — keeps data in-house but sacrifices capability: the catch-22 the risk committee, not engineering, should own.

Threat-driven treatment plan. Input data leak is the primary risk: mitigate via data minimization before send (#DATA MINIMIZE, de-identification of patient-level data), an enterprise license with logging/monitoring disabled or minimized and zero training-on-input, short retention (#SHORT RETAIN) — and record the residual that no contract removes: the provider's government can compel retention of input/output for subpoenas; a US court has already forced one major provider to retain logs for litigation. That residual is accepted or the use case is scoped down (avoidance for the most sensitive data classes). Disclosure-in-output and access-control risks: RAG will not respect the original access rights of source documents unless we build per-user retrieval filtering — a design requirement, not a nice-to-have. Supply-chain risks (LLM03): model and hosting go into supply chain management; run the four provider questions (where does the model run, retention rules, exact logging/monitoring, training on input) into contract schedules. Then the responsibility matrix per component — model and hosting: provider (we verify via audits/assurance reports); application, augmentation data, oversight: ours. Where the provider stays opaque on a control, the recorded options are exactly: accept, compensate ourselves, or avoid.

Compliance and governance wrapper. GDPR/HIPAA-equivalent duties on the personal data (DPIA, processing agreements, purpose-limitation check — was this data collected for this purpose?); EU AI Act classification per use case (clinical decision support can be high-risk, triggering Art. 9 risk management, Art. 12 logging, Art. 14 oversight, Art. 15 cybersecurity); everything lands in the Risk Register with named owners, and #CHECKCOMPLIANCE tracks the moving regulatory floor. Finally, I'd pair approval with an anti-shadow-AI move: the sanctioned, well-configured service must be good enough that scientists stop pasting data into free public chatbots — the secure alternative is a security control.

Go deeper

  • "Legal says the provider's contract forbids audits. Now what?" — Fall back on independent assurance (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001/42001 certificates, pen-test attestations), contractual security schedules with breach-notification SLAs; if assurance stays insufficient for the data class, that's a scoping decision: lower-sensitivity data only, or avoid.
  • "Would a VPC deployment of the provider's model change your analysis?" — Materially, if the model truly runs in our VPC — but verify the 'private instance' claim: it may cover only the API endpoint while inference runs on the vendor's cluster. This distinction is question 1 of the four for a reason.
  • "What monitoring do you stand up on our side regardless?" — Egress DLP on prompt traffic, #MONITOR USE on usage patterns and output, inventory of which teams/apps use the service, and periodic re-verification that logging opt-outs are still configured after every license or API change.

What interviewers listen for

  • Leads with the clear-text reality and frames it as an explicit risk-appetite decision with a fair comparison, not FUD.
  • Concrete treatment per threat plus a responsibility matrix and the accept/compensate/avoid fallback for opacity.
  • Knows the subpoena/court-ordered-retention residual, the RAG access-rights trap, and positions the sanctioned service as the shadow-AI countermeasure.

Model answer

This is shadow AI, and the first staff-level judgment is diagnostic: it's a demand signal, not primarily a policy violation. Employees can reach dozens of capable free models, and they will be strongly tempted when no better alternative exists. Pure blocking fails predictably — it pushes usage to personal devices where you lose all visibility, and it taxes productivity, which erodes security's credibility. The Exchange's position is blunt: the best solution is a good alternative — an AI service deployed and configured in a secure, privacy-preserving way, of sufficient quality, compliant with the organization's values and policies — combined with making the risks of shadow AI very clear to users.

Immediate incident handling: triage what left (data classes, volumes, which services), assess exposure using each service's actual terms — consumer tiers often retain and may train on input, unlike enterprise tiers — trigger any notification duties (personal data → privacy office/regulators; client data → contractual notifications), and treat pasted credentials/secrets as compromised. Handle the people side as education, not punishment, or reporting stops and you lose your detection signal.

Structural response through #AIPROGRAM: (1) Stand up or accelerate the sanctioned alternative with an enterprise license — no-training-on-input, logging controls, SSO, DLP integration — and make it genuinely good, because a bad sanctioned tool recreates the incident. (2) Build the AI usage inventory the incident proved missing: expense/procurement data, egress analytics to known AI endpoints, self-registration. (3) Issue a short, usable AI acceptable-use policy: which data classes may go to which tier of service — decision-table, not essay. (4) AI literacy training using this incident (anonymized) as the case study; #SEC EDUCATE. (5) Register the risk formally: "sensitive data disclosure via unsanctioned GenAI" with likelihood evidence from your own DLP data, owner, and treatment status — this converts an embarrassing incident into the governance mandate and budget. (6) Keep proportionate technical guardrails: DLP on egress, coaching prompts that redirect to the sanctioned tool at the moment of use — nudge-plus-alternative outperforms wall-plus-workaround. Success metric: migration rate to the sanctioned service and declining DLP hits, not tickets closed.

Go deeper

  • "The board asks: was any pasted data used to train the public models?" — Honest answer: for major providers, consumer-tier terms may permit training use, enterprise tiers generally don't; secret misuse at scale would likely surface, but you cannot prove a negative — which is precisely why the treatment is prevention plus a sanctioned path, not forensics.
  • "How do you set the policy line for what may go to the sanctioned tool?" — Data classification mapped to the tool's contractual guarantees and configuration (retention, logging, region); the four provider questions define the ceiling of what the tool may receive.
  • "Six months later usage of the sanctioned tool is low. What went wrong?" — Almost always quality or friction: wrong model tier, no access to internal context, painful login. Treat adoption as the control's effectiveness metric — an unused control mitigates nothing.

What interviewers listen for

  • Reads shadow AI as unmet demand and leads with the secure-alternative strategy rather than prohibition.
  • Runs a real incident process (exposure assessment against actual service terms, notification duties) before the governance build-out.
  • Defines success as adoption and declining incidents — control effectiveness thinking at organizational scale.

How to approach it

Reason asset → threat → control → residual risk, out loud, before drawing anything. Assets: the base model, the system prompt, the retrieval index, per-user conversation memory, any downstream tools, and the user's session/identity. The central architectural fact is that fetched web content is untrusted data that enters the same context window as trusted instructions. That single sentence generates the whole threat model: this is indirect prompt injection (OWASP LLM01, MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051), and the browsing feature is the injection channel. A strong candidate names a trust boundary between "content" and "instructions" and designs around the fact that the model cannot reliably tell them apart.

A strong answer

I lay out a layered design. Input side: treat fetched pages as data, never instructions — wrap them with unambiguous delimiters and an explicit provenance tag (#INPUTSEGREGATION), and keep the retrieved text out of the instruction region of the prompt. I run injection detection on retrieved content (#MODELINPUTMONITORING / a lightweight classifier for imperative phrasing, "ignore previous", tool-call-shaped text, invisible unicode, and markdown/image exfil patterns), but I treat detection as defense-in-depth, not a gate, because it is bypassable.

Privilege side — this is where the real risk lives: the browsing tool runs with the identity of the assistant, not the user, and has no write capability, no access to other tools, and no access to secrets. I apply least agency (LLM06 Excessive Agency, #LEASTMODELPRIVILEGE): the assistant that reads untrusted pages must not also be the one that can email, purchase, or query private user data in the same turn. If both are needed, I split them — a "reader" context that produces a structured, schema-constrained summary, and a separate "actor" context that only ever sees that sanitized structure, never raw page text. This breaks the injection-to-action path even if injection succeeds.

Output side: the model's output is itself untrusted (LLM05 Improper Output Handling). I render it as inert text, strip/enforce-allowlist on markdown, never auto-render remote images (the classic pixel-exfiltration vector where injected content builds a URL with stolen data), and encode anything that reaches a browser DOM. Outbound network egress from tools is allowlisted so a crafted summary cannot beacon data out. Data: minimize what conversation memory retains (#DATAMINIMIZE), and never place high-sensitivity user data in a context that also ingests untrusted web content.

Residual risk: I state it explicitly. Injection detection is probabilistic, so I assume some injected instructions reach the model; the design's job is to make a successful injection boring — it can corrupt one summary but cannot exfiltrate data or take privileged action. I monitor for anomalous tool-call patterns and rate-limit (#RATELIMIT, LLM10 Unbounded Consumption), and I accept the residual "wrong summary" risk formally in the risk register rather than pretending it is eliminated.

Go deeper

  • "The product team wants the assistant to click links and fill forms on those pages." — Now the reader has write agency on untrusted surfaces; I require human confirmation for state-changing actions, per-domain allowlisting, and I would push back that autonomous form-filling on arbitrary sites is a materially different risk tier needing its own sign-off.
  • "Why not just fine-tune the model to ignore injected instructions?" — It raises the bar but is not a boundary; there is no training that makes instruction/data separation reliable, so it cannot be the load-bearing control. Architecture (privilege separation) is the boundary; model hardening is depth.
  • "How do you test this before launch?" — A red-team corpus of malicious pages (visible and hidden injections, homoglyphs, tool-call mimicry) run through the full pipeline, plus canary data in memory to detect exfiltration attempts.

What interviewers listen for

  • Names the content-vs-instruction trust boundary as the root cause, not "prompt injection" as a buzzword.
  • Makes privilege separation (the reader cannot act) the primary control and treats detection/filtering as secondary — the inverse ordering is a junior tell.
  • Treats model output as untrusted and closes the image/markdown exfiltration channel unprompted.
  • States residual risk explicitly and accepts it in a register rather than claiming elimination.

How to approach it

An agent is a control-flow engine whose next action is chosen by a model influenced by everything in its context — user input, tool outputs, and MCP tool descriptions. So the threat model spans the OWASP Agentic Threats taxonomy: memory poisoning, tool misuse, privilege compromise, cascading calls, and identity/trust abuse, on top of LLM01 injection and LLM06 Excessive Agency. The key move is to stop thinking "prompt security" and start thinking "authorization architecture for a nondeterministic actor." I enumerate every trust boundary before proposing controls.

A strong answer

Trust boundaries I draw: (1) between the user and the agent; (2) between the agent and each tool/MCP server — and critically, first-party and third-party MCP servers are different trust tiers; (3) between one tool's output and the agent's next decision (tool results are untrusted data, LLM01 indirect injection); (4) between the agent and the data/systems each tool can reach.

MCP-specific risks: a third-party MCP server can ship malicious or mutating tool descriptions ("tool poisoning" / rug-pull), shadow a first-party tool's name, or return outputs engineered to hijack the agent. Controls: pin and review MCP tool definitions (treat a tool-description change as a supply-chain event, LLM03), namespace tools so a third-party server cannot impersonate a privileged first-party tool, and validate that servers present verifiable identity (mTLS/signed manifests) — this is the identity/trust-abuse class in the agentic taxonomy. I do not let a third-party server's tools operate on first-party sensitive data in the same run.

Least agency, enforced outside the model (#LEASTMODELPRIVILEGE, LLM06): every tool call is authorized by a deterministic policy layer that the model cannot talk its way past — scoped, short-lived credentials per tool, per-user OAuth so the agent acts as the user with the user's permissions (never a god-mode service account), and allowlisted parameters. High-impact/irreversible actions (payment, delete, external send) require human-in-the-loop approval (#OVERSIGHT). I cap blast radius: rate limits and spend/step budgets to contain cascading or looping calls and unbounded consumption (LLM10).

Memory and context integrity: long-lived agent memory is an attack surface (memory poisoning) — I segregate untrusted tool output from durable memory, validate before persistence, and scope memory per user/session so one tenant's poisoned memory cannot influence another. Observability: full, tamper-evident trace of every prompt, tool call, arguments, and result, tied to the user identity, feeding anomaly detection and enabling incident reconstruction (NIST AI RMF Manage/Measure).

Residual risk: a compromised tool output can still steer the agent toward an authorized but undesirable action; the policy layer bounds the damage to what that user could legitimately do, and human approval gates the irreversible tail. I document that residual and monitor for it.

Go deeper

  • "How do you stop tool A's output from triggering tool B maliciously?" — Tool outputs are untrusted; the policy layer authorizes B on its own merits and on user identity, not on the agent's stated intent, and I can require confirmation for chains that cross a sensitivity boundary.
  • "Confused-deputy problem?" — Exactly the risk: the agent holds broad privilege and is tricked into using it for the attacker. The fix is acting-as-the-user with the user's own scopes, not a privileged agent identity.
  • "A vendor MCP server is popular but unaudited — ship it?" — Only sandboxed, with no access to sensitive tools/data, egress-restricted, tool definitions pinned and diffed, and treated as hostile in the threat model until reviewed.

What interviewers listen for

  • Puts authorization in a deterministic layer outside the model — "the model decides, the policy enforces."
  • Recognizes third-party MCP servers as a distinct trust tier and a supply-chain surface (tool poisoning, shadowing, mutating descriptions).
  • Uses act-as-the-user identity to defeat the confused-deputy/excessive-agency problem.
  • Explicitly bounds blast radius (budgets, HITL on irreversible actions) and keeps a full audit trace.

How to approach it

This is a process-design question, not a controls question — but the trap is designing a heavyweight gate that teams route around. Anchor on NIST AI RMF (Govern/Map/Measure/Manage) and treat AI security as an extension of the existing secure-SDLC and security management program (#SECPROGRAM, #SECDEVPROGRAM), not a parallel bureaucracy. Design risk-tiered gates so low-risk features move fast and high-risk ones get scrutiny.

A strong answer

I embed gates at the points that already exist in the SDLC. (1) Intake / Map: a lightweight triage that classifies the feature by risk tier — data sensitivity, autonomy/agency, external exposure, and whether it makes consequential decisions. Tier decides how much of the rest applies. (2) Design / threat model: mandatory for medium+ tiers, using the AI-specific catalogue (OWASP LLM Top 10, Agentic threats, ATLAS) layered on top of the normal application threat model. Output is a short prioritized risk list with chosen controls, mapped to OWASP AI Exchange control ids and owners. (3) Build: guardrail and eval requirements as code — injection tests, output-handling checks, PII/leakage evals in CI, dependency and model-provenance scanning (LLM03). (4) Pre-launch / Measure: red-team the feature proportional to tier, verify controls, and produce a residual-risk statement. (5) Launch / Manage: monitoring, abuse detection, rate limits, and an incident runbook must exist before go-live. (6) Continuous: models and prompts drift and dependencies change, so re-review on material change and on a cadence (#CONTINUOUSVALIDATION).

Ownership: product/engineering owns the risk and the controls; a security partner reviews and advises; a governance body owns the tier definitions and the accept/reject bar and holds the risk register. I explicitly avoid making security the owner of the risk — that diffuses accountability and creates the bottleneck. Anti-bottleneck design: self-service tooling (threat-model templates, an eval harness, an approved-pattern library), pre-approved reference architectures that skip most review, and a fast lane for low tier. Security's scarce attention goes to novel/high-tier work. I measure the process itself: time-in-review, escaped-defect rate, and coverage.

Residual risk: tiering can mis-triage; I mitigate with spot audits of the low-risk fast lane and a cheap escalation path when a reviewer smells something off.

Go deeper

  • "A team says the review is slowing them down." — Ask which tier and which gate; if a low-risk feature is stuck in a high-tier flow, that is a triage bug to fix, not a reason to skip review. Data usually shows the fast lane is under-used because teams do not trust it — so invest in the reference architectures.
  • "How do you handle a model-provider upgrade under the hood?" — It is a material change: re-run the eval suite and safety regression tests before promoting; provider changes have silently broken guardrails in the wild.
  • "What is the single most important artifact?" — The residual-risk statement with a named accountable owner; everything else feeds it.

What interviewers listen for

  • Risk-tiered gates and a genuine fast lane — not one heavyweight process for everything.
  • Keeps risk ownership with the delivery team; security advises, governance sets the bar.
  • Automates evals/threat-model templates so review scales; measures the process.
  • Treats provider/model changes as review-triggering events (continuous validation).

How to approach it

Detection at scale is a data-pipeline and signal-quality problem, not a single classifier. Frame it as: what telemetry gives ground truth, what layered detectors run on it, and how you correlate to catch the attacks that matter (the ones that reached a consequential action). Map to NIST AI RMF Measure/Manage and treat this as SOC engineering for a new telemetry source (#MODELINPUTMONITORING, LLM01).

A strong answer

Telemetry (the foundation): a normalized event per interaction — full prompt with provenance of each segment (user vs retrieved vs tool output), model output, every tool call with arguments and results, user/session identity, and guardrail verdicts — shipped to a central store with tamper-evidence and retention that satisfies IR. Without provenance you cannot tell an injection from a legitimate instruction, so I make segment-tagging a platform requirement, not per-app.

Layered detectors: (1) cheap deterministic signatures on untrusted segments — imperative override phrases, tool-call-shaped text, invisible/homoglyph unicode, markdown-image URLs with encoded payloads, system-prompt-leak patterns (LLM07); (2) an ML injection classifier for novel phrasing; (3) — the highest-value layer — behavioral/outcome detection: did this turn produce an anomalous tool call, an egress to a new domain, a sudden data-access spike, or output containing canary/secret material? Injection that never changes behavior is noise; injection that flips an agent into exfiltration is the alert I care about.

False-positive control: I do not alert on raw detector hits — injection-attempts are constant background. I alert on attempt correlated with consequence: an injection signature on retrieved content and a subsequent privileged tool call or novel egress in the same session. I tune with a labeled corpus, track precision/recall per detector, use canary tokens as high-fidelity tripwires (a fired canary is near-zero-FP), and route low-confidence signals to aggregate dashboards and threat-hunting rather than pages.

Scale/architecture: inline lightweight detectors in the request path (fail-open with logging so a detector outage does not take down apps), heavy ML and correlation asynchronous in the pipeline; a shared platform SDK so every app emits the same schema; feedback loop from confirmed incidents back into signatures and classifier training. Residual risk: a slow, low-and-slow injection that stays within a user's legitimate privilege can evade behavioral detection — I cover that tail with periodic canary sweeps and privilege-scoping so it has little to steal.

Go deeper

  • "Inline blocking or async detection?" — Both: block on high-confidence deterministic signals inline (fail-open), detect-and-respond async for the probabilistic majority; blocking everything inline adds latency and false-positive user pain.
  • "How do you detect injection you have never seen?" — Behavioral anomaly on outcomes and canary tripwires; signatures always lag novel phrasing, so outcome-based detection is the durable layer.
  • "What is your top data-quality risk?" — Missing segment provenance; if you can't distinguish untrusted from trusted text in the log, every downstream detector degrades.

What interviewers listen for

  • Prioritizes outcome/behavioral detection over string-matching — attempts are cheap, consequences are the signal.
  • Correlates attempt-with-consequence to control false positives instead of alerting on raw hits.
  • Designs segment provenance as a platform-level telemetry requirement.
  • Uses canary tokens as high-fidelity tripwires and closes the detection-to-training feedback loop.

How to approach it

Multi-tenancy makes cross-tenant isolation the dominant risk. Enumerate assets per tenant (model weights, inference inputs/outputs, fine-tuning data, KV cache, usage metadata) and ask, for each, "what leaks or crosses to another tenant, and via what shared component?" Layer classic cloud multi-tenancy on top of AI-specific channels. Reason asset → isolation-failure → control → residual.

A strong answer

Isolation failure modes: (1) Control-plane/authorization — a broken tenant check lets tenant A invoke or read tenant B's model; classic and highest-severity. (2) Weights confidentiality/integrity — a tenant's proprietary model exfiltrated, or model-extraction via query access (ATLAS AML.T0024) letting one tenant clone another's model through the shared API. (3) Data-plane residue — batched inference or a shared KV cache leaking one tenant's tokens into another's response; a shared vector store or embedding cache leaking documents (LLM08). (4) Side channels — timing, cache, GPU-memory residue, or a shared-cache prompt-caching feature that leaks whether another tenant asked a similar prompt. (5) Noisy-neighbor / resource DoS — one tenant's load starving others (LLM10 Unbounded Consumption). (6) Poisoning in shared fine-tuning infrastructure (LLM04) crossing tenants.

Containment: strong tenant identity threaded end-to-end with authorization enforced at every hop (never trust a tenant id passed from the client). Choose an isolation model per sensitivity tier — dedicated GPUs/namespaces or confidential-computing enclaves for high-sensitivity weights, versus soft multi-tenancy for low tiers — and be explicit about the trade-off (cost vs blast radius). Encrypt weights at rest and in transit, and scrub GPU/host memory between tenants to kill residue. Never batch across tenants in a way that shares a KV cache; if prompt-caching is offered, scope cache keys per tenant. Per-tenant rate/quota limits and isolation of the scheduler to defeat noisy-neighbor. Against extraction: per-tenant rate limiting, query monitoring for extraction-shaped patterns, and output constraints (#RATELIMIT, #MODELACCESSCONTROL). Full per-tenant audit and monitoring for cross-tenant access anomalies.

Residual risk: soft multi-tenancy always retains some side-channel residual; I document which tiers accept it and reserve hard isolation (dedicated/enclave) for tenants whose data classification requires it — and I put the accept/segment decision in the risk register with the customer's classification driving it. Model-extraction cannot be fully prevented where query access is the product; rate limiting and monitoring reduce it to an accepted, bounded residual.

Go deeper

  • "A customer demands proof their weights are safe from us, the operator." — That is the confidential-computing / attestation conversation (enclaves, remote attestation); soft isolation cannot make that promise, and I would not claim it can.
  • "Where does prompt caching bite you?" — Cross-tenant cache hits leak the existence and content of other tenants' prompts; cache keys must include tenant scope, full stop.
  • "How do you catch model extraction?" — Per-tenant query-pattern monitoring (high-volume, high-coverage, boundary-probing queries) plus rate limits; you detect and throttle, you do not eliminate.

What interviewers listen for

  • Treats cross-tenant authorization as the top risk and enforces tenant identity at every hop.
  • Knows AI-specific leakage channels: KV cache, batched inference, prompt-cache, embedding stores, GPU-memory residue.
  • Matches isolation strength (soft vs dedicated vs enclave) to data classification and names the cost trade-off.
  • Handles model extraction as a monitor-and-bound problem, not a solved one.

How to approach it

AI red-teaming spans two distinct objectives that people conflate: security red-teaming (can an adversary break confidentiality/integrity/availability — injection, exfiltration, extraction, jailbreak-to-tool-abuse) and safety/content red-teaming (can the system be induced to produce harmful, biased, or policy-violating output). Both are needed; they need different expertise and different success criteria. Frame the program against NIST AI RMF Measure and map coverage to OWASP LLM Top 10, Agentic threats, and MITRE ATLAS so you can show what you have and haven't tested.

A strong answer

Scope and coverage: I drive coverage from a taxonomy, not vibes — every LLM Top 10 category and every relevant ATLAS technique becomes a coverage cell per product, so gaps are visible. Scope includes the model, the app scaffolding (prompts, RAG, tools), and the surrounding infra (the biggest real-world wins are often boring infra/authorization bugs exposed via the AI feature, not exotic jailbreaks).

Cadence: layered. Continuous automated adversarial testing in CI (a maintained attack library run on every build) catches regressions cheaply; periodic deep manual campaigns before major launches and on a calendar for high-risk products; and continuous external signal via a bug-bounty / vulnerability-disclosure path for GenAI so the crowd finds what internal teams miss. Staffing: a small core team blending offensive security, ML expertise, and — for safety work — domain and trust-and-safety specialists, augmented by rotating product engineers (transfers knowledge and builds a defensive muscle), plus external specialist firms for high-stakes independent assessment.

Tooling: a shared harness and versioned attack corpus (injection strings, jailbreak templates, extraction probes, multi-turn attack scripts), automated fuzzing/adversarial generation, and a scoreboard tracking attack-success-rate over time per product. Findings drive change (the point of the program): findings land in the same vulnerability-management/risk-register track as all security bugs, with severity, owner, and SLA — not a slide deck. Systemic findings feed back into platform guardrails and the secure-SDLC gates so a class of bug is fixed once for everyone, and successful attacks are added to the automated regression corpus so they can never silently return.

Residual risk: red-teaming is sampling, not proof; a green board means "we didn't find it," not "it's secure." I communicate that honestly, track coverage and trend (is attack-success-rate falling release over release?), and pair red-teaming with runtime monitoring to catch what testing missed.

Go deeper

  • "How do you measure the program is working?" — Coverage against the taxonomy, attack-success-rate trend over releases, time-to-remediate, and rate of findings that convert into reusable platform guardrails/regression tests.
  • "Automated vs manual?" — Automated for breadth, regression, and cost; manual for novel, multi-step, and business-logic attacks a script won't invent. You need both; automation alone gives false confidence.
  • "Safety and security in one team?" — Shared harness and reporting, but different skill sets and success criteria; I keep them coordinated but staffed distinctly.

What interviewers listen for

  • Separates security red-teaming from safety/content red-teaming and staffs each appropriately.
  • Drives coverage from a taxonomy (LLM Top 10 / ATLAS) so gaps are explicit and measurable.
  • Closes the loop: findings become tracked risks, platform guardrails, and permanent regression tests.
  • Is honest that red-teaming is sampling, not assurance, and pairs it with monitoring.

How to approach it

This is incident response with an AI twist: the root cause is likely indirect prompt injection via a poisoned knowledge-base document (LLM01 / LLM04), and the impact is a data-disclosure/privacy incident (LLM02) that may carry breach-notification obligations. Run the standard IR lifecycle — detect/triage, contain, eradicate, recover, post-incident — but adapt each phase to the AI pipeline, and start the clock on legal/regulatory obligations immediately.

A strong answer

Triage & scope: confirm and characterize — what data, whose, how many sessions, over what window. I pull the interaction traces (this is where full prompt+retrieval+output logging pays off) to see which sessions retrieved the offending document and what was emitted. In parallel I invoke the IR plan and pull in legal/privacy early, because breach clocks (GDPR 72h, contractual, sectoral) may already be running.

Contain: stop the bleeding fast and reversibly. Options, chosen by severity: pull the malicious document from the index, disable retrieval or the whole bot, or gate outputs behind stricter filtering — I favor the narrowest action that stops disclosure (yank the document + purge it from any cache/embedding store) and escalate to disabling the feature if scope is unclear. I preserve evidence before mutating anything (snapshot the index, logs, the document) so I don't destroy the forensic trail during containment.

Eradicate & root-cause: find how the document entered the KB (ingestion path, which tenant/source, was it attacker-controlled) and why segregation/output controls didn't stop it. Hunt for other poisoned documents from the same source. The real question is systemic: why could untrusted ingested content cause cross-customer disclosure at all — that points to missing tenant scoping on retrieval, missing input segregation, or missing output checks.

Recover: restore service only once the ingestion gap and the disclosure path are closed and verified — re-scan the KB, add the attack to the regression corpus, re-enable with heightened monitoring and canaries. Post-incident: blameless review, notifications executed on legal's timeline, and durable fixes: tenant-scoped retrieval, ingestion validation/provenance for KB content, output leakage checks, and monitoring for cross-tenant retrieval anomalies. Residual risk gets re-stated: the fix should convert "any poisoned doc can cross tenants" into "poisoned doc can at worst corrupt its own tenant's answer," and that residual is accepted explicitly.

Go deeper

  • "Is this a reportable breach?" — Legal decides, but I give them the facts fast: personal data, unauthorized disclosure to a third party, scope and timeline — which typically does trigger notification duties; I never sit on it.
  • "What if you can't tell how many customers were affected?" — Assume the larger scope for containment and notification prep while forensics narrows it; under-scoping a breach is the expensive mistake.
  • "Biggest failure that makes this incident un-investigable?" — No retrieval/output logging with session and tenant identity; without it you cannot scope disclosure and are forced into worst-case notification.

What interviewers listen for

  • Runs a real IR lifecycle and pulls legal/privacy in at triage because breach clocks start immediately.
  • Preserves forensic evidence before containment mutations.
  • Drives to the systemic root cause (why cross-tenant disclosure was possible at all), not just deleting the bad document.
  • Bakes the attack into regression tests and re-states the accepted residual after the fix.

How to approach it

Poisoning is a development-time integrity attack (OWASP LLM04, MITRE ATLAS AML.T0020) with two flavors: availability poisoning that degrades general performance and, more dangerous, backdoor/targeted poisoning that plants a trigger causing specific malicious behavior while metrics look fine.
