A key strength of the per-sample statistical detectors is that they can catch transferred attacks because they judge each input independently.

The critical caveat — and the reason to push back on "just add anomaly detection" — is that anomaly/deviation detection is largely ineffective against evasion by definition. Adversarial examples are crafted to look like normal input; the whole point of a small perturbation is to stay close to the benign distribution and evade both human and deviation-based detection. So most evasion attacks will not register as anomalous. Some forms do produce detectable anomalies — adversarial patches, being localized and often physically realised — but even there real-world camera noise (the camera fingerprint) complicates detection. Not all anomalous input is malicious and not all malicious input is anomalous.

Detector networks have their own failure mode: adaptive attacks can be crafted to fool both the detector and the main model simultaneously, and detecting adversarial examples has been shown to be nearly as hard as classifying them robustly. So a detector is one fragile layer. The AI Exchange treats these controls as needing constant re-evaluation and combination with rate limiting, series-based detection, model hardening (evasion-robust design, adversarial training, input distortion) and access control — not as a standalone fix. Relevant mappings: MITRE ATLAS AML.M0015 (Adversarial Input Detection), AML.M0010 (Input Restoration), NIST AI 100-2 section 2.2.4 (randomized smoothing).

Go deeper

  • "Where does series-based detection add value over single-input detection?" — #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING catches the iterative search (many highly similar inputs, rising confidence, space-covering probes) that single-sample detectors miss, including attackers who interleave probes with unrelated requests.
  • "Why can input distortion both detect and defend?" — distorting the input can break the adversarial perturbation (defence) and, by comparing outputs on original vs distorted input, flag a likely attack (detection).
  • "Does distortion help against zero-knowledge attacks?" — less so; shattered/masked gradients do not affect query-based attacks that never use gradients.

What interviewers listen for

  • The core insight that adversarial examples are designed to be non-anomalous, so anomaly detection is structurally weak here.
  • Distinguishing anomalous-input handling from evasion-specific detection, and naming concrete methods (PCA, SSIM, feature squeezing, detector networks).
  • Treating detection as one layer needing hardening + series detection + access control, plus the adaptive-attack-on-the-detector risk.

Model answer

In a machine-learning system a confidence score is not just a user-facing explanation — it is a probabilistic signal that leaks internal model behaviour and training characteristics, so it functions as a side channel. Detailed confidence values accelerate a whole family of optimisation-based attacks: evasion (score-based black-box search approximates gradients from confidence deltas), model inversion (input is optimised to maximise confidence), membership inference (abnormally high confidence reveals training membership), and model exfiltration (richer outputs make the manufactured train set more faithful). #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE reduces the information: present the minimum precision needed (round, coarsen into ranges, add noise, or remove scores entirely). It maps to ETSI 6.4.3 and MITRE ATLAS AML.M0002 (Passive AI Output Obfuscation).

The trade-offs are real. First, usability/accuracy: any modification of the confidence or output must be evaluated so it does not unacceptably degrade the intended function. Second, it is incomplete by design — attackers can still approximate confidence by submitting similar inputs and watching whether outputs change, and it does not stop label-only membership inference which uses only the output label. Third, the AI Exchange warns it can resemble gradient masking for zero-knowledge evasion, which is a fragile defence if relied on alone — you are hiding a signal, not removing the underlying vulnerability. Effectiveness depends on architecture, training method and data distribution, so it must be validated by testing, not assumed.

You must keep confidence exposed when it is essential to the intended use — medical decision support or other safety-critical decisions where the confidence level is a genuine input to the human decision. Even then, minimise: round or add noise to expose the least necessary. And note the flip side — unusually high confidence can itself be a weak misuse signal (confidence-based anomaly detection): don't auto-block on it, but flag it for follow-up, since membership-inference probing tends to produce exceptionally high confidence.

Go deeper

  • "If it's fragile, why bother?" — it raises attacker cost across four attack classes at once and is cheap; it's a likelihood-reducer, layered with rate limiting and series detection, not a guarantee.
  • "Who should implement it, provider or deployer?" — whoever is better positioned; the provider should communicate the expectation to the deployer when the deployer owns the output surface.
  • "How does it interact with obscure-confidence-as-signal?" — even while coarsening outward-facing scores you can retain internal high-confidence detection for monitoring.

What interviewers listen for

  • Framing confidence as a side channel serving four named attack families, not just "it helps hackers".
  • The gradient-masking / label-only-MI limitations and the "validate by testing" discipline.
  • The safety-critical exception plus the minimise-anyway nuance.

Model answer

AI resource exhaustion is when specific input to the model causes depletion of funds or availability problems (a slow or unresponsive system, i.e. denial of service). The failure can come from the frequency, the volume, or — and this is the AI-specific twist — the content of the input. It maps to MITRE ATLAS Denial of ML Service and NIST AI 100-2 section 3.4.1 (Availability Violations). The impact is lost money, or an unavailable AI system that breaks dependent processes (business continuity, safety in process control, service outages).

The difference from a classic volumetric DDoS is the content vector. A sponge attack (energy-latency attack) crafts input specifically designed to maximise the model's computation time per request — you don't need a huge request volume if each request is a computational tar pit. When the model runs on metered/paid infrastructure this becomes a denial-of-wallet (DoW) attack: the attacker drains your budget rather than (or in addition to) your capacity, and malicious intensive use of a paid third-party model can rack up costs directly. So classic DDoS is fundamentally about volume overwhelming capacity; sponge/DoW attacks weaponise the per-input cost of inference, and DoW shifts the harm from availability to spend.

Controls combine generic input-threat controls with two specific ones. Generic: #MONITOR USE, #RATE LIMIT (limit request frequency per actor), and #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL (shrink the attacker population). Specific: #DOS INPUT VALIDATION — validate and sanitise to reject or correct malicious content such as excessively large inputs before they hit the model; and #LIMIT RESOURCES — cap the resource usage allowed for a single model input so no one request can consume unbounded compute. Note ISO 27002 has no dedicated control for either, so these are AI-specific gaps you handle in application logic and infrastructure quotas.

Go deeper

  • "Why isn't rate limiting sufficient?" — a sponge attack can exhaust resources within the allowed rate because each request is disproportionately expensive; you also need per-input resource caps and content validation.
  • "Give a DoW example beyond compute." — driving up token consumption or downstream paid-API calls in an agentic flow so the bill, not the server, is the casualty.
  • "How would you detect a sponge attack?" — monitor per-request latency/compute cost distribution per actor, not just request counts.

What interviewers listen for

  • The content dimension (expensive single inputs) as the AI-specific differentiator, not just volume.
  • Distinguishing denial-of-wallet (spend) from denial-of-service (availability).
  • Pairing per-input resource caps + content validation with rate limiting, and knowing rate limiting alone is insufficient.

Model answer

This is the threat that textual model output contains a conventional injection payload — for example cross-site scripting (XSS) — that becomes a vulnerability when a downstream component processes it: rendered on a web page, executed as a command, and so on. It maps to OWASP LLM05:2025 (Improper Output Handling). The AI-specific particularity is that the injection originates from the model's generated output rather than from a user field, so teams that carefully encode user input often forget that model output is equally untrusted and can carry an XSS or command payload — especially if that output was itself influenced by prompt injection.

A dangerous special form is exfiltration through output: the model is manipulated into emitting JavaScript that runs and ships sensitive data to a third party. A subtler sub-form is packing sensitive data into URLs in the output — links for the user to click, or image URLs the browser auto-fetches. By classic definition rendering an image or clicking a link is not injection, but the spirit is identical: data gets "executed" by an HTTP request, so the AI Exchange keeps it in scope. In your scenario, both the dashboard render (XSS) and the link-building (URL exfiltration) are live risks.

The fitting control is #ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT: apply context-appropriate output encoding to text model output, exactly as you would for any untrusted data entering an HTML/JS/command context — OWASP output-encoding and injection-prevention practice. The key mental model is that this is orthogonal to prompt-injection defences: prompt-injection I/O handling tries to stop the model being manipulated; output encoding assumes the output may be hostile regardless of cause and neutralises it at the consumption boundary. You need both, and you specifically need output encoding at every command-interpreting sink (browser DOM, shell, SQL, another model's prompt). For the URL sub-form, encoding is not enough — you also constrain/allow-list outbound destinations and avoid auto-rendering model-supplied image URLs.

Go deeper

  • "Why treat model output as untrusted when we wrote the system prompt?" — because indirect prompt injection or poisoning can make the model emit attacker-controlled content; provenance of the text is not trustworthy.
  • "Output that becomes another model's input?" — apply I/O handling/encoding there too, so a downstream model with weaker handling is protected (upstream application principle).
  • "How does this relate to MITRE ATLAS?" — it is referenced under AML.T0051 (LLM Prompt Injection) because injection is a common way to weaponise the output channel.

What interviewers listen for

  • Treating model output as untrusted data and naming the consumption-context encoding requirement.
  • The URL/image auto-fetch exfiltration sub-form and why encoding alone is insufficient there.
  • Positioning output encoding as orthogonal to, and additive with, prompt-injection defences.

Model answer

The relevant control is #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING. The premise is that many AI-specific attacks — model inversion, evasion search, membership inference probing, sensitive-data extraction, and model exfiltration — are invisible at the single-input level because each request looks benign; they only surface when you analyse behaviour across a series of inputs grouped by actor, source, or session. So the design goal is temporal/statistical analysis of interactions, not per-sample verdicts.

Concretely I would implement three detection families. Statistical analysis of input series: look for a run of small deviations in input space with generally rising confidence (the signature of inversion or evasion search), and for systematic patterns — very uniform, very random, or coverage of the entire input space — which indicate extraction. Behaviour-based usage detection: a single actor issuing far more inferences than normal over a window is itself a signal, complementing rate limiting. Input-optimisation pattern detection: cluster input series to find dense groups of highly similar inputs, measure pairwise similarity within a time window (not just consecutive requests), and analyse frequency/distribution to separate systematic probing from benign repetition. Considering similarity over a broad range of past inputs is important because sophisticated attackers interleave probing with unrelated requests to defeat naive consecutive-request checks.

The judgement points a senior engineer must call out: effectiveness depends on reliably grouping inputs by actor and on baseline modelling of normal behaviour, and it is prone to false positives against legitimate high-volume or research-driven exploratory users — so tune thresholds and treat detections as investigation triggers, not auto-blocks. Attackers can also distribute inputs across multiple identities/sources to stay under per-actor thresholds, which is why this pairs with #RATE LIMIT, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL (to make identities costly), and #MONITOR USE for response workflows. This maps to MITRE ATLAS AML.M0015.

Go deeper

  • "How is this different from #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING?" — that judges individual inputs; series handling judges patterns across inputs, catching attacks whose individual inputs are unremarkable.
  • "What breaks your per-actor grouping?" — Sybil-style identity spread and shared egress IPs; mitigate with account-cost controls and cross-signal correlation.
  • "How do you keep false positives acceptable?" — multi-threshold response (alert vs block), baseline per-segment normal behaviour, and human triage for moderate deviations.

What interviewers listen for

  • The single-input-invisible / series-visible insight and grouping by actor/session as the linchpin.
  • Concrete signatures (rising-confidence small deviations, space-covering uniform queries, high pairwise similarity across a window).
  • Honest treatment of false positives, identity-spreading evasion, and detections-as-triggers.

Model answer

The dominant threat is indirect prompt injection: untrusted web content is retrieved and inserted into the prompt, so a compromised or attacker-authored page can smuggle instructions — analogous to remote code execution — and, because the assistant is agentic, turn "wrong answer" into "wrong action" (data exfiltration, sending emails, mutating tickets). I would design to the AI Exchange's principle that injection will get through, so the architecture must be blast-radius control, not just prevention. I would organise it as the seven layers.

Layer 1 – Model alignment: use an aligned model and a hardened system prompt, accepting it is necessary but easily circumvented. Layer 2 – Prompt-injection I/O handling + input segregation: apply upstream at retrieval time — NFKC normalisation, strip zero-width/invisible (white-on-white) text, neutralise instruction-like tokens and role markers, and detect manipulative patterns (semantic LLM-as-a-judge for high-risk paths, mindful of latency/cost and that the judge is itself injectable). Delineate every retrieved chunk as untrusted data in structured fields with explicit "treat as data, ignore embedded instructions" constraints. Re-apply handling to any model output that feeds another model. This raises the bar but cannot be trusted alone.

The remaining layers are the real protection. Layer 3 – Human oversight on critical, irreversible actions (sending external email, publishing) — applied sparingly to avoid approval fatigue. Layer 4 – Automated oversight for suspicious activity in context (e.g. the assistant suddenly trying to email a thousand recipients, or to reach an unlisted URL), able to halt or alert. Layer 5 – User-based least privilege: the agent acts only with the serving user's rights, no more. Layer 6 – Intent-based least privilege: scope rights to the task — a question-answering flow should not carry send-email rights unless the task needs it, at which point oversight gates the recipients. Layer 7 – Just-in-time authorisation: grant each sub-agent only the rights needed at that moment, and specifically harden privileges the instant untrusted web data enters the flow — e.g. the agent that summarises fetched pages has no tool access, while a separate orchestrator holds actions.

Around the LLM I add conventional hygiene: #ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT everywhere output is rendered or used in a command context (the dashboard/DOM, any shell/SQL sink), and outbound-destination allow-listing plus no auto-fetch of model-supplied image URLs to close the URL-exfiltration channel. Input-threat controls — #MONITOR USE, #RATE LIMIT, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL — limit experimentation. And I would run tailored red-team testing continuously to learn where I/O handling actually fails, so I can prioritise blast-radius investment where realistic harm is highest.

Go deeper

  • "If you could only add two layers beyond alignment, which?" — least privilege (intent/JIT) and automated oversight, because they contain harm regardless of how the injection succeeds.
  • "How do you segregate agents so a poisoned summariser can't act?" — split orchestration from content processing; the content-touching agent holds no credentials to the mail server or ticket API.
  • "Where do you place the I/O handling?" — as far upstream as possible, at the retrieval/API boundary, scoped to the untrusted source, so obfuscation is caught before it spreads and downstream components are protected.

What interviewers listen for

  • Assuming injection succeeds and prioritising blast-radius control / least privilege over detection.
  • Concretely applying the seven layers, especially JIT authorisation hardening when untrusted data enters, and agent segregation.
  • Not forgetting output encoding, URL-exfiltration, and continuous tailored testing.

Model answer

These three input threats share an attack pattern — iterative querying — so the controls overlap heavily, but they also pull in different directions, and a staff-level answer names both. Start from governance: #AI PROGRAM to establish that the model is a trade secret and that evasion/inversion risk applies, then risk-manage which controls are warranted.

Shared query-limiting controls: #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL shrinks the attacker population (authenticated, contracted API access), #RATE LIMIT slows bulk querying, #MONITOR USE plus #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING detect space-covering extraction sequences and rising-confidence inversion/evasion search, and #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING flags the out-of-normal inputs extraction tends to generate. #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE attacks all three at once by starving the side channel that score-based evasion, inversion optimisation, and membership inference rely on. For inversion/membership specifically, #SMALL MODEL and regularisation reduce memorisation. For evasion robustness, development-time model hardening: evasion-robust design, adversarial training, input distortion, adversarial-robust distillation. For post-theft accountability, #MODEL WATERMARKING (ideally entangled watermarks, since ordinary watermarks live in OOD data an exfiltrator won't query). Protect the parameters themselves against direct leak (runtime model confidentiality, TEE, obfuscation) because a stolen exact model makes every other attack trivially efficient and enables offline transfer attacks with no rate limit.

Where they conflict: (1) Confidence exposure — obscuring confidence helps security across all three, but if any customer use case is safety- or decision-critical you may be contractually required to expose calibrated scores; the resolution is minimise-not-remove (round/coarsen/noise) and keep internal full-precision confidence for monitoring. (2) The AI Exchange flags that obscuring confidence can resemble gradient masking, a fragile defence that gives a false sense of security against zero-knowledge evasion — so I would not lean on it alone and would validate by testing. (3) Rate limiting and series detection are strong against on-platform extraction and inversion search, but useless against transfer attacks: an attacker who exfiltrates a rough copy, or uses a similar public surrogate, does the evasion search offline. That means anti-exfiltration and model-hardening controls carry the evasion risk that rate limiting cannot. (4) Aggressive anomalous-input/series thresholds create false positives against legitimate high-volume customers — a paid API's best customers may look like extractors — so detections must be investigation triggers with human triage, not hard blocks. (5) Smaller models reduce inversion risk but may cost accuracy; that is a product/security trade-off to make explicitly.

Finally, the honest caveat: if the model is accessible and permits intensive use, exfiltration is hard to prevent outright; detection collapses to "intensive, wide-coverage use" which is ambiguous. So the realistic strategy is raise cost and preserve legal recourse (watermarking, contracts) rather than promise prevention — unless the model is publicly available, in which case the exfiltration threat is moot.

Go deeper

  • "A key customer trips your extraction detector weekly — what do you do?" — segment baselines per customer tier, raise their threshold with contractual monitoring, and alert-not-block; treat it as an investigation, since benign high-volume use is expected.
  • "Rank the controls if budget is tight." — access control + rate limit + monitoring first (cheap, broad), obscure confidence next (cheap, hits three threats), then model hardening and watermarking.
  • "Why protect parameters so heavily?" — a direct leak yields an exact copy, collapsing exfiltration effort and enabling perfect-knowledge offline evasion that bypasses every runtime control.

What interviewers listen for

  • Recognising the shared query-based root and reusing controls across all three threats.
  • Naming genuine conflicts: confidence exposure vs safety use, gradient-masking fragility, rate-limiting useless vs transfer attacks, false positives on paying power users, small-model accuracy cost.
  • The mature conclusion that exfiltration is often unpreventable, so raise cost + keep legal recourse.

Model answer

Using a supplied pretrained model and fine-tuning it is the textbook supply-chain model poisoning scenario, and because you continue training on it, specifically a transfer learning attack surface: the supplied model may already carry a backdoor (planted via data poisoning at the source, or by direct parameter/architecture manipulation — malicious custom/lambda layers, weight edits, or deserialization attacks that execute during model unpacking), and the unwanted behaviour survives your fine-tuning. This maps to OWASP LLM03 (Supply Chain) and, for the poisoning behaviour, LLM04:2025. In a fraud-decision product a targeted backdoor is the nightmare case: a specific transaction signature (say, a precise amount) is silently classified as not-fraud. It is hard to detect because there is no reviewable code path, parameters are opaque, and your normal test set has blind spots exactly where the trigger lives.

The decisive judgement is which controls are even available to you as the downstream party. Controls that require protecting parameters at the moment of manipulation — i.e. everything that had to happen in the supplier's environment — are out of your hands. You cannot retroactively secure the supplier's training database, and you cannot apply training-data-only controls (#DATA QUALITY CONTROL, #MORE TRAIN DATA, #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION) to poisoning already baked into the pretrained weights, because those apply only during training and not to an already-trained model. What you can apply post-hoc: #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE (provenance, artifact verification — MITRE ATLAS AML.M0014, an AI-BOM per AML.M0023, verify signatures/hashes, prefer reputable sources), #POISON ROBUST MODEL which explicitly applies to externally obtained models (fine-pruning; Selective Amnesia, which removes backdoor effects using a tiny clean dataset without needing to know the trigger and works against unknown backdoors), adversarial-robust distillation and adversarial training to improve generalisation and dilute memorised triggers, and #MODEL ENSEMBLE so a poisoned model's deviating output can be caught. Guard the unpacking step itself: treat model loading as code execution risk (deserialization/lambda-layer malware) and sandbox it — this is #DEV SECURITY, not an ML control.

Operationally I would: verify provenance and integrity before use; load/convert the model only in a segregated, sandboxed environment (#SEGREGATE DATA, #CONF COMPUTE) to contain deserialization attacks; apply poison-robust remediation (fine-pruning + Selective Amnesia) after obtaining it; deploy behind an ensemble and run #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION to catch behavioural deviation over time; and design blast-radius control — a fraud decision that flips to "not fraud" on a narrow trigger should not be fully autonomous for high-value transactions, so keep human oversight / secondary checks on the highest-stakes decisions. STRIP-style backdoor detection at inference (flagging anomalously low prediction entropy under input perturbation) is a useful monitoring addition.

The honest limitation to state: because a targeted backdoor triggers only on attacker-chosen inputs, no amount of normal testing guarantees its absence; the controls reduce the probability and the impact but cannot prove the model is clean. So the risk decision is partly a governance/appetite call — would I accept an unverified upstream model in a fraud-critical path at all?

Go deeper

  • "Why does fine-tuning not just wash out the backdoor?" — the poison is a memorised trigger association; ordinary fine-tuning on clean data may not overwrite it, which is why targeted remediation (Selective Amnesia, pruning) exists.
  • "What's special about model loading?" — deserialization/custom-layer payloads can execute code during unpacking; sandbox and prefer safe serialization formats.
  • "Where does model ensemble break down?" — the more heavily the shared upstream model was poisoned, the less an ensemble derived from it helps; independent provenance matters.

What interviewers listen for

  • Identifying transfer-learning / supply-chain poisoning and the targeted-backdoor detection problem for a fraud product.
  • The sharp distinction between controls you can apply downstream (poison-robust-model, supply-chain-manage, ensemble, sandboxed loading) vs training-time/supplier-only controls you cannot.
  • Naming concrete post-hoc remediation (fine-pruning, Selective Amnesia, STRIP) and closing with the governance-level acceptance decision.
Field Reference

Cheat Sheets

Condensed, exam-ready references distilled from the full guide. Everything here is covered in depth inside the modules.

Controls Quick Reference

Every OWASP AI Exchange control ID on one page — grouped by category, one line each: what it does and where it applies. The Exchange organizes 50+ controls into 4 essential categories (Manage, Have resilient models, Watch, Limit) and 8 sub-categories; general governance controls apply to all threats.

The big picture: 4 categories, 8 sub-categories

CategorySub-categoryControl groups
ManageGovernance, Risk & ComplianceAI program, dev program, compliance, education
Supply chain managementSuppliers of data, models, and hosting
Extended conventional securityDev-time security + runtime security for AI assets
Have resilient modelsData/model engineering controlsRobustness against evasion, poisoning, disclosure — work of data/model engineers
WatchModel I/O handlingRuntime filtering, stopping, alerting on suspicious input/output
MonitoringOverseeing model inference and general usage
LimitMinimize/obfuscate sensitive dataModels can leak or be misled into leaking
Limit model behaviourModels can be wrong or manipulated — apply Murphy's law

Governance (apply to ALL threats)

  • #AI PROGRAM — establish AI governance: inventory of AI applications and ideas, risk analysis, policy, responsibilities; covers risks both by and to AI systems. Applies organization-wide.
  • #SEC PROGRAM — extend the information security management system to include AI assets (training data, models, augmentation data, I/O), AI threats, and AI systems. Drives security risk assessment (ISO/IEC 27005).
  • #DEV PROGRAM — extend software development practices to include AI engineering activities (even if you don't train your own models).
  • #SECDEV PROGRAM — extend the secure development program: AI threat modelling, AI-specific security testing across engineering teams.
  • #CHECK COMPLIANCE — verify compliance with AI regulation (e.g., EU AI Act — outcome-based, requires documented risk assessment, control selection, rationale, and evidence).
  • #SEC EDUCATE — train engineers and security professionals on AI threats and their controls.

Data limitation (Limit — minimize/obfuscate sensitive data)

  • #DATA MINIMIZE — remove or reduce sensitive data that is not needed; applies to training data, augmentation data, and model input/output.
  • #ALLOWED DATA — restrict data used to what is permitted for the purpose (policy on lawful/approved data). Applies at data collection and use.
  • #SHORT RETAIN — keep data (and logs) only as long as needed; shortens the exposure window. Applies dev-time and runtime.
  • #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA — anonymize, pseudonymize, or add noise to sensitive training data so leaks and inference attacks reveal less. Applies dev-time.
  • #DISCRETE — limit publicly available technical details of the AI system (model, data, documentation) that would help attackers prepare attacks. Applies dev-time.

Limit unwanted behaviour (blast radius control)

  • #OVERSIGHT — human or rule-based oversight of model output and actions; a key control for agentic AI (assume prompt injection can still happen). Runtime.
  • #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE — minimize the privileges and capabilities of the model/agent so manipulation cannot escalate; never assign access-control responsibility to the model itself. Runtime architecture.
  • #MODEL ALIGNMENT — (GenAI) train/tune the model to refuse unwanted instructions and behaviour; first defensive layer against prompt injection. Model engineering.
  • #AI TRANSPARENCY — inform users that AI is used and about its limitations, so reliance is calibrated. Runtime/organizational.
  • #EXPLAINABILITY — make model outputs explainable so wrong or manipulated behaviour can be recognized. Model engineering + runtime.
  • #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION — continuously test model behaviour/accuracy to detect degradation or the effects of poisoning. Dev-time and runtime.
  • #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING — test for unwanted bias to detect undesired or manipulated model behaviour. Dev-time and runtime.

Runtime input controls (Watch — Model I/O handling + monitoring)

  • #MONITOR USE — monitor model inference and general usage for suspicious patterns; extends I/O handling and oversight. Runtime, all input threats.
  • #RATE LIMIT — throttle request frequency to hinder exhaustion, model exfiltration, inversion, and evasion probing. Runtime, all input threats.
  • #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL — authenticate and authorize model users; base defense for every model-use attack surface. Runtime.
  • #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING — detect and handle statistically anomalous input (evasion indicator). Runtime.
  • #EVASION INPUT HANDLING — detect and handle inputs crafted as adversarial examples. Runtime.
  • #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING — detect suspicious series of inputs (exfiltration harvesting, inversion, membership inference, probing). Runtime.
  • #PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING — filter/detect prompt injection attempts in model input and output; covers direct and indirect injection. Runtime.
  • #INPUT SEGREGATION — separate untrusted data from instructions so embedded commands aren't followed; the specific control for indirect prompt injection. Runtime.
  • #INPUT DISTORTION — slightly alter input before inference to break adversarial perturbations (evasion). Runtime.
  • #SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING — filter sensitive data out of model output before it reaches the user (disclosure in output). Runtime.
  • #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE — hide or round confidence scores so attackers can't fine-tune evasion/inversion attacks. Runtime.
  • #DOS INPUT VALIDATION — validate/reject input designed to consume excessive resources (AI resource exhaustion). Runtime.
  • #LIMIT RESOURCES — cap compute/resources per request to preserve availability. Runtime.

Evasion-robustness (resilient models — model engineering)

  • #EVASION ROBUST MODEL — build/choose a model inherently robust to adversarial input perturbations. Dev-time model engineering.
  • #TRAIN ADVERSARIAL — include adversarial examples in training so the model resists them; also listed against data poisoning. Dev-time.
  • #ADVERSARIAL ROBUST DISTILLATION — distill a student model with smoothed decision boundaries, harder to evade. Dev-time.

Dev-time controls (conventional security for AI engineering)

  • #DEV SECURITY — secure the development/engineering environment (access control over training data, models, code, config) against dev-time leaks and poisoning.
  • #SEGREGATE DATA — segregate data and environments so a breach of one doesn't expose training data or models.
  • #CONF COMPUTE — confidential computing (e.g., Trusted Execution Environment) to protect data and model parameters while in use.
  • #FEDERATED LEARNING — train on decentralized data so sensitive data stays at its source; reduces central train-data exposure.
  • #SMALL MODEL — limit model capacity so it memorizes less training data; counters model inversion / membership inference. Design-time choice.

Supply chain

  • #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE — vet and manage suppliers of data, models, and hosting: provenance, integrity checks, contracts, and clarity on which controls are the supplier's responsibility. Critical for ready-made models (self-hosted or provider-hosted) — for external hosting, cover the hosting too and ask: where does the model run, what are retention rules, what is logged/monitored, is input used for training?

Poison-robustness (resilient models — model/data engineering)

  • #POISON ROBUST MODEL — train the model so poisoned samples have less influence. Dev-time.
  • #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION — transform/distort training data to neutralize poisoned samples. Dev-time.
  • #DATA QUALITY CONTROL — inspect and clean training data to detect and remove poison. Dev-time.
  • #MORE TRAINDATA — enlarge the training set so any poisoned fraction is diluted. Dev-time.
  • #MODEL ENSEMBLE — combine multiple models so one poisoned/manipulated model can't decide the outcome. Dev-time design, runtime effect.

Runtime conventional controls (protect AI assets in operation)

  • #RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITY — protect the deployed model (files/memory) from tampering — counters direct runtime model poisoning ("reprogramming").
  • #RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY — protect the integrity of data flowing to/from the model in operation.
  • #RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY — protect deployed model parameters from theft (access control, encryption, TEE) — counters direct runtime model leak.
  • #MODEL OBFUSCATION — obfuscate the deployed model so even with access an attacker can't easily extract it.
  • #MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITY — protect confidentiality of model input data in transit and storage — counters input data leak.
  • #ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT — encode/sanitize model output so it can't carry injection attacks (SQL/XSS) into downstream systems.
  • #AUGMENTATION DATA CONFIDENTIALITY — protect augmentation data (RAG data, system prompts) from leaking.
  • #AUGMENTATION DATA INTEGRITY — protect augmentation data from manipulation that would steer model behaviour.

Threat → frontline controls (condensed periodic table)

Governance applies to every row; "limit unwanted behaviour" applies to every model-behaviour-integrity row.

ThreatAsset / impactKey controls on top of the generals
Direct prompt injectionModel behaviour / Integrity#PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING, #MODEL ALIGNMENT, monitor + rate limit + access control
Indirect prompt injectionModel behaviour / Integrity#INPUT SEGREGATION, #PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING, blast radius control (#OVERSIGHT, #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE)
Evasion (adversarial examples)Model behaviour / Integrity#EVASION INPUT HANDLING, #EVASION ROBUST MODEL, #TRAIN ADVERSARIAL, #INPUT DISTORTION, #ADVERSARIAL ROBUST DISTILLATION
Direct runtime model poisoningModel behaviour / Integrity#RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITY, #RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY
Dev-time model poisoningModel behaviour / Integrity#DEV SECURITY, #SEGREGATE DATA, #FEDERATED LEARNING, #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE, #MODEL ENSEMBLE
Data poisoning (train/finetune)Model behaviour / IntegrityDev-time base controls plus #MORE TRAINDATA, #DATA QUALITY CONTROL, #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION, #POISON ROBUST MODEL, #TRAIN ADVERSARIAL
Supply-chain model poisoningModel behaviour / IntegrityProducer: #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE; supplier: dev-time controls; plus #MODEL ENSEMBLE
Disclosure in outputTraining data / ConfidentialityData limitation + #SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING + monitor/rate limit/access control
Model inversion / membership inferenceTraining data / ConfidentialityData limitation + #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE, #SMALL MODEL, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING
Direct training data leakTraining data / ConfidentialityData limitation + #DEV SECURITY, #SEGREGATE DATA, #FEDERATED LEARNING
Model exfiltration (I/O harvesting)Model params / ConfidentialityMonitor + rate limit + access control, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING
Direct runtime model leakModel params / Confidentiality#RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY, #MODEL OBFUSCATION
Direct dev-time model leakModel params / Confidentiality#DEV SECURITY, #SEGREGATE DATA, #FEDERATED LEARNING
AI resource exhaustionModel behaviour / Availability#DOS INPUT VALIDATION, #LIMIT RESOURCES, #RATE LIMIT
Augmentation data leak / manipulationAugmentation data (incl. system prompts) / C+I#AUGMENTATION DATA CONFIDENTIALITY, #AUGMENTATION DATA INTEGRITY
Input data leakModel input / Confidentiality#MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITY
Output contains conventional injectionAny asset / CIA#ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT
Generic runtime threatsAny asset / CIAConventional runtime security controls
Generic dev-environment / supply-chain threatsAny asset / CIAConventional development security + supply chain management

Night-before memory hooks

  • Manage – Resilient – Watch – Limit: the 4 essential categories; 8 sub-categories; 50+ controls.
  • Governance controls apply to all threats; add threat-specific controls on top.
  • Many controls are expensive and involve trade-offs — controls that change the learning process or data distributions can hurt accuracy; introduce with care.
  • Conventional vs AI-specific: encrypting the training database is conventional (see OpenCRE); adding noise to the training set is AI-specific. TEE protecting model parameters = conventional control with an AI-specific aspect.
  • Agentic AI: prompt injection (mostly indirect) is the key threat; after alignment and filtering, assume injection still happens → blast radius control via #OVERSIGHT + #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE; never delegate access control to the model.
  • Ready-made model split: supplier owns training/alignment/dev security; you own runtime, augmentation data, governance, and #SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE. Self-hosted adds runtime model poisoning/leak to your plate; provider-hosted means your data leaves in clear text.
  • The cheapest control of all: remove an unnecessary AI component and all AI-related risks disappear.

Interview Day One-Pager

The 60-second frameworks from the OWASP AI Exchange to have on instant recall when the interviewer says "let's talk about securing AI."

Framework 1 — The reasoning chain: Asset → Threat → Control → Residual risk

  • Asset first. The Exchange organizes everything by asset, impact and attack surface. Name the AI-specific assets: model behaviour (integrity, availability), training data (confidentiality), the model itself (confidentiality / IP), model input and augmentation data incl. system prompts (confidentiality, integrity).
  • Threat = attack surface × asset. A threat becomes a risk only when it exploits a vulnerability. Threat modeling answers three questions: (1) which threats theoretically apply, (2) how could they realistically happen, (3) what would the impact be.
  • Control per threat. Every threat maps to controls in the periodic table; general governance controls (#AIPROGRAM, #SECPROGRAM, #SEC EDUCATE) apply to all threats.
  • Residual risk gets treated. Four treatment strategies: Mitigation, Transfer, Avoidance, Acceptance — tracked in a Risk Register (severity, treatment plan, ownership, status). Likelihood × severity = risk level, visualized as a heatmap (ISO/IEC 27005 for security risk management; ISO 31000 / ISO 23894 for the framework).
  • Filter hard. Use the one-pager logic: "When" column — does the threat apply in theory? "Impact" column — does the harm level warrant treatment? Start big, end with a small list of risks that matter.

Framework 2 — The lifecycle-phase model (where the attack surface lives)

Phase / surfaceRepresentative threatsKey controls
Runtime — model use (provide input / read output)Direct & indirect prompt injection, evasion (adversarial examples), disclosure in output, model inversion / membership inference, model exfiltration via I/O harvesting, resource exhaustion#MONITOR USE, #RATE LIMIT, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL, #PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING, #INPUT SEGREGATION, #EVASION INPUT HANDLING, #SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING, #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE, #DOS INPUT VALIDATION, #LIMIT RESOURCES
Runtime — break into deployed modelDirect runtime model poisoning (reprogramming), direct runtime model leak#RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITY, #RUNTIME MODEL IO INTEGRITY, #RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY, #MODEL OBFUSCATION
Development — engineering environmentDevelopment-time model poisoning, data poisoning of train/finetune data, training data leak, dev-time model leak#DEV SECURITY, #SEGREGATE DATA, #FEDERATED LEARNING, #DATA QUALITY CONTROL, #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION, #POISON ROBUST MODEL, #MODEL ENSEMBLE
Development — supply chainSupply-chain model poisoning; generic dev-environment and supply chain threats#SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGE covering data, models AND hosting suppliers
Runtime — all IT (conventional)Input data leak, output contains conventional injection, generic runtime security threats (SQL injection, password guessing...)#MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITY, #ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT, conventional runtime security controls

Framework 3 — Answering "How would you secure an AI system?" (the 6-move answer)

  • 1. Clarify the architecture first: train your own model, or a ready-made one? Self-hosted (on-prem most secure, VPC secure if the cloud provider is trusted) or provider-hosted? Agentic? This decides which threats even apply — most controls are familiar conventional security unless you train your own model.
  • 2. Govern: inventory AI use, assign responsibilities, policies, compliance (#AIPROGRAM, #SECPROGRAM).
  • 3. Threat model by asset & attack surface (Framework 1 + 2) — architecture, context, domain, use case.
  • 4. Layer controls, four deep-dive layers: General controls (governance, sensitive-data limitation via #DATA MINIMIZE/#SHORT RETAIN/#OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA, limit unwanted behaviour via #OVERSIGHT/#LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE/#MODEL ALIGNMENT); Input-threat controls; Development-time controls; Runtime conventional controls.
  • 5. Watch and constrain: model I/O handling (filter, stop, alert on suspicious input/output), #MONITOR USE, resilient models (#TRAIN ADVERSARIAL, #EVASION ROBUST MODEL, #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION).
  • 6. Close with residual risk and assurance: document risk assessment, control selection, rationale and evidence — mandatory under the EU AI Act, which is outcome-based rather than control-focused like ISO/IEC 27001.
  • For provider-hosted models, ask the four questions: Where does the model run (a "private instance" may be just the API — your data leaves in clear text)? What are the data retention rules (a US court once forced OpenAI to retain logs)? What exactly is logged and monitored (opt-out may need specific licenses)? Is input used for training (usually not)? Remember: a provider-hosted model needs your input in clear text — but the vendor may still protect that environment better than you can protect your own.
  • Org-level version — G.U.A.R.D: Govern, Understand, Adapt, Reduce, Demonstrate. Govern with an AI program; Understand which threats apply and who (you vs. supplier) owns each control; Adapt existing practices — threat modeling, testing, supply chain management, secure development; Reduce risk; Demonstrate with documented evidence.

Top mistakes candidates make

MistakeThe Exchange-grounded correction
Reciting the LLM Top 10 as "AI security"Top 10s are intentionally incomplete awareness lists (e.g., no prompt security). AI is more than GenAI: analytical, discriminative, generative AND heuristic systems — even a rule-based system or statistical lookup table is in scope.
Treating every theoretical threat as a riskDon't protect against model inversion if the training data isn't sensitive; indirect prompt injection on an agent stays theoretical if there's no exfiltration path (e.g., no email capability). Threat ≠ risk until it exploits a vulnerability with real impact.
Forgetting conventional securityMost applicable controls are familiar conventional countermeasures unless you train your own model. SQL injection and password guessing still apply; model output can carry conventional injection (#ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT).
Ignoring the shared-responsibility splitWith ready-made models the provider owns training/alignment/dev-environment security; you own runtime, augmentation data, governance and supply chain. Self-hosting adds direct runtime model poisoning to YOUR threat list because the model artifact lives in your infrastructure.
Defining security too narrowlyThe Exchange definition: preventing unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification or destruction — where modification includes manipulating model behaviour in unwanted ways.
Only technical answers, no governance or assuranceGovernance controls apply to all threats; new regulation (EU AI Act) is outcome-based, so you must show risks are sufficiently mitigated — risk register, rationale, evidence.
No answer for shadow AIEmployees will use free public GenAI. The best fix is a good sanctioned alternative — secure, privacy-preserving, sufficient quality — plus making shadow-AI risks very clear.
Fine-tuning-only view of poisoningPoisoning has three doors: direct dev-time model poisoning, data poisoning of train/finetune data, and supply-chain model poisoning — plus runtime reprogramming of a deployed model.

Number drops that land well

  • OWASP AI Exchange: 300+ pages, OWASP Flagship, CC0 (no attribution needed), feeds ISO/IEC 27090 (AI security), ISO/IEC 27091 (AI privacy) and the EU AI Act (~70 pages contributed each) via official CEN/CENELEC liaison.
  • Risk framework steps: Identify → Evaluate (likelihood × severity heatmap) → Treat (mitigate/transfer/avoid/accept) → Communicate & monitor (risk register) → Repeat.
  • Deployment options for ready-made models: provider-hosted closed source (only option for the largest models), self-hosted open weights (most secure on-prem), open weights at a paid hosting service (convenient).

Prompt Injection & GenAI Defense

The OWASP AI Exchange playbook for LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection: know the two attack types, the ten attack classes, the seven defense layers, and the two core runtime controls — and always assume injection gets through.

Direct vs Indirect — the one distinction you must nail

Direct prompt injectionIndirect prompt injection
Who injectsThe user themselves crafts the malicious promptA third party hides instructions in content the app inserts into the prompt
Mental model"Social engineering of a generative AI""Similar to remote code execution" — untrusted data executed as instructions
Who is harmedUsually only the attacker gets the output (exception: shared context between users)Innocent users / the system — unwanted answers or agentic actions
Examples"Ignore previous directions…"; jailbreaks; system prompt leakage (DPD chatbot 2024, Chevy bot 2023)White-on-white text in a job application ("invite this person"); poisoned website read by an LLM plugin (Rehberger's GitHub demo); hidden pixels in an image
MITRE ATLASAML.T0051.000AML.T0051.001
NIST AI 100-2sec. 3.3sec. 3.4
Extra control—#INPUT SEGREGATION (indirect-specific)
  • Jailbreak = direct injection specifically aimed at defeating alignment/safety training. Two strategies: (1) abuse competing objectives (helpfulness vs. safety); (2) input that is out-of-distribution for the safety training but in-distribution for the base model (e.g., odd encodings).
  • Multimodal injection: models OCR images, fuse modalities into one latent space, and treat every modality as an instruction channel — hidden image text acts as a "soft-prompt"/meta-instruction. Can be direct (attacker uploads the image) or indirect (app auto-pulls untrusted media).

Direct-injection attack classes (a–j)

  • a) Role-play / conditioning — "act as an unrestricted expert", fake example answers.
  • b) Overriding system instructions — "ignore everything you were told"; stronger after system-prompt leakage.
  • c) Encoding / obfuscation — base64, emojis, typos, odd casing, mixed languages to slip past filters.
  • d) Payload splitting — harmless pieces that combine into a harmful instruction; defeats single-input checks.
  • e) Non-text inputs — instructions in images, audio, metadata, embedded objects.
  • f) Context extraction — leak system prompt, prior messages, documents, API keys.
  • g) Format manipulation — change I/O format to dodge checks and output filters.
  • h) Multi-turn gradual steering — innocent start, slow escalation across turns.
  • i) Extremely long prompts — drown safety instructions; warnings "lost" for both model and reviewer.
  • j) Training-data extraction — handled separately as disclosure in model output (LLM02:2025).

The seven layers of prompt-injection protection

OWASP AI Exchange mantra: "weak alone, strong together." No layer suffices; layers 3–7 are blast radius control — assume injection succeeds and minimize harm.

#LayerIdeaKnown flaw
1Model alignmentPre-training, RLHF, system prompts telling the model to behaveModels stay easy to mislead out of the box
2Prompt injection I/O handling ("defense")Sanitize, filter, detect injections in input and outputArms race; false positives/negatives; tailored testing needed to know its limits
3Human oversight (HITL)Human approves selected critical actionsOnly strong if used sparingly — approval fatigue, cost, delay, missing expertise
4Automated oversightLogic flags suspicious in-context activity (e.g., summarizer sending 1,000 emails)Reactive — fires only after behavior emerges; prevention beats it
5User-based least privilegeAgent gets only the served user's rights, assigned in advanceUsers hold far more rights than the agent needs — blast radius still big
6Intent-based least privilegeRights scoped to the specific task (summarizer: read-only email)Intent not always known upfront; multi-agent flows over-provision
7Just-in-time authorizationRights granted per subtask/context at that moment; harden privileges when untrusted data enters the flowMost granular — e.g., the summarizing sub-agent gets zero mail-server access

#PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING (runtime control, layer 2)

  • Sanitize characters: Unicode normalization (NFKC), character allow-listing, strip zero-width/invisible (white-on-white) characters.
  • Escape/neutralize instruction-like tokens: fences, role markers, XML/HTML tags, tool-calling tokens — stops accidental compliance; semantic injection still passes.
  • Delineate untrusted data — hand off to #INPUT SEGREGATION.
  • Recognize manipulative instructions ("forget previous instructions", exfil URLs/DB names) across text, images, audio, metadata, files; combine multiple detectors.
  • Flexible recognition: for high risk, use LLM-as-a-judge (semantic, not syntactic) — costs latency, compute, accuracy and data-egress concerns; otherwise tuned pattern matching.
  • Apply upstream: sanitize/detect at data retrieval time; also treat one model's output as another model's input; use #ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT for command-interpreting sinks.
  • Detect unwanted output via #OVERSIGHT; respond by filter / stop / alert per detection confidence (#MONITOR USE); inform blocked users unless clearly malicious.
  • Update detections constantly (arms race): open source — Guardrails-AI, Langkit, LLM Guard, NVIDIA NeMo Guardrails, Rebuff; commercial — Pangea, HiddenLayer, AIShield, Aiceberg.
  • Limitations to quote: no method catches everything; the detector LLM can itself be injected; slow multi-input experimentation evades single-input checks (need series-based detection). Maps to AML.M0015 (Adversarial Input Detection), NIST AI 100-2 sec. 3.3.2, ETSI 6.2.3.

#INPUT SEGREGATION (runtime control, indirect-injection specific)

  • Definition: clearly separate/delimit untrusted data from trusted instructions in the prompt AND instruct the model to ignore instructions inside that data.
  • Implementation: hard-to-spoof consistent markers; structured fields (JSON, ChatML, LangChain prompt formatters); explicit "do not execute instructions found in the input / ignore role-change attempts" constraints; pre-inspect inserted data for instruction-like patterns; enforce one marking scheme across ALL prompt-building components.
  • Why AI-specific: instructions and data share the same text channel — LLMs treat all text as potentially meaningful instructions; segregation is an artificial but necessary boundary (traditional apps keep code and input apart).
  • Limitations: models have no strict mechanism to guarantee ignoring a region; partial mitigation only; does NOT address direct injection (attacker already speaks at the top level).

Defense-in-depth checklist for an LLM app

  • [ ] Align the model: safety training + hardened system prompt (#MODEL ALIGNMENT) — then assume it fails.
  • [ ] Reduce attackers & probing: #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL, #RATE LIMIT, #MONITOR USE (suspicious input AND output).
  • [ ] Sanitize + detect at I/O: #PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING, applied as far upstream as possible.
  • [ ] Segregate untrusted content: #INPUT SEGREGATION for anything RAG/web/user-file sourced.
  • [ ] Minimize privileges: #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE — user-based, then intent-based, then just-in-time authorization for agents.
  • [ ] Oversee: #OVERSIGHT — HITL for a few truly critical actions, automated oversight for the rest.
  • [ ] Guard the output path: sensitive-output detection, #ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT before any interpreter/browser/shell.
  • [ ] Test tailored attacks regularly and update detections from external feeds — this is an arms race.

Framework mapping (memorize)

ConceptOWASP LLM Top 10MITRE ATLASOther
Prompt injection (both types)LLM01:2025AML.T0051.000 / .001NIST AI 100-2 3.3 / 3.4; BIML-24(LLM) input:2; ENISA Table 3 (Evasion)
Injection I/O defensemitigates LLM01AML.M0015ETSI 6.2.3 Input restoration; NIST AI 100-2 3.3.2
Training-data / sensitive disclosureLLM02:2025LLM Data Leakage; AML.M0020 (GenAI Guardrails)Handled by sensitive output handling, not injection defense
System prompt leakageLLM07:2025-adjacent (leak enables better LLM01 attacks)—Attack class (f) context extraction

Interview sound bites

  • "Direct injection is social engineering of the model; indirect injection is remote code execution via data."
  • "Detection is necessary but insufficient — the real strategy is blast radius control: assume the injection lands and make sure it can't do damage."
  • "Layers 1–2 try to stop the injection; layers 3–7 limit what a successful one can do."
  • "The LLM-as-a-judge that screens prompts is itself an LLM — and itself injectable."
  • "HITL is a strong control precisely until you overuse it — approval fatigue turns it into a rubber stamp."

Standards & Frameworks Crosswalk

How the OWASP AI Exchange relates to every major AI-security standard, regulation, and taxonomy — who does what, and which document to reach for when.

The AI Exchange as the hub

  • The OWASP AI Exchange is a comprehensive, open-source (CC0) body of AI threats, controls, and a risk-analysis process — covering both predictive AI and GenAI, development-time and runtime.
  • Its stated role is to feed international standards: it serves as official OWASP liaison input to ISO/IEC 27090, ISO/IEC 27091, and the EU AI Act security work at CEN/CENELEC JTC 21.
  • Navigation aids to remember: the Periodic table of AI security (maps every threat to its controls), the threat model one-pager (owaspai.org/go/threatmodel/), and the decision tree / threat advisor (owaspai.org/go/threattree/).
  • Control IDs are hash-tagged, e.g. #AIPROGRAM, #SECPROGRAM, #SUPPLYCHAINMANAGE, #LEASTMODELPRIVILEGE, #CONTINUOUSVALIDATION — other frameworks are mapped onto these IDs.
  • Risk-rating vocabulary in the Exchange is deliberately borrowed from existing methods: ISO/IEC 27005, FAIR (Factor Analysis for Information Risk), and OWASP risk rating (attacker motive, opportunity, capability, susceptibility, incident data).

The crosswalk

Framework What it is Scope Relation to the AI Exchange
EU AI Act Binding EU regulation (2024/1689); risk-based tiers: prohibited / high-risk / limited / minimal, plus GPAI-model duties. Legal compliance: prohibited practices (e.g. social scoring), high-risk requirements incl. accuracy, robustness & cybersecurity (Art. 15), transparency, human oversight. Exchange is the security how-to behind the law: liaison input to the harmonized standards drafted at CEN/CENELEC JTC 21. Governance controls #AIPROGRAM + #CHECKCOMPLIANCE operationalize it (impact analysis must check for prohibited use cases).
ISO/IEC 27090 ISO/IEC (SC 27) guidance standard, in development: security guidance for AI systems — addressing security threats and failures in AI. How organizations should address AI-specific security threats; extends the 27000-series into AI. Primary standards destination for Exchange content — the Exchange was built to feed its threat/control material into 27090 via official liaison.
ISO/IEC 27091 Companion ISO/IEC standard, in development: privacy protection guidance for AI and ML. AI privacy risks and their treatment (data minimization, rights of data subjects, etc.). The Exchange’s privacy-relevant controls (e.g. #DATAMINIMIZE, #SHORTRETAIN, #ALLOWEDDATA, #OBFUSCATETRAININGDATA) align here; security–privacy overlap is explicit in the Exchange (transparency, explainability as shared concerns).
NIST AI RMF Voluntary US risk-management framework (AI RMF 1.0, 2023) with a GenAI Profile (NIST AI 600-1). Whole-of-lifecycle AI risk: four functions Govern / Map / Measure / Manage; trustworthiness characteristics (safe, secure & resilient, explainable, privacy-enhanced, fair…). Same “risks TO and BY AI” altitude as the Exchange’s #AIPROGRAM; the Exchange supplies the concrete security threats and controls that RMF’s Measure/Manage functions call for. RMF = process scaffold; Exchange = security content.
OWASP LLM Top 10 Awareness top-10 of the most critical vulnerabilities in LLM applications (OWASP GenAI Security Project). GenAI/LLM apps only; entries like LLM01 Prompt Injection, LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure, LLM05 Improper Output Handling. Subset lens: the Exchange covers all AI (predictive + GenAI) with full controls and maps each GenAI particularity to an LLM Top 10 entry (see quick map below). The Exchange also flags gaps: input data leak and model inversion / membership inference are not covered in the LLM Top 10.
MITRE ATLAS Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems: ATT&CK-style knowledge base of attacker tactics, techniques, and real-world case studies against AI. Offense-oriented: how attacks actually unfold (e.g. AML.T0051 LLM Prompt Injection); fuel for red teaming and threat intel. Complementary axis: ATLAS enumerates attacker TTPs; the Exchange organizes the same attack space by defender decisions — threats with applicability conditions (“When”), impacts, and mapped controls.
OpenCRE Open Common Requirements Enumeration: a graph linking equivalent requirements across standards (OWASP, ISO, NIST, CWE…) via Common Requirement IDs. Standard-to-standard interoperability for all of security, not just AI. The connective tissue: Exchange controls are linked through OpenCRE so one control resolves to its counterparts in ISO 27002, NIST, ASVS, etc. (Same community lineage — Rob van der Veer co-founded both.)
NCSC/CISA Joint Guidelines UK NCSC + US CISA Guidelines for Secure AI System Development, co-sealed by many national agencies. Four lifecycle phases: secure design, development, deployment, operation & maintenance. The Exchange publishes a full mapping of every guideline to its control IDs (e.g. “secure your supply chain” → #SUPPLYCHAINMANAGE; “monitor behaviour” → #CONTINUOUSVALIDATION, #UNWANTEDBIASTESTING). Permalink: owaspai.org/go/jointguidelines/.
DSIT Code of Practice UK DSIT Code of Practice for the Cyber Security of AI — builds on the joint guidelines. Reorganizes into 13 principles, adds governance and post-market aspects (Principle 10: end-user communication; Principle 13: proper data and model disposal). Mapped principle-by-principle onto the same Exchange controls as the joint guidelines (Principle 1 → #SECEDUCATE, Principle 3 → risk analysis under #SECPROGRAM, Principle 7 → #SUPPLYCHAINMANAGE).

Quick map: GenAI particularities → OWASP LLM Top 10

AI Exchange GenAI particularityLLM Top 10 entry
Natural-language control of models → direct & indirect prompt injectionLLM01 Prompt Injection
Huge training sets, no built-in access privileges → sensitive/licensed data in outputLLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure
Pre-trained (foundation) models may be manipulatedLLM03 Supply Chain
Internet-scale, hard-to-control training sources (e.g. hijacked domains)LLM04 Data and Model Poisoning
Output can carry injection payloads (e.g. XSS)LLM05 Improper Output Handling
Confident hallucination + underestimated manipulation risk; mitigate with least model privilegeLLM06 Excessive Agency, LLM09 Misinformation
Costly to run → overload creates unwanted cost (resource exhaustion)LLM10 Unbounded Consumption
Input data leak (prompts + RAG context to cloud, access rights not respected)Not covered in LLM Top 10
Model inversion / membership inference (typically low-to-zero risk for GenAI)Not covered (apart from LLM02’s different angle)

Exam-ready one-liners

  • Regulation vs. guidance: only the EU AI Act is law; NIST AI RMF, the joint guidelines, and DSIT are voluntary; ISO/IEC 27090/27091 become the certifiable-guidance layer.
  • Attack vs. defense taxonomies: MITRE ATLAS = attacker TTPs; OWASP AI Exchange = defender threats + controls; OpenCRE = the crosswalk engine between everything.
  • Breadth: LLM Top 10 = awareness for LLM apps; the AI Exchange = full coverage of predictive AI + GenAI — “from a security threat perspective, GenAI is not that different from predictive AI.”
  • Risk methods reuse: the Exchange doesn’t reinvent risk rating — likelihood factors are sourced from OWASP, FAIR, and ISO/IEC 27005; treatment options are the classic four: mitigate, transfer, avoid, accept.
  • Governance hook: #AIPROGRAM covers risks TO AI (security) and risks BY AI (fairness, safety) — and its impact analysis must check EU AI Act prohibitions (e.g. social scoring) via #CHECKCOMPLIANCE.
  • If asked “where do I start?”: threat model one-pager → decision tree (“When” applies? → “Impact” matters?) → Periodic table for controls → map obligations to EU AI Act / ISO via the crosswalk above.

Threat Taxonomy Cheat Sheet

The complete OWASP AI Exchange threat map on one page: every AI threat located by lifecycle phase and attack surface, each with its primary control(s).

The map in one breath

  • Three threat lanes: (1) development-time threats (data obtained/prepared, model trained or obtained — e.g. data poisoning); (2) runtime via use ("input threats" through inference: provide input, read output — e.g. prompt injection, evasion); (3) runtime not via use (attack the running system directly — e.g. stealing model input).
  • Formula: AI security = threats to AI-specific assets (AI Exchange) + threats to other assets (conventional resources). The Exchange is an extension of your existing security program, not a replacement.
  • Three attacker goals → six impacts: disclose (confidentiality of train/test data; of model IP/parameters; of input or augmentation data), deceive (integrity of model behaviour), disrupt (availability of the model; plus C/I/A of non-AI assets).
  • AI assets: training data, augmentation data (incl. system prompts / RAG data), the model (parameters), model input, model output.
  • Same impact, different surfaces: training-data confidentiality falls to a database hack at development time or to a membership inference attack at runtime through the model API.
  • Multi-step attacks combine lanes: poison the training DB, then trigger the corrupted behaviour with crafted input; or steal the model at development time to craft evasion inputs offline, then present them to the deployed system.

Runtime threats — via use (input attacks through inference)

ThreatAsset / ImpactOne linePrimary control(s)
Direct prompt injectionModel behaviour / IntegrityAttacker-as-user crafts instructions to manipulate model behaviour#PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING, #MODEL ALIGNMENT, then assume failure → #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE, #OVERSIGHT
Indirect prompt injectionModel behaviour / IntegrityAttacker-controlled data (web page, doc, email) reaches the model as content and carries instructions — the key agentic threat#PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING, input provenance/segregation, blast-radius control (#LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE, #OVERSIGHT)
Evasion (adversarial examples)Model behaviour / IntegrityCraft input data to force wrong decisions#EVASION ROBUST MODEL, #TRAIN ADVERSARIAL, #EVASION INPUT HANDLING, #INPUT DISTORTION
Disclosure in outputTraining / augmentation data / ConfidentialityModel output reveals training data or augmentation data (incl. system prompt)#SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING, #DATA MINIMIZE, #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA
Model inversionTraining data / ConfidentialityReconstruct training data from model outputs#OBSCURE CONFIDENCE, #RATE LIMIT, #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA
Membership inferenceTraining data / ConfidentialityFeed a person's data in; output details reveal whether they were in the training set#OBSCURE CONFIDENCE, #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA, #DATA MINIMIZE
Model exfiltration (input–output harvesting)Model parameters / ConfidentialityReplicate the model by mass querying; a stolen copy devalues IP and lets attackers craft input attacks offline, unnoticed#RATE LIMIT, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING, #MONITOR USE, #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE
AI resource exhaustionModel / AvailabilityUse of the model drives excessive cost or outage#LIMIT RESOURCES, #DOS INPUT VALIDATION, #RATE LIMIT

Runtime threats — not via use (break into the deployed system / all IT)

ThreatAsset / ImpactOne linePrimary control(s)
Direct runtime model poisoning ("reprogramming")Model behaviour / IntegrityBreak into the deployed model and alter its parameters/behaviour#RUNTIME MODEL INTEGRITY, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL
Augmentation data manipulationModel behaviour / IntegrityTamper with runtime augmentation data (system prompts, RAG stores) to steer behaviour#AUGMENTATION DATA INTEGRITY, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL
Direct runtime model leakModel parameters / ConfidentialitySteal the deployed model from storage/memory#RUNTIME MODEL CONFIDENTIALITY (e.g. TEE), #CONF COMPUTE, #MODEL OBFUSCATION, #MODEL ACCESS CONTROL
Direct augmentation data leakAugmentation data / ConfidentialityBreak in and read system prompts / RAG data#AUGMENTATION DATA CONFIDENTIALITY
Input data leakModel input / ConfidentialitySensitive prompts (possibly enriched with retrieved company secrets) leak#MODEL INPUT CONFIDENTIALITY, #SHORT RETAIN, #DATA MINIMIZE
Output contains conventional injectionAny asset / C-I-AModel output carries SQLi/XSS into downstream components — treat output as untrusted#ENCODE MODEL OUTPUT
Generic runtime security threatsAny asset / C-I-AConventional attacks on the app & infrastructure (SQL injection, password guessing, phishing)#SEC PROGRAM + conventional runtime controls (see OpenCRE)

Development-time threats

ThreatAsset / ImpactOne linePrimary control(s)
Data poisoning (train/finetune data)Model behaviour / IntegrityManipulate training data to manipulate model behaviour#DEV SECURITY, #DATA QUALITY CONTROL, #POISON ROBUST MODEL, #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION, #SEGREGATE DATA
Direct development-time model poisoningModel behaviour / IntegrityBreak into the engineering environment and tamper with the model itself#DEV SECURITY, #SEGREGATE DATA, #MODEL ENSEMBLE
Supply-chain model poisoningModel behaviour / IntegrityObtained/ready-made model arrives poisoned from a supplierSupply chain management (provenance for data, models, hosting), #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION
Supply-chain data poisoningModel behaviour / IntegrityExternal/sourced data arrives poisonedSupply chain management, #DATA QUALITY CONTROL
Direct training data leakTraining data / ConfidentialityBreak into the training/engineering environment and exfiltrate data#DEV SECURITY, #DATA MINIMIZE, #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA, #DISCRETE
Direct development-time model leakModel parameters / ConfidentialitySteal the model (or its recipe: process + data) from the dev environment#DEV SECURITY, #DISCRETE
Conventional dev-environment & supply-chain threatsAny asset / C-I-AGeneric attacks: vulnerable components, compromised pipelines#SECDEV PROGRAM, #DEV PROGRAM + conventional supply-chain security

Control families (4 categories → 8 sub-categories → 50+ controls)

  • Manage — extend Governance/Risk/Compliance (#AI PROGRAM, #DEV PROGRAM, #CHECK COMPLIANCE, #SEC EDUCATE); extend supply chain management to data, models, hosting; extend conventional security to AI assets (#SEC PROGRAM, #SECDEV PROGRAM, #DEV SECURITY, runtime integrity/confidentiality controls above).
  • Have resilient models — data/model engineering against input attacks: #MODEL ALIGNMENT, #EVASION ROBUST MODEL, #POISON ROBUST MODEL, #TRAIN ADVERSARIAL, #TRAIN DATA DISTORTION, #MODEL ENSEMBLE, #MORE TRAINDATA, #SMALL MODEL, #DATA QUALITY CONTROL, #FEDERATED LEARNING, #ADVERSARIAL ROBUST DISTILLATION.
  • Watch — model I/O handling (filter/stop/alert): #ANOMALOUS INPUT HANDLING, #EVASION INPUT HANDLING, #UNWANTED INPUT SERIES HANDLING, #PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING, #DOS INPUT VALIDATION, #SENSITIVE OUTPUT HANDLING, #OBSCURE CONFIDENCE, #RATE LIMIT; plus #MONITOR USE.
  • Limit — minimize/obfuscate sensitive data (#DATA MINIMIZE, #ALLOWED DATA, #SHORT RETAIN, #OBFUSCATE TRAINING DATA) and limit model behaviour (#OVERSIGHT, #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE, #AI TRANSPARENCY, #EXPLAINABILITY, #CONTINUOUS VALIDATION, #UNWANTED BIAS TESTING). Murphy's law: the model can always be wrong or manipulated — so cap the damage.
  • Trade-off warning: many controls are expensive and trade off against accuracy; controls that change the learning process or data distributions can have unintended downstream side effects.

Agentic AI threats

  • Everything above still applies — agents are AI systems and software systems; agentic AI adds attention points, not a new rulebook.
  • Four properties, four exposures: Action (agents invoke functions → #LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE is key); Autonomous (agents trigger each other → #OVERSIGHT; working memory becomes an attack vector — it holds the agent's state and plan); Complex (behaviour is emergent); Multi-system (access-control responsibility gets delegated to the AI via instructions → manipulable by prompt injection).
  • Never assign access control to the GenAI model/agent — build it into the architecture. Hallucinations and prompt injections can change commands or escalate privileges.
  • Confused deputy: agents deployed with their own permissions are privilege-escalation vectors; revisit trust boundaries because agents change interconnectivity and data flows.
  • Control triad: Prevention (secure inter-model communication e.g. MCP, memory integrity, prompt injection defenses) → Blast radius control (rule-based/human oversight, least model privilege) → Observability (monitoring).
  • Working assumption: after alignment, filtering, and detection, prompt injection (especially indirect) can still happen — blast radius control is therefore critical, not optional.

The lethal trifecta (Simon Willison)

LegConditionBreak it with
1. DataAttacker-controlled data reaches the LLM during a privileged user's session (indirect prompt injection)Input provenance, #PROMPT INJECTION I/O HANDLING
2. AccessThe LLM or connected agents can read sensitive data#LEAST MODEL PRIVILEGE, #DATA MINIMIZE
3. SendThe LLM or connected agents can initiate sending data out to the attackerEgress restriction, #OVERSIGHT
  • All three legs together = sensitive-data exfiltration in agentic AI. Removing any one leg defeats the attack — the cheapest defense is usually cutting the Send capability.

Memory hooks

  • Where can it happen? Development-time vs runtime; runtime splits into via use (inference) vs not via use (break-in / all IT).
  • Poison pairs with leak: every asset (training data, model, augmentation data) can be poisoned (integrity) or leaked (confidentiality), at development time or runtime — that symmetry generates most of the matrix.
  • Attacker with a model copy can prepare input attacks efficiently and without being noticed — why model confidentiality matters beyond IP.
  • Indirect prompt injection is the key threat in most agentic AI systems.
  • Removing an unnecessary AI component eliminates all AI-related risks — think before you build.
About

About this Academy

This learning platform is a study companion built on the OWASP AI Exchange — the flagship OWASP project ("the world's AI security guide", 327 pages, generated 7/7/2026) that feeds directly into the EU AI Act and ISO/IEC 27090/27091 through official standard partnerships. The Exchange is published under CC0 by the AI security community, founded by Rob van der Veer in 2022.

Every module tracks the source document section by section, enriched with cross-references to the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications, MITRE ATLAS, and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework. Permalinks (owaspai.org/go/…) throughout let you jump to the living, continuously updated source.

Your progress, quiz scores, flashcard schedule, streak, and interview drill history are stored in your browser — and when you sign in, synced to your account so you can continue on any device.

Your data & settings

Export a backup of your progress, restore it on another device, or reset everything on this browser. Signed-in progress also syncs automatically.
